ai_sections.why: Without these rules, providers could end up judging care they were personally involved in, or colleagues could review each other's work, which could affect whether reviews are fair.

press_names: Veterans Affairs Peer Review Neutrality Act

title: A bill to prohibit gender transition procedures on minors, to authorize the Secretary of Health and Human Services to impose civil penalties on persons who perform gender transition procedures on minors, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for any doctor or medical professional to perform gender transition procedures — such as puberty-blocking drugs, hormone treatments, or surgeries — on anyone under 18 years old, anywhere in the United States. If a provider violates this ban, the federal government's health department can fine them at least $100,000 per violation, and the minor (or their parent or guardian) can also sue the provider in federal court for medical costs, emotional distress, and potentially additional punitive damages — with up to 25 years after the minor turns 18 to file a lawsuit. The bill exempts certain medical care from this ban, including treatment for intersex conditions, emergency procedures that prevent death or serious bodily harm, and puberty suppression used specifically for the recognized medical condition of precocious (abnormally early) puberty. The bill also creates a grant program, funded by the collected fines, that gives money to private nonprofit organizations to help individuals who want to reverse a prior gender transition procedure, providing them with medical advice, mental health services, and education or employment assistance — though any organization that performs or refers for gender transition procedures (or most abortions) is ineligible for those grants.
ai_headline: Senate bill would ban youth gender transition care
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal bill would ban gender transition procedures for anyone under 18 and allow fines and lawsuits against providers who violate the ban.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it a federal violation for any medical provider to perform gender transition procedures — including puberty blockers, hormone treatments, or surgeries — on a person under 18. Exceptions exist for treating intersex conditions, emergency care that prevents death or serious harm, and puberty suppression for abnormally early puberty. The bill also sets up a grant program to help people who want to reverse a prior gender transition procedure.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects minors under 18, their parents or guardians, and any medical professionals who perform these procedures. Organizations that want to receive grants must not perform or refer patients for gender transition procedures or most abortions.
ai_sections.why: Medical providers who violate the ban could face federal fines and civil lawsuits. Minors or their families would have up to 25 years after the minor turns 18 to file a lawsuit seeking medical costs, emotional distress damages, and potentially more.
ai_sections.cost: Fines start at $100,000 per violation and are paid by the provider who broke the law. Money collected from those fines would fund the grant program for organizations helping people reverse prior gender transition procedures.

title: A bill to amend the Higher Education Act of 1965 to require the use of an identity fraud detection system in reviewing Free Applications for Federal Student Aid.
ai_summary: This bill requires the U.S. Department of Education to use an automated identity fraud detection system to review every FAFSA (Free Application for Federal Student Aid) submitted starting October 1, 2026, looking for signs that someone may be using a fake or stolen identity to apply for financial aid. If an application raises a red flag, the Department must notify both the applicant and any college the applicant listed, explaining that the application is under suspicion. Before that college can hand over any federal financial aid money, it must first confirm the applicant's identity in person or through a live video call, and then report that verification back to the Department. The bill affects college-bound students applying for federal aid, the Department of Education, and colleges and universities that receive and distribute federal financial aid. The Department is also required to report to Congress on how the system works and how effective it is, both before it launches and on an annual basis afterward.
ai_headline: Education Dept. to screen FAFSA applications for identity fraud
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting Oct. 1, 2026, every FAFSA will be screened for identity fraud before federal aid can be released.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the Department of Education to use an automated system to check every FAFSA for signs of fake or stolen identities. If an application looks suspicious, the applicant and their listed colleges must be notified. Before releasing any federal aid money, the college must verify the applicant's identity in person or by live video call.
ai_sections.who: This affects students applying for federal financial aid, colleges and universities that distribute that aid, and the Department of Education. The Department must also report to Congress on how the system works, before it launches and every year after.
ai_sections.why: The screening requirement means some applicants flagged by the system will face extra identity verification steps before receiving aid. Colleges take on a new responsibility to confirm identities and report results back to the Department.

title: A bill to amend the Clayton Act to provide for the divestiture of certain transactions, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill requires that large corporate mergers and acquisitions worth $10 billion or more that took place between January 20, 2025, and January 19, 2029, be undone — meaning the merged companies must be separated back into independent businesses — unless the companies can prove in court that the deal did not significantly reduce competition in any market. It also allows federal antitrust agencies and state attorneys general to review any merger from that same time period, even smaller ones, and order those to be unwound if there is evidence the government's review process was improperly influenced — for example, by political pressure, conflicts of interest, foreign interference, or misrepresentations to regulators. Companies that fail to complete a court-ordered separation on time can face daily fines, penalties tied to the deal's total value, and fines against individual executives and board members, and if no one complies a court-appointed trustee can take over and sell off the assets. The bill also extends the time limit for bringing antitrust lawsuits from four years to ten years, giving the government and private parties a longer window to challenge problematic deals.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets $10B mergers made under Trump
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Large mergers worth $10 billion or more completed between 2025 and 2029 may be forced to reverse unless companies prove they did not harm competition.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require companies that merged or were acquired for $10 billion or more between January 20, 2025, and January 19, 2029, to split back into separate businesses unless they can prove in court the deal did not reduce competition. Federal agencies and state attorneys general could also review smaller mergers from that period and order them reversed if the approval process was improperly influenced, such as through political pressure, conflicts of interest, foreign interference, or false information given to regulators. The bill also extends the time allowed to file antitrust lawsuits from four years to ten years.
ai_sections.who: Large corporations that completed mergers or acquisitions during that four-year window would be directly affected. Federal antitrust agencies, state attorneys general, courts, and in some cases court-appointed trustees would be involved in reviewing and enforcing the rules.
ai_sections.why: Companies that do not complete a required separation on time could face fines and penalties. If no one at a company complies with a court order, a court-appointed trustee could be given control to sell off the company's assets.
ai_sections.cost: Companies that miss court-ordered deadlines could face daily fines, penalties based on the total value of their deal, and fines against individual executives and board members.

title: Dennis and Lois Krisfalusy Act
ai_summary: This bill expands who can receive a free government-issued memorial headstone or marker from the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA). Currently, certain family members of veterans — such as spouses or dependents who were buried somewhere other than a national cemetery — can only get one of these markers if they died on or after November 11, 1998. This bill removes that date restriction, meaning eligible family members who died **before** that date would now also qualify for a VA-furnished memorial marker. In practice, this could allow veterans' families to finally obtain an official government marker for loved ones who passed away years or decades ago and were previously excluded simply because of when they died.
ai_headline: VA memorial markers extended to families of veterans who died before 1998
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1127 removes a 1998 cutoff date so more veterans' family members can receive a free VA memorial marker.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes a date restriction that currently blocks some veterans' family members from receiving a free government memorial headstone or marker. Under current law, certain family members buried outside a national cemetery only qualify if they died on or after November 11, 1998. This bill would extend eligibility to family members who died before that date as well.
ai_sections.who: This change affects veterans' family members, such as spouses or dependents, who were buried somewhere other than a national cemetery and died before November 11, 1998.
ai_sections.why: Families who were previously excluded due to the date their loved one died may now be able to request an official government marker. This means some families could obtain a marker for a loved one who passed away years or decades ago.

title: Women Veterans Specialty Care Access Act
ai_summary: Women veterans who are enrolled in VA healthcare would be able to book their own appointments for certain women's health services — specifically gynecology, obstetrics, maternity, and postpartum care — without first needing a referral or approval from a primary care doctor. This applies both to care provided directly by VA facilities and to care received through the VA's community care program, which allows veterans to see outside providers. Veterans could schedule these appointments by phone, online, or through any other booking method the VA already offers. The VA would also be prohibited from adding any other extra steps or approvals that could slow down access to these services.
ai_headline: VA women veterans gain direct access to gynecologic care
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: Women veterans could book gynecology, maternity, and postpartum care on their own, without a referral, at VA or outside providers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would let women veterans schedule certain health appointments on their own, without getting a referral or approval from a primary care doctor first. It covers gynecology, obstetrics, maternity, and postpartum care. The VA could not add extra steps or approvals that would slow down access to these services.
ai_sections.who: This applies to women veterans who are already enrolled in VA healthcare. They could use VA facilities or outside providers through the VA's community care program.
ai_sections.why: Right now, women veterans must go through a primary care doctor to access these services. This bill would remove that step for the covered types of care.

press_names: Women Veterans Specialty Care Access Act

title: Veteran Burial Benefit Correction Act
ai_summary: This bill raises the amount the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) pays toward burial and funeral costs when a veteran dies from a disability directly caused by their military service. Currently, the VA pays up to $2,000 in those cases; this bill would increase that limit to $3,000. Going forward, the $3,000 cap would also automatically adjust each year to keep pace with inflation, using the same Consumer Price Index measure that tracks general price changes across the country. This change affects the families or estates of veterans whose deaths are officially linked to a service-connected condition, as they would receive a larger reimbursement to help cover funeral and burial expenses.
ai_headline: VA burial benefit for service-connected deaths rises to $3,000
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4108 raises the VA burial payment for service-connected veteran deaths from $2,000 to $3,000, with annual inflation adjustments after that.
ai_sections.what: This bill increases the maximum amount the VA pays toward burial and funeral costs when a veteran dies from a service-connected disability, raising the cap from $2,000 to $3,000. After that, the cap would automatically rise each year to keep up with inflation, tied to the Consumer Price Index.
ai_sections.who: This affects the families or estates of veterans whose deaths are officially linked to a disability caused by their military service.
ai_sections.why: Families in those situations would receive a larger reimbursement from the VA to help cover funeral and burial expenses. The automatic inflation adjustment means the payment amount would not stay frozen over time.
ai_sections.cost: The VA would pay out up to $3,000 per eligible case instead of the current $2,000 limit, with that amount rising over time as inflation adjusts it upward.

title: To clarify that the baseline is based on current laws and the assumption of continuation of current levels of discretionary appropriations, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill changes the rules for how the federal government calculates its official budget "baseline" — the forecast of future spending and revenues used as a starting point for budget decisions. Under the new rules, the baseline must assume that current spending levels simply continue as-is, without automatically adding projected increases for inflation or other factors that the current method includes. It also removes certain other adjustments, such as assuming that some temporary or emergency spending will keep growing over time. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO), which produces these projections, would also be required to publish alternative budget scenarios alongside its main forecast, developed in consultation with the House and Senate Budget Committees. This primarily affects how Congress, budget analysts, and policymakers measure whether new legislation increases or decreases spending relative to the baseline, which in turn can influence how proposed cuts or increases are scored and debated.
ai_headline: Bill would reshape how CBO calculates spending baselines
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Budget.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8569 changes how the government's starting budget forecast is built, removing automatic inflation increases from the baseline calculation.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes the rules for how the federal government builds its official budget baseline, which is the starting forecast used when making spending decisions. Under the new rules, the baseline would hold current spending flat instead of automatically adding increases for inflation or growing temporary spending over time. The Congressional Budget Office would also be required to publish additional budget scenarios alongside its main forecast.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects Congress, the Congressional Budget Office, and the House and Senate Budget Committees. Budget analysts and policymakers who use the baseline to measure the impact of new legislation would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Because the baseline is the measuring stick for scoring new legislation, changing how it is built can affect whether proposed spending changes are counted as cuts or increases. This in turn shapes how budget debates and votes play out in Congress.

title: To amend the Fair Credit Reporting Act to prohibit consumer reporting agencies that furnish consumer reports for tenant screening purposes from providing certain information, to establish duties of users of consumer reports for housing purposes, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill changes what information can be included in a background check report when a landlord is deciding whether to rent housing to someone. Specifically, it would prohibit tenant screening companies from reporting a long list of items, including arrests that didn't lead to a conviction, juvenile records, successfully completed diversion programs, any conviction for which a person has already finished their sentence or is on probation or parole, fines or fees related to court costs or incarceration, and any record that has been expunged, sealed, or pardoned. The bill also requires landlords who reject a rental applicant based on a background check to notify that applicant within three days and explain specifically what information in the report led to the denial. The people most directly affected are renters and rental applicants — particularly those with any kind of criminal history — as well as landlords and the companies that provide tenant screening reports.
ai_headline: Bill would limit criminal history in tenant screening reports
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8588 limits what background check companies can report to landlords and requires landlords to explain rejections within 3 days.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes the rules for tenant screening reports used by landlords. It would ban reporting arrests without convictions, juvenile records, completed diversion programs, finished sentences, probation or parole, court fines and fees, and expunged or sealed records. Landlords who reject an applicant based on a background check would have to notify that person within three days and explain exactly what information led to the denial.
ai_sections.who: Renters and rental applicants — especially those with any criminal history — would be most directly affected. Landlords and tenant screening companies would also face new rules about what they can report and how they handle rejections.
ai_sections.why: Limiting what can appear in a screening report means some applicants with past legal records could be considered for housing that they might otherwise be denied. Requiring landlords to explain denials means applicants would know what specific information was used against them.

title: To require States to hold special elections to fill vacancies in the House of Representatives not later than 180 days after a vacancy occurs in the House of Representatives, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: If a seat in the U.S. House of Representatives becomes empty — because a member dies, resigns, or is unable to serve — this bill would require the affected state to hold a special election to fill that seat within 180 days of the vacancy opening up. The only exception is if a regularly scheduled general election for that same seat is already happening within that 180-day window, in which case the state can simply wait for that election. The bill affects state governments, which currently have wide flexibility in deciding when (or even whether) to hold such special elections. To make sure states follow the new deadline, the bill allows the U.S. Attorney General, private citizens harmed by a violation, or even the House Speaker or Minority Leader to sue the governor of a non-complying state in federal court to force compliance.
ai_headline: House vacancy elections must occur within 180 days
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: States would have 180 days to hold a special election when a U.S. House seat becomes vacant, or face a federal lawsuit.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require states to hold a special election within 180 days whenever a U.S. House seat becomes empty. The one exception is if a regularly scheduled general election for that seat is already set to happen within that same 180-day period.
ai_sections.who: State governments would face a new deadline they currently do not have. The U.S. Attorney General, private citizens harmed by a violation, or the House Speaker or Minority Leader could sue a non-complying state governor in federal court.
ai_sections.why: Right now, states have wide flexibility in deciding when or whether to hold special elections, meaning some vacant seats may go unfilled for extended periods. This bill would change that by setting a firm deadline and creating a legal path to enforce it.

title: To clarify the eligibility of certain student populations for child care assistance under the Child Care and Development Block Grant Act of 1990.
ai_summary: This bill expands who can qualify for federally funded child care assistance by clarifying that parents who are enrolled in certain educational or job-training programs — including programs accredited by a college or university, programs at schools accredited by a Department of Education-recognized agency, apprenticeships or training programs registered with the Department of Labor or a state labor agency, and programs approved by a state to train and certify nursing assistants — are considered to be engaged in an approved "activity" that makes them eligible for child care help. Before this change, the eligibility rules were less specific about which types of programs counted, which may have left some student-parents uncertain about whether they qualified. The bill primarily affects low-income parents who are pursuing education or vocational training and need financial help paying for child care while they attend their programs. It also authorizes $850 million per year in additional funding for child care assistance from 2027 through 2031 to help cover the costs of serving these families.
ai_headline: Student-parents gain clearer path to federal child care aid
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8590 spells out which education and job-training programs make low-income student-parents eligible for federal child care help.
ai_sections.what: This bill clarifies that parents enrolled in certain programs — such as college-accredited courses, Department of Labor-registered apprenticeships, and state-approved nursing assistant training — qualify for federally funded child care assistance. Before this change, the rules were less specific about which programs counted, leaving some student-parents unsure whether they were eligible.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects low-income parents who are pursuing education or vocational training and need help paying for child care while attending their programs.
ai_sections.why: Without clear rules about which programs qualify, some eligible families may not have applied or received child care assistance. This bill removes that uncertainty by naming specific program types in the law.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $850 million per year in additional funding for child care assistance from 2027 through 2031 to help cover the costs of serving these families.

title: To amend the National Nutrition Monitoring and Related Research Act of 1990 to codify the Dietary Guidelines for Americans, 2025-2030, issued jointly by the Secretary of Agriculture and the Secretary of Health and Human Services, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would change how the federal government's official dietary guidelines — the recommendations that tell Americans what to eat for good health — are updated and put into effect. Right now, the Secretaries of Agriculture and Health and Human Services can issue updated guidelines on their own every five years; under this bill, they would still develop and propose new guidelines, but those guidelines could only take effect if Congress formally approves them by passing a law. The bill also locks in the current **Dietary Guidelines for Americans, 2025–2030** (issued January 7, 2026) as the official standard, and states that those guidelines cannot be changed or replaced unless the new process is followed. This affects anyone whose life is touched by federal nutrition policy, including the roughly 40 million Americans who participate in federal food programs like school lunches, SNAP, and WIC, since those programs are required to align with the Dietary Guidelines.
ai_headline: Bill would require Congress to approve dietary guidelines
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would need to approve any future Dietary Guidelines before they take effect, instead of leaving that to federal agencies alone.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes who has the final say over the federal Dietary Guidelines for Americans. Right now, two cabinet secretaries can update those guidelines on their own every five years. Under this bill, they can still write and propose new guidelines, but Congress would have to pass a law to make them official. The bill also locks in the 2025-2030 guidelines as the current standard until any new ones go through this process.
ai_sections.who: This affects the agencies that write the guidelines and the lawmakers who would now vote on them. It also affects the roughly 40 million Americans in federal food programs like school lunches, SNAP, and WIC, since those programs must follow the Dietary Guidelines.
ai_sections.why: Because federal food programs are required to align with the Dietary Guidelines, any delay or disagreement in Congress over approving new guidelines could affect how those programs operate. The 2025-2030 guidelines would stay in place until Congress acts.

title: To prohibit the use of funds to implement, administer, or enforce measures requiring certain employees to refer to an individual by the preferred pronouns of such individual or a name other than the legal name of such individual, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would bar the use of federal money to carry out, manage, or enforce any rule, policy, or guidance that requires federal employees or contractors to use a person's preferred pronouns (when those pronouns differ from the person's biological sex as defined in the bill) or to call someone by a name other than their legal name. In practical terms, this means federal agencies could not make it a workplace requirement for their staff or contractors to use pronouns or names that differ from a person's legal identity as defined by these standards. Any federal employee or contractor who believes their agency has violated this restriction can file a written complaint, and if the agency's response is unsatisfactory, they can sue the agency in court within one year of the alleged violation. A court could order the agency to stop the practice, award the employee money for damages, and require the government to pay the employee's attorney fees, with any punitive damages capped at $100,000.
ai_headline: Bill would ban federal pronoun mandates for workers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies and contractors could not be required to use an employee's preferred pronouns or a name other than their legal name.
ai_sections.what: This bill would stop federal money from being used to carry out any rule that requires federal employees or contractors to use a person's preferred pronouns or a name other than their legal name. The bill defines preferred pronouns as those that differ from a person's biological sex as the bill describes it.
ai_sections.who: Federal employees and federal contractors would be affected by this bill. Any employee or contractor who believes their agency broke this rule could file a written complaint and, if unsatisfied, take the agency to court within one year.
ai_sections.why: If an agency is found to have violated the rule, a court could order it to stop, require it to pay damages to the employee, and cover the employee's attorney fees. Punitive damages would be capped at $100,000.
ai_sections.cost: If a court rules against an agency, the government could be required to pay damages and attorney fees to the employee, with punitive damages limited to $100,000.
costChips: Gov't may pay damages & attorney fees

title: Joint Medical Facilities Fund Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates a permanent, shared bank account — called the Joint Medical Facility Fund — that the Department of Defense (DoD) and the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) can both pay into and draw from to run medical facilities they operate together, serving both active-duty military and veterans under one roof. Both departments can deposit money into the fund from their regular budgets, and the fund can also receive money collected from insurance companies or other third parties when care is provided at these shared facilities. The money in the fund can be used for day-to-day operations, equipment, building upkeep, and smaller construction projects at these joint facilities, with most funds available for about one year and a small portion (up to 2%) lasting an extra year. The bill also requires the two departments to agree on a shared financial management process so each side can track its own contributions, and within 180 days of becoming law, both departments must report to Congress on which additional facilities could potentially become joint facilities in the future.
ai_headline: DoD and VA would share a joint medical facility fund
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3992 creates a shared fund that the Pentagon and VA can both use to run medical facilities serving active-duty troops and veterans together.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets up a permanent shared fund, called the Joint Medical Facility Fund, for medical facilities that the Department of Defense and the Department of Veterans Affairs run together. Both departments can put money in and draw money out to cover operations, equipment, upkeep, and smaller construction projects at those shared sites. Most funds last about one year, and up to 2% can carry over for an extra year.
ai_sections.who: Both the Department of Defense and the Department of Veterans Affairs would use this fund. The facilities it supports serve active-duty military members and veterans who receive care in the same building.
ai_sections.why: Without a shared fund, the two departments have no permanent, common pool of money to manage joint facilities together. The bill also requires both departments to report to Congress within 180 days on which other facilities could potentially become joint facilities in the future.
ai_sections.cost: Both departments can deposit money from their existing budgets into the fund. The fund can also receive money from insurance companies or other third parties when care is provided at joint facilities.

title: A bill to amend the Animal Welfare Act to establish additional requirements for dealers, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would require licensed dog dealers — primarily large-scale commercial breeders — to meet stricter care standards for the dogs they keep and breed. On the housing side, dealers would need to provide solid floors, enough space based on each dog's size, enclosures that aren't stacked on top of each other, and temperatures kept between 45 and 85 degrees Fahrenheit. Each dog would also be entitled to daily outdoor exercise, at least 30 minutes of human and social interaction per day, twice-daily feedings, constant access to clean water, and regular veterinary care including annual exams and vaccinations. The bill places new limits on breeding practices, such as capping how many litters a female dog can have in her lifetime, setting minimum and maximum ages for breeding based on the dog's size, and requiring health screenings before any breeding attempt. Finally, dealers would be required to make genuine efforts to find adoptive homes for dogs retired from breeding, rather than selling them at auction or transferring them to other breeders.
ai_headline: Senate bill sets new welfare rules for licensed dog dealers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry. (text: CR S2131)

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4437 would force licensed dog dealers to meet stricter care, housing, breeding, and adoption standards.
ai_sections.what: This bill would update the Animal Welfare Act to set new rules for licensed dog dealers, who are mostly large commercial breeders. It covers how dogs must be housed, fed, exercised, and cared for by a vet. It also limits how often and at what ages female dogs can be bred, and requires dealers to look for adoptive homes for dogs that are no longer used for breeding.
ai_sections.who: Licensed dog dealers regulated under the Animal Welfare Act would be directly affected. The dogs kept and bred by those dealers would also be impacted by the new standards.
ai_sections.why: If passed, dealers who do not already meet these standards would need to change how they house, feed, and breed their dogs. Dogs retired from breeding programs could be more likely to end up in adoptive homes rather than being sold or transferred.

title: A bill to amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to allow for limited full expensing of certain reforestation expenditures.
ai_summary: This bill changes federal tax rules to make it easier and more financially beneficial for landowners to replant trees on their property. First, it raises the cap on reforestation costs that can be immediately deducted on taxes — from $10,000 to $30,000 for most filers, and from $5,000 to $15,000 for married couples filing separately — and ties those limits to inflation going forward so they keep pace with rising costs. Second, it creates a new, separate tax deduction specifically for landowners who need to replant trees that were damaged or destroyed by a presidentially declared natural disaster, such as a wildfire, hurricane, or flood, allowing deductions of up to $500,000 per property and up to $1,000,000 total across all properties, also adjusted for inflation over time. This disaster-related deduction comes with a catch: if a landowner sells the replanted property within 10 years, a portion of the tax benefit must be paid back, though exceptions exist for casualties, government takings, or the death of the owner. These changes would primarily affect private forest landowners, timber businesses, family farms with woodland, and similar property owners who invest in tree planting.
ai_headline: Senate bill raises reforestation tax deduction limits
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Landowners could deduct up to $30,000 in tree-planting costs immediately, and up to $500,000 per property after a natural disaster.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes federal tax rules for replanting trees. It raises the limit on reforestation costs that can be deducted right away, and it creates a separate larger deduction for landowners who replant trees destroyed by a presidentially declared natural disaster. If a landowner sells disaster-replanted property within 10 years, part of the tax benefit must be repaid, with exceptions for casualties, government takings, or the owner's death.
ai_sections.who: This affects private forest landowners, timber businesses, and family farms with woodland who plant trees on their property.
ai_sections.why: Raising the deduction limits means landowners can reduce their taxable income by more when they spend money on tree planting. The disaster deduction and repayment rule together affect how landowners are taxed when they replant after a major natural disaster and later decide whether to sell.

title: Veterans Outdoor Rehabilitation Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs to create a grant program that sends money to each state's main veterans agency so those agencies can build or expand outdoor recreation programs for veterans. Each qualifying state agency that applies would receive at least $200,000 per year, and the bill sets aside up to $10 million per year total for the program. States could use the money in various ways — such as hiring local guides or outdoor outfitters, covering veterans' equipment and transportation costs, running outreach to let veterans know about the programs, and partnering with federal land agencies like the National Park Service or the Forest Service to find suitable locations. The outdoor activities must be structured and nature-based, aimed at improving veterans' mental and physical health, and must be accessible to veterans with disabilities. In return for the funding, state agencies must track and annually report data such as how many veterans participated, what activities were offered, and whether participants reported improvements in well-being.
ai_headline: VA outdoor recreation grants target veteran wellness
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4197 would create a VA grant program giving state veterans agencies at least $200,000/year for outdoor recreation programs for veterans.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs to set up a grant program for outdoor recreation programs aimed at improving veterans' mental and physical health. Funded activities must be structured, nature-based, and accessible to veterans with disabilities. States could use the money to hire guides, cover equipment and transportation costs, run outreach, and partner with federal land agencies like the National Park Service or Forest Service.
ai_sections.who: State veterans agencies would apply for and manage the grants. Veterans, including those with disabilities, would be the participants in the programs.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the VA would be required to build and run this grant program rather than doing so at its own discretion. State agencies that receive funding would be required to track participation, activities offered, and self-reported well-being data, and report that information annually.
ai_sections.cost: Each qualifying state agency that applies would receive at least $200,000 per year. The bill sets aside up to $10 million per year in total for the program.

title: A bill to amend the Higher Education Act of 1965 to provide for comprehensive student achievement information.
ai_summary: This bill would require colleges and universities to publicly report — and share directly with their students — more detailed information about how long it actually takes students to finish their degrees or certificates. Currently, schools mostly report graduation rates based on whether students finish in 150% of the normal program length (for example, within 6 years for a 4-year degree); this bill would expand that to also show rates at the normal expected time, at 200%, and — for shorter programs — at 300% of the normal time. The reporting would also be broken down by four types of students: first-time full-time students, first-time part-time students, returning full-time students, and returning part-time students, so prospective students can see outcomes for people in situations similar to their own. The bill also requires that all these percentages be displayed in a consistent way and with equal visibility, so no single figure gets buried or highlighted over others. This primarily affects colleges and universities that receive federal financial aid funding, as well as the students and families who use published school data to make decisions about where to enroll.
ai_headline: Colleges would report fuller graduation timelines under S 4431
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (text: CR S2130-2131)

ai_sections.tldr: Colleges would have to publish graduation rates at multiple time points, broken down by student type, in equal-sized displays.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require colleges to publicly report how long students actually take to finish a degree or certificate at several time checkpoints, not just one. Schools would also have to show these numbers separately for full-time, part-time, first-time, and returning students. All figures would need to be displayed in a consistent, equally visible way.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects colleges and universities that receive federal financial aid funding. It also affects students and families who use school data to decide where to enroll.
ai_sections.why: Right now, most schools report only one graduation rate, which may not reflect the experience of many students. Expanding and standardizing what gets reported means prospective students can compare schools using more complete information.

title: Thomas M. Conway Veterans Access to Resources in the Workplace Act
ai_summary: This bill requires employers with 50 or more employees to post a notice in their workplace — in the same area where other required workplace notices are typically displayed — that lists benefits available to military veterans. The federal Departments of Labor and Veterans Affairs would work together to create a separate version of this notice for each state, including information about federal veterans' benefits, how to apply for them, the Veterans Crisis Line, and any additional benefits offered by that particular state. The notice, sized to fit a standard sheet of paper, must be reviewed and updated at least twice a year, and states are given opportunities to contribute and update their own information. The posting requirement applies to private businesses, government agencies, and certain federal entities like the Government Accountability Office and Library of Congress, as long as they employ at least 50 people.
ai_headline: Employers would post veterans' benefits notices at work
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: Employers with 50+ employees would be required to post a workplace notice listing veterans' benefits available in their state.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require employers to post a notice at work that lists benefits available to military veterans. The Departments of Labor and Veterans Affairs would create a version of this notice for each state, covering federal veterans' benefits, how to apply, the Veterans Crisis Line, and state-specific benefits. The notice must fit on a standard sheet of paper and be updated at least twice a year.
ai_sections.who: The requirement applies to private businesses, government agencies, and certain federal entities such as the Government Accountability Office and Library of Congress, as long as they have at least 50 employees.
ai_sections.why: Veterans who work for covered employers would see information about available benefits posted in the same area as other required workplace notices. States would have the opportunity to contribute and update their own information in the notice.

title: A bill to provide for a procedure for Congress to carry out a reversal of a decision by the President not to declare a major disaster requested by the Governor of a State under the Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill creates a way for Congress to override a presidential refusal to declare a major disaster in a state, but only under specific circumstances. When a state governor requests a federal disaster declaration and the President says no — either by going against FEMA's recommendation to approve it, or by following a FEMA recommendation to deny it that itself breaks with past practice — the President must send a written explanation to Congress within 24 hours. After receiving that explanation, members of Congress have 14 days to introduce a joint resolution that would force the President to issue the disaster declaration, and the bill sets out a fast-track process for both the House and Senate to debate and vote on that resolution quickly, with limited debate time and no amendments allowed. This bill primarily affects states that have been denied federal disaster aid, the President, FEMA, and members of Congress who would gain this new oversight tool.
ai_headline: Senate bill lets Congress override disaster denial
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress could override a presidential refusal to declare a major disaster, but only under specific conditions set by this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill gives Congress a way to reverse a presidential refusal to declare a major disaster in a state. It only applies when the President rejects a governor's request either against FEMA's advice or by following a FEMA recommendation that itself breaks with past practice. Congress would have 14 days to introduce a joint resolution to force the declaration, using a fast-track process with limited debate and no amendments allowed.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects state governors who have been denied federal disaster aid and the President and FEMA. Members of Congress would gain a new oversight tool under this process.
ai_sections.why: When a President denies a disaster declaration, the affected state may not receive federal relief. This bill creates a path for Congress to step in and require that declaration under defined circumstances.

title: To direct the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to issue regulations to include strollers in the contract of carriage of air carriers and set a liability limit for damaged strollers, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would require the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to create new rules making strollers an officially recognized item in the legal agreements (called "contracts of carriage") that airlines have with their passengers, ensuring strollers are treated as a standard part of the travel experience rather than an afterthought. Airlines would also be required to tag strollers with a label indicating they should be handled with care. If an airline damages a stroller, the bill sets a specific compensation limit of $2,175 that a passenger could claim, and this amount would count within the overall baggage liability limit airlines are already required to follow. These rules would apply to all air carriers and would need to be consistent with international aviation rules that govern flights between countries. This bill primarily affects families who travel by air with young children and the airlines that handle their strollers.
ai_headline: FAA would set stroller rules and damage limits for flights
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: Airlines would be required to include strollers in their official travel agreements and pay up to $2,175 if a stroller is damaged.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the FAA to create rules making strollers an officially recognized item in airline travel agreements. Airlines would also have to attach a care-handling label to strollers. If a stroller is damaged, the airline's compensation would be capped at $2,175, counted within the existing baggage liability limit.
ai_sections.who: These rules would apply to all air carriers operating under FAA oversight. Families traveling by air with young children would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without these rules, strollers are not formally recognized in airline travel agreements, leaving families with limited or unclear options if a stroller is damaged. The rules would also need to align with international aviation agreements that cover flights between countries.
ai_sections.cost: If a stroller is damaged, passengers could claim up to $2,175 from the airline, within the overall baggage liability limit airlines are already required to follow.

title: To direct the Federal Communications Commission to establish a program to make grants available to States to inform individuals of potential eligibility for the Lifeline program of the Commission, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would create two new federal grant programs run by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) aimed at getting more low-income people enrolled in the Lifeline program, which provides discounted phone and internet service to qualifying households. The first program awards grants to states, U.S. territories, and tribal governments to run outreach campaigns that find and inform people who are eligible for Lifeline but haven't signed up yet — including helping them apply, explaining the program's rules, and partnering with local nonprofits that work on digital inclusion. The second program also provides grants to states and tribal governments, but for a more technical purpose: connecting their government benefit databases (such as those tracking food stamp recipients) to the national system used to verify Lifeline eligibility, so that eligibility can be confirmed more easily and automatically. Both programs primarily affect low-income individuals who may qualify for discounted communications services but are not currently receiving them, as well as the state and tribal agencies that administer related benefit programs.
ai_headline: FCC grants would expand Lifeline enrollment outreach
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8576 would create two FCC grant programs to help more low-income people sign up for discounted phone and internet service.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create two federal grant programs run by the FCC. The first would fund states, territories, and tribal governments to reach out to people who qualify for the Lifeline program but have not enrolled, including helping them apply and working with local nonprofits. The second would fund states and tribal governments to connect their benefit databases to the national system that checks Lifeline eligibility, making it easier to confirm who qualifies.
ai_sections.who: Low-income individuals who may qualify for discounted phone and internet service through Lifeline but are not currently enrolled would be the primary people affected. State, territorial, and tribal agencies that run related benefit programs would also be involved in carrying out the work.
ai_sections.why: Many people who qualify for Lifeline have not signed up, and these programs would create new steps aimed at closing that gap. State and tribal governments would take on new administrative roles to carry out outreach and database connections.

title: Taxpayer Funds Oversight and Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the federal government manages and reports on public money by updating the rules for agency financial officers and the government's financial planning process. Each federal agency's Chief Financial Officer would be given a clearer, broader set of responsibilities — including overseeing internal financial controls, linking budget and performance information, and coordinating with other senior agency officials — and the Deputy CFO would automatically step in if that position becomes vacant. Instead of a 5-year government-wide financial management plan, the Office of Management and Budget would now produce a 4-year plan (tied to each new presidential term) that sets measurable goals for financial performance across all agencies, and each agency's CFO would have to create their own matching plan within 120 days. Every year, alongside the President's budget, a public status report would be released showing how well agencies are meeting those financial management goals, including which agencies have financial systems that don't meet legal standards. The people most directly affected are senior financial officials at federal agencies and the Office of Management and Budget, though the broader goal is to give Congress and the public better visibility into how taxpayer money is being managed across the executive branch.
ai_headline: Federal CFO roles and financial planning cycles would be overhauled
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies would follow a new 4-year financial plan tied to each presidential term, with yearly public reports on how well they manage taxpayer money.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates the rules for how federal agencies manage and report on public money. Each agency's Chief Financial Officer would get a broader set of duties, and the Office of Management and Budget would produce a 4-year financial plan instead of a 5-year one. Agencies would have 120 days to create their own matching plans, and a public status report would come out every year alongside the President's budget.
ai_sections.who: Senior financial officers at federal agencies and the Office of Management and Budget would be most directly affected. Congress and the public would also gain more visibility into how taxpayer money is being managed across the executive branch.
ai_sections.why: Because plans would now align with each presidential term, financial goals would be easier to track from one administration to the next. Yearly public reports would show which agencies are not meeting legal standards for their financial systems.

press_names: Taxpayer Funds Oversight and Accountability Act

title: To amend the Immigration and Nationality Act to protect American workers and values.
ai_summary: This bill proposes major changes to how the United States issues permanent resident ("green card") visas to immigrants. It would replace the current employment-based visa system with a points-based system that scores applicants on factors like salary, education level, English language ability, age, and extraordinary achievements — and applicants would need to score at least 16 points and have a job offer paying well above the average state wage to qualify. The bill would also eliminate the diversity visa lottery program, narrow family-based immigration to focus only on spouses and minor children (removing parents, adult siblings, and other relatives from eligibility), and require employers who sponsor immigrant workers to formally certify they first tried to hire American workers and did not lay off U.S. workers to make room for immigrants. Immigrants who receive green cards through the new points-based system would start with conditional status for two years, and could lose that status if they receive public benefits, are convicted of a crime carrying more than a year in prison, or leave the job they were sponsored for without finding comparable replacement employment.
ai_headline: Bill would shift green cards to a points-based system
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8586 would replace the current green card system with a points-based system and eliminate the diversity visa lottery.
ai_sections.what: This bill would replace the current employment-based green card system with a points-based system. Applicants would be scored on salary, education, English ability, age, and achievements, and would need at least 16 points plus a high-paying job offer to qualify. The bill would also end the diversity visa lottery and limit family-based green cards to spouses and minor children only.
ai_sections.who: This bill would affect immigrants applying for permanent residence, employers who sponsor immigrant workers, and family members of people already living in the United States. Parents, adult siblings, and other relatives who currently can be sponsored would no longer be eligible.
ai_sections.why: Immigrants approved under the new system would begin with a conditional two-year status, which they could lose if they receive public benefits, are convicted of certain crimes, or leave their sponsored job without finding similar work. Employers would be required to certify they tried to hire American workers first and did not lay off U.S. workers to make room for immigrants.

title: Disabled Veterans Dignity Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to create a new program specifically to help veterans with spinal cord injuries or disorders who cannot manage their own bowel and bladder functions and who live at home or in other non-institutional settings. Under the program, eligible veterans could receive this care from a family member, a privately hired caregiver, or a contracted home health agency, and the VA would pay those caregivers a monthly stipend or fee for their work. The VA would assess each veteran individually to figure out how many hours of care they need, and before denying care to anyone, the VA would first have to get sign-off from a specialized Spinal Cord Injuries and Disorders Center. If a veteran has been in the program for three or more continuous years, they would automatically be considered to need the care for the rest of their life unless their own doctor says otherwise. Family members and privately hired caregivers in the program would not be classified as vendors or contractors, a distinction that relates to how they might be taxed or treated under federal rules.
ai_headline: VA to fund home bowel and bladder care for veterans
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: The VA would be required to pay caregivers who help home-based veterans with spinal cord injuries manage bowel and bladder care.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a new VA program to cover bowel and bladder care for veterans with spinal cord injuries or disorders who live at home. Care could be provided by a family member, a privately hired caregiver, or a home health agency, and the VA would pay those caregivers a monthly stipend or fee. Family members and privately hired caregivers would not be classified as vendors or contractors under federal rules.
ai_sections.who: Veterans with spinal cord injuries or disorders who live at home or in non-institutional settings and cannot manage their own bowel and bladder functions would be eligible. Family members, privately hired caregivers, and contracted home health agencies could serve as paid caregivers under the program.
ai_sections.why: If a veteran has been in the program for three or more continuous years, they would automatically be considered to need care for life unless their own doctor says otherwise. Before the VA could deny care to any veteran, it would first need approval from a specialized Spinal Cord Injuries and Disorders Center.
ai_sections.cost: The VA would pay caregivers a monthly stipend or fee. The summary does not specify total program costs or funding sources.

title: Stuck On Hold Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to upgrade its customer service phone lines within one year so that callers are automatically told how long they can expect to wait. If that wait is longer than 10 minutes, the system must offer the caller a callback instead of making them stay on hold. The VA would also be required to issue internal guidance aimed at bringing average hold times down to 10 minutes or less. These changes would apply to all standard VA customer service phone lines, but would **not** apply to the VA's suicide/crisis hotline for veterans or to emergency department phone lines. The people directly affected are veterans and others who call the VA for assistance with benefits, healthcare appointments, or other services.
ai_headline: VA phone lines would get wait-time alerts and callbacks
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: The VA would have to tell callers their wait time and offer a callback if the wait is longer than 10 minutes.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the VA to upgrade its customer service phone lines within one year. Callers would be told how long they expect to wait, and if that wait is over 10 minutes, the system must offer a callback. The VA would also need to issue internal guidance to bring average hold times down to 10 minutes or less.
ai_sections.who: Veterans and others who call the VA about benefits, healthcare appointments, or other services would be affected. These changes would not apply to the VA's veteran crisis or suicide hotline, or to emergency department phone lines.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, callers may wait on hold without knowing how long they will wait or having the option to receive a callback. This bill would require the VA to take steps to reduce and better communicate those wait times.

title: Fraud Prevention and Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill creates two new permanent federal offices focused on catching and preventing fraud in government spending. First, it upgrades the Treasury Department's Bureau of the Fiscal Service to run a voluntary data-sharing program where federal, state, and local agencies can pool information — such as lists of known fraudsters and suspicious transactions — to stop bad actors from receiving government payments they shouldn't get. Second, it establishes a new Inspector General for Fraud, Accountability, and Recovery, a presidentially appointed watchdog housed at the Treasury Department whose job is to investigate fraud, coordinate with other federal inspectors general, and oversee a wide range of government spending — from COVID-19 relief funds and unemployment benefits to infrastructure, veterans, and disaster relief programs. To set up the new Inspector General's office quickly, the bill transfers the staff, data, contracts, and remaining funds from an existing temporary oversight body (the Pandemic Response Accountability Committee) to the new permanent office at the end of 2028, when the temporary body closes. The bill primarily affects federal agencies that hand out money, state and local governments that administer federally funded programs, and anyone who receives federal funds — since those payments could be screened against fraud databases before they go out.
ai_headline: Bill proposes permanent federal anti-fraud watchdog office
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8312 creates two permanent federal offices to find and stop fraud in government payments and spending programs.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets up two new permanent offices focused on fraud in government spending. The first upgrades the Treasury Department's Bureau of the Fiscal Service to run a voluntary data-sharing program where federal, state, and local agencies can pool information about known fraudsters and suspicious transactions. The second creates a new Inspector General for Fraud, Accountability, and Recovery, a presidentially appointed watchdog at the Treasury Department who will investigate fraud and oversee spending across many programs, including COVID-19 relief, unemployment, infrastructure, veterans, and disaster relief funds.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects federal agencies that distribute money, as well as state and local governments that run federally funded programs. Anyone who receives federal funds could have their payment screened against fraud databases before it is sent.
ai_sections.why: Pooling fraud data across agencies could make it harder for bad actors to receive government payments they are not entitled to. Moving staff, data, contracts, and remaining funds from the temporary Pandemic Response Accountability Committee to the new office at the end of 2028 is meant to keep oversight work going without a gap.

title: A bill to require a determination regarding atrocities in Tibet, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would require the U.S. Secretary of State to officially decide, within one year, whether China's actions against Tibetan people in Tibet amount to genocide or crimes against humanity. In making that decision, the Secretary must consider specific types of harm, including killings, torture, forced displacement, policies preventing births, and the removal of Tibetan children from their families through state-run boarding schools. Along with that determination, the Secretary must deliver a written report to Congress explaining the evidence behind the conclusion, reviewing Chinese government policies that suppress Tibetan Buddhism, language, and culture, and recommending possible U.S. responses such as sanctions, visa restrictions, or diplomatic actions. The Secretary is also allowed to consult human rights experts, Tibetan advocacy groups, and members of the Tibetan diaspora while conducting this review. The bill primarily affects how the U.S. government officially characterizes and responds to the treatment of Tibetan people living in the traditional Tibetan regions of Amdo, Kham, and U-Tsang within China.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets U.S. genocide ruling on Tibet
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would require the Secretary of State to decide within one year whether China's treatment of Tibetans counts as genocide or crimes against humanity.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the U.S. Secretary of State to officially determine whether China's actions against Tibetan people amount to genocide or crimes against humanity. The Secretary must also send Congress a written report covering the evidence, Chinese policies affecting Tibetan religion and culture, and possible U.S. responses such as sanctions or visa restrictions.
ai_sections.who: The bill directs the U.S. Secretary of State to lead this review, with the option to consult human rights experts, Tibetan advocacy groups, and Tibetan diaspora members. It most directly concerns Tibetan people living in the regions of Amdo, Kham, and U-Tsang within China.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the U.S. government would be formally required to take an official position on how it characterizes China's treatment of Tibetans. That position could then shape future U.S. diplomatic, economic, or legal responses toward China.

title: To amend title 11 of the United States Code to address misuse of bankruptcy proceedings in cases of child sex abuse, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill changes federal bankruptcy law to make it harder for individuals and organizations — including nonprofits and churches — to use bankruptcy proceedings to escape or limit financial responsibility for child sexual abuse. If an organization facing child sex abuse claims files for bankruptcy, the bill requires an independent financial expert to review its assets, makes financial records publicly available (instead of allowing them to be sealed), and prevents courts from sealing evidence of alleged child sex crimes except to protect the identity of victims. People who were sexually abused as minors can file claims in bankruptcy court regardless of whether their state's time limit for lawsuits has expired, and those debtors who directly committed or were grossly negligent about child sexual abuse cannot have those debts wiped away through bankruptcy. The bill also gives abuse survivors the right to submit victim impact statements to the bankruptcy court and sets strict requirements — including approval from at least 90% of victim-creditors — before a bankruptcy plan can legally release third parties (such as affiliated organizations or individuals) from liability for child sex abuse claims.
ai_headline: Bankruptcy shield for child sex abuse claims tightened
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill limits how organizations, including churches, can use bankruptcy to avoid paying child sexual abuse survivors.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes federal bankruptcy law to make it harder for people and organizations to use bankruptcy to get out of paying for child sexual abuse. Courts could no longer seal financial records or abuse evidence in these cases, except to protect victim identities. Survivors could also file claims in bankruptcy court even if their state's lawsuit deadline has passed.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects any individual or organization, including nonprofits and churches, that faces child sexual abuse claims and files for bankruptcy. It also directly affects survivors, giving them new rights in bankruptcy court including the right to submit victim impact statements.
ai_sections.why: Without these changes, organizations can use bankruptcy proceedings to limit or avoid financial responsibility for child sexual abuse. The bill also means that debts tied to abuse cannot be erased through bankruptcy if the debtor directly committed the abuse or was grossly negligent.

title: To require the establishment of a list identifying program areas and administrative practices presenting the greatest risk to the integrity of Federal funds administered by States and local governments.
ai_summary: This bill directs the head of the Government Accountability Office (GAO) — a nonpartisan federal watchdog agency — to create a "High Risk List" that identifies which federal funding programs run by state and local governments are most vulnerable to waste, fraud, and abuse. The list must be delivered to Congress within two years and updated periodically, and it must explain what is going wrong, highlight practices that have successfully protected federal money, identify federal tools available to help fix the problems, and offer recommendations to Congress for addressing them. To build the list, the GAO would rely on existing public records such as audit reports and findings from inspectors general, rather than conducting new independent audits or forcing states and cities to hand over information. The bill primarily affects federal agencies, state and local governments that receive and manage federal funds, and Congress, which would use the list to guide oversight and potential policy changes.
ai_headline: GAO would flag risky state-run federal programs
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

ai_sections.tldr: GAO would create a list showing which state- and locally-run federal programs are most at risk for waste, fraud, and abuse.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Government Accountability Office (GAO), a nonpartisan federal watchdog, to build a "High Risk List" of federal funding programs run by states and local governments that are most vulnerable to waste, fraud, and abuse. The list must go to Congress within two years and be updated regularly. It would explain what is going wrong, highlight practices that have worked, identify available federal tools, and recommend fixes to Congress.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects the GAO, federal agencies, and state and local governments that receive and manage federal funds. Congress would use the list to guide its oversight work and any policy changes that follow.
ai_sections.why: Without a structured list like this, it can be harder for Congress to know where federal money managed by states and cities is most at risk. The GAO would build the list using existing public records like audit reports and inspector general findings, so states and cities would not be required to submit new information.

title: Federal Water Projects Consultation Improvement Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill requires federal agencies to more actively involve local water suppliers — such as cities, irrigation districts, and farm water associations in western states that receive water through federal dams and canals — whenever those agencies go through the environmental review process required by the Endangered Species Act for operating a federal water project. Specifically, when the government is reviewing whether a water project might harm a protected species, the local water suppliers must be kept informed and given real opportunities to weigh in at every major step: helping shape scientific assessments, reviewing draft decisions before they are finalized, and being told the reasons why any proposed restrictions on water delivery are necessary and why less harmful alternatives were rejected. If the government considers actions that would reduce the amount of water delivered to these local suppliers under their contracts, it must explain the legal basis for doing so and justify why options with smaller impacts on water supply and local economies would not adequately protect the species. The bill applies to western states where the Bureau of Reclamation operates water projects, and it affects any public agency or water users association that holds a contract with the Bureau of Reclamation for municipal or agricultural water.
ai_headline: Western water suppliers gain ESA review consultation rights
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies must inform and consult local water suppliers at every step when reviewing whether a water project harms a protected species.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires federal agencies to keep local water suppliers informed and involved during Endangered Species Act reviews of federal water projects. Water suppliers must have a real chance to help shape scientific assessments, review draft decisions, and receive written explanations when water deliveries may be reduced. The government must also explain why less harmful alternatives to restricting water supply were rejected.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to federal agencies and the Bureau of Reclamation, which operates dams and canals across western states. It also covers local water suppliers — such as cities, irrigation districts, and farm water associations — that hold contracts with the Bureau of Reclamation for municipal or agricultural water.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, local water suppliers may not be included in environmental reviews that could result in less water being delivered to their communities and farms. This bill changes that process so suppliers are notified and consulted before major decisions are finalized.

press_names: Federal Water Projects Consultation Improvement Act

title: A bill to prohibit the importation, manufacture, sale, resale, or introduction into interstate commerce in the United States of connected vehicles and related software and hardware associated with foreign adversaries.
ai_summary: This bill would ban the import, manufacture, sale, and distribution in the United States of internet-connected cars — and the key software and hardware components inside them — if they originate from or are significantly controlled by China, Russia, North Korea, or Iran. The vehicle ban and software restrictions would take effect starting January 1, 2027, while the hardware ban would begin January 1, 2030, with a limited exception allowing older hardware to still be imported for repairs and warranty work. Companies that want to sell connected vehicles or related parts must submit a formal certification to the government confirming their products don't violate these rules, and violations can result in fines of at least $1.5 million or five times the value of the transaction, whichever is greater. The bill primarily affects automakers, auto parts suppliers, importers, and dealers, but it could also indirectly affect consumers by limiting which vehicles and car technologies are available for purchase in the U.S. market.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets Chinese and Russian connected cars
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2027, this bill would ban internet-connected cars and key parts linked to China, Russia, North Korea, or Iran from the U.S. market.
ai_sections.what: This bill would ban the import, manufacture, sale, and distribution of internet-connected vehicles and their key software and hardware components if they come from or are significantly controlled by China, Russia, North Korea, or Iran. The ban on vehicles and software would start January 1, 2027, and the ban on hardware would start January 1, 2030. Older hardware could still be imported for repairs and warranty work under a limited exception.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects automakers, auto parts suppliers, importers, and dealers. Consumers could also be affected indirectly if fewer vehicles and car technologies become available to buy in the U.S.
ai_sections.why: Companies that want to sell connected vehicles or related parts would need to file a formal certification with the government confirming their products follow these rules. If they do not comply, they face serious financial penalties.
ai_sections.cost: Violations can result in fines of at least $1.5 million or five times the value of the transaction, whichever is greater.

title: To prohibit gender transition procedures on minors, to authorize the Secretary of Health and Human Services to impose civil penalties on persons who perform gender transition procedures on minors, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for any doctor, hospital, or other person to perform medical procedures aimed at gender transition on anyone under 18, including puberty blockers, cross-sex hormones, and a wide range of surgeries. If someone violates this ban, the federal Department of Health and Human Services can fine them at least $100,000 per violation, and the minor (or their parent or guardian) can sue the provider in federal court for compensation, emotional distress damages, and potentially punitive damages — with up to 25 years after the person turns 18 to file a lawsuit. The bill explicitly protects minors and their parents or guardians from any fines or penalties; only the providers and those who help them can be held liable. Money collected from fines would go into a special fund used to support grant programs for nonprofit organizations that help individuals who want to reverse prior gender transition procedures, offering services such as medical care, mental health support, and education and employment assistance. The bill does not prevent doctors from discussing treatment options or referring patients to mental health professionals, and it carves out exceptions for treatments related to certain biological conditions, medical emergencies, and naturally occurring early puberty.
ai_headline: Bill would ban gender care for minors under 18
