To receive a grant, states and tribes must meet several requirements, including designating a single coordinator, submitting an upgrade plan, and certifying that local 911 fees are not being spent on unrelated things — and any entity that provides false information loses eligibility and must return the money. The bill also creates a national cybersecurity center focused specifically on protecting these 911 systems, and an advisory board of 16 public safety professionals (from law enforcement, fire and rescue, emergency medical services, and 911 operations) to advise on how the grants and rules should work. Funding is authorized for fiscal years 2026 through 2030, with no more than 4 percent of appropriated funds going to administrative costs.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund Next Generation 9-1-1 upgrades
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal grants would help states, tribes, and territories upgrade 911 systems to handle texts, photos, and videos—not just calls.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a federal grant program to help upgrade 911 systems to "Next Generation 9-1-1," a modern internet-based system that can receive texts, photos, videos, and other data—not just phone calls. Grant money can pay for building and maintaining the new systems, training staff, informing the public, and adding cybersecurity protections. The bill also establishes a national cybersecurity center for these 911 systems and a 16-member advisory board of public safety professionals.
ai_sections.who: States, U.S. territories, and Native American tribes can apply for grants, but must meet requirements such as naming a single coordinator, submitting an upgrade plan, and certifying that local 911 fees are used properly. The program is run by the Assistant Secretary of the National Telecommunications and Information Administration, with guidance from an advisory board drawn from law enforcement, fire and rescue, emergency medical services, and 911 operations.
ai_sections.why: Without upgrades, 911 centers may not be able to receive texts, photos, or videos that callers send during emergencies. Entities that submit false information lose eligibility and must return any grant money received.
ai_sections.cost: Funding is authorized for fiscal years 2026 through 2030. No more than 4 percent of appropriated funds may go to administrative costs, and the bill also sets spending caps on training costs.

title: Dietary Supplement Listing Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require companies that make, package, or distribute dietary supplements sold in the United States to register each of their products with the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and provide detailed information about them, including the product's name, ingredients, serving sizes, directions, allergen warnings, and a copy of its label. Companies with products already on the market would have 18 months to comply, while new products would need to be registered before they go on sale; companies must also notify the FDA within a year if they stop selling a product and update their registration within 30 days if they change a label. The FDA would be required to create a publicly searchable online database where anyone could look up this information, though certain details — like contact information for company owners and the exact amounts of individual ingredients inside proprietary blends — would be kept confidential. A dietary supplement that hasn't been properly registered would be considered "misbranded," meaning it could be treated as an illegal product. This bill primarily affects dietary supplement manufacturers, distributors, and packagers, but also benefits consumers, healthcare providers, and researchers who want easier access to accurate information about what is in the supplements they use or study.
ai_headline: FDA would track and publish dietary supplement data
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (text: CR S259-260)

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 3677 would require dietary supplement makers to register every product with the FDA before it can legally be sold.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require companies that make, package, or distribute dietary supplements to register each product with the FDA and submit details such as ingredients, serving sizes, directions, allergen warnings, and a copy of the label. Products already on shelves would have 18 months to comply; new products must be registered before going on sale. Companies must also update the FDA within 30 days of a label change and within one year of stopping sales of a product.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects dietary supplement manufacturers, distributors, and packagers. Consumers, healthcare providers, and researchers who want accurate information about supplement contents are also affected.
ai_sections.why: A supplement that is not properly registered would be considered misbranded and could be treated as an illegal product. The FDA would be required to maintain a publicly searchable online database of registered products, though some details such as owner contact information and exact amounts within proprietary blends would remain confidential.

title: SHIFT Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the federal government and regional fishery councils manage fish populations that are moving into new ocean areas due to changing environmental conditions. When fish stocks shift significantly into a new region — whether measured by where the fish are, where fishermen are catching them, or where fishing revenues are going — the government must formally recognize that shift and update who is responsible for managing that fishery, using the best available science to account for ecological changes like habitat and food web effects. The bill also tightens the rules for introducing new types of fishing or fishing gear, requiring a formal analysis showing that any new fishery won't seriously harm fish habitat, existing fisheries, or coastal fishing communities before it can be officially authorized. Every five years, regional councils must review their authorized fisheries and gear and propose any needed updates. The people most directly affected are commercial and recreational fishermen, fishing communities along the U.S. coasts, and the regional councils and federal agencies that manage marine fisheries.
ai_headline: Senate bill tracks fish as stocks shift to new waters
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: The SHIFT Act requires the federal government to update who manages a fishery when fish populations move into new ocean areas.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires federal agencies and regional fishery councils to formally recognize when fish populations shift into new ocean regions and to update management responsibilities accordingly. It also requires a formal analysis before any new type of fishing or fishing gear can be approved, to check for harm to fish habitat, existing fisheries, or coastal communities. Every five years, regional councils must review their authorized fisheries and gear and propose updates as needed.
ai_sections.who: Commercial and recreational fishermen and coastal fishing communities are directly affected. Regional fishery councils and the federal agencies that manage marine fisheries are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without these changes, management responsibilities might not keep up when fish populations move due to changing environmental conditions. The new gear and fishery review requirements mean that potential harm to habitats, other fisheries, and fishing communities must be assessed before new fishing activity is authorized.

title: Find It Early Act
ai_summary: This bill would require health insurance plans to cover additional breast cancer screenings — such as 3D mammograms, ultrasounds, MRI scans, and other imaging — at no cost to the patient, meaning no copays, deductibles, or other out-of-pocket charges. The no-cost coverage would apply to two groups: people identified as being at higher risk for breast cancer (including those with dense breast tissue, based on established medical criteria), and people whose doctor determines they need screening based on factors like age, race, ethnicity, or personal or family medical history. The requirement would apply broadly across nearly all types of health coverage, including employer-sponsored plans, individual and marketplace insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE (military health coverage), and VA health care for veterans. The frequency of covered screenings would follow guidelines set by the National Comprehensive Cancer Network, a medical organization that publishes cancer care standards. The bill would take effect on January 1, 2026.
ai_headline: Insurers would cover extra breast cancer scans at no cost
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting January 1, 2026, most health plans would be required to cover extra breast cancer screenings at no out-of-pocket cost for higher-risk patients.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require health insurance plans to cover additional breast cancer screenings — including 3D mammograms, ultrasounds, and MRI scans — with no copays, deductibles, or other out-of-pocket costs. How often screenings are covered would follow guidelines set by the National Comprehensive Cancer Network.
ai_sections.who: The no-cost coverage would apply to people at higher risk for breast cancer, including those with dense breast tissue, and to people whose doctor recommends screening based on age, race, ethnicity, or personal or family medical history. The requirement would apply to most types of health coverage, including employer plans, marketplace insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE, and VA health care.
ai_sections.why: People in the covered groups who currently pay out of pocket for additional screenings would no longer have that cost. The bill would take effect on January 1, 2026.

title: Fiscal State of the Nation Act
ai_summary: Every year, the Treasury Department already submits an audited financial report on the federal government's finances to Congress. This bill would require Congress to hold a public joint hearing — with members of both the House and Senate Budget Committees present — where the Comptroller General (the head of the Government Accountability Office, a nonpartisan watchdog agency) would walk lawmakers through the findings of that audit, including information about deficits, surpluses, long-term debt projections, and the government's overall financial health. The hearing must be held within 45 working days of the Treasury submitting its report, must be open to the public and to media coverage, and any member of Congress — not just Budget Committee members — would be allowed to participate. The presentation itself must follow nonpartisan, fact-based standards set by the Government Accountability Office. This bill primarily affects members of Congress and the Comptroller General, though ordinary citizens would benefit from the increased public visibility into the government's financial condition.
ai_headline: Bill requires public GAO briefing on federal finances
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would require Congress to hold a public hearing each year where a nonpartisan watchdog walks lawmakers through the federal government's financial audit.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require Congress to hold a public joint hearing each year to review the federal government's audited financial report. The Comptroller General would present findings on deficits, debt projections, and the government's overall financial health. The hearing must happen within 45 working days of the Treasury submitting its report, must be open to the public and media, and any member of Congress may participate.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects members of Congress and the Comptroller General, the head of the Government Accountability Office. Ordinary citizens would also be affected through greater public visibility into the government's financial condition.
ai_sections.why: Without this requirement, no formal process exists to ensure Congress publicly reviews the Treasury's annual financial audit together. This bill would create a set time and format for that review to happen on the record, in public.

title: Community Passport Services Access Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow public libraries that are organized as nonprofits, charities, or similar non-governmental organizations to officially accept passport applications and keep the fee they charge customers for that service. Right now, that fee-retention authority is limited to certain government offices and the U.S. Postal Service; this bill adds qualifying public libraries to that list. Libraries that were already acting as passport acceptance facilities before this bill becomes law would be formally authorized to collect and keep that fee within 30 days of enactment, as long as they were following State Department rules at the time. The people most directly affected are library patrons who want a convenient local place to submit a passport application, and the libraries themselves, which would gain a new source of revenue from the execution fees they collect.
ai_headline: Nonprofit libraries could soon accept passport applications
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6997 would let nonprofit public libraries accept passport applications and keep the fee they charge for that service.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds qualifying nonprofit public libraries to the list of places officially allowed to accept passport applications and keep the execution fee charged to customers. That authority currently belongs only to certain government offices and the U.S. Postal Service. Libraries already acting as passport acceptance facilities when the bill becomes law would be formally authorized to collect and keep that fee within 30 days, as long as they were following State Department rules at the time.
ai_sections.who: Library patrons looking for a convenient local place to submit a passport application are directly affected. So are the public libraries themselves, which would gain a new source of revenue from the execution fees they collect.
ai_sections.why: Libraries that already offer this service would move from an informal arrangement to an officially recognized one. Libraries not yet offering the service would have a legal pathway to do so and keep the associated fee.

title: Public Integrity in Financial Prediction Markets Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for a wide range of federal government workers and officials to bet on prediction markets — online platforms where people place wagers on whether specific events will happen — when those bets involve government policy, government actions, or political outcomes. The ban applies to elected members of Congress, their staff, political appointees, and employees of federal agencies. The prohibition specifically targets situations where these individuals either already possess inside government information that the public doesn't have access to, or where their job gives them a reasonable opportunity to obtain such information. For example, a federal employee who knows in advance about an upcoming policy decision before it is announced publicly could not use that knowledge to profit by betting on a prediction market tied to that policy. The core concern the bill addresses is that people with access to non-public government information could have an unfair advantage when betting on political or policy outcomes.
ai_headline: Federal workers would be barred from political prediction market bets
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on House Administration, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal workers and officials with access to inside government information could not bet on prediction markets tied to policy or political outcomes.
ai_sections.what: This bill would ban certain federal government workers and officials from placing bets on prediction markets when those bets involve government policy, government actions, or political outcomes. The ban applies specifically when those individuals already have inside government information the public cannot access, or when their job gives them a reasonable chance to get such information.
ai_sections.who: The ban covers elected members of Congress, their staff, political appointees, and employees of federal agencies.
ai_sections.why: Without this rule, people with access to non-public government information could have an unfair advantage when betting on political or policy outcomes. For example, a federal employee who knows about a policy decision before it is made public could use that knowledge to profit on a prediction market tied to that policy.

title: EB–5 Regional Center Program Advisory Committee Authorization Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new advisory committee inside U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to provide guidance and recommendations on the EB-5 Regional Center Program — the federal program that allows foreign investors to obtain U.S. visas by investing money in American businesses or projects that create jobs. The committee would have up to 35 members drawn from regional centers (the organizations that pool and manage EB-5 investments), city mayors, county and state economic development officials, and federal agencies, and would be organized into subcommittees covering topics like application processing times, job creation, and investment rules. It would meet at least four times a year, submit annual reports to the Secretary of Homeland Security and the USCIS Director, and provide quarterly briefings to Congress — though it could not make recommendations about specific individual visa cases. The people most directly affected are foreign investors seeking EB-5 visas, the regional centers that sponsor investment projects, and the communities — particularly rural areas, high-unemployment areas, and cities of various sizes — where those projects are located.
ai_headline: USCIS would gain an EB-5 investor visa advisory panel
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6992 creates a new advisory committee at USCIS to give guidance on the EB-5 visa investment program.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets up an advisory committee inside U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services to give guidance on the EB-5 Regional Center Program, which lets foreign investors get U.S. visas by investing in American businesses that create jobs. The committee would have up to 35 members, meet at least four times a year, report annually to homeland security leadership, and give quarterly updates to Congress. It would not be allowed to weigh in on individual visa cases.
ai_sections.who: Members would come from regional centers, city mayors, county and state economic development officials, and federal agencies. The people most directly affected are foreign investors seeking EB-5 visas, the regional centers that manage those investments, and communities where projects are located, including rural areas, high-unemployment areas, and cities of different sizes.
ai_sections.why: Creating this committee gives USCIS a formal channel for outside input on topics like application processing times, job creation, and investment rules. Without such a structure, those perspectives would have no organized way to reach agency leadership or Congress.

title: National Landslide Preparedness Act Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill extends and updates a federal program aimed at reducing the dangers of landslides across the United States through at least 2030, and increases the dedicated funding for that program from $25 million to $35 million per year, with at least $10 million of that specifically required to go toward buying, installing, and maintaining early warning systems in high-risk areas. It broadens who is included in landslide preparedness efforts by explicitly adding Native Hawaiian organizations and Tribal organizations alongside state and local governments, and it directs the U.S. Geological Survey to create regional partnerships — starting in Alaska — with universities and research organizations that have landslide expertise. The bill also requires the government to assess the specific landslide risks posed by atmospheric rivers (large weather systems that bring heavy rain or snow) and other extreme rain events, and expands the national landslide database to flag areas threatened by factors like drought, erosion, earthquakes, volcanic activity, and thawing permafrost. People most directly affected include residents of landslide-prone communities, emergency managers, state and local officials, Native Hawaiian and Tribal communities, and researchers who study or respond to landslide hazards.
ai_headline: USGS landslide warning program extended through 2030
last_action_text: Reported (Amended) by the Committee on Natural Resources. H. Rept. 119-431, Part I.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would extend the national landslide preparedness program through at least 2030 and raise its annual funding from $25M to $35M.
ai_sections.what: This bill extends a federal landslide preparedness program through at least 2030. It raises annual funding, requires early warning systems in high-risk areas, and expands a national landslide database to track threats from drought, erosion, earthquakes, volcanic activity, and thawing permafrost. It also requires the government to study landslide risks tied to heavy rain events and atmospheric rivers.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects residents of landslide-prone communities, emergency managers, state and local officials, Native Hawaiian and Tribal organizations, and landslide researchers. Native Hawaiian and Tribal organizations are newly added to the program alongside state and local governments.
ai_sections.why: Without reauthorization, the existing program would expire and funding would end, leaving communities with fewer tools to detect and prepare for landslides. Expanding the database and studying extreme rain events could change how high-risk areas are identified.
ai_sections.cost: Annual funding for the program would increase from $25 million to $35 million. At least $10 million of that each year must go toward buying, installing, and maintaining early warning systems in high-risk areas.

title: Public Health Nursing Act
ai_summary: This bill would direct the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to build up the nation's public health nursing workforce by giving grants to state, local, and territorial public health departments. Those departments could use the money to recruit, hire, and train licensed registered nurses — covering wages, benefits, and necessary medical supplies like protective equipment — with a focus on placing nurses in underserved communities, mobile health clinics, hospitals, and home visitation programs. Communities that face high rates of chronic disease, infant mortality, or maternal health problems, as well as low-income, rural, and medically underserved areas, would be prioritized for funding. Grant recipients would be required to maintain their existing spending on these activities so that federal money adds to — rather than replaces — what they already spend. The bill would authorize up to $5 billion per year for this program from 2026 through 2035.
ai_headline: HHS grants would expand public health nursing nationwide
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6989 would fund hiring and training of public health nurses, prioritizing underserved, rural, and high-need communities, at up to $5B per year.
ai_sections.what: This bill would direct the Department of Health and Human Services to give grants to state, local, and territorial public health departments. Departments could use the money to recruit, hire, and train registered nurses, covering wages, benefits, and protective equipment. Nurses would be placed in underserved communities, mobile health clinics, hospitals, and home visitation programs.
ai_sections.who: State, local, and territorial public health departments would receive the grants. Communities with high rates of chronic disease, infant mortality, or maternal health problems, as well as low-income, rural, and medically underserved areas, would be prioritized.
ai_sections.why: If passed, federal funds would expand the public health nursing workforce in areas that currently have fewer health resources. Grant recipients would be required to keep their existing spending in place, so federal money would add to current efforts rather than replace them.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize up to $5 billion per year from 2026 through 2035.

title: Bycatch Reduction and Research Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal government — primarily the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) — to improve research, technology, and financial tools aimed at reducing "bycatch," which is the unintended capture of fish and other marine life (such as salmon) during commercial fishing. NOAA would be required to study how salmon move through Alaskan waters, examine how trawl fishing gear disturbs the ocean floor, and analyze the broader environmental factors affecting fish populations in the Bering Sea, Aleutian Islands, and Gulf of Alaska — sharing findings in public reports within three years. The bill also calls for building a testing facility (a "flume tank") where fishermen, researchers, and government scientists can try out new gear designs that catch fewer unwanted species and cause less seafloor damage, and it sets up a donation-funded assistance fund — managed by the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation — to help commercial fishermen pay for cleaner, more selective fishing gear and technology. Electronic monitoring systems on fishing vessels would be streamlined and made more consistent, and NOAA would be required to post plain-language observer coverage requirements online so the public can see bycatch data for major federal fisheries. The people most directly affected are commercial fishermen operating in Alaskan and other U.S. federal waters, Alaska Native communities, fishing vessel owners, and the researchers and agencies that manage these fisheries.
ai_headline: NOAA would fund gear to limit accidental fish catches
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal law would require NOAA to study, fund, and publicize efforts to reduce accidental fish catches in U.S. waters.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells NOAA to research how fish move and how fishing gear affects the ocean floor, then share findings in public reports within three years. It would create a testing facility for new gear designs and require NOAA to post bycatch and observer coverage information online in plain language. Electronic monitoring systems on fishing vessels would also be made more consistent.
ai_sections.who: Commercial fishermen in Alaskan and other U.S. federal waters would be most directly affected, along with fishing vessel owners and Alaska Native communities. Researchers and federal agencies that manage fisheries would also be involved.
ai_sections.why: Bycatch — the unintended capture of fish and other marine life during commercial fishing — affects fish populations and ocean ecosystems. This bill changes how the government studies, reports on, and helps address that problem.
ai_sections.cost: A donation-funded assistance fund, managed by the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation, would be set up to help commercial fishermen pay for newer, more selective fishing gear and technology.
costChips: Managed by Nat'l Fish & Wildlife Foundation

title: Revitalizing America’s Housing Act
ai_summary: This bill is a wide-ranging housing package that tries to make homes safer, more affordable, and easier to buy or rent across the United States. It would require the federal government to identify rules that make housing more expensive, offer new or expanded tax breaks to encourage building and selling homes in lower-income neighborhoods, and make it easier for communities to convert unused federal land into affordable housing. It expands access to discounted home-buying programs and public housing for groups like police officers, firefighters, emergency medical technicians, veterans, and volunteer first responders, and it directs studies and new reports on issues like housing near toxic waste sites, homelessness, and inspection backlogs in public housing. People most directly affected include first-time and middle-income homebuyers, longtime homeowners looking to sell, renters in public housing, veterans, first responders, residents near contaminated sites, and local governments seeking more flexibility in their housing and zoning rules.
ai_headline: Federal housing bill expands aid for buyers and renters
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Opportunity.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4856 aims to make homes safer and more affordable by changing federal rules, expanding tax breaks, and opening more programs to veterans and first responders.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the federal government to find and review rules that raise housing costs. It would expand tax breaks to encourage building and selling homes in lower-income areas, and make it easier for communities to turn unused federal land into affordable housing.
ai_sections.who: First-time buyers, middle-income buyers, longtime homeowners, renters in public housing, veterans, police officers, firefighters, EMTs, and volunteer first responders would have access to expanded programs. Local governments would also gain more flexibility in housing and zoning decisions.
ai_sections.why: If passed, more people in certain professions and income levels could qualify for discounted home-buying and public housing programs. Communities near toxic waste sites, people experiencing homelessness, and residents in public housing with inspection backlogs could see new studies and reports directed at their situations.

title: Peace Through Strength Against Russia Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would impose a wide range of economic penalties and restrictions on Russia, primarily to pressure the Russian government to negotiate and maintain a peace deal with Ukraine. Within 30 days of becoming law, the President would be required to freeze the U.S.-held assets of named Russian officials and military leaders, ban American banks and brokers from processing money transfers involving the Russian government, block Russian government-linked companies from trading on U.S. stock exchanges, prohibit Americans from making new investments in Russia, and raise tariffs on Russian imports by up to 500%. The bill also targets people and companies in other countries who help Russia — for example, by supplying military goods, moving Russian oil in violation of price caps, or facilitating North Korean arms transfers to Russia — by blocking their U.S. assets and revoking any U.S. visas they hold. Everyday Americans would mainly feel the effects indirectly, such as through changes in energy markets, while American businesses with ties to Russia, Russian nationals with U.S. assets or visas, and foreign companies that do business with Russia would be most directly affected. The sanctions can only be lifted if Russia signs a peace agreement accepted by Ukraine and stops military hostilities, and even then Congress has a window to block the President from removing them.
ai_headline: Bill targets Russia with sanctions and 500% tariffs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committees on the Judiciary, Financial Services, Ways and Means, Oversight and Government Reform, Energy and Commerce, and Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would place sweeping economic penalties on Russia within 30 days, lasting until Russia signs a peace deal Ukraine accepts.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the President to freeze Russian officials' U.S. assets, block American banks from processing Russian government money transfers, bar Russian government-linked companies from U.S. stock exchanges, ban new American investments in Russia, and raise tariffs on Russian imports by up to 500%. It would also freeze assets and revoke U.S. visas for people or companies in any country that helps Russia with military supplies, oil price cap violations, or arms transfers from North Korea. Sanctions can only be lifted if Russia signs a peace agreement that Ukraine accepts and ends military hostilities, and Congress can block even that removal.
ai_sections.who: American businesses with ties to Russia, Russian nationals with U.S. assets or visas, and foreign companies that do business with Russia would be most directly affected. Everyday Americans would mainly feel effects indirectly, such as through changes in energy markets.
ai_sections.why: The penalties are designed to pressure Russia to negotiate and maintain a peace deal with Ukraine. If enacted, the restrictions would reshape how U.S. banks, investors, and markets interact with Russia and with third parties that support it.

title: Bycatch Reduction and Research Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal government, through the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), to expand research and improve technology aimed at reducing the unintended catch of fish and other marine life (called "bycatch") in Alaskan and other U.S. waters, with a particular focus on the Bering Sea, Aleutian Islands, and Gulf of Alaska. It requires NOAA to study how salmon migrate and where they can be found, fund faster genetic testing to identify what types of salmon are accidentally caught during commercial fishing, and examine how trawl fishing gear affects the ocean floor and broader marine ecosystems. The bill also directs NOAA to build a specialized testing facility (a flume tank) where fishermen, researchers, and industry partners can try out new gear designs, and creates a fund — managed by the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation and supported by donations — to help commercial fishermen buy or upgrade gear that causes less harm to unintended species and seafloor habitats. Commercial fishermen, fishing vessel operators, Alaska Native communities, researchers, and fishery managers are the primary people affected by this bill.
ai_headline: NOAA directed to expand bycatch reduction research
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal bill directs NOAA to research and reduce accidental fish catches in Alaskan waters and fund gear upgrades for fishermen.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells NOAA to expand research on bycatch, which is the unintended catching of fish and other marine life during commercial fishing. It calls for studies on salmon migration, faster genetic testing of accidentally caught salmon, and research on how trawl gear affects the ocean floor. It also directs NOAA to build a flume tank testing facility and creates a donation-supported fund to help fishermen buy or upgrade less harmful gear.
ai_sections.who: Commercial fishermen, fishing vessel operators, Alaska Native communities, researchers, and fishery managers are most directly affected. The bill focuses on fishing activity in the Bering Sea, Aleutian Islands, and Gulf of Alaska.
ai_sections.why: Bycatch can harm fish populations and ocean habitats that commercial fisheries depend on. This bill would change how federal agencies study and respond to those unintended catches.
ai_sections.cost: A fund managed by the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation, supported by donations, would help commercial fishermen purchase or upgrade fishing gear.
costChips: Helps fishermen buy or upgrade gear

title: RESTRICT Act
ai_summary: This bill would ban the sale or transfer of the most powerful AI computer chips — specifically those used in data centers — to companies based in, owned by, or ultimately controlled by certain countries the U.S. considers security risks, including China, Macau, and Hong Kong. Any company wanting to export these chips to other countries outside that restricted list would need to get a government license first. However, U.S. companies can be exempted from that licensing requirement if they are officially approved by the Commerce Department, which means meeting strict rules: no more than 10% of their ownership can belong to entities from restricted countries, they must follow strong security and customer-verification standards, and they must undergo annual audits. The Commerce Department must set up the approval process within 90 days of the law taking effect, and the entire law would automatically expire after five years. This primarily affects U.S. chip manufacturers and technology companies that sell or operate advanced AI hardware abroad, as well as foreign businesses that currently buy or use those chips.
ai_headline: Bill targets AI chip exports to high-risk nations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6879 would ban selling the most powerful AI chips to companies tied to China and other U.S.-designated security-risk countries.
ai_sections.what: This bill would ban selling or transferring advanced AI data center chips to companies based in, owned by, or controlled by countries the U.S. considers security risks, including China, Macau, and Hong Kong. Selling these chips to any other foreign country would require a government license, unless the company qualifies for an exemption from the Commerce Department. To qualify, a company must have less than 10% foreign restricted ownership, meet security and customer-verification standards, and pass annual audits. The approval process must be set up within 90 days, and the law would expire after five years.
ai_sections.who: This primarily affects U.S. chip makers and tech companies that sell or operate advanced AI hardware abroad. Foreign businesses that currently buy or use those chips would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Companies that do not qualify for an exemption would need to apply for a government license before exporting chips to most foreign countries. Companies connected to restricted countries would lose access to these chips entirely.

title: AI OVERWATCH Act
ai_summary: This bill requires companies to obtain a government-issued license before exporting certain high-performance computer chips — the kind used to power advanced artificial intelligence systems — to China, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Russia, and Venezuela. Before approving any such license, the Commerce Department must notify Congress at least 30 days in advance and provide detailed assurances that the chips won't help those countries' militaries or intelligence agencies, that U.S. supplies of the chips won't be reduced, and that U.S. technological leadership won't be undermined — and Congress can block the sale outright during that window. Any licenses that were already approved before this bill becomes law would be immediately cancelled, and no new licenses can be approved until the government completes and submits a national security strategy on this topic to Congress. U.S. companies that meet strict security and ownership requirements can qualify as "trusted" entities, which allows them to move these chips to non-restricted countries without the same licensing hurdles, as long as the chips remain under their ownership and control.
ai_headline: Bill would license AI chip exports to six nations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: Companies would need a government license to export advanced AI chips to six named countries, and Congress could block any sale.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires companies to get a government license before sending certain high-powered computer chips to China, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Russia, and Venezuela. Congress must be notified at least 30 days before any license is approved and can block the sale during that window. Any licenses already approved would be cancelled, and no new ones can be issued until the government submits a national security strategy to Congress.
ai_sections.who: U.S. companies that export advanced AI chips would be directly affected by these requirements. Companies that meet strict security and ownership rules can earn a "trusted" status, which allows them to move chips to non-restricted countries more easily as long as they keep ownership and control of the chips.
ai_sections.why: The bill places conditions on chip exports out of concern that these chips could support foreign militaries or intelligence agencies, reduce U.S. chip supplies, or weaken U.S. technological leadership.

title: BRIDGE for Young-Onset Alzheimer’s Disease Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill removes two waiting periods that currently apply to people diagnosed with young-onset Alzheimer's disease — meaning people who develop Alzheimer's before the traditional retirement age — when they apply for federal disability and health benefits. Right now, most people who qualify for Social Security disability insurance must wait five months after becoming disabled before their payments begin, and then wait an additional 24 months before they can enroll in Medicare; this bill would eliminate both of those delays for people with young-onset Alzheimer's, just as the law already does for people diagnosed with ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease). That means someone diagnosed with young-onset Alzheimer's could receive their disability payments starting from when they are found eligible, with no five-month gap, and could get Medicare health coverage immediately rather than waiting two years. The bill gives the Social Security Administration the authority to define exactly who qualifies as having "young-onset Alzheimer's" through its official guidance documents.
ai_headline: Bill would end Medicare wait for young-onset Alzheimer's
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6799 would end the 5-month and 24-month waits for disability pay and Medicare for people with young-onset Alzheimer's.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes two waiting periods for people diagnosed with young-onset Alzheimer's disease, meaning Alzheimer's that develops before traditional retirement age. Currently, disability insurance recipients wait five months for payments to start and then 24 more months before Medicare coverage begins. This bill would eliminate both waits, matching rules already in place for people diagnosed with ALS.
ai_sections.who: People diagnosed with young-onset Alzheimer's disease would be affected. The Social Security Administration would gain authority to define exactly who qualifies under that term through its official guidance.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, people with young-onset Alzheimer's face gaps in income and health coverage during a period when they can no longer work. The bill would align their timeline with the one that already applies to ALS patients.

title: Restoring Trust in Public Safety Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow the U.S. Attorney General to award federal grants — up to $15 million per year from 2026 through 2031 — to state, tribal, and local governments and their law enforcement agencies to help pay for the physical destruction of firearms. Eligible governments could use the money to buy or maintain equipment (like shredders or metal cutters), hire private companies that offer destruction services, or train staff — as long as the entire gun, including all its parts and accessories, is fully destroyed. To receive funding, applicants must describe their plan, keep detailed records of every firearm destroyed, and certify that no gun will simply be disposed of or resold. One-third of the total funding must be set aside specifically for smaller cities and rural areas. The bill primarily affects state, tribal, and local governments and law enforcement agencies that choose to apply for these grants.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund local firearm destruction programs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal grants up to $15M/year (2026–2031) would help state, tribal, and local governments fully destroy firearms.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets the U.S. Attorney General give federal grants to state, tribal, and local governments to pay for destroying firearms. Governments could use the money for destruction equipment, private destruction services, or staff training. Every part of each firearm must be fully destroyed, and guns cannot be disposed of or resold.
ai_sections.who: State, tribal, and local governments and their law enforcement agencies can apply. One-third of the funding must go specifically to smaller cities and rural areas.
ai_sections.why: Governments that receive grants would need to keep detailed records of every firearm destroyed and certify they followed the rules. Without this funding, some governments may lack the equipment or resources to carry out firearm destruction on their own.
ai_sections.cost: The Attorney General could award up to $15 million per year from 2026 through 2031. Applicants must describe their destruction plan and meet recordkeeping requirements to qualify.
costChips: Must submit destruction plan to qualify

title: Keeping All Students Safe Act
ai_summary: This bill would ban several practices in schools that receive federal money, including locking a student alone in a room (seclusion), using devices to physically restrict a student's movement (mechanical restraint), using medication to control a student's behavior outside of a doctor's prescription (chemical restraint), and any physical hold that cuts off a student's breathing or blood flow. When physical restraint is used at all, the bill sets strict conditions: it can only happen if a student poses an immediate danger of serious physical injury, only trained and certified staff may do it, it must stop as soon as the danger passes, and parents must be notified the same day and given a written report within 24 hours. The bill affects students, parents, school staff, security guards, and law enforcement officers in public schools, Head Start programs, and private schools that enroll students receiving federally funded special education services. States would be required to submit annual plans and data reports on restraint incidents — broken down by race, disability status, and other characteristics — and the federal government could withhold funding from programs that break the rules. Up to $40 million per year from 2026 through 2030 could be used to help states train school staff, improve school climate, and put positive behavior support programs in place.
ai_headline: House bill targets restraint and seclusion in federal schools
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Armed Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal bill would ban seclusion and most restraints in schools that get federal money, with same-day parent notification required.
ai_sections.what: This bill would ban locking students alone in rooms, using devices to restrict movement, giving medication to control behavior without a doctor's order, and any hold that cuts off breathing or blood flow. When physical restraint is used, it can only happen if a student is in immediate danger of serious injury, must be done by trained staff, and must stop as soon as the danger passes. Parents must be told the same day and receive a written report within 24 hours.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to students, parents, school staff, security guards, and law enforcement in public schools, Head Start programs, and private schools that enroll students receiving federally funded special education services.
ai_sections.why: States would have to submit annual plans and data reports on restraint incidents, broken down by race, disability status, and other characteristics. Programs that break the rules could lose federal funding.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $40 million per year from 2026 through 2030 could go toward helping states train school staff, improve school climate, and put positive behavior support programs in place.

title: Homeless Children and Youth Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes the federal rules about who officially counts as "homeless" and how federal homelessness assistance money is awarded, with a particular focus on children, youth, and families. It broadens the definition of homelessness so that people — especially young people under 24 who can't safely live with family, children with disabilities, and people fleeing domestic violence or human trafficking — who are already recognized as homeless under other federal programs (like Head Start, school programs, or domestic violence services) automatically qualify for housing assistance without extra steps. It also changes how federal grants are awarded so that local communities have more say in deciding which homeless populations to prioritize, and prevents the federal government from favoring one type of housing program or one group of homeless people over another unless local data justifies it. Programs serving homeless families and youth would be required to connect children with schools, childcare, and other services, and unaccompanied homeless young people would need to be informed of their rights when applying for college financial aid. Finally, the bill requires that homelessness data — including counts of homeless women, children, and families — be made publicly available online and reported to Congress each year.
ai_headline: Bill expands federal homelessness definition for youth and families
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6403 widens who counts as "homeless" so more children, youth, and families can access federal housing help without extra paperwork.
ai_sections.what: This bill expands the federal definition of homelessness so that people already recognized as homeless under other government programs automatically qualify for housing assistance. It also gives local communities more control over which homeless groups to prioritize with federal grant money, and bars the federal government from favoring one type of housing program or group without local data to back it up. Programs serving homeless families and youth would be required to connect children to schools, childcare, and other services, and unaccompanied young people would have to be told about their rights when applying for college financial aid.
ai_sections.who: The bill focuses on children, youth under 24 who cannot safely live with family, children with disabilities, and people fleeing domestic violence or human trafficking. Local communities, schools, childcare programs, and domestic violence services are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, people already recognized as homeless under some federal programs still have to go through extra steps to qualify for housing help, which can delay or block their access. The bill also requires that homelessness data on women, children, and families be published online and reported to Congress each year, making that information more visible to the public.

title: Artificial Intelligence Civil Rights Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would create new rules requiring any company or government agency that uses AI or other complex computer systems to make important decisions about people — such as decisions about hiring, housing, loans, health care, education, criminal justice, or voting — to test those systems for bias and potential harm before putting them into use, and to keep checking for problems every year afterward using an outside, independent reviewer. People affected by these AI-driven decisions would have the right to request a human instead of a computer, and the right to appeal a decision to a human reviewer. Companies would also have to publicly post plain-language explanations of how their AI systems work, what data they use, and what the audits found, and they would be forbidden from discriminating against or punishing anyone who raises concerns about an AI system. The Federal Trade Commission would be the main agency responsible for enforcing these rules, and individuals, as well as state governments, could also take legal action against companies that violate them.
ai_headline: Bill would require annual AI audits and appeal rights
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6356 would require companies and agencies using AI for major life decisions to test for bias and give people the right to a human review.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require any company or government agency using AI to make important decisions about hiring, housing, loans, health care, education, criminal justice, or voting to test those systems for bias before use and every year after, using an independent reviewer. Companies would have to post plain-language explanations of how their AI works, what data it uses, and what audits found. It would also be illegal to punish anyone who raises concerns about an AI system.
ai_sections.who: Any company or government agency using AI or complex computer systems for major decisions about people would have to follow these rules. People affected by AI-driven decisions would gain the right to request a human decision-maker and to appeal to a human reviewer.
ai_sections.why: Without these requirements, AI systems used in high-stakes decisions could go unchecked for bias or errors that affect people's lives. The Federal Trade Commission would enforce the rules, and individuals and state governments could also take legal action against violators.

title: Low-Income Household Water Assistance Program Establishment Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal grant program — run by the Department of Health and Human Services working with the EPA — that sends money to states and qualifying Native American tribes, which then use those funds to help low-income households pay their drinking water and wastewater (sewer) bills, including past-due amounts they owe. To qualify, a household generally must already receive certain government benefits (such as food stamps, SSI, or TANF) or have an income no higher than 150% of the federal poverty level, 60% of their state's median income, or 60% of their local area's median income, whichever is highest. The bill also sets aside a small portion of funding specifically for tribes and provides separate grants to nonprofit organizations that help water utilities in rural or underserved communities connect their customers to this assistance. Grant recipients are required to use the money as an addition to — not a replacement for — any existing programs that help low-income people afford water service. The bill authorizes $500 million per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund the program.
ai_headline: Federal grants would help low-income households pay water bills
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4733 would create a federal program sending $500M/year to states and tribes to help low-income households pay water and sewer bills.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal grant program to help low-income households pay their drinking water and sewer bills, including amounts they already owe. States and qualifying Native American tribes would receive the money and pass it on to eligible households. Any funds received must add to existing local water assistance, not replace it.
ai_sections.who: To qualify, a household generally must already receive benefits like food stamps, SSI, or TANF, or have an income no higher than 150% of the federal poverty level, 60% of their state's median income, or 60% of their local area's median income, whichever is highest. Nonprofit organizations that help water utilities in rural or underserved communities connect customers to this assistance could also receive separate grants.
ai_sections.why: Without help, low-income households may struggle or fail to pay water and sewer bills, including past-due balances. This program would give states and tribes a dedicated source of federal funding to address those gaps.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $500 million per year from 2026 through 2030. A small portion of that funding is set aside specifically for Native American tribes.
costChips: Portion reserved for Native American tribes

title: AI for America Act
ai_summary: This bill directs two federal offices to produce reports and plans about artificial intelligence. The Office of Science and Technology Policy must create an action plan by mid-2027 — updated every two years — that outlines how the federal government will keep the U.S. competitive in AI, develop the AI workforce, build research infrastructure, and partner with agencies like NASA, the Department of Energy, and the National Science Foundation; that same office must also, within one year, identify government rules that may be slowing AI adoption in healthcare, scientific research, transportation, and other sectors. Separately, the National Institute of Standards and Technology must produce a report within one year examining ways to detect and prevent security risks and ideological bias in AI data, training methods, and decision-making. These requirements primarily affect federal agencies and their leadership, though the findings could eventually influence how AI is used and regulated across industries like healthcare and transportation.
ai_headline: Federal offices ordered to chart U.S. AI strategy
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Two federal offices must produce AI reports and plans—one on U.S. competitiveness, one on security and bias risks—within set deadlines.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires two federal offices to study and plan for artificial intelligence. The Office of Science and Technology Policy must create an AI action plan by mid-2027 and identify rules that may be slowing AI use in healthcare, transportation, and other fields. The National Institute of Standards and Technology must report within one year on ways to detect security risks and ideological bias in AI systems.
ai_sections.who: These requirements apply to federal agencies and their leaders. The findings could eventually shape how AI is used and regulated in industries like healthcare and transportation.
ai_sections.why: The reports and plans would give the government a clearer picture of where AI policy stands and where gaps exist. The results could influence future rules and decisions about AI across multiple sectors.

title: Healthy Competition for Better Care Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for health insurance plans and health care providers to include certain contract terms that limit a plan's ability to shop around for better or cheaper care. Specifically, it would ban contract clauses that prevent an insurer or employer health plan from steering patients toward lower-cost or higher-quality providers, forcing a plan to also do business with a provider's affiliated facilities, setting prices for those affiliated facilities, or blocking other health plans from negotiating lower prices. The rules would apply to insurance companies, employer-sponsored health plans, and the hospitals, doctor networks, and third-party administrators they contract with. There are exceptions: HMOs that work through exclusive physician group arrangements and certain value-based care networks — like accountable care organizations or centers of excellence — would still be permitted to operate under exclusive arrangements. Federal agencies would have one year to write detailed rules putting the law into effect, and the new requirements would apply to any contracts signed, changed, or renewed starting 18 months after the law is enacted.
ai_headline: Bill targets anticompetitive clauses in health care contracts
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Education and Workforce, and Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would ban contract terms that stop health plans from shopping around for lower-cost or higher-quality care providers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make certain contract clauses illegal in health care and insurance agreements. Banned clauses include ones that block insurers from steering patients to cheaper or better providers, force plans to use a provider's affiliated facilities, set prices for those facilities, or stop other plans from negotiating lower prices. Some exceptions apply for HMOs with exclusive physician groups and certain value-based care networks like accountable care organizations or centers of excellence.
ai_sections.who: The rules would apply to insurance companies, employer-sponsored health plans, hospitals, doctor networks, and third-party administrators. Federal agencies would have one year to write the detailed rules, and the requirements would cover contracts signed, changed, or renewed starting 18 months after the law passes.
ai_sections.why: Without these contract restrictions, health plans may have less freedom to direct patients toward lower-cost or higher-quality providers. The bill changes what employers and insurers can legally agree to when they sign contracts with health care providers.

title: SAWMILL Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal loan guarantee program to help private sawmills and wood-processing facilities in rural areas get financing to build, reopen, upgrade, or expand their operations. To qualify, a facility must be located within 250 miles of federal land that the U.S. Department of Agriculture and the Department of the Interior have identified as needing ecological restoration — meaning land where trees or other vegetation need to be removed for environmental health reasons. The idea is that having a nearby mill would lower the government's cost of doing that restoration work, since there would be a local buyer or processor for the removed timber. The total amount the government could guarantee in loans under this program is capped at $220 million. The people most directly affected are rural sawmill owners and wood-processing businesses, as well as federal land managers responsible for forest and land restoration.
ai_headline: Federal loans would back rural sawmill expansions near restoration lands
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Agriculture, and in addition to the Committee on Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: The federal government would guarantee up to $220 million in loans to help rural sawmills build, reopen, or expand near federal lands needing restoration.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal loan guarantee program for private sawmills and wood-processing facilities in rural areas. To qualify, a facility must be within 250 miles of federal land identified by the USDA or Department of the Interior as needing ecological restoration.
ai_sections.who: Rural sawmill owners and wood-processing businesses would be the primary people who could apply. Federal land managers overseeing forest and land restoration would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Having a nearby mill could lower the government's cost of ecological restoration work, since removed timber would have a local buyer or processor. The total loan guarantees under this program would be capped at $220 million.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government could guarantee up to $220 million in loans under this program. The bill does not specify direct federal spending, only a cap on the loan guarantees.

title: Heat Workforce Standards Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would permanently block the federal government's workplace safety agency (OSHA) from putting into effect a rule it proposed in August 2024 that was designed to protect workers from heat-related injuries and illnesses, both outdoors and indoors. If passed, the Department of Labor would be forbidden from finishing, rolling out, or enforcing that specific rule — or any rule that is closely similar to it. The bill would affect any worker in the United States whose job exposes them to heat hazards, such as agricultural workers, construction workers, warehouse employees, and others who work in hot environments. It would also affect employers in those industries, who under the proposed OSHA rule would have had new legal obligations to protect their workers from heat dangers.
ai_headline: Bill would block OSHA's proposed heat safety rule
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6213 would permanently block OSHA from finalizing or enforcing its 2024 proposed rule to protect workers from heat hazards.
ai_sections.what: This bill would permanently stop the federal workplace safety agency, OSHA, from completing or enforcing a rule it proposed in August 2024 to protect workers from heat-related injuries and illnesses. It would also block any future rule that is closely similar to that one.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects workers in jobs with heat hazards, including agricultural, construction, and warehouse workers. It also affects their employers, who would have faced new legal duties to protect workers from heat under the proposed OSHA rule.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the protections that OSHA's proposed heat rule would have required could not be put in place through that rule or a similar one. Employers in affected industries would not face the new legal obligations that rule would have created.

title: Conscience Protections for Medical Residents Act
ai_summary: This bill would change the rules for medical residency programs that receive Medicare funding, specifically around abortion training. Under the bill, a residency program would lose its status as a Medicare-approved program if it automatically enrolls residents in abortion training without their prior agreement — meaning programs could only include such training if residents actively choose to participate ("opt in"). The bill also protects residents from being penalized or treated differently because they choose not to opt in to abortion training, or because they decline to perform, assist with, or provide referrals for abortions. This affects medical residents — doctors in post-graduate training — as well as the hospitals and training programs that rely on Medicare funding to support their residency programs.
ai_headline: Medicare residency programs face new abortion opt-in rules
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Residency programs getting Medicare funds must let doctors opt in to abortion training — not enroll them automatically.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes the rules for medical residency programs that receive Medicare funding. These programs could no longer automatically sign residents up for abortion training. Residents would have to actively choose to join that training, and they could not be penalized for choosing not to.
ai_sections.who: This affects medical residents — doctors completing post-graduate training. It also affects the hospitals and residency programs that receive Medicare funding.
ai_sections.why: Programs that automatically enroll residents in abortion training without their consent would lose their Medicare-approved status. Residents who decline abortion training, or who refuse to perform or refer for abortions, would be protected from negative treatment.

title: NIH IMPROVE Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the National Institutes of Health (NIH) to formally continue and expand a program focused on improving maternal health outcomes in the United States, known as the IMPROVE Initiative. The program would fund research aimed at reducing preventable maternal deaths and serious pregnancy-related health complications, with a particular focus on closing gaps between groups that experience higher rates of these problems compared to the national average — such as communities of color. It would support community-based programs and scientific studies to identify the biological, behavioral, and social factors behind leading causes of maternal death and illness, including research targeted to specific regions of the country. The bill would authorize up to $73.4 million per year from 2026 through 2031 to carry out this work. The people most directly affected are pregnant and postpartum women across the United States, especially those in communities where maternal death and serious complications occur at disproportionately high rates.
ai_headline: NIH maternal health program seeks $73M yearly boost
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 46 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: The NIH IMPROVE Act would keep and grow a federal program focused on reducing preventable maternal deaths, with up to $73.4M per year through 2031.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the National Institutes of Health (NIH) to continue and expand a program called the IMPROVE Initiative. It would fund research and community-based programs to study why pregnant and postpartum women die or suffer serious complications, including research focused on specific regions and communities where these rates are highest.
ai_sections.who: Pregnant and postpartum women across the United States would be most directly affected. The bill places special focus on communities of color and other groups that experience maternal death and serious complications at higher rates than the national average.
ai_sections.why: Without continued federal direction, the IMPROVE Initiative may not receive dedicated funding or coordination. This bill sets a formal structure for that work to continue from 2026 through 2031.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize up to $73.4 million per year from 2026 through 2031 for this program.

title: To direct the Secretary of Defense to establish a pilot program for partnerships between covered basic needs banks and military installations to provide diapers and diapering supplies to military families, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Defense to create a test program connecting military families in financial need with nonprofit diaper banks so those families can receive free diapers and diapering supplies. The program would work by building partnerships between military bases and qualifying nonprofit organizations that are already members of the National Diaper Bank Network and have been distributing diapers for at least five years. The Defense Department would also seek an agreement with the National Diaper Bank Network itself to provide guidance to the participating nonprofits and measure how well the program is working. The people directly affected are military families who are struggling to afford diapers, as well as the nonprofit diaper banks that would partner with nearby military installations to serve them.
ai_headline: Pentagon pilot would give free diapers to military families
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: The Defense Department would run a test program connecting military families who need diapers with nonprofit diaper banks near their bases.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Department of Defense to create a pilot program linking military bases with nonprofit diaper banks to provide free diapers and diapering supplies to military families in financial need. Participating nonprofits must already belong to the National Diaper Bank Network and have been distributing diapers for at least five years. The Defense Department would also work with the National Diaper Bank Network to guide participating organizations and track how well the program is working.
ai_sections.who: Military families who are struggling to afford diapers would be directly served by this program. Nonprofit diaper banks located near military installations would partner with those bases to carry out the work.
ai_sections.why: Without a program like this, military families in financial need may have no clear path to access free diapers through their installation. The pilot structure means the program would be tested before any broader rollout is considered.

title: Fisheries Data Modernization and Accuracy Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires the federal government's fisheries agency (NOAA/National Marine Fisheries Service) to overhaul how it collects data on recreational saltwater fishing — the system currently used to estimate how many fish anglers catch — to make that data more accurate and better suited to local conditions. It sets up a standing expert committee through the National Academies of Sciences to regularly review data quality problems, and when the data for a particular fishery is too imprecise or unreliable, the agency must explore ways to fix it or adjust fishing rules accordingly. States would be allowed to run their own recreational fishing data collection programs, which could replace the federal system for management decisions, and the bill authorizes up to $15 million per year from 2026 through 2031 to help states build or improve those programs. The bill also requires the agency to contract with outside scientific organizations to conduct independent fish population surveys, directs the creation of a formal schedule for updating fish stock assessments, and adds new transparency rules requiring fishery management councils to make their meetings publicly accessible online, including recordings or transcripts. The people most directly affected are recreational saltwater anglers, commercial fishing communities, state fish and wildlife agencies, and the scientists and managers who set fishing rules.
ai_headline: NOAA recreational fishing data rules would get major overhaul
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: Federal law would overhaul how recreational saltwater fishing data is collected and let states run their own programs in place of the federal system.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the federal fisheries agency (NOAA) to improve how it counts fish caught by recreational saltwater anglers, making that data more accurate and locally relevant. States could run their own data collection programs that would count for official fishing management decisions. The bill also calls for independent fish population surveys, a regular schedule for updating fish stock assessments, and requires fishery management councils to post meetings, recordings, or transcripts online.
ai_sections.who: Recreational saltwater anglers, commercial fishing communities, state fish and wildlife agencies, and the scientists and managers who set fishing rules would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: When fishing data is too imprecise or unreliable, the agency would be required to look for ways to fix it or adjust fishing rules. Fishery management meetings would need to be publicly accessible online, changing how those decisions are visible to the public.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $15 million per year from 2026 through 2031 is authorized to help states build or improve their own recreational fishing data collection programs.
costChips: Funds state recreational fishing data programs

title: ASAP Act
ai_summary: Starting January 1, 2028, this bill would require Medicare to cover blood-based tests that can detect Alzheimer's disease and related dementias before or in the early stages of symptoms. To qualify for coverage, a test must be approved or cleared by the FDA and must involve analyzing blood or other biological samples — including types of tests that look at genes, proteins, cell-free nucleic acids, or similar markers. The government health agency overseeing Medicare would have authority to decide which specific tests qualify as long as they produce results comparable to the core blood-based genomic tests described in the bill. This change would directly benefit Medicare enrollees — generally people 65 and older, as well as certain people with disabilities — by potentially removing the out-of-pocket cost barrier for these early detection screenings.
ai_headline: Medicare would cover blood tests for early Alzheimer's detection
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2028, Medicare would be required to cover FDA-approved blood tests that detect Alzheimer's and related dementias early.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require Medicare to cover blood-based tests that can detect Alzheimer's disease and related dementias before or in the early stages of symptoms, starting January 1, 2028. To qualify, a test must be FDA-approved or cleared and must analyze blood or other biological samples, including tests that look at genes, proteins, cell-free nucleic acids, or similar markers. The agency overseeing Medicare would decide which specific tests qualify, as long as results are comparable to the core blood-based tests described in the bill.
ai_sections.who: Medicare enrollees would be affected — generally people 65 and older, as well as certain people with disabilities.
