ai_headline: Staged truck crashes would become a federal crime
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal bill would make staging a crash with a semi-truck or bus a crime punishable by up to 20 years in prison.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it a federal crime to deliberately cause a crash involving a large commercial vehicle, like a semi-truck or bus, or to arrange for someone else to do so. If the staged crash causes serious injury or death, the minimum prison sentence is 20 years.
ai_sections.who: This law would apply to people who plan or carry out staged collisions, often done to fraudulently collect insurance money. A person cannot be charged under this federal law if they have already been convicted or acquitted for the same act under a state or local law.
ai_sections.why: People convicted under this law could face a fine, up to 20 years in prison, or both. Staged crashes involving large commercial vehicles can result in serious injuries or deaths to uninvolved people on the road.

title: Puppy Protection Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would set new federal rules for how licensed dog dealers — businesses that breed and sell dogs commercially — must care for their animals. It requires dealers to give dogs solid-floored enclosures of a minimum size based on the dog's length, keep temperatures between 45 and 85 degrees Fahrenheit, provide food at least twice a day and constant access to clean water, and give dogs over 12 weeks old daily access to an outdoor exercise area. Dealers would also have to give each dog at least 30 minutes of hands-on human interaction every day, follow specific veterinary care and vaccination requirements, and limit how often a female dog can be bred — no more than two litters in any 25-month period and no more than six litters in her lifetime, with age restrictions based on the dog's size. When a breeding dog is retired, the dealer must make reasonable efforts to place her with an adoptive family or rescue organization rather than selling her at auction or sending her to another breeder. The federal government would have 18 months after the bill becomes law to publish official regulations putting these standards into effect.
ai_headline: Federal bill sets care standards for commercial dog breeders
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2253 would require licensed dog dealers to meet new federal standards for housing, care, and breeding limits.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets federal rules for how licensed dog dealers must house and care for their dogs. Rules cover enclosure size and flooring, temperature range, feeding, water, daily exercise, and at least 30 minutes of hands-on human interaction each day. It also limits how often a female dog can be bred and requires dealers to seek adoptive homes for retired breeding dogs.
ai_sections.who: These rules would apply to licensed dog dealers — businesses that breed and sell dogs commercially. The federal government would have 18 months after the bill becomes law to publish the official regulations.
ai_sections.why: Without these standards, federal law does not specify many of these care and breeding requirements for commercial dog dealers. Setting them creates a national baseline that dealers would be legally required to follow.

title: Federal Firearm Licensee Act
ai_summary: This bill would place significantly stricter rules on federally licensed firearm dealers — the gun shops and businesses that sell firearms to the public. It would require dealers to submit and follow physical security plans (including locked safes, alarms, and security cameras), conduct quarterly inventory checks, keep electronic records permanently, and submit to more frequent government inspections — with annual inspections for dealers flagged as high-risk. Online marketplaces that host firearm sales would need to obtain their own federal license and ensure all transactions go through a licensed dealer who performs a background check, and both dealers and their employees would have to pass background checks before handling firearms at a business. The bill would also increase licensing fees, raise penalties for selling guns without conducting background checks, expand the types of guns that trigger multi-sale reporting requirements, require background check records to be kept for at least 90 business days instead of being destroyed, and remove several existing legal protections that have shielded dealers from enforcement actions — affecting gun dealers, their employees, online firearm marketplace operators, and anyone who buys or sells firearms through those channels.
ai_headline: Stricter rules proposed for licensed gun dealers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2618 adds strict new security, recordkeeping, and inspection rules for licensed gun dealers and online firearm marketplaces.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets stricter rules for federally licensed gun dealers. Dealers would need security plans with safes, alarms, and cameras, quarterly inventory checks, permanent electronic records, and more frequent government inspections. Online platforms that host gun sales would need their own federal license and would have to make sure all sales go through a licensed dealer with a background check.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects licensed gun dealers and their employees, online firearm marketplace operators, and anyone who buys or sells firearms through those channels.
ai_sections.why: Dealers and employees would have to pass background checks before handling guns at a business. Background check records would have to be kept for at least 90 business days instead of being destroyed, and several legal protections that have shielded dealers from enforcement actions would be removed.
ai_sections.cost: Licensing fees would increase under this bill. Penalties for selling guns without conducting background checks would also be raised.

title: Consensual Donation and Research Integrity Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would create a new federal oversight system for businesses that collect and sell human bodies or body parts for purposes like medical education, scientific research, or training — not for organ transplants. Companies doing this kind of work for profit would have to register with the federal government, pay fees, pass regular inspections, and keep detailed records showing that donors gave their informed consent, how each body or body part was used, and where it ended up. Every body or body part would also have to be properly labeled and packaged with specific information, including the donor's name, cause of death, any infectious diseases, and a clear notice that the material is "not for transplantation." When the work is done, the business must ensure the remains are properly returned to the donor's family or disposed of according to the donor's written wishes. Businesses that break these rules can be fined, and the government can suspend or revoke their registration; the rules would apply to acquisitions and transfers starting two years after the law is enacted.
ai_headline: Federal rules proposed for human body brokers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal law would require companies that sell human bodies or body parts for research and education to register, keep records, and face fines for violations.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a new federal oversight system for for-profit companies that collect and sell human bodies or body parts for medical education, scientific research, or training — not for organ transplants. These companies would have to register with the federal government, pay fees, pass regular inspections, and keep detailed records of donor consent, how each body or part was used, and where it ended up. Every body or body part would have to be labeled with specific information, and remains would have to be returned to the family or disposed of according to the donor's written wishes.
ai_sections.who: For-profit businesses that collect and sell human bodies or body parts for research, education, or training would have to follow these rules. The federal government would oversee registration, inspections, and enforcement.
ai_sections.why: Without these rules, there is no consistent federal system to verify that donors gave informed consent or to track where human remains end up. Companies that break the rules can be fined and can have their registration suspended or revoked.
ai_sections.cost: Companies would be required to pay registration fees to the federal government. Businesses that violate the rules can be fined, though the specific amounts are not included in the summary.

title: CARE Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require a federal government agency that tests new Medicare payment approaches to launch a five-year pilot program that pays ground ambulance providers for treating Medicare patients on-scene during emergencies — even when no ambulance transport to a hospital takes place. Right now, Medicare generally only pays ambulance providers when they physically transport a patient to a medical facility, so this pilot would create a new payment option for situations where emergency responders arrive, provide care, and the patient does not need to be taken to the hospital. The payment rates in the pilot would be set roughly in line with what ambulance providers would have been paid if they had transported the patient, and if a telehealth visit is provided alongside that on-scene care, the location where the patient is treated would qualify for the telehealth payment rules. The program would primarily affect Medicare beneficiaries who call 911 for emergencies and the ground ambulance companies and providers who respond to those calls. Within four years of the pilot's launch, the Government Accountability Office would be required to report to Congress on how the program affected patient outcomes, access to emergency services, and costs compared to traditional emergency transportation.
ai_headline: Medicare would pay ambulances for on-scene care without transport
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: A 5-year pilot would let Medicare pay ground ambulance providers for on-scene emergency care even when no hospital transport occurs.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a five-year pilot program requiring a federal agency to pay ground ambulance providers for treating Medicare patients on-scene during emergencies, even if the patient is never transported to a hospital. Payment rates would be set roughly in line with what providers would have earned for a transport. If telehealth is also provided during that on-scene visit, the treatment location would qualify under telehealth payment rules.
ai_sections.who: Medicare beneficiaries who call 911 for emergencies are the patients affected. Ground ambulance companies and providers who respond to those calls would receive the new payments.
ai_sections.why: Currently, Medicare generally only pays ambulance providers when they physically transport a patient to a medical facility, leaving on-scene-only care unpaid. The Government Accountability Office would be required to report to Congress within four years on how the pilot affected patient outcomes, access to emergency services, and costs compared to traditional emergency transportation.

title: Secure Family Futures Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes two federal tax rules that apply specifically to most U.S. insurance companies (with a few exceptions, such as certain small insurers, foreign insurers, and nonprofit health organizations). First, it says that when these insurance companies hold debt instruments — like bonds, notes, or other loans — those investments will no longer be treated as "capital assets" for tax purposes, meaning any losses on them can be treated as ordinary losses rather than capital losses, which are subject to stricter tax rules. Second, it extends the period during which these same insurance companies can "carry over" capital losses — that is, use past investment losses to offset future taxable gains — from 5 years to 10 years. Both changes would apply starting with investments and losses occurring after December 31, 2025.
ai_headline: Bill extends insurer capital-loss carryover to 10 years
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2547 changes two tax rules for most U.S. insurance companies, starting with losses after December 31, 2025.
ai_sections.what: This bill makes two changes to federal tax rules for insurance companies. First, losses on debt investments like bonds would be counted as ordinary losses, not capital losses, which follow stricter rules. Second, insurance companies would have 10 years instead of 5 to use past investment losses to offset future taxable gains.
ai_sections.who: These changes apply to most U.S. insurance companies. A few types are excluded, including certain small insurers, foreign insurers, and nonprofit health organizations.
ai_sections.why: Counting debt investment losses as ordinary losses gives insurance companies more flexibility in how those losses are treated at tax time. Extending the carryover period from 5 to 10 years means companies have a longer window to apply past losses against future gains.

title: Freedom of Association in Higher Education Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would prohibit colleges and universities that receive federal funding from punishing or disadvantaging students simply because they belong to a single-sex social organization, such as a fraternity or sorority. Specifically, a school could not expel, suspend, deny scholarships, block housing, withhold job recommendations, or take other negative actions against a student solely because their organization limits membership to one sex. Schools also could not require students to disclose or swear they are not members of such a group, and could not impose special recruiting rules on these organizations that don't also apply to other student groups — unless the organization itself agrees to those rules in writing. The bill does not force schools to officially recognize these organizations, and it still allows schools to discipline students for actual misconduct or because an organization poses a clear harm to the campus community, as long as the discipline isn't based solely on the single-sex membership policy. The bill primarily affects students at federally funded colleges and universities who belong to — or want to join — fraternities, sororities, or similar single-sex social clubs.
ai_headline: Bill protects students in single-sex fraternities and sororities
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Federally funded colleges could not punish students solely for joining a single-sex fraternity, sorority, or similar social group.
ai_sections.what: This bill would stop federally funded colleges and universities from taking negative actions against students just because they belong to a single-sex social organization like a fraternity or sorority. Schools could not expel, suspend, deny scholarships, block housing, or withhold job recommendations for that reason alone. Schools also could not make students disclose or deny membership, or impose special recruiting rules on these groups that do not apply to other student organizations, unless the group agrees in writing.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects students at federally funded colleges and universities who belong to or want to join fraternities, sororities, or similar single-sex social clubs.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, schools that receive federal funding can continue to penalize students solely for belonging to single-sex social organizations. Schools would still be allowed to discipline students for actual misconduct or when an organization poses a clear harm to the campus community, as long as single-sex membership alone is not the reason.

title: Nutrition CARE Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would add Medicare coverage for medical nutrition therapy (one-on-one counseling with a registered dietitian or nutrition professional) for people diagnosed with an eating disorder, starting January 1, 2026. Currently, Medicare does not cover this type of nutritional counseling for eating disorders, so the bill fills that gap by requiring Medicare to pay for at least 13 hours of these sessions in the first year of treatment and at least 4 hours per year after that. To receive covered services, a patient would need a referral from either a doctor or a mental health professional such as a psychologist. The bill affects Medicare beneficiaries — primarily people 65 and older, as well as certain younger people with disabilities — who have been diagnosed with an eating disorder as defined by the most recent edition of the standard psychiatric diagnostic manual.
ai_headline: Medicare would cover eating disorder nutrition therapy
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2026, Medicare would cover nutrition counseling with a dietitian for people diagnosed with an eating disorder.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require Medicare to cover one-on-one nutrition counseling sessions with a registered dietitian or nutrition professional for people with eating disorders. Coverage would begin January 1, 2026, and would include at least 13 hours in the first year of treatment and at least 4 hours each year after that. A patient would need a referral from a doctor or a mental health professional such as a psychologist to receive these covered sessions.
ai_sections.who: Medicare beneficiaries affected would be people 65 and older, as well as certain younger people with disabilities, who have been diagnosed with an eating disorder. The diagnosis must match the definition in the most recent edition of the standard psychiatric diagnostic manual.
ai_sections.why: Currently, Medicare does not cover this type of nutrition counseling for eating disorders, leaving a gap in available services. This bill would fill that gap by adding eating disorders to the conditions for which Medicare pays for nutrition therapy.

title: Transgender Health Care Access Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal government to fund programs that increase the number of health care providers trained to offer gender-affirming care — medical and mental health treatment for people with gender dysphoria. It would provide grants to medical schools, nursing programs, community health centers, and other health care training sites to develop educational materials, train doctors and other clinicians, and build up the capacity of local health clinics to provide this type of care. A separate set of grants focuses specifically on rural providers, funding networks where experienced clinicians can share knowledge and training with providers in less populated areas. The bill would authorize a total of up to $45 million per year (across all four grant programs) for fiscal years 2026 through 2030, and requires the Department of Health and Human Services to report to Congress within two years on the progress and outcomes of these programs. The people most directly affected are transgender individuals seeking care, as well as the health care professionals and institutions that would receive training and funding.
ai_headline: Federal grants would expand gender-affirming care training
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2487 would fund training programs to help more health care providers learn to treat transgender patients.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create federal grants for medical schools, nursing programs, and community health centers to train doctors and other clinicians in gender-affirming care. A separate set of grants would support rural providers by funding networks where experienced clinicians share knowledge with providers in less-populated areas. The Department of Health and Human Services would be required to report to Congress on the results within two years.
ai_sections.who: Transgender people seeking care would be directly affected, along with the health care professionals and institutions that would receive training and funding.
ai_sections.why: Without enough trained providers, some patients may have difficulty finding care. These programs are intended to expand the pool of clinicians who can offer this type of treatment.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize up to $45 million per year across all four grant programs for fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

title: Early Detection of Vision Impairments for Children Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal grant program to help states, U.S. territories, and Tribal organizations build or improve programs that catch vision problems in children early. Eligible recipients — including state health departments, state education agencies, and Tribal health organizations — could use the money for things like setting up vision screening programs in schools and clinics, collecting data on children's eye health, raising public awareness, reducing barriers to eye care in rural and underserved communities, and connecting families to follow-up vision services. A separate set of grants would go to public or nonprofit organizations to provide technical support, conduct research, and help share best practices across states. Grant recipients would be required to submit annual reports on their progress, and those reports would be made publicly available. The bill authorizes $5 million per year from 2026 through 2030 for the main grant program and another $5 million per year over the same period for reporting and evaluation activities.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund children's vision screening programs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2527 would fund federal grants to help states, territories, and Tribes build or improve early vision screening programs for children.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal grant program to help catch vision problems in children early. Grants could be used for school and clinic screenings, data collection, public awareness, and connecting families to follow-up eye care. A separate set of grants would support research and help share best practices across states.
ai_sections.who: State health departments, state education agencies, and Tribal health organizations could apply for the main grants. Public and nonprofit organizations could apply for the separate technical support and research grants.
ai_sections.why: Without programs like these, vision problems in children may go undetected, particularly in rural and underserved communities. Grant recipients would report their progress annually, and those reports would be made public.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $5 million per year from 2026 through 2030 for the main grant program and another $5 million per year over the same period for reporting and evaluation activities.

title: No In-State Tuition for Illegal Immigrants Act
ai_summary: This bill would cut off a state's federal student financial aid funding if that state charges in-state (lower, resident) tuition rates to people who are in the United States without legal immigration status. Specifically, if the U.S. Secretary of Education determines that a state is giving these lower tuition rates to undocumented immigrants at public colleges and universities, the entire state would lose its federal Title IV financial aid funding — things like Pell Grants and federal student loans — for the following year. This would affect all students in that state who rely on federal financial aid, not just undocumented students, since the penalty cuts off funding to the whole state. Currently, a number of states allow undocumented students who attended high school in that state to pay in-state tuition rates, and those states would face a choice between ending that policy or losing federal aid money for all their students.
ai_headline: States risk Title IV funds over undocumented tuition rates
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: States that charge undocumented students in-state tuition could lose all federal student aid funding for every student in that state.
ai_sections.what: This bill would remove all federal Title IV student aid funding from any state that charges undocumented immigrants in-state tuition rates at public colleges and universities. That includes Pell Grants and federal student loans. The penalty applies to the whole state, not just to undocumented students.
ai_sections.who: Any state that currently allows undocumented students to pay in-state tuition would be affected. All students in those states who rely on federal financial aid could lose access to that funding.
ai_sections.why: Affected states would have to choose between ending in-state tuition for undocumented students or losing federal aid for all their students. The U.S. Secretary of Education would be responsible for determining whether a state is out of compliance.
ai_sections.cost: Federal Title IV funding, which includes Pell Grants and federal student loans, would be cut off for the entire following year in any state found to be out of compliance.
costChips: Federal Title IV funding would be cut
Includes Pell Grants and student loans
Noncompliant states lose funds for 1 year

title: America's Red Rock Wilderness Act
ai_summary: This bill would permanently protect approximately 9 million acres of federally owned land in Utah — covering areas like the Colorado Plateau's red rock canyons and the Great Basin Deserts — by officially designating them as wilderness under federal law, which means no new roads, mining, drilling, or development would be allowed there. The land would continue to be managed by the Bureau of Land Management, and activities already permitted at the time the law takes effect — such as livestock grazing — could continue under reasonable rules. People who hunt, fish, hike, camp, or ride horses could still use these areas, and the bill explicitly protects the rights of federally recognized Tribes to continue traditional uses of the land. Water rights sufficient for each wilderness area would be reserved by the federal government, and any state-owned land that happens to fall within a designated wilderness boundary would be exchanged for federal land of roughly equal value elsewhere in Utah. The bill primarily affects landowners, ranchers, mining and energy companies, outdoor recreationists, Indigenous communities, and state and local governments in Utah who have existing or potential interests in these lands.
ai_headline: Bill would permanently protect 9M acres in Utah
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2467 would permanently ban roads, mining, and drilling on about 9 million acres of federal land in Utah.
ai_sections.what: This bill would designate roughly 9 million acres of federally owned land in Utah — including red rock canyons and desert areas — as official wilderness. Once designated, no new roads, mining, drilling, or development would be allowed. Hiking, hunting, fishing, camping, horseback riding, and existing livestock grazing could continue.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects ranchers, mining and energy companies, outdoor recreationists, federally recognized Tribes, and state and local governments in Utah. Tribes would keep the right to use the land for traditional purposes.
ai_sections.why: Wilderness designation under federal law places permanent restrictions on how the land can be used, which would limit future options for resource development and infrastructure in those areas. State-owned land inside the wilderness boundaries would be swapped for federal land of similar value elsewhere in Utah.

title: INFANTS Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require companies that make food for babies and toddlers (children up to 24 months old) to regularly test their products for harmful heavy metals — specifically lead, cadmium, mercury, and arsenic — at least four times a year, and to keep detailed records of those tests for at least two years. Manufacturers of powdered infant formula would also have to set up formal monitoring programs to check their facilities for dangerous bacteria, particularly *Cronobacter* and *Salmonella*, and would be required to notify the FDA within 24 hours if testing confirms the presence of those pathogens in a finished or in-process product. If a company fails to conduct the required testing, its products could be legally classified as adulterated — meaning unsafe — and the FDA would gain the authority to order a mandatory recall of infant and toddler foods found to contain harmful contaminants. The bill primarily affects food manufacturers, processors, and facilities that produce products marketed for babies and young children, and it gives the FDA stronger tools to request records and take action when safety problems are found.
ai_headline: FDA would gain new powers over baby food safety
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2472 would require baby and toddler food makers to test for heavy metals 4x/year and report dangerous bacteria to FDA within 24 hours.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require companies that make food for babies and toddlers up to 24 months old to test their products for lead, cadmium, mercury, and arsenic at least four times a year, keeping those test records for at least two years. Makers of powdered infant formula would also have to monitor their facilities for Cronobacter and Salmonella bacteria and report confirmed cases to the FDA within 24 hours. Products from companies that skip required testing could be classified as unsafe, and the FDA would have the authority to order mandatory recalls.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects food manufacturers, processors, and facilities that make products marketed for babies and young children. The FDA would receive stronger authority to request records and take action when safety problems are found.
ai_sections.why: Without these requirements, there is no federal mandate for routine heavy metal testing or fast reporting of dangerous bacteria in baby food. If a company does not follow the rules, its products can be legally labeled unsafe and removed from the market.

title: Safer Skies Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require certain smaller commercial flight operations to follow the same strict security screening rules that major airlines already must follow. Specifically, it targets air carriers that fly planes with more than nine passenger seats, sell individual tickets to the public in advance, publish their schedules (including departure and arrival locations and times), and operate at airports or terminals where TSA does not already run a security checkpoint. These carriers — which currently operate under less stringent rules — would have to adopt the full "Aircraft Operator Standard Security Program," the comprehensive security standard that governs large commercial airlines. The Transportation Security Administration would have 360 days after the law is enacted to update its rules, guidance, and policies to make this happen. In practical terms, passengers flying on these smaller scheduled charter-style operations would receive security screening similar to what travelers experience on major airlines.
ai_headline: Small charter airlines would face TSA screening rules
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Transportation and Maritime Security.

ai_sections.tldr: Smaller scheduled charter flights would face the same TSA security screening rules as major airlines under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require certain smaller commercial flight operations to follow the same security screening rules that major airlines already follow. To qualify, a carrier must fly planes with more than nine passenger seats, sell individual tickets in advance, publish its schedules, and operate where TSA does not already run a security checkpoint. TSA would have 360 days after the law passes to update its rules.
ai_sections.who: Air carriers running smaller scheduled charter-style operations that currently face less strict security rules would be affected. Passengers flying on those routes would go through security screening similar to what major airline travelers experience.
ai_sections.why: Right now, some smaller scheduled commercial carriers operate under looser security standards than major airlines. This bill would close that gap by applying the full Aircraft Operator Standard Security Program to those carriers.

title: Rhode Island Fishermen's Fairness Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would add Rhode Island to the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council, a federal body that sets fishing rules for waters off the Mid-Atlantic coast of the United States. Currently, Rhode Island is only part of the New England Fishery Management Council, meaning it has no formal seat or voting power on the Mid-Atlantic Council even though Rhode Island fishermen fish in those waters and are directly affected by the rules that council makes. By adding Rhode Island, the bill would also increase the total number of seats on the Mid-Atlantic Council from 21 to 23, and the number of voting members from 13 to 14. This change would primarily affect Rhode Island's commercial and recreational fishing industries by giving the state a voice in decisions about fish species and fishing limits managed by the Mid-Atlantic Council.
ai_headline: Senate bill adds Rhode Island to Mid-Atlantic Fishery Council
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S1874-1875)

ai_sections.tldr: S 1152 would give Rhode Island a formal seat and vote on the federal council that sets fishing rules for Mid-Atlantic waters.
ai_sections.what: This bill would add Rhode Island to the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council, the federal body that sets fishing rules for waters off the Mid-Atlantic coast. It would grow the council from 21 to 23 total seats and from 13 to 14 voting members.
ai_sections.who: Rhode Island's commercial and recreational fishing industries would be most directly affected. Right now, Rhode Island has no formal seat or vote on this council, even though Rhode Island fishermen fish in those waters and live under its rules.
ai_sections.why: Rhode Island fishermen currently fish in Mid-Atlantic waters and are affected by the council's decisions without having any formal say in them. Adding the state would give it a voice in decisions about fish species and fishing limits set by that council.

title: Rhode Island Fishermen’s Fairness Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would add Rhode Island as a full member state of the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council, a federal body that oversees fish populations and sets fishing rules for the waters stretching roughly from New York to North Carolina. Right now, Rhode Island is not included in this council, even though Rhode Island fishermen fish in the same waters that the council manages. By joining, Rhode Island would gain voting seats on the council — the bill increases the council's total membership from 21 to 23 seats, with the number of voting members growing from 13 to 14. This change would most directly affect Rhode Island's commercial and recreational fishing industries, since their state would have a formal say in decisions about fish catch limits, seasons, and other rules that affect their livelihoods.
ai_headline: Rhode Island seeks a seat on Mid-Atlantic fishing council
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2375 would make Rhode Island a full voting member of the council that sets fishing rules for Mid-Atlantic waters.
ai_sections.what: This bill would add Rhode Island as a full member of the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council, the federal body that sets fishing rules for waters from New York to North Carolina. It would grow the council from 21 to 23 total seats and increase voting members from 13 to 14.
ai_sections.who: Rhode Island's commercial and recreational fishermen would be most directly affected. Right now, Rhode Island has no formal seat on this council, even though its fishermen work the same waters the council oversees.
ai_sections.why: With membership, Rhode Island would have a vote on decisions about catch limits, fishing seasons, and other rules that affect how its fishermen can do their work.

title: Healthy Poultry Assistance and Indemnification Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require the federal government to pay poultry farmers — specifically those who raise chickens, turkeys, and similar birds for meat or eggs — when a disease outbreak forces their farm to shut down because it falls within a government-designated "control area," even if their own birds were never infected or destroyed. The payment would be calculated by looking at the average income the farmer earned from their five most recent flocks, then multiplying that by the number of flocks they were prevented from raising while the control area was active. Any money the farmer already received from a state government or other source for the same lost flocks would be subtracted from the federal payment, so farmers cannot be paid twice for the same loss. The government must issue the payment within 60 days of receiving the farmer's request, though farmers who already received separate federal compensation for having their birds destroyed at the same facility during the same period would not qualify for this additional payment.
ai_headline: Poultry farmers near disease zones could receive lost-income payments
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.

ai_sections.tldr: Poultry farmers in a disease control area could receive federal pay for lost flocks even if their own birds were never infected.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the federal government to pay poultry farmers for flocks they could not raise because their farm fell inside a government-designated disease control area. The payment would be based on the farmer's average income from their five most recent flocks, multiplied by the number of flocks they were blocked from raising. Any money already received from a state or other source for the same lost flocks would be subtracted, and farmers who already received federal compensation for having birds destroyed at the same farm during the same period would not qualify.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to poultry farmers who raise chickens, turkeys, or similar birds for meat or eggs. To qualify, their farm must have fallen within a government disease control area during an outbreak, and they must not have already received separate federal compensation for destroyed birds at that facility during the same time.
ai_sections.why: Disease control areas can shut down farms near an outbreak even when those farms have no infected or destroyed birds, leaving farmers without income for that period. This bill creates a federal payment process to address income lost during that forced shutdown.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would be required to issue payments to qualifying poultry farmers within 60 days of receiving a request. The bill does not specify a total funding amount.
costChips: Payments due within 60 days of request

title: Restore Protections for Dialysis Patients Act
ai_summary: This bill would strengthen federal rules that protect people with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) — a condition requiring ongoing dialysis or a kidney transplant — from being treated unfairly by their private health insurance plans. Specifically, it would prohibit those plans from offering dialysis patients fewer or worse benefits than other plan members, or from setting up coverage rules that would disproportionately hurt dialysis patients, such as covering dialysis at a much lower rate than other medical services. The goal is to prevent private insurers from effectively pushing the cost of caring for dialysis patients onto Medicare by making their own coverage so limited that patients have no real choice but to rely on the government program instead. The bill does include one notable exception: private health plans would still be allowed to decide which specific dialysis providers or facilities they include in their network, as long as they are not otherwise discriminating against ESRD patients. The people most directly affected are dialysis patients who have private health insurance — either through an employer or purchased on their own — and the insurers who cover them.
ai_headline: Bill targets insurer discrimination against dialysis patients
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would ban private health insurers from offering dialysis patients worse coverage than other members to push them toward Medicare.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal for private health insurance plans to give dialysis patients fewer or worse benefits than other members. Plans also could not set coverage rules that unfairly hurt dialysis patients, such as paying for dialysis at a much lower rate than other medical care. Private plans could still choose which dialysis providers to include in their networks, as long as they are not otherwise treating dialysis patients unfairly.
ai_sections.who: The people most directly affected are dialysis patients who have private health insurance, whether through an employer or purchased on their own. Private health insurers who cover those patients would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Without these rules, private insurers could make their dialysis coverage so limited that patients feel they have no real choice but to move to Medicare instead. This bill is a response to concern that such coverage decisions shift the cost of caring for dialysis patients from private insurers to the federal government.

title: Physician Led and Rural Access to Quality Care Act
ai_summary: This bill changes federal Medicare rules that currently restrict doctors from referring patients to hospitals in which the doctors themselves have a financial ownership stake. Specifically, it creates a new exception for hospitals located in rural areas that are also far from the nearest hospital — at least 35 miles away by road (or 15 miles in mountainous or remote terrain) — allowing physician-owners of those rural hospitals to refer their Medicare patients there without running into the usual legal restrictions. The bill also removes a long-standing rule that prevented existing physician-owned hospitals from expanding their facilities or number of beds, effectively allowing all physician-owned hospitals (not just rural ones) to grow in size going forward. The people most directly affected are doctors who have ownership shares in hospitals, patients in remote rural areas who may gain or lose access to certain facilities, and the hospitals themselves, particularly those in underserved locations far from other medical centers.
ai_headline: Medicare rural hospital physician-ownership rules would ease
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill lets doctor-owners refer Medicare patients to their own rural hospitals and allows all physician-owned hospitals to expand their facilities.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new exception to federal Medicare rules for rural hospitals that are at least 35 miles from the nearest hospital by road, or 15 miles in mountainous or remote areas. Doctors who own a share of those rural hospitals would be allowed to send their Medicare patients there. The bill also lifts an existing rule that blocked all physician-owned hospitals from adding beds or expanding their buildings.
ai_sections.who: Doctors who have an ownership stake in a hospital are directly affected, as are patients in remote rural areas who live far from other medical centers. Physician-owned hospitals, especially those in underserved locations, are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Removing the referral restriction could change where rural Medicare patients receive care, depending on what facilities are available near them. Lifting the expansion ban could lead to physician-owned hospitals growing in size across the country.

title: Maintaining and Enhancing Hydroelectricity and River Restoration Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new federal tax credit worth 30 percent of the cost of certain improvements made to existing hydroelectric dams. The credit can be used for a range of qualifying projects, including installing or upgrading fish passages to help fish migrate, improving water quality, removing old dams that no longer serve a purpose, making dams safer and more secure, and bringing small remote hydroelectric dams (up to 20 megawatts) into service for communities not connected to the main U.S. power grid. To qualify, a project must receive written approval from the appropriate federal, state, or local authorities before January 1, 2032, and the credit applies to property placed in service after December 31, 2022. The bill primarily affects dam owners and operators — including businesses, utilities, and other organizations — who pay federal taxes or who can transfer or elect direct payment of the credit if they do not owe enough taxes to use it directly.
ai_headline: Bill offers 30% tax credit for hydroelectric dam upgrades
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2160 offers dam owners a 30% federal tax credit for qualifying upgrades like fish passages, safety fixes, or dam removal, if approved before 2032.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a 30% federal tax credit for certain improvements to existing hydroelectric dams. Qualifying projects include installing fish passages, improving water quality, removing old dams, making dams safer, and connecting small remote dams of up to 20 megawatts to off-grid communities. The project must have written approval from a federal, state, or local authority before January 1, 2032, and the property must be placed in service after December 31, 2022.
ai_sections.who: Dam owners and operators, including businesses, utilities, and other organizations that pay federal taxes, may qualify. Those who do not owe enough federal taxes to use the credit directly may be able to transfer it or receive a direct payment instead.
ai_sections.why: Dam owners who make qualifying improvements may be able to reduce their federal tax bill through this credit. Communities not connected to the main power grid could see changes in their local energy supply if nearby small dams are brought into service.
ai_sections.cost: The credit covers 30% of the cost of qualifying improvements. The bill does not specify a total government spending amount, but the credit reduces federal tax revenue for each qualifying project.

title: BO’s Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to study whether home cardiorespiratory monitors — wearable or bedside devices that track a baby's heart rate, blood oxygen levels, and other vital signs — are effective at helping prevent sudden unexpected infant death, and to report the findings to Congress within one year. The report must cover how well these monitors work, how they might improve safe sleep environments for infants, and what standards health insurance plans should use to decide whether to cover them. It also asks for a recommendation on whether the evidence is strong enough to justify having public programs like Medicaid, or private insurance companies, pay for these devices. This bill primarily affects families with newborns or infants, pediatric health care providers, and health insurers, as the findings could eventually influence whether these monitors become a covered medical benefit.
ai_headline: HHS ordered to study infant heart monitors for SIDS prevention
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2168 orders a federal study on whether baby heart and breathing monitors prevent sudden infant death, with results due to Congress in one year.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to study whether home monitors that track a baby's heart rate, blood oxygen, and other vital signs help prevent sudden unexpected infant death. The department must report findings to Congress within one year, covering how well the monitors work, how they relate to safe sleep, and what standards insurers should use to decide whether to cover them. The report must also recommend whether the evidence is strong enough for public programs or private insurers to pay for these devices.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects families with newborns and infants, pediatric health care providers, and health insurers. The findings could influence whether these monitors become a covered medical benefit.
ai_sections.why: If the study finds strong evidence, it could lead public programs like Medicaid or private insurance companies to cover these devices. If evidence is weak, the report would reflect that, and coverage decisions would remain as they are today.

title: ROCR Value Based Program Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new Medicare payment system specifically for radiation therapy — the cancer treatment that uses high doses of radiation — replacing the current per-service billing with a single bundled payment that covers an entire course of treatment (called an "episode of care") for patients with one of 15 listed cancer types, such as breast, lung, prostate, or head and neck cancer. Under the new system, Medicare would pay radiation therapy clinics and hospital outpatient departments a fixed amount per patient per treatment episode, split into two installments, with the payment rate staying the same regardless of whether treatment happens in a hospital or a private clinic. The bill also creates a small add-on payment to help patients who lack reliable transportation get to and from their radiation appointments, and it sets up accreditation requirements — meaning clinics must meet recognized quality standards to receive full payment, or face a small payment reduction. Savings generated by this new payment approach would not be used to cut payments elsewhere in Medicare, which is what the "budget neutrality" exemption means in plain terms. The people most directly affected are Medicare patients undergoing radiation therapy for cancer and the doctors, clinics, and hospital departments that provide that treatment.
ai_headline: Medicare would shift radiation therapy to bundled payments
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Medicare would pay radiation therapy clinics a single fixed amount per cancer patient per treatment, instead of billing for each service separately.
ai_sections.what: This bill replaces Medicare's current per-service payments for radiation therapy with one bundled payment covering a full course of treatment for 15 cancer types, including breast, lung, and prostate cancer. Payment rates would be the same whether treatment happens at a hospital or a private clinic. The bill also adds a small extra payment to help patients with transportation needs get to their appointments, and requires clinics to meet accreditation standards to receive full payment.
ai_sections.who: Medicare patients receiving radiation therapy for cancer are directly affected, as are the doctors, clinics, and hospital outpatient departments that provide that treatment.
ai_sections.why: Clinics that do not meet accreditation standards would receive a reduced payment. Any savings from this payment change would not be used to reduce Medicare payments in other areas.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare would pay clinics a fixed amount per treatment episode in two installments. A small add-on payment would be available for patients who lack reliable transportation.

title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to treat membership in a health care sharing ministry as a medical expense, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: Starting in 2026, this bill would allow people who pay to belong to a health care sharing ministry to count those payments as a medical expense on their federal taxes. A health care sharing ministry is an organization — typically faith-based — where members voluntarily share each other's medical costs instead of buying traditional health insurance. Under current law, only payments for traditional medical insurance and certain direct medical costs qualify as deductible medical expenses; this bill would add ministry membership fees and the money members contribute toward each other's medical bills to that list. The bill also makes clear that belonging to one of these ministries is not the same as having health insurance for federal tax purposes, which affects how members are treated under other tax rules. This change would primarily affect the individuals and families who choose health care sharing ministry membership as an alternative to conventional insurance.
ai_headline: Health care sharing ministry fees may become tax-deductible
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting 2026, members of health care sharing ministries could count their payments as a medical expense on federal taxes.
ai_sections.what: This bill would change federal tax law so that fees paid to join a health care sharing ministry, and money members contribute toward each other's medical bills, count as deductible medical expenses. The bill also clarifies that belonging to one of these ministries does not count as having health insurance for federal tax purposes.
ai_sections.who: This change would affect individuals and families who belong to a health care sharing ministry instead of buying traditional health insurance. A health care sharing ministry is usually a faith-based organization where members voluntarily share each other's medical costs.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, these payments do not qualify as deductible medical expenses, so members cannot reduce their taxable income using them. This bill would change that starting in 2026, and would also affect how these members are treated under other federal tax rules.

title: To amend the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize a grant program for addressing dental workforce needs.
ai_summary: This bill renews and increases federal funding for a grant program that helps address shortages of dental care workers across the country. Previously, the program was authorized to receive about $13.9 million per year from 2019 through 2023; this bill would increase that amount to $15 million per year and extend the program for five more years, from 2026 through 2030. Any money provided under the program can be spent over time and does not have to be used within a single year. The program is intended to help communities — particularly those with limited access to dental care — by supporting efforts to train, place, and retain dental health professionals where they are needed most.
ai_headline: Federal dental workforce grants extended through 2030
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 44 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2001 extends a dental workforce grant program through 2030 and raises annual funding from $13.9M to $15M.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews a federal grant program that helps address shortages of dental care workers. It extends the program from 2026 through 2030 and raises the yearly funding level. Unused funds can carry over from year to year rather than expiring at the end of each year.
ai_sections.who: The program targets communities that have limited access to dental care. It supports efforts to train, place, and keep dental health professionals in the areas that need them most.
ai_sections.why: Without reauthorization, the program would lose its federal funding after its current term ends. Extending it allows communities with dental worker shortages to continue receiving support.
ai_sections.cost: The program was previously funded at about $13.9 million per year from 2019 through 2023. This bill would increase that amount to $15 million per year for 2026 through 2030.

title: 25th Anniversary of 9/11 Commemorative Coin Act
ai_summary: The federal government would create two special commemorative coins — a $5 gold coin and a $1 silver coin — to mark the 25th anniversary of the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks, with sales limited to the year 2028. Up to 50,000 gold coins and 400,000 silver coins would be made, designed to honor those who died, the first responders who risked their lives, and those who survived, with at least one coin bearing the inscription "Never Forget." Anyone who wants to buy one of these coins would pay the coin's face value plus the cost of production, and an additional surcharge — $35 on each gold coin and $10 on each silver coin — that goes directly to the National September 11 Memorial and Museum in New York City to support its operations. The government is required to ensure the entire coin program costs taxpayers nothing, meaning surcharge money is only sent to the Memorial after the Treasury has fully recovered its production costs.
ai_headline: Commemorative coins planned for 9/11's 25th anniversary
last_action_text: Received in the Senate.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would authorize two 9/11 anniversary coins in 2028, with surcharges going to the 9/11 Memorial and Museum in New York City.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a $5 gold coin and a $1 silver coin to mark the 25th anniversary of the September 11, 2001 attacks. Sales would be limited to 2028, with up to 50,000 gold coins and 400,000 silver coins made. At least one coin would carry the inscription "Never Forget."
ai_sections.who: Anyone who wants to buy one of these coins can do so. The National September 11 Memorial and Museum in New York City would receive the surcharge funds to support its operations.
ai_sections.why: The coins would honor those who died, first responders who risked their lives, and survivors of the attacks. Surcharge money would go to the Memorial only after the Treasury fully recovers its production costs, meaning the program would not cost taxpayers anything.
ai_sections.cost: Buyers would pay the face value of the coin plus production costs, and a surcharge of $35 per gold coin or $10 per silver coin. That surcharge money goes directly to the National September 11 Memorial and Museum.
costChips: Buyers pay face value + production costs

title: Tim’s Act
ai_summary: This bill would permanently raise the base pay of federal wildland firefighters — workers at the U.S. Forest Service, the Department of the Interior, and Tribal fire programs who fight wildfires and support fire management — by adding a percentage boost on top of their regular government pay scale, with the largest increases going to lower-grade workers (up to 42% more) and smaller increases at higher grades. When those firefighters are deployed to a qualifying wildfire or fire-related incident, they would also receive additional daily "incident response" premium pay, capped at $9,000 per calendar year. Beyond pay, the bill would provide paid rest-and-recuperation leave after incidents, annual cost-of-living adjustments, hazardous duty pay, a mental health support program with 7 days of paid mental health leave per year, a public database to track cancer and cardiovascular disease linked to the job, tuition assistance, housing allowances during deployments, and improved retirement and workers' compensation benefits. A new casualty assistance program would also be created to support the families of firefighters who are killed, seriously injured, or become ill in the line of duty.
ai_headline: Federal wildland firefighters would see permanent pay raises
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 743 would permanently raise base pay for federal wildland firefighters, with the biggest increases going to lower-grade workers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would permanently raise base pay for federal wildland firefighters, with lower-grade workers receiving up to 42% more than their current government pay scale. When deployed to a qualifying wildfire, firefighters would also receive extra daily pay, capped at $9,000 per year. The bill also adds mental health leave, housing allowances during deployments, tuition assistance, hazardous duty pay, improved retirement and workers' compensation benefits, a disease tracking database, and a program to support families of firefighters killed, seriously injured, or made ill on the job.
ai_sections.who: This bill covers wildland firefighters employed by the U.S. Forest Service, the Department of the Interior, and Tribal fire programs. Families of firefighters who are killed, seriously injured, or become ill in the line of duty would also be supported through a new casualty assistance program.
ai_sections.why: If passed, federal wildland firefighters would take home higher regular and deployment pay, along with new leave, health, and housing benefits. Families of firefighters seriously harmed or killed in the line of duty would have access to a dedicated assistance program.

title: U.S. Engagement in Sudanese Peace Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the U.S. government to impose economic and travel penalties — called sanctions — on foreign individuals and organizations found to have committed serious human rights abuses (such as genocide, war crimes, or blocking humanitarian aid) during Sudan's ongoing conflict, which began in April 2023. Within 60 days of the bill becoming law, the President would have to identify those responsible and then impose at least six types of punishment on them, which could include freezing their assets in the U.S., banning them from entering the country, cutting off their access to U.S. loans and government contracts, and opposing international loans that would benefit them. The bill would also ban the sale of major military equipment to any country found to be supporting either of Sudan's main warring factions — the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) or the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) — and would require the President to develop and report to Congress on a strategy for protecting civilians, delivering humanitarian aid, and working toward peace. Additionally, it directs U.S. diplomats to use American influence at the United Nations to push for humanitarian access and an end to the fighting, and it calls for active efforts to include Sudanese women and youth in peace negotiations. The bill most directly affects foreign individuals and governments connected to the Sudan conflict, U.S. financial institutions and exporters, and federal agencies involved in foreign policy and national security.
ai_headline: Bill targets Sudan conflict actors with U.S. sanctions
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committees on Financial Services, the Judiciary, and Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1939 would impose sanctions on people who commit human rights abuses in Sudan's ongoing conflict and restrict U.S. arms sales tied to that war.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the U.S. government to impose sanctions — such as freezing assets, banning entry to the U.S., and blocking access to loans and contracts — on foreign individuals and organizations responsible for serious human rights abuses in Sudan's conflict. It would also ban U.S. sales of major military equipment to countries supporting either warring side, and require the President to build and share with Congress a strategy for protecting civilians, delivering aid, and pursuing peace. U.S. diplomats would be directed to push at the United Nations for humanitarian access and a ceasefire, and to actively include Sudanese women and youth in peace talks.
ai_sections.who: This bill most directly affects foreign individuals and governments connected to the Sudan conflict, as well as U.S. financial institutions and exporters. Federal agencies involved in foreign policy and national security would also take on new responsibilities under this law.
ai_sections.why: If passed, foreign actors found responsible for abuses in Sudan could face significant financial and travel restrictions. Countries that continue supplying weapons to either side of the conflict could lose access to U.S. military equipment sales.

title: Peer to Peer Mental Health Support Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a temporary, federally funded pilot program — running until September 30, 2029 — that supports mental health peer support programs for high school students. Through the program, states, local governments, U.S. territories, and tribal nations could apply for competitive grants to train students, adult supervisors, and others to recognize mental health and substance use challenges early, respond to them, and connect affected students to professional help. Any peer support activities funded through the program must be supervised by a school-based mental health professional, and student records collected under the program are protected by existing federal student privacy law. At the end of the program, the federal government must evaluate whether it actually improved student mental health outcomes and report those findings to Congress.
ai_headline: Senate bill funds student mental health peer programs
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: A federal pilot program would fund grants to train high school students and adults to recognize and respond to mental health challenges until 2029.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a temporary federal pilot program running until September 30, 2029. It would fund grants to train high school students, adult supervisors, and others to spot mental health and substance use issues early and connect students to professional help. All peer support activities must be supervised by a school-based mental health professional, and student records are protected under existing federal privacy law.
ai_sections.who: States, local governments, U.S. territories, and tribal nations could apply for competitive grants through this program. High school students and school staff would take part in the peer support training.
ai_sections.why: At the end of the program, the federal government must evaluate whether student mental health outcomes actually improved and report those findings to Congress. That review would inform whether this approach to school mental health support is continued or changed.
ai_sections.cost: The bill establishes a federally funded grant program, meaning federal dollars would pay for the training and support activities. The summary does not specify a total funding amount.

title: Mamas and Babies in Underserved Communities Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would allow the federal government to give grants — that is, funding that doesn't need to be repaid — to public and nonprofit health care providers that serve low-income, minority, or medically underserved communities, for the purpose of expanding and improving maternal health care. The money could be used to provide prenatal care (care during pregnancy), care for newborns in the first year of life, and postpartum care for mothers in the year after giving birth. Organizations that apply for these grants must agree to deliver services in a culturally and linguistically appropriate way and can spend no more than 10% of the grant money on administrative costs. Preference would be given to providers that primarily serve these communities, are led by people with personal ties to those communities, and are physically located within them. Funding would be authorized for the years 2026 through 2030, though the exact dollar amounts would be determined through future appropriations.
ai_headline: Federal grants proposed for underserved maternal care
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1966 would create federal grants for clinics serving low-income and minority communities to expand maternal and newborn care from 2026 to 2030.
ai_sections.what: This bill would let the federal government award grants to qualifying health care providers to expand maternal care. Covered services include prenatal care, newborn care in the first year of life, and postpartum care for mothers in the year after giving birth. Grant recipients must deliver services in culturally and linguistically appropriate ways and may spend no more than 10% of grant funds on administrative costs.
ai_sections.who: Eligible providers are public and nonprofit health care organizations that serve low-income, minority, or medically underserved communities. Priority would go to providers physically located within those communities, primarily serving them, and led by people with personal ties to them.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, providers in underserved communities would not have access to this federal grant funding. If passed, it could increase the availability of maternal and newborn health services in communities that currently have fewer of them.
ai_sections.cost: Funding would be authorized for 2026 through 2030. The exact dollar amounts have not been set and would be decided through future appropriations.

title: Richard L. Trumka Protecting the Right to Organize Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes federal rules about workers' rights to join unions and bargain with employers, affecting employees, employers, and labor unions across the private sector. It makes it harder for employers to classify workers as independent contractors (which would exclude them from union protections), limits employers' ability to permanently replace striking workers or require employees to attend anti-union meetings, and makes it an unfair practice for employers to force workers to sign away their right to join class-action lawsuits. It speeds up the union election process — including allowing voting by phone or internet — and creates a new system where, if a newly formed union and employer cannot reach a first contract on their own within set time limits, a neutral arbitration panel can step in and impose a binding contract for two years. It also increases the financial consequences for employers who break labor law, including higher penalties, back pay without deductions, liquidated damages worth twice the harm done, and the ability for individual workers to sue employers directly in federal court.
ai_headline: Workers gain new union rights under HR 20
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal law would change how workers join unions, how strikes work, and what happens when employers break labor rules.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates federal rules about unions and workplace rights in the private sector. It makes it harder to label workers as independent contractors, limits certain employer actions during organizing drives and strikes, speeds up union elections, and sets up a process where a neutral panel can decide a first contract if workers and employers cannot agree. It also raises the consequences for employers who break labor law.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects private-sector workers, employers, and labor unions across the country. Workers who want to form or join a union, employers who deal with union organizing, and newly formed unions negotiating first contracts are all directly involved.
ai_sections.why: If passed, employers would face stricter rules and higher financial penalties for certain actions during union organizing and contract talks. Workers would gain new options, including suing employers directly in federal court and voting in union elections by phone or internet.
ai_sections.cost: Employers who violate labor law could face higher penalties, back pay, and liquidated damages equal to twice the harm done. The bill does not specify government spending amounts.

title: Roberto Clemente Commemorative Coin Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Treasury to create three types of commemorative coins in 2027 honoring baseball legend and humanitarian Roberto Clemente: a $5 gold coin (up to 50,000 made), a $1 silver coin (up to 400,000 made), and a half-dollar coin (up to 750,000 made), all bearing Clemente's image and inscribed with the year 2027. The coins will be sold to the public — including through prepaid and bulk orders — at a price that covers the cost of making them plus an added surcharge ($35 for the gold coin, $10 for the silver, and $5 for the half-dollar). Those surcharges, after the government fully recoups its production costs, will be paid to the Roberto Clemente Foundation to support its work in education, youth sports, disaster relief, and historic preservation. The program is designed to cost taxpayers nothing, and the Foundation will be subject to financial audits on the money it receives.
ai_headline: U.S. Mint would strike Roberto Clemente coins in 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would order three Roberto Clemente commemorative coins in 2027, with surcharge proceeds going to his foundation.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the U.S. Treasury to mint three commemorative coins in 2027 honoring Roberto Clemente: a $5 gold coin, a $1 silver coin, and a half-dollar coin. Each coin would carry Clemente's image and the year 2027. Coins would be sold to the public, including through prepaid and bulk orders.
ai_sections.who: The U.S. Treasury would produce and sell the coins. The Roberto Clemente Foundation would receive the surcharge money and be subject to financial audits on those funds.
ai_sections.why: A surcharge added to each coin's sale price would flow to the Roberto Clemente Foundation after the government recoups its production costs. That money would support the Foundation's work in education, youth sports, disaster relief, and historic preservation.
ai_sections.cost: The sale price of each coin includes the cost of production plus a surcharge — $35 for the gold coin, $10 for the silver, and $5 for the half-dollar. The program is designed to cost taxpayers nothing.

title: PAST Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill tightens federal rules against "soring" — a practice in which horses' legs or hooves are deliberately injured or irritated to produce an exaggerated high-stepping gait for show competitions. It bans the use of certain devices (such as chains, rollers, and weighted shoes or pads) on Tennessee Walking Horses, Racking Horses, and Spotted Saddle Horses at shows, exhibitions, and sales, and makes it illegal to cause or direct someone else to cause a horse to become sore for competitive purposes. The bill replaces the previous industry-run inspection system with a new government-licensed inspector program, requiring inspectors to be free of conflicts of interest and giving preference to licensed veterinarians, while also setting escalating disqualification periods for horses found to be sore (180 days for a first offense, up to 3 or more years for repeat offenses). Penalties for violations are increased — up to $5,000 in fines and up to 3 years in prison per offense — and people with multiple violations can be permanently banned from participating in horse shows, including financing others' participation. The bill primarily affects horse trainers, owners, show managers, exhibitors, judges, and inspectors involved in the Tennessee Walking Horse, Racking Horse, and Spotted Saddle Horse industries.
ai_headline: Bill targets horse soring with stricter federal rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1684 bans painful training devices on show horses and replaces industry inspectors with government-licensed ones.
ai_sections.what: This bill makes it a federal crime to deliberately hurt horses' legs or hooves to create an exaggerated high-stepping walk for competitions. It bans specific devices like chains, rollers, and weighted shoes on Tennessee Walking Horses, Racking Horses, and Spotted Saddle Horses at shows, sales, and exhibitions. It also replaces the old industry-run inspection system with government-licensed inspectors who must avoid conflicts of interest, with preference given to licensed veterinarians.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects horse trainers, owners, exhibitors, judges, show managers, and inspectors in the Tennessee Walking Horse, Racking Horse, and Spotted Saddle Horse industries.
ai_sections.why: Horses found to be sore would be disqualified for 180 days on a first offense and up to three or more years for repeat offenses. People with multiple violations could be permanently banned from participating in or financing horse shows.
ai_sections.cost: Fines for violations can reach up to $5,000 per offense, and individuals found guilty could face up to 3 years in prison per offense.
costChips: Up to 3 years prison per offense

title: Social Security Expansion Act
ai_summary: This bill makes several changes to the Social Security program aimed at increasing benefits and keeping the program financially solvent.
