It raises monthly payments for current and future retirees and disabled workers by adjusting how benefits are calculated, and it switches the formula used for annual cost-of-living increases to one specifically designed to track the spending patterns of older Americans, which would generally produce larger adjustments. It creates a stronger minimum benefit for people who worked for many years but earned low wages, and it extends benefit eligibility for children of disabled or deceased workers who are full-time college students up to age 22 (currently, children of retirees lose benefits after high school or age 19). To pay for these changes and strengthen the program's finances, the bill applies Social Security payroll taxes to wages between roughly the current taxable maximum and $250,000 — a range that is currently exempt — and it raises the tax on investment income (such as dividends and capital gains) from 3.8% to 16.2%, directing a portion of that revenue into Social Security; these tax changes would primarily affect higher-income workers, business owners, and investors. Finally, the bill merges the two existing Social Security trust funds (one for retirees and survivors, one for disability) into a single combined fund.
ai_headline: Social Security benefits would rise under new House bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1700 raises Social Security payments for retirees and disabled workers and applies new taxes on higher earners to fund the changes.
ai_sections.what: This bill would increase monthly Social Security payments for current and future retirees and disabled workers by changing how benefits are calculated. It would also update the formula used for annual cost-of-living increases to one that tracks how older Americans actually spend money, which would generally produce larger yearly raises. It creates a stronger minimum benefit for long-term low-wage workers, extends benefits for college students up to age 22 who have a disabled or deceased parent, and merges the two existing Social Security trust funds into one.
ai_sections.who: People who receive or will receive Social Security retirement or disability benefits would see higher payments. Higher-income workers, business owners, and investors would face new or increased taxes under this bill.
ai_sections.why: Raising benefits would increase the amount of money Social Security pays out each year. The tax changes are designed to bring in more money to keep the program financially stable over time.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would apply Social Security payroll taxes to wages in a range currently exempt from those taxes. It would also raise the tax on investment income such as dividends and capital gains from 3.8% to 16.2%, with a portion of that revenue directed into Social Security.
costChips: Expands SS payroll tax to exempt wages

title: 761st Tank Battalion Congressional Gold Medal Act
ai_summary: This bill would award a Congressional Gold Medal — Congress's highest civilian honor — to the 761st Tank Battalion, a predominantly Black American armored unit that fought in Europe during World War II. The medal would be presented collectively to the battalion in recognition of their combat service, which included 183 days of continuous fighting, key roles in the Battle of the Bulge, and being among the first American units to break through Germany's Siegfried Line. After the ceremony, the gold medal would be permanently housed at the National Museum of African American History and Culture, with Congress expressing its wish that the museum also make the medal available for display at locations connected to the battalion's history. The U.S. Treasury would produce the gold medal as well as bronze copies available for public purchase, with the cost covered by and any sales proceeds returned to the U.S. Mint's existing fund.
ai_headline: Bill would honor 761st Tank Battalion with Gold Medal
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on House Administration, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would award its highest civilian honor to the 761st Tank Battalion, a Black American unit that fought in World War II Europe.
ai_sections.what: This bill would give a Congressional Gold Medal to the 761st Tank Battalion for their service in World War II. The gold medal would be kept at the National Museum of African American History and Culture. Bronze copies of the medal would also be made available for the public to buy.
ai_sections.who: The 761st Tank Battalion was a mostly Black American armored unit that fought in Europe during World War II. They fought for 183 days straight, took part in the Battle of the Bulge, and were among the first American units to break through Germany's Siegfried Line.
ai_sections.why: Passing this bill would make the 761st Tank Battalion one of the groups officially recognized by Congress with its highest civilian honor. It would also create a permanent public home for that recognition at a national museum.
ai_sections.cost: The U.S. Treasury would produce the gold medal and bronze copies. Costs would be covered by the U.S. Mint's existing fund, and any money made from sales of bronze copies would go back into that same fund.

Bronze sales return to same fund

title: Ag Disputes Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the President to create a new government team — called the Agricultural Trade Enforcement Task Force — made up of staff from the Department of Agriculture and the U.S. Trade Representative's office, whose job is to find and challenge unfair trade barriers that block U.S. farm products from being sold in other countries. The task force must report to Congress every three months on which barriers it has identified and what steps are being taken to fight them through international trade agreements. The bill also specifically calls out India, requiring the task force to put together a formal plan — in its very first report — to file a dispute with the World Trade Organization over India's agricultural price support programs, which the bill says far exceed the limits India agreed to under WTO rules and have harmed U.S. farmers in markets like rice, wheat, cotton, and pulses. American farmers, ranchers, and agricultural businesses are the primary people this bill is meant to help, while U.S. trade officials are the ones who would take on new responsibilities under it.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets foreign farm trade barriers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 743 creates a federal task force to identify and challenge foreign trade barriers blocking U.S. farm exports, with India named first.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the President to form the Agricultural Trade Enforcement Task Force, staffed by the Department of Agriculture and the U.S. Trade Representative's office. Their job is to find and challenge foreign trade barriers that block U.S. farm products from being sold abroad. The task force must report to Congress every three months, and its first report must include a formal plan to file a WTO dispute against India over agricultural price support programs that the bill says exceed WTO limits.
ai_sections.who: U.S. trade officials at the Department of Agriculture and the U.S. Trade Representative's office would carry out the new responsibilities. American farmers, ranchers, and agricultural businesses are the people this bill is designed to affect.
ai_sections.why: Foreign trade barriers can prevent U.S. farm products from reaching international markets. The bill states that India's agricultural price support programs have harmed U.S. farmers in markets such as rice, wheat, cotton, and pulses.

title: Ethan's Law
ai_summary: This bill would make it a federal offense for gun owners to store a firearm in their home in a way that allows a minor (anyone under 18) or a person legally prohibited from owning a gun to easily access it. To comply, an owner must either secure the firearm with a lock or safe, keep it in a location a reasonable person would consider secure, or keep it on their person or close enough to grab at any moment. A violation carries a $500 fine, but if an unsecured gun is obtained by a minor or prohibited person and someone is injured or killed as a result, the owner could face up to 5 years in prison, additional fines, and loss of the firearm. The bill also creates a grant program through which states and tribal governments that pass similar laws of their own can receive federal funding to help their law enforcement agencies and courts carry out those laws.
ai_headline: Bill targets unsafe home gun storage practices
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1564 requires gun owners to lock up firearms at home so minors and prohibited people can't easily reach them.
ai_sections.what: This bill makes it a federal crime to store a gun at home where a minor or a person legally banned from owning guns can easily get to it. To follow the law, an owner must use a lock or safe, keep the gun somewhere a reasonable person would call secure, or keep it on their person or within immediate reach.
ai_sections.who: This law applies to gun owners who store firearms in their home. It affects any household where a minor under 18 or a person prohibited from owning a gun could access those firearms.
ai_sections.why: If an owner does not follow the storage rules, they can be fined. If an unsecured gun is then obtained by a minor or prohibited person and someone is hurt or killed, the owner can face prison time, more fines, and loss of the firearm.
ai_sections.cost: A basic violation carries a $500 fine. If harm results from an unsecured gun, the owner may face additional fines, up to 5 years in prison, and loss of the firearm. The bill also creates a grant program that sends federal funding to states and tribal governments that pass similar storage laws of their own.
costChips: Grants for states with storage laws

title: Access Technology Affordability Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill creates a refundable tax credit to help blind individuals and their families cover the cost of technology that converts visual information into formats they can use, such as screen readers, braille displays, or similar hardware and software. The credit can be claimed by a blind person directly, or by a sighted taxpayer who purchases this technology for a blind spouse or dependent. The maximum credit is $2,000 per blind person over any three-year period, with that cap adjusted for inflation starting in 2027, and the credit cannot be combined with any other tax deduction or credit for the same expense. Because the credit is refundable, people can receive it even if they owe little or no federal income tax. The credit would be available for purchases made between January 1, 2026 and December 31, 2030.
ai_headline: Bill would offer blind Americans a $2,000 tech tax credit
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1529 offers a refundable tax credit up to $2,000 every 3 years for assistive technology for blind individuals.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a refundable tax credit for buying technology that helps blind people access visual information, such as screen readers or braille displays. The credit covers purchases made between January 1, 2026 and December 31, 2030. It cannot be combined with any other tax deduction or credit for the same expense.
ai_sections.who: A blind person can claim the credit directly, or a sighted taxpayer can claim it when buying this technology for a blind spouse or dependent. Because the credit is refundable, it is available even to people who owe little or no federal income tax.
ai_sections.why: Without this credit, the cost of assistive technology falls entirely on blind individuals and their families. This credit reduces how much of that cost they must pay out of pocket.
ai_sections.cost: The maximum credit is $2,000 per blind person over any three-year period. Starting in 2027, that cap would be adjusted for inflation.

title: LEOSA Reform Act
ai_summary: This bill expands the places where currently employed and retired law enforcement officers are legally allowed to carry concealed firearms. It closes a gap between two existing laws so that officers permitted to carry concealed weapons under federal law are also clearly exempt from the federal ban on guns near school zones. It removes restrictions that previously prevented those officers from carrying on publicly accessible property and on transportation infrastructure like trains, buses, and ferries — meaning officers could carry in those locations even if a state or local law otherwise prohibited it. It also broadens the options retired officers can use to prove they have met required firearms training standards, allowing them to qualify through their former agency, their home state, any local law enforcement agency in their state, or any certified firearms instructor in their state. Finally, it allows qualified current and retired officers to carry firearms inside lower-security federal buildings that are open to the general public, and it clarifies that "firearm" in this context includes ammunition magazines.
ai_headline: Bill expands concealed carry rights for off-duty officers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill lets active and retired law enforcement officers carry concealed firearms in more places, including near schools and on public transit.
ai_sections.what: This bill expands where active and retired law enforcement officers can legally carry concealed firearms. It removes restrictions that blocked them from carrying on public transit like trains, buses, and ferries, and on publicly accessible property. It also makes clear that these officers are exempt from the federal rule banning guns near school zones, and it counts ammunition magazines as firearms under this law.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to currently employed and retired law enforcement officers who are already permitted to carry concealed firearms under federal law. Retired officers also get more ways to prove they have met required firearms training standards, including through a certified instructor or any local law enforcement agency in their home state.
ai_sections.why: Officers who were previously restricted from carrying in certain public spaces or on transit would now be allowed to do so even if state or local laws said otherwise. Retired officers would have more flexibility in how they stay qualified.

title: Cameron’s Law
ai_summary: This bill would increase a tax break that pharmaceutical companies can claim when developing drugs for rare diseases — often called "orphan drugs" because they target conditions affecting very small numbers of patients. Currently, companies can subtract 25 cents from their tax bill for every dollar they spend on qualifying research and testing costs for these rare-disease drugs; this bill would raise that amount back to 50 cents on the dollar. The change would apply starting in the tax year after the bill becomes law. The companies most directly affected are those investing in treatments for rare medical conditions, and the broader intended benefit is to make it more financially attractive to develop drugs that might otherwise be unprofitable due to small patient populations.
ai_headline: Bill would double orphan drug research tax credit
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1414 would double the tax credit for companies researching rare-disease drugs, from 25 cents to 50 cents per dollar spent.
ai_sections.what: This bill would raise the tax credit that pharmaceutical companies can claim for researching rare-disease drugs. The credit would go from 25 cents to 50 cents for every dollar spent on qualifying research and testing. The change would take effect in the first tax year after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: Pharmaceutical companies that develop drugs for rare diseases would be directly affected by this change.
ai_sections.why: A higher tax credit makes it less costly for companies to research rare-disease drugs, which tend to reach very small numbers of patients. Developing these drugs can otherwise be unprofitable because so few people need them.

title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to increase the employer tax credit for paid family and medical leave.
ai_summary: This bill changes a tax credit that employers can currently claim when they voluntarily offer paid family and medical leave to their workers. Right now, eligible employers can receive a tax credit starting at 12.5% of the wages they pay to employees on qualifying leave, with that percentage rising the more generous the pay — this bill would double those rates, starting the credit at 25% and allowing it to climb higher as employers pay a greater share of an employee's normal wages. The bill also makes this employer tax credit permanent, whereas under current law it was set to expire. These changes would take effect starting in the 2026 tax year and would affect businesses of any size that choose to provide paid family and medical leave to their employees.
ai_headline: Bill would double paid family leave tax credit for employers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1424 doubles the tax credit rate for employers who offer paid family and medical leave and makes that credit permanent starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: This bill doubles the tax credit employers can claim for voluntarily offering paid family and medical leave. The starting credit rate would rise from 12.5% to 25% of wages paid during qualifying leave, and the rate can climb higher when employers pay a greater share of an employee's normal wages. The credit would also be made permanent instead of expiring, beginning with the 2026 tax year.
ai_sections.who: Any business of any size that chooses to offer paid family and medical leave to its employees could be affected.
ai_sections.why: Employers who already offer paid family and medical leave would receive a larger tax credit than they do today. Employers who did not previously offer it may find the higher credit changes their decision about whether to do so.

title: No IRIS Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would permanently bar the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) from using a specific internal database — called the Integrated Risk Information System, or IRIS — when writing new regulations, enforcing existing rules, issuing permits, or creating air quality maps and screening tools. IRIS is a longstanding EPA program that compiles scientific assessments of the health risks posed by chemicals and other substances. Under this bill, even if IRIS contains health risk information about a particular chemical, the EPA could not rely on that information to justify or shape any regulatory or enforcement decision. The bill would primarily affect the EPA itself and its ability to use one of its own scientific tools, but it could also have downstream effects on industries subject to EPA oversight, as well as communities and individuals whose health protections are partly based on IRIS-informed regulations.
ai_headline: Bill would bar EPA from using IRIS chemical risk data
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1415 would permanently ban the EPA from using its IRIS chemical health risk database in any regulation, permit, or enforcement action.
ai_sections.what: This bill would permanently stop the EPA from using its Integrated Risk Information System, known as IRIS, in any regulatory or enforcement work. That includes writing new rules, issuing permits, enforcing existing rules, and creating air quality maps and screening tools.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects the EPA and its ability to use one of its own scientific databases. It could also affect industries the EPA oversees, as well as communities whose health protections are based on rules that relied on IRIS data.
ai_sections.why: Without access to IRIS, the EPA could not use that database's chemical health risk information to justify or shape its decisions. Regulations and protections that were informed by IRIS data could be harder to maintain or update going forward.

title: EAGLES Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill officially continues and strengthens a program within the U.S. Secret Service called the National Threat Assessment Center, which studies and helps prevent targeted violence — such as mass shootings — at schools, workplaces, houses of worship, and other public places. The Center would be required to build a national school safety program that conducts research on school violence, develops training courses, and shares best practices with schools, local law enforcement, mental health professionals, and other community organizations across every state. The Secret Service would be allowed to hire additional staff with expertise in child psychology and school threat assessment, and would be required to report back to Congress within two years on how the program is working, including how many people have been trained and whether that training is actually effective. Up to $10 million per year would be authorized to fund the program from 2026 through 2030, and none of that money may be used to train anyone in the use of a firearm. The bill primarily affects K–12 schools, school districts, local law enforcement agencies, and mental health professionals who would receive training and resources through this program.
ai_headline: Secret Service threat assessment center gets school safety mandate
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: The EAGLES Act funds a Secret Service school safety program that trains schools, law enforcement, and mental health workers to prevent targeted violence.
ai_sections.what: This bill continues and strengthens a Secret Service program called the National Threat Assessment Center, which studies and helps prevent targeted violence in schools and other public places. The Center would build a national school safety program that conducts research, develops training courses, and shares best practices. None of the funding may be used to train anyone in the use of a firearm.
ai_sections.who: The program primarily serves K–12 schools, school districts, local law enforcement agencies, and mental health professionals. The Secret Service would also be allowed to hire additional staff with expertise in child psychology and school threat assessment.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, the National Threat Assessment Center would not have a formal mandate or dedicated funding to run a national school safety program. Schools, law enforcement, and mental health professionals in every state would have less access to research, training, and shared best practices on preventing targeted violence.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $10 million per year is authorized to fund the program from 2026 through 2030. The Secret Service must report to Congress within two years on how the program is working, including how many people have been trained and whether that training is effective.

title: Blind Americans Return to Work Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would set up a 10-year test program, running for up to 20 years total, that changes how Social Security disability benefits work for blind people who go back to work. Under current law, earning too much money can cause a blind person to lose their disability benefits entirely, but this program would instead gradually reduce their benefit by $1 for every $2 they earn above a certain threshold — and it would also subtract any work-related expenses from their earnings before calculating that reduction. Blind people participating in the program could keep receiving some benefit no matter how much they earn, and their benefits would not be cut off simply because they return to work. After the first 10 years, participants would have the option to leave the program if they choose. The goal is to test whether giving blind Social Security disability recipients a smoother financial transition back to employment — rather than an abrupt loss of benefits — encourages more of them to work.
ai_headline: Pilot program would ease benefit cuts for working blind Americans
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1175 would let blind Social Security disability recipients return to work without losing all their benefits at once.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a test program, lasting up to 20 years, that gradually reduces Social Security disability benefits for blind people who return to work instead of cutting them off entirely. For every two dollars earned above a set threshold, benefits would go down by one dollar, and work-related expenses would be subtracted from earnings before that calculation is made.
ai_sections.who: Blind people who receive Social Security disability benefits and return to work could join this program. After the first 10 years, participants would have the option to leave the program if they choose.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, earning too much can cause a blind person to lose their disability benefits entirely. This program tests whether a more gradual reduction encourages more blind people to return to work.

title: Payment Choice Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require physical retail stores and businesses that accept in-person payments to take cash as a valid form of payment for transactions up to $500, and would also ban those businesses from charging cash-paying customers higher prices than customers who pay by card or other methods. Businesses are given a pass if their payment system temporarily breaks down or they temporarily run out of change, and they can also avoid the cash requirement if they provide a free on-site machine that converts cash into a prepaid card — as long as that card has no fees, no minimum deposit above one dollar, no expiration, and collects no personal information from the customer. For the first five years after the law takes effect, businesses would not be required to accept $50 bills or larger, and after that period the Treasury Department would set rules on which large bill denominations businesses must accept, while always being required to accept $1, $5, $10, and $20 bills. Customers who are refused cash can send the business a formal notice, and if the business doesn't fix the problem within 45 days, the customer can sue and may receive at least $250 in damages plus civil penalties up to $500 for a first violation and up to $1,500 for repeat violations; states and localities with stronger cash-acceptance protections can keep their own laws in place.
ai_headline: Retail stores would be required to accept cash payments
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1138 would require most physical stores to accept cash for purchases up to $500 and ban charging cash customers higher prices.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require physical retail stores to accept cash for in-person purchases up to $500. Stores cannot charge cash-paying customers more than customers who pay by card. Stores that offer a free on-site machine converting cash to a no-fee, no-expiration prepaid card that collects no personal information may be exempt from the cash requirement.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to physical retail businesses that accept in-person payments. Customers who are turned away for paying with cash would have the right to send a formal notice and, if unresolved within 45 days, to sue the business.
ai_sections.why: Without this law, some physical stores can legally refuse cash or charge cash customers more, which can affect people who do not use cards or bank accounts. States and localities with stronger cash-acceptance rules would keep their own laws in place.
ai_sections.cost: Customers who successfully sue a business that refuses cash may receive at least $250 in damages. Businesses that violate the law may face civil penalties up to $500 for a first violation and up to $1,500 for repeat violations.

title: Protect Our Letter Carriers Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill takes several steps to make mail delivery safer for U.S. Postal Service workers and to make crimes against them more serious. It would authorize up to $1.4 billion per year from 2026 through 2030 for the Postal Service to install more secure mail collection boxes and replace old-style master keys (called "arrow keys") used to access mailboxes with newer electronic versions — both measures aimed at reducing mail theft and robberies. It requires the Justice Department to designate a specific federal prosecutor in every court district whose main job is to handle crimes involving mail theft, postal robberies, and attacks on mail carriers or others handling the mail. Finally, it directs the U.S. Sentencing Commission to update its guidelines so that assaulting or robbing a postal worker — including dangerous actions taken while fleeing the scene — would be punished at the same level as assaulting a law enforcement officer, meaning tougher potential sentences. The people most directly affected are mail carriers and other postal employees, as well as anyone who commits crimes against them.
ai_headline: Bill targets mail theft and attacks on postal workers
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1065 increases security for mail carriers by funding safer equipment, requiring dedicated prosecutors, and raising penalties for attacks on postal workers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would fund more secure mail collection boxes and replace old master keys with electronic ones to reduce mail theft and robberies. It would require the Justice Department to assign a dedicated federal prosecutor in every court district to handle crimes involving mail theft or attacks on postal workers. It would also direct the U.S. Sentencing Commission to update its guidelines so that assaulting or robbing a postal worker carries the same penalty level as assaulting a law enforcement officer.
ai_sections.who: Mail carriers and other U.S. Postal Service employees are the people most directly affected. People who commit crimes against postal workers would also face changes under this bill.
ai_sections.why: If passed, crimes against mail carriers could result in tougher sentences than they do under current guidelines. Every federal court district would also have a prosecutor whose main focus is mail-related crimes.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize up to $1.4 billion per year from 2026 through 2030 for the Postal Service to pay for more secure mail collection boxes and new electronic keys.
costChips: Funds secure mail boxes & electronic keys

title: Incentivizing Readiness and Environmental Protection Integration Sales Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes the tax code so that landowners who sell certain property rights to qualified organizations under the military's Readiness and Environmental Protection Integration (REPI) program do not have to pay federal income tax on any profit they make from that sale. The REPI program allows the Department of Defense to work with landowners near military bases to protect land from development that could interfere with military training and operations. The tax break applies to sales of a landowner's full ownership interest, a remainder interest (the right to property after someone else's use ends), or a permanent restriction on how the land can be used — and it still applies even if the seller keeps the right to any minerals beneath the land, as long as those minerals aren't extracted through surface mining. However, if the seller is a business entity like a partnership or corporation (rather than an individual), the tax break is not available if that entity bought the property less than three years before selling it to the REPI program — though an exception exists for family-owned partnerships and similar family-controlled businesses.
ai_headline: Bill would exempt REPI land sales from federal income tax
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Landowners who sell certain property rights near military bases under the REPI program would owe no federal income tax on the profit.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes federal income tax on profits when landowners sell certain property rights through the military's REPI program. The tax break covers full ownership, remainder interests, and permanent land-use restrictions. It still applies if the seller keeps mineral rights, as long as those minerals are not extracted through surface mining.
ai_sections.who: Individual landowners near military bases who sell to the REPI program would qualify. Business entities like partnerships or corporations generally must have owned the property for at least three years before the sale to qualify, with an exception for family-owned businesses.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, sellers in REPI transactions would owe federal income tax on any profit from the sale. The bill changes that outcome specifically for qualifying REPI sales.

title: Protect Our Letter Carriers Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill focuses on making the U.S. Postal Service and its workers safer in three main ways. First, it would authorize up to $1.4 billion per year from 2025 through 2029 for the Postal Service to install more secure mail collection boxes and replace the traditional physical "arrow keys" — which mail carriers use to access cluster mailboxes and other mail infrastructure — with electronic versions that are harder to steal or copy. Second, it requires the Justice Department to designate a specific federal prosecutor in every court district whose primary job is to investigate and prosecute crimes against the Postal Service, including mail theft and attacks on postal workers. Third, it directs the U.S. Sentencing Commission to update federal sentencing guidelines so that assaulting or robbing a postal employee — including during an escape attempt that creates serious risk of injury — would be treated as seriously as assaulting a law enforcement officer, which typically carries harsher penalties.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets mail theft and postal worker safety
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 463 would fund safer mailboxes, assign prosecutors to postal crimes, and raise penalties for attacking mail carriers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would do three things: fund more secure mailboxes and electronic keys for mail carriers, require the Justice Department to assign a dedicated federal prosecutor to postal crimes in every court district, and direct the U.S. Sentencing Commission to update guidelines so attacking a postal worker carries penalties similar to attacking a law enforcement officer.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects U.S. Postal Service workers, who would receive new security tools and stronger legal protections. It also involves the Justice Department and the U.S. Sentencing Commission, who would carry out the law's requirements.
ai_sections.why: Mail theft and attacks on postal workers are the problems this bill responds to. Changing sentencing guidelines and adding dedicated prosecutors would alter how these crimes are handled in federal court.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize up to 1.4 billion dollars per year from 2025 through 2029 to pay for secure mailboxes and electronic keys.
costChips: Funds secure mailboxes and electronic keys

title: Dr. Lorna Breen Health Care Provider Protection Reauthorization Act
ai_summary: This bill extends and updates a federal program originally created to support the mental health and well-being of doctors, nurses, and other health care workers. It continues funding for education and awareness efforts that encourage health care professionals to seek mental health and substance use disorder treatment, extending that funding through 2030. It also broadens the types of organizations that can receive grants under the program to now include groups focused on reducing the administrative paperwork and burdens that contribute to burnout among health care workers. A reporting requirement is also updated so that progress is reported annually rather than just once. The people most directly affected are health care workers across the country, as well as hospitals, clinics, and other organizations that may apply for grants to run mental health support programs.
ai_headline: Bill extends health worker mental health grants to 2030
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would extend a mental health support program for health care workers through 2030 and expand who can apply for grants.
ai_sections.what: This bill continues a federal program that funds mental health and substance use support for health care workers. It extends funding through 2030, allows more types of organizations to apply for grants, and requires yearly progress reports instead of just one.
ai_sections.who: Health care workers such as doctors and nurses are the main people this program is designed to reach. Hospitals, clinics, and organizations focused on reducing paperwork burdens on health care workers could also apply for grants.
ai_sections.why: Without reauthorization, the existing program and its funding would expire. Expanding eligible grant recipients means organizations working on administrative burdens, not just mental health education, could now receive federal support.

title: Protecting Life on College Campus Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would cut off all federal funding to colleges and universities that allow on-campus health clinics to provide abortions or abortion-related drugs to their students or clinic employees. The funding ban would apply whether the money flows directly to the school or indirectly through contracts. To keep receiving federal money, any college that has an on-campus health clinic would be required to submit a yearly written certification to the federal government confirming that the clinic does not offer those services. The bill affects colleges and universities that receive federal funding — which includes most schools, since federal money covers things like student financial aid, research grants, and other programs — as well as their students who might otherwise seek those services through campus health clinics. Hospitals are not covered by this bill, and the definition of "abortion drug" includes exceptions for treatments for ectopic pregnancies and for removing a fetus that has already died.
ai_headline: Bill would strip federal funds from campuses offering abortion care
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Colleges that allow on-campus clinics to provide abortions or abortion drugs would lose all federal funding under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill would cut off all federal funding to colleges and universities whose on-campus health clinics offer abortions or abortion-related drugs. Schools with on-campus clinics would need to send the federal government a yearly written confirmation that they do not offer those services. Hospitals are not affected, and the abortion drug definition does not include treatments for ectopic pregnancies or for removing a fetus that has already died.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects colleges and universities that receive federal funding, which includes most schools. Students who might use campus health clinics for these services would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Schools that do not submit the yearly certification or that provide the covered services would lose federal money, including funds tied to student financial aid, research grants, and other programs. Students at those schools could lose access to those services on campus.
ai_sections.cost: Federal funding would be cut off to any college that does not comply, covering both direct payments to schools and money that flows through contracts.
costChips: Federal funding cut for noncompliant colleges

title: BEACH Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill updates a long-standing federal program that gives money to states and local governments to test the water quality at public beaches and warn people when it is unsafe to swim. It expands the program's coverage to include shallow upstream waters near those beaches — not just the beach water itself — and it allows grant money to be used to track down the specific sources of contamination, such as a particular pipe or runoff point, rather than only detecting that contamination exists. The bill also renews the program's funding authorization of up to $30 million per year, which had expired after 2005, for the years 2025 through 2029. Additionally, it requires the Environmental Protection Agency to make sure its guidance to states and local governments keeps up with newer, more advanced water-testing technologies. The people most directly affected are beachgoers at coastal and public recreational waters, as well as the state and local agencies responsible for monitoring beach safety.
ai_headline: EPA beach monitoring program renewed and expanded
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: The BEACH Act renews federal grants for beach water testing through 2029 and expands what the money can be used for.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews a federal program that funds states and local governments to test water quality at public beaches and warn people when swimming is unsafe. It expands coverage to include shallow upstream waters near beaches, not just the beach itself. It also lets grant money be used to find the specific sources of contamination, like a pipe or runoff point, rather than only confirming that contamination exists.
ai_sections.who: Beachgoers at coastal and public recreational waters are most directly affected. State and local agencies responsible for monitoring beach safety are also affected, as they receive and manage the grant funding.
ai_sections.why: Without renewal, the program's funding authorization would remain expired, as it had lapsed after 2005. Expanding the scope means agencies can now look further upstream and trace contamination to its source rather than stopping at detection.
ai_sections.cost: The bill renews funding authorization of up to $30 million per year for 2025 through 2029.

title: Stopping Adversarial Tariff Evasion Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the U.S. government determines where an imported product "comes from" when enforcing certain trade penalties, such as tariffs. Specifically, if a product is made, manufactured, or finally assembled by a company that is owned, headquartered, or organized in one of six named countries — China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, or (while Nicolás Maduro is president) Venezuela — or by any company where one of those countries' governments or entities holds at least 25% ownership, that product is treated as if it came directly from one of those countries, even if it was physically produced elsewhere. This closes a potential loophole where goods tied to those countries could avoid trade penalties simply by being routed through or assembled in a third country. The bill affects importers, manufacturers, and businesses that bring foreign-made goods into the U.S., particularly those with any ownership or business ties to the named countries, as their products could become subject to the same trade penalties that apply to goods made directly in those countries.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets country-of-origin loopholes in trade law
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Products tied to China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, or Venezuela can face U.S. trade penalties even if made in another country.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how the U.S. government decides where an imported product comes from for trade penalty purposes. If a product is made or assembled by a company owned, based, or organized in China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, or Venezuela, it is treated as coming from one of those countries, even if it was physically made somewhere else. The same rule applies if a government or entity from one of those countries owns at least 25% of the company involved.
ai_sections.who: This affects importers, manufacturers, and businesses that bring foreign-made goods into the U.S. It especially affects those with ownership or business ties to any of the six named countries.
ai_sections.why: Products connected to those countries could become subject to the same trade penalties that already apply to goods made directly in those countries. This closes a path that allowed goods to avoid those penalties by being routed through or assembled in a third country.

title: United States-Cuba Trade Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would end the long-standing U.S. trade embargo against Cuba by repealing or removing a series of laws and regulations that have restricted trade, travel, and financial dealings between the two countries for decades. If passed, Americans would be free to travel to Cuba and conduct normal banking transactions related to that travel, U.S. companies could provide telecommunications services to Cuba, and Cuban goods would be treated the same as goods from most other countries when it comes to import tariffs. People in the U.S. could also send unlimited amounts of money to Cuba without government-imposed caps, though anti-money-laundering laws would still apply. The bill affects a wide range of people, including American businesses looking to trade with or invest in Cuba, Cuban Americans who send money to family on the island, and travelers — while also directing the President to pursue negotiations with Cuba over property claims by American citizens and human rights protections.
ai_headline: Senate bill would lift the U.S. trade embargo on Cuba
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would end the U.S. trade embargo against Cuba, letting Americans travel, trade, and send money there freely.
ai_sections.what: This bill would repeal laws that have blocked trade, travel, and financial dealings between the U.S. and Cuba for decades. Americans could travel to Cuba, use normal banking for that travel, and send unlimited amounts of money to the island, though anti-money-laundering rules would still apply. Cuban goods would be taxed at the same rates as goods from most other countries, and U.S. companies could offer telecommunications services in Cuba.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects American businesses interested in trading with or investing in Cuba, Cuban Americans who send money to family on the island, and travelers. It also directs the President to negotiate with Cuba on property claims by American citizens and on human rights protections.
ai_sections.why: If passed, trade and travel between the two countries could resume in ways that have not been legally possible for decades. The President would also be required to pursue formal talks with Cuba on unresolved property and human rights issues.

title: REMAIN in Mexico Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require the Secretary of Homeland Security to keep running a program, commonly known as "Remain in Mexico," that was originally launched in January 2019. Under that program, people who come to the U.S. southern border seeking asylum or other protection — and who are not U.S. citizens or permanent residents — are sent back to Mexico to wait while their immigration cases are processed in U.S. courts, rather than being allowed to stay inside the United States during that process. The bill states this must happen regardless of any other laws that might otherwise apply, meaning the Secretary could not stop the program based on other legal authority. This would primarily affect migrants and asylum seekers arriving at the U.S.-Mexico border, as well as the Department of Homeland Security, which would be legally required to continue operating the program.
ai_headline: Bill would mandate continuation of Remain in Mexico
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 273 would require the U.S. government to keep sending asylum seekers back to Mexico to wait while their cases are decided.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make the "Remain in Mexico" program permanent and required by law. Under the program, people arriving at the southern border to seek asylum or protection are returned to Mexico while U.S. courts process their cases. The Secretary of Homeland Security could not end the program, even if other laws might otherwise allow it.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects migrants and asylum seekers arriving at the U.S.-Mexico border who are not U.S. citizens or permanent residents. It also affects the Department of Homeland Security, which would be legally required to keep the program running.
ai_sections.why: Asylum seekers would wait in Mexico rather than inside the United States while their immigration cases move through U.S. courts. The Department of Homeland Security would lose the authority to stop or pause the program on its own.

title: Sustainable Budget Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would create an 18-member bipartisan panel called the National Commission on Fiscal Responsibility and Reform, made up of presidential appointees and current members of both the Senate and House from both major parties, tasked with developing a plan to get the federal government's spending and debt under control. Within one year, the commission must produce a report with specific policy recommendations aimed at balancing the budget (not counting interest payments on the national debt) within 10 years and making the government's long-term finances more sustainable — including addressing the growth of programs like Social Security and Medicare and the gap between what the government spends and what it collects in taxes. For a report to be officially issued, at least 12 of the 18 members must approve it, including at least 4 members from each major party, meaning meaningful bipartisan agreement is required. Once the commission submits its report, the President must send Congress a proposed law to carry out those recommendations, and Congress would then consider that proposal under a streamlined process designed to force a faster-than-usual vote without the possibility of amendments. This process would affect all Americans, since the commission's recommendations could ultimately change federal taxes, spending programs, and government services.
ai_headline: Bill would form bipartisan panel to rein in federal debt
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Budget, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 222 would create an 18-member bipartisan commission to recommend a federal budget plan that Congress must vote on quickly.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a National Commission on Fiscal Responsibility and Reform with 18 members. The commission would have one year to write a report recommending how to balance the federal budget, not counting interest payments, within 10 years. At least 12 of the 18 members, including at least 4 from each major party, must agree before a report can be officially released.
ai_sections.who: The commission would include presidential appointees and current members of both the Senate and House from both major parties. Once the commission delivers its report, the President must propose a law to carry it out, and Congress must vote on it quickly without the option to make changes.
ai_sections.why: The commission would be directed to look at the gap between what the government spends and what it collects, including the growth of programs like Social Security and Medicare. Its recommendations could lead to changes in federal taxes, spending programs, and government services that affect all Americans.

title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to limit the number of terms that a Member of Congress may serve.
ai_summary: This proposal would change the U.S. Constitution to limit how long a person can serve in Congress. Under the change, a member of the House of Representatives could serve a maximum of three two-year terms, and a U.S. Senator could serve a maximum of two six-year terms. If someone fills a vacant seat for more than one year in the House, or more than three years in the Senate, that partial service counts as a full term. Importantly, any time already served in Congress before this amendment is ratified would not count toward those limits, meaning current members would start with a clean slate. This would affect every current and future member of Congress, but it would only take effect if two-thirds of both the House and Senate approve it and then three-fourths of all state legislatures ratify it within seven years.
ai_headline: Proposed amendment would cap congressional terms
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This proposed constitutional amendment would limit House members to 3 terms and Senators to 2 terms, starting fresh for current members.
ai_sections.what: This is a proposed amendment to the U.S. Constitution that would set term limits for Congress. House members could serve at most three two-year terms, and Senators could serve at most two six-year terms. Partial terms count as full terms if a House member fills a vacancy for more than one year, or a Senator for more than three years.
ai_sections.who: This would affect every current and future member of the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate. Current members would start with a clean slate, meaning their time already served would not count toward the new limits.
ai_sections.why: If passed, members of Congress would be unable to run again once they reach their term limit. The change would only take effect if two-thirds of both chambers approve it and three-fourths of state legislatures ratify it within seven years.

title: Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a 15-member federal commission to research the history of slavery in America and its long-term effects on African Americans, and then recommend what, if anything, the U.S. government should do about it. The commission would examine the history of slavery itself, government policies that discriminated against Black Americans after slavery ended (such as redlining and unequal access to programs like Social Security and the GI Bill), and how those historical harms may still affect African Americans today. Based on that research, the commission would make recommendations to Congress that could include a formal government apology, financial compensation, or other remedies — but the commission itself would not have the power to provide any compensation or enact any policy; it could only study the issues and advise Congress. The commission would have up to 18 months to complete its work, would then shut down 90 days after delivering its report, and up to $20 million is authorized to fund its operations.
ai_headline: Bill would form slavery reparations study commission
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

ai_sections.tldr: HR 40 would create a federal commission to study the history of slavery and recommend—but not enact—possible remedies to Congress.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a 15-member federal commission to study the history of slavery in the U.S., post-slavery discrimination such as redlining and unequal access to programs like Social Security and the GI Bill, and how those harms may still affect African Americans today. The commission would then advise Congress on possible responses, which could include a formal apology, financial compensation, or other remedies. The commission cannot create any policy or provide any compensation on its own.
ai_sections.who: The commission would have 15 members and up to 18 months to complete its work. It would shut down 90 days after delivering its report to Congress.
ai_sections.why: Congress would receive a research-based set of recommendations and could choose to act on them or not. Without this commission, no formal federal body exists to study these questions and present options to lawmakers.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $20 million is authorized to fund the commission's operations.

title: Lowering Costs for Caregivers Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would allow people to use money from certain tax-advantaged health accounts — specifically Health Savings Accounts (HSAs), Flexible Spending Arrangements (FSAs), Health Reimbursement Arrangements (HRAs), and Archer Medical Savings Accounts — to pay for their parents' or in-laws' medical expenses, not just their own or their spouse's and dependents'. Under current law, these accounts can only be used tax-free for a limited group of people, and parents are generally not included unless they qualify as tax dependents; this bill removes that restriction for parents. The change would apply to medical costs paid or incurred after December 31, 2024. This primarily affects working adults who help cover healthcare costs for a parent or parent-in-law and want to use pre-tax dollars from workplace or personal health accounts to do so.
ai_headline: HSAs and FSAs could cover parents' medical bills
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2025, you can use HSA, FSA, HRA, or Archer MSA funds tax-free to pay a parent's or in-law's medical bills.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets people use money from tax-advantaged health accounts — HSAs, FSAs, HRAs, and Archer Medical Savings Accounts — to pay medical expenses for a parent or parent-in-law. Right now, those accounts can only be used tax-free for yourself, your spouse, and your dependents. Parents are generally not included unless they qualify as tax dependents, and this bill removes that restriction.
ai_sections.who: This affects working adults who help pay healthcare costs for a parent or parent-in-law and want to use pre-tax dollars from their health accounts to do so. The change applies to medical costs paid or incurred after December 31, 2024.
ai_sections.why: People who cover a parent's medical bills would be able to use pre-tax dollars from these accounts, which reduces the portion of that spending subject to income tax. Parents who are not tax dependents would now be included, which is a broader group than current law allows.

title: Stopping Adversarial Tariff Evasion Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the United States determines where an imported product comes from when enforcing certain trade penalties, tariffs, or restrictions. Specifically, if a product was made, manufactured, or had its final assembly done by a company that is owned, headquartered in, or significantly controlled by one of seven named countries — China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Syria, or Venezuela under President Maduro — that product will be treated as if it came from one of those countries, even if it was physically assembled somewhere else. The "controlled by" standard includes any company where one or more of those foreign governments or their affiliated entities own at least 25% of the business, including through joint ventures or financial arrangements. This means a product finished in, say, a third country but made by a Chinese-owned company could still face the same trade penalties as a product coming directly from China. The bill would primarily affect importers, businesses that source goods internationally, and companies with foreign ownership ties to any of the seven listed countries.
ai_headline: Bill targets origin rules for goods from adversary-linked firms
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Products made by companies owned or controlled by China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Syria, or Venezuela can face U.S. trade penalties even if finished elsewhere.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how the U.S. decides where an imported product is considered to come from. If a company owned, headquartered in, or at least 25% controlled by China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Syria, or Venezuela makes or assembles a product, that product is treated as coming from one of those countries. This applies even if the product was physically finished in a different country.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects importers and businesses that source goods from other countries. Companies with ownership ties of 25% or more connected to any of the seven listed countries are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: A product assembled in a third country by a foreign-owned company could still face the same trade penalties as one shipped directly from a listed country. Businesses may need to review their supply chains and ownership structures to understand how this rule applies to them.

title: An act to provide for reconciliation pursuant to title II of H. Con. Res. 14.
ai_summary: This law makes sweeping changes across many areas of federal spending and tax policy that will affect nearly every American in some way. On taxes, it permanently extends and expands the lower individual income tax rates and larger standard deduction from the 2017 tax law, raises the child tax credit, eliminates federal income tax on tips and overtime pay, and ends most of the clean-energy tax credits (such as credits for buying electric vehicles or installing solar panels) while increasing military spending significantly. It tightens the rules for programs like food stamps (SNAP) and Medicaid — for example, requiring more adults to work or participate in job training to receive benefits, adding new costs for states with high error rates, restricting eligibility for certain immigrants, and imposing new cost-sharing requirements — which could reduce benefits for some low-income individuals and families. It also changes student loan rules (capping how much graduate students and parents can borrow and restructuring repayment options), increases fees for many immigration applications, expands oil, gas, and coal leasing on federal lands, and cancels previously approved funding for a wide range of environmental and climate programs. The law also raises the federal debt ceiling to allow the government to continue borrowing to meet its existing obligations.
ai_headline: HR 1 reshapes taxes, Medicaid, and student loans
last_action_text: Became Public Law No: 119-21.

ai_sections.tldr: A sweeping new law changes taxes, food and health benefits, student loans, energy policy, and immigration fees for most Americans.
ai_sections.what: This law makes major changes across many parts of the federal government at once. It extends lower tax rates and raises the child tax credit, while ending most clean-energy tax credits. It also tightens eligibility rules for food and health assistance programs, changes student loan limits and repayment options, expands fossil fuel leasing on federal lands, and raises the federal debt ceiling.
ai_sections.who: Nearly every American is affected in some way. People who receive SNAP food assistance or Medicaid, students and parents borrowing for college, workers who earn tips or overtime, and people applying for immigration benefits will see some of the most direct changes.
ai_sections.why: People currently receiving SNAP or Medicaid may get reduced benefits or lose eligibility if they cannot meet new work or job-training requirements. Canceling clean-energy tax credits means people will no longer be able to reduce their tax bill by buying electric vehicles or installing solar panels.
ai_sections.cost: States with high error rates in benefit programs will face new costs. People applying for immigration benefits will pay higher fees. New cost-sharing requirements mean some Medicaid recipients may pay more out of pocket.

States with high error rates face new costs

title: Eliminating Fraud and Improper Payments in TANF Act
ai_summary:
TANF is a federal program that gives money to states to help low-income families pay for basic needs like food, housing, and childcare. This bill would require states to follow the same rules that federal agencies already follow when it comes to tracking and reducing "improper payments," which are payments made in the wrong amount, to the wrong person, or for the wrong reason. These new requirements would take effect in October 2026.

The bill also requires the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to write a report to Congress within one year explaining how it plans to reduce or eliminate those improper payments in TANF over the next 10 years. Right now, TANF is not covered by the federal law that requires this kind of tracking and reporting, so this bill would close that gap.

This bill mainly affects state governments that run TANF programs and the federal agency that oversees them. It could indirectly affect low-income families who receive TANF benefits, since stricter oversight might change how states review and approve payments. It does not directly cut funding or change who qualifies for benefits.
ai_headline: Bill targets improper TANF payments with new federal oversight rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would require states to track and report improper TANF payments using the same rules federal agencies already follow, starting October 2026.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require states that run TANF programs to follow existing federal rules for tracking and reducing improper payments — money sent in the wrong amount, to the wrong person, or for the wrong reason. It would also require the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to send Congress a report within one year explaining its 10-year plan to reduce those improper payments. TANF is currently not covered by the federal law that requires this kind of tracking.
ai_sections.who: This bill mainly affects state governments that run TANF programs and the federal agency that oversees them. It could indirectly affect low-income families who receive TANF benefits.
ai_sections.why: Stricter oversight may change how states review and approve payments to families. The bill does not directly cut funding or change who qualifies for benefits.

title: Improve Transparency and Stability for Families and Children Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes changes to TANF, which stands for Temporary Assistance for Needy Families. TANF is a federal program that gives block grant money to states, which states then use to help low-income families with children pay for basic needs, childcare, job training, and other support. Right now, states have a lot of flexibility in how long they can hold onto that federal money before spending it, and some states have built up large unspent balances over many years. This bill would set new deadlines requiring states to commit those funds within one year of receiving them and fully spend the money within two years.

The bill also creates a new "rainy day fund" option. Under this rule, states could set aside up to 15 percent of their annual TANF grant to save for future emergencies or economic downturns. However, the total amount saved cannot exceed 50 percent of what the state received the previous year. States would also have to notify the federal government if they plan to hold money in reserve. These changes would take effect starting October 1, 2026.

This bill mainly affects state governments that run TANF programs and, indirectly, the low-income families those programs are meant to serve. Supporters of rules like this generally argue that money meant for struggling families should be spent on them promptly. The rainy day fund provision gives states some flexibility to prepare for recessions or sudden spikes in need without sitting on large, indefinite reserves.
ai_headline: Senate bill would cap TANF unspent funds and create state rainy day accounts
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: States would face new deadlines to spend TANF funds within 2 years, with a limited savings option for emergencies, starting October 2026.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets new deadlines for states to commit TANF federal funds within one year of receiving them and fully spend the money within two years. States could also set aside up to 15 percent of their annual TANF grant for emergencies, with total savings capped at 50 percent of the prior year's grant. These rules would take effect October 1, 2026.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects state governments that run TANF programs. Low-income families with children who rely on TANF for basic needs, childcare, and job training are indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Currently, states can hold federal TANF money for long periods, and some have built up large unspent balances over many years. These changes would require states to move that money out more quickly, while still allowing limited savings for economic downturns or sudden spikes in need.

title: PBM Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets a practice where the same large company owns both a pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) and actual pharmacies. PBMs are the middlemen who decide which drugs insurance plans cover, negotiate drug prices, and determine how much pharmacies get paid. Right now, several giant health care companies own PBMs and also run their own retail, mail-order, and specialty pharmacies. This bill would make that illegal. Companies in that situation would have one year to sell off either their pharmacies or their PBM operations so the two businesses are no longer under the same roof.

The bill sets up strong enforcement tools. The Federal Trade Commission and the Department of Justice can take companies to court if they miss divestment deadlines. If a company is slow to comply, 10 percent of its profits get held in escrow each month until it follows through. State attorneys general can also sue on behalf of their residents, and regular people who were harmed can sue too, potentially receiving triple the amount of their damages plus attorney's fees. Any money a company wrongly earned from pharmacy sales during a period of violation could be taken back and used to help communities that were overcharged.

This bill would most directly affect the handful of large health care conglomerates that currently own both PBMs and pharmacies, which together handled more than 90 percent of U.S. prescriptions in 2023. Everyday Americans could be affected because the bill aims to increase competition, which supporters argue would lower prescription drug costs. Independent pharmacies, which have been closing in large numbers, could also see a more level playing field. Health insurance customers and taxpayers who fund programs like Medicare and Medicaid are also named as groups the bill intends to protect.
ai_headline: Bill would ban PBM giants from owning pharmacies under same roof
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8779 would ban companies from owning both a pharmacy benefit manager and pharmacies, requiring them to sell one within a year.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal for the same company to own both a pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) and actual pharmacies. PBMs are the middlemen who decide which drugs insurance covers, negotiate prices, and set how much pharmacies get paid. Companies that currently do both would have one year to sell off either their pharmacies or their PBM operations.
ai_sections.who: This bill would most directly affect the large health care companies that currently own both PBMs and pharmacies, which together handled more than 90 percent of U.S. prescriptions in 2023. Everyday Americans, independent pharmacies, health insurance customers, and taxpayers who fund Medicare and Medicaid are named as groups the bill intends to protect.
ai_sections.why: Separating these businesses could change how drug prices are negotiated and how pharmacies compete with one another. Independent pharmacies, which have been closing in large numbers, could face a different competitive landscape if the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not include direct government spending, but it does set financial consequences for companies that do not comply. Ten percent of a non-compliant company's monthly profits would be held in escrow, and wrongly earned money from pharmacy sales during a violation period could be taken back. People harmed by violations could sue and receive up to triple their damages plus attorney's fees.

title: PBM Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal for the same company to own both a pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) and a pharmacy at the same time. PBMs are the middlemen companies that manage prescription drug benefits for health insurers — they decide which drugs are covered, negotiate prices with drug makers, and set how much pharmacies get paid. Right now, the three biggest PBMs are owned by large corporations that also own major pharmacy chains. This bill would force those corporations to sell off either their PBM or their pharmacy operations within one year of the law passing.

The bill gives the Federal Trade Commission and the Department of Justice power to enforce the breakup and fine companies that miss deadlines. If a company is too slow to comply, 10 percent of its monthly profits would be held in escrow. State attorneys general and even individual patients could also sue companies that break the rules. Courts could order companies to pay back money earned during the period they were in violation, and those funds would go toward health care costs in affected communities.

This bill would most directly affect large health care conglomerates like CVS Health, Cigna, and UnitedHealth Group, which currently own both PBMs and pharmacies. It could also affect everyday consumers, independent pharmacies, employers who provide health benefits, and anyone enrolled in a health plan that uses a PBM to manage drug coverage. Supporters argue the change would lower drug costs and increase competition, while critics could argue it might disrupt how prescription benefits are currently administered.
ai_headline: Bill targets CVS and UnitedHealth over PBM and pharmacy ownership
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

Companies that currently do both would have one year to sell off one side of their business. The Federal Trade Commission, the Department of Justice, state attorneys general, and individual patients could all take legal action against companies that do not comply.
ai_sections.who: This bill would most directly affect large health care companies like CVS Health, Cigna, and UnitedHealth Group, which currently own both PBMs and pharmacies. It could also affect everyday consumers, independent pharmacies, employers who provide health benefits, and anyone enrolled in a health plan that uses a PBM.
ai_sections.why: Supporters say the change would lower drug costs and increase competition. Critics say it could disrupt how prescription drug benefits are currently managed.
ai_sections.cost: Companies that miss compliance deadlines could be fined, and 10 percent of monthly profits could be held in escrow until they comply. Courts could also order companies to return money earned while they were in violation, with those funds directed toward health care costs in affected communities.

title: Independent and Objective Oversight of Ukrainian Assistance Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new government watchdog office called the Special Inspector General for Ukrainian Military, Economic, and Humanitarian Aid. The job of this office would be to audit and investigate how the United States has spent money on military, economic, and humanitarian aid to Ukraine since February 24, 2022. A Special Inspector General, appointed by the President, would lead the office and would have the power to review contracts, look for waste or fraud, investigate overpayments, and refer cases to the Justice Department if illegal activity is found. Every three months, the office would publish detailed public reports in English, Ukrainian, and Russian showing exactly how aid money was spent.

The office would be funded by shifting $20 million already set aside for European assistance programs rather than adding new spending. No government official at the State Department, Defense Department, or other agencies could block the Inspector General from doing audits or investigations. The office would eventually shut down once less than $250 million in Ukraine aid money remains unspent, at which point a final audit report would be required.

This bill mainly affects federal agencies that handle Ukraine aid, including the State Department, the Defense Department, and USAID. It also affects contractors and organizations that receive U.S. funds connected to Ukraine. For everyday Americans, the main impact would be greater public access to information about how their tax dollars have been used in Ukraine.
ai_headline: Bill would create Special Inspector General to audit Ukraine aid
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would create a new watchdog office to audit and publicly report how U.S. aid to Ukraine has been spent since February 2022.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a new federal watchdog office called the Special Inspector General for Ukrainian Military, Economic, and Humanitarian Aid. The office would audit and investigate how the United States has spent aid money in Ukraine since February 24, 2022. Every three months, it would publish public reports in English, Ukrainian, and Russian showing exactly how that money was used.

Federal agencies including the State Department, the Defense Department, and USAID, as well as contractors receiving U.S. funds tied to Ukraine, would fall under the office's oversight.
