Applicants would need to show a higher level of English ability, prove they paid all their taxes, and show they did not receive most government benefits during that time. The bill would also redefine birthright citizenship so that children born in the US would only automatically be citizens if at least one parent is a citizen or a legal permanent resident.

The bill would affect several other groups as well. People seeking asylum would face tougher rules, including a bar for those who passed through another country on the way to the US without first seeking protection there. Sponsors who bring immigrants to the US would need to earn twice the poverty level and post a $20,000 bond. People who overstay their visas could face criminal charges and fines. The bill would also limit the president's ability to create broad immigration relief programs, such as deferred action policies, without approval from Congress.
ai_headline: ASSIMILATION Act would cut family visas, double naturalization wait to 10 years
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: The ASSIMILATION Act would overhaul legal immigration by cutting family visas, ending the diversity lottery, and doubling the naturalization waiting period to 10 years.
ai_sections.what: The ASSIMILATION Act would eliminate the diversity visa lottery, which currently issues 55,000 green cards per year, and end family-based sponsorship for parents, adult children, and siblings of US citizens. It would shift toward a merit-based work visa system, cap H-1B visas at 50,000 per year, end Optional Practical Training for international students, and redefine birthright citizenship to apply only when at least one parent is a citizen or legal permanent resident. The naturalization waiting period would double from five to ten years, with new requirements for English ability, tax payment history, and limited use of government benefits.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects immigrants seeking green cards through family or the diversity lottery, international students, asylum seekers, H-1B workers, and people seeking US citizenship. US citizens with foreign-born parents, adult children, or siblings would lose the ability to sponsor those relatives for permanent residence.
ai_sections.why: Asylum seekers who traveled through another country without first seeking protection there would be barred from applying. Broad presidential immigration relief programs, such as deferred action policies, would require Congressional approval under this bill.
ai_sections.cost: Sponsors bringing immigrants to the US would need to earn twice the federal poverty level and post a $20,000 bond. People who overstay their visas could face criminal charges and fines.

title: Build Nuclear with Local Materials Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC), which oversees nuclear power plants, to start a process to change its rules within 90 days. Specifically, it would allow nuclear power plant builders to use standard commercial-grade steel and concrete, the same materials used in regular construction projects, for parts of nuclear plants that are not directly involved in safety systems. Right now, those materials must meet stricter, specialized standards even for structures that do not affect how a reactor operates safely.

The bill applies to "non-safety-related structures," meaning things like office buildings, storage facilities, or other support structures on a nuclear plant's property, not the reactor core or containment systems that prevent radiation from escaping. The NRC can still require stricter materials if it finds a specific safety reason to do so, but it would need to justify that decision.

This change would most directly affect companies building new nuclear power plants, construction contractors, and steel and concrete suppliers. Supporters say using standard commercial materials could lower construction costs and make it easier to source materials locally. People living near nuclear plants or concerned about nuclear safety would also have a stake in how the NRC carries out the new rules. The bill does not change safety requirements for the critical systems inside a nuclear plant.
ai_headline: NRC directed to ease material standards for non-safety nuclear structures
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8812 requires the NRC to begin rulemaking within 90 days to allow standard commercial materials in non-safety nuclear structures.
ai_sections.what: HR 8812 directs the Nuclear Regulatory Commission to begin a rulemaking process within 90 days to allow standard commercial-grade steel and concrete in non-safety-related nuclear plant structures. These structures include office buildings, storage facilities, and other support buildings on plant property, not reactor cores or containment systems. The NRC retains authority to require stricter materials if it identifies a specific safety justification.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects companies building new nuclear power plants, construction contractors, and steel and concrete suppliers. People living near nuclear plants and those with nuclear safety concerns also have a stake in how the NRC implements the resulting rules.
ai_sections.why: Supporters argue the change could lower construction costs and make local material sourcing easier, though the bill does not alter safety requirements for critical reactor systems. How the NRC carries out the new rulemaking will determine the practical scope of any changes to material standards.

title: Tropospheric Ozone Research Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would require the EPA to create a grant program to fund scientific research on tropospheric ozone, which is the ozone found in the lower atmosphere near Earth's surface. Unlike the protective ozone layer high in the atmosphere, ground-level ozone is a pollutant that forms when gases from cars, factories, and other sources react in sunlight. The research would focus on how this type of ozone affects temperatures, crop yields, forests, and human health, both today and in the future. Congress would authorize about $32 million total for the program from 2027 through 2030.

The grants would go to universities, national laboratories, and nonprofit research organizations. These groups would study how ground-level ozone interacts with climate change, other air pollutants, and natural factors like lightning and wildfires. The bill also calls for expanding the network of equipment that monitors ozone levels, especially in rural and farming areas that currently have less coverage. All data collected and all research reports would be made publicly available.

The bill would affect a wide range of people and institutions. Scientists and research universities could apply for funding. Farmers could be impacted because the research specifically looks at how ozone damages crops. People living in areas with poor air quality could eventually see effects if the research leads to policy changes. The EPA would be required to submit a full report to Congress within four years, summarizing findings and making policy recommendations. The bill has been introduced but has not yet been passed into law.
ai_headline: EPA grant program for ground-level ozone research clears Senate introduction
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4571 would fund $32M in EPA grants from 2027–2030 to study how ground-level ozone harms crops, forests, and human health.
ai_sections.what: S 4571 would require the EPA to establish a grant program funding scientific research on tropospheric, or ground-level, ozone, a pollutant formed when vehicle and factory emissions react in sunlight. Research would examine how this ozone affects temperatures, crop yields, forests, and human health, and how it interacts with climate change, other pollutants, and natural sources like wildfires and lightning. The bill would also expand ozone monitoring equipment networks, particularly in rural and farming areas, and require all data and reports to be made publicly available.
ai_sections.who: Universities, national laboratories, and nonprofit research organizations would be eligible to apply for grants. Farmers, people living in areas with poor air quality, and policymakers could be affected by the research findings and any policy recommendations that follow.
ai_sections.why: Ground-level ozone monitoring is currently less extensive in rural and agricultural areas, leaving gaps in understanding of its effects on crops and ecosystems. The EPA would be required to submit a full report to Congress within four years summarizing research findings and making policy recommendations, which could influence future air quality regulation.
ai_sections.cost: Congress would authorize approximately $32 million total for the grant program, covering the period from 2027 through 2030.

title: Protecting Consumers from Deceptive AI Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs a federal science agency, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), to set up special working groups within 90 days of the bill becoming law. These groups would work on creating technical standards and guidelines for marking and identifying content made by AI. The goal is to develop things like watermarks, digital fingerprints, and hidden data tags that could help people and platforms tell whether a photo, video, audio clip, or piece of text was created or heavily changed by AI. The bill wants these markings to be hard to remove or fake, and as secure as possible.

The working groups would include a wide range of people, not just government officials. Members would come from tech companies, social media platforms, search engines, universities, news organizations, privacy experts, human rights advocates, labor groups, and others. Each group would need to submit recommendations within about nine months and then report to Congress every year for five years. The bill also specifically asks the groups to think about protecting user privacy when it comes to how these AI-content labels and data tags are stored and shared online.

This bill would most directly affect technology companies, social media platforms, and AI developers, who may eventually face new industry standards for labeling AI-generated content. Everyday internet users would be affected too, since the goal is to make it easier for people to know when the images, videos, audio, and text they see online were made by AI rather than a human. The bill does not create any new laws forcing companies to act right away. It focuses on building the technical groundwork and expert consensus needed before any such rules could be written.
ai_headline: Federal agency ordered to develop AI content labeling standards
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8893 directs NIST to form working groups within 90 days to build technical standards for labeling AI-generated content.
ai_sections.what: HR 8893 directs the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to establish working groups within 90 days of enactment. These groups would develop technical standards and guidelines for marking AI-generated or AI-altered photos, videos, audio, and text using tools like watermarks, digital fingerprints, and hidden data tags. The bill requires these markings to be difficult to remove or falsify and as secure as possible.
ai_sections.who: Working group members would include representatives from tech companies, social media platforms, search engines, universities, news organizations, privacy experts, human rights advocates, and labor groups. Technology companies, social media platforms, and AI developers would be most directly affected, along with everyday internet users who encounter AI-generated content online.
ai_sections.why: Each working group must submit recommendations within approximately nine months and report to Congress annually for five years, creating ongoing expert oversight of AI content identification. The bill does not immediately require companies to comply with any rules; it focuses on building the technical foundation and expert consensus needed before enforceable standards could be written.

title: Passenger Rail Crew Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it a federal crime to assault or intimidate passenger train crew members. Right now, attacking an airline crew member is already a federal offense with serious penalties, but train workers do not have that same federal protection. This bill would close that gap by applying similar rules to intercity and commuter rail workers, including engineers, conductors, station staff handling tickets and baggage, and anyone else performing a safety role on or around a passenger train.

The penalties depend on how serious the attack is. A basic assault could mean up to six months in prison. If someone actually strikes or wounds a crew member, that goes up to one year. If a weapon is involved, serious injury results, or the attacker intended to commit another serious crime, the penalty jumps to up to ten years. An attack carried out with the intent to commit murder could bring up to twenty years in federal prison.

This bill mainly affects two groups. Train workers on intercity lines like Amtrak and on commuter rail systems would gain stronger legal protections. Passengers and anyone else present at train stations, on platforms, or onboard operating trains would be subject to these new federal rules. If someone in those locations assaults a crew member, the case could be prosecuted in federal court rather than relying only on state or local laws.
ai_headline: Federal assault protections would extend to passenger train crews
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8894 would make assaulting passenger train crew members a federal crime with penalties up to 20 years.
ai_sections.what: HR 8894 would create a new federal crime of assaulting or intimidating passenger train crew members, closing a gap that currently leaves train workers without the same federal protections airline crew members already have. Covered workers include engineers, conductors, station staff handling tickets and baggage, and anyone in a safety role on or around a passenger train. Penalties range from six months for a basic assault up to twenty years if the attack is carried out with intent to commit murder.
ai_sections.who: Train workers on intercity lines such as Amtrak and on commuter rail systems would gain new federal legal protections under this bill. Passengers and anyone else present at train stations, on platforms, or onboard operating trains would be subject to these new federal rules.
ai_sections.why: Cases involving assault on train crew members could be prosecuted in federal court rather than relying solely on state or local laws. The bill applies a penalty structure similar to existing federal law protecting airline crew members.

title: K–12 AI Literacy and Readiness Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes how federal education money can be spent. Right now, schools receive federal funding under a law called the Elementary and Secondary Education Act, which lists specific things that money can be used for. This bill adds AI education to that approved list. That means schools and states could use existing federal education funds to teach students how to use artificial intelligence tools safely and responsibly, and to train teachers, librarians, and other school staff on how to use and teach AI as well.

The bill does not create a new program or send new money to schools. It simply expands the menu of allowed spending options within funding that already exists. Schools and states would still choose whether or not to spend their money on AI-related instruction. Nothing in the bill requires anyone to adopt AI curriculum.

This bill most directly affects K-12 public school students across the country, along with their teachers, school librarians, paraprofessionals, and administrators. School districts and state education agencies would gain new flexibility in how they use federal dollars. Parents could potentially see AI literacy added to what their children are taught in school, depending on whether their local school or state decides to use funds for that purpose.
ai_headline: Federal education funds could soon cover AI literacy classes in K-12 schools
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8747 lets schools use existing federal education funds for AI literacy instruction without adding new money or mandates.
ai_sections.what: HR 8747 adds AI education to the list of approved uses for federal funding under the Elementary and Secondary Education Act. Schools and states could use existing funds to teach students how to use AI tools safely and responsibly, and to train teachers, librarians, and other staff. The bill does not create a new program or require anyone to adopt AI curriculum.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects K-12 public school students nationwide, along with teachers, librarians, paraprofessionals, and administrators. School districts and state education agencies would gain new flexibility in how they direct existing federal dollars.
ai_sections.why: Whether students receive AI literacy instruction would depend entirely on whether their local school or state chooses to spend funds that way. The bill expands options for spending without directing any particular outcome.
ai_sections.cost: No new money is created or sent to schools. The bill only changes what existing federal education funds are permitted to cover.

title: SILVER Act
ai_summary:
The SILVER Act would change federal rules for how precious metals like gold and silver are stored when they are traded on U.S. futures markets. Right now, the vaults approved to store these metals are almost entirely located near New York City. This bill would require the major financial clearinghouses that handle these trades to find and approve approved storage facilities in every U.S. time zone, with at least two approved vaults in each zone. It also requires these organizations to publish clear, public rules explaining how a vault company can apply to become an approved storage site.

The bill is aimed at reducing the risk that comes from having all the physical metal concentrated in one part of the country. If something disrupted storage in the New York area, it could cause serious problems for the entire precious metals market. By spreading storage locations across the country, the bill's supporters argue the system becomes more stable. It also encourages competition among vault operators, which could lower the cost of storing metals and make the market easier to access for more traders and investors.

This bill would most directly affect large financial clearinghouses, vault and storage companies, and businesses that trade precious metals through futures contracts. Everyday Americans who invest in gold or silver through funds or commodity markets could indirectly benefit if competition among storage facilities lowers costs or makes the market more stable, but most people would not notice a direct change in their daily lives.
ai_headline: Senate bill would spread gold and silver storage across all U.S. time zones
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: The SILVER Act requires approved precious metals vaults in every U.S. time zone, with at least two per zone, to reduce geographic concentration risk.
ai_sections.what: The SILVER Act would require major financial clearinghouses to approve precious metals storage facilities in every U.S. time zone, with at least two approved vaults per zone. Currently, approved vaults are concentrated almost entirely near New York City. The bill also requires clearinghouses to publish public rules explaining how vault companies can apply for approved status.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects large financial clearinghouses, vault and storage companies, and businesses that trade precious metals through futures contracts. Everyday Americans who invest in gold or silver through funds or commodity markets could be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Concentrating physical metal storage in one region creates systemic risk, and a disruption in the New York area could cause serious problems for the entire precious metals market. Spreading storage across the country and increasing competition among vault operators could affect market stability and the cost of storage access for traders and investors.

title: PATCH Act
ai_summary:
The PATCH Act would change the rules about medical debt and credit reports. Right now, unpaid medical bills can show up on your credit report and lower your credit score. This bill would ban credit reporting agencies from including medical debt on consumer credit reports. It would also require the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to update its rules within one year so that lenders cannot use medical debt information when deciding whether to give someone a loan or line of credit.

This bill affects anyone who has received medical care and ended up with unpaid bills. That covers tens of millions of Americans. It would mean that if you owe money to a hospital, doctor, or for medical equipment, that debt could no longer hurt your credit score or be used against you when you apply for a mortgage, car loan, or credit card.

Lenders, credit reporting agencies like Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion, and debt collectors who deal in medical debt would also be affected, since they would have to change how they collect, report, and use this type of information. The bill does not erase medical debt itself — people would still owe the money — it just stops that debt from appearing on credit reports or being considered by lenders.
ai_headline: Senate bill would strip medical debt from consumer credit reports
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: The PATCH Act would ban medical debt from credit reports and bar lenders from using it in loan decisions.
ai_sections.what: The PATCH Act would prohibit credit reporting agencies from including medical debt on consumer credit reports. It would also require the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to update its rules within one year so lenders cannot use medical debt information when deciding whether to approve a loan or line of credit. The bill does not cancel medical debt — people would still owe the money.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects tens of millions of Americans who have unpaid medical bills, including debt owed to hospitals, doctors, or for medical equipment. Lenders, credit reporting agencies including Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion, and medical debt collectors would also need to change how they handle this information.
ai_sections.why: Currently, unpaid medical bills can appear on credit reports and lower credit scores, affecting access to mortgages, car loans, and credit cards. Under this bill, medical debt could no longer be reported or used in lending decisions, which would require lenders and credit agencies to revise existing practices.

title: Medicare Access to Radiology Care Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would allow Medicare to pay for services performed by radiologist assistants, which it currently does not do. Radiologist assistants are highly trained medical professionals who work alongside radiologists during imaging procedures like X-rays, MRIs, and CT scans. Right now, Medicare only recognizes and pays for the radiologist's work directly, which means facilities and patients can face gaps in coverage when a radiologist assistant helps with part of the care. Starting January 1, 2027, Medicare would officially cover and pay for those services.

The bill sets clear rules about how this works. Radiologist assistants must be certified by the American Registry of Radiologic Technologists, and they can only work under a radiologist's direct supervision. They are not allowed to interpret or read imaging results on their own. Payment would go to the supervising radiologist, not directly to the assistant. The services also have to be legal under the state's rules where they are performed.

The people most affected are Medicare patients who receive imaging services, particularly those treated at hospitals, critical access hospitals, and ambulatory surgical centers. It could also benefit radiologists and their practices by making it easier to use radiologist assistants as part of their team without losing Medicare reimbursement. Radiologist assistants themselves benefit because their work gains formal recognition within the Medicare system. Rural and underserved communities could see the biggest improvement in access, since radiologist assistants often help fill gaps where radiologists are in short supply.
ai_headline: Medicare would cover radiologist assistant services starting in 2027
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4624 adds Medicare coverage for radiologist assistants effective January 1, 2027, under direct radiologist supervision.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 4624 would require Medicare to cover and pay for services performed by radiologist assistants beginning January 1, 2027. Radiologist assistants must be certified by the American Registry of Radiologic Technologists and can only work under a radiologist's direct supervision. They are not permitted to interpret or read imaging results independently, and payment goes to the supervising radiologist.
ai_sections.who: Medicare patients receiving imaging services at hospitals, critical access hospitals, and ambulatory surgical centers are directly affected. Radiologists, their practices, and radiologist assistants are also affected, as are patients in rural and underserved communities where radiologists are in short supply.
ai_sections.why: Currently, Medicare does not recognize or reimburse services performed by radiologist assistants, creating coverage gaps when they assist with patient care. Extending coverage could affect the availability of imaging services in settings and communities where radiologist assistants help fill workforce shortages.

title: SAFE through Medicare Act
ai_summary:
This bill would add a new benefit to Medicare called "home resiliency services." Starting January 1, 2027, Medicare would pay for certain home items and equipment that help protect people from natural disasters or emergencies. Examples include heat pumps for people who are sensitive to extreme temperatures, solar batteries for people who rely on electrical medical equipment like ventilators, and special cold storage for medications that must be kept at a safe temperature. Medicare would cover 100% of these costs, up to a set price determined by the government.

Not everyone on Medicare would qualify. To receive these benefits, a person would need to be officially determined "medically at-risk" in a disaster situation. The Secretary of Health and Human Services, working with health and weather agencies, would create a process to decide who qualifies. That process would look at things like a person's medical conditions, what equipment or medications they depend on, and what kinds of climate or disaster risks exist in their area, such as extreme heat, flooding, or power outages.

This bill primarily affects current Medicare enrollees, who are mostly Americans 65 and older, along with some younger people who qualify due to disabilities. It would also affect doctors and health agencies involved in determining eligibility and setting payment rules. The bill has been introduced in the House and referred to committees, meaning it has not yet been passed into law.
ai_headline: Medicare bill would cover home disaster gear for medically at-risk enrollees
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8749 would add Medicare coverage for home disaster equipment starting Jan. 1, 2027, for enrollees deemed medically at-risk.
ai_sections.what: HR 8749 would create a new Medicare benefit called "home resiliency services," covering home items and equipment that protect against natural disasters or emergencies. Covered items could include heat pumps, solar batteries, and special cold storage for temperature-sensitive medications. Medicare would pay 100% of costs up to government-set price limits, beginning January 1, 2027.
ai_sections.who: The benefit would apply to Medicare enrollees who are officially determined to be medically at-risk in a disaster situation, a group that includes primarily Americans 65 and older plus some younger people with disabilities. Doctors and health agencies would also be affected through their roles in determining eligibility and setting payment rules.
ai_sections.why: Eligibility determinations would require a process developed by the Secretary of Health and Human Services in coordination with health and weather agencies, weighing medical conditions, equipment or medication dependencies, and local climate or disaster risks. The bill has been referred to committees and has not yet been passed into law.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare would cover 100% of the cost of qualifying home resiliency items, up to a price set by the government. The summary does not specify a total program cost or funding source.

title: Student Loan Reform Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a voluntary program where colleges and universities can choose to co-sign federal student loans for their own students. Starting July 1, 2026, a school that joins the program agrees to back every new federal loan taken out by its students that year. If a student defaults on their loan and 90 days pass without the situation being resolved, the school becomes responsible for repaying that loan on a standard 10-year schedule. Students who default still face the normal consequences like damaged credit, and any money collected from them gets counted toward what the school owes. As a reward for taking on that financial risk, the school's students get a lower interest rate on their loans than they otherwise would.

The bill also changes the rules around "cohort default rates," which are the percentages of a school's borrowers who fail to repay their loans. Schools that join this co-signer program are allowed to have a higher default rate, up to 40 percent, before facing federal penalties. Schools that do not join are held to the current stricter standard of 30 percent.

This bill mainly affects college and university administrators, who would decide whether to take on the financial risk of co-signing loans, and current and future college students, who could see lower interest rates if their school opts in. Taxpayers and the federal government are also affected, since the schools would absorb some of the cost of student loan defaults instead of the government. Participating schools would be listed publicly on the Department of Education's website each year.
ai_headline: Colleges could co-sign student loans and face repayment if borrowers default
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8759 lets colleges voluntarily co-sign federal student loans, making schools repay defaulted loans over 10 years.
ai_sections.what: HR 8759 creates a voluntary program starting July 1, 2026, where colleges and universities can choose to co-sign federal student loans for their students. Schools that join must back every new federal loan their students take out that year. If a student defaults and 90 days pass without resolution, the school becomes responsible for repaying that loan on a standard 10-year schedule.
ai_sections.who: College and university administrators would decide whether their institution joins the program and takes on the financial risk. Current and future college students at participating schools would receive lower interest rates on their federal loans.
ai_sections.why: Schools that opt in are permitted a higher cohort default rate threshold of 40 percent before facing federal penalties, compared to the 30 percent standard applied to non-participating schools. Participating schools would absorb some costs of student loan defaults that would otherwise fall on the federal government and taxpayers.
ai_sections.cost: Schools that join the program take on repayment responsibility for defaulted loans, shifting some default costs from the federal government to participating institutions. Any amounts collected from defaulted students count toward what the school owes.
costChips: Default costs shift from government to schools

title: TREE Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the TREE Act, would make it illegal starting January 1, 2029, to import into the United States or sell across state lines any product that came from land where forests were cleared after December 31, 2020. The products covered include cattle, cocoa, palm oil, rubber, soy, and wood, along with anything made from those materials, such as beef, chocolate, furniture, or tires. Companies that import these goods would have to prove, through a formal statement to Customs and Border Protection, that their products did not come from deforested land. Lying on that paperwork would also be illegal.

The bill sets up a system where the US Trade Representative ranks every country in the world as high, medium, or low risk for selling deforestation-linked goods to the US. Higher-risk countries would face more frequent inspections of their shipments. Companies that break the rules could be fined up to 4 percent of their US yearly revenue, have their goods seized, and lose the ability to do business with the federal government for up to a year. Repeat or serious violators could be banned from importing anything into the US for up to 12 months.

The bill affects US businesses that import or sell the covered products, including large retailers, food companies, and manufacturers. It also affects foreign countries and suppliers in their supply chains. American consumers could see changes in product availability or prices for everyday goods like food, paper, and rubber products. Half of all fine money collected would go toward helping developing countries manage their forests.
ai_headline: New import law would ban deforestation-linked goods from US markets by 2029
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committees on Foreign Affairs, and Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting 2029, the TREE Act would ban US imports and interstate sales of cattle, cocoa, palm oil, rubber, soy, and wood from land cleared after 2020.
ai_sections.what: The TREE Act would make it illegal starting January 1, 2029, to import or sell across state lines any product derived from land where forests were cleared after December 31, 2020. Covered commodities include cattle, cocoa, palm oil, rubber, soy, and wood, along with finished goods such as beef, chocolate, furniture, and tires. Importers would be required to certify to Customs and Border Protection that their products did not originate from deforested land, and falsifying that certification would also be illegal.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects US importers, large retailers, food companies, and manufacturers that handle the covered products, as well as foreign countries and suppliers throughout those supply chains. American consumers could see changes in the availability or prices of everyday goods including food, paper, and rubber products.
ai_sections.why: The US Trade Representative would rank every country as high, medium, or low risk, with higher-risk countries subject to more frequent shipment inspections. Companies that violate the rules could face fines, seizure of goods, loss of federal contracting privileges, and import bans of up to 12 months for serious or repeat offenders.
ai_sections.cost: Violators could be fined up to 4 percent of their US annual revenue and have goods seized, and they could lose the ability to do business with the federal government for up to one year. Half of all fine revenue collected would be directed toward helping developing countries manage their forests.
costChips: Fines up to 4% of US annual revenue
Half of fines aid developing nations

title: ICE FROST Act
ai_summary:
This bill does two main things. First, it gives extra pay to federal law enforcement officers who work on immigration enforcement, meaning jobs like identifying, arresting, detaining, or removing people who are in the US without legal status. Every officer doing this work would get a bonus equal to 25 percent of their regular yearly salary, paid once a year as a lump sum. Officers working in specific metro areas, including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, Washington DC, and several others listed in the bill, would get an additional 15 percent bonus on top of that, because those areas are labeled as higher-risk. Other cities could be added to that list later by government officials based on factors like crime levels or documented threats to officers.

Second, the bill adds new fees onto wire transfers of money sent from the US to certain foreign countries. On top of an existing 1 percent tax on these transfers, it would add a flat $199 surcharge for money sent to a specific list of countries that includes Haiti, Somalia, Iran, Yemen, and several others. It would also add a $99 surcharge for money sent to any country where more than 2 percent of visa holders have overstayed their visas. If both conditions apply, both charges stack together.

The people most directly affected are federal immigration enforcement officers, who would see a significant pay increase, and people living in the US who regularly send money to relatives or others in the listed countries, who would face much higher transfer fees. The bill does not specify exactly how the extra pay would be funded, though the remittance fees could serve as a revenue source.
ai_headline: Federal immigration officers would get 25% pay bonus under HR 8805
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8805 raises immigration officer pay by 25% and adds fees up to $298 on wire transfers to certain countries.
ai_sections.what: HR 8805 would give federal immigration enforcement officers an annual lump-sum bonus equal to 25 percent of their base salary, with an additional 15 percent for officers working in designated higher-risk metro areas including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, and Washington DC. The bill would also add surcharges on wire transfers sent from the US to specific foreign countries, including a flat $199 fee for transfers to countries such as Haiti, Somalia, Iran, and Yemen, and a $99 fee for transfers to any country where more than 2 percent of visa holders have overstayed. Both surcharges can apply at the same time, stacking on top of an existing 1 percent tax.
ai_sections.who: Federal immigration enforcement officers working on identifying, arresting, detaining, or removing people without legal status would see a significant pay increase. People in the US who send money to relatives or others in the listed countries would face substantially higher transfer fees.
ai_sections.why: Officers in the named metro areas would receive a combined 40 percent bonus because those locations are classified as higher-risk, and additional cities could be added later based on crime levels or documented threats to officers. People who regularly send remittances to the listed countries would pay up to $298 in flat surcharges per transfer, beyond the existing 1 percent tax, with no cap or exemption specified in the summary.
ai_sections.cost: Remittance surcharges of $199 or $99, stacking when both conditions apply, would be paid by people sending wire transfers to the listed countries. The bill does not specify how the officer pay bonuses would be funded, though the remittance fees could serve as a revenue source.

title: Medicare Payment Integrity Enhancement Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes how Medicare checks for improper payments. Right now, Medicare mostly pays claims from doctors, hospitals, and other providers first, and then later looks back to find and recover payments that should not have been made. This bill would allow the government to hire private companies called Recovery Audit Contractors, or RACs, to review some claims before Medicare pays them. If a RAC finds a problem with a claim before payment goes out, Medicare can stop the payment upfront rather than trying to get the money back later. The government must set up rules for this within one year of the bill passing.

The bill also sets up a payment system for these contractors. Instead of paying them only for finding past overpayments, the government would pay them based on how much improper spending they prevent ahead of time. The rules are designed to make sure contractors work quickly and accurately, and do not unfairly target one type of medical claim over others. Funding for these contractor payments would come from the existing Medicare trust funds.

This bill mainly affects healthcare providers like doctors, hospitals, and other medical facilities that bill Medicare. They could face more reviews and possible delays before receiving payment for services they provide. It also affects Medicare patients indirectly, since the goal is to protect the Medicare program's finances. Taxpayers in general have a stake as well, since Medicare is funded through taxes and premiums.
ai_headline: Medicare bill would let private auditors block payments before they go out
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8804 lets private contractors review and stop improper Medicare payments before they are made, not just after.
ai_sections.what: HR 8804 allows the government to hire private companies called Recovery Audit Contractors, or RACs, to review certain Medicare claims before payment is issued. If a RAC identifies a problem, Medicare can block the payment upfront instead of recovering the money after the fact. The government must establish rules for this program within one year of the bill passing.
ai_sections.who: Doctors, hospitals, and other medical facilities that bill Medicare are directly affected and could face pre-payment reviews and payment delays. Medicare patients and taxpayers are indirectly affected, since the program is funded through taxes and premiums.
ai_sections.why: Shifting some audits to before payment goes out changes how and when healthcare providers receive reimbursement for services. The structure of contractor pay, which is tied to improper spending prevented rather than past overpayments recovered, shapes how aggressively and broadly RACs conduct reviews.
ai_sections.cost: Payments to the private contractors would be funded through the existing Medicare trust funds. Contractor pay is based on how much improper spending they prevent ahead of time, not on recovering past overpayments.
costChips: Pay tied to improper spending prevented

title: Cable Security Fleet Expansion Act
ai_summary:
This bill expands a little-known but important government program called the Cable Security Fleet. This fleet is made up of privately owned ships that can lay and repair underwater cables, like the ones that carry internet and phone communications across the ocean. The U.S. government contracts with these ships so they are available in times of national emergency or military need. Right now the law requires at least 2 of these ships. This bill would raise that minimum to at least 6 ships.

The bill also increases the money the government can pay each ship operator, from exactly $5 million per year to up to $7 million per year. On top of that, it extends the program further into the future and significantly increases the total funding, from $10 million per year through 2035 to $56 million per year through 2040. That is a major jump in spending meant to support a larger fleet.

This bill mostly affects the companies that own and operate specialized cable-laying ships, as well as the U.S. military and federal agencies that depend on undersea cables for communications. For everyday Americans, the impact is indirect. Undersea cables carry the vast majority of international internet traffic, so keeping those cables protected and repairable affects everyone who uses the internet, makes international calls, or relies on global financial transactions.
ai_headline: Bill would expand U.S. cable ship fleet from 2 to 6 vessels
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8808 raises the minimum Cable Security Fleet from 2 to 6 ships and boosts funding to $56M/year through 2040.
ai_sections.what: HR 8808 expands the Cable Security Fleet, a government program that contracts with privately owned ships capable of laying and repairing undersea communications cables. The bill raises the minimum number of ships in the fleet from 2 to at least 6. It also extends the program through 2040 and raises per-ship payments from exactly $5 million per year to up to $7 million per year.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects companies that own and operate specialized cable-laying ships, as well as the U.S. military and federal agencies that rely on undersea cables. Everyday Americans are indirectly affected because undersea cables carry the vast majority of international internet traffic, international calls, and global financial transactions.
ai_sections.why: Expanding the fleet increases the number of ships available to the government during national emergencies or military operations that require undersea cable repair or installation. A larger, better-funded program means more capacity to maintain the cables that underpin international communications and internet access.
ai_sections.cost: Total authorized funding rises from $10 million per year through 2035 to $56 million per year through 2040. Individual ship operators can receive up to $7 million per year, up from the current flat rate of $5 million per year.

title: PETS Act
ai_summary:
Prescription Drug Monitoring Programs, or PDMPs, are databases that track when doctors prescribe controlled substances like opioids or sedatives. This bill updates federal rules about who must report to those databases and what information they must share.

The main change is that veterinarians would now be officially required to check and report to PDMP databases before prescribing controlled substances to animals, just like human doctors already must. Since animals cannot be listed as patients in these systems, the vet would report information about the animal's owner or primary caretaker instead. The bill also requires any doctor, vet, or other prescriber to report to the PDMP within 72 hours if a patient has a non-fatal overdose that required their intervention. A smaller change turns optional sharing of drug misuse information between states from something they "may" do into something they "shall" do, making it mandatory.

This bill affects veterinarians across the country, who would face new federal expectations around controlled substance tracking. It also affects anyone who picks up a prescription for their pet, since their personal information would be entered into state drug monitoring databases. Patients who survive overdoses would also be affected, since their overdose would now need to be formally reported. The goal is to close gaps in tracking controlled substances that could be misused or diverted for non-medical purposes.
ai_headline: Vets would face new controlled substance reporting rules under federal bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8983 requires vets to use drug monitoring databases before prescribing controlled substances and mandates overdose reporting within 72 hours.
ai_sections.what: HR 8983 updates federal rules for Prescription Drug Monitoring Programs, the databases that track controlled substance prescriptions. Veterinarians would be newly required to check and report to these databases before prescribing controlled substances, with the animal owner or caretaker listed in place of a patient. Any prescriber must also report a non-fatal overdose requiring their intervention within 72 hours, and cross-state sharing of drug misuse information becomes mandatory rather than optional.
ai_sections.who: Veterinarians nationwide would face new federal reporting requirements when prescribing controlled substances to animals. Pet owners, animal caretakers, and patients who survive overdoses would also have their personal information entered into state drug monitoring databases.
ai_sections.why: The bill is intended to close gaps in controlled substance tracking that could allow misuse or diversion for non-medical purposes. Expanding reporting requirements and making interstate data sharing mandatory increases the scope of personal information collected in these systems.

title: Maternal Health Pandemic Response Act
ai_summary:
This bill would direct federal money toward tracking and studying how public health emergencies, like disease outbreaks, affect pregnant and postpartum women. It would send a total of $190 million to programs at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and the National Institutes of Health. That money would be used to collect data on pregnancy outcomes during outbreaks, expand surveys about maternal health, review pregnancy-related deaths, and research ways to reduce risks to pregnant people and newborns. The bill also requires the government to post this data publicly and update it at least monthly during an emergency, while keeping individual patient information private.

The bill also creates a task force to develop official recommendations for how to provide safe and respectful maternity care during public health emergencies. That task force would look at a wide range of topics, including telehealth options for prenatal visits, mental health screening, access to midwives and doulas, breastfeeding guidance, domestic violence during pregnancy, and care for uninsured or undocumented pregnant people. The recommendations would need to be available in multiple languages and would pay special attention to groups that historically experience higher rates of pregnancy-related deaths or complications.

The people most directly affected would be pregnant women and new mothers across the country, especially those from communities that have historically had worse health outcomes, including Black, Hispanic, Native American, and low-income women. Health care providers, midwives, doulas, public health departments, and state and tribal governments would also be involved in carrying out the programs and following the new guidance.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets maternal health data gaps during disease outbreaks
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8807 sends $190M to CDC and NIH to track pregnancy outcomes during public health emergencies and sets maternity care standards.
ai_sections.what: HR 8807 directs $190 million to the CDC and NIH to collect data on pregnancy outcomes during public health emergencies, expand maternal health surveys, review pregnancy-related deaths, and research ways to reduce risks to pregnant people and newborns. The bill requires the government to publish this data publicly and update it at least monthly during an emergency, while protecting individual patient privacy. It also creates a task force to develop official recommendations for safe and respectful maternity care during emergencies, covering topics such as telehealth, mental health screening, access to midwives and doulas, and care for uninsured or undocumented pregnant people.
ai_sections.who: Pregnant women and new mothers nationwide are the primary people affected, with particular focus on Black, Hispanic, Native American, and low-income women who have historically experienced worse health outcomes. Health care providers, midwives, doulas, public health departments, and state and tribal governments would also be involved in carrying out the programs and following the new guidance.
ai_sections.why: Public health emergencies such as disease outbreaks create gaps in data about how pregnancy outcomes are affected, and no standardized federal guidance currently exists for maternity care during those events. The bill would establish data collection systems and care recommendations that could shape how providers, states, and tribes respond to future emergencies affecting pregnant and postpartum people.
ai_sections.cost: The bill allocates a total of $190 million to programs at the CDC and NIH to fund data collection, research, and maternal health initiatives tied to public health emergencies.

title: To repeal the jurisdiction conferred by section 3243 of title 18, United States Code, to the State of Kansas over offenses committed by or against Indians on Tribal lands in Kansas.
ai_summary:
This bill would undo a longstanding federal law that gave the state of Kansas the power to handle crimes committed on tribal lands in Kansas when Native Americans are involved, either as the person accused or as the victim. Right now, Kansas state courts and law enforcement have authority over those cases. If this bill passes, that authority would be taken away from the state.

Once that state authority is removed, jurisdiction over crimes on tribal lands would shift back to tribal governments and federal authorities, which is the standard arrangement in most other states. Tribal nations would have more control over law enforcement and the justice system on their own lands, and federal agencies like the FBI would play a larger role in handling serious crimes there.

This bill directly affects the tribal nations located in Kansas, their members, and the state and local law enforcement agencies that currently respond to crimes on those lands. It would also affect anyone, Native or non-Native, who commits a crime on those tribal lands or is a victim of one there. The practical impact would be a change in which court system handles those cases and which law enforcement agencies respond.
ai_headline: Bill would strip Kansas of criminal jurisdiction over tribal lands
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8824 would end Kansas's authority over crimes on tribal lands, shifting jurisdiction to tribal governments and federal agencies.
ai_sections.what: HR 8824 would repeal a longstanding federal law that gave Kansas state courts and law enforcement authority over crimes committed on tribal lands when a Native American is involved as either the accused or the victim. If passed, that state authority would be removed. Jurisdiction would then shift to tribal governments and federal authorities, which is the standard arrangement in most other states.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects tribal nations in Kansas and their members, as well as state and local law enforcement agencies that currently respond to crimes on tribal lands. It also affects any person, Native or non-Native, who commits or is a victim of a crime on those lands.
ai_sections.why: Tribal nations would gain greater control over law enforcement and the justice system on their own lands, and federal agencies such as the FBI would take on a larger role in handling serious crimes there. The change would determine which court system hears cases and which law enforcement agencies respond to incidents on tribal lands in Kansas.

title: Putting Patients First by Strengthening Provider Accountability in FECA Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for how the federal government pays doctors and other medical providers who treat injured federal workers. Right now, when a federal employee gets hurt on the job, their medical costs are covered under a program called the Federal Employees' Compensation Act, or FECA. This bill would give the Secretary of Labor the power to stop payments to any medical provider who has been convicted of fraud, whether that fraud involved the FECA program, another federal health program, or even a state health program.

The bill affects medical providers, meaning doctors, clinics, or suppliers of medical equipment, who bill the government for treating injured federal workers. If one of those providers has a fraud conviction on their record, the government could cut off their payments under FECA. The change would take effect 180 days after the bill becomes law, and the Department of Labor would be required to write official rules explaining exactly how the suspension process would work.

Federal employees who were injured at work could also be affected, since their access to certain providers might change if those providers lose their ability to receive FECA payments. The bill does not change the benefits that injured workers are entitled to, only whether specific providers can get paid through the program.
ai_headline: Labor Secretary could cut payments to fraud-convicted medical providers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8823 lets the Labor Secretary block FECA payments to any medical provider convicted of fraud, effective 180 days after enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 8823 gives the Secretary of Labor authority to suspend payments to medical providers who have been convicted of fraud under FECA, another federal health program, or a state health program. The suspension would apply to doctors, clinics, and medical equipment suppliers who bill the government for treating injured federal workers. The Department of Labor would be required to issue formal rules explaining how the suspension process works, starting 180 days after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: Medical providers who bill FECA for treating injured federal workers are directly affected if they carry a fraud conviction. Federal employees injured on the job may also be affected if their current providers lose eligibility to receive FECA payments.
ai_sections.why: Injured workers' access to specific providers could change if those providers are suspended from the program. The bill does not alter the benefits injured workers are entitled to receive, only which providers can be paid to deliver them.

title: Deporting Illegal Poachers Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change US immigration law to make hunting and fishing violations a reason to deny someone entry into the country or to deport them if they are already here. Specifically, it targets non-citizens who have been convicted of, or who admit to, offenses like hunting or fishing without a license, taking protected animals or fish, violating season limits, exceeding bag limits, or using illegal methods or locations. It does not matter whether the violation was a misdemeanor or a felony — even minor infractions would count.

The bill affects non-citizens living in or trying to enter the United States. A non-citizen who commits one of these violations — even a relatively minor one under state law — could be barred from entering the country or removed from it. Current citizens are not affected at all, since immigration law only applies to non-citizens.

It is worth noting that this bill is only in the early stage of the legislative process. It was introduced in the House and sent to the Judiciary Committee, where most bills never advance further. If it became law, immigration judges and federal agencies would be responsible for applying it in individual cases.
ai_headline: House bill would make hunting and fishing violations grounds for deportation
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8847 would let the US bar or deport non-citizens convicted of hunting or fishing violations, including minor infractions.
ai_sections.what: HR 8847 would add hunting and fishing violations to the list of offenses that can bar a non-citizen from entering the United States or lead to their deportation. The bill covers offenses such as hunting or fishing without a license, taking protected species, violating season limits, exceeding bag limits, or using illegal methods or locations. Even minor infractions — regardless of whether they are misdemeanors or felonies — would count under this bill.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects non-citizens who are living in or trying to enter the United States. Current US citizens are not affected, as immigration law applies only to non-citizens.
ai_sections.why: A non-citizen convicted of or admitting to even a relatively minor hunting or fishing offense under state law could be denied entry or removed from the country. If enacted, immigration judges and federal agencies would be responsible for applying the law in individual cases.

title: Democracy Without Intimidation Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes it illegal, with no exceptions, to have armed military troops stationed at or near polling places on Election Day. Current federal law already bans this practice, but it includes a loophole that allows troops to be present if needed to "repel armed enemies of the United States." This bill removes that exception entirely, meaning military forces could never be deployed at polls under any circumstances.

The bill also gives everyday voters a way to fight back if the law is broken. If someone believes troops were unlawfully placed at a polling location, they can sue certain senior federal law enforcement officials or political appointees directly in federal court. Those cases would be fast-tracked, and any final rulings could be appealed straight to the Supreme Court within 10 days, skipping the normal lengthy appeals process.

This bill affects anyone who votes in a federal election, as well as senior officials at federal law enforcement agencies who could face lawsuits if troops appear at polls. It also directs federal courts to hire more judges to handle any cases that come up under this and related election laws. Supporters and opponents both tend to frame this around questions of election security and voter intimidation, but the bill itself is focused narrowly on keeping military personnel away from voting locations.
ai_headline: Bill would ban military at polling places with no exceptions
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8825 closes a federal loophole allowing troops at polls and lets voters sue officials who violate the ban.
ai_sections.what: HR 8825 would make it permanently illegal for armed military troops to be stationed at or near polling places on Election Day, with no exceptions. It removes an existing loophole in federal law that currently allows troops to be present to repel armed enemies of the United States. Voters who believe the law has been broken could sue senior federal law enforcement officials or political appointees directly in federal court.
ai_sections.who: Anyone who votes in a federal election is affected, as is any senior official at a federal law enforcement agency who could face a lawsuit if troops appear at polls.
ai_sections.why: Cases brought under this law would be fast-tracked in federal court, with final rulings appealable directly to the Supreme Court within 10 days, bypassing the normal appeals process. The bill also directs federal courts to hire additional judges to handle cases arising under this and related election laws.

title: No Wasted Space Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a nine-member commission to study what should be done with the Arts and Industries Building, a historic Smithsonian building on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. The building has been largely vacant or underused for years. The commission would spend up to a year researching possible future uses, such as turning it into a modern museum, housing cultural institutions that are not currently near the Mall, or serving families and schools visiting the capital. The commission members would be chosen by congressional leaders from both parties and by the Smithsonian, and they must have backgrounds in museums, education, tourism, or urban planning.

The commission's main job is to produce three things: a report on what kinds of organizations or institutions could use the building, a fundraising plan that looks at how the building could pay for itself over time without relying heavily on taxpayer money, and a set of recommendations for Congress on how to move forward, including possible costs and even a new name for the building. Commission members serve for free, though they can be reimbursed for travel. The commission funds itself through donations and contributions, not a direct government budget line.

This bill mainly affects federal policymakers, the Smithsonian Institution, museums and cultural organizations, Washington D.C. residents, and tourists who visit the National Mall. It does not change anything about the building right away. It simply sets up a study group to gather information and give Congress options to consider for the future.
ai_headline: Nine-member commission would study future of vacant Smithsonian building on the Mall
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on House Administration, and in addition to the Committees on Transportation and Infrastructure, and Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8810 creates a nine-member commission to study future uses for the long-vacant Arts and Industries Building on the National Mall.
ai_sections.what: HR 8810 establishes a nine-member commission to research and recommend future uses for the Arts and Industries Building, a historic but largely vacant Smithsonian building on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. The commission has up to one year to produce a report on potential occupants, a fundraising plan aimed at financial self-sufficiency, and formal recommendations for Congress including possible costs and a potential new name. The bill does not change anything about the building immediately.
ai_sections.who: Commission members are selected by congressional leaders from both parties and by the Smithsonian Institution, and must have backgrounds in museums, education, tourism, or urban planning. Federal policymakers, the Smithsonian, museums and cultural organizations, Washington D.C. residents, and National Mall visitors are the primary parties affected.
ai_sections.why: The commission's work could give Congress concrete options for activating a building that has been underused for years, potentially bringing cultural institutions or educational programming to the National Mall. The outcome depends entirely on what the commission recommends and whether Congress chooses to act on those recommendations.
ai_sections.cost: Commission members serve without pay but may be reimbursed for travel expenses. The commission funds its operations through donations and contributions rather than a direct government budget appropriation.
costChips: Funded by donations, not taxpayer budget line

title: Housing for All Veterans Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new federal rental assistance program specifically for veterans and their families who have low incomes. Starting in 2027, eligible veteran families could receive housing vouchers to help pay rent. The program would be run through local public housing agencies, the same organizations that manage existing federal rental assistance programs. Importantly, landlords who own five or more rental units would not be allowed to refuse to rent to someone just because they are using one of these vouchers. VA disability benefits would not count as income when determining whether a veteran qualifies, which makes it easier for disabled veterans to be eligible.

The program is designed to expand gradually. In the first year, only the very poorest veteran families would qualify, but by 2031 and beyond, eligibility opens up to a broader group of low-income veterans. Once a family is enrolled and receiving help, they can remain in the program as long as their income does not go above the local area median income, even if their earnings grow past the original cutoff. Veteran status must be verified electronically, and anyone who received a dishonorable discharge or was dismissed by a military court would not be eligible.

This bill would affect veterans and their families across the country who are struggling to afford rent, as well as the local housing agencies that would administer the new vouchers. Landlords with five or more units would also be affected by the new anti-discrimination rule. The program is written as a permanent entitlement, meaning funding would automatically be provided each year to cover all eligible families rather than being capped by annual congressional budget decisions.
ai_headline: New federal vouchers would help low-income veterans afford rent starting 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8985 creates permanent federal housing vouchers for low-income veterans beginning 2027, with landlord anti-discrimination rules for those owning five or more units.
ai_sections.what: HR 8985 would establish a permanent federal rental assistance program providing housing vouchers to eligible low-income veteran families beginning in 2027. Eligibility would expand gradually from the very poorest veteran families in the first year to a broader low-income group by 2031 and beyond. VA disability benefits would be excluded from income calculations, and veterans with dishonorable discharges or court-martial dismissals would not qualify.
ai_sections.who: Low-income veterans and their families across the country would be directly affected as potential recipients. Local public housing agencies would administer the vouchers, and landlords owning five or more rental units would be subject to a new anti-discrimination rule prohibiting voucher refusals.
ai_sections.why: Because the program is structured as a permanent entitlement, federal funding would automatically flow each year to cover all eligible families without requiring annual congressional appropriations decisions. Landlords with five or more units would face a new legal obligation they do not currently have under this program, affecting how they screen prospective tenants.
ai_sections.cost: The program is written as a permanent entitlement, meaning funding would be provided automatically each year to cover all eligible families rather than being subject to annual congressional budget caps.

title: Foreign Service Workforce Retention Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes it easier for the U.S. government to bring back experienced Foreign Service workers who have already retired or left their jobs. Right now, the law allows retired Foreign Service officers to be recalled to service. This bill expands that to include people who left for other reasons, not just retirement. It also sets a five-year window after someone retires or separates during which they can be rehired under these easier rules, and it requires the government to complete that rehiring within 180 days of approval.

The bill also adds a reporting requirement. The Secretary of State would have to send Congress a report within 60 days of the law passing, and then every year after that, listing how many former Foreign Service members were brought back, what rank they held when they left, and what rank and job they returned to. This creates more transparency and accountability around the rehiring process.

The people most directly affected are career Foreign Service officers, the diplomats and foreign affairs professionals who work at U.S. embassies and agencies like the State Department around the world. If they retire or leave their positions, this bill makes it simpler and faster for the government to rehire them within a five-year period. American taxpayers and people who rely on U.S. diplomatic services abroad could also be indirectly affected, since the bill aims to help maintain an experienced workforce in the Foreign Service.
ai_headline: Bill expands who can be rehired into the Foreign Service after leaving
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8987 lets the U.S. rehire former Foreign Service workers within 5 years of leaving, not just retirees.
ai_sections.what: HR 8987 expands existing law to allow the government to rehire former Foreign Service workers who left for reasons other than retirement, not only those who retired. A five-year window after separation applies, and the rehiring process must be completed within 180 days of approval. The Secretary of State must report to Congress within 60 days of enactment and annually after that, detailing how many former members were rehired, their former rank, and their new rank and role.
ai_sections.who: Career Foreign Service officers, including diplomats and foreign affairs professionals at the State Department and U.S. embassies abroad, are most directly affected. People who rely on U.S. diplomatic services overseas could be indirectly affected if the bill influences workforce experience levels.
ai_sections.why: The bill changes the legal basis for rehiring, potentially making it faster and simpler to return experienced personnel to the Foreign Service within a defined period. The reporting requirement creates a formal record of rehiring activity that Congress can review each year.

title: Ensuring Rural Health Care Access for Military and Tribal Families Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for which hospitals can receive a special Medicare designation called "critical access hospital" status. Normally, a hospital has to meet strict requirements to earn this label, such as being a certain distance from other hospitals. Critical access status matters because it means Medicare pays the hospital more money for treating patients, which helps small, rural hospitals stay open. This bill would let states bypass some of those usual requirements for certain hospitals starting in October 2026, as long as the hospital meets at least three conditions from a specific list.

The conditions a hospital could qualify under include things like: serving military families covered by TRICARE or veterans living in rural areas, earning at least 8 percent of its total revenue from TRICARE patients, earning at least 15 percent of its maternity care revenue from TRICARE patients, or being located on a Native American reservation. The hospital also cannot already have a separate protected status called "sole community hospital."

The people most directly affected are patients in rural areas, specifically military families, veterans, and Native American tribal communities who rely on small local hospitals for care. Hospitals that serve these groups in rural areas could gain better Medicare payment rates, which might help them stay financially stable and keep their doors open. Medicare, TRICARE, and the federal budget would also be affected, since higher payment rates mean more government spending on these facilities.
ai_headline: Bill would open Medicare critical access status to rural military and tribal hospitals
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8986 lets states grant critical access hospital status to rural hospitals serving military families, veterans, or tribal communities starting October 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 8986 would allow states to bypass standard requirements for critical access hospital designation for certain rural hospitals beginning in October 2026. To qualify, a hospital must meet at least three conditions from a defined list, which includes serving TRICARE-covered military families or rural veterans, earning at least 8 percent of total revenue from TRICARE patients, earning at least 15 percent of maternity care revenue from TRICARE patients, or being located on a Native American reservation. Hospitals that already hold sole community hospital status would not be eligible.
ai_sections.who: Patients most directly affected are rural military families, veterans, and Native American tribal community members who depend on small local hospitals. Rural hospitals serving these groups would be eligible for higher Medicare payment rates under the new pathway.
ai_sections.why: Critical access hospital status triggers higher Medicare reimbursement rates, which can affect whether small rural hospitals remain financially viable. Expanding eligibility to additional hospitals would increase the number of facilities receiving those elevated payments from Medicare and the federal government.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare and the federal budget would face higher spending, since critical access hospital designation results in increased government payments to qualifying facilities.
costChips: Higher Medicare payments to qualifying hospitals

title: Evidence-Based Youth Suicide Prevention Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to create and fund demonstration programs aimed at preventing suicide among children and teenagers. The programs would test and study suicide prevention strategies that are backed by scientific research. Funding would be given out through grants to states, school districts, nonprofits, tribal organizations, and colleges. The programs would focus mainly on schools and other places where young people spend time, and they would be required to work together with local education and public health agencies.

The bill puts a strong emphasis on using approaches that actually have evidence behind them. It defines different levels of proof, from "strong evidence" based on multiple rigorous studies, to "moderate evidence," to "promising" approaches that have a solid plan to be evaluated. Money would be directed first toward programs with the strongest proof, but some funding would also go toward newer, innovative ideas that are being carefully studied.
