The government would track a wide range of results, including suicide attempts, school attendance, grades, and student well-being.

This bill would most directly affect school-age children and teenagers across the country, as well as the schools, community organizations, and health systems that serve them. School staff, mental health professionals, and researchers working on youth mental health programs would also be involved. Funding is authorized from 2027 through 2032, and the Department of Health and Human Services would be required to report results to Congress each year.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets youth suicide prevention through school-based grants
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8989 funds evidence-based youth suicide prevention programs in schools and communities through grants from 2027 to 2032.
ai_sections.what: HR 8989 directs the Department of Health and Human Services to create and fund demonstration programs testing evidence-based suicide prevention strategies for children and teenagers. Grants would go to states, school districts, nonprofits, tribal organizations, and colleges. The government would track outcomes including suicide attempts, school attendance, grades, and student well-being, and report results to Congress annually.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects school-age children and teenagers nationwide, along with the schools, community organizations, and health systems that serve them. School staff, mental health professionals, and youth mental health researchers would also be involved.
ai_sections.why: Programs would be required to coordinate with local education and public health agencies, and funding would be prioritized for approaches with the strongest scientific evidence. Some funding would also be directed toward newer, innovative approaches that are being formally evaluated.
ai_sections.cost: Funding is authorized for the period from 2027 through 2032, distributed through grants to states, school districts, nonprofits, tribal organizations, and colleges.

title: To amend title 18, United States Code, to establish nationwide concealed carry reciprocity for certain members of the Armed Forces, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill would allow active-duty military members to carry a concealed firearm anywhere in the United States, as long as they have completed an official military firearms qualification within the past 12 months. Right now, a federal law called the Law Enforcement Officers Safety Act already gives qualified off-duty police officers this nationwide carry right. This bill would expand that same law to cover certain military members, putting them in the same legal category as those officers.

To use this right, a service member would need to show two things as proof: their military ID card, known as a Common Access Card, and documentation showing they passed a military firearms qualification recently. The qualification must have been completed within the previous 12 months, so it is not a permanent or one-time approval. The person must also be on active duty, meaning reservists or veterans who are not currently serving would not be covered under this bill.

This bill directly affects active-duty members of the U.S. Armed Forces and the states where they live or travel. Currently, concealed carry laws vary widely by state, and a service member moving between states or stationed in a new location may face different rules. This bill would remove that patchwork of state laws for qualifying active-duty personnel. It would not affect civilians, veterans, or members of the National Guard or Reserves unless they are on active-duty orders.
ai_headline: Active-duty troops would get nationwide concealed carry rights under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8855 would let active-duty military members carry concealed firearms nationwide if they passed a military firearms qualification within the past 12 months.
ai_sections.what: HR 8855 would expand the Law Enforcement Officers Safety Act to allow active-duty military members to carry a concealed firearm anywhere in the United States. To qualify, a service member must present a Common Access Card and proof of a military firearms qualification completed within the previous 12 months. The 12-month requirement means the approval is recurring, not permanent.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to active-duty members of the U.S. Armed Forces only. Reservists, National Guard members, and veterans who are not on active-duty orders would not be covered.
ai_sections.why: Currently, concealed carry laws vary by state, meaning active-duty personnel moving between states or stationed in new locations face different legal requirements. This bill would replace that state-by-state framework with a single federal standard for qualifying active-duty personnel.

title: Protecting Taxpayers from Fraudulent Providers Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for health care providers who are convicted of fraud and similar crimes. Right now, when a doctor, hospital, or other health care provider is convicted of fraud, theft, embezzlement, or similar financial crimes, they can be banned from Medicare, Medicaid, and other federal health programs for a set period of time, after which they may be allowed back in. This bill would make that ban permanent for convictions that happen after the law takes effect, meaning they could never participate in those programs again.

The bill applies specifically to two categories of exclusions under existing law: cases where someone was convicted of a program-related crime, and cases involving fraud, theft, or breach of financial responsibility connected to health care. It also removes the ability of those permanently excluded providers to appeal for reinstatement, which is currently an option for people who have served out their exclusion period.

The people most directly affected are health care providers, such as doctors, nurses, clinics, and medical equipment suppliers, who are convicted of fraud-related crimes involving federal health programs. It also affects patients who rely on Medicare or Medicaid, since providers in those programs would be permanently removed if convicted. Taxpayers are the stated target of protection, since federal health care fraud costs the government billions of dollars each year.
ai_headline: Fraud convictions would permanently bar health providers from Medicare and Medicaid
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8865 would make permanent the Medicare and Medicaid ban for providers convicted of fraud, ending all reinstatement options.
ai_sections.what: HR 8865 would permanently ban health care providers convicted of fraud, theft, embezzlement, or similar financial crimes from participating in Medicare, Medicaid, and other federal health programs for convictions occurring after the law takes effect. Current law allows a set exclusion period after which providers may seek reinstatement. This bill eliminates that reinstatement option entirely for the covered categories of convictions.
ai_sections.who: Health care providers — including doctors, nurses, clinics, and medical equipment suppliers — convicted of qualifying fraud-related crimes are directly affected. Patients who rely on Medicare or Medicaid are also affected, as their providers would be permanently removed from those programs upon conviction.
ai_sections.why: Permanently excluding convicted providers removes an existing pathway for return to federally funded health programs, regardless of time served or other circumstances. Federal health care fraud costs the government billions of dollars each year, and the bill is stated to target protection of taxpayers.

title: Next GEN Act of 2026
ai_summary:
The federal government has a program that lets Medicare negotiate lower prices on certain prescription drugs. Under current law, a drug can be selected for negotiation after it has been on the market for a set number of years, depending on what type of drug it is. This bill would create a special category called "engineered cyclic peptides," which are a newer type of lab-designed medicine taken by mouth, inhaled, or self-administered. For drugs that qualify under this new category, the waiting period before Medicare can negotiate their price would be extended from 7 years to 11 years.

Engineered cyclic peptides are a relatively new kind of drug. They are small, specially designed molecules made of amino acids, built using advanced screening technology and not found in nature. Because they are newer and more complex to develop, supporters of extending the timeline argue these drugs need more time on the market before facing price negotiation. The bill sets specific technical requirements a drug must meet to qualify for this longer protection period.

This bill would most directly affect pharmaceutical companies that make these types of drugs, since it delays when the government can negotiate their prices. It would also affect Medicare, which covers tens of millions of older Americans and people with disabilities, as well as patients who use these medications. If negotiation is delayed, Medicare and its enrollees could potentially pay higher prices on these drugs for a longer period of time compared to what the current rules would allow.
ai_headline: Bill would delay Medicare drug price talks for a new class of lab-made medicines
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8857 extends the Medicare price negotiation waiting period for engineered cyclic peptides from 7 to 11 years.
ai_sections.what: HR 8857 creates a new drug category called "engineered cyclic peptides" — small, lab-designed molecules made of amino acids that are taken by mouth, inhaled, or self-administered. Drugs that qualify under this category would wait 11 years before Medicare can negotiate their prices, instead of the current 7 years. The bill sets specific technical requirements a drug must meet to qualify for this longer protection period.
ai_sections.who: Pharmaceutical companies that manufacture engineered cyclic peptides are most directly affected, since the bill delays when the government can negotiate their prices. Medicare, which covers tens of millions of older Americans and people with disabilities, and patients who use these medications are also affected.
ai_sections.why: If price negotiation is delayed, Medicare and its enrollees could potentially pay higher prices on these drugs for a longer period of time compared to what current rules would allow. The extended timeline applies only to drugs that meet the bill's specific technical definition of engineered cyclic peptides.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare and its enrollees could potentially pay higher prices on qualifying drugs for up to four additional years compared to current law, though no specific dollar figures are provided in the bill summary.
costChips: Medicare enrollees may face higher costs

title: Radiation Health Research Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the National Institutes of Health (NIH) to create and run a long-term research program studying how nuclear radiation affects women's health. The program must pay special attention to pregnant women, teenage girls, and younger girls, since research suggests these groups may be more vulnerable to radiation's effects. The study would run for at least 10 years, following the same participants over time to track how health outcomes develop.

The bill requires NIH to work with other federal agencies, including the Department of Energy, and to follow the best available science. Participants' personal information would be kept private. Every year, NIH would report back to Congress on what the research has found, where knowledge gaps still exist, and what steps the country might take to reduce harm from radiation exposure, including possible public education efforts.

This affects women and girls across the United States, particularly those who may have been or could be exposed to nuclear radiation, such as people living near nuclear facilities, workers in certain industries, or those affected by past nuclear testing or accidents. It also affects researchers and federal health agencies that would be involved in designing and carrying out the studies. The bill only authorizes the program if Congress separately approves the funding needed to run it.
ai_headline: NIH would track radiation's effects on women and girls for at least 10 years
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8852 directs NIH to run a 10-plus-year study on how nuclear radiation affects women and girls, with priority on pregnant women and minors.
ai_sections.what: HR 8852 directs the National Institutes of Health to create and operate a long-term research program examining how nuclear radiation affects women's health. The program must run for at least 10 years, following the same participants over time. NIH would be required to collaborate with other federal agencies, including the Department of Energy, and to apply the best available science.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects women and girls across the United States, especially those who may have been exposed to nuclear radiation through proximity to nuclear facilities, certain industries, or past nuclear testing or accidents. Researchers and federal health agencies involved in designing and conducting the studies would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Pregnant women, teenage girls, and younger girls are identified as groups that may be more vulnerable to radiation's effects, driving the focus on those populations. Annual reports to Congress would document findings, identify remaining knowledge gaps, and outline possible steps to reduce harm from radiation exposure, including potential public education efforts.
ai_sections.cost: The bill only authorizes the program if Congress separately approves the funding needed to run it; no specific funding amount is stated in the summary.

title: End Fentanyl Trafficking with Local Task Forces Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new federal grant program to help law enforcement agencies fight fentanyl and other opioid trafficking. The grants would be run through the COPS Office, which is part of the Department of Justice. State and local police departments, tribal law enforcement agencies, and groups of agencies working together across different areas could all apply for funding. The money could be used to hire and train officers, buy equipment, pay overtime, and set up or expand task forces that combine officers from multiple agencies. The program would be funded at $70 million per year from 2027 through 2031.

At least 20 percent of the grant money is set aside specifically for tribal law enforcement and for multi-jurisdictional task forces, which are teams made up of officers from different agencies working together. The bill also gives priority to applicants who plan to create new task forces or grow existing ones. Regional consortiums, meaning groups of task forces that work across state lines, are also eligible. The COPS Office would also be required to offer training and technical support to help agencies use the grants effectively.

This bill would most directly affect local and state police departments, tribal law enforcement agencies, and the communities they serve, especially those in areas heavily impacted by fentanyl and opioid trafficking. It does not create new criminal penalties or change drug laws. Instead, it focuses on giving law enforcement agencies more resources and better coordination tools to investigate and stop the flow of illegal opioids.
ai_headline: Federal bill funds $70M yearly to boost opioid trafficking enforcement
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8818 would fund $70M per year from 2027–2031 for police grants to fight fentanyl and opioid trafficking.
ai_sections.what: HR 8818 creates a federal grant program administered by the COPS Office within the Department of Justice to help law enforcement agencies combat fentanyl and opioid trafficking. Grant funds may be used to hire and train officers, purchase equipment, pay overtime, and establish or expand multi-agency task forces. The bill does not create new criminal penalties or change existing drug laws.
ai_sections.who: Eligible applicants include state and local police departments, tribal law enforcement agencies, and multi-jurisdictional groups of agencies. Communities in areas heavily affected by fentanyl and opioid trafficking would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: At least 20 percent of funding is reserved for tribal law enforcement and multi-jurisdictional task forces, and priority is given to applicants planning to create or expand task forces. The COPS Office would be required to provide training and technical support alongside the grants.
ai_sections.cost: The program is authorized at $70 million per year for fiscal years 2027 through 2031, for a potential total of $350 million over five years.

title: Build to Scale Reauthorization Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill renews a federal grant program called Build to Scale, which is run by the Department of Commerce. The program gives money to state agencies and nonprofit organizations that help new technology-based businesses grow in specific regions of the country. These groups use the funding to connect startups with investors, help researchers turn their ideas into actual products, and support entrepreneurs in building new companies. The bill would keep this program running and funded at up to $50 million per year from 2026 through 2030.

The bill also makes some changes to how the program works. It updates the rules so that grants require the recipient to cover at least half the cost, though that requirement can be adjusted based on how economically struggling an area is. It also expands outreach efforts so that rural communities, areas hurt by trade, and places with long-term economic hardship are more actively encouraged to apply for funding. Recipients can also partner with local workforce boards to connect job training programs with the businesses being supported.

The people most directly affected are entrepreneurs, startup founders, and small technology-based businesses, especially those in regions that have struggled economically. State and nonprofit organizations that run innovation programs would also be affected since they apply for and distribute these grants. More broadly, communities in rural or economically distressed areas could see new business activity and job opportunities if local organizations successfully apply for funding under this renewed program.
ai_headline: Federal startup grant program renewed with expanded reach to distressed regions
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8866 renews the Build to Scale grant program at up to $50M/year through 2030, targeting tech startups in economically distressed areas.
ai_sections.what: HR 8866 renews the Build to Scale program, run by the Department of Commerce, which funds state agencies and nonprofits that help technology-based startups grow in specific regions. The bill authorizes up to $50 million per year from 2026 through 2030. It also updates cost-sharing rules, requiring grant recipients to cover at least half the cost, with adjustments allowed for economically struggling areas.
ai_sections.who: Entrepreneurs, startup founders, and small technology-based businesses in economically distressed regions are most directly affected. State agencies and nonprofit organizations that administer innovation programs are also affected, as they apply for and distribute the grants.
ai_sections.why: Renewing the program extends federal support for startup ecosystems in regions that have faced long-term economic hardship, trade-related job losses, or rural isolation. The expanded outreach requirements mean more communities in those categories would be actively encouraged to compete for funding, and workforce boards could be brought in to link job training with supported businesses.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes funding for the Build to Scale program at up to $50 million per year for fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

title: No Special Immunity for Violating Our State Laws Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would change the legal rules for federal immigration officers when they injure or kill someone while doing their job. Right now, federal officers generally cannot be prosecuted under state criminal laws because of a legal concept called federal immunity. This bill would remove that protection specifically for immigration enforcement officers if their actions result in serious injury or death to another person.

In practical terms, if a federal immigration officer were to seriously hurt or kill someone during an immigration enforcement operation, state prosecutors could charge that officer under their state's criminal laws, the same way they could charge any other person. The bill only applies to officers enforcing immigration laws, not federal law enforcement officers in general.

This would directly affect federal immigration officers, such as ICE and Border Patrol agents. It could also affect people who are injured or killed during immigration enforcement actions, giving their cases a potential path through state courts. State governments would also be affected, since they would gain the authority to investigate and prosecute these cases if they chose to do so.
ai_headline: Bill strips federal immunity from immigration officers who injure or kill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8862 would let state prosecutors charge ICE and Border Patrol agents under state criminal law if they seriously injure or kill someone.
ai_sections.what: HR 8862 would remove federal immunity protections specifically for immigration enforcement officers whose actions result in serious injury or death. Under current law, federal officers generally cannot be prosecuted under state criminal laws. This bill would create an exception limited to immigration enforcement officers only.
ai_sections.who: Federal immigration officers, including ICE and Border Patrol agents, would be directly subject to the new rules. People seriously injured or killed during immigration enforcement operations, and state governments that choose to investigate or prosecute such cases, would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: State prosecutors would gain authority to bring criminal charges against immigration officers under the same state laws that apply to any other person. Cases involving serious injury or death during immigration enforcement could move through state court systems rather than being blocked by federal immunity.

title: Federal Funding Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill would give federal district courts clear legal authority to hear lawsuits filed against the federal government when a person, organization, or government entity believes a federal grant was wrongly canceled. Right now, there can be confusion about which court has the power to handle these kinds of cases. This bill clears that up by specifically allowing district courts to take up cases where someone is asking a judge to reverse a grant termination tied to a disputed government action.

Who does this affect? It primarily affects grant recipients such as universities, nonprofits, state and local governments, and research institutions that receive federal funding. If a federal agency cuts off their grant and they believe that decision was improper or illegal, this bill would give them a clearer path to challenge it in federal district court.

It is worth noting this bill has been introduced but not yet passed. It would only change where and how these disputes get resolved in court. It does not guarantee anyone would win such a lawsuit, restore any specific funding, or prevent the government from terminating grants. It simply ensures there is a recognized legal venue for these challenges to be heard.
ai_headline: HR 8993 would let district courts hear disputed federal grant terminations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8993 clarifies that federal district courts can hear lawsuits challenging wrongful federal grant terminations.
ai_sections.what: HR 8993 would give federal district courts explicit legal authority to hear cases in which a grant recipient challenges a federal agency's decision to terminate a grant. Current law creates confusion about which court has jurisdiction over these disputes. The bill does not restore any funding, guarantee legal victories, or stop the government from canceling grants.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects grant recipients including universities, nonprofits, research institutions, and state and local governments that receive federal funding. Any of these entities that believes a grant was wrongly canceled would gain a clearer legal pathway to challenge that decision in federal district court.
ai_sections.why: Without a designated venue, grant recipients facing terminations may struggle to find a court willing to hear their case. This bill resolves that jurisdictional uncertainty by establishing district courts as a recognized forum for such challenges, which could affect how often and how easily disputes are litigated.

title: PLAN Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the Department of Health and Human Services to create a nationwide public education campaign about long-term care planning. Long-term care refers to the help people need with daily activities as they age or deal with disabilities, such as home health aides, assisted living, or nursing homes. The campaign would spread information through TV, radio, social media, community programs, and partnerships with doctors, employers, financial advisors, and even social media influencers. The goal is to make sure Americans understand the costs and options involved in long-term care before they actually need it.

The bill puts special focus on reaching people while they are still working age, not just near retirement. It requires materials to be available in multiple languages and accessible to people with disabilities. It also targets middle-income people who earn too much to qualify for Medicaid but cannot easily afford to pay for long-term care on their own, a group that often falls through the cracks of the current system. HHS would need to report to Congress within one year on its plan, and then send annual updates showing how many people the campaign reached and whether public awareness actually improved.

This bill primarily affects working-age adults, retirees, people with disabilities, and family caregivers across the country. It also involves state and local governments, nonprofit organizations, and private businesses that would partner in delivering the education effort. The bill does not create a new long-term care benefit or insurance program. It uses existing funding authorized under the Older Americans Act and does not include a new dedicated funding stream of its own.
ai_headline: HHS ordered to launch national education push on long-term care planning
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8867 requires HHS to run a national campaign teaching working-age Americans about long-term care costs and options before they need them.
ai_sections.what: HR 8867 directs the Department of Health and Human Services to create and run a nationwide public education campaign about long-term care planning, covering options such as home health aides, assisted living, and nursing homes. The campaign would use TV, radio, social media, community programs, and partnerships with doctors, employers, financial advisors, and social media influencers. Materials must be available in multiple languages and accessible to people with disabilities.
ai_sections.who: The campaign primarily targets working-age adults, retirees, people with disabilities, and family caregivers. It places special emphasis on middle-income people who earn too much to qualify for Medicaid but cannot easily afford long-term care on their own.
ai_sections.why: HHS would be required to report to Congress within one year on its plan and then submit annual updates showing how many people the campaign reached and whether public awareness actually improved. The bill does not create a new long-term care benefit or insurance program, meaning its reach depends entirely on whether the education effort changes how people plan ahead.
ai_sections.cost: The bill uses existing funding authorized under the Older Americans Act and does not include a new dedicated funding stream of its own.
costChips: Uses existing Older Americans Act funds

title: National Dam and Hydropower Safety Improvements Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill strengthens safety rules for dams that produce hydropower and are licensed by the federal government through the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, or FERC. Right now, dam operators must follow safety rules, but this bill makes those requirements an official condition of every license. It also says that when a dam owner applies to renew their license, FERC can only approve that renewal if the dam already meets safety standards or if the owner has a clear plan to fix any problems. FERC would also have to check whether license applicants have enough money to actually keep their dams safe and running.

The bill also improves communication between the federal government and state governments. FERC would be required to hold a conference with states by October 2027 to share information about dam maintenance, flood risks, and how climate change may affect dam safety. States would also get automatic notifications when a dam in their area fails an inspection, when the dam owner still has not made required repairs after five years, or when FERC starts the process of revoking a license. If a license is revoked or given up, FERC must hand over all relevant records to the state so it knows what condition the dam is in.

This bill affects dam and hydropower plant operators who hold federal licenses, the communities that live near those dams, and state governments that oversee local safety. There are roughly 1,600 dams licensed by FERC across the country. People who live downstream from these dams would potentially benefit from stronger oversight and faster action when safety problems are found.
ai_headline: Federal hydropower dam safety rules would become binding license conditions
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8889 makes dam safety standards a formal license condition for roughly 1,600 FERC-licensed dams and requires state notification of safety failures.
ai_sections.what: HR 8889 makes dam safety requirements an official condition of every federal hydropower license issued by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC). License renewals could only be approved if a dam already meets safety standards or if the owner has a documented plan to fix deficiencies. FERC would also be required to assess whether license applicants have sufficient financial resources to maintain safe dam operations.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects operators of the roughly 1,600 dams licensed by FERC across the country, as well as state governments that oversee local dam safety. Communities and residents living downstream from these dams are also affected by the oversight changes.
ai_sections.why: States would receive automatic notifications when a dam fails an inspection, when required repairs remain incomplete after five years, or when FERC begins license revocation proceedings, giving states earlier awareness of safety problems. If a license is revoked or surrendered, FERC must transfer all relevant records to the state, and a required federal-state conference by October 2027 would address dam maintenance, flood risks, and climate-related safety concerns.

title: No Bailouts for Cashless Bail Jurisdictions Act
ai_summary:
This bill would cut off all federal funding to any city, county, or state that has largely gotten rid of cash bail for certain crimes. Cash bail is the money a person pays to get out of jail while waiting for their court date, with the idea that they will show up to court to get that money back. Some places around the country have moved away from this system, releasing people without requiring a cash payment. Under this bill, the U.S. Attorney General would review jurisdictions every three months and decide which ones have substantially eliminated cash bail for crimes like violence, sex offenses, burglary, vandalism, and looting. Any place found to have done so would lose access to federal money until at least 180 days after that finding, and only after the Attorney General confirms they have brought cash bail back.

The people most directly affected would be residents of cities, counties, or states that have changed their bail systems. Federal funds cover a wide range of things, including road repairs, schools, housing assistance, law enforcement grants, and more. If a jurisdiction lost that funding, everyday services and programs that local residents depend on could be reduced or cut. The bill does not specify which federal funding streams would be cut, meaning it could potentially affect many different programs at once.

Local and state governments that have already reformed or eliminated cash bail would face a choice: restore cash bail or lose federal dollars. Courts, lawmakers, and local officials in those places would need to respond to the Attorney General's determinations within the timelines set by the bill. The bill was introduced in the House and has been sent to two committees, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: House bill would strip federal funds from cities that dropped cash bail
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: A House bill would cut all federal funding to any jurisdiction the Attorney General finds has largely eliminated cash bail.
ai_sections.what: This bill would cut off all federal funding to cities, counties, or states that the U.S. Attorney General determines have substantially eliminated cash bail for crimes including violence, sex offenses, burglary, vandalism, and looting. The Attorney General would conduct reviews every three months. A jurisdiction found in violation would lose federal funding until at least 180 days after that finding, and only after the Attorney General confirms cash bail has been restored.
ai_sections.who: Residents of cities, counties, or states that have reformed or eliminated cash bail would be most directly affected. Local and state officials in those jurisdictions would face pressure to restore cash bail or lose access to federal dollars.
ai_sections.why: Federal funds support a wide range of services including road repairs, schools, housing assistance, and law enforcement grants, so a funding cutoff could reduce or eliminate programs local residents depend on. The bill does not specify which federal funding streams would be cut, meaning the impact could extend across many programs at once.
ai_sections.cost: Federal funding across multiple program areas, including roads, schools, housing, and law enforcement grants, would be withheld from any jurisdiction found to have substantially eliminated cash bail. The bill does not identify specific dollar amounts or limit which funding streams would be affected.
costChips: All federal funds at risk for violators

title: SCCOTUS Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new 13-judge panel to take over one of the Supreme Court's current jobs: deciding which cases the Court will hear. Right now, the nine Supreme Court Justices themselves choose which cases to accept by voting on "certiorari petitions," which are requests from losing parties asking the Court to review their case. Under this bill, that screening job would be handed to a rotating group of federal appeals court judges, one randomly selected from each of the 13 U.S. circuit courts. These judges would serve one term at a time and could not serve two terms in a row. If the panel approves a case, it automatically goes onto the Supreme Court's docket, and the Justices then handle the actual arguments and ruling as they always have.

The panel would use the same basic standards that currently guide whether a case gets accepted, such as when different federal appeals courts have ruled differently on the same legal question, or when an important issue of federal law needs to be settled. It would need at least four of the thirteen judges to agree before accepting any case. When a case is accepted, the panel must briefly explain why in writing. The panel would meet at least once a month and could work electronically. At the end of each term, it would release a public report with statistics on how many petitions it reviewed and accepted.

This bill would affect anyone who petitions the Supreme Court to hear their case, since a new body rather than the Justices themselves would be making that gateway decision. It also directly changes how the Supreme Court operates as an institution. The nine Justices would still hear and decide every accepted case, but they would lose their current control over which cases land on their schedule in the first place.
ai_headline: New 13-judge panel would screen Supreme Court cases under HR 8992
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8992 would transfer Supreme Court case selection to a rotating 13-judge panel drawn from federal appeals courts.
ai_sections.what: HR 8992 would create a 13-judge rotating panel of federal appeals court judges to decide which cases the Supreme Court hears, replacing the current system in which the nine Justices make that decision themselves. One judge would be randomly selected from each of the 13 U.S. circuit courts, and no judge could serve two consecutive terms. At least four of the thirteen panel judges would need to agree before a case could be accepted, and the panel would be required to briefly explain each acceptance in writing.
ai_sections.who: Anyone who files a petition asking the Supreme Court to review their case would be affected, since the new panel rather than the Justices would make that gateway decision. The nine Supreme Court Justices would retain full authority to hear and decide every accepted case but would lose control over which cases reach their docket.
ai_sections.why: Separating the screening function from the Justices changes the institutional structure of the Supreme Court by placing an intermediate body between petitioners and the Court. Cases approved by the panel would automatically be placed on the Supreme Court's docket, removing Justices' discretion at the intake stage.

title: SHADOW Act
ai_summary:
This bill focuses on a specific but important part of how the Supreme Court operates. Right now, when a case is being appealed, the Supreme Court or an individual justice can issue a "stay," which temporarily pauses or freezes a lower court's ruling while the appeal is being decided. The Court can also issue special orders called writs. Currently, the justices do not have to publicly explain their reasons when granting or denying these stays and writs. This bill would require them to write out and publicly post their reasons on the Court's official docket, meaning anyone could read why the Court made that decision. In urgent situations, they would have up to seven days to publish their explanation.

The bill also sets rules for what the Court must consider before granting a stay. The person asking for a stay would need to show they face a real, serious harm that cannot be undone. The Court would also have to weigh whether the stay would hurt other people involved in the case and whether granting it serves the public interest. Additionally, these stay decisions could not be used as legal precedents in future, unrelated cases, and they could not be used to signal how the Court might ultimately rule on the full case.

This bill primarily affects the Supreme Court justices themselves and the lawyers and parties involved in high-stakes federal cases where stays or emergency orders are sought. More broadly, it affects any American with an interest in knowing how the Court makes these temporary but often consequential decisions. Critics and supporters of the Court's current practices would both be affected, since the bill is essentially about transparency and accountability at the highest level of the judicial system.
ai_headline: Bill would force Supreme Court to publicly explain stays and emergency orders
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8991 would require Supreme Court justices to post written reasons for stay and writ decisions on the public docket.
ai_sections.what: HR 8991 would require Supreme Court justices to publicly post written explanations whenever they grant or deny a stay or writ, with up to seven days to publish in urgent situations. The bill also establishes standards the Court must apply before granting a stay, including proof of serious irreversible harm, impact on other parties, and the public interest. Stay decisions made under these rules could not be used as legal precedents or as signals of how the Court might rule on the underlying case.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects Supreme Court justices and the lawyers and parties involved in high-stakes federal cases seeking stays or emergency orders. Any member of the public with an interest in how the Court makes these consequential temporary decisions is also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without a written-explanation requirement, the public currently has no formal way to understand the reasoning behind stay and writ decisions, which can have immediate and significant real-world effects. The bill introduces a transparency and accountability mechanism at the highest level of the federal judiciary, which could alter how justices approach and communicate these decisions.

title: FAFO Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes the legal definition of "domestic terrorism" under federal law. Currently, domestic terrorism requires acts that are dangerous to human life. This bill would expand that definition to also include acts that cause significant property damage. On top of that, the act being committed must either block a law enforcement officer or first responder from doing their job, or prevent someone from getting medical care.

This bill would most directly affect people who participate in protests, blockades, or demonstrations that damage property and interfere with police, emergency responders, or people trying to reach medical help. If someone is charged under this expanded definition, they could face the serious legal consequences that come with a domestic terrorism label, which can include heavier penalties and broader federal involvement in prosecution.

It is worth noting that existing federal and state laws already cover property damage and obstruction separately. What this bill changes is the threshold for when those actions could be treated as terrorism under federal law. Civil liberties organizations and law enforcement groups would likely have different views on how this change should be applied in practice, and courts would ultimately decide how broadly or narrowly to interpret it.
ai_headline: Federal terrorism definition would expand to cover property damage and obstruction
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8843 would let federal law treat property-damaging protests as domestic terrorism if they also block police, emergency responders, or medical access.
ai_sections.what: HR 8843 expands the federal legal definition of domestic terrorism to include acts that cause significant property damage, not just acts dangerous to human life. To meet the new definition, the act must also block a law enforcement officer or first responder from doing their job, or prevent someone from reaching medical care. Existing federal and state laws already address property damage and obstruction separately; this bill raises the threshold at which those acts could be classified as terrorism.
ai_sections.who: This bill most directly affects people who participate in protests, blockades, or demonstrations that damage property and interfere with police, emergency responders, or people seeking medical help. Civil liberties organizations and law enforcement groups would likely interpret and apply this change differently.
ai_sections.why: Someone charged under this expanded definition could face the heavier penalties and broader federal involvement in prosecution that come with a domestic terrorism label. Courts would ultimately determine how broadly or narrowly to interpret the new definition.

title: Right to Representation Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require states to provide free legal representation to both children and parents whenever there is a court case involving child abuse or neglect. That includes cases about foster care, adoption, guardianship, and termination of parental rights. Right now, many states do not guarantee lawyers for parents in these situations, even though the outcomes can be life-changing. Under this bill, states would have to make sure a real attorney-client relationship exists, not just a lawyer who shows up in name only.

The bill ties this requirement to federal foster care funding. States that receive money under the federal foster care and child welfare program would need to follow this rule starting October 1, 2026. If a state needs to pass its own legislation to make this happen, it gets extra time to do so, but it would still eventually need to comply to keep receiving federal funds. Every two years, the Government Accountability Office would report to Congress on how well states are actually carrying out the requirement.

This bill mainly affects families involved in the child welfare system, including parents who are at risk of losing custody of their children and children who have been abused or neglected. It also affects state governments, which would need to set up or expand their legal aid systems to meet the new standard. Court systems and lawyers who work in family law would also be impacted, since demand for representation in these cases could increase significantly.
ai_headline: States would have to provide free lawyers in child welfare cases
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8997 requires states to provide free attorneys to parents and children in all abuse, neglect, and foster care cases or lose federal funds.
ai_sections.what: HR 8997 would require states to guarantee free legal representation for both children and parents in court cases involving child abuse, neglect, foster care, adoption, guardianship, and termination of parental rights. The bill requires a genuine attorney-client relationship, not merely a lawyer present in name only. States must comply starting October 1, 2026, with extra time granted if state legislation is needed to implement the requirement.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects parents at risk of losing custody, children who have been abused or neglected, and state governments that would need to build or expand legal aid systems. Family law attorneys and court systems would also be affected as demand for representation in these cases could increase significantly.
ai_sections.why: Many states currently do not guarantee lawyers for parents in child welfare cases, even when outcomes such as loss of custody or termination of parental rights are at stake. The Government Accountability Office would report to Congress every two years on how well states are carrying out the requirement.
ai_sections.cost: States that receive federal foster care and child welfare funding would need to meet the new legal representation standard to keep receiving those funds. States failing to comply would risk losing that federal money.
costChips: Tied to federal foster care funds
States risk losing funds if noncompliant

title: Restoring Overtime Pay Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules about which salaried workers must be paid overtime. Under current law, employers do not have to pay overtime to workers in executive, administrative, or professional roles if those employees earn above a certain salary. This bill would set that salary cutoff at $45,000 when the law takes effect, then raise it each year — to $55,000 in 2027, $65,000 in 2028, and $75,000 in 2029. Starting in 2030, the cutoff would automatically adjust each year to match the 55th percentile of what full-time salaried workers earn nationally, so it keeps pace with wages over time without needing new legislation.

The bill also changes another rule about job duties. Right now, some employees can be classified as exempt from overtime even if a large portion of their actual work is non-managerial or non-professional tasks. This bill tightens that standard, requiring that at least 20 percent of an exempt employee's time must be spent on non-exempt tasks before an employer can use that as a reason to deny overtime — effectively making it harder for employers to avoid overtime pay by claiming a job title without matching responsibilities.

This bill primarily affects salaried workers who are currently classified as exempt from overtime but earn below these new thresholds. It also affects employers who would need to either raise those workers' salaries or begin paying them overtime for hours worked beyond 40 per week. Workers in management-titled roles who spend significant time on non-managerial tasks could also gain overtime eligibility under the updated duties rules.
ai_headline: Overtime pay threshold would rise to $75,000 by 2029 under HR 8868
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8868 raises the overtime exemption salary cutoff from $45,000 to $75,000 by 2029 and tightens the job-duties test.
ai_sections.what: HR 8868 raises the salary level below which employers must pay overtime to workers in executive, administrative, or professional roles, setting it at $45,000 upon enactment, then $55,000 in 2027, $65,000 in 2028, and $75,000 in 2029. From 2030 onward, the threshold adjusts automatically each year to match the 55th percentile of full-time salaried workers' earnings nationally. The bill also tightens the job-duties test, requiring that at least 20 percent of an exempt employee's work time be spent on non-exempt tasks before an employer can use job duties as a basis to deny overtime.
ai_sections.who: Salaried workers currently classified as overtime-exempt but earning below the new thresholds are directly affected, as are workers in management-titled roles who spend significant time on non-managerial tasks. Employers who rely on the current exemption rules would need to either raise affected workers' salaries or begin paying overtime for hours worked beyond 40 per week.
ai_sections.why: Employers face the choice of increasing salaries or paying overtime to a broader set of workers, raising labor costs for those currently relying on the exemption. The automatic annual adjustment starting in 2030 removes the need for future legislation to keep the threshold aligned with national wage levels.

title: One Giant LEAP Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of Transportation to build an online portal for companies that want to launch rockets or other commercial spacecraft. Right now, those companies have to go through a licensing process with the government, and this bill would move that process to a single electronic system. The portal would need to be up and running within one year of the bill becoming law.

The portal would handle the full licensing workflow in one place. Companies could submit applications, send and receive messages with government officials, upload documents, and track any changes to their application. When the government needs to consult with other federal agencies as part of its review, those communications would be logged in the system too. The portal would also show both the applicant and the government key dates, such as when the application was submitted, how long the review is taking, and whether it was approved or denied.

Part of the information in the portal would be visible to the general public, including application statuses and key dates in the review process. The people most directly affected are companies in the commercial space launch industry, such as private rocket companies seeking government approval to conduct launches. The Department of Transportation, specifically the office that oversees commercial space, would be responsible for building and maintaining the system. Ordinary Americans would mainly be affected indirectly, through greater public visibility into how the government handles these approvals.
ai_headline: DOT ordered to build online portal for commercial rocket launch licenses
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8851 requires DOT to launch a single online portal for commercial spacecraft licensing within one year of enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 8851 directs the Department of Transportation to build a single electronic portal for the commercial space launch licensing process. The portal must be operational within one year of the bill becoming law. It would handle applications, document uploads, government communications, agency consultations, and tracking of key review dates in one place.
ai_sections.who: Private rocket companies and commercial spacecraft firms seeking government launch approval are the primary parties affected. The Department of Transportation, specifically its commercial space oversight office, is responsible for building and maintaining the system.
ai_sections.why: Moving the licensing process to a single system would consolidate what is currently a multi-step government review into one trackable workflow. Part of the portal would be publicly visible, giving ordinary Americans greater insight into how the government processes commercial launch approvals.

title: Border Patrol Supervisors Retention Act
ai_summary:
This bill would extend a special overtime pay benefit to higher-ranking U.S. Border Patrol agents. Right now, only agents at the GS-12 pay grade can receive this particular overtime pay rate. The bill would expand that to include agents at grades GS-13, GS-14, and GS-15 as well, which are supervisor and senior-level positions. The name of the bill suggests the main goal is to keep experienced supervisors from leaving the agency by making their pay more competitive.

The people directly affected are mid-to-upper-level Border Patrol supervisors who currently work overtime but do not qualify for this specific pay boost. If passed, these agents would see higher earnings when they work beyond their regular scheduled hours. It would also affect the federal budget, since paying more overtime to a broader group of employees would increase government spending on Border Patrol salaries.

Everyday Americans would not feel a direct change in their paychecks or daily lives, but the bill could indirectly affect border security operations. The idea is that better pay might encourage experienced supervisors to stay in their jobs longer rather than retiring early or taking other positions, which supporters argue could lead to more stable and effective leadership within the Border Patrol.
ai_headline: Border Patrol supervisor overtime pay would expand under HR 8956
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8956 would extend a special overtime pay rate to Border Patrol agents at GS-13, GS-14, and GS-15, up from GS-12 only.
ai_sections.what: HR 8956 would expand eligibility for a special overtime pay rate within U.S. Border Patrol. Currently, only agents at the GS-12 pay grade qualify for this rate. The bill would add GS-13, GS-14, and GS-15 grades, which cover supervisor and senior-level positions.
ai_sections.who: The agents directly affected are mid-to-upper-level Border Patrol supervisors who work overtime but do not currently qualify for this specific pay boost. Everyday Americans would not see a direct change in their paychecks or daily lives.
ai_sections.why: The bill is framed around retaining experienced supervisors who might otherwise retire early or take other positions. Supporters argue that more stable supervisory leadership could affect the effectiveness of border security operations.
ai_sections.cost: Extending the overtime pay rate to a broader group of employees would increase federal government spending on Border Patrol salaries. No specific dollar amount is identified in the summary.
costChips: Raises federal Border Patrol salary spending

title: CRUSADE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would add a new requirement for certain immigrants applying for a specific category of work-based visas. Under current law, Section 203(b)(4) of the Immigration and Nationality Act covers visas for special immigrants, which include groups like religious workers and certain long-term foreign employees of the U.S. government. If this bill became law, anyone applying for that category of visa would have to formally disavow sharia law and swear an oath to uphold the U.S. Constitution before being approved.

The bill directly affects foreign nationals who are trying to enter the United States through this particular visa category. Sharia law refers to a code of religious law based on Islamic teachings that many Muslims follow in varying ways in their personal and religious lives. Critics of the bill argue it singles out Muslim immigrants, while supporters argue it is a security and loyalty measure. The bill does not define what "disavowing sharia law" would mean in practice or explain how compliance would be verified.

The bill was introduced in the House of Representatives in May 2026 and sent to the House Judiciary Committee, which is the standard first step in the legislative process. It has not become law. Broader legal questions exist about whether such a religious test for immigrants would conflict with the First Amendment or other constitutional protections, though those debates would play out in courts if the bill ever passed.
ai_headline: House bill would require visa applicants to renounce sharia law
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8756 would bar Section 203(b)(4) visa applicants who won't renounce sharia law and swear a constitutional oath.
ai_sections.what: HR 8756 would add a new requirement to the Section 203(b)(4) visa category under the Immigration and Nationality Act, which covers special immigrants such as religious workers and certain long-term U.S. government employees abroad. Applicants would have to formally disavow sharia law and swear an oath to uphold the U.S. Constitution before being approved. The bill does not define what disavowing sharia law means in practice or explain how compliance would be verified.
ai_sections.who: The requirement would apply to foreign nationals applying for visas under Section 203(b)(4) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. Critics argue the bill singles out Muslim immigrants; supporters describe it as a security and loyalty measure.
ai_sections.why: Broader legal questions exist about whether a religious test for immigrants would conflict with the First Amendment or other constitutional protections, though those debates would only play out in courts if the bill passed. The bill was introduced in May 2026 and referred to the House Judiciary Committee; it has not become law.

title: Traumatic Births Research Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the federal government to fund and conduct research on traumatic birth experiences and PTSD related to childbirth. Specifically, it authorizes up to $1 million per year from 2027 through 2031 for studies on how difficult or traumatic births affect mothers' mental health, how those experiences impact babies and families, and how traumatic births connect to serious medical complications during or after delivery. The bill also requires that research findings be broken down by race and ethnicity, so that differences in outcomes across different groups can be identified.

The bill also creates a separate pilot program that funds research comparing midwife-led care to standard medical care during childbirth. The goal is to find out whether one approach leads to fewer traumatic birth experiences or better mental health outcomes for mothers. The Department of Health and Human Services would oversee both efforts and must report progress to Congress by 2028 and again by 2030.

This bill would primarily affect pregnant and postpartum women, their newborns, and their families. It also affects researchers and healthcare providers, including midwives and hospitals, who might apply for grants. States, Native American tribes, and tribal organizations are specifically named as eligible to receive funding. The bill does not directly change how any individual receives care, but its findings could eventually influence medical guidelines and healthcare policy.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets traumatic birth research with $1M yearly grants
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8869 funds up to $1M/year (2027–2031) to study traumatic birth and postpartum PTSD, with a separate midwife-care pilot program.
ai_sections.what: HR 8869 directs the federal government to fund research on traumatic childbirth experiences and PTSD related to childbirth, including effects on mothers, newborns, and families, and links to serious medical complications. A separate pilot program would fund studies comparing midwife-led care to standard medical care to assess whether either approach reduces traumatic births or improves maternal mental health. Research findings must be broken down by race and ethnicity to identify differences in outcomes across groups.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects pregnant and postpartum women, their newborns, and their families. Researchers, healthcare providers including midwives and hospitals, states, Native American tribes, and tribal organizations are eligible to apply for grants.
ai_sections.why: Results could eventually influence medical guidelines and healthcare policy around childbirth. The Department of Health and Human Services must report progress to Congress by 2028 and again by 2030, creating structured accountability for how findings develop over time.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $1 million per year from 2027 through 2031 for the research program. Funding may go to states, Native American tribes, tribal organizations, researchers, and healthcare providers through grants.

title: Truck Stop Safety Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires truck stops and similar roadway facilities that receive certain federal funding to post two specific types of safety notices. The first notice must include information about human trafficking, such as a clear definition of what it is, warning signs to look for, and the National Human Trafficking Hotline number. The second notice must include information about suicide prevention, specifically the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline, along with a reminder that calls, texts, and chats to 988 are free, confidential, and available any time of day.

The bill applies to facilities that are being built, reopened, or improved using money from a federal program tied to the MAP-21 transportation law, which funds truck stops and rest areas along highways. It would be the responsibility of whoever is running those construction or improvement projects to make sure the notices are put up in a visible spot inside the facility.

The people most directly affected are truck drivers, travelers, and anyone else who stops at these locations, since they would see the posted information. Facility operators and contractors handling federally funded projects would also be affected, as they would be required to comply with the new posting rules.
ai_headline: Federally funded truck stops would be required to post trafficking and suicide notices
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9004 requires federally funded truck stops to post human trafficking warnings and the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline number.
ai_sections.what: HR 9004 requires truck stops and similar roadway facilities receiving certain federal funding to display two notices: one covering human trafficking definitions, warning signs, and the National Human Trafficking Hotline, and one covering the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline. The bill specifies that calls, texts, and chats to 988 are free, confidential, and available at any hour. The requirement applies to facilities being built, reopened, or improved using money from a federal program tied to the MAP-21 transportation law.
ai_sections.who: Truck drivers, travelers, and others who stop at these facilities would see the posted information. Facility operators and contractors overseeing federally funded construction or improvement projects would be responsible for posting the notices in a visible location inside each facility.
ai_sections.why: People present at covered facilities would have direct access to trafficking warning information and crisis lifeline contact details. Operators and contractors at affected sites would face a new compliance obligation tied to their receipt of federal project funding.

title: A bill to amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to modify the railroad track maintenance credit.
ai_summary:
This bill changes a tax credit that small and mid-sized railroad companies can use when they spend money fixing and maintaining their train tracks. Right now, these railroads can get a tax credit worth $3,500 for every mile of track they maintain. This bill would raise that amount to $6,100 per mile. It would also add a rule that automatically adjusts that number each year to keep up with inflation, so the credit does not lose value over time.

The bill also updates a definition in the tax code. Currently, only spending tied to track conditions as of January 1, 2015 qualifies for the credit. This bill moves that date to January 1, 2024, which means more recent track conditions and maintenance work would count toward the credit. These changes would apply to money spent starting January 1, 2025.

The people most directly affected are the owners and operators of short-line and regional railroads, which are smaller railroad companies that are not the large national carriers. Businesses and communities that depend on those smaller railroads for shipping goods, like farmers, manufacturers, and rural towns, could also be indirectly affected if the credit encourages more track upkeep and keeps those rail lines running safely.
ai_headline: Senate bill would nearly double the per-mile tax credit for small railroads
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1532 raises the small railroad track maintenance tax credit from $3,500 to $6,100 per mile, with annual inflation adjustments starting in 2025.
ai_sections.what: S 1532 raises the per-mile tax credit for track maintenance from $3,500 to $6,100 for small and mid-sized railroad companies. The bill adds an automatic annual inflation adjustment so the credit retains its value over time. It also updates a reference date in the tax code from January 1, 2015 to January 1, 2024, allowing more recent maintenance work to qualify, with all changes taking effect for spending on or after January 1, 2025.
ai_sections.who: Short-line and regional railroad owners and operators are the primary parties affected. Farmers, manufacturers, and rural communities that rely on those smaller railroads to ship goods could also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: The higher credit and inflation adjustment could influence how much small railroads invest in track upkeep going forward. Communities and businesses dependent on those rail lines may see effects on service reliability and safety depending on how railroads respond to the change.
ai_sections.cost: The bill increases a tax credit available to small and mid-sized railroads from $3,500 to $6,100 per mile of maintained track, reducing the amount those companies owe in federal taxes, with the change applying to spending beginning January 1, 2025.
costChips: Credit raised from $3,500 to $6,100 per mile

title: Nursing is a Professional Degree Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how nursing degrees are classified under federal student loan rules. Right now, nursing students can only borrow a limited amount in federal loans because nursing is not officially counted as a "professional degree" the way law, medicine, or dentistry are. This bill would add nursing — along with several specific degree types like the MSN, DNP, and Ph.D. in nursing — to the official list of professional degrees in federal education law. That change would allow nursing students to qualify for higher federal loan limits.

The main practical effect is on how much money nursing graduate students can borrow through the federal Direct Loan program. Students in programs that are already on the professional degree list, like medical or dental school, can borrow more than students in programs that are not on the list. By adding nursing to that list, nursing students pursuing advanced degrees would be able to access those same higher borrowing limits to help cover the cost of their programs.

This bill would affect people currently enrolled in or planning to enroll in graduate-level nursing programs, such as nurse practitioners, nurse anesthetists, and nursing researchers. It could also affect nursing schools and hospitals that train advanced practice nurses, since easier access to loans might make those programs more affordable and attract more students. It does not directly change tuition costs or grant money — it only affects how much a student can borrow in federal loans.
ai_headline: Senate bill would reclassify nursing as a professional degree for federal loans
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4568 would add nursing graduate degrees to the federal professional degree list, unlocking higher Direct Loan borrowing limits.
ai_sections.what: S 4568 would add nursing — including the MSN, DNP, and Ph.D. in nursing — to the official list of professional degrees under federal education law. That classification change would qualify nursing graduate students for the same higher federal Direct Loan borrowing limits available to students in law, medicine, and dentistry. The bill does not change tuition costs or grant funding, only the amount a student may borrow.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects people enrolled in or planning to enroll in graduate-level nursing programs, including nurse practitioners, nurse anesthetists, and nursing researchers. Nursing schools and hospitals that train advanced practice nurses may also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Because nursing is not currently classified as a professional degree under federal law, nursing graduate students face lower federal loan limits than students in law, medicine, or dental programs. Adding nursing to the professional degree list could affect enrollment in advanced nursing programs and the pipeline of advanced practice nurses.

title: Loan Forgiveness for Educators Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would expand student loan forgiveness for educators who work in high-need schools or early childhood education programs. Under the bill, a qualifying educator who completes 5 years of service would have 100 percent of their remaining federal student loan balance wiped out, including interest and fees. On top of that full forgiveness, the government would also cover the educator's minimum monthly loan payment during every month they are actively working in a qualifying position, even during summer breaks. The 5 years of service do not need to be back-to-back, and past service can count toward the requirement. If an educator leaves before finishing a school year due to medical leave, military duty, or a disaster, that year can still count.

A high-need school is generally defined as a public school where more than 30 percent of students live in poverty, or a school flagged by the state as needing improvement. Qualifying early childhood programs include Head Start, federally subsidized child care centers, family child care providers, and Tribal or Native Hawaiian programs. To be eligible, teachers and school leaders must hold full state or Tribal certification, though educators who teach Native languages are exempt from that requirement. Parents who took out federal PLUS loans to pay for a child's education can also qualify if either the parent or the child is the qualifying educator.

This bill primarily affects teachers, school principals, early childhood educators, child care center directors, and family child care providers who carry federal student loan debt. It would also affect parents who borrowed federal loans on behalf of children who become educators. Schools, child care programs, and local education agencies in lower-income communities would be involved in verifying workers' service. The federal government would take on the cost of the loan payments and forgiveness. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on.
ai_headline: Senate bill would erase all federal student debt for teachers at high-need schools
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4567 would fully cancel federal student loans for educators at high-need schools or early childhood programs after 5 years of service.
ai_sections.what: S 4567 would cancel 100 percent of remaining federal student loan balances, including interest and fees, for qualifying educators who complete 5 years of service at high-need schools or eligible early childhood programs. The government would also cover the educator's minimum monthly loan payment during every month they are actively working in a qualifying position, including summer breaks. The 5 years of service do not need to be consecutive, and prior service can count toward the requirement.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects teachers, school principals, early childhood educators, child care center directors, and family child care providers who carry federal student loan debt. Parents who took out federal PLUS loans on behalf of a child who becomes a qualifying educator are also eligible.
ai_sections.why: Schools, child care programs, and local education agencies in lower-income communities would take on a role in verifying workers' service records. The bill has been referred to committee in the Senate and has not yet been voted on.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would bear the cost of both the monthly loan payments made during active qualifying service and the full loan forgiveness at the 5-year mark.
costChips: Federal government pays monthly loan costs
Full loan balance wiped by federal government

title: REDI Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change the rules for federal student loans held by doctors and dentists who are completing their required internship or residency training after medical or dental school. Right now, if those borrowers pause their loan payments during residency, interest still builds up on their loans. This bill would stop that interest from growing during the deferment period, meaning their loan balance would stay the same rather than getting larger while they are in training.

Residency and internship programs typically last three to seven years, and residents are generally paid modest salaries compared to their future earnings as fully licensed doctors or dentists. Many carry very large student loan balances from medical or dental school. Under current law, choosing to defer payments during this lower-income period can cost them significantly because interest keeps adding up. This bill would remove that extra cost.

The bill affects medical and dental residents and interns who have federal student loans. It does not apply to private student loans or to other types of graduate or professional borrowers. It was introduced in the Senate in March 2025 and has been sent to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions, where it would need to be approved before moving forward.
ai_headline: Senate bill would freeze interest on medical residents' federal loans during training
last_action_text: Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 942 would stop interest from accruing on federal student loans for medical and dental residents during deferment.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 942 would eliminate interest accrual on federal student loans during deferment periods for medical and dental residents and interns. Under current law, interest continues to build on deferred loans, increasing the total balance owed. This bill would keep the loan balance flat during the training period rather than allowing it to grow.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to medical and dental residents and interns who hold federal student loans. It does not cover borrowers with private student loans or other graduate and professional borrowers.
ai_sections.why: Residency and internship programs typically last three to seven years, during which trainees earn modest salaries relative to their future income while carrying large loan balances from medical or dental school. Under current law, deferring payments during this lower-income period results in a larger loan balance by the time training ends.

title: Renewable Fuel for Ocean-Going Vessels Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes a rule under the Clean Air Act to allow renewable fuels used in large ocean-going ships to earn credits under the federal Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) program.
