The RFS is an existing government program that requires a certain amount of renewable fuel, like ethanol or biodiesel, to be blended into the nation's fuel supply each year. Companies that blend renewable fuels earn tradeable credits for doing so. Right now, those credits can be earned for renewable fuels used in transportation, home heating oil, and jet fuel, but not for fuel used in large cargo ships and ocean vessels. This bill adds ocean-going vessel fuel to that list.

If the bill passes, the EPA would have one year to write new regulations to make it work, and the changes would take effect starting the second full calendar year after the law is enacted. The EPA would also have to report back to Congress on how the program is going within a year of finalizing those rules.

This bill mainly affects fuel producers and blenders who make renewable fuels, shipping companies that operate large ocean-going vessels, and the EPA, which would oversee the expanded program. Fuel producers could potentially earn more credits by selling renewable fuel to the shipping industry, which might encourage more use of renewable fuels in that sector. Ordinary consumers are not directly affected, though changes in the shipping industry's fuel use can have broader effects on goods transportation costs over time.
ai_headline: Senate bill would extend renewable fuel credits to ocean shipping
last_action_text: Committee on Environment and Public Works. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 881 adds ocean-going vessels to the Renewable Fuel Standard, letting shippers earn credits for using renewable fuel.
ai_sections.what: S 881 amends the Clean Air Act to let renewable fuels used in large ocean-going ships earn credits under the federal Renewable Fuel Standard program. Currently, RFS credits can be earned for renewable fuels used in transportation, home heating oil, and jet fuel, but not for ocean vessel fuel. The bill adds ocean-going vessel fuel to the eligible list, with the EPA given one year to write implementing regulations and changes taking effect in the second full calendar year after enactment.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects fuel producers and blenders who make renewable fuels, shipping companies that operate large ocean-going vessels, and the EPA, which would oversee the expanded program. Ordinary consumers are not directly affected by the bill's provisions.
ai_sections.why: Fuel producers could potentially earn more RFS credits by selling renewable fuel to the shipping industry, which may encourage greater renewable fuel use in that sector. The EPA would also be required to report to Congress on program implementation within one year of finalizing its new rules.

title: STOP CSAM Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill, called the STOP CSAM Act of 2025, targets child sexual abuse material (CSAM) online and in federal courts. It updates existing laws to better protect child victims during court proceedings. For example, it expands privacy protections so that personal information about child victims and witnesses is kept confidential by default. It also clarifies legal definitions around abuse and exploitation and requires that court records involving child victims be handled more carefully. Courts would be required to appoint advocates to represent child victims, and more money would be authorized to fund those roles.

The bill puts new legal obligations on tech companies and online platforms. Large platforms would have to report child sexual abuse material to the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children faster and more completely than current law requires. Companies that fail to report or that knowingly host this material could face fines ranging from hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars. Platforms would also have to file annual public reports describing what steps they take to detect and prevent child exploitation on their services.

The bill also makes it easier for victims to sue for money damages. Child victims could sue not just individual abusers but also online platforms and app stores if those companies intentionally or recklessly helped enable the abuse or hosted the illegal material. Victims could receive at least $300,000 in guaranteed damages plus attorney fees, and there would be no time limit for filing these lawsuits. The bill affects tech companies of all sizes, federal courts, law enforcement agencies, and people who were abused as children and are seeking justice.
ai_headline: STOP CSAM Act would let abuse survivors sue platforms for at least $300,000
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 106.

ai_sections.tldr: The STOP CSAM Act of 2025 lets child abuse survivors sue online platforms and removes the filing deadline for those lawsuits.
ai_sections.what: The STOP CSAM Act of 2025 requires large online platforms to report child sexual abuse material to the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children faster and more completely than current law requires. Courts would be required to appoint advocates for child victims and keep their personal information confidential by default. Child victims could sue platforms and app stores that intentionally or recklessly enabled abuse or hosted illegal material, with no time limit for filing.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects tech companies of all sizes, federal courts, and law enforcement agencies. People who were abused as children and are seeking justice are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Platforms that fail to report or knowingly host child sexual abuse material could face substantial fines, creating financial pressure to comply. Survivors gain a path to civil damages that did not previously exist against platforms, which could reshape how companies approach content moderation.
ai_sections.cost: Companies that fail to report or knowingly host child sexual abuse material face fines ranging from hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars. Victims who sue successfully are guaranteed at least $300,000 in damages plus attorney fees. Additional funding would be authorized to support court-appointed advocates for child victims.

title: Reauthorizing Support and Treatment for Officers in Crisis Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill renews a federal grant program that provides mental health support to law enforcement officers and their families. The original program was set to expire after 2024, so this bill simply extends its funding authorization through 2029. It does not create a new program or change how the existing one works.

The grants help police departments and other law enforcement agencies pay for things like mental health counseling, crisis intervention services, and support programs aimed at reducing officer suicide and burnout. The money can also go toward helping officers' families who may be dealing with the stress and trauma that often comes with law enforcement work.

This bill directly affects law enforcement agencies across the country, as well as the officers and their families who rely on these mental health programs. It was introduced with support from senators of both parties, and any actual funding would still need to be approved separately through the regular budget process.
ai_headline: Senate bill extends law enforcement mental health grants through 2029
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 79.

ai_sections.tldr: S 419 extends an expiring federal grant program for law enforcement mental health services through 2029 without changing how it works.
ai_sections.what: S 419 extends the authorization of an existing federal grant program for law enforcement mental health support through 2029. The program was set to expire after 2024. It does not create a new program or alter the structure of the existing one.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects law enforcement agencies across the country that use these grants to fund mental health services. Officers and their families who rely on counseling, crisis intervention, and suicide and burnout prevention programs are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without reauthorization, the grant program would have expired after 2024, ending a funding pathway agencies have used for mental health services. Any actual funding still requires separate approval through the regular federal budget process.
ai_sections.cost: The bill reauthorizes the grant program, which can pay for mental health counseling, crisis intervention services, and family support programs. No specific funding amount is stated; appropriations must be approved separately.
costChips: Final amounts set by separate budget vote

title: National Plan for Epilepsy Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the federal government to create a formal, written plan for tackling epilepsy in the United States. The plan would be run by the Secretary of Health and Human Services and would coordinate epilepsy-related work across many federal agencies, including the NIH, CDC, FDA, and the Departments of Defense and Veterans Affairs. The goal is to improve how the government handles epilepsy research, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention, and to track how much federal money is being spent on it. The plan would run until the end of 2035.

The bill also creates an Advisory Council made up of people from both inside and outside the government. That includes federal agency representatives, doctors, researchers, people who personally live with epilepsy, family caregivers, and leaders from epilepsy nonprofit groups. The council would meet at least four times a year, hold public meetings, and regularly send reports to Congress with recommendations on what the government should do better. The Secretary would also send Congress yearly progress reports.

This bill most directly affects the roughly 3.4 million Americans living with epilepsy, as well as their families and caregivers. It could also affect doctors and researchers who work on epilepsy, and federal agencies that would need to share data and coordinate with each other. The bill does not directly fund new treatments or programs on its own, but it sets up a structure meant to make existing and future government efforts more organized and effective.
ai_headline: Federal epilepsy plan and advisory council proposed through 2035
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 494 would require a national epilepsy action plan through 2035 and a new advisory council reporting to Congress.
ai_sections.what: S 494 directs the Secretary of Health and Human Services to create a formal written plan coordinating epilepsy research, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention across federal agencies including NIH, CDC, FDA, and the Departments of Defense and Veterans Affairs. The plan would run through the end of 2035. The bill also establishes an Advisory Council that must meet at least four times a year, hold public meetings, and submit regular recommendations to Congress.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects the roughly 3.4 million Americans living with epilepsy, along with their families and caregivers. It would also affect doctors, researchers, and federal agencies required to share data and coordinate efforts.
ai_sections.why: The bill creates a structured coordination mechanism for existing and future federal epilepsy efforts but does not establish or fund new programs on its own. The Secretary would be required to submit yearly progress reports to Congress, adding an ongoing accountability layer to federal epilepsy policy.

title: Health Care Fairness for Military Families Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to TRICARE Young Adult, which is the health insurance program that allows adult children of military service members to stay on their parent's military health coverage. First, it removes a rule that currently cuts off eligibility for young adults who qualify for health insurance through their own employer. Right now, if a military family's adult child is offered job-based insurance at work, they lose access to TRICARE Young Adult even if they don't take that employer coverage. This bill would eliminate that restriction.

Second, the bill removes the separate monthly premium that young adults currently have to pay to be enrolled in TRICARE Young Adult. Under current law, these young adults pay their own premium on top of whatever their military parent already pays for family coverage. This bill would get rid of that extra charge, making the coverage free for eligible young adults just as it is for younger dependents on standard TRICARE plans.

The people most directly affected are adult children of active duty service members, National Guard members, and military retirees who are between the ages of 21 and 26. Currently about 600,000 to 700,000 young adults fall into this general age range within military families. The change would also affect the Department of Defense, which would absorb the cost of coverage that young adults currently pay for themselves through premiums.
ai_headline: Bill would drop premiums and employer rule for military dependents' health plan
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2448 would make TRICARE Young Adult free and available even when a young adult is offered employer insurance.
ai_sections.what: S 2448 makes two changes to TRICARE Young Adult, the program letting adult children of military members stay on family coverage. It removes the rule that cuts off eligibility when a young adult is offered employer-based insurance, even if they do not enroll in it. It also eliminates the separate monthly premium that enrolled young adults currently pay on top of what their military parent already pays.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects adult children of active duty service members, National Guard members, and military retirees between the ages of 21 and 26. Approximately 600,000 to 700,000 young adults in military families fall within that age range.
ai_sections.why: Removing the employer-offer restriction would extend eligibility to young adults who currently lose access simply by being offered a job-based plan. Eliminating the premium would shift the cost of that coverage from enrolled young adults to the Department of Defense.
ai_sections.cost: The Department of Defense would absorb the cost of coverage that eligible young adults currently pay through their own monthly premiums.

title: Stop CCP Money Laundering Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill tells two U.S. government agencies to investigate Hong Kong's financial system and report their findings to Congress. First, the Treasury Department has 180 days to decide whether Hong Kong should be officially labeled a "primary money laundering concern," which is a serious designation that can trigger restrictions on how U.S. banks do business there. Second, within a year, the State Department, working with Treasury and Commerce, must produce a detailed report on whether banks in Hong Kong are being used to move money or send restricted technology to countries like Russia and Iran in ways that break U.S. law.

The report must also look at whether Chinese national security laws passed in 2020 and 2024 have made it harder for banks in Hong Kong to follow standard anti-money laundering rules, such as verifying who their customers really are. It must also cover how well Hong Kong and U.S. authorities are working together to enforce trade and financial penalties.

This bill does not directly ban any transactions or punish anyone right now. It mainly affects government agencies that will have to do the research and write the reports. Down the road, American banks and businesses that operate in Hong Kong could be affected if the investigations lead to new restrictions. U.S. consumers would not feel a direct impact from the bill itself, but the findings could eventually influence broader U.S. trade and financial policy.
ai_headline: Senate bill orders U.S. agencies to probe Hong Kong money laundering ties
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1339 directs Treasury and State to investigate Hong Kong's financial system for money laundering and sanctions evasion within one year.
ai_sections.what: S 1339 requires the Treasury Department to determine within 180 days whether Hong Kong warrants a "primary money laundering concern" designation. Within one year, the State Department, working with Treasury and Commerce, must report on whether Hong Kong banks are being used to move money or transfer restricted technology to countries such as Russia and Iran in violation of U.S. law. The report must also assess whether Chinese national security laws passed in 2020 and 2024 have weakened standard anti-money laundering practices in Hong Kong.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly requires action from the Treasury Department, the State Department, and the Commerce Department. American banks and businesses operating in Hong Kong could face consequences if the investigations lead to new restrictions.
ai_sections.why: A "primary money laundering concern" designation can trigger restrictions on how U.S. banks conduct business in Hong Kong. The findings could eventually influence broader U.S. trade and financial policy, though the bill imposes no direct bans or penalties at this time.

title: Streamline Transit Projects Act
ai_summary:
This bill would let large local transit agencies take over certain environmental review duties that the federal government currently handles. Specifically, it deals with something called "categorical exclusions," which are routine transit projects that have already been determined to cause little to no environmental harm and therefore do not require a full environmental study. Right now, a federal agency signs off on these determinations. Under this bill, qualifying transit agencies could do that work themselves, as long as they sign an agreement with the federal government and prove they have the staff, legal expertise, and money to handle it properly.

To qualify, a transit agency must serve an urban area with more than 200,000 people and meet certain standards set by the U.S. Department of Transportation. The agency would take on full legal responsibility for making sure environmental rules are followed, and the federal government would no longer be liable for those decisions. The agreement between the agency and the federal government would be reviewed every three years, or every five years for agencies with at least ten years of experience doing this work. The federal government can take back control if an agency is not meeting its responsibilities.

This bill mainly affects large city and regional transit agencies that receive federal funding, as well as the communities those agencies serve. Supporters of this general approach argue it could speed up the start of transit construction projects by cutting down on waiting time for federal approvals. Critics of similar policies have raised concerns about whether local oversight provides the same level of environmental protection as federal review. Everyday riders, local residents near transit projects, and taxpayers who fund public transportation could all be affected depending on how the change plays out in practice.
ai_headline: Large transit agencies could self-approve routine environmental reviews
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 3284 would let large transit agencies handle their own federal environmental sign-offs for routine, low-impact projects.
ai_sections.what: S 3284 would allow large local transit agencies to take over from the federal government the task of approving categorical exclusions, which are routine transit projects already determined to cause little or no environmental harm. To qualify, an agency must serve an urban area of more than 200,000 people and meet U.S. Department of Transportation standards. The agency must sign an agreement with the federal government, demonstrate adequate staff, legal expertise, and funding, and accept full legal responsibility for environmental compliance.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects large city and regional transit agencies that receive federal funding. It also affects communities and residents who live near transit projects, everyday riders, and taxpayers who fund public transportation.
ai_sections.why: Supporters of this approach argue it could reduce waiting time for federal approvals and speed up the start of transit construction projects. Critics have raised concerns about whether local oversight provides the same level of environmental protection as federal review.

title: Power for the People Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tries to stop regular electricity customers from paying higher bills because of the growing power demands of large data centers. Right now, when a massive data center connects to the electric grid, the cost of upgrading power lines and other infrastructure often gets spread across all electricity customers in an area. The bill would require data centers to pay for those upgrade costs themselves. It also tells the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to create a special waiting line, called a load queue, that data centers must go through before connecting to the grid. Data centers that bring their own clean energy sources, agree to have their power cut during emergencies, and pay construction workers fair wages would get moved up in that line.

The bill also pushes states to create separate billing categories just for data centers, rather than lumping them in with homes and businesses. Under these special rate classes, data centers could be required to pay minimum charges based on how much power they asked for, sign longer contracts with utilities, and put up more money upfront when applying to connect to the grid. States would have two years to study and decide whether to adopt these rules. The federal government would offer grants and technical help to states working through this process.

The people most directly affected are data center owners and operators, who would face new costs and requirements before connecting to the grid. Ordinary households and small businesses that pay electric bills could potentially benefit if the policy successfully shifts infrastructure costs away from them. Workers building data centers and power facilities could also be affected, since the bill includes requirements around union-friendly labor agreements and apprenticeship programs for data center construction projects.
ai_headline: Senate bill would make data centers pay their own grid upgrade costs
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 3682 would require data centers to cover grid upgrade costs instead of spreading them to all electricity customers.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 3682 would require data centers to pay for power infrastructure upgrades themselves rather than passing those costs to other ratepayers. The bill would direct the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to create a load queue that data centers must enter before connecting to the grid. Data centers that bring clean energy, accept power cuts during emergencies, and pay fair wages to construction workers would move up in that queue.
ai_sections.who: Data center owners and operators would face new costs and requirements before connecting to the grid. Households and small businesses that pay electric bills could be affected by whether infrastructure costs are shifted away from them, and construction workers are affected through labor and apprenticeship requirements tied to data center projects.
ai_sections.why: Currently, when a large data center connects to the electric grid, infrastructure upgrade costs are often spread across all electricity customers in the area. The bill aims to change who bears those costs, which could alter how utilities and states structure electricity rates and contracts going forward.
ai_sections.cost: Data centers would be required to cover grid infrastructure upgrade costs themselves and put up more money upfront when applying to connect. States would receive federal grants and technical assistance to study and decide whether to adopt separate data center rate classes, a process they would have two years to complete.
costChips: Data centers pay own upgrade costs

title: Biological Intellectual Property Protection Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require American companies and researchers to get a government license before sharing certain biotechnology information with foreign entities from countries considered threats to the United States, like China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. Specifically, it targets digital files containing the designed sequences of synthetic DNA or RNA — basically the blueprints for lab-created genetic material. Right now, those files can often be sent overseas without any special government approval. Under this bill, the Department of Commerce would have one year to set up that licensing system.

The bill is focused on stopping intellectual property theft in the field of synthetic biology, which is an area of science where researchers design new genetic material for purposes like making medicines, agricultural products, or industrial chemicals. Congress is concerned that when a US company sends its custom DNA or RNA sequence designs to a lab or company in a country like China, that information could end up in the hands of foreign military or intelligence services. The bill does include an exception for certain information that is already considered publicly available or freely shared, so basic scientific publishing would not automatically be affected.

The people most directly affected would be US biotechnology companies, university research labs, and scientists who currently share or sell synthetic biology data with partners, customers, or collaborators in countries flagged as national security concerns. Those organizations would need to apply for and receive a government export license before sending that kind of file. Foreign companies and research institutions in countries of concern would face restricted access to US-designed genetic sequence information.
ai_headline: US biotech firms would need export licenses to share DNA designs with rival nations
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3452 would require US companies and researchers to get a Commerce license before sending synthetic DNA/RNA files to China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea.
ai_sections.what: S 3452 would require American companies and researchers to obtain a government export license before sharing digital files of synthetic DNA or RNA sequences with entities in countries designated as national security threats. The Department of Commerce would have one year to build and launch the licensing system. Information already considered publicly available would be exempt from the requirement.
ai_sections.who: US biotechnology companies, university research labs, and scientists who share or sell synthetic biology data with partners in flagged countries would need to apply for licenses before sending that information. Foreign companies and research institutions in countries of concern would face restricted access to US-designed genetic sequence files.
ai_sections.why: Congress has expressed concern that custom DNA and RNA sequence designs sent to countries like China could reach foreign military or intelligence services. Organizations currently sharing synthetic biology data internationally would face a new approval process that did not previously exist for these digital files.

title: AFTER Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes the Animal Welfare Act to require federal agencies that use animals in research to have a plan for retiring those animals when the research is done. Right now, there is no federal requirement for what happens to lab animals after they are no longer needed for experiments. Under this bill, any federal research facility would need to create rules, within 90 days of the law passing, that actively try to place healthy retired animals with rescue organizations, animal sanctuaries, shelters, or private individuals who want them as pets. Non-human primates like monkeys would specifically be placed in sanctuaries rather than private homes.

The bill sets clear definitions for what counts as a legitimate sanctuary or rescue organization. For example, a qualifying sanctuary cannot sell animals, breed them, let the public have direct contact with them, or use them for performances. An animal has to be evaluated by a licensed veterinarian and found mentally and physically healthy before it can be retired through this process. The bill also says agencies should look beyond just nearby organizations and consider placing animals with groups anywhere in the country. Common lab rats and mice are not covered by this bill.

The people most directly affected are the employees and administrators at federal research facilities, who would need to create and follow new retirement procedures. Animal rescue groups and sanctuaries that meet the bill's standards could receive retired research animals. Animals used in federally funded research, such as dogs, cats, rabbits, and primates, would have a clearer path to adoption or sanctuary placement. States are also allowed to pass their own stricter animal retirement laws if they choose.
ai_headline: Federal labs would need retirement plans for research animals under new bill
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S1581-1582)

ai_sections.tldr: S 4172 would require federal research facilities to find homes or sanctuaries for healthy retired lab animals within 90 days of enactment.
ai_sections.what: S 4172 amends the Animal Welfare Act to require federal research facilities to establish retirement procedures within 90 days of the law passing. Healthy animals cleared by a licensed veterinarian could be placed with rescue organizations, shelters, sanctuaries, or private adopters. Non-human primates must go to sanctuaries rather than private homes, and common lab rats and mice are not covered.
ai_sections.who: Employees and administrators at federal research facilities would be responsible for creating and following the new retirement procedures. Qualifying rescue groups and sanctuaries nationwide could receive retired animals such as dogs, cats, rabbits, and primates.
ai_sections.why: Currently no federal requirement exists for what happens to lab animals after research ends. The bill creates a defined pathway that includes national placement searches and specific standards qualifying organizations must meet, such as prohibitions on selling, breeding, public contact, or use of animals in performances.

title: No Rewards for January 6 Rioters Act
ai_summary:
This bill would block the federal government from using taxpayer money to pay or reimburse people who were charged with crimes related to the January 6, 2021 attack on the U.S. Capitol. That includes money from the Judgment Fund, which is a pool of federal money used to pay out certain legal claims against the government. The ban would apply even to people who were later pardoned for their involvement. The bill also prevents the creation of any new fund specifically set up to compensate those individuals.

The bill also addresses money that January 6 convicts already paid to the courts, such as fines or restitution. It says the government cannot refund those payments, even if a person's conviction was later reversed or they received a pardon. Any money that might otherwise be returned would instead go to the Architect of the Capitol, the office responsible for maintaining the Capitol building that was damaged during the attack.

This bill would directly affect people who were prosecuted in connection with January 6 and who might otherwise seek government compensation, particularly those who were pardoned and believe they are owed money back. It would also affect federal agencies that handle legal payouts. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been sent to the Judiciary Committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on or become law.
ai_headline: Senate bill bars federal payouts to pardoned January 6 defendants
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3582 would bar federal compensation to Jan. 6 defendants and block refunds of fines they already paid, even after a pardon.
ai_sections.what: S 3582 would prohibit the federal government from using taxpayer money, including the Judgment Fund, to pay or reimburse people charged in connection with the January 6, 2021 Capitol attack. The bill also bars refunds of fines or restitution those individuals already paid to courts, even if their convictions were later reversed or they received pardons. It additionally blocks the creation of any new fund set up to compensate those individuals.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects people prosecuted in connection with January 6 who might seek government compensation, particularly those who received pardons. It also affects federal agencies that handle legal payouts.
ai_sections.why: Any money that would otherwise be refunded to January 6 defendants would instead go to the Architect of the Capitol, the office responsible for maintaining the Capitol building damaged during the attack. The bill has been referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee and has not yet been voted on or become law.
ai_sections.cost: The bill targets the Judgment Fund, a federal pool of money used to pay legal claims against the government, as a specific source it would block from being used for January 6 defendants. Fines and restitution already paid by those defendants would be redirected to the Architect of the Capitol rather than returned.
costChips: Judgment Fund blocked for Jan. 6 payments
Fines redirected to Architect of the Capitol

title: NOPAIN for Veterans Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the Department of Veterans Affairs to add FDA-approved non-opioid pain medications to its official drug list, called the national formulary. Right now, the VA controls which medications doctors can prescribe to veterans, and new drugs sometimes take a long time to become available through the VA system. Under this bill, once a non-opioid pain medication is approved by the FDA, the VA would have to make it available to veterans within 18 months, or within one year of it qualifying for certain Medicare payment programs, whichever comes first. The VA would have 90 days after the bill becomes law to start following these new rules.

The bill specifically targets non-opioid drugs used to treat pain after surgery or for acute pain conditions. These are medications that work without affecting the brain's opioid receptors, meaning they are not addictive in the same way opioids can be. The goal is to make sure veterans have faster access to these newer pain treatment options as alternatives to opioid-based drugs.

This bill directly affects veterans who receive healthcare through the VA and are dealing with pain management, particularly after surgeries or procedures. It also affects VA doctors, who would have more non-opioid treatment options available to prescribe. The bill includes a note that money from the Cost of War Toxic Exposures Fund, which is set aside for veterans harmed by toxic exposures like burn pits, cannot be used to pay for this program.
ai_headline: Senate bill would speed VA access to non-opioid pain drugs
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3209 requires the VA to add FDA-approved non-opioid pain drugs to its formulary within 18 months of approval.
ai_sections.what: S 3209 requires the Department of Veterans Affairs to add FDA-approved non-opioid pain medications to its national formulary. The VA must make these drugs available within 18 months of FDA approval, or within one year of the drug qualifying for certain Medicare payment programs, whichever comes first. The VA would have 90 days after enactment to begin complying with the new rules.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects veterans who receive VA healthcare and need pain management, particularly after surgeries or for acute pain conditions. VA doctors would also be affected, as they would gain access to a broader set of non-opioid medications to prescribe.
ai_sections.why: Currently, new medications can take a long time to become available through the VA system, and this bill would impose mandatory timelines to shorten that gap. Expanding available non-opioid options could shift how VA providers approach pain treatment for veterans.
ai_sections.cost: The bill specifies that funds from the Cost of War Toxic Exposures Fund, which is reserved for veterans harmed by toxic exposures such as burn pits, cannot be used to pay for this program.
costChips: Toxic Exposures Fund barred from paying for this
Burn pit veterans' fund explicitly excluded

title: Restoring Overtime Pay Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill deals with overtime pay rules for salaried workers. Under current federal law, employees classified as executive, administrative, or professional workers can be exempt from overtime pay requirements if their salary meets a certain minimum. This bill would raise that minimum salary level in steps: starting at $45,000 per year when the law takes effect, then rising to $55,000 in 2027, $65,000 in 2028, and $75,000 in 2029. Starting in 2030, the threshold would automatically adjust each year to match the 55th percentile of what full-time salaried workers earn nationally, so it would keep pace with wages over time without needing new legislation.

The bill also changes a rule about job duties. Right now, some workers can lose overtime protections if a large portion of their work involves non-managerial tasks. This bill tightens that rule, requiring that at least 20 percent of a worker's time be spent on genuine executive or administrative duties in order to qualify for the overtime exemption. The idea is to make it harder for employers to classify workers as managers or professionals just on paper while most of their actual work is routine.

This affects salaried workers across many industries, particularly those earning between roughly $35,000 and $75,000 a year who are currently classified as exempt from overtime. It also affects employers, who may need to either raise salaries to keep certain workers exempt or begin paying those workers overtime. Small businesses with salaried supervisors or office workers could see the most significant changes.
ai_headline: Senate bill would raise overtime salary threshold to $75,000 by 2029
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4551 raises the overtime-exempt salary floor from roughly $35,000 to $75,000 by 2029 and tightens the duties test for exempt workers.
ai_sections.what: S 4551 raises the minimum salary at which salaried workers can be classified as exempt from overtime pay, moving in steps from $45,000 at enactment to $55,000 in 2027, $65,000 in 2028, and $75,000 in 2029. Beginning in 2030, the threshold adjusts automatically each year to match the 55th percentile of full-time salaried worker earnings nationally. The bill also requires that at least 20 percent of a worker's time be spent on genuine executive or administrative duties to qualify for the overtime exemption.
ai_sections.who: Salaried workers currently earning between roughly $35,000 and $75,000 who are classified as overtime-exempt are directly affected, particularly those in executive, administrative, or professional roles. Employers across many industries, especially small businesses with salaried supervisors or office workers, may need to restructure pay or begin paying overtime.
ai_sections.why: Employers who currently classify workers as exempt below the new thresholds would need to either raise those workers' salaries or pay them overtime, changing labor cost structures. The tightened duties test makes it harder for employers to designate workers as managers or professionals on paper when most of their actual work is routine.

title: VISIT USA Act
ai_summary:
This bill would move $160 million from a government account called the Travel Promotion Fund directly to Brand USA, which is a nonprofit organization that markets the United States as a travel destination to people in other countries. The money in that fund comes from fees that foreign visitors pay when they apply to enter the US through the visa waiver program. The bill directs the Treasury Department to make this transfer within 30 days of the law being signed.

Brand USA uses funds like this to run advertising campaigns, attend travel trade shows, and promote American tourism attractions abroad. The bill waives a normal cap on how much can be transferred at once but still requires Brand USA to match the government money with private funding, as existing law already requires. The goal is to encourage more international tourists to visit the US, which supporters argue brings in revenue and supports jobs in the travel and hospitality industry.

This bill would most directly affect Brand USA and the federal Treasury. Indirectly, it could affect businesses in the US tourism industry such as hotels, airlines, and attractions that benefit from international visitors. Foreign travelers who might be influenced by Brand USA marketing campaigns could also be affected. US taxpayers are not directly charged, since the funding comes from fees paid by foreign visa applicants rather than general tax revenue.
ai_headline: Senate bill sends $160 million to US tourism nonprofit within 30 days
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3220 transfers $160M from the Travel Promotion Fund to Brand USA within 30 days of enactment to boost international tourism marketing.
ai_sections.what: S 3220 directs the Treasury Department to transfer $160 million from the Travel Promotion Fund to Brand USA, a nonprofit that markets the US as a travel destination to international audiences. The transfer must occur within 30 days of the law being signed. The bill waives a normal cap on transfer amounts while still requiring Brand USA to match government funds with private money.
ai_sections.who: Brand USA and the federal Treasury are most directly affected. Hotels, airlines, attractions, and other US tourism businesses, as well as foreign travelers influenced by Brand USA campaigns, could be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Supporters contend that more international tourists generate revenue and support jobs in the travel and hospitality industry. The bill's effect on actual tourism levels would depend on the reach and results of Brand USA's marketing campaigns.
ai_sections.cost: The $160 million comes from fees paid by foreign visitors applying to enter the US through the visa waiver program, not from general tax revenue. Brand USA is also required to match the government funds with private funding under existing law.
costChips: Funded by foreign visa waiver fees
Private match required by existing law

title: Title X Abortion Provider Prohibition Act
ai_summary:
Title X is a federal program that pays for family planning services like birth control, STI testing, and health checkups for people with low incomes. This bill would change the rules so that any clinic or health organization performing abortions could no longer receive that federal funding. To keep getting Title X money, a provider would have to certify that it does not perform abortions and does not send any of its own funds to other groups that do. Hospitals are treated slightly differently and can still receive funding as long as they do not pass money along to outside abortion providers.

There are two exceptions built into the bill. An organization can still receive Title X funding and perform abortions if the pregnancy resulted from rape or incest, or if a doctor certifies that continuing the pregnancy would put the woman's life at risk. The bill also requires the federal government to report to Congress each year listing which organizations receive Title X grants and how many abortions were performed under those exceptions.

The people most directly affected are low-income individuals who rely on Title X-funded clinics for basic reproductive health care. If a clinic that currently offers both family planning services and abortions loses its Title X funding, it may have fewer resources to provide other services like contraception and cancer screenings. Organizations such as Planned Parenthood, which receive Title X dollars and also perform abortions, would have to choose between stopping abortion services or giving up the federal funding. Patients in areas where that clinic is the main affordable option could find it harder to access care.
ai_headline: Bill would strip federal family planning funds from abortion providers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4329 bars abortion providers from receiving Title X family planning funds, with narrow exceptions for rape, incest, or life-threatening pregnancies.
ai_sections.what: S 4329 would prohibit clinics and health organizations that perform abortions from receiving Title X federal funding, which pays for family planning services including birth control, STI testing, and health checkups for low-income people. To keep their funding, providers would have to certify they do not perform abortions and do not send their own money to outside groups that do. Abortions remain fundable only when the pregnancy resulted from rape or incest, or when a doctor certifies the pregnancy threatens the woman's life.
ai_sections.who: Low-income individuals who depend on Title X-funded clinics for reproductive health care are most directly affected. Organizations that both receive Title X dollars and perform abortions, such as Planned Parenthood, would face a choice between halting abortion services or forfeiting federal funding.
ai_sections.why: Clinics that lose Title X funding may have fewer resources to provide other services such as contraception and cancer screenings. Patients in areas where an affected clinic is the main affordable option could find it harder to access basic reproductive health care.
ai_sections.cost: Title X is a federal program that funds family planning services; the bill would redirect or eliminate that funding for any provider that performs abortions. The bill also requires the federal government to submit an annual report to Congress listing grant recipients and abortion counts under the exceptions.
costChips: Title X funds family planning services
Abortion providers would lose federal grants

title: Taxing Buybacks from Big Oil Windfalls Act
ai_summary:
This bill would raise the tax on stock buybacks made by large oil and gas companies. Right now, any corporation that buys back its own stock pays a 1 percent excise tax on those purchases. This bill would jump that rate to 25 percent, but only for oil and gas companies that average at least $1 billion in yearly revenue over the prior three years. The higher tax would apply to companies involved in producing, refining, processing, transporting, or distributing oil and natural gas.

The higher 25 percent rate is not permanent. It would automatically stop applying once the national average price of regular gasoline drops below $2.937 per gallon for five weeks in a row. In other words, the extra tax is designed to be a temporary measure that stays in place while gas prices remain above that threshold.

This bill would most directly affect large, established oil and gas corporations that use profits to buy back their own stock. Stock buybacks are a way companies return money to shareholders, often boosting the stock price. Everyday Americans would not pay this tax directly, but some economists argue that higher taxes on corporations can influence business decisions, prices, or investment. Consumers, investors, and workers in the energy industry could all potentially feel indirect effects, depending on how affected companies respond.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets big oil buybacks with 25% excise tax
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4588 would raise the stock buyback tax from 1% to 25% for oil and gas companies earning $1B+ yearly.
ai_sections.what: S 4588 would increase the excise tax on stock buybacks from 1 percent to 25 percent, but only for oil and gas companies that have averaged at least $1 billion in annual revenue over the prior three years. The higher rate applies to companies involved in producing, refining, processing, transporting, or distributing oil and natural gas. The 25 percent rate would automatically end once the national average price of regular gasoline falls below $2.937 per gallon for five consecutive weeks.
ai_sections.who: The tax would directly affect large, established oil and gas corporations that use profits to buy back their own stock. Consumers, investors, and energy industry workers could experience indirect effects depending on how those companies respond.
ai_sections.why: The bill is structured as a temporary measure tied to gas prices remaining above a specific threshold, not as a permanent change to corporate tax law. Some economists note that higher corporate taxes can influence business decisions, pricing, and investment, meaning effects may extend beyond the companies paying the tax.
ai_sections.cost: Oil and gas companies meeting the $1 billion average revenue threshold would pay a 25 percent excise tax on stock buybacks, up from the current 1 percent rate. Everyday Americans would not pay this tax directly.

title: MAP for Care Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a voluntary program within Medicare to help people set up advance directives, which are legal documents that spell out what kind of medical care a person wants if they become too sick to speak for themselves. Examples include a living will, which lists specific treatment wishes, or a durable power of attorney for health care, which names someone to make medical decisions on a person's behalf. The government would build an online system where Medicare enrollees could create, store, update, or cancel these documents securely. The program would have to be up and running within five years of the bill becoming law.

The bill sets rules for companies and organizations that want to offer these advance directive services. To be approved, they must meet security and privacy standards, allow near-instant online access to documents, follow each state's laws about what makes an advance directive valid, and survey users every year about their experience. The Medicare website would also post links to official advance directive forms for every state, as well as alternative forms submitted by outside groups, so people can compare options before deciding what fits their situation.

The people directly affected are the roughly 67 million Americans enrolled in Medicare, which mainly includes adults 65 and older as well as some younger people with disabilities. Participation is entirely optional, and the bill makes clear that no one is required to create an advance directive. It also states that the federal program does not override individual state laws on the topic, so existing state rules about what makes these documents legally valid still apply.
ai_headline: Medicare bill would let enrollees store advance directives online
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3473 creates a voluntary Medicare online system for advance directives, affecting ~67 million enrollees; must launch within 5 years.
ai_sections.what: S 3473 establishes a voluntary program within Medicare that lets enrollees create, store, update, or cancel advance directives through a secure online system. Covered documents include living wills and durable powers of attorney for health care. The system must be operational within five years of enactment, and the Medicare website would also post links to official and alternative advance directive forms for every state.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 67 million Americans enrolled in Medicare are directly affected, including adults 65 and older and some younger people with disabilities. Participation is entirely optional and no one is required to create an advance directive.
ai_sections.why: Approved service providers would be required to meet security and privacy standards, follow state validity laws, allow near-instant online document access, and conduct annual user surveys. The federal program does not override state laws, so existing state rules governing what makes an advance directive legally valid continue to apply.

title: West Bank Violence Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require the President to impose penalties on foreign individuals who commit or support violent acts in the West Bank, a disputed territory between Israel and the Palestinian Authority. Specifically, it targets people who attack civilians, destroy property, seize land, or commit terrorism there. The punishments include freezing any money or assets those people have in the United States and banning them from entering the country. The President would also have to report to Congress every six months on who has been sanctioned and what is happening on the ground in the West Bank.

The bill applies only to foreign persons, not American citizens. It would not block humanitarian aid like food, medicine, or medical supplies from reaching people in the region. The President can waive the sanctions if national security requires it, and sanctions can be lifted if a sanctioned person stops their harmful behavior and provides reliable assurances they will not repeat it.

American residents would not be directly penalized under this bill, but it could affect anyone in the United States who does business with or sends money to a sanctioned person. People with family or financial ties to individuals operating in the West Bank could find those connections complicated if those individuals end up on the sanctions list. The bill was introduced in the Senate and sent to the Foreign Relations Committee, so it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets foreign actors behind West Bank violence with asset freezes and travel bans
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2667 would freeze U.S. assets and ban entry for foreign individuals who commit or support violence in the West Bank.
ai_sections.what: S 2667 would require the President to impose sanctions on foreign individuals who attack civilians, destroy property, seize land, or commit terrorism in the West Bank. Sanctions would include freezing U.S.-held assets and banning those individuals from entering the country. The President would report to Congress every six months on who has been sanctioned and conditions on the ground.
ai_sections.who: The bill targets foreign persons only and does not directly penalize American citizens. U.S. residents who do business with or send money to a sanctioned person could still be affected by those connections.
ai_sections.why: People in the United States with financial or family ties to individuals operating in the West Bank could face complications if those individuals are placed on the sanctions list. The bill has been referred to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee and has not yet become law.

title: No Settlements for January 6 Law Enforcement Assaulters Act
ai_summary:
This bill would block the federal government from paying any legal settlement money to people who were convicted of assaulting a police officer during the January 6, 2021 Capitol breach. Normally, people can sue the government and receive payment from a federal account called the Judgment Fund if they win or settle a civil lawsuit. This bill would make an exception: anyone with a qualifying assault conviction from that day could not receive that kind of payout, even if they claimed they were harmed during the events themselves or during their prosecution.

The bill targets a specific group of people who meet two conditions at the same time. First, they must have been convicted of assaulting a law enforcement officer in connection with January 6. Second, their lawsuit must be based on harm they say they suffered either during the Capitol events or as a result of being prosecuted for those events. Both conditions have to apply for the restriction to kick in.

This legislation would affect a relatively small number of people who were convicted on assault charges related to January 6 and who have filed or might file civil claims against the federal government. It would not affect the general public, law enforcement officers, or people convicted of non-assault offenses from that day. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to the Judiciary Committee, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Senate bill would bar Jan. 6 assault convicts from federal civil settlements
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3581 would bar anyone convicted of assaulting a police officer on Jan. 6 from receiving federal Judgment Fund settlements.
ai_sections.what: S 3581 would prevent the federal government from paying civil lawsuit settlements or judgments, drawn from the Judgment Fund, to people convicted of assaulting a law enforcement officer during the January 6, 2021 Capitol breach. The restriction applies only when two conditions are both met: the person holds a qualifying assault conviction from that day, and their lawsuit involves harm they claim to have suffered during the Capitol events or their subsequent prosecution.
ai_sections.who: The bill targets a relatively small number of people who were convicted of assault charges related to January 6 and have filed or may file civil claims against the federal government. It does not affect the general public, law enforcement officers, or people convicted of non-assault offenses from that day.
ai_sections.why: People who meet both conditions would be blocked from receiving any Judgment Fund payout, even if they allege harm during the events or their prosecution. The bill has been referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee and has not yet become law.

title: Stand Strong for Medicare Act
ai_summary:
This bill would add coverage for certain fall prevention products to Medicare for the first time. Specifically, Medicare would pay for items like grab bars, non-slip mats, shower chairs, and bed rails when a doctor or other qualified medical provider writes an order for them. Right now, Medicare does not cover these kinds of home safety products, so seniors who need them must pay out of pocket.

The bill affects people who are on Medicare, which is primarily Americans age 65 and older, as well as some younger people with disabilities. If passed, a Medicare enrollee whose doctor recommends fall prevention equipment could have Medicare help pay for it, rather than covering the full cost themselves. The coverage would take effect 60 days after the bill is signed into law.

The bill also includes a rule that says Medicare payments for these items cannot be reduced through the automatic budget cuts known as sequestration. This is meant to protect the new benefit from being trimmed down through future across-the-board federal spending cuts. The bill was introduced in the Senate in September 2025 and has been sent to the Senate Finance Committee, where it would need to be approved before moving forward.
ai_headline: Senate bill would add fall prevention gear to Medicare coverage
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2831 would let Medicare pay for grab bars, shower chairs, and similar fall prevention items when a doctor orders them.
ai_sections.what: S 2831 would make Medicare cover certain fall prevention products for the first time, including grab bars, non-slip mats, shower chairs, and bed rails. Coverage would apply when a doctor or other qualified medical provider writes an order for the items. The benefit would take effect 60 days after the bill is signed into law.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects Medicare enrollees, primarily Americans age 65 and older, as well as some younger people with disabilities. Currently these individuals must pay for such home safety products entirely out of pocket.
ai_sections.why: If enacted, Medicare would share the cost of fall prevention equipment that a doctor recommends, instead of the enrollee bearing the full cost. The bill also includes a provision shielding these payments from automatic federal budget cuts known as sequestration.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare would pay for covered fall prevention items when a doctor orders them, a cost currently borne entirely by the enrollee. The bill specifies that these payments cannot be reduced through sequestration.

title: EARLY Act Reauthorization of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill extends an existing federal program focused on breast health education for young women. The original law, passed in 2009, created public awareness campaigns and educational efforts aimed at helping young women learn about breast health and understand their personal risk for breast cancer. Without this reauthorization, the program's funding authority would expire in 2026. This bill simply moves that expiration date to 2031, keeping the program alive for five more years.

The people most directly affected are young women, particularly those in their teens, twenties, and thirties, who benefit from the outreach and education this program supports. Healthcare providers and community organizations that receive funding or guidance through this program would also be affected, as they use it to spread information about early detection and risk awareness.

This is a fairly narrow bill that does not create a new program or change how the existing one works. It only renews the government's authority to continue funding it. Congress must periodically reauthorize programs like this to keep them operating, and this bill is that routine renewal step.
ai_headline: Breast health education program for young women extended five years
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 48 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4541 moves the funding expiration for a young women's breast health education program from 2026 to 2031.
ai_sections.what: HR 4541 reauthorizes a federal breast health education program originally created in 2009. The bill moves the program's funding expiration date from 2026 to 2031, a five-year extension. It does not create a new program or change how the existing one operates.
ai_sections.who: Young women, particularly those in their teens, twenties, and thirties, are the primary audience for the program's outreach and education efforts. Healthcare providers and community organizations that receive funding or guidance through the program are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without this reauthorization, the program's authority to receive funding would expire in 2026, ending its operations. Periodic reauthorization is the routine step Congress takes to keep programs like this running.

title: Stem Cell Therapeutic and Research Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill renews federal funding and authorization for two existing government programs related to cord blood and stem cell transplants. The main program, called the C.W. Bill Young Cell Transplantation Program, helps match patients who need stem cell or cord blood transplants with donors. The bill keeps that program running through 2031 and slightly increases its yearly funding from about 31 million dollars to about 33 million dollars starting in 2027. It also extends the federal cord blood inventory program, which maintains a supply of donated umbilical cord blood that can be used for transplants, through 2031 as well.

These programs primarily help people who have blood diseases like leukemia, sickle cell disease, or other conditions that may require a stem cell or bone marrow transplant. Without a matching donor in their own family, patients depend on national registries and cord blood banks that these programs support. The funding helps cover the costs of finding matches, collecting cord blood, and storing it so it is available when a patient needs it.

This bill does not create anything new. It simply continues programs that have been in place since 2005 and makes sure they have federal funding for the next several years. People who could be affected include patients seeking transplants, hospitals and transplant centers that use these registries, and cord blood banks that store donated blood for future use.
ai_headline: Federal stem cell and cord blood match programs extended to 2031
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 48 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5160 renews two federal transplant-match programs through 2031 and raises yearly funding to roughly $33M starting in 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 5160 renews the C.W. Bill Young Cell Transplantation Program and the federal cord blood inventory program through 2031. Both programs have been in place since 2005 and are unchanged in structure. The bill does not create any new programs.
ai_sections.who: Patients with blood diseases such as leukemia or sickle cell disease who need stem cell or bone marrow transplants and lack a family donor are the primary population served. Hospitals, transplant centers, and cord blood banks that rely on these national registries are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without continued federal authorization, the national registries and cord blood banks that support donor matching would lose their funding base. Patients who depend on those registries to find unrelated donors would face gaps in the availability of matching and stored cord blood supplies.
ai_sections.cost: Annual funding increases from approximately 31 million dollars to approximately 33 million dollars starting in 2027 for the C.W. Bill Young Cell Transplantation Program. Funding covers donor matching, cord blood collection, and storage.

title: To amend the Export Control Reform Act of 2018 to increase the civil penalties that may be imposed under such Act.
ai_summary:
This bill changes the fines that can be charged to people or companies that break U.S. export control laws. Export controls are rules that restrict selling or sending certain goods, technology, and information to other countries, especially when those items could be used for military purposes or by unfriendly nations. Right now, someone who breaks these rules can be fined up to $300,000 per violation or twice the value of the illegal transaction, whichever is greater. This bill would raise that to $1.2 million per violation or four times the value of the transaction.

The change would apply to any violation that happens on or after the date the bill becomes law. It does not affect past violations. The goal is to make the penalty steep enough to discourage businesses and individuals from trying to illegally export controlled items.

This bill mainly affects businesses, manufacturers, researchers, and individuals who deal in goods or technology that require a U.S. export license. That includes things like advanced electronics, software, weapons components, and certain industrial equipment. Most everyday Americans would not be directly affected, but the law could indirectly impact companies that do international trade, especially those in defense, technology, or manufacturing industries.
ai_headline: Export violation fines would quadruple under House bill HR 5853
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported by the Yeas and Nays: 44 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5853 raises max export control fines from $300,000 to $1.2 million per violation, applying only to future violations.
ai_sections.what: HR 5853 would increase the maximum fine for violating U.S. export control laws from $300,000 to $1.2 million per violation, or from twice to four times the value of the illegal transaction, whichever is greater. The higher penalties would apply only to violations occurring on or after the date the bill becomes law. Past violations would not be affected.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects businesses, manufacturers, researchers, and individuals who deal in goods or technology requiring a U.S. export license, including advanced electronics, software, weapons components, and certain industrial equipment. Companies involved in international trade, particularly in defense, technology, or manufacturing, face the most exposure.
ai_sections.why: The stated goal is to make penalties large enough to discourage illegal exports of controlled items. Raising the fine ceiling increases the financial risk for anyone who violates export control rules going forward.
ai_sections.cost: Fines per violation would rise from a maximum of $300,000 to $1.2 million, or from twice to four times the transaction value, whichever amount is greater.
costChips: New max fine: $1.2 million per violation

title: American Farmers First Act
ai_summary:
This bill does two main things. First, it would block a U.S. government fund called the Exchange Stabilization Fund from being used to financially support Argentina. This fund is managed by the Treasury Department and can be used to help stabilize foreign currencies or financial markets. The bill would ban any new financial deals with Argentina using this fund and would require any existing deals to be ended within 7 days of the bill becoming law. This ban would last until December 10, 2027.

Second, the bill would take whatever money comes back from unwinding those Argentina-related deals and hand it over to the U.S. Department of Agriculture. That money would then be paid out as one-time relief checks to American farmers who grew crops that lost overseas buyers during the 2025 crop marketing year. The Agriculture Secretary would decide which crops qualify based on which ones saw exports drop.

The people most directly affected are American farmers, particularly those who have struggled to sell their crops to foreign customers in 2025. The bill also affects how the Treasury Department can operate the Exchange Stabilization Fund going forward. It does not directly affect average consumers, though U.S.-Argentina financial relationships and farm commodity prices can have broader ripple effects on food supply chains over time.
ai_headline: Bill would cut U.S. financial aid to Argentina and redirect funds to farmers
last_action_text: Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, Risk Management, and Credit Discharged

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6061 blocks U.S. Treasury's Exchange Stabilization Fund from supporting Argentina and sends returned funds to American farmers as one-time payments.
ai_sections.what: HR 6061 would prohibit the U.S. Treasury Department's Exchange Stabilization Fund from entering any new financial deals with Argentina and would require existing deals to be unwound within 7 days of enactment. The prohibition would remain in effect until December 10, 2027. Money recovered from those unwound deals would be transferred to the U.S. Department of Agriculture and paid out as one-time relief checks to American farmers.
ai_sections.who: American farmers who lost overseas buyers for their crops during the 2025 crop marketing year are the primary intended recipients. The Treasury Department's authority to operate the Exchange Stabilization Fund would also be directly constrained.
ai_sections.why: The Agriculture Secretary would determine which crops qualify based on which ones experienced a drop in exports during 2025. U.S.-Argentina financial relationships and farm commodity prices can have broader effects on food supply chains over time.
ai_sections.cost: The funds paid to farmers would come from money returned when existing Argentina-related Exchange Stabilization Fund deals are unwound. The Agriculture Department would distribute those recovered funds as one-time payments to qualifying farmers.
costChips: Source is unwound Argentina ESF deals

title: VOTE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require states to give voters advance warning when their polling place has been changed before a federal election. If a voter's assigned polling place is different from where they voted last time, the state must notify them at least 7 days before Election Day by mail, phone, text, and email if those contact methods are available. If the change happens less than 7 days out and a voter shows up at their old polling place, election officials must make every reasonable effort to still let that person vote.

The bill also requires that closed polling locations post signs on Election Day directing voters to their new polling place, including the address, directions, and contact information for local election officials. States must also post this information on their websites and social media. Areas that use vote centers instead of assigned polling places must notify all eligible voters of all available voting locations at least 2 days before early voting begins. All notices must be provided in languages required under the Voting Rights Act for communities with large non-English-speaking populations.

This bill affects all registered voters across the country who vote in federal elections, meaning elections for President, Senate, and House of Representatives. It also directly affects state and local election officials, who would be responsible for carrying out these notification requirements. The goal is to make sure voters are not surprised or confused by polling place changes, but the bill does not change where polling places can be located or who is eligible to vote.
ai_headline: New polling place rules would require states to warn voters 7 days out
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7182 requires states to notify voters at least 7 days before a federal election if their polling place has changed.
ai_sections.what: HR 7182 would require states to notify voters at least 7 days before a federal election if their assigned polling place has changed, using mail, phone, text, and email when available. Closed polling sites must post signs on Election Day with the new address, directions, and local election official contact information, and states must also post this information online and on social media. Areas using vote centers instead of assigned polling places must notify all eligible voters of all available locations at least 2 days before early voting begins.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects all registered voters who participate in federal elections, meaning elections for President, Senate, and House of Representatives. State and local election officials would be responsible for carrying out the notification requirements.
ai_sections.why: If a polling place change occurs less than 7 days before an election and a voter arrives at their old location, election officials must make every reasonable effort to allow that person to vote. All notices must be provided in languages required under the Voting Rights Act for communities with large non-English-speaking populations.

title: STEADFAST Act
ai_summary:
This bill would end the existing federal program that uses taxpayer money to help fund presidential campaigns. Right now, when people file their taxes, they can check a box to send $3 to the Presidential Election Campaign Fund, which gives money to candidates running for president. The STEADFAST Act would shut that program down completely and redirect the money — along with any leftover funds already in the account — to a new purpose: helping states upgrade and secure their election systems.

The new program would send grants to states, territories like Puerto Rico and Guam, and Washington D.C. to pay for things like new voting machines, cybersecurity improvements, paper ballots, and physical security for election equipment. States would apply for the money through the Election Assistance Commission and would have to report publicly on how they spent it. The size of each state's grant would be based on how many registered voters it has. Vendors hired to do the work would need to be pre-approved by the commission, and companies that have previously donated money to help run elections would be blocked from being approved.

Not every state would be treated equally under this bill. States that use paper ballots with a verifiable paper trail, require photo ID to vote in person, and use federal databases to check that non-citizens are not registered to vote would be moved to the front of the line when grants are handed out. States that do not meet those conditions could still apply but would receive lower priority. The bill affects all U.S. states and several territories, election administrators, voting equipment vendors, and any taxpayers who previously used the presidential campaign checkoff on their tax returns.
ai_headline: Federal presidential campaign fund would be scrapped to pay for election security upgrades
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 8 - 3.

ai_sections.tldr: The STEADFAST Act would end public presidential campaign financing and redirect those funds to state election security grants.
ai_sections.what: HR 7418, the STEADFAST Act, would permanently shut down the Presidential Election Campaign Fund, which currently lets taxpayers direct $3 of their taxes to presidential candidates. The money in that fund, including any leftover balance, would instead be distributed as grants to states, territories, and Washington D.C. to pay for voting equipment upgrades, cybersecurity improvements, paper ballots, and physical security for election infrastructure.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects all U.S. states, Puerto Rico, Guam, Washington D.C., election administrators, voting equipment vendors, and taxpayers who previously used the presidential campaign checkoff on their tax returns. Grants would be administered through the Election Assistance Commission.
ai_sections.why: States meeting specific conditions — use of paper ballots with a verifiable trail, photo ID requirements, and use of federal databases to screen non-citizen registrations — would receive higher priority for grants than states that do not meet those conditions. Vendors previously approved to work on elections and those with prior campaign donation ties would face different eligibility rules, shaping which companies can access the new program.
ai_sections.cost: The source of funding is the existing Presidential Election Campaign Fund, redirected from presidential campaign use. Grant amounts to each state or territory would be scaled to the number of registered voters in that jurisdiction.
costChips: Grant size tied to registered voter count

title: Modernizing and Improving the National Terrorism Advisory System Act of 2026
