ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of Homeland Security to come up with a plan to update the National Terrorism Advisory System, which is the official system the government uses to warn the public about terrorism threats. Right now, that system issues bulletins and alerts, but the bill suggests it may not be working as well as it should. The Secretary of Homeland Security would have one year to write a strategy and send it to Congress.

The strategy would have to look at several things, including who inside DHS is in charge of the system, how alerts are created and ended, how easy they are for the public to find and understand, and how well they reach as many people as possible. DHS would also need to talk to local and state police, tribal governments, emergency responders, and private businesses before finishing the plan. Two years after the bill passes, a government watchdog called the Comptroller General would check in and report to Congress on whether any of this is actually happening.

This bill mainly affects the Department of Homeland Security and the people who work within it, but it also touches state and local law enforcement, emergency responders, and the general public. Everyday Americans could eventually see changes in how they receive terrorism warnings, though this bill does not change the alert system itself right now. It only requires a plan to be written.
ai_headline: DHS would have one year to draft a terrorism alert reform plan
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7448 requires DHS to write a reform strategy for the National Terrorism Advisory System within one year and send it to Congress.
ai_sections.what: HR 7448 requires the Secretary of Homeland Security to develop a strategy within one year for updating the National Terrorism Advisory System, the official government system for public terrorism warnings. The strategy must examine who inside DHS manages the system, how alerts are created and ended, how understandable they are, and how broadly they reach the public. The bill does not change the alert system itself; it only requires a plan to be written.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the Department of Homeland Security and requires it to consult state and local law enforcement, tribal governments, emergency responders, and private businesses before finalizing the strategy. The general public could eventually see changes in how terrorism warnings are delivered, and the Comptroller General is tasked with reporting to Congress two years after passage on whether the strategy is being carried out.
ai_sections.why: The bill signals concern that the current advisory system may not be functioning as effectively as it should in reaching and informing the public. A separate government watchdog review two years after enactment adds an accountability layer over whether any reforms actually follow from the planning requirement.

title: ELO Realignment and Strategic Engagement Reform Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill requires the Department of Homeland Security to reorganize a specific internal office called the Engagement, Liaison, and Outreach Office, known as the ELO Office. Within 120 days of the bill becoming law, the Secretary of Homeland Security would have to submit a detailed plan to Congress explaining how to restructure that office, cut any unnecessary positions or programs, and move its core work into a related division called the Partner Engagement directorate. The goal is to make the office run more efficiently, improve how it shares information with law enforcement partners, and reduce duplicated work across different parts of the department.

The bill also places limits on what the Secretary can do with the ELO Office while that plan is being developed. The department cannot grow the office, increase its budget, or create new offices that do the same job until the reorganization plan has been submitted and its launch certified to Congress. This is meant to prevent the department from expanding the very structure it is supposed to be fixing.

The people most directly affected are DHS employees who work in or alongside the ELO Office, since some positions may be reassigned or eliminated. State, local, tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies that currently receive intelligence support and access to secure information systems through the ELO Office would also be affected, though the bill specifically requires the plan to protect continuity of those services. Ordinary residents would not feel a direct impact, but the changes are intended to improve how federal and local law enforcement agencies work together on national security and public safety matters.
ai_headline: DHS must submit ELO Office restructuring plan within 120 days
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7574 gives DHS 120 days to submit a restructuring plan for its ELO Office and bars expansion until that plan is certified.
ai_sections.what: HR 7574 requires the Department of Homeland Security to submit a detailed plan to Congress within 120 days of enactment explaining how to restructure the Engagement, Liaison, and Outreach Office, eliminate unnecessary positions or programs, and move its core functions into the Partner Engagement directorate. While that plan is being developed, DHS is prohibited from expanding the office, increasing its budget, or creating duplicate offices. The freeze lifts only after the plan has been submitted and its launch certified to Congress.
ai_sections.who: DHS employees working in or alongside the ELO Office face potential reassignment or elimination of positions. State, local, tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies that rely on the ELO Office for intelligence support and access to secure information systems are also affected, though the plan must protect continuity of those services.
ai_sections.why: The stated purpose is to reduce duplicated work across the department and improve how DHS shares information with law enforcement partners. The restrictions on expansion are intended to prevent the department from enlarging the structure it is simultaneously required to reform.

title: National Threat Evaluation and Reporting Program Reassignment and Funding Reform Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill moves a Department of Homeland Security program called the National Threat Evaluation and Reporting Program, or NTER, from one DHS office to another. Right now, NTER sits inside the Office of Intelligence and Analysis, which focuses on national intelligence work. The bill would move it to the Office for State and Local Law Enforcement, which is the DHS office that works directly with police departments, sheriffs, and other law enforcement agencies at the state, local, tribal, and territorial level. The move must happen within 180 days of the bill becoming law, and all staff, equipment, and funding tied to the program would transfer along with it.

The bill also changes how the program is paid for. Currently, NTER is funded through the National Intelligence Program budget, which is the pool of money used for national spy and intelligence agencies. The bill says that funding source must stop once the transfer is complete, and DHS would need to find other funding streams, such as state and local preparedness grants or other non-intelligence budget lines, to keep the program running.

The people most directly affected are state, local, tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies and officials who use NTER training and tools to spot and respond to potential threats of targeted violence in their communities. The bill requires DHS to report to Congress every six months for two years on how the transition is going, so lawmakers can check that the program keeps working without interruption for those local partners.
ai_headline: DHS threat reporting program shifted to state and local law enforcement office
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7764 moves DHS's NTER program to its state and local law enforcement office within 180 days, ending its intelligence budget funding.
ai_sections.what: HR 7764 transfers the National Threat Evaluation and Reporting Program (NTER) from the DHS Office of Intelligence and Analysis to the DHS Office for State and Local Law Enforcement. The move must be completed within 180 days of the bill becoming law, and all staff, equipment, and funding tied to NTER transfer along with it. DHS must report to Congress every six months for two years on how the transition is progressing.
ai_sections.who: State, local, tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies and officials are the primary users of NTER's training and tools for identifying and responding to potential threats of targeted violence. DHS and Congress are also directly involved, as the department must execute the transfer and report on it regularly.
ai_sections.why: Moving NTER to the office that works directly with police departments and sheriffs aligns the program's administrative home with the agencies it primarily serves. The mandatory biannual reporting requirement reflects congressional concern that local partners experience no interruption in access to the program during the transition.
ai_sections.cost: Funding for NTER through the National Intelligence Program budget must stop once the transfer is complete. DHS would then need to identify other funding sources, such as state and local preparedness grants or other non-intelligence budget lines, to sustain the program.
costChips: Intel budget funding cut at transfer
New funding from grants or other lines
All staff and resources move with program

title: Rural Depositories Revitalization Study Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells federal banking regulators to study why banks and credit unions in rural areas are struggling and what could be done to help them grow and stay financially healthy. Specifically, the Federal Reserve, the FDIC, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, and the National Credit Union Administration would each look into what methods might help rural financial institutions succeed, and also identify whether any existing federal laws or regulations are making it harder for those institutions to operate or for new ones to open. The agencies would have one year after the bill becomes law to send their findings to Congress.

The bill does not create any new programs, spend any money, or change any rules on its own. It is purely a research and reporting requirement. The results could inform future legislation, but this bill itself only asks for a study.

People most directly affected would be residents of rural communities who rely on local banks and credit unions for services like checking accounts, loans, and mortgages. Rural areas have seen many bank branches close in recent decades, so this study is aimed at understanding that trend and exploring possible solutions. Small community banks and credit unions operating in rural areas would also be directly relevant to the study's scope.
ai_headline: Federal regulators ordered to study rural bank closures and barriers to growth
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4517 requires four federal banking regulators to study rural bank and credit union struggles and report findings to Congress within one year.
ai_sections.what: S 4517 directs four federal banking regulators to study why rural banks and credit unions are struggling and what could help them survive and grow. The agencies must also identify whether existing federal laws or regulations are blocking rural financial institutions from operating or new ones from opening. No new programs, spending, or rule changes are created by this bill.
ai_sections.who: The four agencies required to act are the Federal Reserve, the FDIC, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, and the National Credit Union Administration. Residents of rural communities who depend on local banks and credit unions, along with small community banks and credit unions in rural areas, are the populations most directly relevant to the study.
ai_sections.why: Rural areas have seen many bank branches close in recent decades, and this bill is a direct response to that ongoing trend. The findings could inform future legislation, though this bill itself makes no policy changes.

title: Pandemic Unemployment Fraud Enforcement Act
ai_summary:
During the COVID-19 pandemic, the federal government created special unemployment programs to help people who lost work. Unfortunately, a large amount of money from these programs was stolen through fraud, including identity theft, fake claims, and other schemes. Normally, the government only has about five years to bring criminal charges or sue someone for fraud. This bill would extend that window to 10 years for fraud connected to those pandemic unemployment programs.

The bill applies to anyone suspected of committing fraud against programs like Pandemic Unemployment Assistance, Federal Pandemic Unemployment Compensation, and Pandemic Emergency Unemployment Compensation. This includes crimes like wire fraud, identity theft, money laundering, and filing false claims. One important limit is that the extension does not apply if the original deadline for a case had already passed before this bill becomes law.

This bill mainly affects two groups. First, it affects people who may have committed fraud against these programs, since they could face charges for a longer period of time. Second, it affects federal and state law enforcement agencies, which would have more time to investigate and prosecute these cases. The bill also cancels 5 million dollars in unused pandemic-related funds to help cover its costs.
ai_headline: Pandemic unemployment fraud window doubled to 10 years under HR 1156
last_action_text: Read the second time. Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 29.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1156 extends the prosecution deadline for pandemic unemployment fraud from 5 to 10 years, giving investigators more time to pursue cases.
ai_sections.what: HR 1156 extends the statute of limitations for fraud connected to federal pandemic unemployment programs from 5 years to 10 years. It covers crimes including wire fraud, identity theft, money laundering, and filing false claims. The extension does not apply to any case whose original deadline had already expired before the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects people suspected of committing fraud against pandemic unemployment programs, who now face a longer window of legal exposure. It also affects federal and state law enforcement agencies, which would gain additional time to investigate and prosecute these cases.
ai_sections.why: A large amount of money from pandemic unemployment programs was stolen through fraud, including identity theft and fake claims, but the standard 5-year window limits how long prosecutors have to act. Extending the deadline to 10 years changes the legal timeline for any fraud tied to programs such as Pandemic Unemployment Assistance, Federal Pandemic Unemployment Compensation, and Pandemic Emergency Unemployment Compensation.
ai_sections.cost: The bill cancels 5 million dollars in unused pandemic-related funds to help cover its costs.

title: Bureau of Land Management Mineral Spacing Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for drilling oil and gas on certain lands where the federal government owns less than half of the underground mineral rights but a private landowner owns the surface. Right now, companies need both a federal permit and a state permit to drill in these situations. Under this bill, if the federal government owns less than 50% of the mineral rights below ground, the company would only need a state permit. The company could start work 30 days after submitting that state permit to the federal government, without waiting for federal approval.

The bill also removes several federal review requirements for these drilling projects. They would no longer need to go through the National Environmental Policy Act review process, which typically requires studying environmental impacts. They would also be exempt from checks under the National Historic Preservation Act and the Endangered Species Act. However, the federal government would still collect its share of royalty payments from whatever oil and gas is produced, and federal inspectors could still visit sites to make sure production is reported and measured correctly.

This bill mainly affects oil and gas companies that operate on privately owned land sitting above federally owned mineral deposits, as well as the federal agencies that currently oversee those permits. It could also affect communities and wildlife near drilling sites, since some environmental and historic preservation reviews would no longer apply. The bill specifically does not apply to any Native American or tribal lands.
ai_headline: Bill would strip federal environmental review from some oil and gas permits
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1555 lets drillers skip federal environmental, historic, and species reviews when the U.S. owns under 50% of mineral rights.
ai_sections.what: HR 1555 removes the federal permit requirement for oil and gas drilling on lands where the federal government owns less than 50% of the underground mineral rights. Companies would need only a state permit and could begin work 30 days after submitting it to the federal government, without waiting for federal approval. These projects would also be exempt from review under the National Environmental Policy Act, the National Historic Preservation Act, and the Endangered Species Act.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects oil and gas companies operating on privately owned surface land above federally owned mineral deposits, and the federal agencies that currently oversee those permits. Communities and wildlife near drilling sites are also affected, as are historic preservation interests, since the reviews that previously applied to them would no longer be required. The bill explicitly excludes Native American and tribal lands.
ai_sections.why: Removing the three federal review requirements means environmental impacts, effects on historic or cultural sites, and risks to protected species would no longer be formally assessed before drilling begins on these lands. Communities and wildlife near affected sites would lose the procedural protections those reviews currently provide.

title: Fisher House Availability Act of 2025
ai_summary:
Fisher Houses are free or low-cost temporary homes located near VA and military medical centers. Right now, they are mainly available to veterans receiving VA care and their family members. This bill would expand who can stay in a Fisher House when there are open rooms. Specifically, it would allow active-duty military members and their families to use these facilities on a space-available basis, meaning they can only stay if a room is not already needed by a veteran or their family.

The bill also broadens access in another way. Currently, the focus is on the patient traveling for care. This bill adds situations where a family member of a veteran or active-duty service member is the one traveling far to receive medical care. In those cases, the veteran or service member could stay at the Fisher House too, to support that family member. The key phrase throughout is "space-available," which means veterans and their families still have priority, and these expanded groups only get access to rooms that would otherwise sit empty.

This affects active-duty military members and their families, as well as family members of veterans who need to travel for their own medical care. It also directs the VA to set up rules for how this space-available access will work in practice. Fisher House facilities are donated and built by the Fisher House Foundation, and the VA manages them. The bill does not change any existing benefits for veterans already using these facilities.
ai_headline: Bill would open Fisher House rooms to active-duty families on space-available basis
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Ordered to be reported without amendment favorably.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3119 lets active-duty military and their families use Fisher House rooms only when veterans and their families aren't already using them.
ai_sections.what: S 3119 expands eligibility for Fisher Houses, which are free or low-cost temporary lodging near VA and military medical centers. Active-duty service members and their families could use open rooms on a space-available basis, meaning veterans and their families retain priority. The bill also covers situations where a family member is the one traveling for medical care, allowing the veteran or service member to stay and provide support.
ai_sections.who: Active-duty military members and their families gain new access to Fisher Houses under this bill. Family members of veterans who must travel for their own medical care, and the veterans supporting them, are also newly included.
ai_sections.why: Rooms that would otherwise sit empty could be used by a broader group of military-connected individuals. The VA would be required to establish rules governing how this space-available access works in practice.

title: Repeal the TikTok Ban Act
ai_summary:
This bill would undo a law passed in 2024 that required ByteDance, the Chinese company that owns TikTok, to either sell the app or face a ban in the United States. That earlier law said TikTok posed a national security risk because a foreign adversary could potentially access American users' data or influence what content people see. This bill would wipe that law off the books entirely and cancel any official government designations that were made under it.

If passed, this would mean TikTok and any other apps that were labeled "foreign adversary controlled applications" under the old law could continue operating in the US without facing a forced sale or ban. It would also apply retroactively, meaning actions already taken under the old law would be treated as if they never happened.

This bill directly affects the roughly 170 million Americans who use TikTok, as well as the companies that own or operate apps that were flagged under the original law. It also affects businesses that advertise on TikTok and creators who rely on the platform for income. The bill was introduced by two members of Congress from opposite parties, and it has been sent to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, where it would need to be approved before the full House could vote on it.
ai_headline: Bipartisan bill would repeal 2024 TikTok forced-sale law
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 564 would fully repeal the 2024 law requiring ByteDance to sell TikTok or face a US ban, affecting roughly 170 million users.
ai_sections.what: HR 564 would repeal the 2024 law that required ByteDance, TikTok's Chinese parent company, to sell the app or face a ban in the United States. It would also cancel all government designations made under that law and apply retroactively, meaning actions already taken under it would be treated as if they never occurred. Any app previously labeled a "foreign adversary controlled application" under the old law could continue operating in the US without facing a forced sale or ban.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the roughly 170 million Americans who use TikTok, as well as companies that own or operate apps flagged under the original law, businesses that advertise on TikTok, and creators who rely on the platform for income. It was introduced by two members of Congress from opposite parties and has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
ai_sections.why: The original 2024 law was passed on the grounds that TikTok posed a national security risk, with concerns that a foreign adversary could access American users' data or influence the content people see. Repealing that law would remove the legal basis for any forced sale or ban and reverse the national security designations tied to it.

title: Food Date Labeling Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a national standard for the date labels printed on food packaging. Right now, food companies use many different phrases like "Best By," "Sell By," "Expires On," and others, which confuses shoppers about whether food is still safe to eat or just past its peak quality. If this bill becomes law, companies that choose to put a date on their packaging would have to use one of two specific phrases. "BEST If Used By" would mean the food is still safe to eat after that date but might not taste or look as good. "USE By" would mean the food should not be eaten after that date. Companies are not required to include any date at all, but if they do, they must use the correct standard phrase.

The bill affects food manufacturers and grocery stores, since they would need to update their packaging within two years of the law passing. It also affects everyday shoppers, who would see clearer, more consistent labels at the store. The government would also be required to run public education campaigns explaining what the two phrases mean. Infant formula is specifically left out of this bill and keeps its existing, stricter rules.

States cannot create their own different versions of these two label phrases, but they can still pass laws that ban the sale or donation of food after a "USE By" date. States cannot, however, ban the sale of food just because it has passed a "BEST If Used By" date. The bill applies to all meat, poultry, egg products, and other packaged foods regulated by the federal government.
ai_headline: Senate bill would standardize food date labels to two federally defined phrases
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2541 would require food makers who print dates to use only "BEST If Used By" or "USE By," replacing today's inconsistent labels.
ai_sections.what: S 2541 establishes a national standard limiting food date labels to two phrases: "BEST If Used By," indicating the food remains safe but may decline in quality after that date, and "USE By," indicating the food should not be eaten after that date. Companies are not required to print any date, but if they do, they must use one of these two phrases. Infant formula is excluded and keeps its existing rules.
ai_sections.who: Food manufacturers and grocery stores must update their packaging within two years of the law passing. Everyday shoppers, and state governments whose own label regulations would be partially preempted, are also affected.
ai_sections.why: States would be barred from creating their own versions of the two label phrases and from prohibiting the sale of food solely because it has passed a "BEST If Used By" date, though they could still restrict sale or donation of food past a "USE By" date. The federal government would be required to run public education campaigns explaining the meaning of both phrases.

title: ENFORCE Act
ai_summary:
This bill strengthens federal laws against child sexual abuse material in several ways. It closes a loophole by making it clearer that creating AI-generated or drawn images of child sexual abuse is a federal crime, even when no real child was directly used in the production. It also removes the time limit for prosecutors to bring charges for this type of material, meaning someone can be charged years or even decades after the crime occurred.

The bill also changes how people accused of these crimes are treated before and after a trial. Suspects charged under the updated law would be more likely to be held in jail while awaiting trial rather than released. After serving a prison sentence, offenders would face longer terms of supervised release, meaning more monitoring by authorities once they are out. The bill also requires that illegal images used as evidence stay under government or court control during legal proceedings, rather than being copied and shared in the discovery process.

This bill mostly affects law enforcement agencies, federal prosecutors, and the courts, giving them stronger tools to investigate and punish crimes involving child sexual abuse material. It also affects people convicted of or charged with these offenses, who would face stricter conditions before, during, and after a trial. Advocates for child protection and legal defense attorneys would also be impacted, since the changes shift how these cases are handled throughout the justice system.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets AI-generated child abuse images and removes statute of limitations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4831 criminalizes AI-generated child sexual abuse images, removes the filing deadline for charges, and tightens pretrial detention and supervised release rules.
ai_sections.what: HR 4831 closes a legal loophole by explicitly making AI-generated or drawn child sexual abuse material a federal crime, even when no real child was involved in production. The bill eliminates the statute of limitations for these offenses, allowing prosecution years or decades after the crime. It also restricts how evidence images are handled during legal proceedings, requiring they remain under government or court control rather than being copied in discovery.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects federal prosecutors, law enforcement agencies, and courts, which would gain expanded investigative and charging tools. People charged with or convicted of these offenses would face stricter pretrial detention, longer supervised release after prison, and tighter evidence-handling procedures.
ai_sections.why: Removing the statute of limitations means cases that were previously time-barred could move forward, expanding the pool of prosecutable offenses. Stricter pretrial detention and longer post-sentence supervision alter conditions for accused and convicted individuals throughout the justice process, which also shifts how defense attorneys and child protection advocates engage with these cases.

title: Protecting Americans from Russian Litigation Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would block foreign courts' rulings and arbitration decisions from being enforced in the United States when those rulings stem from someone following American sanctions or export control laws. For example, if a U.S. company stopped doing business with a Russian company because U.S. sanctions required it, and a Russian court then ordered the U.S. company to pay damages, that Russian court order could not be collected on in any U.S. federal or state court. The bill also lets defendants immediately move any such case to federal court, where it must be thrown out.

The bill is aimed at protecting American businesses and individuals who cut ties with sanctioned countries, like Russia, and then got sued in foreign courts as a result. It applies to cases already in progress, not just future ones. There are carve-outs to make sure the bill does not block terrorism victims, people harmed by torture or hostage-taking, or parties who voluntarily agreed to settle disputes in U.S. courts or through U.S.-based arbitration.

People most directly affected include U.S. companies that do or did international business and faced foreign lawsuits after complying with sanctions, as well as foreign entities trying to collect on those overseas judgments inside the United States. American consumers and courts would be indirectly affected since it changes what kinds of foreign legal decisions can be enforced here. The bill was introduced with bipartisan support and has been sent to the Senate Judiciary Committee.
ai_headline: Senate bill shields U.S. firms from foreign court orders tied to sanctions compliance
last_action_text: Held at the desk.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2934 bars U.S. courts from enforcing foreign judgments against Americans who complied with U.S. sanctions or export controls.
ai_sections.what: S 2934 would prevent foreign court rulings and arbitration decisions from being enforced in any U.S. federal or state court when those rulings result from someone following American sanctions or export control laws. Defendants facing such cases can immediately move them to federal court, where they must be dismissed. The bill applies to cases already in progress, not only future ones, and includes carve-outs for terrorism victims, victims of torture or hostage-taking, and parties who voluntarily agreed to U.S.-based dispute resolution.
ai_sections.who: U.S. companies that do or did international business and faced foreign lawsuits after complying with sanctions are most directly affected, as are foreign entities attempting to collect on those overseas judgments inside the United States. American consumers and courts are indirectly affected because the bill changes which foreign legal decisions can be enforced in the country.
ai_sections.why: Foreign courts, including those in sanctioned countries such as Russia, have issued damage awards against U.S. businesses that cut ties to comply with American law, and those awards can currently be pursued in U.S. courts. This bill would close that enforcement pathway, altering the legal exposure of any party on either side of such disputes.

title: Protecting Americans from Russian Litigation Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill blocks foreign courts and arbitration panels from using US sanctions as a basis to sue American individuals or companies in US courts. Right now, when the US government puts sanctions on a country like Russia, American businesses sometimes have to cancel contracts or stop doing business with certain people. Those foreign companies or governments can then go to a foreign court or arbitration panel, win a judgment against the American business, and try to collect that money through US courts. This bill would make US courts refuse to recognize or enforce those foreign judgments and awards.

The law applies to any situation where an American person or company broke a contract specifically because they were trying to follow US sanctions or export control laws. If a foreign party tries to bring that kind of case to a US court, the American defendant can have it moved to federal court and thrown out. The title references Russia, but the bill's language covers sanctions against any foreign country, so it would apply broadly wherever US sanctions exist.

This primarily affects American businesses, banks, and individuals who deal in international trade and have faced or could face foreign legal judgments for complying with US sanctions. It also affects foreign companies and governments that might have tried to use US courts to recover money lost because an American partner walked away from a deal due to sanctions. American victims of terrorism and certain other serious crimes are specifically protected and can still pursue their own cases under existing laws.
ai_headline: Bill would bar US courts from enforcing foreign rulings tied to sanctions compliance
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6194 would stop US courts from enforcing foreign judgments against Americans who broke contracts to comply with US sanctions law.
ai_sections.what: HR 6194 would prohibit US courts from recognizing or enforcing foreign court judgments and arbitration awards that arise from an American person or company following US sanctions or export control laws. If a foreign party brings such a case to a US court, the American defendant can have it moved to federal court and dismissed. Although the bill title references Russia, its language applies broadly to sanctions against any foreign country.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects American businesses, banks, and individuals involved in international trade who have faced or could face foreign legal judgments for complying with US sanctions. It also affects foreign companies and governments that might have sought to recover money through US courts after an American partner exited a deal due to sanctions.
ai_sections.why: American victims of terrorism and certain other serious crimes are specifically protected and may still pursue their own cases under existing laws. The bill creates a legal mechanism that limits the reach of foreign courts and arbitration panels over conduct that US law required or encouraged.

title: Bank Fraud Technology Advancement Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells federal banking regulators to study how well banks and credit unions are using advanced technology, like artificial intelligence and data analytics, to catch and stop fraud. The agencies have 18 months to complete the study and send a report to Congress. The report must cover what fraud-fighting tools are currently being used, how well they work, what is stopping financial institutions from using them more, and whether current rules make it harder or easier to adopt new technology.

A big focus of the bill is on smaller, community-based banks and credit unions. These smaller institutions often cannot afford the same high-tech fraud detection tools that large national banks use. The study must look at whether that puts everyday customers at smaller banks at a disadvantage and explore options like shared services or group buying arrangements that could help smaller institutions access better tools without bearing the full cost alone.

After the study is done, regulators may set up a voluntary pilot program to help community banks and credit unions actually try out some of these advanced fraud detection tools. This program could include shared purchasing deals, technical help, and clearer rules about how to use AI responsibly. The bill affects federal banking regulators, banks, credit unions of all sizes, and ultimately everyday bank customers, since better fraud detection could mean fewer people lose money to scams or identity theft.
ai_headline: Federal regulators ordered to study AI fraud detection at banks and credit unions
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 52 - 1.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8671 directs federal banking regulators to study AI fraud-detection tools and report to Congress within 18 months.
ai_sections.what: HR 8671 requires federal banking regulators to study how banks and credit unions use advanced technology, including artificial intelligence and data analytics, to detect and prevent fraud. The agencies must complete the study and deliver a report to Congress within 18 months. The report must address what tools are currently in use, how effective they are, what barriers limit adoption, and whether existing rules help or hinder new technology.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects federal banking regulators, banks, and credit unions of all sizes. Everyday customers at those institutions are also affected, since fraud detection quality can determine whether individuals lose money to scams or identity theft.
ai_sections.why: Smaller community banks and credit unions often lack access to the same high-tech fraud tools available to large national banks, which may put their customers at a disadvantage. After the study, regulators may launch a voluntary pilot program offering shared purchasing arrangements, technical assistance, and clearer AI guidelines to help smaller institutions access better tools.

title: Build Nuclear with Local Materials Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC), which oversees nuclear power plants, to start writing new rules that would allow builders to use regular commercial-grade steel and concrete at nuclear plants. Right now, nuclear plants often have to use specially certified materials even for parts of the facility that are not directly connected to nuclear safety, like office buildings, warehouses, or other support structures on the property. The bill would have to be acted on within 90 days of becoming law.

The key distinction in the bill is between "safety-related" and "non-safety-related" structures. Safety-related parts of a nuclear plant, like the reactor containment building, would not be affected and would keep their strict material requirements. Only structures that do not play a direct role in preventing a nuclear accident or protecting the public from radiation would be eligible for the looser standard. The NRC can still require stricter materials for non-safety structures if it finds a specific safety reason to do so.

The bill would most directly affect companies that build and operate nuclear power plants, construction contractors, and material suppliers. Supporters say it could lower construction costs and make it easier to source materials locally. Critics may question whether the line between safety and non-safety structures is always clear. Everyday Americans could be affected if the change influences how quickly or cheaply new nuclear plants can be built, which could in turn affect electricity supply and prices in areas where new plants are developed.
ai_headline: Senate bill would let nuclear builders use standard commercial materials on non-safety structures
last_action_text: Committee on Environment and Public Works Subcommittee on Clean Air, Climate, and Nuclear Innovation and Safety. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4529 directs the NRC to draft rules within 90 days allowing commercial-grade steel and concrete in non-safety nuclear structures.
ai_sections.what: S 4529 directs the Nuclear Regulatory Commission to begin writing new rules that would allow standard commercial-grade steel and concrete to be used in non-safety-related structures at nuclear plants, such as office buildings, warehouses, and other support facilities. Safety-related structures like reactor containment buildings would keep their existing strict material requirements. The NRC would retain authority to require stricter materials for non-safety structures if a specific safety reason is identified.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects companies that build and operate nuclear power plants, construction contractors, and material suppliers. Everyday Americans in areas where new plants are developed could be indirectly affected through potential changes to electricity supply and prices.
ai_sections.why: Supporters say the change could lower construction costs and make it easier to source materials locally. Critics raise questions about whether the boundary between safety-related and non-safety-related structures is always clearly defined in practice.

title: Cultural Trade Promotion Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the federal government to do more to help American creative workers sell their products and services in other countries. It updates existing trade laws to include musicians, artists, craftspeople, writers, performers, designers, and others in creative fields when federal agencies plan their export promotion strategies. It also expands protections and export support that previously applied only to American Indian arts and crafts to now include Native Hawaiian arts and crafts as well.

The bill makes a few specific changes. It tells the U.S. Commercial Service, which is the government office that helps American businesses sell abroad, to also focus on very small businesses called microenterprises. It requires trade officials from the Commerce Department and the U.S. Postal Service to work together to find better international shipping options for small and micro businesses. It also adds a permanent seat on the federal Travel and Tourism Advisory Board for someone who represents creative industries.

The people most directly affected are small and independent creative workers, including freelancers, artists, musicians, craftspeople, authors, and small creative businesses across the country, as well as Native Hawaiian artists and cultural workers. Indirectly, it could affect communities where creative industries play a significant role in the local economy, including Native American communities. The bill does not create a new federal agency or spend new money directly; it mainly updates existing programs and committees to pay more attention to this group of workers.
ai_headline: Senate bill adds creative workers to federal export programs
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1125 updates trade laws to include artists, musicians, and other creative workers in federal export promotion programs.
ai_sections.what: S 1125 directs federal agencies to include creative workers such as musicians, artists, writers, performers, designers, and craftspeople in export promotion strategies. The bill also expands arts and crafts protections and export support previously limited to American Indian communities to now cover Native Hawaiian artists as well. It updates existing programs and committees rather than creating any new federal agency.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects small and independent creative workers, including freelancers, small creative businesses, and Native Hawaiian artists and cultural workers across the country. Communities where creative industries are significant to the local economy, including Native American communities, could also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Federal export promotion efforts have not formally included creative workers or microenterprises, leaving this group without dedicated support from offices like the U.S. Commercial Service. Adding a permanent seat on the Travel and Tourism Advisory Board for a creative industries representative and requiring the Commerce Department and U.S. Postal Service to coordinate on international shipping options could shift how small creative businesses access global markets.

title: Small Business Health Options Awareness Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill requires the Small Business Administration (SBA) to spread the word to small businesses about a health benefits option called an Individual Coverage Health Reimbursement Arrangement, or ICHRA. An ICHRA lets an employer give employees a set amount of tax-free money each month, which workers then use to buy their own individual health insurance plans. Right now, many small business owners may not know this option exists. The bill would have the SBA share information about ICHRAs through its local offices, Small Business Development Centers, its website, social media, and press releases.

The bill does not create a new program or change any existing health insurance rules. It simply directs the SBA to act as an information channel, passing along guidance already developed by federal agencies like the Treasury Department, the Department of Health and Human Services, and the Department of Labor.

Small business owners are the main group this bill targets, since they are often the ones trying to figure out affordable ways to offer health benefits to employees. Workers at small businesses could also be indirectly affected, since learning about ICHRAs might lead their employer to offer this kind of benefit. The bill does not require any business to change what it does — it only aims to make sure small business owners know this option is available to them.
ai_headline: SBA would be required to inform small businesses about a lesser-known health benefit option
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 13 - 11.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5498 directs the SBA to publicize ICHRAs, a benefit letting employers give workers tax-free money to buy their own health insurance.
ai_sections.what: HR 5498 requires the Small Business Administration to spread awareness about Individual Coverage Health Reimbursement Arrangements, known as ICHRAs. An ICHRA allows an employer to give employees a set amount of tax-free money each month that workers use to purchase their own individual health insurance plans. The bill does not create a new program or change any existing health insurance rules.
ai_sections.who: Small business owners are the primary audience, as they are often seeking affordable ways to offer health benefits to employees. Workers at small businesses could be indirectly affected if their employer learns about and decides to offer this type of benefit.
ai_sections.why: Many small business owners may currently be unaware that the ICHRA option exists, and the bill aims to close that information gap by directing the SBA to share existing federal guidance through local offices, Small Business Development Centers, its website, social media, and press releases. The bill does not require any business to change its practices.

title: Integrated Ocean Observation System Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill renews funding and updates the rules for a network of sensors, buoys, and monitoring systems along America's coasts and oceans. That network collects real-time data on water conditions, weather, tides, and other ocean-related information. The bill keeps the program running through 2030 with $56 million per year and expands its focus to include meteorological, meaning weather-related, observations alongside the existing ocean and coastal monitoring. It also requires that after any major storm, officials must review how well the system's data helped forecasters predict that storm.

The bill also makes sure that regional monitoring stations around the country get a guaranteed share of the funding, with each established regional system receiving at least 7.5 percent of the annual budget. It updates some internal terminology, changing references from a "Council" to a "Committee" to reflect how the program is currently organized, and adds requirements for better data sharing between federal agencies and local regional systems.

This bill most directly affects federal agencies like NOAA, regional coastal monitoring organizations, researchers, and emergency managers who rely on ocean and weather data. It also indirectly affects coastal communities, fishermen, shipping operators, and anyone who depends on accurate storm forecasting and ocean conditions for safety or business decisions.
ai_headline: Senate bill extends coastal ocean monitoring network through 2030
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 405.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2126 funds a U.S. coastal sensor and buoy network at $56M/year through 2030 and expands it to cover weather data.
ai_sections.what: S 2126 renews and updates a national network of sensors, buoys, and monitoring systems that collect real-time data on water conditions, weather, tides, and ocean information along U.S. coasts. The bill expands the program's scope to include meteorological observations and requires a formal review of the network's data performance after any major storm. It also updates internal terminology and adds requirements for better data sharing between federal agencies and regional systems.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects federal agencies like NOAA, regional coastal monitoring organizations, researchers, and emergency managers who rely on ocean and weather data. Coastal communities, fishermen, shipping operators, and anyone depending on accurate storm forecasting are indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Guaranteeing at least 7.5 percent of the annual budget to each established regional system determines how monitoring resources are distributed across the country. Post-storm review requirements create new accountability standards for how well forecast data performs during major weather events.
ai_sections.cost: The program is funded at $56 million per year and is authorized to run through 2030. Regional systems are each guaranteed at least 7.5 percent of that annual budget.
costChips: Funding split between federal and regional

title: Blast Overpressure Research and Mitigation Task Force Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of Veterans Affairs to create a special task force focused on blast overpressure injuries. Blast overpressure happens when an explosion sends a powerful pressure wave through the air that can damage the brain and body, even without a visible wound. The task force would be a joint effort between the VA and the Department of Defense, and it must be up and running within 180 days of the bill becoming law.

The task force would have several jobs. It would work to improve medical care and benefits for veterans and active-duty troops who were hurt by blasts, including those with traumatic brain injuries, PTSD, balance problems, hearing or vision loss, and gut or nervous system issues. It would also coordinate research between the VA and the military to make sure scientists are studying the most important questions, and it would help develop better tools to diagnose these injuries, including portable devices that could be used in the field or at home.

Every year, the task force would report to Congress on what it has learned and recommend improvements to how the VA reviews disability claims tied to blast injuries. The people most directly affected are veterans and active-duty military members who have been exposed to explosions during their service, a group that includes many combat veterans as well as troops who train with heavy weapons. The task force is set to automatically end on September 30, 2029.
ai_headline: VA ordered to launch blast injury task force within 180 days
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6444 directs the VA and DoD to form a blast overpressure injury task force within 180 days, reporting annually to Congress until 2029.
ai_sections.what: HR 6444 directs the Department of Veterans Affairs and the Department of Defense to jointly establish a task force focused on blast overpressure injuries, which are caused by explosion-generated pressure waves that can harm the brain and body without visible wounds. The task force would improve care and benefits, coordinate research, develop better diagnostic tools including portable field devices, and report annually to Congress on findings and disability claims improvements. It is set to automatically end on September 30, 2029.
ai_sections.who: Veterans and active-duty military members exposed to explosions during service are the primary population affected, including combat veterans and troops who train with heavy weapons. Those with traumatic brain injuries, PTSD, balance problems, hearing or vision loss, and gut or nervous system issues are specifically identified.
ai_sections.why: The task force would directly shape how the VA reviews and processes disability claims tied to blast injuries, potentially altering outcomes for a large number of claimants. Annual congressional reports would create an ongoing public record of gaps in care, research, and diagnostic capacity for this category of injury.

title: Rural and Municipal Utility Cybersecurity Act
ai_summary:
This bill renews and updates a federal grant program that helps smaller electric utilities improve their cybersecurity. It authorizes up to $250 million over five years, from 2026 through 2030, for the Department of Energy to hand out grants, prizes, and technical assistance. The money is meant to help these utilities buy better security technology, defend against cyberattacks, and share information about threats with other utilities and the government.

The program focuses on smaller and local power providers that often have fewer resources to protect themselves than large private companies do. Eligible recipients include rural electric cooperatives, city- and county-owned utilities, and smaller investor-owned utilities that sell less than 4 million megawatt-hours of electricity per year. Priority goes to utilities that have especially limited cybersecurity budgets, or that provide power to military or other critical defense facilities.

The bill also includes a privacy protection for utilities that participate. Any information a utility shares with the government through this program is kept confidential and cannot be released through Freedom of Information Act requests or similar state public-records laws. This is meant to encourage utilities to openly share details about vulnerabilities or attacks without worrying that sensitive information could become public.
ai_headline: Federal grants target cybersecurity gaps at smaller electric utilities
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 545.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7266 authorizes $250M over five years for DOE grants to help smaller electric utilities defend against cyberattacks.
ai_sections.what: HR 7266 renews and updates a federal grant program run by the Department of Energy that supports cybersecurity improvements at smaller electric utilities. The program can provide grants, prizes, and technical assistance to help these utilities buy better security technology, defend against cyberattacks, and share threat information with other utilities and the government. Information that participating utilities share with the government is kept confidential and cannot be released under the Freedom of Information Act or similar state public-records laws.
ai_sections.who: Eligible recipients include rural electric cooperatives, city- and county-owned utilities, and smaller investor-owned utilities that sell less than 4 million megawatt-hours of electricity per year. Priority goes to utilities with especially limited cybersecurity budgets or those that supply power to military or critical defense facilities.
ai_sections.why: Smaller and local power providers typically have fewer resources to protect themselves from cyber threats than large private companies do. The confidentiality provision is intended to encourage utilities to share details about vulnerabilities or attacks without concern that sensitive information could become public.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $250 million over five years, covering 2026 through 2030, administered by the Department of Energy.

title: SECURE Grid Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires states to update their energy security plans to specifically address the safety and reliability of local power distribution systems. These are the smaller electrical networks, running at lower voltages, that deliver electricity directly to homes, businesses, and neighborhoods. Right now, state energy plans focus more on the larger power grid, so this bill fills a gap by making sure states also plan for threats to the "last mile" of electricity delivery.

The bill expands what states must cover in those plans. States would need to address physical threats like extreme weather and attacks on equipment, cybersecurity threats targeting local power lines and systems, and risks in the supply chain for electrical equipment. States must also outline how they would respond to and recover from those threats. The bill also extends the program's funding authorization from 2025 to 2030, keeping federal support available longer.

The people most directly affected are state energy officials, who must revise their planning documents, and electric utilities, who would be consulted in that process. Equipment suppliers for the power grid are also brought into the conversation. Everyday residents are indirectly affected, since stronger local grid planning could mean faster recovery after storms, cyberattacks, or other disruptions that cause power outages in their communities.
ai_headline: States would be required to plan for local grid threats under HR 7257
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 561.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7257 requires states to add local power distribution safety to energy plans and extends federal funding through 2030.
ai_sections.what: HR 7257 requires states to update their energy security plans to cover threats to local power distribution systems, the lower-voltage networks that deliver electricity directly to homes and businesses. States must address physical threats such as extreme weather and equipment attacks, cybersecurity threats, and supply chain risks for electrical equipment. Plans must also outline response and recovery procedures for those threats.
ai_sections.who: State energy officials are directly required to revise their planning documents, with electric utilities and equipment suppliers consulted in the process. Everyday residents are indirectly affected, as the planning changes target disruptions that cause local power outages.
ai_sections.why: Current state energy plans focus primarily on the larger power grid, leaving local distribution systems without dedicated planning requirements. This bill extends those requirements to the final stage of electricity delivery, which means states would be formally accountable for the segment most residents experience during outages.
ai_sections.cost: The bill extends the program's federal funding authorization from 2025 to 2030, keeping federal support available for an additional five years.
costChips: Authorization previously set to end in 2025

title: Pipeline Cybersecurity Preparedness Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the Department of Energy to create a program focused on protecting pipelines and liquefied natural gas facilities from both physical attacks and cyberattacks. The program would work to improve communication and coordination between the federal government, state governments, and the energy industry when it comes to keeping these facilities safe. It also calls for developing new cybersecurity technologies and tools, running test projects to try out security improvements, and creating training programs to build a skilled workforce in this area.

Most of the tools and technologies developed under this program would be available for voluntary use, meaning pipeline and energy companies could choose to adopt them but would not be required to. The bill also makes clear that it does not take away any authority that other federal agencies, like the Transportation Security Administration, already have over pipeline security.

The people most directly affected are workers and companies that operate natural gas pipelines, hazardous liquid pipelines, and liquefied natural gas facilities across the country. More broadly, everyday Americans could be affected indirectly, since these pipelines carry the fuel used to heat homes, generate electricity, and power businesses. Better security for these systems could reduce the risk of disruptions caused by cyberattacks or physical incidents.
ai_headline: Energy bill targets pipeline and LNG facility security against cyber and physical threats
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7272 directs the Dept. of Energy to build a voluntary pipeline and LNG security program covering cyber tools, testing, and workforce training.
ai_sections.what: HR 7272 directs the Department of Energy to establish a program to protect natural gas pipelines, hazardous liquid pipelines, and liquefied natural gas facilities from physical and cyberattacks. The program would develop cybersecurity technologies and tools, run pilot projects to test security improvements, and create workforce training programs. Most tools and technologies developed would be available for voluntary adoption by pipeline and energy companies.
ai_sections.who: Workers and companies that operate natural gas pipelines, hazardous liquid pipelines, and liquefied natural gas facilities are most directly affected. Everyday Americans who rely on these systems for home heating, electricity, and business energy use could be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Improved security measures could reduce the risk of disruptions to energy supply caused by cyberattacks or physical incidents. The bill also preserves existing authority that other federal agencies, such as the Transportation Security Administration, already hold over pipeline security.

title: Energy Threat Analysis Center Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill renews and expands a Department of Energy program focused on protecting the U.S. energy grid from cyberattacks. The program, which was set to expire, would be funded through 2031 instead. The bill also creates something called an Energy Threat Analysis Center, where government officials and energy companies can work together to share information about cyber threats, study how hackers might attack power systems, and develop ways to stop or limit the damage from those attacks.

The bill allows classified and unclassified threat information to be shared between the government and energy companies. Importantly, any information shared through this program is protected from public disclosure laws, meaning it cannot be obtained through Freedom of Information Act requests or similar state laws. The government also keeps full control over who gets help or information through the program, and no company or group is guaranteed access just because others received it.

This affects people and organizations across the U.S. energy sector, including electric utilities, natural gas companies, and other energy providers, as well as the federal, state, tribal, and local governments that work with them. For everyday Americans, the goal is to reduce the risk of cyberattacks that could knock out electricity or disrupt other energy services that people rely on at home and at work.
ai_headline: Energy grid cyber program extended, new threat-sharing center created
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 563.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7305 extends a DOE grid cybersecurity program to 2031 and creates a center for government-industry threat sharing.
ai_sections.what: HR 7305 renews a Department of Energy program that protects the U.S. energy grid from cyberattacks, extending its funding through 2031. The bill also establishes an Energy Threat Analysis Center where government agencies and energy companies can share classified and unclassified threat information and develop defenses against attacks. Information shared through the program is shielded from Freedom of Information Act requests and similar state disclosure laws.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects electric utilities, natural gas companies, and other energy providers across the U.S. energy sector, as well as federal, state, tribal, and local governments that work with them. The government retains full discretion over which companies or groups receive access, and no participant is guaranteed help simply because others received it.
ai_sections.why: Cyberattacks on power systems could knock out electricity or disrupt energy services that households and businesses depend on. Closer coordination between government and industry is intended to reduce that risk, though participation and access remain entirely at the government's discretion.

title: To prohibit the District of Columbia from requiring tribunals in court or administrative proceedings in the District of Columbia to defer to the Mayor of the District of Columbia's interpretation of statutes and regulations, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill is about who gets the final say when there is a disagreement over what a DC law or regulation means. Right now, courts and other official bodies in Washington DC can be required to simply accept the Mayor's or a city agency's interpretation of the law when settling disputes. This bill would ban that practice, meaning judges and other decision-makers would have to read and interpret the law for themselves, rather than automatically siding with the Mayor or agency on what the law means.

This matters because many legal disputes involve questions like "what does this rule actually require?" When a government agency is allowed to define its own rules, it has more power in those fights. Removing that advantage means courts act as a more independent check on the Mayor and city agencies.

This bill would affect Washington DC residents who challenge city government decisions, businesses or individuals in disputes with DC agencies, and the DC government itself, which would lose some of its legal edge in those proceedings. It also repeals a recent DC law that had moved things in the opposite direction. Because Congress has special authority over DC, it can step in and change how DC courts operate in ways it cannot do with state courts.
ai_headline: Bill strips DC Mayor and agencies of automatic win in legal disputes over local rules
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 565.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3766 bans DC courts from automatically deferring to the Mayor or city agencies when interpreting DC laws and regulations.
ai_sections.what: HR 3766 prohibits courts and official decision-makers in Washington DC from being required to accept the Mayor's or a city agency's interpretation of local laws and regulations. Judges would instead have to read and interpret those laws independently. The bill also repeals a recent DC law that had moved in the opposite direction.
ai_sections.who: DC residents, businesses, and individuals who challenge city government decisions would be affected, as would the DC government itself. Congress is using its special authority over Washington DC to make these changes, which it cannot do with state courts.
ai_sections.why: When agencies can define their own rules in disputes, they hold a structural advantage over the people and businesses challenging them. Requiring courts to interpret laws independently shifts that balance and makes courts a more independent check on the Mayor and city agencies.

title: International Trafficking Victims Protection Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill renews and updates a longstanding U.S. law aimed at fighting human trafficking in other countries. It keeps key anti-trafficking programs running through 2029 or 2030 and increases the funding authorized for those programs. For example, it raises one major funding level from $65 million to $102.5 million per year. It also extends the International Megan's Law, which requires the government to notify other countries when a registered sex offender plans to travel there.

The bill makes several practical changes to how the U.S. deals with countries that have human trafficking problems. It renames the "Special Watch List" to the "Tier 2 Watch List" to make the system clearer, and it directs U.S. officials at international banks to push for anti-trafficking protections in development projects in high-risk countries. It also clarifies exactly what types of foreign aid can be cut off when a country is failing to address trafficking, while making sure that humanitarian aid, health programs, and food assistance are not interrupted.

The bill also adds new protections for a specific group of vulnerable workers inside the United States: household employees who work for foreign diplomats and international organization staff, holding special A-3 or G-5 visas. It requires the government to expand a national registration program for these workers, inform them of their legal rights annually, and require their employers to report wages. Diplomats who abuse these workers could face visa suspensions or criminal consequences. Congress would also receive more regular briefings on which countries are improving or worsening on trafficking and why waivers from penalties are granted.
ai_headline: U.S. anti-trafficking law extended and expanded through 2029–2030
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 2647 renews U.S. anti-trafficking programs through 2029–2030 and raises a key funding level from $65M to $102.5M per year.
ai_sections.what: S 2647 reauthorizes longstanding U.S. anti-human-trafficking programs, extending them through 2029 or 2030 depending on the program. It renames the Special Watch List to the Tier 2 Watch List, clarifies which foreign aid can be cut when countries fail to act on trafficking, and extends International Megan's Law requiring governments to be notified when a registered sex offender plans to travel abroad. It also creates new protections for household workers inside the United States who hold A-3 or G-5 visas and work for foreign diplomats or international organization staff.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects foreign countries subject to U.S. trafficking ratings, registered sex offenders who travel internationally, and household workers on A-3 or G-5 visas employed by diplomats or international organization staff in the United States. U.S. officials at international banks and members of Congress receiving updated briefings are also implicated.
ai_sections.why: Countries rated poorly on trafficking could face more clearly defined cuts to specific categories of foreign aid, while humanitarian, health, and food assistance would remain protected. Diplomats who abuse household workers under their employ could face visa suspensions or criminal consequences, and employers would be required to report wages for those workers.
ai_sections.cost: One major anti-trafficking funding authorization is raised from $65 million to $102.5 million per year. The bill also requires expansion of a national registration program for A-3 and G-5 visa workers and mandates annual rights notifications and employer wage reporting for that group.
costChips: Funding raised from $65M to $102.5M per year
Expanded registration program for visa workers

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 324 Washington Avenue, Suite 1 in Grand Haven, Michigan, as the "Ell Thomas Simantz Post Office Building".
ai_summary:
This bill would officially rename the post office located at 324 Washington Avenue in Grand Haven, Michigan. It would give the building the name "Ell Thomas Simantz Post Office Building." The name change would apply to all official government documents, maps, and records that refer to that location.

Bills like this are a common and routine way for Congress to honor individuals by naming a public building after them. The bill was introduced by Representative Scholten along with several other Michigan representatives from both parties. It has been sent to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform for review before any further votes.

This bill mainly affects residents of Grand Haven, Michigan, and anyone who uses or corresponds with that specific post office. It does not change how the post office operates, affect postal services, or spend any federal money. It is purely a symbolic designation to recognize someone named Ell Thomas Simantz.
ai_headline: Grand Haven post office to be renamed for Ell Thomas Simantz
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8999 would rename the Grand Haven, MI post office at 324 Washington Ave after Ell Thomas Simantz, at no federal cost.
ai_sections.what: HR 8999 would officially rename the post office at 324 Washington Avenue in Grand Haven, Michigan, giving it the name "Ell Thomas Simantz Post Office Building." The new name would apply to all official government documents, maps, and records that reference that location. The bill makes no changes to postal operations or services.
ai_sections.who: The bill was introduced by Representative Scholten along with several other Michigan representatives from both parties. It primarily affects residents of Grand Haven and anyone who uses or corresponds with that specific post office.
ai_sections.why: The renaming is a symbolic designation intended to recognize Ell Thomas Simantz. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform for review before any further votes.

title: A bill to designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 12109 New Kent Highway in New Kent, Virginia, as the "Patricia Paige Post Office".
ai_summary:
This bill would officially rename a post office in New Kent, Virginia, in honor of a person named Patricia Paige. The building at 12109 New Kent Highway would carry the new name in all official government documents, maps, and records going forward. It was introduced by Virginia's two U.S. Senators and has been sent to a Senate committee for review.

Bills like this are common in Congress and are usually a way to honor a local person who made a meaningful contribution to their community. The bill does not change how the post office operates, affect postal services, or spend any federal money. It is purely a symbolic recognition.

This bill directly affects residents of New Kent, Virginia, who use that post office, since the building's official name would change. It has no practical impact on the rest of the country.
ai_headline: Senate bill would rename New Kent, Virginia post office for Patricia Paige
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4623 would rename the post office at 12109 New Kent Highway in Virginia after local honoree Patricia Paige.
ai_sections.what: S 4623 would officially rename the post office at 12109 New Kent Highway in New Kent, Virginia, in honor of Patricia Paige. The new name would appear in all official government documents, maps, and records. The bill does not change postal operations or services in any way.
ai_sections.who: The bill was introduced by Virginia's two U.S. Senators and has been referred to a Senate committee for review. It directly affects residents of New Kent, Virginia, who use that post office.
ai_sections.why: Bills of this type are a common congressional practice used to recognize individuals who made a meaningful contribution to their community. The change is symbolic and carries no practical effect beyond the building's official name.

title: Scratch Cooked Meals for Students Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a pilot grant program run by the U.S. Department of Agriculture to help school districts prepare more meals from scratch using fresh, unprocessed ingredients instead of pre-packaged or heavily processed foods. The program would award competitive grants to school food authorities, meaning the local agencies that manage cafeteria programs. Each grant would last two school years, and up to $20 million per year could be approved for the program from 2027 through 2031. Schools that receive grants could use the money to train cafeteria staff, buy new kitchen equipment, update facilities, pay workers for the extra labor involved, and educate students about food through things like taste tests and cooking lessons.

Not all schools would have equal chances of receiving a grant. The program gives top priority to districts where the most students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, meaning lower-income communities would be first in line. Priority is also given to school districts that run their own food service rather than outsourcing it to a private company, and to districts where cafeteria workers have or are working toward a union contract or similar labor agreement. Schools that do not yet meet those conditions can still qualify if they commit to meeting them before the grant ends.

This bill mainly affects public school students, school cafeteria workers, and school administrators across the country. Students in lower-income districts stand to benefit the most since those schools are prioritized.
