Cafeteria employees could see changes in their job duties, training opportunities, and potentially their wages. The bill is currently in the early stages and has only been introduced in the Senate, so it has not become law yet.
ai_headline: Senate bill would fund scratch cooking in school cafeterias
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: A Senate bill would give USDA grants to schools to cook fresh meals from scratch, prioritizing low-income districts, with up to $20M per year available.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 4599 would create a pilot grant program at the U.S. Department of Agriculture to help school districts shift from pre-packaged or heavily processed foods to meals made from scratch using fresh, unprocessed ingredients. Grants would last two school years each and could fund staff training, kitchen equipment, facility updates, additional labor costs, and student food education. The program would run from 2027 through 2031 and has only been introduced in the Senate so far.
ai_sections.who: The program would affect public school students, cafeteria workers, and school administrators nationwide. Students in lower-income districts would be first in line, as the program gives top priority to schools where the most students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch.
ai_sections.why: Cafeteria workers could see changes in job duties, training opportunities, and potentially wages. Districts that outsource food service to private companies or lack union contracts for cafeteria workers would receive lower priority unless they commit to changing those conditions before the grant ends.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $20 million per year could be approved for the program, covering the years 2027 through 2031.

title: GROUSE Act of 2026
ai_summary:
The GROUSE Act would create a new federal program that pays private landowners to restore wildlife habitat on their forest land. If a landowner's forest was recently logged, is currently being logged, or was damaged by a natural disaster like a wildfire or tornado, they can apply to sign a contract with the U.S. Department of Agriculture. Under that contract, the government would cover 75 percent of the costs to plant native trees, shrubs, and other vegetation that benefits upland birds and wildlife, such as grouse, quail, and similar species. The contracts last up to five years, and landowners must work with a wildlife biologist or forester to create a habitat restoration plan.

There are limits on how much land qualifies. A landowner can only receive payments for up to 250 acres or 5 percent of their eligible forest land, whichever is smaller. The land must also be at least 10 acres and must be privately owned, non-industrial forest land. The government can also provide hands-on technical help to landowners who need guidance carrying out their restoration plan.

This bill mainly affects small and mid-sized private forest landowners across the country, particularly in rural areas where logging and natural disasters are common. It would also benefit hunters, wildlife enthusiasts, and conservation groups who care about upland bird populations. The program would be run through the USDA and built into the existing framework of the Food Security Act of 1985, which already manages several farmland and conservation programs.
ai_headline: Federal program would pay landowners to restore bird habitat on logged forests
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: The GROUSE Act would fund 75% of habitat restoration costs for private forest owners whose land was logged or disaster-damaged, up to 250 acres.
ai_sections.what: The GROUSE Act would create a federal program paying private forest landowners to restore wildlife habitat on recently logged, actively logged, or disaster-damaged land. The government would cover 75 percent of costs to plant native trees, shrubs, and vegetation that supports upland birds like grouse and quail. Contracts last up to five years and require a habitat restoration plan developed with a wildlife biologist or forester.
ai_sections.who: The program applies to small and mid-sized private, non-industrial forest landowners, particularly in rural areas. Hunters, wildlife enthusiasts, and conservation groups focused on upland bird populations would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Upland bird and wildlife populations in logged or disaster-affected forests could increase as native vegetation is restored under government-funded contracts. The program would be administered through the USDA within the existing framework of the Food Security Act of 1985.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would pay 75 percent of restoration costs per contract. Payments are limited to no more than 250 acres or 5 percent of a landowner's eligible forest land, whichever is smaller, and the parcel must be at least 10 acres.
costChips: Max 250 acres or 5% of eligible land

title: African Development Foundation Termination Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would shut down the United States African Development Foundation, a small, independent federal agency created in 1980. The Foundation's main job has been to give grants and loans directly to grassroots community groups and small businesses in African countries to help them grow economically. If this bill becomes law, the Foundation would officially close 120 days after it is signed. No new grants or loans could be issued from the moment the bill is signed, and any unspent government money would go back to the U.S. Treasury. Existing multi-year grants that are already in place would be managed by the State Department until they naturally expire.

The bill also spells out what happens to the Foundation's staff, property, and records. Employees would receive legally required notices about losing their jobs, following standard federal layoff rules. All files, contracts, and other assets would be handed over to the State Department, which would only use them to wrap up whatever is already underway, not to continue the Foundation's work in any new form.

This bill mainly affects the Foundation's roughly 50 or so federal employees, who would lose their positions. It also affects the African community organizations, farmers, and small business owners who currently receive or were hoping to receive funding from the Foundation. American taxpayers are affected in the sense that the agency's annual budget, which has been around 30 to 40 million dollars in recent years, would no longer be spent on this program.
ai_headline: Bill to shut down U.S. African Development Foundation clears path to closure
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported by the Yeas and Nays: 26 - 20.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7605 would permanently close the U.S. African Development Foundation 120 days after signing, ending all new grants and loans to African community groups.
ai_sections.what: HR 7605 would permanently close the United States African Development Foundation, a small independent federal agency established in 1980. The Foundation would shut down 120 days after the bill is signed, with no new grants or loans permitted from the moment of signing. Existing multi-year grants would transfer to the State Department solely to be wound down, not continued.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the Foundation's roughly 50 federal employees, who would lose their jobs under standard federal layoff rules. It also affects African community organizations, farmers, and small business owners who currently receive or were expecting to receive funding from the Foundation.
ai_sections.why: All unspent federal funds held by the Foundation would be returned to the U.S. Treasury. The Foundation's files, contracts, and assets would pass to the State Department, which would use them only to close out existing work with no authority to extend the Foundation's mission in any new form.
ai_sections.cost: The Foundation's annual budget has been approximately 30 to 40 million dollars in recent years. That funding would no longer be spent on this program if the bill becomes law.
costChips: No new grants or loans upon signing

title: Biological Intellectual Property Protection Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require U.S. companies and researchers to get a government license before sending digital genetic sequences to certain foreign adversaries. Specifically, it covers synthetic DNA and RNA sequences that were designed by humans or artificial intelligence. The targets are countries the U.S. already considers threats, most notably China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. The idea is that even sharing a digital file containing a gene sequence could allow a foreign government to copy or steal American biotech research, so the bill adds that kind of data to existing export control rules.

The bill affects biotechnology companies, university research labs, and scientists who work with synthetic biology and might collaborate with or send data to researchers or organizations in those countries. If this becomes law, those groups would need to apply for a federal license before transferring that type of genetic data internationally. There is an exception for information that is already considered publicly available or educational under current export rules, so basic academic publishing would likely not be affected.

The broader goal is to protect American intellectual property in the fast-growing field of synthetic biology, which has commercial, medical, and potential military uses. Congress is expressing concern that foreign governments, particularly China, could use shared genetic design data to advance their own military or industrial programs. The bill does not ban the research itself, just the transfer of certain genetic sequence files to entities connected to countries on the foreign adversary list.
ai_headline: New bill would require licenses to share synthetic gene data with foreign adversaries
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 30 - 14.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6624 would require a federal license before sending synthetic DNA or RNA sequence files to China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea.
ai_sections.what: HR 6624 would add synthetic DNA and RNA sequences designed by humans or artificial intelligence to existing U.S. export control rules. Companies and researchers would need to apply for a federal government license before transferring those genetic sequence files to entities in designated foreign adversary countries. Information already considered publicly available or educational under current export rules would be exempt, so basic academic publishing would likely not be affected.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects U.S. biotechnology companies, university research labs, and scientists working in synthetic biology who collaborate with or send data to researchers or organizations in China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea. Those groups would be required to obtain a federal license before making such international transfers.
ai_sections.why: Congress has expressed concern that foreign governments, particularly China, could use shared genetic design data to advance their own military or industrial programs. The bill does not ban the underlying research, only the transfer of certain genetic sequence files to entities connected to countries on the foreign adversary list.

title: Abolish Super PACs Act
ai_summary:
This bill would bring Super PACs under the same donation limits that already apply to regular political action committees. Right now, individuals, corporations, and other groups can give unlimited amounts of money to Super PACs. Under this bill, those contributions would be capped. The existing limit for donations to regular PACs is $5,000 per year per donor, and that same kind of restriction would now apply to Super PACs and any committees that funnel money into Super PACs. The bill defines a Super PAC broadly, so groups that pool money to run political ads independently of campaigns would all fall under these new rules.

The bill affects anyone who currently donates large sums to Super PACs, including wealthy individuals, corporations, and other organizations. It would also affect the Super PACs themselves, since they would have far less money available to spend on political ads and other election activities. Candidates for federal office would feel an indirect impact too, since Super PACs often run expensive advertising campaigns that support or oppose specific candidates without directly coordinating with them.

Everyday voters are not directly regulated by this bill, but they would be affected by changes in how elections are funded and what political advertising looks like. The bill was introduced in the Senate in May 2026 and sent to the Committee on Rules and Administration, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on by the full Senate.
ai_headline: Senate bill would cap Super PAC donations at $5,000 per year
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4602 would apply the existing $5,000 annual PAC donation limit to Super PACs, ending unlimited contributions.
ai_sections.what: S 4602 would apply the same $5,000 per year donation cap that currently applies to regular PACs to Super PACs and any committees that funnel money into them. The bill defines Super PACs broadly, covering groups that pool money to run political ads independently of campaigns. It was introduced in the Senate in May 2026 and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration, where it has not yet been debated or voted on.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects wealthy individuals, corporations, and other organizations that currently donate large sums to Super PACs, as well as the Super PACs themselves. Candidates for federal office face indirect effects, since Super PACs that support or oppose them would have significantly less funding available.
ai_sections.why: Super PACs would have far less money to spend on political advertising and other election activities. The broader landscape of how federal elections are funded and what political advertising looks like would change for all voters, though everyday voters are not directly regulated by the bill.

title: CCP Sanctions Shot Clock Act
ai_summary:
The United States government keeps a list called the Non-SDN Chinese Military-Industrial Complex Companies List. This list identifies companies believed to be connected to China's military. American investors are restricted from putting money into companies on this list. Right now, even when the President's own report identifies a company as qualifying for this list, there is no deadline requiring the Treasury Department to actually add it.

This bill would fix that by creating a one-year deadline. Once the President submits a report naming companies that belong on the list, the Secretary of the Treasury would have up to one year to officially add those companies and update the published list. Think of it like a shot clock in basketball — once the timer starts, the government has to act before time runs out.

This bill mainly affects American investors and financial institutions, since they face restrictions on buying stock or investing in companies that appear on the list. It also affects the foreign companies that might be added, since being placed on the list limits their access to U.S. investment dollars. The bill does not directly impact most everyday Americans, but it could affect people who hold certain mutual funds or retirement accounts that include shares of Chinese companies.
ai_headline: Senate bill sets one-year clock on Chinese military company listings
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4581 gives Treasury up to one year to add companies to the Chinese military firms list after the President identifies them.
ai_sections.what: The Non-SDN Chinese Military-Industrial Complex Companies List identifies companies believed to be connected to China's military, and American investors are restricted from investing in those companies. Currently, no deadline requires the Treasury Department to add companies even after the President's report names them as qualifying. S 4581 would create a one-year deadline for the Secretary of the Treasury to officially add those companies and update the published list.
ai_sections.who: American investors and financial institutions are directly affected because they face restrictions on buying stock in listed companies. People holding certain mutual funds or retirement accounts that include shares of Chinese companies could also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without a deadline, companies identified by the President's report can remain off the official list indefinitely, leaving investment restrictions unenforced. Foreign companies named for the list would face limits on their access to U.S. investment dollars once formally added.

title: South China Sea Strategy Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill requires the U.S. Secretary of State to create a formal written plan, within 180 days of the law passing, for how the United States will handle diplomacy in the South China Sea. The South China Sea is a major waterway in Asia where China, the Philippines, Vietnam, and several other countries have overlapping territorial claims. The plan must lay out U.S. goals for the region, identify which government office is in charge of each goal, and describe how the U.S. will work with nearby countries and allies on issues like trade routes, maritime law enforcement, and avoiding military conflict.

The bill also requires the State Department to report back to Congress within about a year on what programs, policies, and funding would be needed to actually carry out that plan through 2029. A follow-up briefing to congressional committees must happen within 30 days after that report. The strategy document would be made publicly available, though it can include a classified section for sensitive information.

The people most directly affected are U.S. diplomats and government agencies who would carry out the strategy, as well as citizens and businesses that depend on safe shipping lanes through the South China Sea, which carries a large share of global trade. Countries bordering the sea, specifically Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Vietnam, would also be involved as partners in the diplomatic efforts described in the plan.
ai_headline: State Department ordered to draft South China Sea diplomacy plan within 180 days
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4600 gives the Secretary of State 180 days to produce a formal South China Sea diplomatic strategy and report funding needs to Congress by 2029.
ai_sections.what: S 4600 requires the U.S. Secretary of State to produce a written diplomatic strategy for the South China Sea within 180 days of enactment. The plan must define U.S. goals, assign responsibility to specific government offices, and address trade routes, maritime law enforcement, and conflict prevention with regional partners. The State Department must also report to Congress on the programs, policies, and funding needed to carry out the strategy through 2029, followed by a congressional briefing within 30 days.
ai_sections.who: U.S. diplomats and federal agencies would be responsible for executing the strategy. Citizens and businesses relying on South China Sea shipping lanes, plus the governments of Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Vietnam, would be directly involved or affected.
ai_sections.why: The South China Sea carries a large share of global trade and is subject to overlapping territorial claims by China, the Philippines, Vietnam, and others. Formalizing a U.S. diplomatic strategy creates an accountable framework for managing those disputes and coordinating with regional allies.
ai_sections.cost: The bill requires the State Department to identify what programs, policies, and funding would be needed to carry out the strategy through 2029, but no specific dollar amounts are established in the bill.
costChips: Funding needs to be identified, not set
State Dept. reports requirements to Congress

title: To amend title 5, United States Code, to add certain employees of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives to the definition of employees in fire protection activities for the purpose of compensation for certain illnesses and diseases deemed to be proximately caused by employment in fire protection activities.
ai_summary:
This bill changes who qualifies for a specific federal workers' compensation benefit. Right now, federal law gives special protections to employees who work in fire protection, meaning if they get certain illnesses or diseases, the government assumes those health problems were caused by their job. This bill would extend that same protection to two groups of ATF employees: Certified Fire Investigators and members of the National Response Team. These are ATF workers who regularly investigate fires, explosions, and arson scenes, where they can be exposed to smoke, toxic chemicals, and other hazardous materials.

In practical terms, this matters because of how workers' compensation claims work. Normally, a sick worker has to prove their illness was caused by their job, which can be difficult and time-consuming. Under the existing fire protection employee rule, that burden is flipped, and the government is presumed responsible unless proven otherwise. By adding these ATF employees to that category, the bill makes it easier for them to receive compensation if they develop illnesses linked to their work at fire and explosion scenes.

This bill directly affects ATF Certified Fire Investigators and National Response Team members who are federal employees. It would apply to any compensation claims filed after the bill becomes law. It does not affect private sector workers, state or local fire investigators, or other ATF employees not in those two specific roles.
ai_headline: ATF fire investigators would gain job-illness presumption under HR 8863
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8863 lets ATF Certified Fire Investigators and National Response Team members skip proving their illness was job-caused.
ai_sections.what: HR 8863 extends an existing federal workers' compensation presumption to two groups of ATF employees. Under current law, fire protection employees do not have to prove their illness was caused by their job — the government is presumed responsible unless shown otherwise. This bill adds ATF Certified Fire Investigators and National Response Team members to that protected category, applying to claims filed after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects ATF Certified Fire Investigators and members of the ATF National Response Team who are federal employees. It does not apply to private sector workers, state or local fire investigators, or other ATF employees outside those two roles.
ai_sections.why: These ATF employees regularly work at fire, explosion, and arson scenes where they can be exposed to smoke, toxic chemicals, and other hazardous materials. Shifting the burden of proof away from the worker removes a difficult and time-consuming step in the claims process for those who develop related illnesses.

title: CARE for RPA Crews Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with how the U.S. military recognizes drone operators who take part in combat. Right now, military members who fight in traditional combat zones can receive a "combat status identifier," which is an official marker on their record showing they engaged in combat. Drone crews, who remotely pilot aircraft and carry out real combat missions from locations that may be far from the battlefield, do not currently receive an equivalent recognition. This bill would require the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marine Corps to create a similar status marker for drone crews within 180 days of the bill becoming law.

This affects military personnel who operate remotely piloted aircraft, commonly known as drones, during combat operations. These service members perform missions that can be just as consequential as those of traditional combat troops, including airstrikes and other combat activities, but their official records do not currently reflect that combat experience in the same way. The new status identifier would give their combat service formal recognition equal in standing to what other combat veterans receive.

The practical impact could include how these service members are evaluated for promotions, benefits, and veteran status recognition, since official military records and combat identifiers often play a role in those decisions. The bill does not specify exact benefits but requires military leadership to design a system that treats drone crew combat service as equally significant to other forms of direct combat participation.
ai_headline: Bill would give drone crews the same combat record status as ground troops
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8934 requires all four military branches to create a combat status marker for drone crews within 180 days of enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 8934 would require the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marine Corps to create a combat status identifier for drone crews within 180 days of the bill becoming law. Currently, service members who fight in traditional combat zones receive an official marker on their records showing they engaged in combat, but remotely piloted aircraft crews do not receive an equivalent recognition. The new identifier would give drone crew combat service formal standing equal to that of other direct combat participants.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects military personnel who operate remotely piloted aircraft during combat operations across all four branches of the armed services. Military leadership in each branch would be responsible for designing the new status system.
ai_sections.why: Because official military records and combat identifiers can factor into promotions, benefits, and veteran status recognition, the absence of an equivalent marker may affect how drone crews' service is evaluated compared to other combat veterans. The bill does not specify exact benefits but requires the branches to treat drone crew combat service as equally significant to other forms of direct combat participation.

title: Combat Action Recognition and Evaluation (CARE) for Remotely Piloted Aircraft (RPA) Crews Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill requires the military to create an official recognition status for drone pilots and crew members who fly combat missions. Right now, there is a combat status identifier that traditional combat troops can receive, but drone operators who fly missions remotely do not have an equivalent recognition, even though they are directly involved in combat operations. The bill gives the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marine Corps 180 days to set up this new status after the law is passed.

The bill affects military personnel who operate remotely piloted aircraft, commonly known as drones, during combat. These crew members control aircraft from ground stations, sometimes located far from the actual battlefield, but they carry out the same kinds of combat missions as pilots flying in person. Currently, the lack of an official combat status for these roles can affect how their service is recognized and recorded.

The practical impact could include how drone crews are formally classified in military records and whether their combat experience is officially acknowledged in a way comparable to other service members in combat roles. The bill does not specify exact benefits or medals, only that the military departments must create a status of equivalent merit to existing combat status identifiers.
ai_headline: Senate bill would give drone combat crews official military status
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2218 orders all four military branches to create a combat status for drone crews within 180 days of enactment.
ai_sections.what: S 2218 requires the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marine Corps to establish an official combat status identifier for remotely piloted aircraft operators who fly combat missions. The four branches have 180 days after the law is passed to create this new status. The bill does not specify particular benefits or medals, only that the status must be of equivalent merit to existing combat identifiers.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects military personnel who operate drones from ground stations during combat operations across all four service branches. These crew members currently have no official combat status equivalent to that held by traditional combat troops, even when conducting the same types of missions.
ai_sections.why: Without an official combat status, drone crews' combat experience may not be formally acknowledged or recorded in military records on the same basis as other service members in combat roles. How personnel are classified in service records can have lasting effects on how their military service is recognized.

title: NEDD Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill is about drones near nuclear facilities. Right now, federal law bans government agencies from buying or using drones made by certain foreign countries considered security risks, like China. A 2024 defense law already gave some cabinet departments exceptions to that ban for special security purposes. This bill adds the Department of Energy to that list of exceptions, meaning the Energy Secretary could buy and use those foreign-made drones if needed, under specific conditions.

The reason this matters is that the Department of Energy oversees the country's nuclear weapons stockpile and nuclear research sites. The bill also expands the Energy Department's authority to counter drones flying over a broader range of nuclear-related facilities, including places that make non-nuclear parts for nuclear weapons, not just sites that handle nuclear materials directly. This gives the department more tools to protect those locations from drone threats.

Most everyday Americans would not be directly affected by this bill. It mainly changes how the federal government manages security at nuclear facilities. Workers at Energy Department sites, contractors who build nuclear components, and federal security personnel are the groups most directly touched by these changes. The bill was introduced with support from both a Democratic and a Republican senator, and it has been sent to the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee for review.
ai_headline: Senate bill lets Energy Dept use foreign-made drones at nuclear sites
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1762 would let the Energy Department buy foreign-made drones banned for most agencies and expand its authority to counter drone threats at nuclear facilities.
ai_sections.what: S 1762 adds the Department of Energy to a list of cabinet departments allowed to buy and use drones made by foreign countries that are otherwise banned as security risks. The bill also expands the department's authority to counter drones flying over a broader set of nuclear-related facilities, including sites that make non-nuclear components for nuclear weapons, not just sites handling nuclear materials directly.
ai_sections.who: Workers at Department of Energy sites, contractors who build nuclear components, and federal security personnel are the groups most directly affected. Most everyday Americans would not be directly affected by this bill.
ai_sections.why: The Department of Energy oversees the country's nuclear weapons stockpile and nuclear research sites, making drone security at those locations a national security concern. Expanding both the drone-purchase exception and the counter-drone authority changes how the federal government manages protection at a wider range of nuclear-related facilities.

title: Department of Energy Drone Defense Act
ai_summary:
This bill gives the Department of Energy (DOE) the same special permissions that a few other federal agencies already have when it comes to drones made by certain foreign companies. Right now, federal law generally bans government agencies from buying, using, or spending federal money on drones that come from countries considered security risks, like China. Some agencies, such as the State Department and Transportation Department, were already given exceptions to that rule so they could use those drones for specific security-related purposes, like detecting or studying drone threats. This bill adds the Secretary of Energy to that list of exceptions.

In practice, this means DOE could buy and operate foreign-made drones that are otherwise restricted, as long as it follows the same rules and reporting requirements the other excepted agencies must follow. The most likely reason is that DOE oversees nuclear weapons sites and national laboratories, which face growing drone threats. Being able to use or study those same drones could help the department figure out how to detect and defend against them.

This bill mainly affects the Department of Energy and the facilities it protects, including nuclear sites like the Nevada National Security Site and national laboratories across the country. It does not change the rules for any other federal agency, private companies, or everyday Americans. It is a narrow technical change that adds one agency to an existing exemption already in federal law.
ai_headline: Energy Department gains access to restricted foreign drones under HR 8935
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8935 adds the Department of Energy to a short list of federal agencies allowed to buy and use otherwise-banned foreign-made drones.
ai_sections.what: Federal law generally prohibits government agencies from buying or using drones from countries considered security risks, including China. HR 8935 adds the Secretary of Energy to an existing list of exceptions already granted to agencies such as the State Department and the Transportation Department. The Department of Energy would be allowed to buy and operate those restricted foreign-made drones as long as it follows the same rules and reporting requirements the other excepted agencies must meet.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects the Department of Energy and the facilities it oversees, including nuclear weapons sites such as the Nevada National Security Site and national laboratories across the country. It does not change the rules for any other federal agency, private companies, or individual Americans.
ai_sections.why: DOE facilities face growing drone threats, and the ability to acquire and study restricted foreign-made drones could inform the department's efforts to detect and defend against them. The change is narrow, adding one agency to an exemption that already exists in federal law without altering the broader restrictions on drone purchases.

title: Epstein Files Transparency Act
ai_summary:
This law requires the U.S. Department of Justice to release its files related to Jeffrey Epstein within 30 days of the bill being signed. That includes records from the FBI and federal prosecutors' offices covering investigations, prosecutions, flight logs, immunity deals, internal communications, and documents about Epstein's detention and death. It also covers records related to his associate Ghislaine Maxwell and any individuals or organizations connected to his criminal network. The documents must be posted online in a searchable, downloadable format so the public can access them.

The law sets clear limits on what the government is allowed to hide. Officials cannot withhold records simply because releasing them would be embarrassing or politically inconvenient. However, they are permitted to redact information that would identify victims, contains child sexual abuse material, could harm an active investigation, or is legitimately classified for national security reasons. Any redaction must be explained in writing and reported to Congress. If something is classified, the Attorney General must try to declassify it or at minimum release a summary of what was withheld.

Within 15 days after the release is complete, the Attorney General must send Congress a full report listing what was released, what was held back, and why. The report must also name any government officials or public figures mentioned in the documents, with no redactions allowed for those names. This law primarily affects the Department of Justice and FBI, but it also directly involves any public figures, government officials, and organizations whose names appear in Epstein-related files, as well as survivors whose privacy protections are written into the law.
ai_headline: Justice Department ordered to publish Epstein files within 30 days
last_action_text: Became Public Law No: 119-38.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4405 gives DOJ 30 days to post Epstein investigative records online in searchable form, with strict limits on what can be redacted.
ai_sections.what: HR 4405 requires the Department of Justice to release records from the FBI and federal prosecutors related to Jeffrey Epstein, including investigations, flight logs, immunity deals, internal communications, and documents about his detention and death. Records tied to Ghislaine Maxwell and others connected to Epstein's criminal network are also covered. All documents must be posted online in a searchable, downloadable format.
ai_sections.who: The law primarily affects the Department of Justice and FBI. It also directly involves public figures, government officials, and organizations named in the files, as well as survivors, whose privacy protections are written into the law.
ai_sections.why: Officials are prohibited from withholding records solely because release would be embarrassing or politically inconvenient. Within 15 days after the release, the Attorney General must send Congress a report listing what was released, what was withheld, and why, including unredacted names of any government officials or public figures mentioned.

title: Section 232 Public Transparency Act
ai_summary:
Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 allows the federal government to investigate whether certain imports pose a threat to national security. If a threat is found, the president can take action such as placing tariffs or other restrictions on those imports. Right now, the reports from these investigations can be kept from the public for a long time, or sometimes are never fully released.

This bill would require the government to publish the unclassified, non-business-sensitive parts of those investigation reports within 270 days of starting an investigation, or when the report is handed to the president, whichever comes first. The idea is to make the process more open so the public can see what findings led to any trade actions taken in the name of national security.

This bill mainly affects businesses that import or export goods, industries that compete with foreign imports, and anyone who wants to understand why the government has imposed certain tariffs or trade restrictions. It also matters to the general public, since Section 232 tariffs can affect the prices of everyday products like steel, aluminum, and the goods made from them.
ai_headline: Senate bill would open national security trade reports to public within 270 days
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4563 requires unclassified parts of Section 232 trade investigation reports to be published within 270 days of an investigation's start.
ai_sections.what: S 4563 would require the federal government to publish the unclassified, non-business-sensitive portions of Section 232 trade investigation reports. The publication deadline is 270 days after an investigation begins or when the report reaches the president, whichever comes first. Currently, these reports can be withheld from the public for extended periods or never fully released.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects importers, exporters, and industries that compete with foreign imports. It also affects the general public, since Section 232 tariffs can influence prices of products such as steel, aluminum, and goods made from them.
ai_sections.why: Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 gives the president authority to impose tariffs or other trade restrictions based on national security findings, but the public often cannot see the underlying evidence for those decisions. Greater disclosure of investigation reports would allow businesses, industries, and the public to understand what findings led to specific trade actions.

title: Golden Mussel Eradication and Control Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a federal program to fight the golden mussel, an invasive species that has been spreading into U.S. waterways, starting with the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta in California. A federal task force would work with states, cities, universities, port authorities, and nonprofits to research the mussel's biology, track where it is spreading, and develop ways to stop and remove it. The task force would also be required to publish guidelines within one year for controlling its spread, including setting up inspection stations for boats.

The bill also sets up a competitive grant program, meaning organizations like universities, nonprofits, and local governments can apply for funding to develop new technologies for removing golden mussels from water pipes, boat hulls, fish screens, canals, and other places they attach themselves. Any useful technologies developed through these grants could be shared or licensed to speed up the response effort nationwide.

The people most directly affected include residents and workers near infested waterways, particularly in California, as well as those who manage water infrastructure, fishing operations, and ports. Boaters could face new inspection requirements at watercraft stations. Farmers and communities that rely on irrigation systems or clean water supplies could also be impacted, since golden mussels are known to clog water intake systems and harm ecosystems. The bill proposes spending up to 15 million dollars per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund these efforts.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets golden mussel invasion in U.S. waterways
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4603 creates a federal task force and grants program to stop golden mussels, funded up to $15M/year from 2026 to 2030.
ai_sections.what: S 4603 establishes a federal task force to research, track, and combat the golden mussel, an invasive species entering U.S. waterways beginning with the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta in California. The task force must publish control guidelines within one year, including setting up boat inspection stations. A competitive grant program would fund universities, nonprofits, and local governments to develop removal technologies for pipes, boat hulls, canals, and other affected infrastructure.
ai_sections.who: Residents, workers, water managers, farmers, fishing operations, and port operators near infested waterways are most directly affected, particularly in California. Boaters could face new inspection requirements at watercraft stations.
ai_sections.why: Golden mussels are known to clog water intake systems and harm ecosystems, posing risks to irrigation-dependent communities and clean water supplies. The spread of the species into U.S. waterways creates pressure on water infrastructure and aquatic environments.
ai_sections.cost: The bill proposes spending up to 15 million dollars per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund these efforts.

title: American Sovereignty and Monterey Historic Military Site Study Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Department of the Interior to study Lower Presidio Historic Park in Monterey, California, to find out whether it should become a National Park. The Secretary of the Interior would have three years after funding is approved to complete the study and report the findings to Congress. The study would look at how historically significant the site is, whether making it a National Park is practical and affordable, and what it would cost the federal government to manage it. It would also consider other options, like having state, local, or tribal governments or private groups take a leading role in preserving the site.

The park has a long and layered history. It was home to the Esselen and Rumsen peoples for thousands of years, was explored by Spanish explorers in the 1500s and 1600s, served as California's capital under Spanish and Mexican rule, and was the spot where the U.S. Navy raised the American flag over California in 1846. It later became an active Army post where future Civil War generals served, where the Buffalo Soldiers were stationed before becoming some of America's first national park rangers, and where the Defense Language Institute was eventually established.

This bill directly affects the federal government, particularly the Interior Department, which would carry out the study. It also involves the City of Monterey, which currently leases and operates the park, as well as local Native American tribes and any organizations connected to the site. Regular residents would not face any immediate changes, since the bill only orders a study, not an actual change in the park's status.
ai_headline: Interior Department ordered to study Monterey historic park for national status
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8735 orders a federal study of Lower Presidio Historic Park in Monterey, CA to assess whether it qualifies as a National Park.
ai_sections.what: HR 8735 directs the U.S. Department of the Interior to study Lower Presidio Historic Park in Monterey, California, to determine whether it should become a National Park. The Secretary of the Interior would have three years after funding is approved to complete the study and report findings to Congress. The study would assess historical significance, practicality, affordability, federal management costs, and alternative preservation arrangements involving state, local, tribal, or private entities.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the federal government, particularly the Interior Department, which would carry out the study. It also involves the City of Monterey, which currently leases and operates the park, as well as local Native American tribes and organizations connected to the site.
ai_sections.why: Because the bill only orders a study rather than a change in park status, residents face no immediate changes to the site or its operations. The study's findings would be reported to Congress, which could inform future decisions about the park's management and designation.
ai_sections.cost: The study would examine what it would cost the federal government to manage the site, but the bill does not specify a funding amount. Funding approval triggers the three-year study timeline.
costChips: Federal management costs to be studied
No funding amount specified in bill

title: Washington’s Trail—1753 National Historic Trail Feasibility Study Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Secretary of the Interior to study whether a roughly 500-mile route through Virginia and Pennsylvania should be officially named a national historic trail. The route follows the path that a young George Washington traveled in late 1753, when he was sent by Virginia's colonial governor to deliver a message to French military forces. That mission took place just before the French and Indian War began. Right now, the bill only orders a study to see if the designation makes sense, not the designation itself.

If the trail is eventually designated after the study, it would join the National Trails System, which includes well-known routes like the Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail. Designation can bring federal attention, funding, and tourism to the communities along the trail. The route would pass through parts of Virginia and Pennsylvania, so residents, local businesses, and governments in those states could be most directly affected.

The people most likely to notice this bill are history enthusiasts, outdoor recreation groups, local tourism businesses, and state and local governments in western Pennsylvania and Virginia. Federal taxpayers would cover the cost of the study, though feasibility studies like this are generally a small expense. No trail is being built or officially named yet. Congress would need to act again based on the study's findings before anything permanent happens.
ai_headline: Interior Department ordered to study 500-mile Washington trail route
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

The route follows the path George Washington traveled in late 1753, when Virginia's colonial governor sent him to deliver a message to French military forces shortly before the French and Indian War. No trail is being officially named or built yet; Congress would need to act again after the study before any permanent designation occurs.
ai_sections.who: History enthusiasts, outdoor recreation groups, local tourism businesses, and state and local governments in western Pennsylvania and Virginia are most likely to be directly affected. Federal taxpayers would cover the cost of the study.
ai_sections.why: If the trail is eventually designated, it would join the National Trails System alongside routes like the Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail, which can bring federal attention, funding, and tourism to communities along the way. The study itself determines only whether designation makes sense, leaving any broader effects dependent on a future act of Congress.
ai_sections.cost: Federal taxpayers would cover the cost of the feasibility study, which the summary describes as generally a small expense.

title: GEO Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with geothermal energy, which is power generated from heat stored underground. Right now, companies that want to drill and develop geothermal energy on federal land have to apply for permits and approvals from the government. Sometimes those applications sit without a decision for a long time, especially when there are ongoing lawsuits related to the project. This bill would require the federal government to make a decision, either approving or denying each application, within 60 days after finishing all required environmental and legal reviews. The rule would apply even if a lawsuit is pending, unless a court has specifically put a stop to the project through a judge's order.

The bill affects energy companies and developers who want to tap into geothermal resources on federal land. It also affects the federal agencies, mainly the Department of the Interior, that process these permit applications. Those agencies would now be held to a firm deadline. The bill does not change the rights of people or groups to sue over a project, and it does not give courts any new or reduced power to block projects. A court can still step in and halt things if it chooses to.

Everyday Americans could be indirectly affected if faster permitting leads to more geothermal energy development, which is a source of renewable electricity. People living near proposed geothermal sites or those involved in related legal challenges would be more directly touched by this change. Overall, the bill is narrowly focused on speeding up the government's paperwork and decision-making process for one specific type of energy project.
ai_headline: Federal agencies face 60-day deadline on geothermal permit decisions
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 568.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 301 sets a 60-day deadline for federal agencies to approve or deny geothermal energy permits on federal land after reviews are done.
ai_sections.what: HR 301 requires the federal government to approve or deny geothermal energy permit applications on federal land within 60 days of completing all required environmental and legal reviews. The deadline applies even when a lawsuit related to the project is pending, unless a court has issued a specific order halting the project.
ai_sections.who: Energy companies and developers seeking to build geothermal projects on federal land are directly affected, as are the federal agencies that process these applications, primarily the Department of the Interior. People living near proposed geothermal sites and parties involved in related legal challenges would also be more directly affected than the general public.
ai_sections.why: Currently, permit applications can sit without a decision for a long time, and this bill would impose a firm deadline on agency decision-making for this specific energy type. Courts retain their existing authority to block projects, and the bill does not change any party's right to sue or reduce judicial power.

title: Protecting Veteran Community Care Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it easier for veterans to get mental health and substance use treatment outside of VA facilities when the VA cannot serve them quickly enough. Right now, the VA's Community Care Program lets veterans see outside doctors under certain conditions, but the bill's sponsors say the VA has been making it too hard for veterans to use that option for mental health care specifically. The bill would add a new rule saying that if a veteran qualifies for a VA Mental Health Residential Rehabilitation Treatment Program but there is no open spot, or if the VA cannot meet its own wait-time standards for that care, the veteran can go to an approved outside treatment program instead and have it covered.

The bill also adds some consumer-style protections. Veterans could not be told they are ineligible for outside care just because no community provider happens to meet the VA's scheduling standards at that moment. If more than one care option is available, the veteran gets to choose which one they want. Outside programs used under this rule would have to be state-licensed and accredited by recognized behavioral health organizations, though the VA could waive those requirements in limited cases where no other option exists.

Finally, the bill requires the VA to start tracking and reporting detailed data on community care requests, including how many were approved, denied, or appealed, with a special focus on mental health requests. It also requires Congress to formally approve any future changes the VA tries to make to the rules that govern when veterans can access outside care, giving Congress more direct oversight over those decisions. Veterans seeking residential mental health or substance use treatment would be most directly affected, particularly those in areas where VA facilities are limited.
ai_headline: Senate bill would expand outside mental health care access for veterans when VA falls short
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1912 lets veterans use approved outside mental health and substance use programs when the VA cannot offer a timely spot.
ai_sections.what: S 1912 would allow veterans who qualify for VA Mental Health Residential Rehabilitation Treatment but face no open spot or a wait that exceeds VA standards to use approved outside treatment programs at VA expense. The bill adds consumer protections, including the right to choose among available options and a ban on denying eligibility solely because no nearby provider meets scheduling standards. Outside programs used under this rule would need to be state-licensed and accredited by recognized behavioral health organizations, with limited VA waivers allowed when no other option exists.
ai_sections.who: Veterans seeking residential mental health or substance use treatment are most directly affected, especially those in areas where VA facilities are limited. The bill also shifts oversight authority so that Congress must formally approve future VA rule changes governing when veterans can access outside care.
ai_sections.why: Sponsors say the VA has made it too difficult for veterans to use the existing Community Care Program for mental health care specifically. The bill's data-reporting requirement and congressional approval mandate reflect concern that without oversight, access restrictions could be tightened without public accountability.

title: Never Fight Alone Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it easier for veterans to get mental health and substance use treatment from private, community-based providers when the VA cannot serve them quickly enough. Right now, veterans can sometimes see outside providers through the VA Community Care Program, but the rules have made it hard to use that option for mental health and residential treatment programs specifically. This bill adds a new rule saying that if a veteran qualifies for a VA Mental Health Residential Rehabilitation Treatment Program but cannot get in promptly, or if the VA cannot meet its own wait-time standards for that care, the veteran can go to an approved outside facility and have the VA cover it. It also says the VA cannot deny community care just because outside providers happen to be unavailable either, and it gives veterans a say in choosing between available options.

The bill sets quality requirements for outside mental health and residential treatment facilities, requiring them to be state-licensed and nationally accredited. It also requires the VA to start reporting detailed data each year on how many community care requests were made, approved, denied, and appealed, including a breakdown for mental health services. Additionally, it puts a check on VA bureaucracy by requiring Congress to pass a resolution before the VA can tighten the rules that determine when veterans qualify for community care.

This bill primarily affects veterans who need mental health treatment or help with substance use and are struggling to get timely care through the VA system. It would also affect private mental health treatment facilities that could become eligible VA community care providers, and VA administrators who would face new reporting requirements and limits on their ability to change eligibility rules without congressional approval.
ai_headline: Bill would open private mental health care to veterans stuck on VA wait lists
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8737 lets veterans use VA-covered private mental health and residential treatment if the VA cannot serve them promptly.
ai_sections.what: HR 8737 would allow veterans who qualify for VA Mental Health Residential Rehabilitation Treatment but cannot get timely access to instead use approved outside facilities at VA expense. The bill also bars the VA from denying community care solely because outside providers are unavailable, and gives veterans a role in choosing among available options. Outside facilities must be state-licensed and nationally accredited to qualify.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects veterans who need mental health or substance use treatment and cannot get timely care through the VA. It also affects private mental health facilities seeking to become VA community care providers and VA administrators who would face new reporting requirements.
ai_sections.why: The VA would be required to report detailed annual data on community care requests, approvals, denials, and appeals, including a breakdown for mental health services. Any VA effort to tighten community care eligibility rules would require Congress to pass a resolution first, limiting the agency's ability to act unilaterally.
ai_sections.cost: The VA would cover the cost of approved outside mental health and residential treatment for qualifying veterans who cannot receive timely VA care.

Covers residential rehab for eligible vets

title: ORBITS Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill, called the ORBITS Act of 2025, tries to address the growing problem of space junk orbiting Earth. Right now, there are thousands of broken satellites, rocket parts, and other human-made objects floating in orbit that no longer work. The bill directs NASA to run a competitive program where private companies, universities, and nonprofits can apply for funding to develop and test technology that would actually go up and remove or redirect that junk. The government would publish a public list of the most dangerous pieces of debris within 90 days of the bill passing, and up to $150 million would be available between 2026 and 2030 to support this work.

The bill also directs several federal agencies to update the official rulebook for how satellites should be built and disposed of after their missions end. This updated rulebook would cover things like how to avoid collisions, how quickly old satellites must be brought down, and how to track smaller pieces of debris that are currently too small to monitor. After those rules are updated, agencies like the FCC and FAA would use them to shape future regulations for anyone launching things into space.

The bill affects a wide range of people and organizations. Companies in the commercial space industry, satellite operators, universities, and nonprofit research groups could all apply to participate in the debris removal demonstration program. Anyone who uses technology that depends on satellites, which includes most Americans using GPS, weather forecasts, or internet services, has an indirect stake in this, since space junk poses a real threat to those systems.
ai_headline: NASA would fund private debris removal under new space junk bill
last_action_text: Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. Ordered to be reported without amendment favorably.

ai_sections.tldr: The ORBITS Act of 2025 would give up to $150M to private groups to remove space debris between 2026 and 2030.
ai_sections.what: The ORBITS Act of 2025 directs NASA to run a competitive funding program for private companies, universities, and nonprofits to develop and test orbital debris removal technology. The government would also publish a public list of the most dangerous debris within 90 days of passage. Separately, federal agencies would update rules governing how satellites are built, disposed of, and tracked after their missions end.
ai_sections.who: Commercial space companies, satellite operators, universities, and nonprofit research groups could apply for debris removal demonstration funding. Anyone who relies on GPS, weather forecasts, or internet services has an indirect stake, since those systems depend on satellites that space debris threatens.
ai_sections.why: Thousands of broken satellites and rocket parts currently orbit Earth with no mechanism requiring their removal. Collisions or damage to active satellites could disrupt GPS, weather, and internet services that most Americans use daily.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $150 million would be available for the debris removal demonstration program, covering the period from 2026 through 2030.

title: Kenya Merritt Renewing our PACT Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a legal shortcut for certain civilian federal employees who got sick after working near open burn pits overseas. Right now, these workers have to prove their illness was caused by their job, which can be very difficult. The bill would create a "presumption," meaning the government automatically assumes the illness was job-related if the worker qualifies. This matches a benefit that military veterans already received under a 2022 law. The bill is named after Kenya Merritt, likely someone personally affected by burn pit exposure.

To qualify, a civilian federal employee must have worked at least 30 days in a country where the U.S. was running a military operation on or after August 2, 1990. They must also work for one of several specific agencies, such as the Departments of Defense, State, Justice, Homeland Security, or the intelligence community, or be a federal law enforcement officer. The diseases covered by the presumption are the same ones already recognized for veterans, and the list can grow over time as new conditions are added to the veterans program.

The people this affects most are civilian government contractors and federal workers who served alongside the military in places like Iraq and Afghanistan and later developed serious health conditions. It does not cover private sector workers or military service members, who are handled under separate laws. The Department of Labor would manage the program and must report to Congress within one year on how many people are eligible and how the rollout is going.
ai_headline: Federal workers sickened by burn pits abroad gain presumed coverage under HR 8524
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8524 removes the burden of proof for federal civilian workers sickened by overseas burn pits, matching benefits veterans got in 2022.
ai_sections.what: HR 8524 creates a legal presumption that certain civilian federal employees' illnesses are job-related if they worked near burn pits overseas, eliminating the need for workers to individually prove causation. To qualify, a worker must have served at least 30 days in a country where the U.S. was conducting a military operation on or after August 2, 1990, and must be employed by a qualifying agency such as the Departments of Defense, State, Justice, or Homeland Security, the intelligence community, or serve as a federal law enforcement officer. The covered diseases mirror those already recognized for veterans and can expand as that veterans list grows.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects civilian federal employees and federal law enforcement officers who worked alongside the military in overseas locations such as Iraq and Afghanistan and later developed serious health conditions. It does not apply to private sector workers or military service members, who are covered under separate laws.
ai_sections.why: Without the presumption, affected workers face significant difficulty proving a direct link between their illness and their work environment, a standard that has blocked or delayed claims. Extending the presumption aligns civilian workers' legal standing with that of veterans, who received the same protection under a 2022 law.

title: Renewing our PACT Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would make it easier for certain civilian federal employees to get workers' compensation if they got sick after being exposed to burn pits or other toxic hazards while working overseas. Right now, these workers often have to prove that their illness was caused by their job, which can be very difficult. This bill would create a legal "presumption," meaning the government would automatically assume the illness was connected to their work, without requiring the employee to provide direct proof.

The bill covers civilian employees from several federal agencies, including the Departments of Defense, State, Justice, Homeland Security, Treasury, Commerce, and Agriculture, as well as federal law enforcement officers and intelligence community workers. To qualify, a person must have worked at least 30 days in a country where the U.S. was conducting a military operation on or after August 2, 1990. The list of covered illnesses would mirror the same diseases already recognized for military veterans under existing veterans' benefits law, and it would be updated automatically whenever the Department of Veterans Affairs adds new diseases to that veterans' list.

This bill affects civilian federal workers who served alongside the military in places like Iraq and Afghanistan and may have been exposed to the smoke from burn pits, which were large open-air fires used to dispose of waste. It does not apply to military service members, who are covered under separate laws. The bill was introduced in May 2026 and has been referred to the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
ai_headline: Senate bill eases workers' comp for feds exposed to burn pits abroad
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4554 would let civilian feds skip direct proof of illness if exposed to burn pits or toxic hazards while working overseas.
ai_sections.what: S 4554 would create a legal presumption that illnesses in covered civilian federal employees are work-related if those employees were exposed to burn pits or other toxic hazards while working overseas, removing the requirement to provide direct proof of causation. The list of covered illnesses would mirror diseases already recognized for military veterans under existing veterans' benefits law and would update automatically when the Department of Veterans Affairs adds new conditions to that list. Employees must have worked at least 30 days in a country where the U.S. was conducting a military operation on or after August 2, 1990.
ai_sections.who: The bill covers civilian employees from the Departments of Defense, State, Justice, Homeland Security, Treasury, Commerce, and Agriculture, as well as federal law enforcement officers and intelligence community workers. It does not apply to military service members, who are covered under separate laws.
ai_sections.why: Without the presumption, affected civilian workers have often been unable to obtain workers' compensation because proving a direct link between overseas toxic exposure and a specific illness is difficult. If enacted, the bill would align the legal standard for covered civilian federal employees more closely with the standard that already applies to military veterans.

title: Crime Victims Fund Stabilization Act of 2025
ai_summary:
The Crime Victims Fund is a federal account that pays for services like shelters for domestic violence survivors, rape crisis centers, and support programs for crime victims. It gets its money mainly from fines and penalties paid by people convicted of federal crimes. This bill would add a new source of money to that fund: settlements and penalties collected under the False Claims Act, which is the law used to sue companies or individuals who defraud the federal government. This change would be temporary, running from when the bill is signed through the end of fiscal year 2029.

The bill does include two limits on what False Claims Act money can flow into the fund. First, the portion of any settlement that goes to a whistleblower, meaning the private citizen who reported the fraud and helped bring the case, cannot be deposited into the Crime Victims Fund. Second, money that the government uses to cover its own losses from the fraud is also kept out. Only the remaining penalty amounts would go into the fund.

This bill would mainly affect organizations that receive Crime Victims Fund grants, such as local victim assistance programs, domestic violence shelters, and sexual assault service providers, because it would give them access to more stable or increased funding. It could also affect companies and individuals who settle False Claims Act cases with the government, since more of those settlement dollars would be directed to this specific fund rather than going into the general federal treasury. Ordinary crime victims who use services funded by this account could also benefit if the additional money allows more programs to keep running or expand.
ai_headline: False Claims Act settlements would funnel money to crime victim services through 2029
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 909 routes False Claims Act penalty money into the Crime Victims Fund through fiscal year 2029, excluding whistleblower shares and fraud restitution.
ai_sections.what: HR 909 adds False Claims Act settlements and penalties as a new funding source for the Crime Victims Fund, a federal account that supports domestic violence shelters, rape crisis centers, and victim assistance programs. The change would be temporary, lasting from enactment through the end of fiscal year 2029. Two portions of any settlement are excluded: the share paid to whistleblowers and any amount the government uses to recover its own losses from the fraud.
ai_sections.who: Organizations that receive Crime Victims Fund grants, including local victim assistance programs, domestic violence shelters, and sexual assault service providers, would have access to additional funding. Companies and individuals who settle False Claims Act cases with the federal government would also be affected, as more of their settlement dollars would flow to this specific fund rather than the general federal treasury.
ai_sections.why: Directing False Claims Act penalties to the Crime Victims Fund could increase or stabilize the money available to programs serving crime victims. If additional funding allows more programs to continue operating or expand, ordinary crime victims who rely on those services could be affected.
ai_sections.cost: The Crime Victims Fund currently draws from fines and penalties paid by people convicted of federal crimes. Under this bill, it would also receive penalty portions of False Claims Act settlements, minus whistleblower shares and amounts used to offset government losses from fraud.
costChips: Fund currently paid by federal criminal fines
False Claims Act penalties added as new source
Whistleblower shares and fraud losses excluded

title: Researching Innovative Shipbuilding and Next-Generation Transit Infrastructure for Durable and Efficient Systems
ai_summary:
This bill tells a federal research group called the Transportation Research Board to study new ways to build the hulls of boats and ferries used for public transportation. Specifically, the study would look at using modern materials like carbon fiber and other composite materials instead of traditional materials like steel or aluminum. The goal is to find out whether these newer materials could make passenger ferries and other water transit vessels more efficient and able to carry more people or cargo.

The bill does not build any ships or spend money on new vessels. It only requires a research evaluation, meaning experts would analyze and report on whether these new designs and materials are worth using. No immediate changes to any existing ferry systems would happen as a result of this bill alone.

The people most directly affected would be researchers at the Transportation Research Board and, down the road, communities that rely on ferries and water buses for daily transportation. Cities and regions with busy waterway transit systems, such as those along major rivers, bays, or coastlines, could eventually benefit if the research leads to better and more cost-effective vessels. Taxpayers who fund public transit systems could also be affected if the findings lead to cheaper or longer-lasting boats in the future.
ai_headline: Federal study ordered on carbon fiber hulls for public ferries
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8998 directs the Transportation Research Board to study composite materials for passenger ferry hulls; no ships are built or funded.
ai_sections.what: HR 8998 directs the Transportation Research Board to evaluate whether modern composite materials such as carbon fiber could replace steel or aluminum in passenger ferry and water transit vessel hulls. The study would result in an analysis and report from experts. No vessels would be built and no existing ferry systems would change as a direct result of this bill.
ai_sections.who: Researchers at the Transportation Research Board are the most immediately affected, as they would conduct the evaluation. Communities that depend on ferries and water buses for daily transportation could be affected further down the road if the research influences future vessel decisions.
ai_sections.why: The research could inform whether composite hull materials produce ferries that are more efficient or capable of carrying more passengers or cargo. If findings are acted upon in the future, taxpayers who fund public transit systems could see changes in vessel costs or longevity.

title: Our Doctors First Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would stop Medicare from paying hospitals and other medical training sites for the cost of training doctors who are not U.S. citizens or nationals. Right now, Medicare helps fund graduate medical education, which is the residency and internship training that medical school graduates must complete before they can practice on their own. This funding helps hospitals afford to run those training programs. Under this bill, only trainees who are U.S. citizens or nationals would count toward the federal payments hospitals receive for running those programs.

The bill sets up a penalty system for hospitals and clinics that break the rule. A first violation would bring a fine equal to 25 percent of the payment that would have gone toward the non-citizen trainees. A second violation would mean a $1 million fine. A third violation would cut off all Medicare graduate medical education funding to that facility for five years, and repeated violations after that would result in ten-year funding cutoffs.

This affects teaching hospitals, clinics, and other facilities that train doctors and currently receive Medicare graduate medical education money. It also directly affects international medical graduates, who make up a significant share of doctors in U.S. residency programs, especially in fields like primary care and in rural or underserved areas. Patients in communities that rely heavily on those doctors could feel an impact if training programs shrink or facilities lose funding.
ai_headline: Medicare would stop funding residency training for non-citizen doctors
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8943 bars Medicare from counting non-citizen medical trainees when paying hospitals for graduate medical education programs.
ai_sections.what: HR 8943 would prohibit Medicare from including non-U.S. citizens or nationals when calculating graduate medical education payments to hospitals and training sites. Only trainees who are U.S. citizens or nationals would count toward the federal funding hospitals receive for running residency and internship programs. Facilities that violate the rule face a tiered penalty system starting with fines and escalating to full funding cutoffs.
ai_sections.who: Teaching hospitals, clinics, and other facilities that train doctors and receive Medicare graduate medical education funding are directly subject to the bill's requirements and penalties.
