International medical graduates currently in U.S. residency programs are also directly affected, as are patients in rural and underserved communities that rely heavily on those doctors.
ai_sections.why: If training programs shrink in response to reduced funding eligibility, access to physicians in rural and underserved areas could be affected, given that international medical graduates make up a significant share of doctors in those settings. Facilities that lose funding through penalties or repeated violations could face further strain on their ability to run training programs.
ai_sections.cost: A first violation carries a fine equal to 25 percent of the payment that would have gone toward the non-citizen trainees. A second violation brings a $1 million fine, a third results in a five-year cutoff of all Medicare graduate medical education funding to that facility, and repeated violations after that trigger ten-year funding cutoffs.

title: Elder Pride Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new federal grant program under the Older Americans Act, specifically targeting LGBTQI seniors who live in rural areas. The federal government would make up to $5 million per year available in 2027 and 2028 for states, tribal governments, nonprofits, and local agencies to apply for. The money would be used for outreach, reducing social isolation, improving access to care, and providing sexual health services including HIV-related care to older adults in rural communities.

Organizations that receive these grants could use the funds for things like training local service providers to work more effectively with LGBTQI seniors, building connections between those seniors and community resources, and expanding nondiscrimination policies at organizations that serve older adults. Grant recipients would also be required to work alongside existing local agencies and organizations rather than operating on their own. The bill specifies that grant money must add to existing state and local funding, not replace it.

The people most directly affected would be LGBTQI seniors living in rural parts of the United States, where services of any kind can be harder to reach and where social isolation is often a greater concern. Nonprofits, senior centers, area agencies on aging, and other organizations that serve older adults in rural areas would also be affected, as they would be eligible to apply for this funding. The bill was introduced in the House and referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: New federal grants would fund rural LGBTQI senior services starting 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8952 would create a federal grant program offering up to $5M yearly in 2027-2028 for rural LGBTQI senior services.
ai_sections.what: HR 8952 would establish a new federal grant program under the Older Americans Act to fund services for LGBTQI seniors in rural areas. Eligible uses include outreach, reducing social isolation, improving care access, sexual health services, and HIV-related care. Grant recipients must coordinate with existing local agencies and may not use federal funds to replace state or local funding already in place.
ai_sections.who: LGBTQI seniors living in rural parts of the United States are the primary population this bill targets. States, tribal governments, nonprofits, area agencies on aging, and other local organizations serving older adults in rural areas would be eligible to apply for grants.
ai_sections.why: Rural areas generally present greater challenges in reaching services of any kind, and social isolation tends to be a more significant concern in those communities. The bill would also require participating organizations to expand nondiscrimination policies and train local service providers on working with LGBTQI older adults.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would make up to $5 million per year available in 2027 and 2028. Grant funds must supplement, not replace, existing state and local funding.

title: GME Transparency Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill requires hospitals that receive Medicare funding to train doctors to report data about the immigration and citizenship status of their medical residents. Medical residents are doctors who have finished medical school and are doing their required hands-on training at a hospital, usually for several years. The bill covers hospitals that get federal Medicare money specifically for these training programs, which is a large share of teaching hospitals across the country. If a hospital refuses to submit the required information, it could lose its Medicare funding for those training programs.

The reported information would be broken down into five categories: US citizens, permanent residents (green card holders), J-1 visa holders (a common visa for international medical graduates doing residency), H-1B visa holders, and others who are lawfully present in the country. The data would be de-identified, meaning individual residents would not be named. Hospitals would have to file this report within 180 days after each fiscal year ends, and then the federal government would send a summary report to Congress within 270 days, with numbers broken down by state.

This bill mainly affects teaching hospitals and the medical residents training in them. The US relies heavily on international medical graduates to fill residency slots, especially in rural areas and specialties with shortages, so this could draw attention to that practice. It does not restrict who can participate in residency programs or change any visa rules. It only creates a new reporting and transparency requirement tied to existing Medicare funding.
ai_headline: Teaching hospitals would report immigration status of medical residents under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8942 requires Medicare-funded teaching hospitals to report immigration status of medical residents in five categories, with no names attached.
ai_sections.what: HR 8942 requires hospitals that receive Medicare funding for residency training programs to collect and report data on the immigration and citizenship status of their medical residents. The data is broken into five categories: US citizens, permanent residents, J-1 visa holders, H-1B visa holders, and others lawfully present. Individual residents would not be named in the reports.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects teaching hospitals that receive federal Medicare money specifically for graduate medical education training programs, which covers a large share of teaching hospitals nationwide. Medical residents, who are doctors completing required hands-on training after medical school, are the subject of the reported data.
ai_sections.why: The United States relies heavily on international medical graduates to fill residency slots, particularly in rural areas and specialties with shortages, and this reporting requirement could draw public and congressional attention to that practice. The bill does not restrict who can participate in residency programs or alter any visa rules.
ai_sections.cost: Hospitals that refuse to submit the required information could lose their Medicare funding for residency training programs.

title: Vacancy to Value Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a five-year test program run by the General Services Administration (GSA) to sell or give away federal buildings and land that the government is no longer using. Instead of selling these properties at full price, the government could let them go for less than market value if the buyer agrees to turn them into something useful for the surrounding neighborhood, such as affordable housing, health clinics, childcare centers, schools, or job-creating businesses. Buyers must submit a redevelopment plan and start construction within five years, or the government can take the property back.

The bill also sets up a separate grant program through the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) to help fund the cost of actually fixing up and building on those properties. Grants could cover things like planning, cleaning up pollution on a site, tearing down old structures, building new ones, or adding infrastructure. Priority for both the property transfers and the grants goes to nonprofit organizations, local governments, tribal governments, public housing agencies, and community land trusts, especially those serving low-income or historically underserved areas.

This bill mainly affects communities that have unused or abandoned federal properties nearby, along with the nonprofits, local governments, and developers who might redevelop them. Residents in those neighborhoods could see new housing or community facilities built on land that was previously sitting empty. After the five-year pilot ends, GSA must report back to Congress on how well the program worked.
ai_headline: Federal pilot would sell unused government properties below market value
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and in addition to the Committees on Oversight and Government Reform, and Financial Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9011 lets GSA sell or give away idle federal properties below market value if buyers redevelop them for community use within five years.
ai_sections.what: HR 9011 creates a five-year pilot program under GSA to sell or transfer unused federal buildings and land, potentially below market value, to buyers who commit to redeveloping them for community purposes such as affordable housing, health clinics, childcare centers, schools, or job-creating businesses. Buyers must submit a redevelopment plan and begin construction within five years or the government may reclaim the property. A separate HUD grant program would fund redevelopment costs including planning, pollution cleanup, demolition, construction, and infrastructure.
ai_sections.who: Priority for property transfers and grants goes to nonprofit organizations, local governments, tribal governments, public housing agencies, and community land trusts, particularly those serving low-income or historically underserved areas. Residents living near currently unused or abandoned federal properties are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Communities with idle federal land nearby could see those sites converted into housing or public facilities instead of remaining vacant. After the five-year pilot concludes, GSA must report to Congress on the program's results, which could influence whether the approach continues or expands.
ai_sections.cost: HUD grants are available to cover redevelopment costs such as planning, cleanup, demolition, construction, and infrastructure, though the summary does not specify a total funding amount.

title: Promoting Childhood Independence and Resilience Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tries to protect parents from being investigated or charged with child neglect simply for allowing their kids to do things on their own, like playing at a park, walking to school, riding a bike, running errands, or staying home alone for a short time. It creates a legal definition for these kinds of activities, calling them "reasonable childhood independence activities," and says that allowing a child to do them does not count as child abuse or neglect. It also requires the federal Department of Health and Human Services to study the issue and report back to Congress with recommendations for how states can support parents who give their kids this kind of freedom.

The bill changes two major federal laws that govern child welfare funding. States that receive federal child welfare money would be required to update their plans and train their workers to understand that kids doing age-appropriate things on their own is not a reason to open a child abuse investigation or separate a family. It also says states must put protections in place so that parents cannot face criminal charges, civil penalties, or placement on a child abuse registry just because they let their child walk to school or play outside unsupervised.

This bill would affect parents and guardians across the country, as well as state child protective services agencies, caseworkers, and investigators. Families who have faced investigations or legal trouble for allowing normal childhood activities would be most directly helped. States would need to update their policies and worker training to stay in compliance and keep receiving federal funding for child welfare programs.
ai_headline: Federal bill shields parents who let kids play outside or walk to school alone
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8757 would bar states from treating unsupervised childhood activities as neglect, protecting parents from investigations and criminal charges.
ai_sections.what: HR 8757 defines "reasonable childhood independence activities" — such as playing at a park, walking to school, riding a bike, running errands, or staying home alone briefly — and states that allowing these activities does not constitute child abuse or neglect. The bill amends two major federal child welfare funding laws to require states to reflect this standard in their policies and worker training. States must also ensure parents cannot face criminal charges, civil penalties, or placement on a child abuse registry for permitting these activities.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects parents and guardians nationwide, as well as state child protective services agencies, caseworkers, and investigators. Families who have previously faced investigations or legal trouble for allowing normal childhood activities would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: States that receive federal child welfare funding would be required to update their plans and train workers accordingly to remain in compliance. The Department of Health and Human Services would be required to study the issue and report recommendations to Congress on how states can support parents who allow children this kind of independence.

title: Worker Rights and Support Act
ai_summary:
This bill would add new federal rules to the Fair Labor Standards Act requiring employers across the country to give workers specific types of breaks. Workers would get at least a 30-minute meal break for every 6 hours worked, at least a 10-minute rest or restroom break for every 4 hours worked, and additional short breaks of up to 20 minutes when a doctor says a worker needs them for a medical condition. The meal break can be unpaid if the worker is fully free from work duties, but rest, restroom, and medical breaks must be paid at the worker's regular rate. If a worker is expected to keep working or stay on-site during a meal break, the employer must pay them at one and a half times their normal rate for that time.

Workers can voluntarily give up their meal break if they choose, but employers are not allowed to pressure them into doing so. The bill also makes violating these rules illegal under existing labor law, which means workers could sue their employers or have the federal government take action on their behalf. States and union contracts that already provide stronger protections than this bill would not be weakened by it.

This bill would affect most private-sector employees and their employers across the United States, particularly workers in industries where breaks are not always guaranteed, such as warehousing, retail, food service, and transportation. It does not currently apply to workers covered by union contracts that set their own break rules, and it would not override state laws that already give workers more protections.
ai_headline: Federal bill sets mandatory meal and rest breaks for most US workers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9008 would require 30-min meal breaks every 6 hrs and paid 10-min rest breaks every 4 hrs for most US private-sector workers.
ai_sections.what: HR 9008 would amend the Fair Labor Standards Act to require employers to provide workers a 30-minute meal break for every 6 hours worked, a paid 10-minute rest or restroom break for every 4 hours worked, and additional paid breaks of up to 20 minutes when a doctor certifies a medical need. Meal breaks may be unpaid only when workers are fully relieved of duties, but if workers must remain on-site or continue working during a meal break, employers must pay one and a half times the worker's regular rate for that time. Workers may voluntarily waive their meal break, but employers are prohibited from pressuring them to do so.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects most private-sector employees and their employers across the United States, with particular impact on industries such as warehousing, retail, food service, and transportation. It does not apply where union contracts already set break rules and does not override state laws that provide stronger protections.
ai_sections.why: Violations would be enforceable under existing federal labor law, allowing workers to sue employers or triggering federal government action on their behalf. States and collective bargaining agreements that already exceed these standards would not be weakened by the bill.

title: SURS Extension Act
ai_summary:
This bill extends a federal support program that helps small, rural, and underserved medical practices participate in Medicare's Quality Payment Program (QPP). The QPP is a system where doctors and other healthcare providers are judged on the quality of care they give Medicare patients, and their Medicare payments can go up or down based on their performance. The SURS program provides extra technical help and resources to smaller or less-resourced practices so they can meet those quality reporting requirements. Without this extension, that support program would have expired. The bill keeps it running through 2031.

The people most directly affected are doctors and healthcare providers who work in small offices, rural areas, or communities that are considered medically underserved. These practices often lack the staff and technology that large hospital systems have, making it harder to handle the paperwork and data reporting that Medicare requires. The SURS program gives them guidance and assistance so they are not at a disadvantage compared to bigger providers.

Indirectly, patients in rural and underserved areas could also be affected. If their local doctors struggle to meet Medicare requirements without support, those providers could face payment penalties or may choose to stop seeing Medicare patients altogether. By keeping this assistance program in place, the bill aims to help ensure that smaller practices can continue serving Medicare patients in communities that already have fewer healthcare options.
ai_headline: Federal support program for small and rural Medicare providers extended to 2031
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9002 extends the SURS program through 2031, keeping technical support alive for small, rural, and underserved medical practices in Medicare.
ai_sections.what: HR 9002 extends the SURS program, which provides technical help and resources to small, rural, and underserved medical practices participating in Medicare's Quality Payment Program. The QPP ties Medicare payment rates to provider performance on quality reporting requirements. Without this bill, the SURS program would have expired.
ai_sections.who: Doctors and healthcare providers in small offices, rural areas, and medically underserved communities are directly affected. Patients in those same communities are indirectly affected, as their access to Medicare-participating providers may depend on whether local practices can meet federal reporting requirements.
ai_sections.why: Small and rural practices typically lack the staff and technology of large hospital systems, making quality reporting more difficult and leaving them vulnerable to payment penalties. Providers who cannot meet Medicare requirements without support may face reduced payments or may stop accepting Medicare patients.

title: No Housing Welfare for Illegal Aliens Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to federal housing law. First, it ends a long-standing practice called "prorated" housing assistance. Under current rules, if a family has some members who are eligible for federal housing help (like U.S. citizens) and others who are not (like undocumented immigrants), the family can still receive a reduced amount of aid based on how many eligible members there are. This bill would end that. Every single family member, including children, would have to prove they are legally eligible before the family receives any federal housing assistance at all.

Second, the bill would cut off two major federal grant programs — Community Development Block Grants (CDBG) and HOME grants — from cities, counties, states, tribes, or other organizations that operate as so-called "sanctuary" jurisdictions. That means any government or group that limits cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, refuses to hold people in jail at ICE's request, or fails to notify federal authorities before releasing certain immigrants could lose this funding entirely. These grants pay for things like affordable housing construction, neighborhood improvements, and community services in thousands of localities across the country.

The people most directly affected are mixed-immigration-status families who currently receive partial housing assistance, as well as residents of cities and counties that have sanctuary policies, since those local governments could lose significant federal dollars for housing and community development programs. Local governments, nonprofits, and tribes that run housing programs would also face new eligibility requirements tied to their immigration enforcement cooperation.
ai_headline: Bill would strip housing aid from mixed-status families and sanctuary cities
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8941 ends partial housing aid for mixed-status families and cuts federal housing grants to sanctuary jurisdictions.
ai_sections.what: HR 8941 ends the federal practice of providing prorated housing assistance to families with mixed immigration status, requiring every family member to prove legal eligibility before any aid is received. The bill also strips Community Development Block Grants and HOME grants from cities, counties, states, tribes, and other organizations that operate as sanctuary jurisdictions by limiting cooperation with federal immigration enforcement.
ai_sections.who: Mixed-immigration-status families currently receiving partial housing assistance would lose all federal housing aid. Residents, local governments, nonprofits, and tribes in sanctuary jurisdictions would face loss of federal funding for affordable housing, neighborhood improvements, and community services.
ai_sections.why: Families with even one ineligible member would receive no assistance rather than a reduced amount. Localities with sanctuary policies could lose significant federal dollars that currently fund housing construction and community development programs.
ai_sections.cost: Community Development Block Grants and HOME grants, which fund affordable housing construction, neighborhood improvements, and community services across thousands of localities, would be cut off entirely from sanctuary jurisdictions.

title: AVERT Future Violence Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Department of Justice to study the connection between animal cruelty and human violence. Specifically, the National Institute of Justice would have three years to research why people commit animal cruelty, what risk factors are involved, and whether harming animals is a warning sign that someone may later commit violence against people. After the study, the agency would send Congress a report with specific recommendations for laws and programs at the federal, state, and local levels.

The bill also creates a voluntary grant program that the Attorney General can offer to states, local governments, tribes, courts, and nonprofits. These groups could use the money to train law enforcement officers, judges, prosecutors, and mental health professionals to better recognize and respond to animal cruelty. Funds could also go toward building better data systems that track animal cruelty cases and toward mental health treatment and rehabilitation programs for people who have committed acts of animal cruelty.

The people most directly affected include law enforcement agencies, courts, animal welfare organizations, domestic violence service providers, and mental health professionals who could receive grant funding and training. Researchers and policymakers would be shaped by the study's findings. Individuals convicted of animal cruelty offenses could be affected by new intervention or diversion programs that result from the bill. The total cost authorized is $4 million, split between the study and the grant program.
ai_headline: Justice Department would study animal cruelty as a predictor of human violence
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8911 orders a 3-year DOJ study on animal cruelty and human violence links, plus a $4M grant program for training and intervention.
ai_sections.what: HR 8911 directs the National Institute of Justice to spend three years studying why people commit animal cruelty, what risk factors are involved, and whether harming animals predicts later violence against people. After the study, the agency must send Congress a report with recommendations for federal, state, and local laws and programs. The bill also creates a voluntary grant program for states, local governments, tribes, courts, and nonprofits to fund training, data systems, and rehabilitation programs related to animal cruelty.
ai_sections.who: Law enforcement agencies, courts, animal welfare organizations, domestic violence service providers, and mental health professionals are eligible for grant funding and training. Researchers and policymakers would be shaped by the study's findings, and individuals convicted of animal cruelty could be affected by new intervention or diversion programs.
ai_sections.why: The study's findings and resulting recommendations could lead to new laws and programs at the federal, state, and local levels addressing animal cruelty. People convicted of animal cruelty offenses could face new intervention or diversion requirements depending on programs that emerge from the legislation.
ai_sections.cost: The total authorized cost is $4 million, divided between the study conducted by the National Institute of Justice and the voluntary grant program.
costChips: Covers both the study and grants
Grants go to govts, courts, nonprofits

title: Affordable Housing and Area Median Income Fairness Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would authorize up to $5 billion per year for each of three existing federal housing programs from 2026 through 2035. The three programs are the HOME Investment Partnerships Program, Community Development Block Grants, and the National Housing Trust Fund. All three already exist and send money to local governments and nonprofits to build or preserve affordable housing. The new funding would only go to areas where the federal government has already recognized that housing costs are unusually high compared to local incomes, a designation called a "high housing cost adjustment."

The bill also directs the Department of Housing and Urban Development to study how "area median income," or AMI, is calculated. AMI is a key number used to decide who qualifies for affordable housing programs and what the maximum rents on subsidized units can be. Critics have argued that the way AMI is currently calculated — often by averaging incomes across large regions that include wealthy suburbs — can inflate the number, which then raises the income cutoffs and allowed rents in cities, making subsidized housing less affordable than intended. The bill requires HUD to start this study within 180 days and deliver a full report to Congress within two years. The bill specifically asks HUD to look at how the wealthy suburbs of Westchester and Rockland Counties affect the AMI calculation for New York City.

This bill would most directly affect low- and middle-income renters living in high-cost cities, local housing agencies, and nonprofit developers who build affordable housing. Landlords with tax-subsidized units would also be affected if AMI calculation rules change, since those rules determine the maximum rents they can charge. Authorizing funding does not guarantee the money will be spent — Congress would still need to pass separate appropriations bills to actually release the funds.
ai_headline: Bill targets $5B yearly for affordable housing in high-cost cities
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9014 would authorize up to $5B/year through 2035 for three housing programs, but only in federally designated high-cost areas.
ai_sections.what: HR 9014 would authorize up to $5 billion per year from 2026 through 2035 for three existing federal programs: the HOME Investment Partnerships Program, Community Development Block Grants, and the National Housing Trust Fund. The new funding would be limited to areas designated by the federal government as having unusually high housing costs relative to local incomes. The bill also requires HUD to study how area median income is calculated, with a report due to Congress within two years.
ai_sections.who: Low- and middle-income renters in high-cost cities, local housing agencies, and nonprofit affordable housing developers would be most directly affected. Landlords with tax-subsidized units could also be affected if AMI calculation rules change, since those rules set the maximum rents they are permitted to charge.
ai_sections.why: Authorizing this funding does not guarantee it will be spent, as Congress must still pass separate appropriations bills to release the money. If the AMI study leads to changes in how the figure is calculated, income eligibility cutoffs and maximum rents on subsidized units could shift, altering who qualifies for affordable housing and what they pay.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $5 billion per year for each of three housing programs from 2026 through 2035. Authorized funding is not automatically spent and requires further congressional action.
costChips: Up to $5B per year per program

title: POWER Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the U.S. Department of Energy to track and report to Congress how much electricity and water large data centers are using. Starting two years after the bill becomes law, and every year after that, the Energy Secretary must submit a detailed report covering things like how much water each data center draws from public water supplies, whether they generate their own electricity or buy it from the power grid, and who paid the costs to connect them to the electrical grid. The bill focuses specifically on data centers that were fast-tracked through the federal permitting process under a recent executive order, but it leaves out data centers run by the military and certain government agencies.

The bill affects large, privately operated data centers that received special federal permits to build faster. These are often massive facilities built by major tech companies to power things like artificial intelligence and cloud computing. By requiring this reporting, Congress would have a clearer picture of how much strain these facilities place on local water supplies and the broader electrical grid.

Everyday residents could be indirectly affected, since data centers in their area may be competing for the same water and electricity that homes, farms, and businesses rely on. The bill also looks at who is footing the bill when a data center needs to connect to the power grid, which could matter to utility customers if those costs are passed on to them. The bill does not ban or limit data centers in any way, it only requires the government to gather and share information about their resource use.
ai_headline: Bill would require annual federal reports on data center power and water use
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9019 requires the Energy Dept. to report annually to Congress on electricity and water use by large, fast-tracked data centers.
ai_sections.what: HR 9019 requires the U.S. Department of Energy to track and annually report to Congress how much electricity and water large data centers consume. Starting two years after enactment, the Energy Secretary must detail each facility's water draws from public supplies, whether it generates or buys electricity, and who paid to connect it to the power grid. The bill covers only data centers fast-tracked through federal permitting under a recent executive order and excludes military and certain government-run facilities.
ai_sections.who: The bill targets large, privately operated data centers that received special federal permits to build faster, often massive facilities used for artificial intelligence and cloud computing. Everyday residents near these facilities may be indirectly affected, as data centers can compete with homes, farms, and businesses for local water and electricity.
ai_sections.why: Without this reporting, Congress lacks a clear picture of how much strain large data centers place on local water supplies and the broader electrical grid. The bill does not ban or limit data centers in any way; it only requires the government to gather and share information about their resource use.
ai_sections.cost: The bill examines who pays the costs to connect data centers to the electrical grid, which could matter to utility customers if those costs are passed on to them.

title: Physician Education for Fistula Treatment Act
ai_summary:
This bill would allow the U.S. government to fund training programs for OB-GYN doctors and related specialists in the world's poorest countries. The main focus is on a painful childbirth injury called obstetric fistula, which happens when prolonged, difficult labor creates an abnormal opening in a woman's body that causes lifelong incontinence and other serious health problems. The bill would create fellowship and residency programs, as well as training centers, to build up the supply of doctors and midwives who can prevent and surgically repair these injuries. It would be managed through the National Institutes of Health's Fogarty International Center.

The bill also requires the White House to develop a formal 10-year strategy for reducing the shortage of doctors in the poorest countries. That plan would need to cover medical training, community education, research into both the medical and social causes of the problem, and how to build lasting local health care systems. A report on that strategy would be due to Congress within two years, and annual progress reports would be required after that.

This bill does not directly affect most U.S. residents. It is a foreign aid measure aimed at women in very low-income countries, particularly in parts of Africa and Asia where obstetric fistula is most common. U.S. taxpayer dollars would fund the program, but the medical care and training would be delivered abroad. American universities and medical schools could potentially be involved as training partners.
ai_headline: Bill would fund OB-GYN training abroad to fight childbirth injuries
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9015 would use U.S. funds to train OB-GYN doctors in the world's poorest countries and repair obstetric fistula injuries.
ai_sections.what: HR 9015 would allow the U.S. government to fund fellowship and residency programs, plus training centers, to increase the number of doctors and midwives who can prevent and surgically repair obstetric fistula. Obstetric fistula is a childbirth injury caused by prolonged, difficult labor that creates an abnormal opening in a woman's body, leading to lifelong incontinence and serious health problems. The program would be managed through the National Institutes of Health's Fogarty International Center, and American universities and medical schools could serve as training partners.
ai_sections.who: The primary beneficiaries would be women in very low-income countries, particularly in parts of Africa and Asia where obstetric fistula is most common. U.S. taxpayers would fund the program, while doctors, midwives, and medical institutions abroad would receive the training and support.
ai_sections.why: The bill would direct the White House to produce a formal 10-year strategy for reducing the doctor shortage in the world's poorest countries, with a report due to Congress within two years and annual progress reports required after that. The strategy would need to address medical training, community education, research into medical and social causes, and building lasting local health care systems.
ai_sections.cost: U.S. taxpayer dollars would fund the program, though the summary does not specify a total dollar amount. Medical care and training would be delivered abroad, not within the United States.
costChips: Care delivered abroad, not in the U.S.

title: REAADI for Disasters Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the REAADI for Disasters Act, aims to make sure that older adults and people with disabilities are better protected before, during, and after disasters like hurricanes, floods, or public health emergencies. Right now, these groups die at higher rates during disasters, often because evacuation warnings are not communicated in accessible formats like sign language or plain language, shelters lack proper accommodations, and emergency plans rarely involve disabled or elderly people in their creation. The bill would require organizations receiving federal disaster funds to follow disability laws, set up advisory committees that include people with disabilities and older adults, and make sure emergency shelters and communications are accessible to everyone.

The bill also creates new federal funding programs. It would set up a Disaster Human Services Emergency Fund with up to $100 million per year to quickly get services like personal care, transportation, housing, and case management to vulnerable people during and after disasters. Separately, it would fund regional training and research centers, run by organizations led by or focused on people with disabilities and older adults, to study what works and train local emergency agencies. Another $300 million per year in grants would help state, local, and tribal agencies build their capacity to serve at-risk people during emergencies.

The bill affects a wide range of people and organizations. People with disabilities and adults 65 and older would have stronger legal protections and more accessible services during disasters. State and local governments, emergency management agencies, nonprofits, and healthcare providers would face new requirements to include these groups in planning and to follow civil rights laws even during emergencies. A national advisory committee would also be expanded to include more voices from the disability community, tribal governments, and rural areas to guide federal policy going forward.
ai_headline: Disaster bill targets gaps in emergency services for disabled and elderly people
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7029 would require accessible emergency services for disabled and elderly people, with up to $400M/year in new federal funding.
ai_sections.what: The REAADI for Disasters Act would require organizations receiving federal disaster funds to follow disability civil rights laws, make shelters and emergency communications accessible, and include people with disabilities and older adults in emergency planning. New federal funding programs would provide direct services, regional research and training, and grants to state, local, and tribal agencies. A national advisory committee would be expanded to include more voices from the disability community, tribal governments, and rural areas.
ai_sections.who: People with disabilities and adults 65 and older would gain stronger legal protections and access to services during disasters. State and local governments, emergency management agencies, nonprofits, healthcare providers, and tribal agencies would face new planning requirements and civil rights obligations.
ai_sections.why: Older adults and people with disabilities currently die at higher rates during disasters, in part because evacuation warnings are not communicated in accessible formats and shelters lack proper accommodations. Emergency plans rarely involve these groups in their creation, leaving gaps in preparedness that this bill would compel agencies to address.
ai_sections.cost: A Disaster Human Services Emergency Fund would provide up to $100 million per year for direct services such as personal care, transportation, housing, and case management. Separate grants of up to $300 million per year would go to state, local, and tribal agencies, with additional funding directed to regional training and research centers.
costChips: Up to $100M/year for direct disaster services
Up to $300M/year in state and tribal grants
Regional training and research centers funded

title: To designate the Palm Beach International Airport in Florida as the "Donald J. Trump International Airport", and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill would officially rename Palm Beach International Airport in West Palm Beach, Florida to "Donald J. Trump International Airport." If passed, all official U.S. government documents, maps, laws, and records would use the new name. The bill also directs the Federal Aviation Administration to work with international aviation organizations to change the airport's short code identifier to "DJT," replacing its current code of "PBI."

The bill was introduced in the House of Representatives in March 2026 by three Florida members of Congress and was sent to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure. It would only change the name and identifier of this one airport — it does not affect how the airport operates, who runs it, or how it is funded.

This bill most directly affects travelers flying in or out of the West Palm Beach area, airline companies, and local businesses that use the airport's name or code in their materials. Name changes like this are fairly common in Congress — lawmakers regularly propose renaming federal buildings, roads, and other public landmarks to honor individuals. Whether this specific airport name change becomes law depends on whether the bill advances through committee and receives votes in both the House and Senate.
ai_headline: House bill would rename West Palm Beach airport after Donald Trump
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8054 would rename Palm Beach International Airport to Donald J. Trump International Airport and change its FAA code from PBI to DJT.
ai_sections.what: HR 8054 would officially rename Palm Beach International Airport in West Palm Beach, Florida to Donald J. Trump International Airport. All U.S. government documents, maps, laws, and records would use the new name. The bill also directs the Federal Aviation Administration to work with international aviation organizations to change the airport's short code from PBI to DJT.
ai_sections.who: Three Florida members of Congress introduced the bill in the House in March 2026. Travelers flying through West Palm Beach, airline companies, and local businesses that use the airport name or code in their materials would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: The bill is currently in the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and whether it becomes law depends on committee advancement and votes in both chambers. The name and identifier change would require airlines, businesses, and government agencies to update any materials referencing the airport.

title: Local School Foods Expansion Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would expand an existing government program that helps schools buy fresh, unprocessed fruits and vegetables grown in the United States. Right now, the program runs as a small pilot project limited to 8 states. This bill would make it a permanent program and open it up to 14 states at a time. It also sets aside $25 million per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund it, with part of that money going to help schools and farmers handle the paperwork and logistics of participating.

The bill adds new priorities for which states get picked to join the program. States that show a commitment to supporting small, local, and socially disadvantaged farmers would be favored, as would states that work with Native American tribal food producers and communities that use traditional foods. States that serve a high share of children from low-income or disadvantaged backgrounds would also get extra consideration. The bill also requires the federal government to study how well the program is working and report back to Congress within four years.

The people most directly affected are students in participating states, who could see more fresh produce in their school meals. School food programs, local and small-scale farmers, tribal agricultural producers, and state agencies would also be involved, since they would need to coordinate to make the program run. Schools and farmers in states not yet in the program could receive help getting ready to apply in the future.
ai_headline: Bill would expand fresh produce program to 14 states with $25M yearly
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5234 would make a small school fresh-produce pilot permanent, fund it at $25M/year 2026–2030, and open it to 14 states at a time.
ai_sections.what: HR 5234 would convert an existing pilot program — currently limited to 8 states — into a permanent program allowing up to 14 states to participate at a time. The program helps schools purchase fresh, unprocessed fruits and vegetables grown in the United States. The bill also requires a federal study on program effectiveness with a report to Congress within four years.
ai_sections.who: Students in participating states, school food programs, local and small-scale farmers, tribal agricultural producers, and state agencies would be directly involved. Schools and farmers in states not yet in the program could receive assistance preparing future applications.
ai_sections.why: Expanding the program increases the number of states competing for participation, meaning some states would still be excluded at any given time. States prioritizing small, local, and socially disadvantaged farmers, tribal food producers, and high shares of low-income students would receive preference in selection.
ai_sections.cost: The bill sets aside $25 million per year from 2026 through 2030. A portion of that funding is designated to help schools and farmers manage the administrative and logistical demands of participating.

title: To amend title 40, United States Code, to permit commercial filmmaking and photography on the United States Capitol grounds, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill would change current law to allow commercial film crews and photographers to use the outdoor grounds of the U.S. Capitol for paid, professional work. Right now, commercial filming and photography on Capitol grounds is not allowed. Under this bill, the Chief of the U.S. Capitol Police could issue permits for these activities, but only in outdoor areas and only when Congress is not in session. Permit holders would also have to pay a fee to cover any costs the government incurs.

The types of projects allowed would be limited to work similar to what was previously permitted in Union Square, a nearby area that used to be managed by the National Park Service before it came under Capitol authority in 2012. The fees charged would also be set at a similar level to what the Park Service used to charge. Any money collected from these permits would go into a special Capitol Trust Account used to support the Capitol grounds.

This bill mainly affects professional filmmakers, commercial photographers, and production companies who want to use the iconic Capitol backdrop for commercial projects like advertisements, TV shows, or movies. It also affects the U.S. Capitol Police, who would take on the new responsibility of reviewing and approving permit requests. Everyday visitors and tourists would not be affected, as the bill only covers paid, commercial activity.
ai_headline: Bill would open Capitol grounds to commercial filming for the first time
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7162 would let the Capitol Police issue paid permits for commercial film and photo shoots on outdoor Capitol grounds when Congress is not in session.
ai_sections.what: HR 7162 would change current law to allow commercial filming and photography on the outdoor grounds of the U.S. Capitol, which is not permitted under existing rules. The U.S. Capitol Police Chief could issue permits, but only for outdoor areas and only when Congress is not in session. Permitted projects would be limited to work similar to what was previously allowed in nearby Union Square when it was managed by the National Park Service.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects professional filmmakers, commercial photographers, and production companies seeking to use the Capitol as a backdrop for projects such as advertisements, TV shows, or movies. The U.S. Capitol Police would take on new responsibilities reviewing and approving permit applications. Everyday visitors and tourists are not affected.
ai_sections.why: Permit holders would be required to pay fees comparable to what the National Park Service previously charged for similar activity in Union Square before that area came under Capitol authority in 2012. Revenue collected from permits would go into the Capitol Trust Account, which supports the Capitol grounds.
ai_sections.cost: Permit applicants would pay a fee set at a level similar to what the National Park Service previously charged for Union Square. All fees collected would be deposited into the Capitol Trust Account to cover government costs incurred.
costChips: Fees mirror prior NPS Union Square rates
Revenue goes to Capitol Trust Account

title: John W. Walsh Alpha-1 Home Infusion Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require Medicare Part B to cover treatment for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency, a rare inherited disorder where the body does not make enough of a protein that protects the lungs and liver. The specific treatment is called augmentation therapy, which involves receiving an infusion of that missing protein directly into the bloodstream. Right now, Medicare does not clearly cover this treatment when it is done at home, which can force patients to travel to clinics or pay out of pocket. The bill would change that by officially adding home infusion for this condition to the list of services Medicare Part B covers, starting January 1, 2027.

The bill also sets up a payment system so that home infusion companies can be paid by Medicare for both the supplies needed to deliver the IV treatment and for up to two hours of nursing care during each infusion visit. Medicare would cover 80 percent of the approved cost, meaning patients would generally be responsible for the remaining 20 percent, the same cost-sharing structure used for most other Part B services.

The people most directly affected are Medicare enrollees who have Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency and need regular infusion therapy to manage their emphysema. This is a relatively small group, since the condition is rare, but the change could make treatment much more convenient and affordable for them by allowing care at home instead of a medical facility. Home infusion therapy suppliers and the nurses who provide this service would also be affected, as they would now have a clear Medicare billing pathway for this specific treatment.
ai_headline: Medicare would cover at-home infusions for rare lung disorder starting 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2343 adds Medicare Part B coverage for at-home augmentation therapy for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency starting January 1, 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 2343 would add home infusion augmentation therapy for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency to the list of services covered under Medicare Part B, effective January 1, 2027. Augmentation therapy delivers a missing protein directly into the bloodstream to protect the lungs and liver. Currently, Medicare does not clearly cover this treatment when performed at home, which can require patients to travel to clinics or pay out of pocket.
ai_sections.who: Medicare enrollees with Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency who need regular infusion therapy to manage their emphysema would be directly affected, though the condition is rare. Home infusion therapy suppliers and nurses providing this service would also be affected, as the bill creates a clear Medicare billing pathway for this treatment.
ai_sections.why: Patients who currently lack clear Medicare coverage for home infusion may travel to medical facilities or pay out of pocket for each treatment. Establishing a billing pathway and payment structure would shift where and how this care is delivered and reimbursed.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare would cover 80 percent of the approved cost for each home infusion visit, with patients responsible for the remaining 20 percent, consistent with standard Part B cost-sharing. The payment structure would cover both the supplies needed to deliver the IV treatment and up to two hours of nursing care per visit.
costChips: Covers supplies plus up to 2 hrs nursing

title: Access to Breast Cancer Diagnosis Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require health insurance plans to cover certain breast exams at no cost to the patient. Specifically, it eliminates deductibles, copays, and coinsurance for two types of exams: diagnostic exams, which are used when a doctor notices something unusual and needs a closer look, and supplemental exams, which are extra screenings for people at higher risk due to family history or other factors. These exams can include MRIs, ultrasounds, and diagnostic mammograms. The rule would take effect for insurance plan years starting January 1, 2026.

The bill applies to most private health insurance, including job-based group plans and plans people buy on their own. It also covers older "grandfathered" health plans that were locked in before the Affordable Care Act, which normally do not have to follow newer coverage rules. Plans linked to Health Savings Accounts, known as high-deductible health plans, are also included without losing their special tax status under this bill.

People most directly affected are those who need follow-up breast imaging after an abnormal screening result, or those whose doctors recommend extra screening because of elevated personal risk. Currently, these diagnostic and supplemental exams often come with out-of-pocket costs even when a routine mammogram is fully covered. Insurance companies and employers who offer health coverage would be required to absorb those costs instead of passing them on to patients.
ai_headline: Bill would end out-of-pocket costs for diagnostic and supplemental breast exams
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3037 bans deductibles and copays for diagnostic and supplemental breast exams starting January 1, 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 3037 would require health insurance plans to cover diagnostic and supplemental breast exams with no deductibles, copays, or coinsurance charged to patients. Covered exams include MRIs, ultrasounds, and diagnostic mammograms. The requirement would take effect for plan years beginning January 1, 2026.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects people who need follow-up breast imaging after an abnormal screening result or who are recommended extra screening due to elevated personal risk. It applies to most private health insurance, including job-based group plans, individual plans, grandfathered plans, and high-deductible health plans linked to Health Savings Accounts.
ai_sections.why: Currently, diagnostic and supplemental breast exams often carry out-of-pocket costs even when a routine mammogram is fully covered. Insurance companies and employers offering health coverage would be required to absorb those costs rather than passing them to patients.
ai_sections.cost: Insurance companies and employers who offer health coverage would be required to absorb the costs of diagnostic and supplemental breast exams rather than charging patients.
costChips: Insurers and employers absorb exam costs
No cost sharing passed to patients
Applies to most private health plans

title: To include the Czech Republic in the list of foreign states whose nationals are eligible for admission into the United States as E1 nonimmigrants if United States nationals are treated similarly by the Government of the Czech Republic.
ai_summary:
This bill would add the Czech Republic to a list of countries whose citizens can apply for a special type of US visa called an E-1 visa, also known as a "treaty trader" visa. An E-1 visa allows people from certain countries to come to the United States temporarily to carry out significant trade in goods, services, or technology between their home country and the US. Right now, Czech citizens cannot get this visa because the Czech Republic is not on the approved list. There is one condition: the Czech government must offer the same kind of visa access to American citizens in return.

This bill affects Czech nationals who work in international trade and want to do business in the United States, as well as US companies that trade with Czech partners. Currently, a Czech citizen involved in trade with the US has no clear path to enter the country under this specific visa category. If the bill passes and the Czech Republic holds up its end by giving Americans the same treatment, Czech traders could apply for E-1 status and work in the US temporarily.

The broader impact is relatively narrow. It does not change immigration law in a major way or affect most people. It is mainly relevant to business owners, traders, and companies on both sides that deal in US-Czech commerce. The bill applies equally and only if both countries agree to treat each other's citizens the same way, making it a mutual arrangement rather than a one-sided policy change.
ai_headline: HR 1056 would open US treaty trader visas to Czech citizens
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1056 adds Czech Republic to the E-1 treaty trader visa list, but only if Czech Republic grants Americans the same access.
ai_sections.what: HR 1056 would add the Czech Republic to the list of countries whose citizens are eligible for E-1 treaty trader visas, which allow temporary entry into the US to conduct significant trade in goods, services, or technology. Currently, Czech citizens have no path to this visa category because the Czech Republic is not on the approved list. The change takes effect only if the Czech government grants equivalent visa access to American citizens in return.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects Czech nationals working in international trade who seek to do business in the United States, as well as US companies that trade with Czech partners. It does not affect most immigrants or travelers and is narrowly relevant to business owners and traders on both sides of US-Czech commerce.
ai_sections.why: If passed and the Czech Republic meets the reciprocity requirement, Czech traders would gain a formal visa pathway to work temporarily in the United States. The bill does not make a major change to broader immigration law, and its reach is limited to US-Czech commercial activity.

title: FEMA Accountability Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires FEMA to publish a detailed monthly report on how money from the Disaster Relief Fund is being spent. Starting within 60 days of becoming law, FEMA's administrator must send these reports to key congressional committees every month. The reports must cover things like how much money is available, how much has been officially committed to projects, how much has actually been paid out, and which projects have been stuck waiting for a decision for more than 180 days. FEMA must also post each report on its public website within 10 days of submitting it to Congress.

The bill also requires FEMA to create a standardized format for reporting this financial data within 90 days, following federal open data rules. This is meant to make the information easier to understand and compare over time. It also requires FEMA to explain any money that has been held back, delayed, or moved to a different purpose, including when that money is expected to be released.

This bill would directly affect FEMA as an agency, since it creates new reporting requirements. It would also affect state and local governments, tribal communities, and U.S. territories that receive federal disaster aid, since they could track the status of funds meant for their communities. Ordinary residents recovering from declared disasters could also benefit, since the public reporting would make it easier to see whether promised federal money is actually being delivered.
ai_headline: New law would force FEMA to publish monthly disaster fund reports
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7461 requires FEMA to publish monthly Disaster Relief Fund spending reports to Congress and online within strict deadlines.
ai_sections.what: HR 7461 requires FEMA to submit detailed monthly reports on Disaster Relief Fund spending to key congressional committees, beginning within 60 days of enactment. Reports must include available balances, committed funds, actual payments made, and projects stalled for more than 180 days. FEMA must also post each report publicly on its website within 10 days of congressional submission and adopt a standardized open-data reporting format within 90 days.
ai_sections.who: FEMA is directly affected as the agency responsible for producing and publishing the reports. State, local, tribal, and territorial governments receiving federal disaster aid, as well as residents recovering from declared disasters, would have access to real-time fund tracking information.
ai_sections.why: The reporting requirements would create a public record of whether committed disaster funds are being delivered, and would require FEMA to explain any money that has been held back, delayed, or redirected, including expected release timelines. Over time, the standardized data format is intended to make spending patterns easier to track and compare across disaster events.

title: PREEMIE Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill renews federal funding and programs focused on premature births in the United States. The original PREEMIE Act was first passed years ago and has been updated a few times since. This version extends the law through 2029, keeping money flowing to research on why babies are born early, how to prevent it, and how to better care for premature and low birthweight infants.

The bill also makes one important change to a previous requirement. An earlier version of the law said the government "may" create a team of agencies working together on premature birth issues. This bill changes that word to "shall," meaning the Department of Health and Human Services is now required to create that interagency working group within 18 months. On top of that, it directs the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to bring together a panel of maternal health experts to conduct a study and deliver a full report to Congress within two years. That report must cover the financial costs of premature births, factors that lead to early deliveries, and ways to detect risks sooner.

This bill mainly affects premature babies and their families, pregnant women who may be at risk of early delivery, and the doctors and researchers working in maternal and infant health. It also shapes how federal health agencies spend money and focus their work. It does not directly give money to families or change insurance rules, but the research and programs it funds are aimed at reducing the number of premature births and improving outcomes for babies who are born early.
ai_headline: Federal preemie research law extended to 2029 with new mandates
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1197 extends the PREEMIE Act through 2029 and requires a new interagency working group and a National Academies study on premature births.
ai_sections.what: HR 1197 renews the PREEMIE Act through 2029, continuing federal research programs on the causes, prevention, and care of premature and low birthweight births. The bill changes a prior optional provision to a firm requirement, directing the Department of Health and Human Services to establish an interagency working group on premature birth within 18 months. It also directs the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to convene a maternal health expert panel and deliver a report to Congress within two years covering costs, risk factors, and early detection.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects premature infants and their families, pregnant women at risk of early delivery, and medical and research professionals in maternal and infant health. It also shapes how federal health agencies allocate funding and direct their work.
ai_sections.why: Extending the law keeps federal research funding active and creates new accountability structures for how agencies coordinate on premature birth. The required study and working group may influence future policy, spending priorities, and clinical practice in maternal and infant health.
ai_sections.cost: The bill extends existing federal funding for premature birth research and programs through 2029, though the summary does not specify a dollar amount. No direct payments to families or changes to insurance rules are included.

title: Disaster Aid Without Delay Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill targets a specific rule about how FEMA, the federal disaster relief agency, sends out money after a declared disaster. It would ban the Department of Homeland Security from setting a minimum dollar amount that disaster relief funds must reach before they can be approved or sent out. In other words, the government could not hold back aid just because a request falls below some set dollar figure.

Right now, FEMA operates under the Stafford Act, which is the main law that allows the federal government to send money to states, communities, and individuals after major disasters like hurricanes, floods, or wildfires. The bill says that no policy, rule, or internal guidance can create a financial cutoff point that slows down or blocks that money from flowing to people who need it.

This would most directly affect people in areas that have been hit by disasters, particularly smaller communities whose damage costs might not reach a high dollar threshold. It would also affect state and local governments that apply for federal disaster assistance. FEMA itself would face new legal limits on how it manages its internal approval process for releasing funds.
ai_headline: Bill would bar FEMA from setting minimum thresholds on disaster aid approvals
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7829 would prohibit DHS from requiring disaster relief requests to meet a minimum dollar amount before FEMA can release funds.
ai_sections.what: HR 7829 would ban the Department of Homeland Security from establishing any minimum dollar threshold that disaster relief funds must reach before being approved or distributed. No policy, rule, or internal guidance could create a financial cutoff that delays or blocks aid. The bill applies within the existing framework of the Stafford Act, the main federal law governing disaster assistance to states, communities, and individuals.
ai_sections.who: People in disaster-affected areas, particularly those in smaller communities whose damage costs may not reach high dollar thresholds, are most directly affected. State and local governments that apply for federal disaster assistance, and FEMA itself, would also be subject to the bill's requirements.
ai_sections.why: Removing minimum thresholds could change how quickly and broadly FEMA releases funds after declared disasters such as hurricanes, floods, or wildfires. FEMA would face new legal limits on how it manages its internal approval process for disbursing disaster relief money.

title: Sport Fish Restoration, Recreational Boating Safety, and Wildlife Restoration Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill updates and extends several federal programs that support fishing, boating, and wildlife conservation. First, it extends the funding rules under the Dingell-Johnson Sport Fish Restoration Act through 2031, instead of letting them expire in 2026. This law directs money from taxes on fishing equipment and motorboat fuel to state fish and wildlife agencies. The bill also adjusts how much money can go to interstate fisheries commissions, which are groups of states that work together to manage shared fish populations like striped bass or salmon.

The bill also expands what counts as an allowed use of federal boating infrastructure grants. Right now, those grants help build and maintain docks and facilities for recreational boaters. This bill would add a new category: building or upgrading fueling stations that offer alternative marine fuels, such as biodiesel or fuels made from cooking oil, animal fats, or plant materials. The idea is to give boaters more options beyond standard gasoline or diesel at the marina.

Finally, the bill lowers the federal excise tax on a specific product: portable, battery-powered bait containers that keep live bait aerated. Currently those are taxed at 10 percent at the manufacturer level, the same as most fishing tackle. This bill would cut that tax to 3 percent. People who are most affected by this bill overall include recreational fishermen, boaters, state fish and wildlife agencies, and marina operators across the country.
ai_headline: Bill extends fishing and boating programs, cuts tax on live bait aerators
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported by Unanimous Consent.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3858 extends sport fish funding rules to 2031, expands boating grants, and cuts the excise tax on live bait aerators from 10% to 3%.
ai_sections.what: HR 3858 extends the funding rules under the Dingell-Johnson Sport Fish Restoration Act from 2026 through 2031, directing taxes on fishing equipment and motorboat fuel to state fish and wildlife agencies. It expands federal boating infrastructure grants to cover construction or upgrades of alternative marine fuel stations, including biodiesel and fuels derived from cooking oil, animal fats, or plant materials. It also reduces the federal excise tax on portable, battery-powered live bait aeration containers from 10 percent to 3 percent.
ai_sections.who: Recreational fishermen, boaters, marina operators, and state fish and wildlife agencies across the country are directly affected. Interstate fisheries commissions, which manage shared fish populations such as striped bass and salmon, are also subject to adjusted funding allocations under the bill.
ai_sections.why: Extending the funding rules maintains the flow of excise tax revenues to state agencies past the current 2026 expiration date. Adding alternative fuel stations to eligible grant uses and lowering the bait container tax rate each change what federal dollars can support and what manufacturers and consumers pay.
ai_sections.cost: Taxes on fishing equipment and motorboat fuel fund state fish and wildlife agencies under the Dingell-Johnson Sport Fish Restoration Act, with the bill extending those rules through 2031. The excise tax on portable battery-powered live bait aeration containers would drop from 10 percent to 3 percent at the manufacturer level.
costChips: Fishing/boating fuel taxes fund state agencies

title: Advanced Wastewater Treatment Assistance Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a federal grant program run by the EPA that would send money to every state to upgrade their wastewater treatment systems. Congress would authorize up to one billion dollars between 2026 and 2030 for these upgrades. The goal is to fund "advanced" treatment, meaning systems that can remove more harmful substances from wastewater than older technology can handle. States and the EPA can each keep up to 1 percent of the funds for administrative costs, and the EPA would set a formula to decide how much each state receives.

There is a significant focus on lower-income and underserved areas. At least 49 percent of the total funding must go toward projects serving disadvantaged communities, small towns, rural areas, or tribal treatment facilities. Normally a state or local government would need to pay at least half the project cost itself, but that cost-sharing requirement is waived entirely for projects serving qualifying disadvantaged communities, meaning those places could receive fully federally funded upgrades.

The bill also requires the EPA to work with the National Academies of Sciences to study how well advanced wastewater technologies remove emerging contaminants, specifically PFAS chemicals (sometimes called "forever chemicals") and nanomaterials. An interim report would be due within three years and a final report within five years. This bill affects local governments and water utilities across the country, particularly in rural, small, and low-income communities that rely on publicly owned treatment systems.
ai_headline: EPA grant program would send $1B to states for wastewater upgrades
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8027 authorizes up to $1B in EPA grants (2026–2030) for advanced wastewater treatment, with 49% reserved for disadvantaged and rural communities.
ai_sections.what: HR 8027 establishes a federal grant program administered by the EPA to fund advanced wastewater treatment system upgrades in every state. Congress would authorize up to one billion dollars between 2026 and 2030. At least 49 percent of total funding must go to projects serving disadvantaged communities, small towns, rural areas, or tribal treatment facilities.
ai_sections.who: The program affects local governments and water utilities across the country, particularly those in rural, small, and low-income communities that rely on publicly owned treatment systems. Tribal treatment facilities are also specifically included as eligible recipients.
ai_sections.why: Advanced treatment systems can remove more harmful substances from wastewater than older technology, and the bill targets resources toward areas that may lack the financial capacity to fund such upgrades independently. The bill also directs the EPA and the National Academies of Sciences to study how well advanced wastewater technologies remove PFAS chemicals and nanomaterials, with an interim report due in three years and a final report in five.
ai_sections.cost: Congress would authorize up to one billion dollars for the program between 2026 and 2030. States and the EPA may each retain up to 1 percent of funds for administrative costs. The normal requirement that states or local governments pay at least half of project costs is waived entirely for qualifying disadvantaged communities, making those projects fully federally funded.
costChips: States and EPA each keep up to 1% for admin

title: SAFE Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets a specific problem in the trucking industry called "chameleon carriers." These are trucking companies that have been caught breaking safety rules, fined, or shut down, and then simply restart under a new company name to avoid their bad record and keep operating. The bill directs the federal government to study how widespread this problem is, including how many crashes, deaths, and injuries these carriers may have caused since 2012.

The bill also requires the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration to build a computer tool that automatically screens applications for new trucking operating licenses. The tool would look for warning signs that a new applicant is actually an old unsafe company in disguise, checking things like shared addresses, phone numbers, equipment, drivers, managers, and insurance history. A human employee would still make the final decision on every application, and the bill specifically says the computer cannot make the final call on its own. Anyone who gets flagged and denied must be told why within 30 days and given a chance to appeal.

This bill mainly affects trucking companies, freight brokers, and equipment providers who apply for federal operating licenses. It would also affect federal regulators who enforce trucking safety rules. For everyday Americans, the goal is safer roads by making it harder for companies with dangerous safety records to quietly restart and continue hauling freight on public highways.
ai_headline: Federal bill takes aim at unsafe trucking firms that reopen under new names
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.
