ai_sections.tldr: HR 7539 would require an automated screening tool to catch unsafe trucking companies that dodge their records by restarting under a new name.
ai_sections.what: HR 7539 directs the federal government to study how many unsafe trucking companies have evaded enforcement by restarting under new names, a practice called "chameleon carriers," including crash, death, and injury data going back to 2012. It also requires the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration to build an automated screening tool that flags new license applications showing signs of being a disguised unsafe carrier, checking shared addresses, phone numbers, equipment, drivers, managers, and insurance history. A human employee must make every final licensing decision, and any denied applicant must receive a written reason within 30 days and an opportunity to appeal.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects trucking companies, freight brokers, and equipment providers applying for federal operating licenses, as well as federal regulators who enforce trucking safety rules. Everyday drivers and passengers on public highways are the broader population affected by its road safety goals.
ai_sections.why: Chameleon carriers that restart under new names can continue hauling freight on public highways despite prior safety violations, fines, or shutdowns, allowing unsafe operations to continue without consequence. The automated screening tool and study requirement are intended to close the gap that currently allows these companies to obtain new federal licenses undetected.

title: Follow the Science Act
ai_summary:
This bill would place new restrictions on political appointees at the National Institutes of Health (NIH), which is the main federal agency that funds medical and scientific research. Specifically, it would bar most political employees from working at NIH or influencing how it runs. It would also stop political appointees from having any role in deciding which research projects get funded, such as reviewing grant applications or awarding contracts. The only exceptions are the heads of NIH and two specific sub-agencies. The bill also requires NIH to report to Congress within 30 days on whether political employees have been involved in funding decisions going back to January 2021.

The bill also addresses NIH's ability to cancel or pause research grants and contracts. Under this proposal, NIH could only cancel or suspend a funded project if there is written proof of specific problems like financial mismanagement, research fraud, or misconduct. NIH would then have to notify Congress within 30 days. Otherwise, all funded agreements would have to be carried out fully, on the original timeline and terms.

This bill primarily affects NIH as an institution, the researchers and universities that receive NIH funding, and political appointees in the executive branch. Indirectly, it could affect patients and the public who benefit from federally funded medical research. The bill was introduced in January 2026 and has been referred to the House Energy and Commerce Committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on.
ai_headline: Bill would ban most political appointees from NIH funding decisions
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7173 would bar most political appointees from NIH grant and contract decisions, with limited exceptions for agency heads.
ai_sections.what: HR 7173 would prohibit most political employees from working at or influencing the National Institutes of Health, and would bar them from any role in reviewing grant applications or awarding contracts. Exceptions exist only for the NIH director and heads of two specific sub-agencies. The bill would also restrict NIH from canceling or pausing funded projects unless written proof of financial mismanagement, research fraud, or misconduct is provided.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects NIH as an institution, political appointees in the executive branch, and researchers and universities that receive NIH funding. Patients and the general public who rely on federally funded medical research could also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Removing political appointees from funding decisions could change how grant and contract awards are made and reviewed at NIH. Restricting NIH's ability to cancel or suspend grants without documented cause could affect the agency's flexibility in managing its research portfolio.

title: Economic Recovery for Nuclear-Affected Communities Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets towns and counties that are stuck hosting leftover nuclear waste after a power plant has shut down or is shutting down. When a nuclear plant closes, the spent fuel often cannot be moved because there is no national storage facility to send it to, so it sits on-site in large steel-and-concrete canisters. The bill would set up a federal grant program, run by the U.S. Economic Development Administration, that pays these communities $15 for every kilogram of spent fuel sitting in their backyard. Communities that lost a big share of their tax revenue when the plant closed can also apply for separate grants that phase in over eight years, starting at 80 percent of what they lost and stepping down each year, up to $10 million per year. The government would be allowed to spend up to $110 million a year from 2026 through 2031, and $120 million a year from 2032 through 2036.

The bill also creates a $500,000 prize competition where people and organizations can submit ideas for what these communities could do with the land or workforce left behind after a plant closes. The winning idea would then be turned into a small pilot program funded by the same pool of money.

Finally, the bill revives a first-time homebuyer tax credit, but limits it to people buying their first home specifically in one of these affected communities. This is meant to encourage people to move into or stay in areas that might otherwise struggle to attract new residents because of the presence of stored nuclear waste. The communities most directly affected are in states like New York, Illinois, California, Michigan, Vermont, and about ten others that currently have closed or closing nuclear plants.
ai_headline: Federal grants would pay nuclear host towns $15 per kg of stored spent fuel
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7796 would send up to $110–120M/year to communities stranded with nuclear waste after plant closures.
ai_sections.what: HR 7796 creates a federal grant program, administered by the U.S. Economic Development Administration, that pays communities $15 per kilogram of spent nuclear fuel stored on-site after a plant closes. Communities that lost significant tax revenue from a closure can also receive phased grants starting at 80 percent of lost revenue and stepping down over eight years, capped at $10 million per year. A $500,000 prize competition would solicit ideas for reusing the land or workforce left behind, with the winning concept funded as a pilot program.
ai_sections.who: The bill targets towns and counties that are currently hosting stored spent nuclear fuel because no national storage facility exists to receive it. Communities in roughly a dozen states — including New York, Illinois, California, Michigan, and Vermont — are most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without a national storage site, spent fuel remains on-site in steel-and-concrete canisters indefinitely after plants close, leaving communities bearing the presence of nuclear waste with reduced tax bases. The bill also revives a first-time homebuyer tax credit, restricted to buyers purchasing in these specific communities, which may affect residential population trends in affected areas.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government could spend up to $110 million per year from 2026 through 2031 and up to $120 million per year from 2032 through 2036. Tax-revenue-loss grants are capped at $10 million per community per year, and the prize competition carries a $500,000 award.

title: PARA–EMT Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill tries to fix a serious shortage of emergency medical technicians (EMTs) and paramedics across the country. It would create a grant program giving up to $1 million each to ambulance and EMS agencies to help them hire, train, and keep more EMTs and paramedics. The money could be used for things like training programs, apprenticeships, mental health support for workers, and even recruiting high school students into the field. At least 20 percent of the grants would have to go to agencies in rural areas. Congress would authorize spending $50 million per year from 2026 through 2030 for this program.

The bill also has a separate piece focused on military veterans. Many veterans already received solid EMT or paramedic training while serving in the armed forces, but they often have to jump through expensive hoops to get civilian certification when they return home. This bill would give states $20 million per year to help cover those transition costs, like testing fees and licensing expenses, so veterans can more easily move into civilian EMS jobs.

Finally, the bill directs the Departments of Labor and Health and Human Services to study exactly how bad the EMT and paramedic shortage is expected to be through 2034 and report back to Congress within one year. The people most directly affected by this bill are current and future EMTs and paramedics, EMS agencies (especially small and rural ones), veterans with medical training, and anyone who might someday need emergency medical care.
ai_headline: Bill targets EMT shortage with $50M yearly grants and veteran licensing aid
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2220 would spend up to $50M/year on EMS hiring and training grants, plus $20M/year to help veterans get civilian EMT licenses.
ai_sections.what: HR 2220 would create a federal grant program giving EMS and ambulance agencies up to $1 million each to hire, train, and retain EMTs and paramedics. Eligible uses include training programs, apprenticeships, mental health support, and high school recruitment. A separate provision would help states cover the licensing and testing costs veterans face when transitioning their military medical training to civilian EMS credentials.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects current and future EMTs and paramedics, EMS agencies especially in rural areas, and veterans with military medical training. Anyone who may someday need emergency medical care is also among those affected.
ai_sections.why: The bill responds to a documented national shortage of EMTs and paramedics, and directs the Departments of Labor and Health and Human Services to study how severe that shortage is expected to be through 2034. Without action, gaps in emergency medical staffing could affect response capacity, particularly in rural communities where at least 20 percent of grants would be directed.
ai_sections.cost: The grant program for EMS agencies is authorized at $50 million per year from 2026 through 2030. The veteran transition assistance program is authorized at $20 million per year for the same period.
costChips: Rural agencies get at least 20% of grants

title: World LEAP Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new federal grant program run by the Department of Education. School districts could apply for three-year grants to start new foreign language programs or improve ones they already have. The program would be funded at $15 million per year starting in 2026. To receive money, districts would need to show a plan for how their language program will keep running after the grant ends, and they must include teacher training as part of their program.

The bill puts a strong focus on building up the teacher workforce for language education. At least 20 percent of any grant money must go toward training teachers, including helping teacher's aides get certified as full language teachers. The bill also encourages districts to partner with community language schools, colleges, and state licensing offices to make it easier for people to become qualified language teachers. Grants would go to programs that teach students a second language for at least half the school day, starting as early as pre-kindergarten and continuing through middle school.

The people most directly affected are K-12 students across the country, particularly English learners and students who speak a language other than English at home. School districts, their teachers, and teacher's aides would also be affected, since the bill creates new job training and certification opportunities. Congress included this bill partly because it found that many U.S. employers say they cannot find enough workers with foreign language skills, and that shortages of language teachers have made it harder for schools to offer these programs.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund new school foreign language programs starting in 2026
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1572 creates a $15M/year federal grant program for school foreign language programs, requiring 20% of funds go to teacher training.
ai_sections.what: HR 1572 would create a federal grant program administered by the Department of Education, allowing school districts to apply for three-year grants to start or expand foreign language programs. Grants would support programs that teach a second language for at least half the school day, from pre-kindergarten through middle school. Districts must submit a sustainability plan showing how their program will continue after the grant period ends.
ai_sections.who: K-12 students nationwide would be affected, especially English learners and students who speak a language other than English at home. School districts, teachers, and teacher's aides would also be affected, as the bill creates new training and certification pathways.
ai_sections.why: At least 20 percent of each grant must fund teacher training, including pathways for teacher's aides to become certified language teachers. The bill also encourages partnerships with community language schools, colleges, and state licensing offices to expand the pool of qualified language teachers.
ai_sections.cost: The program would be funded at $15 million per year beginning in 2026.
costChips: $15 million per year in federal funds

title: Myakka Wild and Scenic River Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would officially add about 34 miles of the Myakka River in Sarasota County, Florida to the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System. That is a federal program that protects rivers from being dammed or developed in ways that would harm their natural character. The river would be divided into several segments, each labeled as "wild," "scenic," or "recreational" depending on how developed or natural that stretch already is. A wild designation means the most protected and undisturbed, scenic means some access roads or land use nearby, and recreational means more human activity is present.

The federal government would share management of the river with an existing local group called the Myakka River Management Coordinating Council, which already includes state agencies, local governments, farmers, landowners, and environmental groups. The federal government cannot take anyone's private land by force to protect the river. Any land the government acquires would have to be donated or purchased with the owner's agreement. The river would also not become part of the National Park System, so it would not be run like a national park.

This bill mainly affects people who live near, own land along, or regularly use the Myakka River in Sarasota County. Local governments like the City of Venice and City of North Port would be involved in cooperative agreements with federal officials. Visitors and outdoor recreation users of the river could see stricter protections put in place to preserve the river's natural condition. Florida state agencies would continue managing lands they already control, just with added federal coordination.
ai_headline: Bill would add 34 miles of Myakka River to federal wild and scenic protection
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 642 would add about 34 miles of the Myakka River in Sarasota County, FL to the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System.
ai_sections.what: HR 642 would officially designate about 34 miles of the Myakka River in Sarasota County, Florida as part of the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System, a federal program that prohibits damming or development that would harm a river's natural character. The river would be divided into segments labeled wild, scenic, or recreational based on how developed or natural each stretch already is. The river would not become part of the National Park System.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects residents, landowners, and regular users of the Myakka River in Sarasota County. Local governments including the City of Venice and City of North Port would enter cooperative agreements with federal officials, and Florida state agencies would continue managing lands they already control with added federal coordination.
ai_sections.why: Federal designation would place stricter protections on the river, limiting certain development and damming activities along the affected 34-mile stretch. Management responsibility would be shared between the federal government and the existing Myakka River Management Coordinating Council, which includes state agencies, local governments, farmers, landowners, and environmental groups.

title: American Petroleum First Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with a longstanding federal rule called the Jones Act, which requires that goods shipped between U.S. ports must travel on ships that are built in the United States, owned by Americans, and crewed by American citizens or permanent residents. HR 8021 would create an exception to that rule specifically for ships carrying crude oil and petroleum products. In other words, foreign-built or foreign-owned tankers could move oil between U.S. ports, something they currently cannot do legally.

However, the bill does not open the door to all foreign ships. It specifically bans any vessel that is owned, flagged, or crewed by Russian or Chinese nationals or their governments from taking advantage of this new exception. So while the bill relaxes the rules for most of the world's shipping fleet, it keeps a hard restriction against Russian and Chinese involvement in moving American oil domestically.

The people most directly affected are oil and gas companies that ship petroleum between U.S. locations, such as from Gulf Coast refineries to East Coast ports. Right now they must use a limited number of American-built tankers, which can drive up costs. If this bill passed, they would have access to a much larger pool of ships, potentially lowering shipping costs. American shipbuilders and the sailors who work on Jones Act vessels could face more competition, since demand for their services in the oil transport sector could decrease. Consumers might see indirect effects on fuel prices, though how large those effects would be is debated among economists.
ai_headline: House bill would let foreign tankers carry U.S. oil between domestic ports
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Coast Guard and Maritime Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8021 carves out a Jones Act exception for foreign oil tankers, while barring Russian and Chinese vessels entirely.
ai_sections.what: HR 8021 would create an exception to the Jones Act, the federal law requiring that goods shipped between U.S. ports travel on American-built, American-owned, American-crewed vessels. The exception applies specifically to ships carrying crude oil and petroleum products. Ships owned, flagged, or crewed by Russian or Chinese nationals or their governments are explicitly prohibited from using this exception.
ai_sections.who: Oil and gas companies that ship petroleum between U.S. locations, such as from Gulf Coast refineries to East Coast ports, would gain access to a larger pool of available ships. American shipbuilders and sailors who work on Jones Act vessels could face increased competition in the oil transport sector.
ai_sections.why: Because Jones Act rules currently limit oil shippers to a smaller pool of American-built tankers, passage could reduce shipping costs for petroleum transport. Economists disagree on how large any indirect effects on consumer fuel prices would be.

title: Food Secure Strikers Act of 2025
ai_summary:
Current federal law makes workers who go on strike ineligible for SNAP benefits, commonly known as food stamps, unless they were already receiving SNAP before the strike began. This bill would remove that restriction. Under the Food Secure Strikers Act of 2025, a worker's decision to go on strike could no longer be used as a reason to deny or cut off their SNAP benefits. Workers on strike would be treated the same as anyone else when applying for food assistance, meaning eligibility would be based on income and household size, not on whether they are striking.

This change would affect workers who participate in a labor strike and their family members living in the same household. If a strike reduces a worker's income enough to meet SNAP income limits, they and their household could apply for or continue receiving food assistance. It would also affect state agencies that run SNAP programs, since they would no longer screen out applicants based on strike status.

The bill does not guarantee SNAP benefits to all striking workers. It simply removes the automatic disqualification. A striking worker would still need to meet the same income and resource requirements as any other applicant. Whether someone qualifies would depend on how much, if any, pay they receive during the strike and what other income their household has.
ai_headline: Bill would let striking workers apply for food stamps on equal footing
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2357 removes the federal rule that blocks striking workers from receiving SNAP benefits based on their strike status.
ai_sections.what: Current federal law bars workers who go on strike from SNAP eligibility unless they were already receiving benefits before the strike began. HR 2357, the Food Secure Strikers Act of 2025, would eliminate that disqualification. Striking workers would be evaluated for SNAP using the same income and household-size criteria applied to all other applicants.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects workers who participate in a labor strike and their household members. It also affects state agencies that administer SNAP, which would no longer screen applicants based on strike status.
ai_sections.why: Removing the disqualification means a striking worker whose income falls below SNAP limits could apply for or continue receiving food assistance. State agencies would need to update their eligibility screening processes to stop treating strike participation as a disqualifying factor.

title: Ceasing Age-Based Trucking Restrictions Act
ai_summary:
Current federal law requires commercial truck drivers engaged in interstate transportation to be at least 21 years old. If a truck carries imported goods from a port to another location, even within the same state, federal rules have treated that as interstate transportation because the cargo originated from outside the country. That means young drivers between 18 and 20 years old cannot legally haul those loads, even on a short in-state route.

This bill changes that by saying that moving goods from a port of entry to another spot within the same state does not count as interstate transportation for commercial driver's license purposes. Under this change, a driver who is at least 18 and holds a valid state commercial license could legally haul imported goods on those in-state routes without needing to meet the federal interstate age requirement of 21.

This bill mainly affects young truck drivers aged 18 to 20, trucking companies that operate near ports, and businesses that receive imported goods. Ports that handle large amounts of cargo, like seaports or land border crossings, would see the most direct impact. Supporters of similar ideas often say it helps fill truck driver shortages, while critics raise safety concerns about younger drivers on commercial routes. The bill does not change any other federal trucking rules beyond this specific age and licensing question.
ai_headline: Bill would let 18-year-olds haul imported goods on in-state routes
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6691 would let drivers aged 18-20 haul imported port cargo on in-state routes, bypassing the federal 21-year-old interstate age requirement.
ai_sections.what: Federal law currently requires commercial truck drivers in interstate transportation to be at least 21 years old. Trucks carrying imported goods from a port to another in-state location have been treated as interstate transportation, blocking drivers aged 18 to 20 from those routes. HR 6691 would reclassify that port-to-in-state movement as non-interstate for commercial driver's license purposes, allowing drivers as young as 18 with a valid state commercial license to legally operate on those routes.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects commercial truck drivers aged 18 to 20 and trucking companies operating near ports. Businesses that receive imported goods and ports handling large cargo volumes, such as seaports and land border crossings, would also be impacted.
ai_sections.why: Extending driving eligibility to younger drivers on these routes could affect the overall commercial driver workforce near ports. Supporters link the change to addressing truck driver shortages, while critics have raised safety concerns about younger drivers on commercial routes.

title: AirFAIR Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets a specific pricing problem that can happen during disasters. When a state or U.S. territory officially declares a disaster or emergency, airlines and ticket sellers would be banned from raising airfare prices by 30% or more above normal levels. The Department of Transportation would write the specific rules defining what counts as an "excessive" price increase, with that 30% figure as the minimum threshold. Breaking this rule would be considered an unfair and deceptive business practice under existing federal law.

The bill also directs the Federal Aviation Administration to study whether airlines and ticket sellers have already been doing this kind of pricing during past disasters. The FAA would have one year to finish that study and report the findings to Congress.

This bill would affect anyone who buys airline tickets, particularly people trying to evacuate or travel during a declared disaster or emergency. It would also directly affect airlines, online travel booking sites, and any other businesses that sell plane tickets. People living in disaster-prone areas, such as those facing hurricanes or wildfires, would most likely feel the biggest impact, since they are the ones most often in urgent need of last-minute flights.
ai_headline: Airfare price hikes capped at 30% during declared disasters
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5502 bans airlines and ticket sellers from raising fares 30% or more above normal during declared disasters or emergencies.
ai_sections.what: HR 5502 would prohibit airlines and ticket sellers from raising airfare prices by 30% or more above normal levels during a declared disaster or emergency. The Department of Transportation would write rules defining what counts as an excessive price increase, using 30% as the minimum threshold. Violations would be treated as unfair and deceptive business practices under existing federal law.
ai_sections.who: The bill would directly affect airlines, online travel booking sites, and any other businesses that sell plane tickets. Travelers buying tickets during disasters or emergencies, especially those evacuating, would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: People in disaster-prone areas, such as those facing hurricanes or wildfires, often need last-minute flights urgently, making them vulnerable to sharp price increases. The bill also directs the FAA to study whether this kind of pricing has already occurred during past disasters and report findings to Congress within one year.

title: Maryland Whole Watershed Program Federal Partnership Act
ai_summary:
This bill lets a federal office called the Chesapeake Bay Program Office officially join the management team for Maryland's Whole Watershed Program. Right now, that program is run at the state level. This bill gives the federal office a seat at the table as an advisory member, meaning it can offer advice, share expertise, and help coordinate efforts between state, local, and federal agencies working to protect the Chesapeake Bay and the streams and rivers that flow into it.

The bill also allows the federal office to provide technical help and funding for projects within the Chesapeake Bay watershed. It could apply beyond just Maryland, covering similar programs in other states that border the watershed as well.

The people most directly affected are those who live and work near the Chesapeake Bay and its watershed, which spans parts of Maryland, Virginia, Pennsylvania, and several other states. This includes farmers, local governments, environmental agencies, and communities that depend on clean water in the region. The bill does not create new regulations on residents but could influence how water quality projects in the area are planned and funded.
ai_headline: Federal office gains advisory role in Maryland watershed program
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7788 gives the Chesapeake Bay Program Office an advisory seat in Maryland's Whole Watershed Program and lets it fund related projects.
ai_sections.what: HR 7788 allows the Chesapeake Bay Program Office, a federal agency, to formally join the management team of Maryland's Whole Watershed Program as an advisory member. In that role, the office can offer guidance, share expertise, and coordinate efforts among state, local, and federal agencies. The bill could also extend to similar programs in other states bordering the Chesapeake Bay watershed.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects farmers, local governments, environmental agencies, and communities that depend on clean water across parts of Maryland, Virginia, Pennsylvania, and other states in the watershed. The Chesapeake Bay Program Office and state-level program managers are also centrally involved.
ai_sections.why: Adding a federal advisory presence could shift how water quality projects in the region are planned and coordinated across multiple levels of government. The office's ability to provide technical help and funding may influence which projects move forward and how resources are distributed across the watershed.
ai_sections.cost: The bill allows the Chesapeake Bay Program Office to provide funding for projects within the Chesapeake Bay watershed, though no specific amounts are stated in the summary.
costChips: Federal funding allowed for watershed projects

title: EGG SAVE Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a temporary federal tax credit for egg-hatching businesses that buy and install technology that can identify the sex of a chicken egg before it hatches. The credit covers 50% of the equipment cost if installed in 2026, 40% in 2027, and 30% in 2028, and it expires completely after 2028. The equipment must be used at a U.S.-based commercial hatchery, must correctly identify the sex of embryos at least 95% of the time, and the credit also covers installation costs and any building changes needed to make the equipment work.

The reason this matters is that egg-laying hens are female, so male chicks hatched at egg production facilities currently have no commercial use and are typically killed shortly after birth. Technology that identifies male eggs before hatching could allow hatcheries to remove those eggs earlier in the process. The tax credit is designed to lower the financial barrier for hatcheries to adopt this kind of equipment, since it can be expensive to purchase and set up.

The businesses directly affected are commercial egg hatcheries across the United States. American consumers who buy eggs could also be indirectly affected if the technology lowers production costs or influences how major egg suppliers operate over time. The bill does not require any hatchery to use this technology; it simply makes it cheaper for those who choose to do so.
ai_headline: Tax credit would cover up to 50% of egg-sex detection equipment costs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5776 offers U.S. egg hatcheries a temporary federal tax credit of 30–50% on in-ovo sex-detection equipment through 2028.
ai_sections.what: HR 5776 creates a temporary federal tax credit for commercial egg hatcheries that purchase and install technology to identify the sex of a chicken egg before it hatches. The credit equals 50% of equipment costs for installations in 2026, 40% in 2027, and 30% in 2028, expiring entirely after 2028. Eligible costs include the equipment itself, installation, and any building modifications required to make the equipment operational.
ai_sections.who: Commercial egg hatcheries operating in the United States are the businesses directly affected. American consumers who buy eggs could be indirectly affected if the technology influences production costs or the practices of major egg suppliers over time.
ai_sections.why: Because only female chickens lay eggs, male chicks hatched at egg production facilities currently have no commercial use and are typically killed shortly after birth. This credit is intended to lower the financial barrier to adopting equipment that could allow hatcheries to remove male eggs earlier in the process, before hatching occurs.
ai_sections.cost: The tax credit covers 50% of equipment and related costs in 2026, 40% in 2027, and 30% in 2028, with the credit paid through reduced federal tax liability for participating hatcheries. The equipment must correctly identify embryo sex at least 95% of the time to qualify.

title: Zero Tolerance for Fraudsters Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates mandatory minimum prison sentences for people convicted of large-scale federal fraud crimes. Right now, judges have flexibility in deciding how long a fraud convict must serve. Under this bill, that flexibility goes away for big-dollar cases. If someone is convicted of fraud involving between $1 million and $5 million, they must serve at least one year in prison. If the fraud involved $5 million or more, they must serve at least five years. The bill covers a range of existing federal fraud laws, including mail fraud, wire fraud, healthcare fraud, and making false statements to the government.

The bill affects anyone prosecuted under these specific federal fraud statutes where the dollar amounts meet those thresholds. This could include corporate executives, contractors who overbill the government, healthcare providers who commit billing fraud, and others involved in large financial schemes. Judges would still set the final sentence within the ranges written in the bill, but they could no longer go below the minimum, even if they felt the circumstances called for a lighter sentence.

The bill does not affect smaller fraud cases below the $1 million mark, and it only applies to federal prosecutions, not state-level fraud cases. It was introduced in May 2026 and referred to the House Judiciary Committee, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: New bill sets mandatory minimums for million-dollar federal fraud
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8951 would require at least 1–5 years in prison for federal fraud topping $1 million, removing judges' ability to sentence below that floor.
ai_sections.what: HR 8951 would create mandatory minimum prison sentences for large-scale federal fraud convictions. Fraud involving $1 million to $5 million would carry a minimum of one year in prison; fraud of $5 million or more would carry a minimum of five years. The bill covers mail fraud, wire fraud, healthcare fraud, and making false statements to the government.
ai_sections.who: The bill would affect anyone convicted under the covered federal fraud statutes where the dollar thresholds are met, including corporate executives, government contractors who overbill, and healthcare providers who commit billing fraud. It applies only to federal prosecutions and not to state-level fraud cases.
ai_sections.why: Judges would retain the ability to set final sentences within the bill's ranges but could no longer impose a sentence below the mandatory minimum, even when they consider circumstances to warrant a lighter punishment. Cases involving fraud below $1 million and all state-level fraud prosecutions would remain unaffected.

title: Protecting Circuit Boards and Substrates Act
ai_summary:
This bill aims to bring more printed circuit board manufacturing back to the United States. Printed circuit boards are the flat green boards found inside nearly every electronic device, from smartphones to military equipment. Right now, most of these boards are made overseas, especially in Asia. The bill has two main tools: a tax credit and a grant program. Starting in 2026, any business that buys circuit boards made in the US can get a tax credit worth 25 percent of what they spent on those boards. Separately, the Department of Commerce would run a grant program with up to $3 billion available, giving money to companies that want to build, expand, or upgrade US factories that make circuit boards or related components called integrated circuit substrates.

Companies applying for grants would need to show they have a realistic plan to keep the factory running without ongoing government support, and larger companies must commit to worker training programs and opportunities for economically disadvantaged workers. Grants are generally capped at $300 million per project unless national security needs require more. Companies that miss their construction deadlines or share technology with foreign adversaries can be required to pay the money back. Foreign entities of concern, meaning companies tied to adversarial foreign governments, are completely blocked from receiving any funds.

The bill affects several groups. US manufacturers of electronics, defense contractors, and tech companies would benefit most directly through the tax credit if they buy American-made boards. Factories and companies that make circuit boards in the US could receive grants to grow their operations. Workers, particularly those from disadvantaged backgrounds, could gain access to new training programs connected to these facilities. Small businesses, minority-owned businesses, women-owned businesses, and veteran-owned businesses get preferential treatment in the grant process. Colleges, especially historically Black colleges, tribal colleges, and minority-serving institutions, are encouraged to partner with manufacturers on workforce training.
ai_headline: Bill offers tax credits and $3B in grants to rebuild US circuit board industry
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committees on Energy and Commerce, and Science, Space, and Technology, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3597 pairs a 25% tax credit on US-made circuit boards with up to $3B in factory grants starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 3597 would create a 25 percent tax credit for businesses that purchase printed circuit boards manufactured in the United States, beginning in 2026. It would also establish a grant program administered by the Department of Commerce, with up to $3 billion available for companies that build, expand, or upgrade US factories making circuit boards or integrated circuit substrates. Individual grants are generally capped at $300 million per project, with exceptions allowed for national security reasons.
ai_sections.who: US electronics manufacturers, defense contractors, and tech companies would be eligible for the tax credit, while domestic circuit board factory operators could apply for grants. Small businesses, minority-owned businesses, women-owned businesses, and veteran-owned businesses receive preferential treatment in the grant process, and historically Black colleges, tribal colleges, and minority-serving institutions are encouraged to participate in workforce training partnerships.
ai_sections.why: Most printed circuit boards are currently manufactured overseas, particularly in Asia, creating supply chain exposure for electronics and defense industries. Shifting production to the United States could change where these components are made and who makes them, with consequences for domestic manufacturing employment and national security supply chains.
ai_sections.cost: The grant program makes up to $3 billion available through the Department of Commerce, with most individual awards capped at $300 million. Companies that miss construction deadlines or share technology with foreign adversaries can be required to repay grant funds.

title: Hemp Planting Predictability Act
ai_summary:
This bill would delay when new hemp farming rules take effect. A recently passed government spending law made changes to how hemp can be legally grown in the United States. Those changes were originally set to kick in after 365 days. This bill would push that deadline back to 3 years instead, giving farmers and the industry more time before the new rules apply.

The people most directly affected are hemp farmers, hemp businesses, and the states that oversee hemp growing programs. Hemp is a legal crop used to make products like fiber, food, and CBD. When rules around growing it change, farmers need time to adjust their operations, apply for permits, and plan their planting seasons. A longer runway before the new rules start would give them more time to prepare.

This bill does not change what the new hemp rules actually say. It only changes when those rules begin. Supporters would likely argue farmers need more notice before major changes hit. Those with concerns might say the delay slows down updates that were already agreed upon. The bill was introduced in January 2026 and sent to the House Agriculture Committee for review.
ai_headline: Hemp farmers would get 3 years instead of 1 before new rules apply
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7024 would extend the deadline for new hemp farming rules from 365 days to 3 years, giving farmers more time to adjust.
ai_sections.what: HR 7024 would delay when recently enacted hemp farming rules take effect, extending the deadline from 365 days to 3 years. The bill does not change the content of the new rules, only when they begin. It was introduced in January 2026 and referred to the House Agriculture Committee.
ai_sections.who: Hemp farmers and hemp businesses are most directly affected, along with the states that run hemp growing programs. Hemp is a legal crop used to produce fiber, food, and CBD products.
ai_sections.why: A longer delay gives farmers more time to adjust operations, apply for permits, and plan planting seasons before the new rules take effect. Those who supported the original timeline may see the delay as slowing down updates that were already agreed upon.

title: Aviation Innovation and Global Competitiveness Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets the FAA's process for officially approving new aircraft designs, called "type certification." Right now, companies developing new types of aircraft, including electric air taxis and other advanced flying vehicles, often face long, unpredictable wait times and unclear rules when trying to get their designs approved. The bill would require the FAA to publish a public plan for making that approval process faster and more transparent, set standard expected timelines for key steps, and reduce paperwork by turning repeat approvals into standing policies instead of starting from scratch each time.

The bill also updates how the FAA can hand off certain routine certification tasks to companies and their authorized representatives, rather than having FAA staff do everything directly. It requires the FAA to clearly spell out which tasks can be delegated, which ones need closer government oversight for safety reasons, and how the agency will keep track when it chooses not to delegate. The FAA would need to post this updated guidance publicly within 90 days of the bill becoming law and report to Congress each year on how well it is meeting its own timelines.

The people most directly affected are aviation companies, especially startups working on new technologies like electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft, as well as traditional aircraft manufacturers. Airport operators and workers at the FAA, including safety inspectors and engineers, would also be involved. For everyday Americans, the practical effect would be felt down the road if faster certification leads to new types of air travel becoming available sooner, though the bill itself does not guarantee any specific outcome.
ai_headline: FAA aircraft approval overhaul bill sets 90-day deadline for new public guidance
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7553 requires the FAA to publish a faster, clearer aircraft certification plan and report annually to Congress on its own deadlines.
ai_sections.what: HR 7553 requires the FAA to publish a public plan to speed up and clarify its type certification process, the official approval required for new aircraft designs. The bill sets standard expected timelines for key steps and allows repeat approvals to become standing policies rather than restarting from scratch each time. The FAA must also clearly define which certification tasks can be delegated to companies and their representatives, which require direct government oversight, and how the agency will track cases where it chooses not to delegate.
ai_sections.who: Aviation companies, especially startups developing electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft and other advanced flying vehicles, along with traditional aircraft manufacturers, are most directly affected. FAA employees including safety inspectors and engineers, as well as airport operators, would also be involved in carrying out the changes.
ai_sections.why: Companies developing new aircraft types currently face long, unpredictable wait times and unclear rules in the certification process. If the bill results in faster certification, new types of air travel could become available to the public sooner, though the bill does not guarantee any specific outcome.

title: FERRIES Act
ai_summary:
This bill sets up and funds four federal grant programs to support passenger ferry services across the country from 2027 through 2031. It provides money for building new ferry boats and terminal facilities, improving ferry service in cities, helping rural communities that depend on ferries, and replacing older ferry fleets with newer vessels. In total, the bill authorizes and directly appropriates about 3.5 billion dollars spread across those five years. The programs were originally created under a 2021 infrastructure law, and this bill makes them permanent parts of federal transportation law while also increasing the funding levels.

The people most directly affected are riders who use ferries as part of their daily transportation, especially in places where ferries are one of the few practical ways to get around. Rural communities that rely on ferries to connect to basic services like grocery stores, medical care, and jobs would receive at least 80 percent of the rural ferry funding. Cities with existing ferry systems would also see more money available to maintain and expand those services. Shipyard workers could benefit as well, since one of the grant programs is specifically aimed at modernizing aging ferry fleets, which would require work done at American shipyards.

State and local transportation agencies would apply to the federal Department of Transportation for these grants on a competitive basis. The federal government would cover a share of the costs, and the money cannot be taken away or reduced by other budget rules that normally limit transportation spending. The bill does not create a new federal agency or office; it works through existing programs run by the Federal Highway Administration and the Federal Transit Administration.
ai_headline: Federal bill would fund passenger ferry systems with $3.5 billion through 2031
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7774 permanently funds four federal ferry grant programs at about $3.5 billion from 2027 to 2031.
ai_sections.what: HR 7774 establishes four permanent federal grant programs supporting passenger ferry services from 2027 through 2031. The programs fund new ferry boats and terminals, urban ferry service, rural ferry access, and fleet modernization. The bill makes these programs a permanent part of federal transportation law, expanding on programs first created in a 2021 infrastructure law.
ai_sections.who: Daily ferry riders, rural communities, state and local transportation agencies, and shipyard workers are the populations most directly affected. State and local agencies apply competitively to the Department of Transportation for the grants.
ai_sections.why: Rural communities that depend on ferries for access to groceries, medical care, and jobs would receive at least 80 percent of rural ferry funding. Funding is protected from budget rules that normally reduce transportation spending, which affects how reliably the money reaches recipients.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes and directly appropriates approximately $3.5 billion spread across fiscal years 2027 through 2031. The federal government covers a share of project costs, with state and local agencies contributing the remainder on a competitive grant basis.
costChips: Fed pays a share of each project

title: School Bus Stop-Arm Safety Camera Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal grant program run by the U.S. Department of Transportation to help schools get stop-arm cameras on their school buses. These are the cameras that record drivers who illegally pass a school bus when its stop arm is extended and its red lights are flashing. State education agencies could apply for the money and use it to buy new buses that already have the cameras, add cameras to buses they already own, or pay for installation and upkeep. The bill also requires two federal safety agencies to study whether cameras should be required on all school buses and publish their findings and recommendations within one year, covering topics like privacy, how footage gets shared with police, and how these programs can pay for themselves over time.

The people most directly affected are school-age children who ride buses, since the cameras are meant to deter drivers from illegally passing stopped school buses, which is a leading cause of student pedestrian injuries and deaths. School districts and state education departments would also be affected because they would be the ones applying for and managing the grant funds.

This bill has only been introduced in the House and referred to committee. It has not yet been voted on, so none of these changes are in effect yet.
ai_headline: Federal bill would fund stop-arm cameras on school buses
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: A House bill would create a federal grant program to put stop-arm cameras on school buses; no vote has taken place yet.
ai_sections.what: HR 7204 would establish a federal grant program administered by the U.S. Department of Transportation to help schools purchase and install stop-arm cameras on school buses. State education agencies could apply for funds to buy new camera-equipped buses, add cameras to existing buses, or cover installation and maintenance costs. Two federal safety agencies would also be required to study whether cameras should be mandatory on all school buses and publish findings and recommendations within one year, addressing privacy, footage sharing with police, and long-term program funding.
ai_sections.who: School-age children who ride buses are the most directly affected, as the cameras are intended to deter drivers from illegally passing stopped school buses, a leading cause of student pedestrian injuries and deaths. School districts and state education departments would manage the application and administration of any grant funds received.
ai_sections.why: Illegal passing of stopped school buses is identified as a leading cause of student pedestrian injuries and deaths, and the cameras are designed to deter that behavior. The required federal study would examine privacy implications, how footage is shared with law enforcement, and how these programs can sustain themselves financially over time.
ai_sections.cost: State education agencies would be the ones applying for and managing the federal grant funds, which could be used for new buses, camera installations, or upkeep.
costChips: State agencies apply and manage funds

title: To direct the Secretary of Transportation to convey all right, title, and interest to certain property to the San Mateo County Transit District.
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Department of Transportation to hand over ownership of a specific parking lot to a local transit agency. The property is the Colma Park and Ride Lot, located in Colma, California, near Interstate 280. Right now the federal government holds the title to this land, and the bill would transfer full ownership to the San Mateo County Transit District, which is the public agency that runs buses and other transit services in San Mateo County. The transfer would happen for free, meaning the transit district would not have to pay the federal government anything for the property.

Park and ride lots are facilities where commuters can leave their cars and switch to public transportation. The Colma lot is used by people in the San Mateo County area who want to connect to buses or other transit options. By transferring ownership to the local transit district, the agency would gain full control over how the lot is managed, maintained, and potentially developed in the future.

This bill mainly affects residents of San Mateo County, California, particularly commuters who use the Colma Park and Ride Lot. It also affects the San Mateo County Transit District, which would take on full responsibility for the property. For most Americans outside that area, this bill would have no direct impact.
ai_headline: Federal government would give Colma parking lot to San Mateo transit agency at no charge
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7440 transfers ownership of the Colma Park and Ride Lot in California from the federal government to San Mateo County Transit District for free.
ai_sections.what: HR 7440 directs the U.S. Department of Transportation to transfer ownership of the Colma Park and Ride Lot, located near Interstate 280 in Colma, California, to the San Mateo County Transit District. The federal government currently holds the title to the property. The transfer would occur at no cost to the transit district.
ai_sections.who: The San Mateo County Transit District, the public agency that operates buses and other transit services in San Mateo County, would receive full ownership and responsibility for the lot. Commuters in San Mateo County who use the Colma Park and Ride Lot to connect to public transportation are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Transferring the title would give the San Mateo County Transit District full control over the management, maintenance, and potential future development of the lot. The bill has no direct impact on Americans outside the San Mateo County area.
ai_sections.cost: The transit district would pay nothing to the federal government for the property. The transfer is designated as free of charge.

title: READ AI Models Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells a government agency called the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to create a standard "label" or fact sheet for AI models, similar to a nutrition label on food. The label would include basic information about an AI system, such as who built it, when it was released, what languages it supports, and what data it was trained on. NIST would run this as a pilot program first, gather public feedback over at least 60 days, and then report to Congress within a year on whether it worked and how to make it permanent.

The rules would not be mandatory. Companies and government agencies could voluntarily use the template when they build or release AI systems. The template is also designed to be flexible, so different industries could fill out only the parts that apply to them. NIST would work with businesses, universities, nonprofits, and international standards groups to make sure the template is practical and useful across many different situations.

This bill affects a wide range of people. AI developers, both in the private sector and in government, are the main group being asked to do something, even though participation is voluntary. Everyday people who use AI-powered tools, like chatbots, hiring software, or medical apps, could benefit if companies choose to use the labels, since it would make it easier to understand what an AI system is and where it came from. The bill does not create any penalties or requirements for private citizens.
ai_headline: Bill would require NIST to develop voluntary AI labeling standard
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6461 directs NIST to pilot a voluntary AI fact-sheet label and report results to Congress within one year.
ai_sections.what: HR 6461 directs the National Institute of Standards and Technology to develop a standardized label for AI systems, similar to a nutrition label, displaying basic information such as the builder, release date, supported languages, and training data. NIST would first run a pilot program, collect public feedback over at least 60 days, and then report to Congress within a year on results and a path to making the label permanent. The template is designed to be flexible so different industries can complete only the sections relevant to them.
ai_sections.who: AI developers in both the private sector and government are the primary audience, though use of the label is entirely voluntary. Everyday users of AI-powered tools such as chatbots, hiring software, or medical apps are also affected as potential recipients of the disclosed information.
ai_sections.why: If companies adopt the label, users of AI systems would have a clearer, standardized source of information about what a system is and where it came from. No penalties exist for non-participation, and no requirements are placed on private citizens.

title: Stop Militarizing Law Enforcement Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets a federal program called the 1033 program, which allows the Department of Defense to give surplus military equipment to local, state, and federal law enforcement agencies for free. The bill would ban police departments from receiving many types of military-grade gear through that program, including grenades, grenade launchers, mine-resistant vehicles, weaponized drones, silencers, and long-range sound devices. Some items like certain non-automatic firearms and non-combat vehicles could still be transferred, but only if the police agency proves a specific need, completes required training, and promises not to use the equipment for everyday patrol work.

The bill also adds new rules around transparency and accountability. Before receiving equipment, local police would have to publicly post a notice in their community for at least 30 days and get approval from their city council or local governing body. Agencies would also have to track every piece of controlled equipment they receive, and the Defense Department would have to report to Congress regularly on what was transferred and how it was used. If an agency cannot account for all its equipment or is found to have a pattern of civil liberties violations involving that equipment, it loses access to the program.

The people most directly affected are local and state police departments that currently use the 1033 program to acquire military surplus gear, as well as the communities those departments serve. Federal law enforcement agencies would also face new rules. Taxpayers and community members gain new rights to be notified and to weigh in before their local police department acquires military equipment through this program.
ai_headline: Bill would strip police access to grenades and armored gear from Pentagon surplus program
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7766 would ban police from receiving grenades, mine-resistant vehicles, and weaponized drones through the Pentagon's 1033 surplus program.
ai_sections.what: HR 7766 would prohibit law enforcement agencies from receiving specific military-grade equipment through the Department of Defense 1033 program, including grenades, grenade launchers, mine-resistant vehicles, weaponized drones, silencers, and long-range sound devices. Some items such as certain non-automatic firearms and non-combat vehicles could still be transferred, but only if the agency demonstrates a specific need, completes required training, and commits to not using the equipment for routine patrol. The bill also requires agencies to track all controlled equipment received and mandates regular Defense Department reports to Congress on transfers and usage.
ai_sections.who: Local and state police departments that currently acquire military surplus gear through the 1033 program are directly affected, as are the communities those departments serve. Federal law enforcement agencies, local governing bodies such as city councils, and community members who gain new notification and input rights are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Agencies found unable to account for their equipment or shown to have a pattern of civil liberties violations tied to that equipment would lose access to the program. Communities would be required to receive at least 30 days of public notice and local government approval before any transfer takes place.

title: To designate the portion of Interstate Route 680 in Omaha, Nebraska, as the "Hal Daub Freeway".
ai_summary:
This bill would give an honorary name to a stretch of Interstate 680 in Omaha, Nebraska. Specifically, it covers the section of I-680 starting at mile marker 0 and running to where the highway meets the Missouri River. That portion of the road would officially be called the "Hal Daub Freeway" in all government records, maps, and documents. Hal Daub is a former U.S. congressman and former mayor of Omaha.

This type of bill is called a commemorative designation. It does not change how the road is built, maintained, or funded. It does not redirect any money. The physical highway stays exactly the same — only the official name changes.

The bill mainly affects people in the Omaha area who use that section of I-680, as they may eventually see new signs reflecting the name. It also affects any government agencies that maintain official records or maps of that highway, since they would need to update their documents to reflect the new designation. Bills like this are common in Congress and are typically noncontroversial in nature.
ai_headline: Section of I-680 in Omaha would be named for former mayor Hal Daub
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7090 would rename part of I-680 in Omaha the "Hal Daub Freeway" in official records, with no funding changes.
ai_sections.what: HR 7090 would give the honorary name "Hal Daub Freeway" to the section of Interstate 680 in Omaha, Nebraska, running from mile marker 0 to where the highway meets the Missouri River. The designation would appear in all official government records, maps, and documents. No changes are made to the road's construction, maintenance, or funding.
ai_sections.who: Omaha-area residents who use that section of I-680 may eventually see new signs reflecting the name. Government agencies that maintain official records or maps of the highway would need to update their documents.
ai_sections.why: Hal Daub is a former U.S. congressman and former mayor of Omaha. Commemorative designations of this type are common in Congress and are typically noncontroversial.

title: Providing for congressional disapproval of the proposed foreign military sale to Israel of certain defense articles and services.
ai_summary:
This resolution is Congress's way of trying to block a specific arms sale. Under U.S. law, when the government wants to sell military equipment to another country, it must notify Congress first. Congress then has the option to pass a resolution saying no. That is exactly what this bill does — it would officially prohibit the U.S. from selling a specific set of military bulldozers and related parts and services to Israel. The sale in question was formally notified to Congress on March 3, 2025.

The items being blocked are D9R and D9T Caterpillar bulldozers, along with spare parts, maintenance support, technical documents, and other related services. These are heavy-duty armored bulldozers commonly used in military construction and demolition operations. The bill does not address any other weapons sales or U.S. policy toward Israel more broadly — it is limited strictly to this one transaction.

This resolution was introduced by a group of House members and sent to the Foreign Affairs Committee. If it passed both the House and Senate and became law, it would cancel this particular sale. It directly affects the U.S. defense industry companies involved in the deal, the U.S. government agencies that process arms sales, and Israel as the intended buyer. American taxpayers are not directly paying for the sale, since foreign military sales are purchases made by the foreign government, but U.S. export policy and diplomatic relationships are involved.
ai_headline: House resolution seeks to block military bulldozer sale to Israel
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HJRES 84 would cancel a U.S. sale of armored Caterpillar bulldozers to Israel notified to Congress on March 3, 2025.
ai_sections.what: HJRES 84 is a congressional resolution that would prohibit the United States from completing a specific arms sale to Israel. The items covered are D9R and D9T Caterpillar armored bulldozers, along with spare parts, maintenance support, technical documents, and related services. The sale was formally notified to Congress on March 3, 2025, which triggered Congress's legal window to block it.
ai_sections.who: The resolution was introduced by a group of House members and referred to the Foreign Affairs Committee. It directly affects U.S. defense industry companies involved in the deal, U.S. government agencies that process arms sales, and Israel as the intended buyer.
ai_sections.why: If passed by both the House and Senate and signed into law, the resolution would cancel this specific transaction. It does not alter any other U.S. weapons sales or broader U.S. policy toward Israel.
ai_sections.cost: Israel, as the foreign buyer, would be paying for the sale rather than American taxpayers, as foreign military sales are purchases made by the foreign government. U.S. export policy and diplomatic relationships are nonetheless involved in the transaction.
costChips: Foreign government pays, not U.S. taxpayers
Sale is a foreign military purchase

title: Golden Mussel Eradication and Control Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a government program to fight the golden mussel, an invasive species that has been spreading in California waterways. The program would focus on the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta and any other U.S. waters where golden mussels are found or might show up. A federal task force would work with states, local governments, universities, ports, and nonprofits to research the mussel's biology, track where it spreads, and develop ways to control or eliminate it. Within one year of the bill passing, the task force must also publish guidelines for stopping the mussel's spread, including setting up boat inspection stations.

The bill also sets up a competitive grant program, meaning organizations can apply for funding to test and develop technologies that remove golden mussels from water pipes, boat hulls, fish screens, canals, and other places the mussel can attach and grow. If useful new technology comes out of this research, the task force can help get it into wider use by partnering with states, ports, or private companies.

The people most directly affected are those who live or work near California's waterways, including farmers and water utilities that depend on water infrastructure, commercial and recreational boaters, fishermen, and local governments. The bill would authorize up to 15 million dollars per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund these efforts, though Congress would still need to approve that spending each year.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets golden mussel invasion in California waterways
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3717 creates a federal task force and grant program to fight golden mussels, authorized at up to $15M/year from 2026 to 2030.
ai_sections.what: HR 3717 establishes a federal program to combat the golden mussel, an invasive species spreading in California waterways, with primary focus on the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta and any other U.S. waters where the mussel is found or may appear. A federal task force would research the mussel's biology, track its spread, and develop control or elimination methods. Within one year of passage, the task force must publish guidelines for stopping the mussel's spread, including establishing boat inspection stations.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects people who live or work near California's waterways, including farmers, water utilities, commercial and recreational boaters, fishermen, and local governments. Organizations such as states, universities, ports, and nonprofits could apply for competitive grants to develop removal technologies.
ai_sections.why: The golden mussel can attach and grow on water pipes, boat hulls, fish screens, and canals, threatening water infrastructure and aquatic environments. Without coordinated research and control measures, the mussel's spread across U.S. waterways could continue unchecked.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $15 million per year from 2026 through 2030, though Congress must still approve the spending each year.

title: Nonprofit Security Grant Program Transparency Act
ai_summary:
The Nonprofit Security Grant Program gives federal money to nonprofit organizations to help them improve their physical security, like installing cameras, fences, or alarm systems. This bill does not change who gets the money or how much is available. Instead, it changes the reporting rules around the program to make it more transparent. Before grants are awarded, the Department of Homeland Security would have to give Congress a report listing each nonprofit set to receive a grant, how much they will get, and where they are located. After the fiscal year ends, a second report would detail how that money was actually spent.

The bill affects two main groups. First, it gives Congress better oversight by requiring these reports be sent to the relevant House and Senate committees. Second, it affects nonprofits that apply for or receive these security grants, since their names, addresses, and grant amounts would be included in the reports. The program is used by a wide range of nonprofits, including religious institutions, community centers, and other organizations that may be at higher risk of hate crimes or terrorist attacks.

This is essentially a government accountability measure. It does not create a new program or add new funding. It just requires the agency running the program to document and share more information about how applications are processed and how grant money is used. Residents who belong to or rely on nonprofits that receive this type of security funding could be indirectly affected.
ai_headline: HR 7382 adds pre- and post-award reporting rules to nonprofit security grants
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7382 requires DHS to report nonprofit grant recipients, amounts, and locations to Congress before and after awards are made.
ai_sections.what: HR 7382 changes the reporting requirements for the Nonprofit Security Grant Program, which provides federal money to nonprofits for physical security improvements such as cameras, fences, and alarm systems. Before grants are awarded, the Department of Homeland Security must submit a report to Congress listing each intended recipient, the grant amount, and the recipient's location. After the fiscal year ends, a second report must detail how that money was actually spent.
ai_sections.who: Congressional oversight committees in the House and Senate would receive the required reports. Nonprofits that apply for or receive grants would have their names, addresses, and grant amounts included in those reports.
ai_sections.why: The change would make the grant program more transparent by requiring DHS to formally document and share information about how applications are processed and how awarded funds are used. Residents who belong to or rely on nonprofits receiving this funding could be indirectly affected by the increased public accountability.

title: Toll Evasion Prevention and Plate Visibility Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes it illegal to sell products designed to hide or obscure license plates, like tinted plate covers or spray coatings that block cameras. It also bans selling fake, counterfeit, or altered license plates, as well as reselling real state-issued plates without permission. Sellers who break these rules can be penalized by the Federal Trade Commission, the agency that enforces consumer protection laws. The rules kick in 90 days after the bill becomes law.

The bill also directs the Federal Highway Administration to publish guidance within 90 days on what makes license plates easier for cameras and toll readers to scan. It would offer advice on things like plate frames that block part of the plate. This guidance is advisory only, meaning states do not have to change their current plate designs or rules.

Finally, the bill creates a grant program, backed by up to $10 million per year from 2027 through 2030, to help states, local governments, law enforcement agencies, and toll road operators build better technology systems to catch repeat toll evaders and share information across agencies. The bill mainly affects online and physical retailers selling plate-obscuring products, people who evade tolls, and state and local agencies that manage roads and tolls.
ai_headline: Bill bans license plate covers and fake plates, funds toll evasion crackdown
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7797 bans plate-obscuring products and fake plates, and offers up to $10M/year in grants to catch toll evaders.
ai_sections.what: HR 7797 makes it illegal to sell products that hide license plates, such as tinted covers or spray coatings that block cameras. It also bans selling fake, counterfeit, or altered plates, and reselling real state-issued plates without permission. Rules take effect 90 days after enactment, and the Federal Highway Administration must publish advisory guidance on plate readability within the same window.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects online and physical retailers selling plate-obscuring products, people who evade tolls, and state and local agencies that manage roads and tolls. The Federal Trade Commission enforces the sales prohibitions, while the Federal Highway Administration issues the readability guidance.
ai_sections.why: States and local agencies are not required to change existing plate designs or rules, as the guidance is advisory only. The grant program is intended to help agencies build technology systems and share information to identify repeat toll evaders across jurisdictions.
ai_sections.cost: A grant program funded at up to $10 million per year from 2027 through 2030 supports states, local governments, law enforcement agencies, and toll road operators in building toll evasion detection technology.
costChips: Up to $10M per year in grants

title: Trucking Security and CCP Disclosure Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill requires trucking companies that want to haul cargo for the U.S. military to go through a new security screening process. Specifically, any motor carrier, subcontractor, or independent truck owner-operator doing business with the Department of Defense must sign a legal certification saying they are not owned by, controlled by, or doing significant business with companies on the U.S. government's list of Chinese military-linked firms. If a company lies on that certification, it can be banned from future government contracts and face federal criminal penalties. The Department of Defense has 180 days after the law passes to write the rules for how this works in practice.

The bill also creates a new official list called the Secure Defense Freight Carrier Registry, managed by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration working alongside the Pentagon. To get on this registry, a trucking company must pass a national security background check, meet existing federal safety standards, and have its drivers and staff vetted for security clearance. Once the registry is up and running, no trucking company can bid on or carry out a military freight contract unless it is on the list. Companies must be re-screened at least every two years to stay on it. The Defense Secretary can make exceptions in emergencies.

This bill primarily affects trucking companies, subcontractors, and independent owner-operators who haul freight for the U.S. military. It also affects the Pentagon and the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration, which would both take on new administrative responsibilities.
