American consumers and the broader trucking industry could be indirectly affected if the new requirements change which companies are able to compete for military shipping contracts.
ai_headline: Military trucking firms would face security screening and a new federal registry
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7924 bars trucking companies from military freight contracts unless they pass security vetting and join a new federal registry.
ai_sections.what: HR 7924 requires motor carriers, subcontractors, and independent owner-operators hauling freight for the Department of Defense to pass national security background checks and join a new Secure Defense Freight Carrier Registry managed by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration and the Pentagon. Companies must also certify they have no ownership, control, or significant business ties to firms on the U.S. government's list of Chinese military-linked companies. No trucking company may bid on or perform a military freight contract unless it appears on the registry, and companies must be re-screened at least every two years to remain eligible.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects trucking companies, subcontractors, and independent owner-operators that haul military freight, as well as the Pentagon and the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration, which both take on new administrative duties. American consumers and the broader trucking industry could be indirectly affected if the requirements change which companies can compete for military shipping contracts.
ai_sections.why: Companies that submit false certifications can be banned from future government contracts and face federal criminal penalties. The Defense Secretary retains authority to grant exceptions in emergencies, and the Pentagon has 180 days after enactment to finalize the implementing rules.
ai_sections.cost: Companies that submit false certifications face the possibility of being banned from future government contracts and federal criminal penalties.

title: Stop Gang Violence Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how the federal government hands out a type of law enforcement grant called a Byrne-JAG grant. These grants give money to local governments, like cities and counties, to help pay for law enforcement and crime prevention programs. Under this bill, local governments that report gang-related crime information to the FBI would be moved to the front of the line when it comes to receiving that funding. In other words, sharing gang data with federal agencies becomes a factor that can improve a locality's chances of getting grant money.

The bill itself does not require any local government to report gang affiliation data. It simply rewards those that already do, or choose to start doing so, by giving them priority consideration when grants are being awarded. The information would go to the FBI and its National Gang Intelligence Center, which collects and analyzes gang-related data from across the country.

This would mainly affect local governments, police departments, and sheriff's offices that apply for Byrne-JAG funding. It could also indirectly affect residents in those communities, since more grant money can mean more resources for local law enforcement programs. People in areas where local agencies decide to start tracking and reporting gang data in order to compete for funding could see changes in how crimes are categorized and reported in their communities.
ai_headline: Bill would push gang-data sharing localities ahead in federal grant line
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8856 gives local governments priority for Byrne-JAG grants if they report gang data to the FBI.
ai_sections.what: HR 8856 changes how Byrne-JAG grants are awarded by giving priority consideration to local governments that report gang-related crime information to the FBI and its National Gang Intelligence Center. The bill does not require any local government to report gang affiliation data. It only rewards those that already do, or choose to start doing so, with improved chances of receiving grant funding.
ai_sections.who: The bill mainly affects local governments, police departments, and sheriff's offices that apply for Byrne-JAG funding. Residents in those communities may also be indirectly affected, since grant funding supports local law enforcement programs and influences how crimes are categorized and reported.
ai_sections.why: Local agencies that choose to begin tracking and reporting gang data in order to compete for funding may change how they categorize and record crimes in their communities. Areas that do not report gang data are not penalized but may be passed over in favor of localities that do.
ai_sections.cost: Byrne-JAG grants provide federal money to local governments for law enforcement and crime prevention programs, and this bill alters the priority order in which that existing funding is distributed.
costChips: Byrne-JAG grants fund local law enforcement
Priority order for existing funds shifts
No new spending created by the bill

title: Equitable Access to School Facilities Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change how the federal government helps charter schools get and pay for their buildings. Right now, charter schools often struggle to find and afford facilities compared to traditional public schools. The bill would allow the U.S. Department of Education to award competitive grants to state-level organizations so they can create or improve programs that help charter schools access buildings, pay rent or renovation costs, and explore different ownership arrangements. States that already have stronger pro-charter facility policies would get priority for these grants. The federal government would cover up to 60 percent of the costs, with states or partner organizations covering the rest.

The bill also makes a few other changes. It allows states to set up small loan funds using grant money, so charter schools can borrow money to cover early operating costs or building improvements. It requires organizations that previously received a related type of federal grant to file annual reports for ten years afterward. It also removes certain federal property-tracking requirements that currently apply when federal money is used to buy or improve school facilities, meaning charter schools would face fewer strings attached to federally funded buildings.

The people most directly affected are charter school operators and students, particularly those in low-income or rural areas where finding affordable school space is hardest. State education agencies and nonprofit organizations that manage charter school programs would also be involved, since they would apply for and run the grants. Traditional public schools are not directly targeted by the bill, though some provisions encourage charter schools and local school districts to share public buildings.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund state programs helping charter schools secure buildings
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 586.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7086 lets the Education Dept. fund state programs that help charter schools find, rent, or renovate facilities, covering up to 60% of costs.
ai_sections.what: HR 7086 would authorize the U.S. Department of Education to award competitive grants to state-level organizations that create or improve programs helping charter schools access buildings, pay rent or renovation costs, and explore ownership arrangements. The bill also allows states to set up small loan funds from grant money so charter schools can borrow for early operating costs or building improvements. Additionally, it removes certain federal property-tracking requirements that currently apply when federal money is used to buy or improve school facilities.
ai_sections.who: Charter school operators and students, especially those in low-income or rural areas, are the most directly affected. State education agencies and nonprofit organizations would apply for and administer the grants, and some provisions encourage charter schools and local school districts to share public buildings.
ai_sections.why: Organizations that previously received a related type of federal grant would be required to file annual reports for ten years afterward, adding a long-term reporting obligation. Removing federal property-tracking requirements would reduce the conditions attached to federally funded buildings for charter schools.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would cover up to 60 percent of program costs, with states or partner organizations responsible for the remaining share. States with stronger existing pro-charter facility policies would receive priority for the competitive grants.
costChips: Federal covers up to 60% of costs
States or partners fund the remainder

title: Pursuing Equity in Mental Health Act
ai_summary:
This bill focuses on improving mental health care for young people, especially youth of color. It would increase funding for existing grant programs that connect primary care with mental health services, and it directs those grants to give extra attention to health clinics and providers who serve large numbers of racial and ethnic minority communities. It also authorizes hundreds of millions of dollars in new funding for the National Institutes of Health and the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities to study why mental health gaps exist between different racial groups and to find better ways to close those gaps.

The bill also calls for a national study to look at what research is missing when it comes to mental health in minority communities, including how things like community violence and difficult childhood experiences affect mental health outcomes. Separately, it would update training programs for mental health professionals, including social workers, counselors, and psychiatrists, to better prepare them to serve patients from diverse racial and ethnic backgrounds.

Finally, the bill directs the federal government to create a public outreach and education campaign aimed at reducing the stigma around mental health and substance use in minority communities. That campaign would need to be available in multiple languages and tailored to different cultures and age groups. The people most directly affected by this bill would be youth and young adults of color, the mental health providers who serve them, researchers studying health disparities, and the federal agencies responsible for public health programs.
ai_headline: Bill targets mental health gaps for youth of color with new funding and training
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2904 expands mental health funding, research, and provider training to reduce racial disparities among youth.
ai_sections.what: HR 2904 would increase funding for grant programs linking primary care with mental health services, directing grants toward clinics serving large minority populations. It authorizes new funding for the National Institutes of Health and the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities to study racial mental health gaps. The bill also updates training for mental health professionals and creates a multilingual, culturally tailored public outreach campaign to reduce stigma around mental health and substance use in minority communities.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects youth and young adults of color, along with the mental health providers who serve them. Researchers studying health disparities and federal agencies responsible for public health programs are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Expanding grant eligibility toward minority-serving clinics would shift how federal mental health dollars are distributed across health care settings. Mandating updated professional training and a national stigma-reduction campaign would place new requirements on federal agencies and health workforce programs.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes hundreds of millions of dollars in new funding for the National Institutes of Health and the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities. It also increases funding for existing grant programs connecting primary care with mental health services.
costChips: Hundreds of millions authorized for NIH research
New funds for primary care-mental health grants

title: NASA C-UAS Act
ai_summary:
This bill gives NASA the legal authority to detect, identify, monitor, and track drones that pose a credible threat to its facilities, such as launch sites and space support infrastructure. Right now, laws covering aircraft, electronic communications, and wiretapping make it legally unclear whether NASA can intercept drone signals or track unauthorized drones without special permission. This bill would carve out an exception to those laws specifically for NASA, allowing it and its trained security personnel to monitor drone communications and movements without needing the drone operator's consent. The authority would expire on September 30, 2031, unless extended by Congress.

NASA would first have to build an inventory of its high-risk facilities and run a formal risk assessment before any site qualifies for these drone-monitoring powers. The bill also sets strict privacy rules: any communications intercepted from a drone can only be kept for up to 180 days, must be used only as much as necessary, and can only be shared with law enforcement or other agencies under specific conditions. NASA must also brief Congress every six months on how the authority is being used, including any incidents where people or property were affected.

This bill most directly affects NASA employees and security contractors who work at launch sites and other key facilities. Drone hobbyists, commercial drone operators, and photographers who fly near NASA property could be monitored under this authority if they are considered a potential threat. It also affects federal agencies like the FAA, the Department of Homeland Security, and the Department of Defense, which NASA would coordinate with when using these powers.
ai_headline: NASA gains authority to monitor drones near launch sites through 2031
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7379 lets NASA track and intercept drone signals at high-risk facilities without operator consent, with authority expiring Sept. 30, 2031.
ai_sections.what: HR 7379 grants NASA legal authority to detect, identify, monitor, and track drones that pose a credible threat to its facilities, including launch sites and space support infrastructure. The bill creates explicit exceptions to laws governing aircraft, electronic communications, and wiretapping so NASA can act without drone operator consent. The authority sunsets on September 30, 2031, unless Congress extends it.
ai_sections.who: NASA employees and security contractors at launch sites and key facilities are directly affected, as are drone hobbyists, commercial operators, and photographers flying near NASA property who are deemed a potential threat. Federal agencies including the FAA, the Department of Homeland Security, and the Department of Defense would coordinate with NASA under this authority.
ai_sections.why: Intercepted drone communications may be retained for up to 180 days and shared with law enforcement or other agencies under specific conditions, expanding the scope of surveillance activity at federal facilities. NASA must brief Congress every six months on how the authority is used, including any incidents involving people or property.

title: ADINA Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require prescription and over-the-counter drug labels to clearly list any ingredients that come from major food allergens or gluten-containing grains like wheat, barley, and rye. Right now, food products must disclose allergens like milk, eggs, peanuts, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, soy, wheat, and sesame, but that same rule does not apply to medicines. Some drug ingredients, such as fillers, coatings, or binders, can be made from these substances without it being obvious on the label. This bill closes that gap by treating drug labels more like food labels when it comes to allergen information.

If passed, drug manufacturers would need to update their product labels to specifically name any allergen-derived ingredients and identify which gluten-containing grain is involved. A drug would be considered mislabeled under federal law if it contains those ingredients but does not disclose them. Manufacturers would have up to two years after the law is enacted to comply, or sooner if the Department of Health and Human Services sets an earlier deadline.

This bill mainly affects people with food allergies, celiac disease, or gluten sensitivities who take medications regularly. It also affects pharmaceutical companies, which would need to review and update their labeling. Patients who currently have to do their own research or contact manufacturers to find out what is in their medicine would instead get that information directly on the bottle or package.
ai_headline: Drug labels would have to name allergen-derived ingredients under HR 3821
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3821 would require drug labels to disclose allergen-derived ingredients, giving manufacturers up to two years to comply.
ai_sections.what: HR 3821 would require prescription and over-the-counter drug labels to clearly identify any ingredients derived from major food allergens or gluten-containing grains, including wheat, barley, and rye. A drug would be considered mislabeled under federal law if it contains those ingredients without disclosing them. Manufacturers would have up to two years after enactment to update their labels, or sooner if the Department of Health and Human Services sets an earlier deadline.
ai_sections.who: People with food allergies, celiac disease, or gluten sensitivities who take medications regularly are the primary population affected. Pharmaceutical companies would also be affected, as they would need to review and update product labeling across their drug lines.
ai_sections.why: Current federal allergen disclosure rules apply to food products but not to medicines, meaning drug fillers, coatings, and binders derived from allergens can appear on labels without clear identification. Patients who need this information currently must conduct their own research or contact manufacturers directly rather than finding it on the packaging.

title: Fireworks Trafficking and Money Laundering Prevention Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how federal law treats the illegal movement of fireworks across state lines. Right now, smuggling fireworks between states is a federal crime, but it carries a maximum penalty of only one year in prison. This bill adds illegal fireworks trafficking to a list of crimes that count as "specified unlawful activity" under federal money laundering laws. That means if someone moves illegal fireworks across state lines and profits from it, prosecutors can also charge them with money laundering, which carries a penalty of up to 20 years in prison.

The practical effect is that law enforcement gets stronger legal tools to go after people running illegal fireworks operations. Instead of being limited to a minor charge with a short sentence, investigators can now follow the money, freeze assets, and pursue much more serious charges when they can show that profits from illegal fireworks were handled like laundered money. The bill does not change what kinds of fireworks are legal or illegal, and it does not affect everyday consumers who legally buy fireworks.

This bill would most directly affect people involved in organized illegal fireworks distribution, particularly those who transport large quantities across state lines for profit. It could also affect law enforcement agencies, federal prosecutors, and communities dealing with public safety risks from illegal fireworks. Ordinary residents who legally purchase fireworks in states where they are permitted would not be impacted.
ai_headline: Illegal fireworks trafficking added to federal money laundering law
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9013 lets prosecutors charge illegal interstate fireworks traffickers with money laundering, raising the max penalty from 1 to 20 years.
ai_sections.what: HR 9013 adds illegal interstate fireworks trafficking to the list of crimes that qualify as "specified unlawful activity" under federal money laundering laws. This allows prosecutors to pursue money laundering charges against people who profit from moving illegal fireworks across state lines. The bill does not change which fireworks are legal or illegal and does not affect consumers who legally buy fireworks.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects people involved in organized illegal fireworks distribution, particularly those transporting large quantities across state lines for profit. It also affects federal prosecutors and law enforcement agencies, who would gain tools to freeze assets and pursue more serious charges.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, illegal interstate fireworks smuggling carries a maximum penalty of only one year in prison, limiting prosecutors to a relatively minor charge. Adding it to money laundering statutes raises the potential sentence to up to 20 years and allows investigators to follow the money in illegal fireworks operations.

title: Connecting Students with Mental Health Services Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal grant program run by the Department of Education, working alongside the Department of Health and Human Services. School districts could apply for funding by partnering with a local health care provider or educational service agency. The money could be used to set up or improve telehealth programs, which means students could meet with mental health counselors or behavioral health professionals through video calls or other technology, without having to travel to a separate clinic or office. The bill authorizes up to $5 million per year from 2026 through 2029.

Not every school district would be equally likely to receive funding. The bill tells the government to give priority to schools in high-poverty areas, rural communities, and places that already have a shortage of health care professionals. These are areas where students often have the hardest time getting mental health support. Any student data collected through these programs must follow existing privacy laws, and the grant money cannot be used to replace funding that was already in place.

The people most directly affected are K-12 students, especially those in underserved communities who currently have little or no access to mental health services. School districts, their partner health providers, and school staff who might be hired or trained to run these programs would also be affected. By 2027, the two federal agencies would need to report to Congress on how many students used these services and make recommendations about whether the program should be continued or expanded.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund school telehealth mental health services
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4186 would fund school telehealth mental health programs at up to $5M/year from 2026–2029, prioritizing high-poverty and rural districts.
ai_sections.what: HR 4186 would create a federal grant program administered by the Department of Education with the Department of Health and Human Services. School districts that partner with a local health care provider or educational service agency could apply for funds to set up or improve telehealth programs, allowing students to meet with mental health or behavioral health professionals by video call or similar technology. Priority would go to districts in high-poverty areas, rural communities, and places with existing shortages of health care professionals.
ai_sections.who: K-12 students in underserved communities with limited or no access to mental health services are the primary population affected. School districts, their partner health providers, and school staff hired or trained to run these programs would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Students in high-poverty, rural, and health-professional-shortage areas currently face the greatest barriers to mental health support, and this program targets those gaps. By 2027, the two federal agencies would be required to report to Congress on program usage and make recommendations on whether to continue or expand it.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $5 million per year for fiscal years 2026 through 2029. Grant funds cannot be used to replace funding that was already in place.

title: Army Corps Congressional Engagement Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to hold yearly briefings with the congressional offices that represent areas where the Corps is working on water-related projects. These briefings must cover things like how far along each project is, whether there are any delays, how much money has been spent, any environmental effects, and whether there might be future projects in the same area. Members of Congress and their staff would also get a chance to ask questions during these meetings.

If a district commander from the Army Corps does not hold the required briefing by December 1 of any given year, they must send a written explanation to the relevant congressional offices by December 31. If they skip both the briefing and the written explanation, the Army Inspector General steps in to investigate and report back to Congress on what went wrong and how to fix it.

This bill mainly affects Army Corps of Engineers district commanders, who would have new reporting duties, and the congressional offices that represent areas with Corps water projects, who would gain a regular information-sharing channel. Indirectly, it could benefit everyday residents living near rivers, flood control systems, harbors, and other waterway projects by making sure their elected representatives stay informed about what is happening in their communities.
ai_headline: Army Corps water project briefings for lawmakers would become mandatory yearly
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7519 requires Army Corps of Engineers to brief congressional offices annually on water projects in their districts or face investigation.
ai_sections.what: HR 7519 requires the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to hold yearly briefings with congressional offices representing areas where the Corps has active water projects. Briefings must cover project progress, delays, spending, environmental effects, and potential future projects, with time for questions. If a district commander misses the December 1 deadline, a written explanation must be sent by December 31, or the Army Inspector General is required to investigate and report to Congress.
ai_sections.who: Army Corps of Engineers district commanders face new mandatory reporting duties under this bill. Congressional offices representing districts with Corps water projects would receive a formal, recurring information channel.
ai_sections.why: The requirement creates an accountability mechanism for Army Corps commanders, with escalating consequences up to Inspector General review for noncompliance. Residents living near Corps-managed waterways, flood systems, and harbors may be indirectly affected by how well their representatives are kept informed.

title: End DWI Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a national standard requiring states to make people convicted of drunk driving use ignition interlock devices on their vehicles. An ignition interlock is a small breathalyzer connected to a car's ignition. The driver must blow into it and pass a breath test before the car will start. Under this bill, states would need to require DWI offenders to use these devices for at least 180 days, and the driver would also need to go a certain period without any violations before the device can be removed. States would still get to decide some details, like whether the requirement starts before or after a court conviction, and whether any exceptions apply.

The bill uses federal highway funding as the enforcement tool. States that do not pass and enforce a qualifying ignition interlock law would lose a portion of their federal road funding. Starting in 2027, non-complying states would lose 3 percent of certain federal highway funds. That penalty rises to 5 percent in 2028 and beyond. States that eventually come into compliance can get back the money that was withheld, as long as they act within a set window of time. States that never comply permanently lose those funds.

This bill most directly affects people who are convicted of drunk driving, since they would be required to use interlock devices in states that currently do not mandate them. It also affects all 50 states and their transportation budgets, since federal highway dollars are tied to compliance. Indirectly, it could affect anyone who drives or rides on roads, since the bill aims to reduce drunk driving crashes and fatalities nationwide.
ai_headline: Federal bill would force all states to require breath locks for drunk drivers
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2788 sets a national ignition interlock mandate for DWI offenders and docks federal highway funds from states that don't comply.
ai_sections.what: HR 2788 would create a national standard requiring states to make people convicted of drunk driving install ignition interlock devices on their vehicles. An ignition interlock is a small breathalyzer connected to a car's ignition that must be passed before the car will start. States would be required to mandate the device for at least 180 days, and drivers would need to complete a violation-free period before the device can be removed.
ai_sections.who: People convicted of drunk driving would be directly affected, as they would be required to use interlock devices in states that do not currently mandate them. All 50 states are also affected, because their federal highway funding is tied to whether they pass and enforce a qualifying law.
ai_sections.why: States that do not comply face losing a portion of federal road funding, starting at 3 percent in 2027 and rising to 5 percent in 2028 and beyond. The bill aims to reduce drunk driving crashes and fatalities, which means the outcome could affect any driver or passenger on public roads nationwide.
ai_sections.cost: Non-complying states lose 3 percent of certain federal highway funds starting in 2027, rising to 5 percent in 2028 and beyond. States that come into compliance within a set window can recover withheld funds; states that never comply lose those funds permanently.
costChips: Compliant states can recover withheld funds

title: The Dalilah Law
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal for states to give commercial driver's licenses (CDLs) to people who are not U.S. citizens, green card holders (lawful permanent residents), or holders of specific work visas. CDLs are the licenses required to drive large trucks, buses, and other big commercial vehicles. Anyone who drives a commercial vehicle without meeting these immigration status requirements would be permanently banned from ever getting a CDL in the future. The bill also requires that all CDL knowledge and skills tests be given only in English, and that drivers must demonstrate English proficiency to get or keep a license.

People who already have CDLs would have 180 days after the law passes to go through a recertification process. During that process, states would verify each driver's immigration status and English ability. If someone fails to recertify in time, or does not meet the new requirements, their CDL would be revoked. States that do not follow through on these recertifications and revocations would lose federal transportation funding, which is money the federal government sends to states for roads, highways, and related projects.

This bill would most directly affect immigrants who currently hold or are working toward a CDL but are not citizens, green card holders, or holders of the specific work visas listed. It would also affect trucking and transportation companies that employ those drivers, and potentially the broader supply chain and freight industry that depends on commercial drivers. States would face significant administrative work to recertify all existing CDL holders within the six-month window.
ai_headline: Bill would bar non-citizens from holding commercial driver's licenses
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7758 would restrict CDLs to citizens, green card holders, and select visa holders, and require English-only testing.
ai_sections.what: HR 7758 would make it illegal for states to issue commercial driver's licenses to people who are not U.S. citizens, lawful permanent residents, or holders of specific work visas. All CDL knowledge and skills tests would be required in English only, and drivers would need to demonstrate English proficiency to obtain or keep a license. Anyone driving a commercial vehicle without meeting these immigration status requirements would be permanently banned from ever receiving a CDL.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects immigrants who currently hold or are working toward a CDL but do not meet the new immigration status requirements, as well as trucking and transportation companies that employ those drivers. States and their licensing agencies would also be significantly affected, facing the administrative burden of recertifying all existing CDL holders within a 180-day window.
ai_sections.why: Drivers who do not recertify within 180 days or fail to meet the new requirements would have their CDLs revoked, which could reduce the available pool of licensed commercial drivers. The trucking, transportation, and freight industries, which depend on commercial drivers, could be affected by the resulting change in the driver workforce.
ai_sections.cost: States that fail to carry out the required recertifications and revocations would lose federal transportation funding, which is money the federal government sends to states for roads, highways, and related projects.
costChips: States risk losing federal road funding
No dollar amount specified in bill

title: VA Work-Study Improvement Act
ai_summary:
The VA Work-Study program lets veterans who are using their education benefits work part-time for the VA or related organizations in exchange for a hourly payment. This bill makes several updates to that program. First, it expands the types of jobs veterans can do by allowing them to work for state or local government agencies and nonprofit organizations, as long as the work benefits veterans or military members in some way. Before this change, eligible jobs were more limited. Second, it raises the pay floor, requiring that veterans in the program be paid at least the highest of three wage rates: the federal pay scale minimum, the state minimum wage, or the local minimum wage, whichever is greatest.

The bill also makes the program more modern and easier to use. It requires the VA to set up an electronic timesheet system so veterans can log their hours online and supervisors can approve them digitally, replacing what is currently a paper-based process. Additionally, the VA would be required to publish yearly data about the program, including how many veterans participated, their demographics, what kinds of work they did, how much they were paid, and which schools were involved.

This bill mainly affects veterans and active-duty service members who are enrolled in school and using VA education benefits, as well as the colleges and universities they attend. Nonprofit organizations and state and local governments could also be affected since they would become eligible to host work-study participants. The changes would take effect starting January 1, 2028.
ai_headline: VA work-study program opens to nonprofits and local governments under HR 6011
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Opportunity.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6011 expands VA work-study job sites, raises pay floors, and requires digital timesheets, taking effect January 1, 2028.
ai_sections.what: HR 6011 updates the VA Work-Study program by allowing veterans to work for state or local government agencies and nonprofit organizations, provided the work benefits veterans or military members. The bill raises the pay floor to the highest of the federal minimum, state minimum wage, or local minimum wage. It also requires the VA to replace paper timesheets with an electronic system and to publish yearly program data including participation, demographics, job types, pay, and schools involved.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects veterans and active-duty service members enrolled in school and using VA education benefits, along with their colleges and universities. Nonprofit organizations and state and local governments would also be affected as newly eligible host sites for work-study participants.
ai_sections.why: Expanding eligible job sites and raising the pay floor changes both the pool of veterans who may find suitable placements and the compensation those veterans receive. Annual public reporting would create a documented record of how the program operates and who it reaches.

title: Campaign Finance Transparency Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes new rules for how political campaigns can accept donations online. If you give money to a federal campaign using a credit or debit card on a website or app, the campaign would be required to collect your card's security code (the CVV number on the back) and the ZIP code tied to your billing address. The name on the card must match the name of the person donating. Gift cards and store cards would be completely banned as a way to donate to campaigns. People who live outside the U.S. would need to provide a passport or similar ID to prove they are American citizens or legal permanent residents before their donation can be accepted.

The bill also removes the current $200 threshold for reporting donations. Right now, campaigns only have to report the names and details of donors who give more than $200 total in a year. This bill would require campaigns to report every donor, no matter how small the contribution. It also makes it illegal not just to donate in someone else's name, but to help or assist anyone else in doing so. If a campaign treasurer suspects a donation was made in someone else's name, they would be required to report it to the Federal Election Commission.

This bill affects anyone who donates money to a federal political campaign, as well as the campaigns and political committees that receive those donations. Campaigns would need to update how they collect and verify payment information online. Small donors who currently give under $200 and are not reported publicly would now have their information recorded. The Federal Election Commission would be responsible for writing the specific rules to carry all of this out within 90 days of the bill becoming law.
ai_headline: Online campaign donation rules tightened under new federal bill
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 11 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8720 requires CVV codes, ZIP matching, and full donor reporting for all online federal campaign donations.
ai_sections.what: HR 8720 sets new verification requirements for online donations to federal campaigns, including mandatory collection of the card's CVV code and billing ZIP code, and a name-match requirement between the card and the donor. Gift cards and store cards would be banned entirely as donation methods. The bill also eliminates the current $200 reporting threshold, requiring campaigns to report every donor regardless of contribution size.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects anyone who donates to a federal political campaign online, as well as the campaigns and political committees that receive those donations. People living outside the U.S. would need to provide a passport or equivalent ID to confirm citizenship or legal permanent resident status before a donation can be accepted.
ai_sections.why: Campaigns would need to update their online payment systems to collect and verify new information, and small donors who currently give under $200 without public disclosure would now have their information recorded. Campaign treasurers who suspect a donation was made in someone else's name would be required to report it to the Federal Election Commission, which must issue implementing rules within 90 days of enactment.

title: Strong Communities Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new grant program within the existing COPS (Community Oriented Policing Services) program, which already provides federal money to help local police departments. Under this new program, local law enforcement agencies could apply for grants to pay for police officers or recruits to attend training schools or academies. The catch is that anyone who receives this training money must agree to work as a full-time officer for at least 4 years within the next 8 years, and they must work close to where they already live, specifically within 7 miles of a home where they have lived for at least 5 years. In rural counties with fewer than 150,000 people, that distance expands to 20 miles. If a person takes the money but does not fulfill the service requirement, they have to pay it back, though the Attorney General can create rules allowing exceptions in special circumstances.

The bill mainly affects local police departments, sheriff's offices, tribal law enforcement agencies, and people who want to become police officers in or near their own neighborhoods. Colleges and universities that run law enforcement training programs could also be involved as partners. The idea is to encourage people who are already rooted in a community to become officers there, rather than moving away after getting their training paid for.

The bill also requires the Attorney General to report to Congress every year on how the money is being used, including how many officers received training and how many stayed on the job afterward. This transparency requirement is meant to help Congress track whether the program is actually working to keep trained officers in local communities.
ai_headline: New federal grants would pay for local police training with a 4-year service requirement
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3458 adds a COPS grant program funding police training for recruits who agree to serve 4 years within their home community.
ai_sections.what: HR 3458 creates a new grant program under the existing COPS program that funds training academies for police officers and recruits. Recipients must commit to working full-time as an officer for at least 4 years within an 8-year window. Those who do not meet the service requirement must repay the funds, with limited exceptions allowed by the Attorney General.
ai_sections.who: Local police departments, sheriff's offices, and tribal law enforcement agencies are eligible to apply. Prospective officers and colleges or universities that run law enforcement training programs may also be involved.
ai_sections.why: The program targets people already rooted in a community, requiring recipients to work within 7 miles of a home where they have lived for at least 5 years, or within 20 miles in rural counties with fewer than 150,000 people. The Attorney General must report to Congress annually on training recipients and officer retention, giving lawmakers data to assess whether the program keeps trained officers in local communities.
ai_sections.cost: Local law enforcement agencies can apply for grant funds through the COPS program to cover training costs. Recipients who fail to complete the service requirement must repay the money.
costChips: AG may grant exceptions by rule

title: The Dalilah Law
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal for states to give commercial driver's licenses (CDLs) to people who are not U.S. citizens, green card holders, or holders of certain temporary work visas. A CDL is the special license required to drive large trucks, buses, and other big commercial vehicles. States that do not follow these rules would lose federal transportation funding. The bill also requires that all CDL tests be given only in English, and that applicants must demonstrate English proficiency to qualify.

People who already hold a CDL would have 180 days after the law passes to go through a recertification process. During that process, their immigration status and English proficiency would be checked. Anyone who does not recertify in time, or who does not meet the new requirements, would have their CDL taken away. Anyone who drives a commercial vehicle without meeting these requirements would be permanently banned from ever holding a CDL in the future.

This bill directly affects people currently working or hoping to work as truck drivers, bus drivers, or in other commercial driving jobs who are not citizens, green card holders, or holders of the specific work visas named in the bill. It also affects the trucking and transportation industry broadly, since those employers would have a smaller pool of eligible drivers. State governments would be affected too, since they must update their licensing processes or risk losing federal transportation money.
ai_headline: Bill would bar non-citizen commercial driver's licenses and require English tests
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7793 would restrict CDLs to citizens, green card holders, and select visa holders, and strip federal funding from states that don't comply.
ai_sections.what: HR 7793 would prohibit states from issuing commercial driver's licenses to anyone who is not a U.S. citizen, a green card holder, or a holder of certain temporary work visas. All CDL tests would be required to be given in English, and applicants would have to demonstrate English proficiency. People who currently hold a CDL would have 180 days to recertify under the new immigration and English requirements or lose their license.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects non-citizen commercial drivers currently working or seeking work as truck drivers, bus drivers, or in other commercial driving roles. It also affects trucking and transportation employers, who would have a smaller pool of eligible drivers, and state governments, which must update their licensing processes to remain compliant.
ai_sections.why: States that do not follow the new rules would lose federal transportation funding. Anyone who drives a commercial vehicle without meeting the requirements would be permanently banned from ever holding a CDL.
ai_sections.cost: States risk losing federal transportation funding if they do not update their licensing processes to comply with the bill's requirements.

title: LIGHT Safety Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the federal government to set a legal limit on how bright car headlights can be. Right now, there is no cap on the maximum brightness of low beam headlights on vehicles sold in the United States. The Department of Transportation would have one year to write and finalize a new rule setting that brightness limit, measured in lumens or a similar unit of light measurement.

The bill is a response to growing complaints that modern headlights, especially those using LED and other newer technologies, are too bright and blind oncoming drivers. Supporters argue that extremely bright headlights create a safety hazard on roads, particularly on two-lane highways where there is no barrier between traffic going in opposite directions.

This bill would affect automakers, who would have to design future vehicles to meet the new standard, and it could also affect vehicle owners if rules eventually apply to aftermarket headlight upgrades. Most directly, it would impact everyday drivers who share the road with vehicles that have very bright headlights. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration would be the agency responsible for determining and enforcing the specific brightness limit.
ai_headline: Federal bill would cap how bright car headlights can be
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7772 requires the Dept. of Transportation to set a maximum headlight brightness limit within one year.
ai_sections.what: HR 7772 would require the federal government to establish a legal maximum brightness limit for low beam headlights on vehicles sold in the United States. The Department of Transportation would have one year to write and finalize the new rule, with brightness measured in lumens or a comparable unit.
ai_sections.who: The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration would determine and enforce the specific brightness limit. Automakers, vehicle owners who use aftermarket headlight upgrades, and everyday drivers who share roads with bright-headlight vehicles would all be affected.
ai_sections.why: There is currently no federal cap on low beam headlight brightness, and complaints have grown that LED and other modern headlight technologies blind oncoming drivers. The bill's supporters contend this creates a safety hazard, particularly on two-lane highways with no barrier separating opposing traffic.

title: Next-Gen Collision Avoidance Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to create a written plan for rolling out a newer, smarter collision-avoidance system called ACAS-X. Right now, most aircraft use an older system to warn pilots when planes get too close to each other. ACAS-X is a more advanced version that uses better technology to reduce the risk of midair collisions. The FAA would have 180 days after the bill becomes law to finish the plan and send it to Congress, and then make it publicly available within 10 days after that.

The plan itself must cover several things: a step-by-step roadmap for getting the technology onto aircraft, what research and testing still needs to happen before it can be officially certified, and how the FAA will work with pilots, airlines, manufacturers, and safety experts during the process. The bill also requires the FAA to coordinate with aviation authorities in other countries so that international standards line up, and to look at how ACAS-X would work with equipment already installed on many planes today.

This bill would mainly affect the FAA, commercial airlines, general aviation pilots, helicopter operators, and companies that make aircraft and avionics equipment. For everyday passengers, the goal is simply safer skies. The bill does not require anyone to install the new system yet — it only requires the FAA to come up with a plan for eventually doing so.
ai_headline: FAA given 180 days to draft rollout plan for advanced collision-avoidance system
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7239 requires the FAA to deliver a written ACAS-X deployment plan to Congress within 180 days of enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 7239 directs the FAA to create a written plan for deploying ACAS-X, a more advanced aircraft collision-avoidance system than the one currently in widespread use. The plan must include a step-by-step rollout roadmap, remaining research and certification needs, a stakeholder engagement process, international coordination with foreign aviation authorities, and an assessment of compatibility with equipment already installed on aircraft. The FAA must submit the plan to Congress within 180 days of enactment and make it publicly available within 10 days after that.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects the FAA, commercial airlines, general aviation pilots, helicopter operators, and aircraft and avionics manufacturers. Passengers are an indirect stakeholder, as the stated purpose is reducing the risk of midair collisions.
ai_sections.why: The bill does not require any aircraft operator to install ACAS-X at this time, so near-term operational changes for airlines or pilots are limited. How the FAA shapes the eventual rollout plan will determine the timeline and scope of requirements that industry and international partners may face later.

title: Whale CHARTS Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) to build a more detailed, up-to-date system for tracking where whales travel, feed, breed, and give birth in U.S. waters. NOAA would create high-quality maps showing whale locations and migration routes, conduct surveys of whale species that are not well understood, and develop new tools like underwater sound monitors, drones, and satellites to detect whales more accurately. Those maps would be shared publicly online and could eventually be added to navigation systems used on ships and boats.

The bill also sets up a grant program, managed through the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation, to fund projects that reduce dangerous run-ins between whales and vessels or fishing gear. Grants could go toward new detection technology, better outreach to mariners, and approaches that help ocean industries and whales share the same waters more safely. Priority goes to projects that also benefit small businesses in fishing, tourism, and boating. Grant money can only flow to U.S.-based people or organizations.

The people most directly affected include commercial shippers, recreational boaters, fishermen, and coastal tourism operators, who would have better information about where whales are located. Researchers, universities, tribes, and local governments could apply for grants. The bill authorizes roughly $18 million over five years for the mapping, survey, and grant programs, and requires NOAA to report to Congress every three years on progress.
ai_headline: NOAA whale tracking maps would go public under $18M bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Coast Guard and Maritime Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7332 gives NOAA $18M over five years to map whale habitats and fund projects cutting whale-vessel and whale-gear collisions.
ai_sections.what: HR 7332 directs NOAA to build a detailed, current system tracking where whales travel, feed, breed, and give birth in U.S. waters. NOAA would produce maps of whale locations and migration routes, survey poorly understood species, and deploy underwater sound monitors, drones, and satellites to improve detection. Those maps would be published online and could be integrated into vessel navigation systems.
ai_sections.who: Commercial shippers, recreational boaters, fishermen, and coastal tourism operators would gain access to better whale location data. Researchers, universities, tribes, and local governments based in the United States could apply for grants.
ai_sections.why: Better whale location data would give mariners more information about where whales are present, which may affect how vessels and fishing operations are planned. Shipping, fishing, and tourism industries would be operating alongside improved monitoring infrastructure in shared ocean waters.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes approximately $18 million over five years for the mapping, survey, and grant programs. Grants are administered through the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation and may only go to U.S.-based individuals or organizations.
costChips: Grants via National Fish and Wildlife Foundation

title: ADOPT Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill makes certain adoption-related activities a federal crime. Specifically, it targets unlicensed "middlemen" who connect birth parents with prospective adoptive parents for pay, run ads to find children or families for adoption, or give more than $2,500 in financial support to a birth parent before that parent has spoken with a licensed agency or attorney. People who break these rules could face fines up to $50,000 and up to five years in prison. Organizations could be fined up to $100,000 per violation. The law would only apply when the adoption crosses state lines or uses tools like the internet, phone, or wire transfers.

The bill does not ban private adoption. It carves out clear exceptions for licensed adoption agencies, attorneys licensed in the relevant state, government child welfare agencies, and certain nonprofit organizations working under government contracts. Those groups can still run ads, connect families, and provide financial help to birth parents under existing rules. The point is to stop unregulated, for-profit middlemen from operating outside the oversight that licensed professionals face.

This bill affects anyone involved in a private domestic adoption that crosses state lines, including birth parents, prospective adoptive parents, and especially third-party individuals or businesses that try to arrange adoptions for a fee without being licensed or acting as an attorney. It was introduced in the House in November 2025 and referred to the Judiciary Committee, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets unlicensed adoption middlemen with fines and prison time
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6170 would criminalize paid, unlicensed adoption matching across state lines, with fines up to $50,000 and five years in prison.
ai_sections.what: HR 6170 would make it a federal crime for unlicensed, for-profit middlemen to connect birth parents with prospective adoptive parents for pay, advertise to find children or families for adoption, or give more than $2,500 to a birth parent before that parent has consulted a licensed agency or attorney. The law applies only when the adoption crosses state lines or uses the internet, phone, or wire transfers. Licensed adoption agencies, state-licensed attorneys, government child welfare agencies, and certain nonprofits under government contracts are explicitly exempt.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects unlicensed individuals and businesses that arrange adoptions for a fee, as well as birth parents and prospective adoptive parents involved in interstate private domestic adoptions. Licensed professionals and agencies operating under existing oversight are not restricted by this bill.
ai_sections.why: Unlicensed middlemen currently operate outside the oversight requirements that licensed adoption professionals must meet, and this bill would expose them to federal criminal and financial penalties. It has been referred to the House Judiciary Committee and has not yet become law.
ai_sections.cost: Individuals who violate the law could face fines up to $50,000 per violation, while organizations could be fined up to $100,000 per violation, in addition to up to five years in prison for individuals.
costChips: Individuals fined up to $50,000 per violation
Organizations fined up to $100,000 per violation
Up to 5 years in prison for individuals

title: PATH to Education Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new federal grant program to help public transit agencies improve bus and rail service to certain schools. Transit providers could apply for grants to add new bus routes or stops near campuses, run buses more often, or adjust schedules so that students can actually get to and from class. The schools that qualify include community colleges, minority-serving colleges, rural colleges, vocational and technical schools, and Head Start early childhood centers. Transit agencies would have to apply for the money and partner with at least one of these qualifying schools. Priority would go to transit systems serving schools where more than a quarter of students receive Pell Grants, which are federal financial aid awards given to lower-income students.

A separate but similar piece of the bill focuses on rural transit, using a different existing federal program to fund the same kinds of improvements in areas outside cities. Both parts of the bill also specifically help families who use Head Start programs for young children, making it easier for parents to get their kids to and from those early education centers. Funding would start at one million dollars in 2027 and grow to five million dollars by 2031 for each of the two grant programs.

This bill mainly affects college students, especially lower-income ones, who rely on public transportation to get to school. It also affects parents of young children enrolled in Head Start programs, as well as local transit agencies and the schools themselves. People living in rural areas are specifically included through the rural transit portion of the bill.
ai_headline: New federal grants would route more buses to community colleges and Head Start centers
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7099 creates two federal grant programs to expand transit service to community colleges, rural schools, and Head Start centers, starting at $1M in 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 7099 would establish two new federal grant programs for public transit agencies to add or improve bus and rail service near qualifying schools and early childhood centers. Eligible improvements include new routes or stops, more frequent service, and adjusted schedules. Qualifying institutions include community colleges, minority-serving colleges, rural colleges, vocational and technical schools, and Head Start centers.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects lower-income college students and parents of young children in Head Start programs who rely on public transportation. Local transit agencies and qualifying schools are also directly affected, including those in rural areas reached through the bill's rural transit provisions.
ai_sections.why: Transit agencies that receive grants would be required to partner with at least one qualifying school, and priority would go to systems serving schools where more than a quarter of students receive Pell Grants. People in rural areas are addressed through a separate funding mechanism using an existing federal program.
ai_sections.cost: Each of the two grant programs would be funded at one million dollars in 2027, growing to five million dollars by 2031.

title: VISIT USA Act
ai_summary:
This bill moves $160 million from a government fund called the Travel Promotion Fund directly to Brand USA, which is a nonprofit organization that advertises the United States as a travel destination to people in other countries. The money in that fund was collected from fees that foreign visitors pay when they apply to travel to the US under the Visa Waiver Program. The bill directs the Treasury Department to make this transfer within 30 days of the law being signed, and it bypasses a normal cap on how much money can be moved to Brand USA at one time.

Brand USA would use this money to run marketing campaigns overseas, such as ads and promotions encouraging international tourists to visit American cities, national parks, and other destinations. The bill still requires Brand USA to follow existing rules about matching the government funds with private money raised from businesses in the travel industry, like hotels and airlines.

This bill mainly affects the US travel and tourism industry, including hotels, airlines, restaurants, and attractions that benefit when more foreign tourists visit. It could also affect foreign travelers who might see more advertising about visiting the US. American taxpayers are not directly funding this, since the money comes from fees paid by foreign visitors, but how effective Brand USA's campaigns are could influence how many international tourists come to the US and how much they spend here.
ai_headline: $160 million shifted to Brand USA for overseas tourism ads
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6128 moves $160M from a foreign-visitor fee fund to Brand USA within 30 days of signing, bypassing normal transfer limits.
ai_sections.what: HR 6128 directs the Treasury Department to transfer $160 million from the Travel Promotion Fund to Brand USA within 30 days of the law being signed. The money comes from fees paid by foreign visitors under the Visa Waiver Program. The bill bypasses the normal cap on how much can be transferred to Brand USA at one time.
ai_sections.who: Brand USA, a nonprofit that markets the United States as a travel destination to international audiences, receives the funds. The US travel and tourism industry, including hotels, airlines, restaurants, and attractions, is the primary affected sector.
ai_sections.why: Brand USA would use the money to run overseas marketing campaigns, such as ads and promotions encouraging international tourists to visit American cities, national parks, and other destinations. How effective those campaigns are could influence how many international tourists visit the US and how much they spend.
ai_sections.cost: The bill moves $160 million from the Travel Promotion Fund, which holds fees collected from foreign visitors applying to enter the US under the Visa Waiver Program. Brand USA must still match government funds with private money raised from travel industry businesses; American taxpayers are not the source of these funds.

title: Southeast Asian Deportation Relief Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would stop the U.S. government from deporting people from Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam who entered the United States on or before January 1, 2008, and have lived here continuously since then. Even if these individuals have an existing deportation order, the government could not remove or detain them under this law. They would also be eligible for work permits, and those required to check in regularly with immigration officers would only need to do so virtually, once every five years instead of more frequently in person.

The bill also addresses people from those three countries who were already deported or left under a deportation order on or after April 24, 1996. It would allow them to ask the government to reopen their immigration cases, and if they qualify, their deportation orders would be canceled. The U.S. government would even pay for their travel back to the country so their cases could be heard again. People who were deported would be treated legally as if the deportation never happened, which could help them qualify for other immigration benefits.

The people most directly affected are an estimated 15,000 long-term U.S. residents of Cambodian, Laotian, and Vietnamese origin who currently have final deportation orders. Many came as refugees fleeing war and genocide decades ago, arrived as children, and have lived most of their lives in the United States. The bill also affects those who have already been deported and might want to return to have their cases reconsidered.
ai_headline: Bill would halt deportations of 15,000 Southeast Asian long-term residents
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7608 would bar deportation of Cambodian, Laotian, and Vietnamese residents who arrived by Jan. 1, 2008 and stayed continuously.
ai_sections.what: HR 7608 would prohibit the U.S. government from deporting or detaining people from Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam who entered the United States on or before January 1, 2008, and have lived here continuously since then, even if they already have a deportation order. Qualifying individuals would be eligible for work permits, and those required to check in with immigration officers would do so virtually, once every five years. People from those three countries who were deported on or after April 24, 1996, could ask to have their cases reopened, and if they qualify, their deportation orders would be canceled and they would be treated legally as if the deportation never happened.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects an estimated 15,000 long-term U.S. residents of Cambodian, Laotian, and Vietnamese origin who currently have final deportation orders, many of whom came as refugees fleeing war and genocide and arrived as children. It also affects individuals from those countries who have already been deported and may seek to return to have their cases reconsidered.
ai_sections.why: If enacted, thousands of people with existing deportation orders would be shielded from removal and gain access to work authorization, altering the legal status of cases that are currently considered final. Those already deported could have their removal legally nullified, potentially making them eligible for additional immigration benefits they did not previously qualify for.
ai_sections.cost: The U.S. government would pay for the travel back to the United States for individuals who were deported and seek to have their cases reopened.
costChips: U.S. govt pays return travel costs
To allow cases to be reheard

title: Real Courts, Rule of Law Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a brand-new, independent court system specifically for immigration cases. Right now, immigration judges work for the Department of Justice, which is part of the executive branch and reports to the President. This bill would move immigration courts out of the executive branch entirely and set them up as a separate court system under Article I of the Constitution, similar to how the U.S. Tax Court or bankruptcy courts are set up. The new system would have two main levels: trial courts where cases are first heard, and an appellate division that reviews those decisions. Judges at the appellate level would be appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate, while trial judges would be appointed by the appellate court itself, not by a political official.

The bill is designed to give immigration judges more independence from the President and political appointees. Under the current system, the Attorney General can override immigration court decisions and set policies that affect how judges rule. Under this proposal, immigration courts would set their own rules, control their own budget, and judges could only be removed for specific reasons like misconduct or disability, not for political reasons. The bill also requires written opinions explaining decisions, sets up a program to help people understand court procedures, and mandates annual public reports on how the courts are performing.

This bill would directly affect anyone involved in an immigration court case, including immigrants facing deportation, asylum seekers, and their lawyers. It would also affect the roughly 700 current immigration judges, who would transition into the new system during a changeover period of up to four years. The federal government agencies like the Department of Homeland Security that bring cases before immigration courts would also be affected, since they would now be arguing before a truly independent court rather than one housed within their own branch of government.
ai_headline: Bill would move immigration courts out of executive branch control
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7836 would create an independent Article I immigration court system, removing the Attorney General's power to override judge decisions.
ai_sections.what: HR 7836 would establish a new, independent immigration court system under Article I of the Constitution, separate from the Department of Justice and the executive branch. The system would have two levels: trial courts and an appellate division. Appellate judges would be confirmed by the Senate, while trial judges would be appointed by the appellate court itself.
ai_sections.who: The bill would directly affect immigrants facing deportation, asylum seekers, and their attorneys. Approximately 700 current immigration judges would transition into the new system over a changeover period of up to four years.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, the Attorney General can override immigration court decisions and set policies that shape how judges rule. Removing immigration courts from the executive branch would end that authority and make judges removable only for misconduct or disability, not for political reasons.

title: No Flight, No Fight Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal for airlines to ship adult male chickens (roosters) as air cargo in the United States or on international flights. There is one main exception: large commercial farms that earn $350,000 or more per year in farm income can still fly roosters if they provide paperwork proving they qualify. Any airline that accepts a rooster for shipping without that documentation could face financial penalties. The law would take effect six months after it is signed.

The bill's stated reasons include aviation safety, the spread of bird diseases, and concern that fast air transport of roosters makes it easier to move birds for cockfighting, which is illegal in all 50 states. By requiring certification from senders, the bill aims to make it harder to ship roosters for illegal purposes while keeping the door open for the large-scale egg and meat industries that have legitimate reasons to move breeding birds quickly.

This bill mainly affects small rooster owners, breeders, and hobbyists who currently ship birds by air and would no longer be allowed to do so. Large commercial poultry operations meeting the income threshold would mostly be unaffected. Airlines would need to add a new step to their cargo intake process to check paperwork before accepting any rooster shipment. Individual states could also pass their own stricter rules on top of this federal law.
ai_headline: Federal bill would ban rooster air cargo with limited farm exemptions
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7371 bans airlines from shipping roosters unless the sender is a farm earning $350,000+ yearly and provides paperwork.
ai_sections.what: HR 7371 would make it illegal for airlines to ship roosters as cargo on domestic or international flights originating in the United States. Farms earning $350,000 or more per year in farm income may still ship roosters if they submit qualifying documentation. Airlines that accept roosters without that paperwork would face financial penalties.
ai_sections.who: Small rooster owners, breeders, and hobbyists who currently ship birds by air would lose that option entirely. Large commercial poultry operations meeting the income threshold would be mostly unaffected, while airlines would need to add a documentation review step to their cargo intake process.
ai_sections.why: Congress cited aviation safety, the risk of spreading bird diseases, and the concern that air transport makes it easier to move roosters for cockfighting, which is illegal in all 50 states. The certification requirement is intended to make illegal rooster shipments harder to complete without blocking legitimate large-scale breeding operations.
ai_sections.cost: Airlines that ship roosters without required documentation could face financial penalties. No specific penalty amounts are stated in the summary.

title: To amend title 5, United States Code, to restrict the employment in the Federal Government of individuals who are not citizens or nationals, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
HR 9063 would make it harder for non-citizens to work for the federal government. Right now, federal law allows some exceptions that let certain non-citizens, like lawful permanent residents (green card holders), work in federal jobs under specific circumstances. This bill would tighten those rules and limit federal employment more strictly to U.S. citizens and nationals.

The bill affects people who are not U.S. citizens or nationals but currently work for, or are seeking to work for, federal agencies. It would also affect federal agencies that currently hire or have pathways to hire non-citizens in certain roles. U.S. nationals include people from places like American Samoa who owe permanent allegiance to the United States but are not full citizens.

Because the full text of the bill is not available here, some specific details, such as which jobs might still be allowed exceptions or how existing non-citizen federal workers would be handled, are unclear. What is clear is that the bill's main goal is to reduce or eliminate the situations where the federal government can bring on workers who are not citizens or nationals of the United States.
ai_headline: Bill would tighten federal hiring rules for non-citizens
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9063 would limit federal jobs more strictly to U.S. citizens and nationals, reducing pathways for non-citizens to work in federal agencies.
ai_sections.what: HR 9063 would tighten rules on who can work for the federal government by restricting employment more strictly to U.S. citizens and nationals. Current law allows some exceptions that permit certain non-citizens, such as lawful permanent residents, to hold federal jobs under specific circumstances. This bill would reduce or eliminate those exceptions, though which roles might still qualify and how current non-citizen workers would be handled remain unclear.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects non-citizens currently working for or seeking to work for federal agencies, including lawful permanent residents. It also affects federal agencies that hire or have pathways to hire non-citizens in certain roles.
ai_sections.why: Non-citizens who currently hold or are pursuing federal employment could lose eligibility or face new barriers under the changed rules. Federal agencies that rely on existing exceptions to fill certain positions would have fewer options for bringing on workers who are not U.S. citizens or nationals.

title: PRECEPT Nurses Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a $2,000 tax credit for experienced nurses who spend time training new nurses or nursing students. To qualify, a nurse must work at least 200 hours in a year as a "preceptor," which means a mentor who supervises and teaches someone learning the job in a real clinical setting. The credit only applies to preceptors working in areas officially labeled as having a shortage of health professionals, meaning places where there are not enough medical providers to meet community needs. The credit would be available starting in the 2026 tax year and would expire after 2032.

This bill is aimed at addressing a well-known problem in nursing: there are not enough experienced nurses willing or able to take time to train the next generation. Training new nurses takes significant time and effort, and preceptors are rarely paid extra for it. The $2,000 credit is meant to make that commitment more financially worthwhile.
