To claim it, a nurse would need official paperwork from either a nursing school or their employer confirming they completed the required hours.

The people most directly affected are licensed registered nurses and other qualified healthcare providers who mentor nursing students or newly hired nurses in underserved communities. Nursing students and new nurses in those areas could also benefit indirectly if the credit encourages more experienced nurses to take on training roles. The government would track how many people use the credit each year and evaluate whether it actually increased the number of preceptors by mid-2033.
ai_headline: Bill offers $2,000 tax credit to nurses who train new colleagues in shortage areas
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 392 gives a $2,000 annual tax credit to nurses who complete 200+ preceptor hours in health professional shortage areas, starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 392 would create a $2,000 federal tax credit for licensed registered nurses and other qualified healthcare providers who serve as preceptors, meaning mentors who supervise and teach nursing students or newly hired nurses in real clinical settings. To qualify, a nurse must complete at least 200 preceptor hours in a given year and work in an area officially designated as having a shortage of health professionals. The credit would be available for tax years 2026 through 2032.
ai_sections.who: The credit directly affects licensed registered nurses and other qualified healthcare providers who take on preceptor roles in underserved communities. Nursing students and newly hired nurses in those areas are indirectly affected, as the credit is intended to increase the number of experienced nurses willing to mentor them.
ai_sections.why: Preceptors currently receive little or no additional pay for the significant time and effort required to train new nurses, which limits how many are willing to take on that role. The government would track annual usage of the credit and evaluate whether it measurably increased the number of preceptors by mid-2033.
ai_sections.cost: To claim the credit, a nurse must obtain official documentation from either a nursing school or their employer confirming completion of the required hours.

title: DROUGHT Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for a federal loan and financing program called WFIA, which helps pay for water infrastructure projects like pipes, treatment plants, and reservoirs. Right now, the federal government can only cover up to a certain share of a project's cost. This bill creates exceptions that would allow the federal government to cover up to 90 percent of the cost for certain qualifying water projects, meaning local governments or water utilities would only need to come up with the remaining 10 percent themselves.

To qualify for this higher level of funding, a project has to meet at least one of three conditions. First, it must be located in a state or county that has experienced severe drought conditions within the past five years. Second, it must serve a lower-income community where the average household earns at or below 200 percent of the federal poverty level. Third, it must be a project that federal officials consider regionally or nationally significant because it meaningfully improves drinking water supply, promotes water recycling, reduces overall water use, lowers costs for water customers, or provides major public health or environmental benefits. Projects that qualify also get moved to the front of the line when the government is deciding which projects to finance.

This bill would most directly affect state and local governments, water districts, and utilities in drought-prone areas, particularly in the western United States. Lower-income communities that struggle to fund expensive water infrastructure upgrades would also be affected. Everyday residents in those areas could benefit if local governments use the extra federal funding to improve their water systems at lower cost to ratepayers.
ai_headline: Federal water bill would raise government's share to 90% for qualifying projects
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7845 lets the federal government fund up to 90% of certain water infrastructure projects, leaving locals to cover just 10%.
ai_sections.what: HR 7845 amends the federal WFIA loan and financing program, which funds water infrastructure such as pipes, treatment plants, and reservoirs. Under current rules, the federal government is limited in how much of a project's cost it can cover. This bill creates exceptions allowing federal funding to reach up to 90 percent of project costs for projects that meet at least one of three qualifying conditions.
ai_sections.who: State and local governments, water districts, and utilities would be directly affected, particularly those in drought-prone regions of the western United States. Lower-income communities where households earn at or below 200 percent of the federal poverty level would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Qualifying projects would be moved to the front of the line for federal financing decisions, changing how limited federal funds are distributed among competing projects. Local governments and utilities in drought-affected or lower-income areas would face a reduced local funding requirement, which could alter how and whether they pursue infrastructure upgrades.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government could cover up to 90 percent of eligible project costs under WFIA, with local governments or water utilities responsible for the remaining 10 percent. Projects serving communities at or below 200 percent of the federal poverty level, in drought-affected areas, or deemed regionally or nationally significant would qualify for this higher federal share.
costChips: Federal share up to 90% of project cost
Income threshold at 200% of poverty level

title: MARINA Act
ai_summary:
The MARINA Act tells the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers how it must handle rent and fees charged to marinas that operate on federal land managed by the Corps. Right now, marina operators pay rent based on a percentage of their total sales. This bill would lower the rent calculation by capping the percentage rate on certain categories of sales, like fuel, boats, food, and beverages, at no more than 1 percent. It also requires the Corps to create one clear, uniform fee schedule for all of its districts, since currently each district can charge marinas different administrative fees with little consistency. Those fees would be capped depending on how complex a given activity is, ranging from $1,000 for routine matters up to $50,000 for large projects requiring multiple government agencies to sign off.

The bill also extends how long marina leases can last. Under current law, lease lengths are set at the Corps' discretion. This bill would require initial leases and first renewals to last at least 50 years, and any renewals after that to last at least 25 years. Additionally, the bill says the Corps cannot require marina operators to pay their workers more than the federal minimum wage as a condition of their lease, unless a separate federal contracting law already requires higher pay.

This bill mainly affects the private businesses that run marinas on Corps of Engineers lakes and reservoirs, which are mostly recreational boating facilities spread across the country. It could also indirectly affect everyday boaters and lakeside visitors who use those marinas. The Corps of Engineers itself would need to rewrite its rules within one year of the bill becoming law.
ai_headline: New bill caps marina rents and sets 50-year leases on federal land
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: The MARINA Act caps rent rates, standardizes fees, and sets minimum 50-year leases for marinas on U.S. Army Corps of Engineers land.
ai_sections.what: The MARINA Act changes how the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers calculates rent and fees for marina operators on federal land. It caps the rent percentage on categories like fuel, boats, food, and beverages at no more than 1 percent of sales, and requires a single uniform fee schedule across all Corps districts. It also sets minimum lease lengths of 50 years for initial leases and first renewals, and at least 25 years for subsequent renewals.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects private businesses operating marinas on Corps of Engineers lakes and reservoirs across the country. Recreational boaters and lakeside visitors who use those marinas could also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: The Corps of Engineers would be required to rewrite its rules within one year of the bill becoming law. The bill removes the Corps' current discretion over lease lengths and fee amounts, replacing them with legislatively set minimums and caps.
ai_sections.cost: Administrative fees charged to marina operators would be capped based on activity complexity, ranging from $1,000 for routine matters up to $50,000 for large projects requiring multiple government agencies to approve.

title: SMART Kids Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the U.S. Surgeon General to create official guidelines on how much screen time children should have each day. The guidelines would be based on scientific evidence and would cover six different age groups, from babies under 2 years old all the way up to teenagers aged 16 to 18. The Surgeon General would also have the option to look at whether certain types of screen use, like video chatting with family versus scrolling social media, might be more or less harmful than others. An outside organization without financial ties to the tech industry would help develop the recommendations. The final guidelines must be published online and sent to Congress within one year of the bill becoming law.

The bill does not create any laws, fines, or enforcement rules. It only requires the government to produce and share recommendations, similar to dietary guidelines or vaccine schedules. Parents, schools, and others could choose to follow the advice or not.

This bill would most directly affect parents and caregivers of children under 18, pediatricians and child health professionals, and school administrators. It could also affect tech companies indirectly if the published guidelines draw public attention to screen use habits. Children of all ages are the focus of the health guidance being requested.
ai_headline: Surgeon General ordered to set children's daily screen time guidelines
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8743 directs the Surgeon General to publish evidence-based daily screen time guidelines for children under 18 within one year.
ai_sections.what: HR 8743 requires the U.S. Surgeon General to develop and publish official, evidence-based guidelines on daily screen time for children, covering six age groups from infants under 2 through teenagers aged 16 to 18. The guidelines must be published online and submitted to Congress within one year of the bill becoming law. The bill creates no laws, fines, or enforcement mechanisms — compliance with any resulting recommendations is entirely voluntary.
ai_sections.who: Parents, caregivers, pediatricians, child health professionals, and school administrators would be the most directly affected audiences for the guidelines. Tech companies could be indirectly affected if published guidelines shift public attention toward children's screen use habits.
ai_sections.why: Publishing government-backed screen time recommendations could influence how families, schools, and health providers approach children's daily device use. Because the guidelines carry no legal weight, any changes in behavior would depend entirely on voluntary adoption.

title: Military ADS–B Out Loophole Act
ai_summary:
ADS-B Out is a tracking technology that aircraft are required to broadcast so that air traffic control and other planes can see them on radar. Current FAA rules allow military and other government aircraft to turn off this tracking during a "sensitive government mission," but the rules do not clearly define what that means. This bill would require the FAA to narrowly define "sensitive government mission" so the tracking exemption can only be used during the specific part of a flight where sensitive activities are actually happening, not for an entire flight just because part of it involves sensitive work.

The bill also sets up a system of oversight and accountability. Government agencies that use the exemption would have to file regular reports with the FAA listing each flight where tracking was turned off, including the location, time, and reason. The FAA would then report that information to Congress every six months. If an agency turns off its tracking five or more times in a single month, Congress must be notified within two weeks. The Government Accountability Office would also review how the exemption has been used, and the Transportation Department's Inspector General would conduct annual audits starting three years after the bill passes.

This bill primarily affects federal agencies like the Department of Defense, as well as state, local, and tribal agencies that fly aircraft on sensitive government missions. It does not directly change anything for private pilots or commercial airlines. The goal is to make sure that when government planes go invisible to radar, there is a legitimate and limited reason for it, and that someone is keeping track.
ai_headline: Bill would limit when government planes can hide from radar tracking
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7240 narrows a federal exemption letting government aircraft disable ADS-B Out tracking and adds mandatory reporting to Congress.
ai_sections.what: HR 7240 would require the FAA to narrowly define "sensitive government mission" so aircraft can only disable ADS-B Out tracking during the specific portion of a flight where sensitive activities occur, not for an entire flight. Government agencies would have to file regular reports with the FAA for each flight where tracking was disabled, including location, time, and reason. The FAA would report that data to Congress every six months, and if an agency disables tracking five or more times in one month, Congress must be notified within two weeks.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects federal agencies such as the Department of Defense, as well as state, local, and tribal agencies that fly aircraft on sensitive government missions. Private pilots and commercial airlines are not directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Current FAA rules allow government aircraft to disable tracking for an entire flight even if only part of it involves sensitive work, leaving the exemption open to broad use without a clear standard. The bill would add GAO review of how the exemption has been used and annual audits by the Transportation Department's Inspector General beginning three years after passage.

title: Trump Accounts for All Generations Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with special savings accounts for children called "Trump Accounts." Under a program that currently runs only through 2028, the federal government deposits $1,000 into a savings account for every newborn child in the United States. This bill would make that program permanent so it does not expire, and it would adjust the $1,000 starting deposit over time to keep up with inflation after 2028.

The bill affects every American child born in the United States, since each newborn would receive a government-funded account. The money in these accounts is meant to grow over time and can be used later in life. Parents and others can also add their own contributions to the accounts. The inflation adjustment means the initial deposit amount would gradually increase over the years to match rising prices.

The main practical change this bill makes is removing the expiration date from the existing program and adding the inflation adjustment, so future generations continue to receive accounts of roughly equal real value. Children born today and in the future, along with their families, are the primary people affected.
ai_headline: Bill would make $1,000 newborn savings accounts permanent and inflation-adjusted
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8313 removes the 2028 expiration on government-funded $1,000 newborn savings accounts and ties the deposit to inflation.
ai_sections.what: HR 8313 would make permanent a federal program that currently expires in 2028, under which the government deposits $1,000 into a savings account for every child born in the United States. The bill also adds an inflation adjustment to the initial $1,000 deposit starting after 2028, so the real value of the account keeps pace with rising prices over time. Parents and others may also contribute their own money to the accounts.
ai_sections.who: Every child born in the United States would receive a government-funded account under this program. Families of newborns are the primary people directly affected by the change.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, the existing newborn savings account program would end in 2028, and no new accounts would be created after that date. Making the program permanent and inflation-adjusted means future generations would continue receiving accounts of roughly equal real value rather than a fixed dollar amount that loses purchasing power over time.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government provides the initial $1,000 deposit for each newborn born in the United States. After 2028, that deposit amount would increase over time in line with inflation.

title: Stop Executive Renaming for Vanity and Ego (SERVE) Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal to name or rename any federal property after a sitting president while that person is still in office. This includes federal buildings, public lands, and any other government-owned assets. It also bars the use of federal money to carry out any such naming. If any federal property has already been given a sitting president's name by the time this bill becomes law, it would have to be changed back to whatever name was assigned to it by a previous law.

The bill also applies retroactively, meaning it is not just a forward-looking rule. Any recent renamings that have already taken place would need to be undone if the president is still in office when the bill passes.

This bill would primarily affect the executive branch and federal agencies that manage government property, such as the General Services Administration, the National Park Service, and others. It would also affect any communities or facilities connected to federal properties that have been recently renamed. Ordinary Americans would mostly notice this if a local federal building, park, or landmark had recently received a new presidential name and then had to revert to its old one.
ai_headline: Bill would bar naming federal property after sitting presidents
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7039 bans naming or renaming federal property after a sitting president and reverses any such renamings already made.
ai_sections.what: HR 7039 would make it illegal to name or rename any federal property after a sitting president while that person remains in office. The ban covers federal buildings, public lands, and all other government-owned assets. It also applies retroactively, requiring any federal property already given a sitting president's name to be restored to its previously designated name.
ai_sections.who: Federal agencies that manage government property, including the General Services Administration and the National Park Service, would be directly responsible for compliance. Communities and facilities connected to recently renamed federal properties would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Any federal properties renamed after a sitting president while the bill is in effect would have to be renamed again to their prior designations. Ordinary Americans would notice the change most directly if a local federal building, park, or landmark recently received a presidential name and then reverted to its former one.
ai_sections.cost: The bill bars the use of federal money to carry out any naming of federal property after a sitting president.
costChips: Federal funds banned for presidential naming

title: Parks to People Active Transportation Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new federal grant program run by the Department of Transportation. The program would give money to state and local governments, tribal nations, and regional planning groups to build or improve greenway paths — paved trails and shared walkways designed for walking, biking, and other non-car travel. These paths would need to cross multiple communities, counties, or even state lines, and would be selected through a competitive application process. The federal government would pay up to 80 percent of project costs in most cases, with higher federal shares available for rural areas (90 percent) and very low-income communities (100 percent). The bill authorizes $300 million per year from 2027 through 2031 for these grants.

The program is aimed at connecting communities to parks, public transit, schools, and jobs through trails that people can use without a car. When deciding who gets grants, the Transportation Department would look at factors like community support, safety improvements, and whether the project helps close gaps in existing trail networks. Projects must cost at least $15 million to qualify, though smaller planning and design grants are available for as little as $100,000.

The people most directly affected are residents who live near proposed trail routes, especially in lower-income communities and rural areas that are given priority for funding. Local and state governments, metropolitan planning agencies, and tribal nations are the ones who would apply for the money and manage the projects. Cyclists, pedestrians, and people using wheelchairs or mobility devices would be the primary users of any paths built under this program.
ai_headline: Federal bill would fund multistate greenway trails at up to $300M yearly
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7751 creates a $300M/year federal grant program to build multi-community walking and biking trails through 2031.
ai_sections.what: HR 7751 would establish a new federal grant program through the Department of Transportation to fund construction or improvement of greenway paths — paved, shared-use trails for walking, biking, and non-car travel. Eligible projects must cross multiple communities, counties, or state lines and cost at least $15 million, though smaller planning and design grants start at $100,000. Grants would be awarded through a competitive application process based on factors including community support, safety improvements, and whether a project closes gaps in existing trail networks.
ai_sections.who: State and local governments, tribal nations, and regional planning organizations would apply for and manage the grants. Residents near proposed routes — particularly those in lower-income and rural areas — would be the primary people affected, along with cyclists, pedestrians, and people who use wheelchairs or other mobility devices.
ai_sections.why: The program is designed to connect communities to parks, public transit, schools, and jobs through trails accessible without a car. Rural areas and very low-income communities are given funding priority, which could shift which projects get built and where.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $300 million per year from 2027 through 2031. The federal government would cover up to 80 percent of project costs in most cases, 90 percent for rural projects, and 100 percent for very low-income communities.

title: Thermal Runaway Reduction Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of Transportation to create new safety rules for shipping lithium-ion batteries by truck or other commercial vehicles. The main concern is "thermal runaway," which is when a battery overheats and catches fire in a way that is hard to stop. Within two years of the bill becoming law, the government would require that batteries be shipped at no more than 30 percent of their full charge, since a less-charged battery is less likely to catch fire. The bill also requires the government to create clear rules for how to safely package and move batteries that are already damaged, recalled, or defective.

The bill also sets up a grant program with up to $10 million available for fire testing facilities to study new ways to put out lithium battery fires. Researchers would test tools and methods that work without using certain harmful chemicals known as PFAS. They would also look at how a battery's charge level affects how hard the fire is to fight, and how emergency responders can quickly figure out how charged a battery is during an accident.

Finally, the bill creates a separate grant program to help local fire departments, both volunteer and paid, get the equipment they need to handle lithium battery fires on the road. This includes fire suppression blankets, containment tools, and environmental cleanup gear. The people most directly affected include shipping companies that transport batteries, manufacturers who ship battery-powered products, fire departments across the country, and consumers who could be safer as a result of better-regulated battery transport.
ai_headline: New federal rules would limit battery charge levels during truck shipments
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7928 requires lithium-ion batteries to be shipped at no more than 30% charge and funds fire safety research and equipment.
ai_sections.what: HR 7928 directs the Department of Transportation to write new safety rules for shipping lithium-ion batteries by commercial vehicle, including a requirement that batteries travel at no more than 30 percent charge within two years of enactment. The bill also requires clear rules for packaging and moving batteries that are damaged, recalled, or defective. Two grant programs would fund fire research and help local fire departments acquire suppression and containment equipment.
ai_sections.who: Shipping companies and manufacturers that transport battery-powered products would face new packaging and charge-level requirements. Fire departments, both volunteer and paid, and emergency responders across the country are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Lithium-ion batteries can experience thermal runaway, an overheating process that causes fires that are difficult to stop, posing risks during road transport. Stricter charge limits and clearer handling rules could change how a broad range of industries package and move battery products.
ai_sections.cost: A grant program of up to $10 million would fund fire testing facilities to study ways to extinguish lithium battery fires. A separate grant program would help local fire departments obtain equipment such as fire suppression blankets, containment tools, and environmental cleanup gear.
costChips: Up to $10M for fire testing research
Grants for local fire dept. equipment

title: Defending the Human Rights of Palestinian Children and Families Living Under Israeli Military Occupation Act
ai_summary:
This bill would place restrictions on how US financial assistance to Israel can be used. Specifically, it would ban any American funds from being spent to support the military detention of Palestinian children, the destruction or seizure of Palestinian homes and property in the West Bank, or actions that help Israel annex additional Palestinian land. The bill also requires the Secretary of State to report to Congress each year, either confirming that US funds were not used for those purposes or explaining in detail how they were.

The bill also adds new requirements to an existing law, the Foreign Assistance Act of 1961, so that the State Department's annual human rights report must include specific information about how Palestinian children are treated in Israeli military detention, how Palestinian property is handled in the West Bank, and whether Israel is following a 2016 United Nations Security Council resolution about settlements. On top of that, the Government Accountability Office would be required to produce yearly reports examining how a special category of US military aid, called offshore procurement funding, has been spent in Israel and whether any of it has been connected to settlement activity.

The people most directly affected are Palestinians living in the West Bank, particularly children who have been detained by Israeli military authorities, and families whose homes have been demolished. It also affects the Israeli government, which currently receives significant US military aid with relatively few restrictions attached. US taxpayers and policymakers are affected as well, since the bill creates new oversight and transparency requirements around how American money is being used abroad.
ai_headline: Bill would ban US funds from supporting Israeli detention of Palestinian children
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7545 would bar US aid to Israel from funding detention of Palestinian children, West Bank demolitions, or annexation of Palestinian land.
ai_sections.what: HR 7545 would prohibit US financial assistance to Israel from being used to support military detention of Palestinian children, destruction or seizure of Palestinian homes and property in the West Bank, or actions that advance annexation of Palestinian land. The bill also requires the Secretary of State to report to Congress annually confirming those funds were not used for those purposes or detailing any violations. Additionally, the Government Accountability Office would produce yearly reports examining how offshore procurement funding has been spent in Israel and whether any is connected to settlement activity.
ai_sections.who: Palestinians in the West Bank are most directly affected, particularly children held in Israeli military detention and families whose homes have been demolished. The Israeli government, US taxpayers, and policymakers are also affected, as the bill adds new restrictions and oversight requirements to existing US military aid.
ai_sections.why: The bill creates new transparency and accountability mechanisms around how American foreign assistance funds are used in Israel. It also expands existing human rights reporting requirements under the Foreign Assistance Act of 1961 to include specific information about Palestinian children in military detention, Palestinian property in the West Bank, and Israeli compliance with a 2016 UN Security Council resolution on settlements.

title: CARGO Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would stop the National Institutes of Health (NIH) from giving money to research projects that use live animals if those projects take place outside the United States. Right now, NIH can send grant money to foreign universities, labs, and organizations for animal research. If this bill becomes law, any research involving live animals would have to happen on U.S. soil, including U.S. territories, in order to receive NIH funding.

The bill's supporters point out that NIH spent about $2.2 billion on foreign animal research between 2011 and 2021, and that NIH does not physically inspect those overseas labs. Foreign organizations currently self-report their own animal welfare information, which Congress argues creates a risk of inaccurate reporting and potential mistreatment of animals.

This bill mainly affects foreign research institutions and universities that currently receive NIH funding for animal studies. It would also affect U.S. scientists who partner with overseas labs on animal research, since those projects could lose NIH support unless the animal work is moved to the United States. Domestic research institutions and labs would be unaffected in terms of their eligibility, though the overall pool of NIH-funded research partners would shrink.
ai_headline: NIH would lose authority to fund animal research in foreign labs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1085 would bar NIH from funding live-animal research conducted outside the U.S. or its territories.
ai_sections.what: HR 1085 would prohibit the National Institutes of Health from awarding grant money to research projects that use live animals if that work takes place outside the United States or its territories. Foreign universities, labs, and organizations currently receive NIH funding for animal research, but this bill would end that eligibility. Any animal research seeking NIH support would have to be conducted on U.S. soil.
ai_sections.who: Foreign research institutions and universities that currently receive NIH funding for animal studies would lose that eligibility under this bill. U.S. scientists who partner with overseas labs on animal research could also lose NIH support unless the animal work is relocated to the United States.
ai_sections.why: NIH does not physically inspect overseas labs, and foreign organizations currently self-report their own animal welfare information, which Congress argues creates a risk of inaccurate reporting and potential animal mistreatment. Domestic research institutions would remain eligible for NIH funding, though the overall pool of NIH-funded research partners would shrink.
ai_sections.cost: NIH spent approximately $2.2 billion on foreign animal research between 2011 and 2021.

title: TRACK ICE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change the rules about flight privacy for planes used by immigration enforcement agencies. Right now, the FAA has a program that lets private plane owners and operators hide their flight information from the public. The TRACK ICE Act would make that privacy protection unavailable for any private aircraft that is flying for ICE or Customs and Border Protection, paid for with federal money, and being used to detain or deport people. In other words, if a private company is getting paid by the government to fly immigration detainees, the public could see the flight data.

The bill also requires the Department of Homeland Security to post detailed flight information online within 72 hours after each deportation or detention flight. That information would include departure and arrival locations, times, the aircraft registration number, and how many people were on board. It would also require demographic details about the people being transported, such as their nationality, age group, and family status, as well as what kinds of physical restraints, like handcuffs or shackles, were used on them during the flight.

This bill most directly affects ICE, Customs and Border Protection, private aviation companies that contract with those agencies, and people who are detained and transported by immigration authorities. It would also affect any member of the public, journalist, or researcher interested in tracking how immigration enforcement flights are carried out. The bill was introduced in the House and has been sent to committee, meaning it has not become law yet.
ai_headline: Bill would strip flight privacy from private planes hired for deportations
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7172 would ban flight-privacy shields for private planes paid by the US government to detain or deport immigrants.
ai_sections.what: HR 7172 would remove FAA flight-privacy protections from any private aircraft contracted by ICE or Customs and Border Protection using federal funds to detain or deport people. It would also require the Department of Homeland Security to publish flight details online within 72 hours of each deportation or detention flight, including locations, times, aircraft registration, passenger counts, demographic information, and types of physical restraints used.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects ICE, Customs and Border Protection, private aviation companies that contract with those agencies, and people detained and transported by immigration authorities. Journalists, researchers, and members of the public who want to track immigration enforcement flights would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Removing the privacy shield would make flight data for federally funded immigration enforcement flights visible to the public. Mandatory disclosure of restraint use, demographics, and flight logistics could increase public and press scrutiny of how detainees are treated during transport.

title: Stopping Border Surges Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes several changes to immigration law focused on two main areas: how unaccompanied migrant children are handled, and how the asylum system works. For children who arrive at the border without a parent, the bill would require them to be interviewed by officers specially trained to work with child trafficking victims, and it would speed up their court hearings to within 14 days of screening. It also removes a rule that previously gave children from non-neighboring countries more legal protections than those from Mexico and Canada, applying the same faster process to all unaccompanied children regardless of where they come from. Before placing a child with a sponsor, the government would have to share that sponsor's personal information with immigration authorities.

On asylum, the bill tightens the rules in several ways. People who passed through another country on their way to the US would generally have to show they already tried to get asylum there first. The deadline to apply for asylum would shrink from one year to six months after arriving. Asylum seekers would only be allowed to apply if they entered at an official port of entry, not between border crossings. Work permits for asylum applicants would be delayed until one year after filing instead of 180 days, and would expire sooner. People who return to the country they fled could lose their asylum status. Filing a false asylum claim could result in up to 10 years in prison.

This bill primarily affects migrants and asylum seekers at the US border, including families and children traveling without parents. It also affects immigration judges, border officers, and federal agencies like Homeland Security and Health and Human Services that manage migrant cases and child placements.
ai_headline: HR 116 cuts asylum deadlines and tightens rules for migrant children
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 116 shortens the asylum application window to 6 months, limits entry to ports of entry, and speeds up hearings for unaccompanied children.
ai_sections.what: HR 116 tightens asylum rules by cutting the application deadline from one year to six months, requiring applicants to have sought asylum in transit countries first, and restricting applications to those who entered at official ports of entry. The bill also changes how unaccompanied migrant children are processed, requiring specialized interviews, faster court hearings within 14 days, and equal treatment regardless of country of origin. Sponsors receiving migrant children would have their personal information shared with immigration authorities before placement.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects migrants and asylum seekers at the US border, including unaccompanied children and their potential sponsors. It also affects immigration judges, border officers, and federal agencies including Homeland Security and Health and Human Services.
ai_sections.why: People who entered between ports of entry or did not seek asylum in a transit country would be barred from applying under the new rules. Asylum seekers would wait longer for work permits, up to one year after filing, and could lose status if they return to their home country, while false claims could result in up to 10 years in prison.

title: Hemp Enforcement, Modernization, and Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new federal system for regulating hemp-derived cannabinoid products, such as CBD oils, edibles, vapes, and lotions. Right now, these products exist in a legal gray area with little consistent federal oversight. The bill puts the FDA in charge of setting safety standards, requiring manufacturers to register with the government, and listing their products. It defines exactly what counts as a cannabinoid hemp product and draws clear lines about what is and is not allowed to be sold. Products that contain too much intoxicating material, contain fully synthetic cannabinoids, or are designed to be absorbed through the skin in a way that enters the bloodstream would be banned outright.

The bill sets specific rules depending on the type of product. Edibles and drinks would have limits on how much intoxicating cannabinoid content is allowed per serving and per package. Vapes and smokable products would need to use prefilled, sealed cartridges and could only contain ingredients approved by the FDA. All products would need clear labels showing cannabinoid content, health warnings, allergen disclosures, and a QR code linking to lab test results. No one under 21 could legally buy any of these products, and companies could face criminal penalties of up to 10 years in prison for serious violations.

This bill affects a wide range of people. Hemp product manufacturers, especially smaller businesses, would need to register facilities, list products, and meet new testing and labeling requirements. Retailers would be required to enforce the age-21 rule. Consumers would get more consistent product information and safety protections. Foreign companies selling these products in the US would also be subject to inspection requirements.
ai_headline: FDA would take charge of hemp cannabinoid products under new federal bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7212 puts the FDA in charge of hemp-derived cannabinoid products, banning some outright and requiring age-21 sales, registration, and labeling.
ai_sections.what: HR 7212 creates a new federal regulatory system for hemp-derived cannabinoid products such as CBD oils, edibles, vapes, and lotions, ending the current legal gray area. The FDA would set safety standards, define which products are permitted, and ban those with too much intoxicating content, fully synthetic cannabinoids, or transdermal bloodstream absorption. Edibles and drinks would face per-serving and per-package limits on intoxicating content, while vapes would require prefilled sealed cartridges and FDA-approved ingredients only.
ai_sections.who: Hemp product manufacturers would need to register facilities, list products, and meet testing and labeling requirements, with smaller businesses among those affected. Retailers would be required to enforce a minimum purchase age of 21, and foreign companies selling into the US market would face inspection requirements.
ai_sections.why: All products would require labels disclosing cannabinoid content, health warnings, allergen information, and a QR code linking to lab test results, giving consumers more consistent product information. Companies that commit serious violations could face criminal penalties of up to 10 years in prison.
ai_sections.cost: Companies found in serious violation of the law could face criminal penalties of up to 10 years in prison.
costChips: Criminal penalty up to 10 years prison

title: Linemen Legacy Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes a legal definition in the Homeland Security Act of 2002. Right now, that law lists who counts as an "emergency response provider" — groups like police, firefighters, and emergency medical workers. The Linemen Legacy Act would officially add utility line technicians, such as power line workers and electrical crews, to that list when they are responding to a presidentially declared major disaster or emergency.

Being recognized as an emergency response provider can matter in practical ways. It can affect things like access to disaster zones, coordination with federal agencies during emergencies, and eligibility for certain federal programs or protections. When a hurricane or major storm knocks out power across a region, utility crews are often among the first responders on the ground, but they have not always been treated the same way under federal law as traditional first responders.

This bill would most directly affect utility line technicians and the companies or utilities that employ them. It could also affect state and local emergency managers who coordinate disaster response, and indirectly affect residents in disaster-affected areas if clearer federal recognition helps utility crews get to work faster or with fewer obstacles during emergencies.
ai_headline: Utility line workers would gain federal first responder status under disaster law
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7996 adds utility line technicians to the federal definition of emergency response provider during presidentially declared disasters.
ai_sections.what: HR 7996 amends the Homeland Security Act of 2002 to add utility line technicians to the legal definition of emergency response provider. This recognition currently applies to groups like police, firefighters, and emergency medical workers. The change would apply only when technicians are responding to a presidentially declared major disaster or emergency.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects utility line technicians and the companies or utilities that employ them. State and local emergency managers who coordinate disaster response would also be affected, as would residents in disaster-affected areas.
ai_sections.why: Federal recognition as an emergency response provider can affect access to disaster zones, coordination with federal agencies, and eligibility for certain federal programs or protections. Utility crews have not always been treated the same way under federal law as traditional first responders, even when working in the immediate aftermath of major storms or hurricanes.

title: SECURES Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells the U.S. Department of Transportation that it must start the official process of creating new federal rules about seat belts on school buses. Right now, there is no federal law requiring seat belts on school buses. Under this bill, the Department of Transportation would have 180 days after the law passes to publish a formal proposal for new safety standards. The proposal would apply to all new school buses, no matter how large or small they are.

The bill also lists specific things the government must think about when writing that proposal. These include the safety benefits of lap and shoulder belts, also called three-point belts, which work like the seat belts in most cars. It also asks the government to look at technology that could detect whether belts are being worn and alert drivers or others if they are not. The government would also need to look at what has already worked in states that already require school bus seat belts.

This bill most directly affects students who ride school buses, the school districts and bus companies that buy new buses, and the bus manufacturers who build them. It does not immediately require seat belts on any bus. It only requires the government to officially propose new rules, which would then go through a public comment period before any final rules could take effect.
ai_headline: Federal agency ordered to propose school bus seat belt rules within 180 days
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7428 requires the Dept. of Transportation to formally propose school bus seat belt standards within 180 days of enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 7428 directs the U.S. Department of Transportation to begin the official rulemaking process for seat belt standards on school buses within 180 days of the law taking effect. The proposal would apply to all newly manufactured school buses regardless of size. No seat belts are immediately required; any final rules would only take effect after a public comment period.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects students who ride school buses, school districts and bus companies that purchase new buses, and manufacturers that build them. The Department of Transportation is the agency responsible for carrying out the rulemaking.
ai_sections.why: Currently no federal law requires seat belts on school buses, and this bill would set a formal timeline for the government to propose one. The outcome of that process could eventually lead to new purchase and manufacturing requirements for school buses nationwide.

title: Miranda’s Law
ai_summary:
This bill would create a national system that automatically alerts employers when one of their commercial drivers gets a ticket, is in an accident, has their license suspended or revoked, or has any other negative change to their driving record. Right now, employers often have to check on their drivers manually, which can mean problems get missed. Under this bill, the federal government would have one year to set up the rules for this system, and states would have two years after that to start using it. Employers who use this system would no longer be required to do their current yearly manual checks of driver records.

The bill places special focus on school bus drivers. Any employer whose workers hold a commercial driver's license with a school bus endorsement would be required to sign up for this notification system. This includes not just private bus companies, but also school districts and schools themselves. If a school pays a private company to handle transportation, both the school and the company would be considered employers under this law and both would have to participate.

The bill affects commercial truck and bus drivers across the country, their employers, and especially anyone involved in transporting children to and from school or school events. It also directly affects students and families who rely on school transportation, since the goal is to make sure schools know quickly if a bus driver has a dangerous driving record. Drivers are also protected by a requirement that they receive a copy of any report sent to their employer at the same time the employer receives it.
ai_headline: Federal bill would auto-notify employers when drivers get tickets or violations
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7429 would create a national alert system notifying employers when commercial drivers get tickets, accidents, or license changes.
ai_sections.what: HR 7429 would establish a national system that automatically notifies employers when a commercial driver's record changes, including tickets, accidents, and license suspensions or revocations. The federal government would have one year to set up the rules, and states would have two years after that to begin participating. Employers using the system would no longer be required to conduct their current yearly manual checks of driver records.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects commercial truck and bus drivers nationwide and their employers, with mandatory participation required for any employer whose workers hold a commercial driver's license with a school bus endorsement, including school districts, private bus companies, and schools. Drivers are also directly affected, as they must receive a copy of any report sent to their employer at the same time the employer receives it.
ai_sections.why: Current employer oversight relies on manual checks that can allow driving record problems to go undetected. The bill targets school transportation in particular, as missed violations could affect the safety of students relying on school buses.

title: Nuclear Plant Decommissioning Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes how nuclear power plants are shut down and cleaned up. Right now, when a nuclear plant closes, the company that owns it creates a plan for how to safely tear it down and handle the radioactive waste. Under this bill, those companies would first have to consult with the state, tribal, and local governments near the plant before submitting that plan. States within 50 miles of a plant get a formal say, and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission would have to officially approve the shutdown plan rather than just review it. States could support, conditionally support, or oppose the plan, and the Commission would have to seriously consider state-recommended changes unless there is strong evidence those changes violate the law or cost far more than they are worth.

The bill also creates grant programs to help communities near shutting-down plants. In the short term, about $12.5 million would go to states and tribal governments to fund local advisory boards, which are groups that keep residents informed and involved in the cleanup process. For the long term, nuclear plant owners would pay $500,000 per plant into a fund that would keep supporting those community boards. Separately, local governments stuck storing leftover nuclear waste would receive $15 for every kilogram of spent fuel sitting in their community. Nuclear plant owners would also be required to set aside 2 percent of their cleanup savings funds into special accounts that go directly to local communities for economic development planning.

People most affected include residents living near any of the roughly two dozen nuclear plants currently being decommissioned or expected to close, particularly those in small, rural, or lower-income communities who get extra protections from cost-sharing requirements. State and tribal governments gain real influence over cleanup decisions they previously had little formal power over. Nuclear plant owners face new consultation requirements, fees, and mandatory contributions to community funds. The Nuclear Regulatory Commission takes on a stronger approval role and must run the new grant programs.
ai_headline: Nuclear plant closures would require state approval under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6613 gives states formal approval power over nuclear plant shutdowns and funds community boards near closing plants.
ai_sections.what: HR 6613 changes how nuclear power plants are shut down and cleaned up by requiring plant owners to consult with state, tribal, and local governments before submitting a decommissioning plan. States within 50 miles of a plant gain a formal role, and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission would have to officially approve shutdown plans rather than simply review them. States may support, conditionally support, or oppose a plan, and recommended changes must be seriously considered unless they violate the law or cost far more than they are worth.
ai_sections.who: Residents near roughly two dozen nuclear plants currently being decommissioned or expected to close are directly affected, with extra protections for small, rural, and lower-income communities. State and tribal governments gain formal influence over cleanup decisions, while nuclear plant owners face new consultation requirements, fees, and mandatory fund contributions.
ai_sections.why: The Nuclear Regulatory Commission takes on a stronger approval role and must administer new grant programs, expanding its existing responsibilities. Plant owners face additional financial obligations and process requirements that did not previously exist under decommissioning rules.
ai_sections.cost: Approximately $12.5 million in short-term grants would go to states and tribal governments to fund local advisory boards near closing plants. Nuclear plant owners would pay $500,000 per plant into a long-term fund supporting those community boards, and local governments storing spent fuel would receive $15 per kilogram of that waste. Plant owners must also set aside 2 percent of their cleanup savings funds into accounts directed to local communities for economic development planning.
costChips: $12.5M in grants to states and tribes
$15 per kg of stored spent fuel

title: Ceasefire Compliance Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would require the U.S. government to regularly check whether Israel is following the terms of a ceasefire agreement reached on October 10, 2025, as well as a broader 20-point peace plan. Every 90 days, the Secretary of State would have to submit a public report to Congress certifying whether Israel is meeting a list of specific conditions. Those conditions include things like not violating the ceasefire in Gaza, allowing enough humanitarian aid into Gaza, not forcing Palestinian civilians to leave, not annexing land in the West Bank or Gaza, and taking steps to stop settler violence against Palestinians. If Israel is found to be breaking any of those conditions, the U.S. would be blocked from selling, exporting, or transferring American-made weapons to Israel for use in the West Bank or Gaza. The bill also sets up a monitoring group to track whether U.S.-supplied weapons are actually being used in those areas.

There are some important exceptions built into the bill. It would not stop the U.S. from helping Israel defend itself against missile attacks, and it specifically keeps funding flowing for Israeli missile defense systems like Iron Dome, David's Sling, and Arrow 3. The President could also override a weapons ban in specific cases by certifying to Congress that doing so is vital to U.S. national security. The entire law would expire automatically five years after it is signed.

This bill primarily affects U.S. government agencies involved in foreign policy and defense, American defense contractors that sell weapons to Israel, and the Israeli government. It would also have indirect effects on Palestinians living in the West Bank and Gaza, as it is designed to use U.S. weapons sales as leverage to encourage humanitarian access and compliance with the ceasefire. American taxpayers and aid organizations working in the region could also be affected by the humanitarian provisions.
ai_headline: Bill would cut weapons sales to Israel over ceasefire violations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7645 blocks U.S. weapons transfers to Israel if the Secretary of State finds ceasefire or peace plan violations every 90 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 7645 requires the Secretary of State to submit a public report to Congress every 90 days certifying whether Israel is meeting specific conditions tied to a ceasefire agreement and a 20-point peace plan. Those conditions include not violating the ceasefire in Gaza, allowing humanitarian aid in, not displacing Palestinian civilians, not annexing West Bank or Gaza land, and acting to stop settler violence. If Israel is found non-compliant, the U.S. would be blocked from selling, exporting, or transferring American-made weapons to Israel for use in the West Bank or Gaza.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects U.S. foreign policy and defense agencies, American defense contractors that sell weapons to Israel, and the Israeli government. Palestinians in the West Bank and Gaza, American taxpayers, and aid organizations working in the region are also affected by its provisions.
ai_sections.why: The bill uses the threat of weapons restrictions to press for humanitarian access and adherence to the ceasefire. Exceptions preserve U.S. support for Israeli missile defense systems including Iron Dome, David's Sling, and Arrow 3, and the President can override a weapons ban by certifying to Congress it is vital to national security. The law would expire automatically five years after signing.
ai_sections.cost: The bill maintains funding for Israeli missile defense systems including Iron Dome, David's Sling, and Arrow 3, though the summary does not specify dollar amounts.

title: VERIFY CDL Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require anyone applying for or renewing a commercial driver's license (CDL) to first be checked through E-Verify, a federal online system that confirms whether a person is legally allowed to work in the United States. Right now, states issue CDLs based on driving tests, medical requirements, and similar criteria. This bill would add a new step: states could not issue or renew a CDL unless the applicant passes an E-Verify work authorization check.

A CDL is the license required to drive large trucks, buses, and other commercial vehicles professionally. This bill would affect anyone seeking a CDL for the first time or renewing an existing one. People who cannot be verified as work-authorized through E-Verify would be denied a CDL or unable to renew theirs. It would also place a new responsibility on state licensing agencies to run these checks as part of their standard process.

The bill would most directly impact workers in trucking, transportation, and other industries that require a CDL. It could also affect employers in those industries who rely on CDL holders. The E-Verify system is run by the federal government and is already used by many employers to check new hires, but this bill would extend its use specifically to the CDL licensing process itself.
ai_headline: New CDL rule would require E-Verify check before license is issued or renewed
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7200 would bar states from issuing or renewing a commercial driver's license without a federal E-Verify work authorization check.
ai_sections.what: HR 7200 would require all applicants for a new or renewed commercial driver's license to pass an E-Verify work authorization check before a CDL can be issued. E-Verify is a federal online system already used by many employers to confirm whether a person is legally allowed to work in the United States. States would be prohibited from issuing or renewing a CDL unless the applicant clears this check.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects anyone applying for or renewing a CDL, which is required to professionally drive large trucks, buses, and other commercial vehicles. It also places a new obligation on state licensing agencies to run E-Verify checks as part of their standard CDL process.
ai_sections.why: Applicants who cannot be verified as work-authorized through E-Verify would be denied a CDL or unable to renew an existing one. Employers in trucking, transportation, and other CDL-dependent industries could be affected by changes in the pool of licensed drivers.

title: National Bridge Funding Reform Act
ai_summary:
This bill would shut down two existing federal transportation programs and use that money to create a new program focused specifically on fixing and replacing bridges. The two programs being eliminated are the Carbon Reduction Program, which currently funds projects that cut transportation-related emissions, and the PROTECT Program, which funds projects that make roads and bridges more resistant to natural disasters and extreme weather. All the money those programs received would instead go into a new National Bridge Program.

The new program sends money to each state based mainly on how much bridge surface area that state has. Specifically, 75 percent of the funding is divided among states based on the total square footage of bridge deck they have on major federal highways. The other 25 percent goes to states based on how much of their bridge deck area is rated in poor condition. States can use the money to repair, preserve, or build bridges on major federal highways, and they also have the option to spend it on smaller local bridges if they choose.

This bill mostly affects state transportation departments, which would gain a dedicated new source of bridge funding but lose access to the climate and disaster-resilience grant programs. Drivers and communities in states with a lot of bridge infrastructure, or with many deteriorating bridges, would likely see the most benefit. Communities or projects that were counting on the eliminated programs for emissions-reduction or storm-resilience work would no longer have access to those funds.
ai_headline: House bill scraps two federal programs to fund bridge repairs nationwide
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7442 ends climate and disaster-resilience road programs, redirecting all their funding to a new National Bridge Program.
ai_sections.what: HR 7442 would eliminate the Carbon Reduction Program and the PROTECT Program, then redirect all their funding into a new National Bridge Program. States would receive money based on bridge deck square footage (75 percent) and the share of bridge deck in poor condition (25 percent). Funds may be used to repair, preserve, or build bridges on major federal highways, with an option to spend on local bridges.
ai_sections.who: State transportation departments would gain a dedicated bridge funding source but lose access to the two eliminated programs. Drivers and communities in states with extensive or deteriorating bridge infrastructure would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Communities and projects that relied on the Carbon Reduction Program would lose access to emissions-reduction funding. Projects depending on PROTECT for storm and disaster resilience would similarly lose that dedicated funding stream.
ai_sections.cost: The new National Bridge Program would be funded entirely by the money previously allocated to the Carbon Reduction Program and the PROTECT Program, with no new spending identified in the bill.

title: FREEDOM Act
ai_summary:
This bill aims to speed up the federal government's process for approving energy and mining projects. Right now, companies that want to build things like pipelines, power plants, mines, solar farms, or carbon capture facilities often wait years for the government to review and approve their applications. This bill sets strict deadlines for those approvals. Routine permits would need to be decided within 90 days, and more complex ones within one to two years. If a government agency misses its deadline, that counts as an illegal delay, and the company can take the agency to court on a fast-track schedule with a decision required within 120 days.

The bill also creates a voluntary insurance-like program called the De-Risking Compensation Program. Energy companies can pay an annual fee of about 1.5 percent of their project costs to enroll. If a federal agency then cancels a permit, misses deadlines, or otherwise causes the project to collapse, the company can sue in a special federal court and potentially recover the money they lost. The program is funded by those company premiums plus money from Congress. Companies need to have invested at least five million dollars in a project to qualify for a payout.

The bill also limits the government's ability to stop projects that have already received most of their permits. Agencies could only halt such a project if there is a clear and immediate harm with no other workable solution. Outside groups challenging approved projects in court would face tighter filing deadlines and stricter rules for getting a project stopped. The bill covers a broad range of energy types, including oil, gas, renewables, geothermal, and mining, so it affects energy companies, mining businesses, federal agencies, environmental groups, and potentially anyone who lives near these projects.
ai_headline: Federal energy permits would face strict deadlines under HR 7329
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7329 sets 90-day to 2-year permit deadlines for energy and mining projects and lets companies sue agencies that miss them.
ai_sections.what: HR 7329 sets mandatory deadlines for federal approval of energy and mining projects, ranging from 90 days for routine permits to one to two years for complex ones. Companies can sue agencies that miss deadlines on a fast-track court schedule requiring a decision within 120 days. The bill also creates the De-Risking Compensation Program, an optional insurance-like system that lets enrolled companies seek recovery in federal court if a permit is canceled or delayed.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects energy companies, mining businesses, and federal agencies that review permits. Environmental groups, outside challengers, and people living near affected projects are also subject to its new filing deadlines and stricter rules for stopping approved projects.
ai_sections.why: Agencies that miss deadlines face automatic legal exposure, and courts are required to resolve those cases within 120 days, shifting how regulatory disputes over energy projects are handled. Outside groups would face tighter rules for filing legal challenges and for obtaining court orders that halt projects already holding most of their permits.
ai_sections.cost: Companies that enroll in the De-Risking Compensation Program pay an annual fee of approximately 1.5 percent of their project costs. The program is funded by those company premiums combined with congressional appropriations, and companies must have invested at least five million dollars in a project to qualify for a payout.

title: Counter Drone State and Local Defender Act
ai_summary:
Right now, only certain federal agencies are allowed to track, jam, or take down drones that pose a threat to public safety. This bill would change that by letting state, local, tribal, and territorial police departments use similar anti-drone technology. The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) would be in charge of approving which agencies can participate, what equipment they can use, and what training they need before they can act. Agencies would have to apply, get approved, and follow strict rules set by the FAA.

The bill sets up the program in stages. First, there would be a trial period lasting about three years, during which up to 4,000 law enforcement agencies could be approved to use anti-drone systems near high-risk locations like stadiums, government buildings, or other sensitive sites. There is also a separate, faster-tracked version of the program specifically for cities hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup. After the trial period ends, a permanent program would open up to any qualifying law enforcement agency that registers with the FAA. Agencies could use existing federal grant money to pay for the equipment and training.

The bill affects local and state police departments across the country, as well as tribal and territorial law enforcement agencies. It also affects drone operators, since their aircraft could be tracked, controlled, or disabled by local authorities if deemed a credible threat. Privacy protections are included, requiring that any communications intercepted from drones be handled in line with the First and Fourth Amendments and deleted after a threat is handled unless needed for a legal investigation.
ai_headline: Local police could gain authority to disable threatening drones under new federal bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7525 would let up to 4,000 state and local law enforcement agencies use anti-drone technology, subject to FAA approval and training rules.
ai_sections.what: HR 7525 would expand authority to track, jam, and disable threatening drones beyond federal agencies to include state, local, tribal, and territorial law enforcement. The FAA would approve participating agencies, permitted equipment, and required training. A three-year pilot program would precede a permanent open-enrollment program.
ai_sections.who: State, local, tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies across the country would be eligible to apply. Drone operators would also be directly affected, as their aircraft could be tracked, controlled, or disabled by local authorities if deemed a credible threat.
ai_sections.why: Expanding anti-drone authority to thousands of additional agencies increases the number of entities with power to intercept or disable aircraft. Drone operators near approved jurisdictions would face a broader range of authorities with legal power to act against their aircraft.
ai_sections.cost: Agencies could use existing federal grant money to cover the cost of anti-drone equipment and training.

title: Railroad Safety and Accountability Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes the Railroad Safety Advisory Committee a permanent, official part of federal law. This committee already exists, but right now it operates informally. Writing it into law means it cannot simply be dissolved by an administration without Congress changing the law. The committee's job is to advise the Federal Railroad Administration on creating and updating safety rules for railroads across the country.

The committee brings together a wide range of groups to work on railroad safety issues together. Members would include freight and passenger railroad companies, Amtrak, local governments, passenger groups, and multiple types of railroad workers such as engineers, maintenance crews, and dispatchers. The head of the Federal Railroad Administration would be required to meet with the committee every three months, and the committee would have to send Congress a report each year on what it has been doing.

This bill mainly affects the railroad industry, railroad workers, and federal regulators. Indirectly, it could affect anyone who rides trains or lives near railroad lines, since the committee's work is focused on improving safety rules. The committee does not make laws or final regulations itself. It gives recommendations that the Federal Railroad Administration can choose to follow when deciding on new or updated safety rules.
ai_headline: Bill would make railroad safety advisory panel permanent in federal law
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7338 writes the Railroad Safety Advisory Committee into federal law, shielding it from being dissolved without an act of Congress.
ai_sections.what: HR 7338 would codify the Railroad Safety Advisory Committee into permanent federal law. The committee currently exists informally and advises the Federal Railroad Administration on creating and updating railroad safety rules. Codifying it means no administration could dissolve it without Congress first changing the law.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the railroad industry, railroad workers, and federal regulators. Anyone who rides trains or lives near railroad lines could be indirectly affected, since the committee focuses on improving safety rules.
ai_sections.why: Making the committee permanent in law gives it protections against being eliminated by executive action alone. The committee's advice could shape future safety regulations, though it issues recommendations only and does not have authority to create binding rules.

title: Army Security Agency Monument Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the Secretary of the Army to place a monument at Arlington National Cemetery honoring veterans of the Army Security Agency (ASA), a military intelligence unit that gathered signals and communications intelligence. The monument would honor those who served in the ASA between 1945 and 1976, covering World War II, the Korean War, the Vietnam War, and the Cold War. If no suitable spot is available inside the cemetery, the monument would be placed just outside the entrance instead.

The bill sets some specific rules for the monument. It must be small, no larger than about 52 by 18 inches at the base, and placed on a non-burial area. The design has to be approved by both the Army and the National Army Security Agency Association, a veterans organization. Importantly, the federal government would not pay for it. The National Army Security Agency Association would be responsible for covering all costs of building, installing, and maintaining the monument through a formal agreement with the Army.

This bill mainly affects ASA veterans and their families, who would finally have a dedicated public memorial recognizing their service. Because much of the ASA's work was classified, these veterans often could not speak openly about what they did, meaning their contributions went largely unrecognized for decades. The bill does not require taxpayer funding and has no direct financial impact on the general public.
ai_headline: Bill would place ASA veterans monument at Arlington at no federal cost
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8211 requires an Arlington National Cemetery monument for Army Security Agency veterans (1945–1976), fully funded by a veterans group.
ai_sections.what: HR 8211 would require the Secretary of the Army to place a monument at Arlington National Cemetery honoring veterans who served in the Army Security Agency (ASA) between 1945 and 1976. The monument must be small, no larger than approximately 52 by 18 inches at the base, and placed in a non-burial area. If no suitable location exists inside the cemetery, it would be placed just outside the entrance.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects veterans of the Army Security Agency and their families. The National Army Security Agency Association, a veterans organization, would be responsible for the monument's design approval, funding, and maintenance.
ai_sections.why: Because much of the ASA's work involved classified signals and communications intelligence, veterans were often unable to speak publicly about their service, leaving their contributions unrecognized for decades. This monument would create a dedicated public memorial for that service across World War II, the Korean War, the Vietnam War, and the Cold War.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would not pay for the monument. The National Army Security Agency Association would cover all costs of building, installing, and maintaining it through a formal agreement with the Army.

title: Small Nonprofit Retirement Security Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill fixes a gap in existing tax law. Right now, small businesses can get federal tax credits to help cover the cost of setting up a retirement plan for their workers, and another credit if they automatically enroll employees in that plan. The problem is those credits only help employers who actually pay income taxes. Nonprofit organizations, like charities and religious groups, are tax-exempt, so they could not use those credits even if they qualified in every other way. This bill changes the law so that eligible small nonprofits can claim the same credits, applied against the payroll taxes they do pay, rather than against income taxes they don't owe.
