ai_sections.why: Removing the EPA's existing authority to grant monitoring exemptions and adding community-triggered maintenance requests would change how compliance is enforced at the facility level. Expanded public data requirements, including multilingual online posting within one week of collection, would increase the volume and accessibility of emissions information available to affected communities.
ai_sections.cost: Congress would need to approve approximately $271 million over two years to fund the program.
costChips: Covers EPA, state, and local roles

title: To direct the Architect of the Capitol to install an adult changing room in the Library of Congress and the Capitol Visitor Center.
ai_summary:
This bill would require the Architect of the Capitol, the official who manages and maintains Capitol Hill buildings, to install at least one adult changing room in both the Library of Congress and the Capitol Visitor Center. An adult changing room is a specially equipped private restroom that includes a large changing bench, safety rails, a hoist or lift device, and enough space for one or two caregivers to assist. These are sometimes called "Changing Places" facilities.

The people most affected are adults with significant physical disabilities who cannot use standard accessible restrooms on their own and need a caregiver's help, as well as the family members, aides, and nurses who assist them. Standard ADA-compliant restrooms were not designed with this level of care in mind, so people needing this type of assistance often have no safe or dignified option when visiting public buildings.

Right now these facilities do not exist in those two locations, which means some people with disabilities effectively cannot visit the Library of Congress or the Capitol Visitor Center. If passed, this bill would change that by directing a specific government official to make the installations happen, though it does not set a deadline or specify a budget for the work.
ai_headline: Bill would require adult changing rooms at two Capitol Hill venues
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7614 directs the Architect of the Capitol to install adult changing rooms at the Library of Congress and Capitol Visitor Center.
ai_sections.what: HR 7614 would require the Architect of the Capitol to install at least one adult changing room in both the Library of Congress and the Capitol Visitor Center. These facilities, sometimes called "Changing Places," include a large changing bench, safety rails, a hoist or lift device, and space for one or two caregivers. The bill does not set a deadline or specify a budget for the installations.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects adults with significant physical disabilities who cannot use standard accessible restrooms without caregiver assistance. It also affects the family members, aides, and nurses who help those individuals during visits to these locations.
ai_sections.why: Standard ADA-compliant restrooms were not designed to accommodate this level of physical care, leaving some visitors with no safe or dignified option at these two locations. Without these facilities, some people with disabilities are effectively unable to visit the Library of Congress or the Capitol Visitor Center.

title: No Tax on Overtime for All Workers Act
ai_summary:
This bill would let workers subtract their overtime pay from their taxable income when they file federal taxes. Right now, overtime wages are taxed just like regular wages. Under this bill, the extra money you earn for working more than 40 hours a week would not count as taxable income, meaning you would owe less in federal income taxes. The bill also covers certain workers in industries like airlines and railroads, where overtime is calculated differently based on union contracts rather than a strict 40-hour week rule.

The bill applies to a broad range of workers. It covers anyone who gets overtime pay required by the Fair Labor Standards Act, which is the federal law that mandates time-and-a-half pay for hours worked beyond 40 in a week. It also covers workers whose overtime arrangements are set through agreements with their employer or a union, as long as those agreements were made before the extra work was performed. This means both hourly workers and certain contracted employees could benefit.

If passed, the change would take effect starting with the 2025 tax year, so workers would see the benefit when they file taxes in early 2026. People who regularly work overtime, such as those in manufacturing, healthcare, transportation, and construction, would likely feel the biggest impact. The federal government would collect less tax revenue as a result, since a portion of workers' income would no longer be taxed.
ai_headline: Overtime pay would become tax-free under House bill HR 5475
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5475 would let workers deduct overtime pay from federal taxable income starting with the 2025 tax year.
ai_sections.what: HR 5475 would allow workers to subtract overtime pay from their federally taxable income, so hours worked beyond 40 per week would no longer be counted as taxable wages. The bill covers overtime required under the Fair Labor Standards Act as well as overtime arranged through employer or union agreements made before the extra work was performed. It would take effect starting with the 2025 tax year, meaning workers would first see the benefit when filing taxes in early 2026.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to anyone who receives overtime pay under the Fair Labor Standards Act, including hourly workers and certain contracted employees. Workers in industries such as manufacturing, healthcare, transportation, and construction are identified as those most likely to be affected, along with airline and railroad workers whose overtime is calculated under union contracts.
ai_sections.why: Because overtime income would no longer be counted as taxable, workers who regularly log extra hours would owe less in federal income taxes. The federal government would collect less tax revenue as a result, since a portion of workers' income would be removed from the taxable base.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would collect less tax revenue because a portion of workers' overtime income would no longer be subject to federal income tax.
costChips: Overtime income removed from tax base

title: Build HUBS Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes it easier for builders and developers to get low-cost federal loans for housing and mixed-use projects built near train stations, bus rapid transit lines, and other public transit hubs. It updates two existing federal loan programs, the TIFIA program (which funds transportation infrastructure) and the RRIF program (which funds railroad projects), by expanding what types of projects qualify for financing. Under this bill, developers could use these federal loan programs to build apartments, offices, and community spaces within a half-mile of a transit stop, as long as the project helps bring in more riders or revenue for the transit system. Projects focused on affordable or middle-income housing would get especially favorable loan terms, including interest rates cut in half compared to standard rates.

The bill also tries to make the application and approval process faster and less burdensome. It creates a system where approved private lenders can handle much of the loan processing on behalf of the federal government, similar to how HUD already handles certain housing loans. It also removes some environmental review requirements for specific project types, like converting old office buildings into apartments or building on land already used for transportation purposes. Developers would no longer need a formal investment-grade credit rating in all cases, making it easier for smaller or newer projects to qualify.

The bill would most directly affect real estate developers, transit agencies, local governments, and private lenders who work on housing or mixed-use construction near public transit. It could also impact renters and homebuyers if more housing gets built in transit-accessible areas as a result. The bill does not override any local or state zoning laws, so communities still control what can be built in their areas.
ai_headline: Federal loan programs expanded to finance housing near transit stops
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7062 lets developers use federal transit loans to build housing and offices within a half-mile of transit stops.
ai_sections.what: HR 7062 expands two existing federal loan programs, TIFIA and RRIF, to cover housing and mixed-use projects built within a half-mile of a transit stop. Qualifying projects must help increase riders or revenue for the transit system. Projects focused on affordable or middle-income housing would receive interest rates cut in half compared to standard rates.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects real estate developers, transit agencies, local governments, and private lenders working on housing or mixed-use construction near public transit. Renters and homebuyers in transit-accessible areas could also be affected if more housing is built as a result.
ai_sections.why: Allowing approved private lenders to process loans on behalf of the federal government, removing some environmental review requirements, and eliminating the investment-grade credit rating requirement in certain cases would change how projects are reviewed and who can apply. Because the bill does not override local or state zoning laws, communities retain control over what can be built in their areas.

title: HOWIE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require railroad companies to report accidents and incidents to the federal government when they have a "reasonable suspicion" that something their company did caused the damage. Right now, railroads have to report certain accidents, but this bill specifically adds incidents like fires alongside train tracks, including brush fires, to the list of things that must be reported. The Secretary of Transportation would write the specific rules spelling out how and when these reports need to be made.

The bill is aimed at closing a gap in current reporting requirements. If a train sparks a fire along the tracks, for example, and the railroad company reasonably suspects its own operations caused it, the company would be legally required to report that to federal regulators. The key phrase is "reasonable suspicion," meaning the railroad does not have to be 100 percent certain it caused the damage, just have good reason to think so.

This bill mainly affects railroad companies operating in the United States, as they would take on new reporting responsibilities. It also affects the Federal Railroad Administration, which would need to create the actual rules. Indirectly, it could benefit people who live near railroad tracks, since better reporting could lead to faster emergency responses and greater accountability when railroad operations cause fires or other damage to surrounding areas.
ai_headline: Railroad companies would have to report track fires under reasonable suspicion standard
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8012 requires railroads to report fires and other incidents to federal regulators whenever they reasonably suspect their operations caused the damage.
ai_sections.what: HR 8012 would require railroad companies to report accidents and incidents to the federal government when they have reasonable suspicion their operations caused the damage. The bill specifically adds fires alongside train tracks, including brush fires, to the list of reportable incidents. The Secretary of Transportation would write the rules spelling out how and when these reports must be made.
ai_sections.who: Railroad companies operating in the United States would take on new reporting responsibilities under this bill. The Federal Railroad Administration would also be affected, as it would need to create the actual reporting rules.
ai_sections.why: The bill is aimed at closing a gap in current reporting requirements for railroad-caused fires and damage. Better reporting could lead to faster emergency responses and greater accountability for people living near railroad tracks.

title: Real Education and Access for Healthy Youth Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a federal grant program to fund comprehensive sex education in schools, colleges, and community organizations across the country. The Department of Health and Human Services would hand out up to $100 million per year from 2026 to 2031 to schools, universities, health departments, and nonprofits. The money would pay for teaching materials, educator training, and direct health services for young people ages 10 to 29. To receive funding, programs must be medically accurate, age-appropriate, and cover topics like puberty, contraception, STIs, consent, healthy relationships, and gender identity. The bill also repeals the existing federal abstinence-only education program and redirects its unspent funds toward these new grants.

The bill focuses special attention on groups it identifies as underserved, including low-income youth, LGBTQ+ youth, youth in foster care or the justice system, young people experiencing homelessness, and youth from communities of color. Grant money would be prioritized for schools and colleges that serve these populations. Health services funded under the bill could include contraception, STI testing and treatment, mental health services, and support for survivors of interpersonal violence. Programs that withhold health information, promote stereotypes, or are medically inaccurate would not be eligible for funding.

This bill would most directly affect students from middle school through college age, their teachers and school administrators, and community health organizations. Schools and colleges would not be required to participate, but those that apply and qualify could receive five-year grants to build or improve their sex education programs. Parents, educators, and local communities would still make decisions about whether to apply for funding, but any program that accepts the money would have to meet the federal standards spelled out in the bill.
ai_headline: Federal bill would fund sex ed grants and end abstinence-only program
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3527 offers up to $100M/year for medically accurate sex ed while repealing the federal abstinence-only program.
ai_sections.what: HR 3527 would create a federal grant program providing up to $100 million per year from 2026 to 2031 for sex education in schools, colleges, and community organizations. Funded programs must be medically accurate, age-appropriate, and cover topics including puberty, contraception, STIs, consent, healthy relationships, and gender identity. The bill also repeals the existing federal abstinence-only education program and redirects its unspent funds to the new grants.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects students from middle school through college age, their teachers and administrators, and community health organizations. Schools and colleges are not required to participate but may apply for five-year grants; any program accepting funds must meet the federal standards in the bill.
ai_sections.why: Programs that accept funding would be required to follow specific federal content standards, limiting local discretion over curriculum for participating schools and organizations. Groups identified as underserved — including low-income youth, LGBTQ+ youth, youth in foster care or the justice system, youth experiencing homelessness, and youth from communities of color — would receive prioritized consideration for grant awards.
ai_sections.cost: The Department of Health and Human Services would distribute up to $100 million per year between 2026 and 2031. Unspent funds from the repealed abstinence-only program would also be redirected toward these grants.

title: MTS CYBER Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the Government Accountability Office (GAO), which is the independent watchdog agency of Congress, to study whether the U.S. Coast Guard has enough money, staff, and tools to protect America's ports and waterways from cyberattacks. The Coast Guard was recently given new legal responsibilities to oversee cybersecurity for ships, ports, and related infrastructure, and this bill asks for an outside review to find out if the agency can actually handle that job. The GAO would have 270 days after the bill becomes law to complete the review and send its findings to several congressional committees.

The review would look at three specific things: whether the Coast Guard's budget is enough to hire and train cybersecurity workers and enforce the rules, whether Coast Guard staff can actually check that ports and shipping companies are following cybersecurity requirements, and whether the guidance given to businesses in the maritime industry is clear and useful. Congress is concerned because the Coast Guard's responsibilities in this area have grown significantly, but funding may not have kept pace.

This bill mostly affects the Coast Guard as an institution, along with the ports, shipping companies, terminals, and vessels that are already subject to federal cybersecurity rules. Everyday Americans are indirectly affected because the marine transportation system handles about 41 percent of the country's international trade, meaning disruptions from a cyberattack could affect prices and the supply of goods. The bill does not create new regulations or spend new money; it simply orders a study and a report.
ai_headline: GAO ordered to review Coast Guard's cybersecurity capacity at ports
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Transportation and Maritime Security.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7625 orders a GAO study on whether the Coast Guard has enough funding and staff to enforce maritime cybersecurity rules.
ai_sections.what: HR 7625 directs the GAO to review whether the U.S. Coast Guard has sufficient budget, personnel, and tools to fulfill its cybersecurity responsibilities over ships, ports, and related infrastructure. The GAO must complete the review and report to several congressional committees within 270 days of the bill becoming law. The bill does not create new regulations or authorize new spending.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the Coast Guard and the ports, shipping companies, terminals, and vessels already subject to federal cybersecurity rules. Everyday Americans are indirectly affected because the marine transportation system handles about 41 percent of the country's international trade.
ai_sections.why: The Coast Guard's cybersecurity responsibilities have grown significantly but funding may not have kept pace, raising questions about the agency's ability to enforce the rules. A cyberattack disrupting the marine transportation system could affect prices and the supply of goods for American consumers.

title: National Transit Frontline Workforce Training Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Department of Transportation to create a National Transit Workforce Center. The government would award a grant to a qualified nonprofit organization to run the center. That organization would be responsible for developing training programs, educational materials, and technical help for public transit agencies across the country, covering city bus systems, suburban routes, rural services, and transit systems serving tribal communities.

The center's main job would be to help transit agencies recruit, hire, train, and keep frontline workers, meaning the drivers, operators, and maintenance staff who run day-to-day transit operations. It would also focus on preparing workers to use new and emerging technologies, track workforce trends through data analysis, and share that information with transit providers to help them plan for the future.

This bill would affect public transit agencies of all sizes nationwide, the workers they employ, and the communities that depend on those services. Riders could indirectly benefit if better-trained transit workers lead to safer and more reliable service. The bill also requires the center to work with labor representatives and transit agency leadership together, meaning both workers and management would have a voice in shaping training programs.
ai_headline: Federal bill would create national training center for public transit workers
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8068 directs the U.S. DOT to fund a nonprofit-run center that trains and supports frontline public transit workers nationwide.
ai_sections.what: HR 8068 directs the U.S. Department of Transportation to establish a National Transit Workforce Center by awarding a grant to a qualified nonprofit organization. The center would develop training programs, educational materials, and technical assistance for public transit agencies of all sizes, including city, suburban, rural, and tribal systems. Its responsibilities would include workforce recruitment and retention support, preparation for emerging technologies, and data-driven workforce trend tracking.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects public transit agencies nationwide, their frontline employees such as drivers, operators, and maintenance staff, and the communities that rely on those services. Both labor representatives and transit agency management would have a role in shaping the center's training programs.
ai_sections.why: If better-trained transit workers result from the center's programs, riders could see indirect effects on service safety and reliability. The bill also establishes a formal structure requiring cooperation between workers and management in developing training, which could shape how workforce decisions are made across the industry.

title: Rural Veterans Transportation to Care Act
ai_summary:
This bill updates an existing Department of Veterans Affairs grant program that helps pay for transportation services for veterans living in rural areas. Right now, the program only covers veterans in "highly rural" areas, but this bill would expand it to also include veterans in areas simply classified as "rural." It also removes the old spending cap of $3 million per year and replaces it with open-ended funding, meaning Congress would approve whatever amount is needed each year.

The bill also changes who can apply for these transportation grants. Currently, only state veterans service agencies and veterans service organizations can apply. Under this bill, county veterans service organizations and tribal organizations would also be eligible to apply for funding. Grant amounts could go up to $60,000, or up to $80,000 if the organization needs to buy a vehicle that meets Americans with Disabilities Act requirements for accessibility.

This bill would most directly affect veterans who live in rural parts of the United States and have trouble getting to VA medical facilities or other care. It would also affect the local and tribal organizations that serve those veterans, since more of them would qualify for grant money to run transportation programs. The changes are intended to make it easier for rural veterans, including those on tribal lands, to get rides to their medical appointments.
ai_headline: VA rural veteran transport grants expanded to reach more communities
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1733 expands VA transport grants to rural veterans and adds county and tribal groups as eligible applicants.
ai_sections.what: HR 1733 expands an existing VA grant program that funds transportation services for veterans in rural areas. The bill broadens eligibility from only "highly rural" areas to include all "rural" areas, and raises individual grant amounts to up to $60,000, or up to $80,000 when an ADA-compliant vehicle must be purchased. It also removes the current $3 million annual spending cap and replaces it with open-ended annual appropriations.
ai_sections.who: Veterans living in rural areas, including those on tribal lands, who have difficulty reaching VA medical facilities would be directly affected. County veterans service organizations and tribal organizations would become newly eligible to apply for grants, joining state veterans service agencies and veterans service organizations already allowed under current law.
ai_sections.why: Removing the spending cap and broadening area eligibility could result in more federal dollars flowing to rural transportation programs than currently permitted. Adding county and tribal organizations as eligible applicants increases the number of entities that can seek funding to run those programs.
ai_sections.cost: The bill removes the existing $3 million annual cap and shifts to open-ended funding approved by Congress each year. Individual grants may reach $60,000, or $80,000 when an ADA-accessible vehicle purchase is required.

title: Railway Safety Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill is called the Railway Safety Act of 2026. It is designed to make trains safer, especially trains that carry dangerous materials like flammable liquids, toxic gases, explosives, and radioactive waste. The bill sets new speed limits for these trains, requires railroads to use stronger and safer tank cars by the end of 2027, and demands that railroads share real-time information about what hazardous materials are on each train with emergency responders and local officials. It also requires railroads to create detailed emergency response plans and to install more equipment along the tracks that can detect problems with train wheels and other parts before those problems cause accidents.

The bill also addresses how trains are operated and inspected. It requires most freight trains run by the largest railroads to have at least two crew members, a conductor and an engineer, on board. It increases how often trains and their equipment must be inspected, and it says railroads cannot rush workers through safety inspections. Fines for breaking rail safety rules would go up significantly, reaching as high as five million dollars for violations that cause death or serious injury. The bill also orders studies into problems like trains blocking road crossings for long periods of time, and into better protections for railroad workers on the tracks.

The people most affected by this bill include railroad companies and their workers, communities located near rail lines where hazardous materials travel, local emergency responders like firefighters and police who would respond to a train accident, and federal agencies like the Federal Railroad Administration that oversee rail safety. Everyday Americans who live near freight rail routes, especially those carrying dangerous cargo, could see increased safety protections as a result of the requirements in this bill.
ai_headline: Rail safety bill sets speed limits and crew rules for hazmat trains
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7748 tightens hazmat train speed, crew, inspection, and equipment rules and raises fines up to $5M for deadly violations.
ai_sections.what: HR 7748, the Railway Safety Act of 2026, sets new speed limits for trains carrying hazardous materials, requires stronger tank cars by end of 2027, and mandates real-time sharing of hazmat data with emergency responders. Most freight trains run by the largest railroads must carry at least two crew members, and inspection frequency must increase without pressuring workers to rush. Railroads must also install more trackside equipment to detect wheel and mechanical problems before accidents occur.
ai_sections.who: Railroad companies, their workers, and communities near hazmat rail routes are most directly affected. Local emergency responders, federal agencies including the Federal Railroad Administration, and everyday Americans living near freight rail lines are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Raising fines to as much as five million dollars for violations causing death or serious injury could change how railroads weigh compliance costs against operational pressures. Communities near hazmat routes would face altered emergency response logistics as railroads are required to produce detailed emergency response plans and share real-time cargo data.
ai_sections.cost: Fines for rail safety violations would increase significantly, reaching as high as five million dollars for violations that cause death or serious injury.
costChips: Fines up to $5M for deadly violations
Triggered by death or serious injury

title: Plastic Pellet Free Waters Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to create new rules banning plastic pellets from being released into waterways. Plastic pellets, also called "nurdles," are tiny bead-like pieces of plastic that factories use as the raw material to make plastic products. Right now, these pellets sometimes spill or wash into rivers, lakes, and oceans during manufacturing, shipping, and packaging. The bill would give the EPA 60 days after passage to put a final rule in place making that discharge illegal.

The ban would apply to any facility that makes, uses, packages, or transports these pellets. That includes plastic production plants, molding factories, and businesses along the supply chain. The new rules would be written into the water permits those facilities already have to hold under the Clean Water Act, so there would be a legal enforcement mechanism already built in.

The people most directly affected are companies in the plastics industry, who would need to prevent pellet spills and runoff from reaching waterways. Communities near plastic manufacturing or shipping hubs, as well as people who rely on nearby water for fishing, recreation, or drinking, could see environmental benefits if the rule is enforced. Consumers who buy plastic products would not face any direct requirements under this bill.
ai_headline: EPA would ban plastic pellet discharges into waterways under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7543 gives the EPA 60 days to finalize rules banning plastic pellet discharges from facilities into U.S. waterways.
ai_sections.what: HR 7543 would require the EPA to issue a final rule within 60 days of passage banning the discharge of plastic pellets into waterways. The ban would cover any facility that makes, uses, packages, or transports these pellets. Enforcement would run through existing Clean Water Act water permits those facilities already hold.
ai_sections.who: Plastic production plants, molding factories, and other businesses along the plastics supply chain would be directly regulated. Communities near manufacturing or shipping hubs and people who use nearby water for fishing, recreation, or drinking would be affected by whether the rule is enforced.
ai_sections.why: Plastic pellets currently spill or wash into rivers, lakes, and oceans during manufacturing, shipping, and packaging. Requiring facilities to prevent that discharge could affect water quality in areas near plastics industry operations.

title: Safe Intersections for Buses and Pedestrians Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the federal government to create new safety standards for motorcoaches, which are large buses used for things like charter trips, school groups, and long-distance travel. Specifically, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration would have one year to write rules requiring that all newly manufactured motorcoaches sold in the United States give the driver a clear, unobstructed view of the road directly in front of the bus. The idea is that large buses often have wide front frames and dashboards that can block a driver's view of pedestrians, cyclists, or other hazards at intersections and crosswalks.

The bill allows bus manufacturers to meet this requirement in different ways. Instead of redesigning the physical structure of the bus, a company could install cameras or other technology that give the driver a clear view of areas that would otherwise be blocked. Any camera system used would have to meet existing federal standards for vehicle controls and displays.

This bill would affect motorcoach manufacturers who build and sell buses in the United States, as they would need to update their designs to meet the new rules. It would also affect bus operators, such as charter companies and transit agencies, since the new standards would apply to any new buses they purchase going forward. Passengers and pedestrians could also be affected, as the goal of the bill is to reduce accidents at intersections by improving what bus drivers can see.
ai_headline: Federal bill would mandate clearer sightlines on newly built motorcoaches
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7263 would require all new motorcoaches sold in the U.S. to give drivers an unobstructed forward view, using redesign or cameras.
ai_sections.what: HR 7263 would require the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration to issue rules within one year mandating that newly manufactured motorcoaches sold in the United States provide drivers with a clear, unobstructed view of the road directly in front of the bus. Manufacturers could meet this standard either by redesigning the physical structure of the bus or by installing cameras or other technology covering otherwise-blocked areas. Any camera system used would need to meet existing federal standards for vehicle controls and displays.
ai_sections.who: The rules would apply to motorcoach manufacturers that build and sell buses in the United States, as well as bus operators such as charter companies and transit agencies purchasing new buses going forward. Passengers and pedestrians are also identified as affected parties.
ai_sections.why: Large buses can have wide front frames and dashboards that block a driver's view of pedestrians, cyclists, or other hazards at intersections and crosswalks. The bill aims to reduce accidents at intersections by expanding what bus drivers can see.

title: Election Infrastructure Integrity Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require states and local governments to report information about the private companies they hire to help run federal elections. Within 30 days after each federal election, election officials would have to tell the Election Assistance Commission which vendors they used, what their contracts looked like, and who owns those companies, including whether any foreign individuals or entities have an ownership stake. The Commission would then post all of this information in a public database that anyone could look at.

The goal is to make it easier for the public to see which outside companies are involved in running elections and who is behind those companies. Currently, this information is not collected or displayed in one central place. The bill covers a wide range of election technology, including voting machines, voter registration systems, ballot tabulation software, and electronic poll books.

States and local governments that do not comply would lose access to federal funding for election administration. This bill affects election officials at the state and local level, private technology companies that work with elections, and ordinary voters who may want to know more about how elections in their area are run. It would apply starting with federal elections held in 2026.
ai_headline: States would have to disclose election vendors and foreign ownership ties
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8118 requires states to report election vendors and ownership details to a public federal database, starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 8118 requires state and local governments to report to the Election Assistance Commission within 30 days after each federal election, naming which private vendors they used, what the contracts contained, and who owns those companies, including any foreign ownership. The Commission must publish all of this in a publicly accessible database. The requirement covers voting machines, voter registration systems, ballot tabulation software, and electronic poll books.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects state and local election officials and the private technology companies that contract with them. Ordinary voters who want to understand how elections in their area are administered are also affected as intended users of the public database.
ai_sections.why: Currently no single central source collects or displays this vendor and ownership information, so the bill would create one. States and localities that do not comply would lose access to federal funding for election administration.
ai_sections.cost: States and local governments that fail to comply would lose federal election administration funding. No specific dollar amounts or cost figures are stated in the summary.
costChips: Noncompliance triggers loss of federal funds

title: Fatigued Pilot Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to update its rules so that all airline pilots flying commercial passenger flights follow the same limits on how many hours they can fly and how much rest they must get before a flight. Right now, there is a gap in the rules — some pilots who fly under a certain category of airline operations are not required to follow the stricter rest and duty rules that most commercial pilots must follow. The FAA would have 180 days after the bill becomes law to close that gap.

The rules being extended are from a section of federal aviation law called Part 117, which sets strict science-based limits on pilot work hours and required rest periods. These rules were created to fight pilot fatigue, which has been a factor in deadly plane crashes. The bill would require that any pilot flying commercial airline operations under what is known as Part 121 — the rules governing large commercial airlines — must follow these stronger protections, with no exceptions.

This bill most directly affects airline pilots and the airlines that employ them, particularly any carriers that currently operate under Part 121 but have been allowed to skip the Part 117 rest requirements. It also affects everyday air travelers, since the goal is to ensure that the pilot flying your commercial flight has had adequate rest regardless of which airline or type of operation is involved.
ai_headline: FAA would have to apply uniform pilot rest rules to all commercial flights
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7191 gives the FAA 180 days to extend strict pilot fatigue rules to every Part 121 commercial airline, closing a current exemption.
ai_sections.what: HR 7191 directs the FAA to eliminate an existing gap that lets some Part 121 commercial airline pilots skip the stricter rest and duty-hour limits set by Part 117. The FAA would have 180 days from enactment to update its rules so that no Part 121 pilot operation is exempt from those science-based fatigue protections.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects airline pilots and the carriers that employ them, specifically any Part 121 airlines currently operating outside Part 117 rest requirements. Commercial air travelers are also affected, since the change would apply to the pilots operating their flights regardless of airline or operation type.
ai_sections.why: Pilot fatigue has been identified as a contributing factor in fatal plane crashes, which was the basis for creating Part 117 rules in the first place. Extending those rules uniformly would mean no commercial passenger flight operates with a pilot who is exempt from the federal rest standards.

title: Coastal Trust Fund Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new government savings account called the Coastal Storm Risk Management Trust Fund. Every year, one billion dollars would be deposited into this fund, drawn from money the federal government already collects from oil and gas companies that drill on the Outer Continental Shelf, which is federally owned ocean floor off the coasts. That money would be set aside specifically for coastal protection work and could not be used for anything else. Any unused money in the fund would earn interest while waiting to be spent.

The money in the fund would go to the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to pay for projects that protect coastlines from hurricanes and storms. This includes building and maintaining things like beach replenishment, which adds sand back to eroding shores, as well as constructing and repairing other storm damage barriers. Only projects that Congress has specifically approved would qualify, and the spending would still need to go through the normal annual budget process. The Army Secretary would also have to report to Congress each year on how the money was spent.

This bill most directly affects people who live along U.S. coastlines, particularly in areas prone to hurricanes and major storms, such as the Gulf Coast, Atlantic Coast, and Pacific shorelines. Local governments in coastal areas could benefit since the federal government would be covering its share of protection project costs. The bill also adjusts existing federal budget rules so that spending from this fund is treated separately and does not trigger automatic budget cuts under deficit control laws.
ai_headline: New $1B annual fund would direct oil and gas revenue to coastal storm defense
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7459 would deposit $1B yearly from offshore drilling fees into a dedicated coastal storm protection fund for Army Corps projects.
ai_sections.what: HR 7459 establishes the Coastal Storm Risk Management Trust Fund, depositing $1 billion annually from existing federal oil and gas royalties collected on the Outer Continental Shelf. The money would be reserved exclusively for coastal storm protection projects approved by Congress and managed by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. Unspent funds would earn interest, and the Army Secretary would report to Congress yearly on expenditures.
ai_sections.who: People living along U.S. coastlines in hurricane- and storm-prone regions, including the Gulf Coast, Atlantic Coast, and Pacific shorelines, are the most directly affected. Local governments in those areas would also be affected, as the fund would cover the federal share of qualifying protection project costs.
ai_sections.why: Establishing a dedicated fund with mandatory annual deposits could provide a more predictable and protected source of financing for coastal infrastructure work. The bill also modifies existing federal budget rules so that fund spending is accounted for separately and does not trigger automatic cuts under deficit control laws.
ai_sections.cost: $1 billion per year would be deposited into the fund, drawn from royalties already collected from oil and gas companies drilling on the federally owned Outer Continental Shelf. No new taxes or fees are created; the bill redirects existing federal revenue.
costChips: No new taxes or fees created

title: Health Care Fairness for Military Families Act of 2025
ai_summary:
TRICARE Young Adult is a health insurance program that lets military families add their adult children, up to age 26, to their coverage. Right now, those young adults can only join if they are not already eligible for employer-sponsored health insurance through their own job. This bill removes that restriction, meaning a young adult could keep TRICARE coverage even if their employer offers them health insurance.

The bill also removes the separate monthly premium that young adults currently have to pay out of pocket to be on the TRICARE Young Adult plan. Under current law, the young adult pays their own premium on top of whatever the military parent already pays. This bill eliminates that extra charge, making the coverage free to the young adult dependent, the same way regular dependent coverage works for minor children.

This bill affects military families, specifically service members and veterans whose adult children are between roughly 21 and 26 years old. It would make it easier and cheaper for those young adults to stay on family military health coverage rather than having to choose between a job's insurance plan and TRICARE. The bill has been referred to the House Armed Services Committee and has not become law yet.
ai_headline: Bill would let military families keep TRICARE past employer coverage bar
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4768 would let adult military dependents keep TRICARE Young Adult even if their employer offers health insurance, and drops their monthly premium.
ai_sections.what: HR 4768 would remove two restrictions from TRICARE Young Adult, the program that covers military family members up to age 26. First, it would eliminate the rule that bars enrollment when a young adult has access to employer-sponsored health insurance. Second, it would remove the separate monthly premium that young adults currently pay out of pocket to participate in the plan.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects service members and veterans who have adult children between roughly 21 and 26 years old. Those young adult dependents would be directly affected by both the eligibility change and the elimination of their out-of-pocket premium.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, young adults must choose between employer-offered insurance and TRICARE Young Adult, and must pay a separate monthly premium for TRICARE coverage. Removing these requirements would change the conditions under which military families access and pay for health coverage for adult dependents.
ai_sections.cost: Young adults currently pay a separate monthly premium out of pocket to be enrolled in TRICARE Young Adult. This bill would eliminate that charge, making the coverage free to the young adult dependent in the same way coverage works for minor children under the plan.
costChips: Young adults now pay a monthly premium
Coverage would become free to the dependent

title: Humane Transport of Farmed Animals Act
ai_summary:
This bill does two main things related to how farm animals are treated when they are moved across the country. First, it requires the U.S. Department of Transportation, working with the Department of Agriculture, to create a real enforcement system for existing animal transport laws. Right now, rules about how long animals can go without food, water, and rest during transport exist, but this bill says the government must set up a way to actually inspect vehicles, ships, and records to check if those rules are being followed. The government would have 180 days after the bill becomes law to put that system in place.

Second, the bill makes it illegal to move livestock across state lines if the animals are considered "unfit to travel." The bill spells out what that means in detail. Animals that are sick, badly injured, too weak to stand, blind in both eyes, or unable to move without causing them more pain would not be allowed to be shipped. Pregnant animals in the final stretch of pregnancy and mothers that gave birth in the last 48 hours without their newborns are also included. There is an exception that allows moving an unfit animal if the purpose is to get it veterinary care.

The people most directly affected are farmers, ranchers, livestock trucking companies, and shipping companies that move animals like cattle, pigs, and poultry between states. Meat processors and slaughterhouses that receive transported animals could also be affected. Federal agencies would take on new responsibilities for inspections and writing new rules. Consumers would likely see little direct change, though the bill could influence how the livestock industry operates nationwide.
ai_headline: Bill would ban interstate shipment of sick or injured livestock
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5286 bans moving unfit livestock across state lines and gives DOT 180 days to build an enforcement system.
ai_sections.what: HR 5286 prohibits transporting livestock across state lines if they are sick, badly injured, too weak to stand, blind in both eyes, unable to move without added pain, in late pregnancy, or mothers separated from newborns within 48 hours. Moving an unfit animal to receive veterinary care is permitted. The bill also requires the Department of Transportation, working with the Department of Agriculture, to build an inspection system for vehicles, ships, and records within 180 days of enactment.
ai_sections.who: Farmers, ranchers, livestock trucking companies, and shipping companies that move cattle, pigs, and poultry between states are directly affected. Meat processors and slaughterhouses receiving transported animals, as well as federal agencies taking on new inspection duties, would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Existing rules on food, water, and rest during animal transport currently lack a dedicated enforcement mechanism, which this bill would require the government to create. The ban on transporting unfit animals and the new inspection requirements could alter how livestock is handled and moved throughout the industry nationwide.

title: LCBP Enhancements Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes changes to how the Lake Champlain Basin Program (LCBP) is managed. The LCBP is a federal program that works to protect and improve the water quality and environment of Lake Champlain, which sits on the border of New York and Vermont and also touches Canada. The main change is about who handles the program's money and paperwork. Right now, a specific organization called the New England Interstate Water Pollution Control Commission does that job. This bill replaces that fixed arrangement with a flexible system where a "fiscal agent" is chosen by program leaders and reviewed at least every five years to make sure they are doing a good job.

The bill also allows the Great Lakes Fishery Commission, which normally focuses on the Great Lakes, to officially do work in the Lake Champlain region. That work could include studying fish, controlling invasive sea lampreys, preventing other invasive species from spreading, and educating the public. The bill also extends the program's authorization through 2032, meaning Congress is approving it to keep operating and receiving funding for several more years.

The people most directly affected are residents of New York and Vermont, especially those who live near Lake Champlain and depend on it for fishing, drinking water, recreation, or their livelihoods. Organizations, universities, and local governments in the region that partner with the program could also be affected. For most everyday residents, the practical impact would be felt through ongoing efforts to keep Lake Champlain's water cleaner and its ecosystem healthier.
ai_headline: Lake Champlain program gets flexible funding rules and extended life through 2032
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7560 replaces a fixed fiscal agent for Lake Champlain's federal water program and extends its authorization through 2032.
ai_sections.what: HR 7560 changes how the Lake Champlain Basin Program manages its finances by replacing a fixed administrator, the New England Interstate Water Pollution Control Commission, with a flexible fiscal agent chosen by program leaders and reviewed at least every five years. The bill also allows the Great Lakes Fishery Commission to work in the Lake Champlain region on fish research, invasive species control, and public education. The program's authorization is extended through 2032, allowing it to continue operating and receiving funding.
ai_sections.who: Residents of New York and Vermont, particularly those near Lake Champlain who rely on it for fishing, drinking water, recreation, or their livelihoods, are most directly affected. Organizations, universities, and local governments that partner with the program could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: The shift to a reviewable fiscal agent introduces an accountability mechanism that did not exist under the prior fixed arrangement. Expanding the Great Lakes Fishery Commission's role into Lake Champlain broadens the scope of invasive species management and fish research in the region.

title: Helping Heroes Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new program inside the Department of Veterans Affairs called the Veteran Family Resource Program. The VA would hire special staff members called "family coordinators" and place at least one in each of its regional networks across the country within five years. These coordinators would work at VA medical centers and act as guides, helping veterans and their families figure out what benefits and services they qualify for, both through the VA and through outside community organizations. The goal is to address things like mental health, financial stress, and other everyday challenges that can affect a veteran's overall well-being at home.

The bill also focuses specifically on the children and caregivers of disabled veterans. It requires the VA to survey disabled veterans and their families to find out what support they are getting for their children and what needs are going unmet. Based on the bill's definition, "supportive services" includes things like mental and physical health care, peer support programs for kids, and other activities aimed at helping children in veteran households.

This bill would most directly affect veterans receiving VA health care, their family members, their caregivers, and surviving family members of deceased veterans. It would also affect VA staff, since new coordinator positions would be created. The VA would be required to report to Congress on the program's results, including data on who used it and whether it made a measurable difference in veterans' health and family well-being. Standard federal nondiscrimination laws would apply to all program activities.
ai_headline: VA bill would place family coordinators at every regional medical network
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2077 would create a VA program placing family coordinators at every regional network to connect veterans and families with benefits and services.
ai_sections.what: HR 2077 would establish the Veteran Family Resource Program within the Department of Veterans Affairs. The VA would be required to hire family coordinators and place at least one in each of its regional networks nationwide within five years. These coordinators would work at VA medical centers to help veterans and their families identify benefits and services available through the VA and community organizations.
ai_sections.who: The bill would most directly affect veterans receiving VA health care, their family members, caregivers, and surviving family members of deceased veterans. VA staff would also be affected, as new coordinator positions would be created.
ai_sections.why: The VA would be required to report to Congress on program results, including data on who used it and whether it produced measurable differences in veterans' health and family well-being. The bill also requires the VA to survey disabled veterans and their families to identify unmet support needs for children in veteran households.

title: Oral Health Products Inclusion Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change the tax code to allow people to use money from their Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) or Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs) to buy everyday oral care products without paying taxes on that money. Right now, those accounts can only be used for certain medical expenses, and most basic dental hygiene items do not qualify. If this bill becomes law, products like toothbrushes, electric toothbrushes, water flossers, toothpaste, mouthwash, and similar over-the-counter products would count as approved medical expenses under these accounts.

The change would apply to HSAs, FSAs, Health Reimbursement Arrangements, and Archer Medical Savings Accounts. To qualify, oral care products would need to be recognized as safe and effective by the Food and Drug Administration. The new rules would take effect for any purchases made after the bill is signed into law.

This bill would affect the tens of millions of Americans who have an HSA or FSA through their employer or on their own. Since contributions to these accounts are not taxed, being able to spend that money on toothbrushes and similar products would let people stretch their pre-tax dollars further on routine dental care. People without these types of accounts would not be directly affected by the change.
ai_headline: HSA and FSA funds could soon cover toothbrushes and toothpaste tax-free
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1219 would let HSA and FSA holders buy toothbrushes, toothpaste, and mouthwash with pre-tax dollars.
ai_sections.what: HR 1219 would amend the tax code to classify FDA-recognized over-the-counter oral care products as approved medical expenses under health savings accounts. Eligible products would include toothbrushes, electric toothbrushes, water flossers, toothpaste, and mouthwash. The change would apply to HSAs, FSAs, Health Reimbursement Arrangements, and Archer Medical Savings Accounts, effective for purchases made after the bill is signed into law.
ai_sections.who: The bill would directly affect the tens of millions of Americans who hold an HSA or FSA through an employer or on their own. People without these types of accounts would not be directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Because contributions to HSAs and FSAs are not taxed, expanding eligible purchases to include routine oral care products would allow account holders to apply pre-tax dollars to everyday dental hygiene expenses. Under current law, most basic oral care items do not qualify as approved medical expenses under these accounts.

title: Jobs for a Carbon Free Transportation System Act
ai_summary:
This bill has three main parts. First, it creates a grant program for states, cities, and tribal governments to build "low carbon corridors," which are transportation networks that connect different ways of getting around, like buses, trains, bike lanes, and electric vehicle roads. The goal is to cut carbon emissions while improving how people move from place to place. Any materials used in these projects would need to be made in the United States, and workers on the projects would be guaranteed standard wage and safety protections under existing federal labor laws.

Second, the bill sets up a financing tool called "value capture" to help pay for public transportation and affordable housing near transit stations. The basic idea is that when the government invests in a new transit stop or rail line, nearby property values often rise. This bill would let local governments capture some of that increased tax revenue and use it to fund more transit improvements. It also creates a new type of bond that private developers could use to finance housing and commercial projects within a half-mile of major transit stations.

Third, the bill creates programs to help workers currently employed in fossil fuel industries, like oil, gas, and coal, find jobs in cleaner energy sectors. It would fund local planning grants to help communities figure out how to retrain these workers. If those efforts fall short, a new National Employment Corps would step in to guarantee jobs paying at least fifteen dollars an hour to any worker who lost their job due to the energy transition. The people most directly affected by this bill include transportation workers, fossil fuel workers, residents of communities near transit stations, and local governments looking for new ways to fund infrastructure.
ai_headline: Bill would fund clean transit corridors and guarantee jobs for fossil fuel workers
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6923 links clean transit grants, property-value financing, and a $15/hr job guarantee for fossil fuel workers displaced by the energy transition.
ai_sections.what: HR 6923 does three things: it creates grants for states, cities, and tribal governments to build low-carbon transportation networks; it lets local governments fund transit and affordable housing by capturing tax revenue generated when nearby property values rise after transit investments; and it establishes retraining programs and a National Employment Corps to place displaced fossil fuel workers in jobs paying at least fifteen dollars an hour.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects transportation workers, fossil fuel workers in oil, gas, and coal, residents of communities near transit stations, and local governments seeking new infrastructure funding tools. Private developers building housing or commercial projects within a half-mile of major transit stations would also be affected through a new bond mechanism.
ai_sections.why: The bill would shift some infrastructure financing from direct appropriations toward locally captured property-tax revenue and private development bonds, changing how transit projects are funded at the local level. Workers leaving fossil fuel industries could be directed into the National Employment Corps if community retraining efforts prove insufficient, creating a federal backstop for employment in those communities.
ai_sections.cost: The bill requires that materials used in low-carbon corridor projects be made in the United States and that workers receive standard federal wage and safety protections. A National Employment Corps would guarantee jobs paying at least fifteen dollars an hour to eligible displaced workers, though no total program cost is specified in the summary.

title: Puerto Rico Water Infrastructure Resilience Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to take a close look at Puerto Rico's water and wastewater systems. Within 180 days of becoming law, the Army Corps must complete a full assessment of what those systems need to be modernized and made more reliable. After finishing that study, they must send a written report to Congress explaining what they found.

The report would have to cover four main things: the current condition and weak points of Puerto Rico's water infrastructure, the status of water projects already planned or underway there, what improvements are needed to help the systems hold up better against hurricanes, droughts, and other failures, and suggestions for how federal and local agencies can work together more efficiently to get projects done faster.

This bill primarily affects residents of Puerto Rico, who have long dealt with aging pipes, water outages, and damage from past storms like Hurricane Maria. It also involves federal agencies like the Army Corps of Engineers and the Puerto Rico Aqueduct and Sewer Authority. The bill does not directly spend any money or fund construction — it only requires a study and a report to Congress, which could later inform future funding decisions.
ai_headline: Army Corps ordered to assess Puerto Rico water systems within 180 days
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7397 requires the Army Corps of Engineers to study Puerto Rico's water infrastructure and report findings to Congress within 180 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 7397 directs the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to complete a full assessment of Puerto Rico's water and wastewater systems within 180 days of enactment. The study must cover the current condition and vulnerabilities of the infrastructure, the status of planned or ongoing projects, improvements needed to withstand hurricanes and droughts, and recommendations for better coordination among federal and local agencies. After completing the assessment, the Army Corps must submit a written report to Congress.
ai_sections.who: Puerto Rico residents, who have faced aging pipes, water outages, and storm damage, are the primary people affected by this bill. The federal agencies involved are the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and the Puerto Rico Aqueduct and Sewer Authority.
ai_sections.why: The bill does not directly fund construction or spend money, but the resulting report could inform future federal funding decisions. How Congress responds to the findings may determine whether and when improvements to Puerto Rico's water infrastructure move forward.

title: CLEAN–UP Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with cleaning up polluted sediment at the bottom of rivers, harbors, and other waterways. Right now, a legal hurdle exists: the Army Corps of Engineers can be held financially liable under federal environmental law if harmful substances spread during a cleanup project, even when the Corps is trying to help. This bill removes that liability as long as the Corps follows an approved cleanup plan. The plan must be developed together with a local or state partner, approved by the EPA, and must protect public health and the environment.

The bill sets up a clear process for these joint cleanup plans. They must spell out how the contaminated material will be removed and disposed of, who is responsible for what, and how the work will be funded. The Corps is also required to consult with government officials and the public before work begins, and must document what pollutants are present and try to identify whoever originally caused the contamination. Importantly, the bill does not let polluters off the hook — the government can still sue responsible parties to recover cleanup costs.

This bill mainly affects the Army Corps of Engineers and local or state governments that partner with them on water cleanup projects. It could also benefit people who live near polluted waterways, since reducing legal risk for the Corps may make it easier and faster to move forward with cleanups that have been stalled. Businesses or individuals originally responsible for the pollution could still face financial consequences under existing environmental law.
ai_headline: Bill shields Army Corps from liability during river sediment cleanups
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7268 removes federal liability for the Army Corps of Engineers during approved waterway sediment cleanups, potentially accelerating stalled projects.
ai_sections.what: HR 7268 removes the legal liability the Army Corps of Engineers currently faces under federal environmental law when pollutants spread during sediment cleanup projects. The liability shield applies only when the Corps follows an approved cleanup plan developed with a state or local partner and approved by the EPA. The plan must detail how contaminated material will be removed and disposed of, assign responsibilities, and outline funding.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects the Army Corps of Engineers and the state or local governments that partner with them on waterway cleanup projects. People living near polluted rivers, harbors, or other waterways may also be affected, as may businesses or individuals originally responsible for the contamination.
ai_sections.why: Reducing legal risk for the Corps could make it easier and faster to move forward with sediment cleanups that have been stalled. The bill does not eliminate accountability for original polluters, who can still be sued by the government to recover cleanup costs under existing environmental law.
ai_sections.cost: Cleanup plans must spell out how the work will be funded, and the government retains the ability to pursue responsible parties to recover costs. The bill does not specify a dollar amount or federal appropriation.

title: Same-Day Paratransit Innovation Act
ai_summary:
This bill aims to expand same-day paratransit service for people with disabilities. Right now, the Americans with Disabilities Act only requires transit agencies to offer rides booked at least 24 hours in advance. This bill encourages agencies to also offer same-day rides by increasing how much federal money the government will pay for those programs. Normally the federal government covers 50 percent of operating costs, but under this bill agencies offering same-day service could receive 70 to 80 percent federal funding, and up to 90 percent for related equipment and vehicles. This applies to cities, suburbs, and rural areas.

The bill also requires the Federal Transit Administration to create minimum technology standards within one year for the software transit agencies use to run paratransit. Those standards would cover things like app accessibility for people with disabilities, cybersecurity protections, real-time trip scheduling, and making sure transit agencies own their own rider data. After those standards are published, any transit agency using federal money to buy paratransit software must choose products that meet the new requirements.

The people most directly affected are the roughly 18 million Americans with travel-limiting disabilities who rely or could rely on paratransit, as well as older adults whose numbers are expected to grow significantly in coming decades. Local and regional transit agencies would also be affected, since they would need to meet new technology standards and could choose to restructure their services to qualify for higher federal funding levels.
ai_headline: Federal funding boost targets same-day rides for disabled passengers
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8128 raises federal paratransit funding to up to 80% for same-day service and sets new tech standards for booking software.
ai_sections.what: HR 8128 would incentivize transit agencies to offer same-day paratransit rides, which current ADA rules do not require. Agencies that add same-day service could receive higher federal reimbursement rates, and any agency using federal funds to buy paratransit software would have to meet new technology standards set by the Federal Transit Administration within one year of the bill's passage. Those standards would cover app accessibility, cybersecurity, real-time scheduling, and transit agency ownership of rider data.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 18 million Americans with travel-limiting disabilities, as well as older adults whose numbers are expected to grow in coming decades, are the primary people affected. Local and regional transit agencies across cities, suburbs, and rural areas would also need to adapt their technology and could restructure services to qualify for higher funding.
ai_sections.why: Transit agencies that restructure for same-day service would become eligible for substantially higher federal reimbursement, which changes the financial calculus for offering expanded service. Agencies purchasing paratransit software with federal money would face mandatory compliance with new technology standards once those standards are published.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government currently covers 50 percent of paratransit operating costs. Under this bill, agencies offering same-day service could receive 70 to 80 percent federal funding for operations and up to 90 percent for related equipment and vehicles.
costChips: Current federal share: 50% of operating costs

title: Preventing Foreign Interference in American Elections Act
ai_summary:
This bill has two main parts. The first part expands existing federal law that bans foreign money in U.S. elections. Right now, it is already illegal for foreign nationals to donate to candidates or political parties. This bill would extend that ban to cover more types of political activity, including voter registration drives, ballot collection, get-out-the-vote efforts, voter ID programs, and the running of elections themselves. It also makes it illegal to help someone else get around these rules, and it closes a loophole where foreign money could flow through a middleman organization before reaching these activities. Political committees would also have to sign a sworn statement on their regular reports certifying they followed these rules.

The second part of the bill deals with donor privacy for nonprofit organizations. It would prohibit most federal government agencies from collecting or publicly releasing the names of people who donate to tax-exempt nonprofits, such as charities and advocacy groups. There are exceptions for the IRS, the Federal Election Commission, and congressional lobbying disclosure offices, which can still collect or share donor information as their existing laws already allow. Any federal employee who illegally leaks donor information could face up to five years in prison and a $250,000 fine.

The people most affected by this bill include nonprofit organizations, political campaigns, outside spending groups, voter outreach organizations, and anyone who donates to a nonprofit. Election administrators would face new compliance requirements. The bill would also affect federal regulators like the FEC, which would have to stay within tighter boundaries when investigating potential violations.
ai_headline: Bill extends foreign money ban to voter drives and election administration
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 8 - 3.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8721 bans foreign money from voter outreach and election ops, and bars most federal agencies from disclosing nonprofit donor names.
ai_sections.what: HR 8721 expands the existing federal ban on foreign money in U.S. elections to cover voter registration drives, ballot collection, get-out-the-vote efforts, voter ID programs, and election administration itself. The bill closes a loophole that allowed foreign funds to pass through middleman organizations before reaching those activities. It also prohibits most federal agencies from collecting or publicly releasing the names of donors to tax-exempt nonprofits.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects nonprofit organizations, political campaigns, outside spending groups, voter outreach organizations, election administrators, and anyone who donates to a nonprofit. Federal regulators, including the FEC, would face tighter boundaries on investigating potential violations.
ai_sections.why: Political committees would be required to include a sworn certification of compliance on their regular filings, adding a new administrative requirement for election-related organizations. Election administrators and federal agencies would need to adjust existing practices to meet the new compliance standards.
ai_sections.cost: Federal employees who illegally disclose nonprofit donor information could face up to five years in prison and a $250,000 fine.
costChips: Up to 5 years prison for illegal leaks

title: Stop Comstock Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change several old federal laws that currently ban sending certain materials through the mail or importing them into the country. Specifically, it removes language from laws that date back to the 1800s — known as the Comstock Act — that prohibit mailing or importing items related to abortion, contraception, and things labeled "indecent" or "immoral." The bill keeps in place existing rules against mailing or importing obscene material, but strips out the portions that go beyond that to cover abortion-related drugs and devices.

The practical effect is that federal law would no longer be used as a basis for blocking abortion pills or contraception from being sent through the mail or brought into the United States. Currently, some legal advocates and officials have argued that the old Comstock Act language could be used to restrict abortion medication even in states where abortion is legal. This bill would remove that legal tool from the books entirely.

This would most directly affect people who receive abortion medication or contraception by mail, healthcare providers who prescribe or ship those items, and pharmacies or companies that distribute them. It could also affect how federal agencies and courts interpret what the government is allowed to block at the border or through the postal system. The bill was introduced in the House and has been referred to committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on.
ai_headline: House bill would strip 1800s Comstock Act abortion mail ban from federal law
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2029 would remove Comstock Act language banning mail or import of abortion pills and contraception, leaving obscenity rules intact.
ai_sections.what: HR 2029 would amend federal laws dating to the 1800s, known as the Comstock Act, by removing language that prohibits mailing or importing items related to abortion, contraception, and material labeled indecent or immoral. Rules against mailing or importing obscene material would remain in place. The bill would eliminate a legal argument that has been used to claim federal law can block abortion medication even in states where abortion is legal.
ai_sections.who: People who receive abortion medication or contraception by mail, healthcare providers who prescribe or ship those items, and pharmacies or companies that distribute them would be most directly affected. Federal agencies and courts interpreting what the government may block at the border or through the postal system could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Without this language in federal law, the Comstock Act could no longer be used as a legal basis to block abortion pills or contraception from being mailed or imported into the United States. The bill has been referred to committee in the House and has not yet been voted on.

title: To authorize the Land Port of Entry Community Infrastructure Program to address deficiencies in community infrastructure supportive of land ports of entry, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill creates a grant program called the Land Port of Entry Community Infrastructure Program, run by the Department of Homeland Security. It lets the federal government give money to state, tribal, and local governments, as well as nonprofit utility companies, to fix or improve infrastructure near land border crossings with Mexico and Canada. Eligible projects include roads, water and sewer systems, utilities, and other improvements located within 25 miles of a border crossing. The goal is to help communities that are strained by the heavy traffic and activity that comes with having a major border crossing nearby.
