To get funding, most applicants would need to put up at least 30 percent of the project cost from their own non-federal money. However, smaller rural communities and projects with strong national security benefits could have that requirement reduced or waived entirely. The program also allows governments and utilities to apply for reimbursement for qualifying projects they already paid for going back to November 2021.

This bill mainly affects people living in border communities, especially towns and cities near official land crossings along the U.S.-Mexico and U.S.-Canada borders. It would also impact Customs and Border Protection workers and their families who live in those communities, since improving local infrastructure is one stated goal. The program has no set dollar amount approved yet — Congress would still need to pass a separate spending bill to actually fund it.
ai_headline: New grant program would fund infrastructure fixes near U.S. land border crossings
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Transportation and Maritime Security.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6791 creates a DHS grant program for roads, utilities, and sewer projects within 25 miles of U.S. land border crossings.
ai_sections.what: HR 6791 establishes the Land Port of Entry Community Infrastructure Program under the Department of Homeland Security. The program would fund roads, water and sewer systems, utilities, and related improvements located within 25 miles of a land border crossing with Mexico or Canada. Governments and utilities may also apply for reimbursement for qualifying projects already completed back to November 2021.
ai_sections.who: State, tribal, local governments, and nonprofit utility companies near land border crossings are eligible to apply. Customs and Border Protection workers and their families living in those communities are among those the program specifically aims to serve.
ai_sections.why: Border communities face strain from the heavy traffic and activity generated by major land crossings, and this program would direct federal resources toward offsetting that pressure. Whether communities actually receive funding depends on Congress passing a separate appropriations bill, since no dollar amount has been approved yet.
ai_sections.cost: Most applicants must contribute at least 30 percent of project costs from non-federal funds. Rural communities and projects with strong national security benefits may have that match requirement reduced or waived. No total funding amount has been authorized; Congress must pass a separate spending bill before any money can flow.
costChips: Rural and security projects may get waiver

title: Railroad Safety Enhancement Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill is called the Railroad Safety Enhancement Act of 2026. It is a wide-ranging set of new rules and requirements aimed at making freight trains safer, especially trains carrying dangerous materials like flammable liquids, toxic gases, explosives, or radioactive waste. It sets lower speed limits for those trains in certain areas, requires better equipment on tank cars used to haul flammable liquids, and sets a deadline of December 31, 2027, for older, less-safe tank cars to be replaced or upgraded. The bill also requires railroads to share real-time information about what hazardous materials are on a train with emergency responders and state agencies, so that if something goes wrong, local firefighters and police know exactly what they are dealing with.

The bill also addresses broader safety concerns beyond just hazardous materials. It requires most freight trains run by large railroads to have at least two crew members on board. It calls for more frequent inspections of railcars, locomotives, and brake systems, and prohibits railroads from pressuring workers to rush through or skip those inspections. It sets up a formal system for detecting equipment defects along the tracks, like overheated wheel bearings, before they can cause a derailment. Fines for breaking rail safety rules would go up significantly, with penalties reaching as high as five million dollars for serious violations. The bill also calls for studies on blocked railroad crossings and new protections for track workers.

The bill affects a wide range of people. Railroad companies, especially the largest ones, would face new operating requirements, higher costs for equipment upgrades, and stricter government oversight. Railroad workers would get stronger inspection rights and minimum crew size protections. State and local emergency responders would receive better information about hazardous cargo moving through their communities. People who live near rail lines or railroad crossings, particularly in cities, would benefit from the safety improvements this bill is designed to create. The federal government, specifically the Department of Transportation and the Federal Railroad Administration, would gain new responsibilities and authority to enforce these rules.
ai_headline: Rail safety bill sets speed limits, crew rules, and fines up to $5 million
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7662 tightens freight rail safety rules, mandates two-person crews, and raises penalties to $5M for violations.
ai_sections.what: HR 7662, the Railroad Safety Enhancement Act of 2026, establishes new speed limits for trains carrying hazardous materials, requires equipment upgrades on tank cars hauling flammable liquids, and sets a December 31, 2027 deadline to replace or upgrade older tank cars. It mandates real-time sharing of hazardous cargo information with emergency responders and state agencies. The bill also requires most large-railroad freight trains to carry at least two crew members, increases inspection frequency, and creates a formal trackside system to detect equipment defects before they cause derailments.
ai_sections.who: Large railroad companies face new operating requirements, mandatory equipment upgrades, and stricter federal oversight from the Department of Transportation and the Federal Railroad Administration. Railroad workers, state and local emergency responders, and people living near rail lines or crossings are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Railroads would face significantly higher compliance and equipment costs, and federal agencies would take on expanded enforcement responsibilities. Penalties for safety violations would rise sharply, reaching as high as five million dollars for serious violations.
ai_sections.cost: Fines for violating rail safety rules would increase significantly, with penalties reaching as high as five million dollars for serious violations. The bill does not specify overall spending figures or identify who bears equipment upgrade costs.
costChips: Fines up to $5 million for serious violations
Older tank cars must be upgraded by Dec 31, 2027

title: Connecting Communities Through Transit Planning Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill updates a federal grant program that helps communities plan development around public transit hubs, like bus or train stations. It expands who can apply for the money and what the money can be used for. For example, grants could now cover things like engineering studies, community meetings, utility planning, and accessibility reviews — steps that happen before actual construction begins. The bill also authorizes up to $75 million per year from 2027 through 2031 to fund these activities.

The bill also broadens the types of transit projects that qualify. Previously, only certain new rail or transit lines were eligible. Under this bill, bus rapid transit projects and improvements to existing transit corridors would also qualify, opening the door for more cities and towns to participate.

People who could be affected include residents in cities and suburbs near existing or planned transit stations, particularly those who depend on public transit — such as seniors, veterans, and people with disabilities. Local governments, transit agencies, and urban planners would be the ones actually applying for and using these grants to shape how neighborhoods grow around transit stops. It does not directly give money to individuals, but it could influence what gets built in communities near transit lines across the country.
ai_headline: Federal transit planning grants expanded to cover more cities and project types
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7298 expands federal transit-area planning grants to include bus rapid transit and pre-construction steps, funding up to $75M yearly from 2027–2031.
ai_sections.what: HR 7298 updates a federal grant program that funds community planning around public transit hubs such as bus and train stations. The bill expands eligible uses to include pre-construction activities like engineering studies, community meetings, utility planning, and accessibility reviews. It also widens which transit projects qualify, adding bus rapid transit and improvements to existing transit corridors alongside previously eligible new rail lines.
ai_sections.who: Local governments, transit agencies, and urban planners would apply for and use these grants to shape neighborhood development near transit stops. Residents in cities and suburbs near existing or planned transit stations could be affected, particularly seniors, veterans, and people with disabilities who depend on public transit.
ai_sections.why: Broadening eligibility to include bus rapid transit and corridor improvements means more cities and towns could access federal planning funds than under the current program. The expansion of covered activities to pre-construction steps could influence what gets built in communities near transit lines across the country.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $75 million per year from 2027 through 2031 for these grant activities.
costChips: Grants go to local govts and agencies

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 1444 Main Street in Ramona, California, as the "Archie Moore Post Office Building".
ai_summary:
This bill would officially rename the post office at 1444 Main Street in Ramona, California, after Archie Moore. The new name would be the "Archie Moore Post Office Building." Any official government documents, maps, or records that refer to that building would use the new name going forward.

Archie Moore was a professional boxer from the mid-20th century who held the world light heavyweight championship for nearly a decade and is considered one of the greatest boxers in history. He also had ties to the San Diego area, where Ramona is located. Naming post offices after notable individuals is a common and routine act of Congress, typically done to honor local or national figures.

This bill directly affects very few people in a practical sense. Residents and businesses in Ramona who use that post office would see the new name on the building and in official references. There is no change to postal services, hours, staffing, or costs. The bill was introduced by Representative Darrell Issa and sent to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform for review.
ai_headline: Post office in Ramona, California to be named after boxer Archie Moore
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4982 would rename the Ramona, CA post office at 1444 Main Street the "Archie Moore Post Office Building."
ai_sections.what: HR 4982 would officially rename the post office at 1444 Main Street in Ramona, California as the "Archie Moore Post Office Building." All official government documents, maps, and records referring to that building would use the new name going forward. No changes to postal services, hours, staffing, or costs are included in the bill.
ai_sections.who: Residents and businesses in Ramona who use that post office would see the new name on the building and in official references. The bill was introduced by Representative Darrell Issa and referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

Naming post offices after notable individuals is described as a common and routine act of Congress used to honor local or national figures.

title: CHIERS Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a federal grant program run jointly by the Department of Health and Human Services and the Department of Housing and Urban Development. The grants would go to nonprofits, local governments, health clinics, tribal organizations, and similar groups to help pay for rides to and from substance use disorder treatment. The transportation would be free to the people using it and could be provided through scheduled rides, on-demand rides, taxis, rideshare apps, or vehicles bought with grant funds. Grant money can also be used to train drivers, pay for required licenses, and spread the word about available rides.

The program is specifically designed for people who have a substance use disorder and face extra barriers getting to treatment. That includes people experiencing homelessness, people living in low-income areas, and people in places where transportation is generally hard to access. The bill makes clear that rides cannot be used to transport someone to involuntary treatment, and law enforcement cannot use the funds.

Anyone who receives a grant must report back on how well the program is working, particularly whether it reduces missed or cancelled appointments. The grant money is meant to add to existing state and local funding, not replace it. This bill has not yet become law and would still need to pass both chambers of Congress and be signed by the President.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund free rides to addiction treatment under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Financial Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8916 would create a federal grant program funding free transportation to substance use disorder treatment for people facing access barriers.
ai_sections.what: HR 8916 would establish a federal grant program run jointly by the Department of Health and Human Services and the Department of Housing and Urban Development. Grants would fund free rides to and from substance use disorder treatment, available through scheduled rides, on-demand rides, taxis, rideshare apps, or grant-purchased vehicles. Grant funds can also cover driver training, required licenses, and outreach about available transportation.
ai_sections.who: Eligible grant recipients include nonprofits, local governments, health clinics, tribal organizations, and similar groups. The program targets people with substance use disorders who face extra barriers to treatment, including people experiencing homelessness, those in low-income areas, and those in places where transportation is generally hard to access.
ai_sections.why: Grant recipients must report on program outcomes, with particular attention to whether the funding reduces missed or cancelled appointments. The bill prohibits using rides for involuntary treatment transport and bars law enforcement from accessing the funds.
ai_sections.cost: Grant money is intended to supplement existing state and local funding, not replace it. The summary does not specify a total funding amount.
costChips: Adds to, not replaces, state and local funds
No total cost specified in bill summary

title: IMPACT to Save Moms Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a test program run by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) from 2027 to 2031. The program would let states try out new ways of paying doctors, midwives, doulas, and other providers for pregnancy and postpartum care under Medicaid and the Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP). Instead of paying for each individual service separately, these new payment models would tie payments to the quality of care and health outcomes. The goal is to figure out which payment approaches lead to better results for mothers and babies.

The program specifically focuses on reducing maternal deaths and serious health complications during and after pregnancy. It requires that care teams be diverse and that the models account for things like mental health, substance use, nutrition, and social factors such as housing and income. Non-hospital birth settings like freestanding birth centers would also be considered. After the program ends, the government must publish a report to Congress on whether it worked and whether it should be expanded nationwide.

The people most directly affected are pregnant and postpartum individuals enrolled in Medicaid or CHIP, particularly those from communities that already experience higher rates of maternal death or serious complications. Healthcare providers who deliver maternity care, including doctors, midwives, and doulas, would also be affected since how they get paid could change. States would need to apply to participate, so not every state would be involved right away.
ai_headline: Federal pilot would test new Medicaid maternity payment models from 2027 to 2031
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8907 launches a 2027–2031 CMS pilot letting states test outcome-based maternity payment models under Medicaid and CHIP.
ai_sections.what: HR 8907 would create a pilot program run by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) from 2027 to 2031, allowing states to test new ways of paying providers for pregnancy and postpartum care under Medicaid and CHIP. Instead of paying for each service separately, payment models would be tied to care quality and health outcomes. After the program ends, the government must deliver a report to Congress on results and whether the program should expand nationwide.
ai_sections.who: Pregnant and postpartum individuals enrolled in Medicaid or CHIP are most directly affected, particularly those in communities with higher rates of maternal death or serious complications. Providers including doctors, midwives, and doulas would also be affected because how they are paid could change, and states must apply to participate.
ai_sections.why: The program is specifically focused on reducing maternal deaths and serious health complications during and after pregnancy. Whether payment model changes produce better outcomes for mothers and babies would be determined by the program's results and the subsequent congressional report.

title: CLEAN UP Mines Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill updates a 1977 law that regulates surface coal mining, also called strip mining. The main goal is to make mining companies clean up and restore land faster after they finish digging. Right now, companies can sometimes leave disturbed land sitting for a long time before they fix it. Under this bill, companies would have to start backfilling and regrading the land within 180 days of their last coal production, finish replanting vegetation within 36 months, and complete all major land restoration before removing their heavy equipment from the site. If a mine sits idle for more than six months without any active mining or cleanup, it would automatically be considered out of compliance with its permit unless the company submits an approved plan to get back on track within a year.

The bill also tightens environmental monitoring requirements. Mining companies and regulators would be required to check both surface water and groundwater every quarter while a mine is active or being reclaimed. Government inspectors would also need to assess the health of nearby streams every year and inspect water runoff structures after any major storm event, specifically one as intense as a so-called 100-year storm.

This bill would primarily affect coal mining companies that operate surface mines, the communities and ecosystems near those mines, and the state and federal agencies that oversee mining permits. Workers in the reclamation industry could also be affected, since faster cleanup timelines could shift when and how that work is done. The bill does not ban coal mining but sets stricter deadlines and oversight rules for how land must be restored afterward.
ai_headline: Bill sets 180-day deadline for strip mine land restoration
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9023 requires surface coal mines to begin land backfilling within 180 days of last coal production and finish replanting within 36 months.
ai_sections.what: HR 9023 updates a 1977 surface coal mining law to require faster land restoration after mining ends. Companies must begin backfilling and regrading within 180 days of their last coal production, finish replanting vegetation within 36 months, and complete all major restoration before removing heavy equipment. Mines idle for more than six months without active mining or cleanup would automatically fall out of permit compliance unless the company submits an approved recovery plan within one year.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects surface coal mining companies, the communities and ecosystems near those mines, and the state and federal agencies that oversee mining permits. Workers in the reclamation industry could also be affected, since faster cleanup timelines could shift when and how that work is done.
ai_sections.why: The bill tightens environmental monitoring by requiring quarterly checks of both surface water and groundwater while a mine is active or being reclaimed. Government inspectors would also be required to assess nearby stream health annually and inspect water runoff structures after any storm event as intense as a 100-year storm.

title: Coal Cleanup Taxpayer Protection Act of 2026
ai_summary:
When a coal company mines land, the law requires it to clean up and restore that land after mining ends. This process is called reclamation. To make sure the money is there to pay for cleanup, companies must post a bond before they start mining, similar to a security deposit. This bill changes the rules around those bonds to reduce the chances that taxpayers end up footing the bill if a coal company goes bankrupt or walks away from its cleanup duties.

The biggest change is that companies would no longer be allowed to use what is called a self-bond, which is essentially a company's promise to pay based on its own financial strength rather than putting up actual money or assets. Critics of self-bonding say it leaves little protection when a company fails. Under this bill, companies would have to use real financial instruments like cash, letters of credit, or third-party insurance bonds instead. The bill also sets new rules for outside insurers who back those bonds, requiring them to hold enough cash and assets to actually cover the costs. It also limits what counts as acceptable collateral, for example coal-related property could no longer be used to secure a bond since that type of property could lose value precisely when a company is in financial trouble.

This bill primarily affects coal mining companies and the federal and state agencies that regulate them. It could also affect surety companies, which are the insurers that back mining bonds. Communities near coal mines and the general public are indirectly affected, since the goal is to ensure cleanup money is available without requiring government agencies and taxpayers to cover the costs when a mining company cannot.
ai_headline: House bill bans self-bonding for coal mine cleanup guarantees
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9029 eliminates self-bonding for coal mine cleanup, requiring companies to back obligations with cash, credit, or insurer-backed instruments.
ai_sections.what: HR 9029 changes the rules for bonds that coal companies must post before mining to guarantee land cleanup after operations end. It bans self-bonds, which are promises backed only by a company's own financial standing, and requires real financial instruments such as cash, letters of credit, or third-party insurance bonds instead. The bill also restricts acceptable collateral, barring coal-related property, and sets financial requirements for the insurers that back these bonds.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects coal mining companies and the federal and state agencies that regulate them, as well as surety companies that provide mining bonds. Communities near coal mines and the general public are indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: When coal companies go bankrupt or abandon cleanup duties, government agencies and taxpayers can end up responsible for reclamation costs. The bill changes bonding rules to reduce the likelihood of that outcome by requiring more secure financial backing before mining begins.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not shift cleanup costs to a new program or set specific dollar amounts, but it addresses who bears reclamation costs if a coal company fails, aiming to keep that burden off taxpayers and government agencies.
costChips: No set dollar amounts in bill
Reclamation costs target shifted from taxpayers
Insurers must hold sufficient cash and assets

title: NO FAKES Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new federal right for every person to control whether their voice or face can be copied by AI. If someone uses artificial intelligence to make a realistic fake audio or video of you without your permission, that is illegal under this law. You would have the right to sue them and collect damages. The law applies to living people and continues for up to 70 years after a person dies, allowing their family or estate to protect their likeness. Any license allowing someone to use your AI-generated likeness must be in writing, must describe exactly how it will be used, and can last no longer than 10 years while you are alive.

The bill puts responsibilities on websites and apps where users upload content, like social media platforms. Once a platform is notified that an unauthorized AI fake of someone is posted there, it must take it down quickly. Platforms must also register a contact person with the Copyright Office so rights holders know where to send complaints. If a platform follows these rules, it is shielded from most lawsuits. If it ignores them, it can face fines of up to $750,000 per fake posted. The law does not require platforms to constantly scan all content on their own, but they must act once they are notified.

The bill affects a wide range of people. Everyday individuals gain legal protection against deepfakes of themselves. Celebrities, musicians, and actors gain stronger control over AI versions of their image. Families of deceased public figures can also enforce these rights. AI tool developers, social media companies, and anyone creating or sharing AI-generated likenesses must get permission or face lawsuits. Importantly, news reporting, criticism, satire, and parody are generally exempt, but using someone's fake likeness in sexually explicit content is never protected under those exceptions.
ai_headline: New federal law would give people the right to sue over AI voice and face fakes
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8915 creates a federal right to block unauthorized AI copies of your voice or face, with fines up to $750,000 per violation for noncompliant platforms.
ai_sections.what: HR 8915 establishes a federal right for every person to control whether their voice or face can be copied by AI without permission. Anyone who creates or shares an unauthorized AI-generated likeness can be sued for damages. The right lasts up to 70 years after death, and any license to use someone's AI likeness must be written, specific, and no longer than 10 years while the person is alive.
ai_sections.who: All living individuals, as well as families and estates of deceased persons, gain enforceable rights under this law. AI developers, social media platforms, and anyone creating or distributing AI-generated likenesses face new legal obligations and liability.
ai_sections.why: Platforms that fail to remove unauthorized AI fakes after notification and ignore registration requirements with the Copyright Office lose their liability shield and face fines up to $750,000 per fake posted. News reporting, criticism, satire, and parody are generally exempt, but no exemption applies to sexually explicit content using someone's fake likeness.
ai_sections.cost: Platforms that do not follow takedown and registration rules face fines of up to $750,000 per unauthorized AI fake posted. Platforms that comply with the rules are shielded from most lawsuits.
costChips: Up to $750,000 fine per fake posted

title: PRC Broker-Dealers and Investment Advisers Moratorium Act
ai_summary:
This bill would block certain financial firms connected to China from registering with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Specifically, it would bar brokers, dealers, and investment advisers from getting or keeping their SEC registration if they are organized under Chinese law, owned or controlled by Chinese companies or citizens living in China (meaning someone holds more than 15% of their voting shares), or if they rely on Chinese-connected companies for key services like software, tech infrastructure, or product development. Without SEC registration, these firms cannot legally operate in U.S. financial markets. The ban would last five years from the date the law is signed, after which it would automatically expire.

The bill affects financial firms with ties to China that currently operate or want to operate in the United States, including any company that uses Chinese-affiliated businesses to run parts of its technology or customer service operations. It also indirectly affects American investors who use platforms or advisers that fall under these restrictions, since those firms would have to either cut their Chinese connections or stop doing business in the U.S.

Ordinary Americans who invest through a brokerage or use a financial adviser would not be directly targeted by this bill, but they could be affected if a firm they use loses its registration as a result. The bill is aimed at reducing what its sponsors describe as security risks from Chinese influence in U.S. financial markets.
ai_headline: Bill would bar China-linked brokers and advisers from SEC registration
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9028 would ban China-linked brokers, dealers, and investment advisers from SEC registration for five years.
ai_sections.what: HR 9028 would prohibit brokers, dealers, and investment advisers from obtaining or keeping SEC registration if they are organized under Chinese law, more than 15% owned or controlled by Chinese companies or citizens living in China, or dependent on Chinese-connected firms for key services such as software, technology infrastructure, or product development. Without SEC registration, these firms cannot legally operate in U.S. financial markets. The ban would last five years from the date the law is signed, then expire automatically.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects financial firms with ties to China that currently operate or seek to operate in the United States, including companies that rely on Chinese-affiliated businesses for technology or customer service functions. American investors who use platforms or advisers falling under these restrictions could also be affected if those firms lose their SEC registration.
ai_sections.why: Firms with Chinese connections would need to either sever those ties or exit U.S. financial markets entirely. Investors using affected platforms or advisers could face disruptions to their access to those services.

title: State and Local Public Sector Innovation Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal grant program, run by the Commerce Department, to help state and local governments update their technology. Up to $500 million per year would be available from 2027 through 2030. Half the money would be divided among states based on population, and the other half would go to states with the greatest technology needs. At least 70 percent of each state's share must be passed down directly to local governments like cities and counties.

States could use the money for things like buying new computer systems, improving cybersecurity, training government workers on new technology, and upgrading to more advanced encryption to protect against future cyber threats. Any government office that uses artificial intelligence to interact with the public would be required to tell people they are talking to an AI. States that are not meeting certain basic technology standards, like having a data privacy system or following federal cybersecurity guidelines, could be required to set aside a portion of their funds specifically to come into compliance.

This bill would affect residents across the country indirectly, since it targets the government offices that handle things like permits, public records, and other local services. State and local government employees, especially those in technology roles, would be directly involved in carrying out the changes. Federally recognized tribal governments, U.S. territories, and the District of Columbia are also included alongside the 50 states.
ai_headline: Federal grants up to $500M a year would modernize state and local tech
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8926 would fund up to $500M/year from 2027–2030 for state and local governments to upgrade technology and cybersecurity.
ai_sections.what: HR 8926 would create a federal grant program run by the Commerce Department to help state and local governments update their technology systems. Eligible uses include buying new computer systems, improving cybersecurity, training government workers, and upgrading encryption. Any government office using artificial intelligence to interact with the public would be required to disclose that fact to users.
ai_sections.who: State and local governments, including cities, counties, tribal governments, U.S. territories, and the District of Columbia would be eligible recipients. Government employees in technology roles would be directly involved in carrying out the changes, and residents would be indirectly affected through the public-facing offices this bill targets.
ai_sections.why: States not meeting basic technology standards such as having a data privacy system or following federal cybersecurity guidelines could be required to set aside part of their funds to come into compliance. At least 70 percent of each state's share must be passed down directly to local governments, shaping how funding authority is distributed within states.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $500 million per year would be available from 2027 through 2030. Half the money would be divided among states by population, and the other half would go to states with the greatest technology needs.
costChips: Half split by population, half by need

title: Federal Biotechnology Workforce Assessment Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the federal government to take stock of how many workers it has who specialize in biotechnology and how many more it might need. The Office of Personnel Management would lead this effort, working with 14 major agencies including the Defense Department, Health and Human Services, the EPA, and NASA. Within 180 days of the bill becoming law, those agencies would have to deliver a report to Congress laying out how many biotech jobs they currently have filled, how many they will need over the next 5 and 10 years, and what qualifications those jobs require.

The assessment also looks at some practical hurdles. For example, it asks how many biotech workers would need security clearances, whether current funding is enough to pay for those clearances, and whether job classification codes used by the government even do a good job of tracking biotech workers right now. It also asks whether retraining current employees or temporarily swapping workers between agencies could help fill gaps.

The bill mainly affects federal agencies that work with biotechnology in some way, the employees who hold or might hold those jobs, and the Office of Personnel Management, which would manage the whole review. Ordinary Americans are not directly affected, but the findings could eventually shape how the government hires and trains workers in areas like disease research, food safety, national security, and environmental monitoring.
ai_headline: Federal agencies would count and forecast their biotech workforce needs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8930 requires 14 federal agencies to report current and projected biotech staffing to Congress within 180 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 8930 directs the Office of Personnel Management to lead a review of how many biotechnology workers the federal government currently employs and how many it will need. Fourteen agencies including the Defense Department, Health and Human Services, the EPA, and NASA must submit a report to Congress within 180 days of enactment. The report must cover current filled positions, projected needs at the 5- and 10-year marks, required qualifications, security clearance demands, and whether existing job classification codes accurately track biotech workers.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the Office of Personnel Management, the 14 participating federal agencies, and current or prospective federal employees in biotechnology roles. Ordinary Americans are not directly affected, though findings could influence future hiring and training in disease research, food safety, national security, and environmental monitoring.
ai_sections.why: The assessment is meant to reveal gaps between the government's current biotech staffing and what it may need over the next decade. It also examines whether retraining existing employees or temporarily moving workers between agencies could address shortfalls.
ai_sections.cost: The bill asks whether current funding is sufficient to cover security clearances for biotech workers, but no specific spending figures or appropriations are stated in the summary.

title: Digital Opportunity Foundation Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new nonprofit organization called the Foundation for Digital Opportunity. The Foundation's job is to help more Americans get access to the internet, affordable devices, and the skills needed to use digital technology. It would work alongside existing federal agencies like the FCC and the National Telecommunications and Information Administration, but it would not be a government agency itself. It would raise and distribute money from private companies, foundations, and other sources to fund programs that promote internet access and digital skills, especially in communities where people are less likely to have broadband or know how to use it.

The Foundation would be run by a Board of Directors made up of people from education, industry, nonprofits, local governments, and community organizations. Several federal officials would also sit on the board but without voting power. The Foundation could award grants to local programs, hold training events, collect data on digital gaps, and even set up for-profit investment funds to attract private money toward its goals. It would have to report to Congress regularly and undergo audits to make sure it is operating honestly and effectively.

The people most affected by this bill would be those who currently lack reliable internet access or digital skills, including low-income households, older adults, people with disabilities, rural communities, and tribal communities. Schools, libraries, local governments, and community organizations that run digital literacy or broadband adoption programs could also apply for funding from the Foundation. Taxpayers would fund the startup costs, while the Foundation would be expected to bring in outside money over time to support its ongoing work.
ai_headline: New nonprofit would expand internet access for underserved Americans
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8936 creates a private nonprofit, the Foundation for Digital Opportunity, to fund internet access and digital skills programs for underserved Americans.
ai_sections.what: HR 8936 establishes the Foundation for Digital Opportunity, a new nonprofit organization tasked with expanding internet access, affordable devices, and digital skills training across the United States. The Foundation would raise money from private companies, foundations, and other outside sources to fund grants, training events, and data collection on digital gaps. It could also create for-profit investment funds to attract private capital toward its goals.
ai_sections.who: The Foundation would be governed by a Board of Directors drawn from education, industry, nonprofits, local governments, and community organizations, with federal officials serving in a non-voting capacity. Low-income households, older adults, people with disabilities, rural communities, and tribal communities are the primary intended beneficiaries, along with schools, libraries, and local governments that run digital literacy or broadband programs.
ai_sections.why: The Foundation would operate alongside existing agencies such as the FCC and the National Telecommunications and Information Administration without replacing them, adding a privately funded mechanism to address gaps those agencies do not fully close. Regular congressional reporting and independent audits would be required to evaluate whether the Foundation is operating effectively and honestly.
ai_sections.cost: Taxpayers would cover the Foundation's startup costs, after which the Foundation would be expected to raise outside funding from private sources to sustain its ongoing operations.

title: To establish the Commission on Sustaining Medicare and Social Security, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new federal commission made up of appointed members whose job is to study the long-term financial health of Medicare and Social Security. The commission would look at why these programs are projected to run short of money in the coming decades and develop recommendations for how to keep them funded and working. It would be a temporary group that reports its findings and suggestions to Congress.

The commission's members would include people appointed by congressional leaders from both parties, meaning both Republicans and Democrats would have representation in the process. After doing its research and holding discussions, the commission would produce a formal report with specific proposals that Congress could then choose to act on or ignore. The bill itself does not change Medicare or Social Security benefits or funding directly.

This bill would most directly affect current and future retirees, people with disabilities, and anyone who pays into Social Security and Medicare through payroll taxes, which is most working Americans. Essentially, that covers the vast majority of US residents either now or at some point in their lives. The commission would have no power to make changes on its own, but its recommendations could shape future decisions lawmakers make about these programs.
ai_headline: New federal commission would study Social Security and Medicare finances
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9089 creates a temporary bipartisan commission to study Social Security and Medicare funding shortfalls and report recommendations to Congress.
ai_sections.what: HR 9089 would establish a temporary federal commission to study projected funding shortfalls in Social Security and Medicare and develop recommendations for keeping both programs financially viable. Members would be appointed by congressional leaders from both parties. The commission would deliver a formal report with specific proposals to Congress, which could then choose to act on or disregard them.
ai_sections.who: The commission's work would most directly affect current and future retirees, people with disabilities, and working Americans who pay into Social Security and Medicare through payroll taxes. That broadly covers the vast majority of US residents at some point in their lives.
ai_sections.why: Social Security and Medicare are projected to run short of money in the coming decades, and the commission is intended to examine the reasons why and propose solutions. The commission itself would have no power to make changes, so any real-world impact on benefits or funding would depend entirely on future action by Congress.

title: Advancing Menopause Care and Mid-Life Women’s Health Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the federal government to do more about menopause and related health issues that affect women in middle age. It tells the National Institutes of Health to expand research on symptoms like hot flashes, sleep problems, joint pain, heart disease, cognitive changes, and mental health issues that are connected to perimenopause, menopause, and the years after. It also creates grants for universities, hospitals, and research institutions to study better treatments, both medical and non-medical, for these conditions. About $25 million per year from 2027 to 2031 would be authorized for this research.

The bill also sets up public education programs so that women and their doctors have better information about menopause care. It funds training programs so that physicians, nurses, pharmacists, and other health workers learn how to recognize and treat menopausal symptoms more effectively. It creates special "Centers of Excellence" at health institutions around the country that would develop resources and best practices for menopause care, with attention paid to reaching rural areas and underserved communities.

The people most directly affected are women going through perimenopause and menopause, which typically happens between the late 30s and mid-50s. Health care providers who treat those women would also be affected through new training opportunities. The bill specifically tries to reach women in rural areas and minority communities who currently have less access to quality care. It requires the Department of Health and Human Services to report to Congress on progress and remaining gaps every year.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets menopause research and care gaps with $25M a year
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9090 authorizes $25M per year from 2027–2031 to expand menopause research, provider training, and public education.
ai_sections.what: HR 9090 directs the National Institutes of Health to expand research on symptoms linked to perimenopause, menopause, and post-menopause, including hot flashes, sleep problems, joint pain, heart disease, and cognitive and mental health changes. The bill creates grants for universities, hospitals, and research institutions to study medical and non-medical treatments, funds public education programs, and establishes Centers of Excellence at health institutions nationwide to develop best practices for menopause care. The Department of Health and Human Services would be required to report annually to Congress on progress and remaining gaps.
ai_sections.who: Women going through perimenopause and menopause, typically between their late 30s and mid-50s, are the primary population addressed, with specific attention to those in rural areas and minority communities. Health care providers including physicians, nurses, and pharmacists would also be affected through new federally funded training opportunities.
ai_sections.why: The bill responds to identified gaps in research, provider knowledge, and public information about menopausal health. Women in rural areas and underserved communities currently have less access to quality care, and the legislation directs resources toward closing those gaps.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $25 million per year from 2027 through 2031 for research grants and related programs.

title: Secure Family Futures Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes two tax rules specifically for certain insurance companies, like life insurance companies that sell policies to families. First, it says that when these companies buy bonds or loans (called debt), those investments will no longer be counted as "capital assets" under the tax code. This matters because it changes how gains or losses from those investments get taxed when the companies sell them. The goal is to make it easier for these companies to manage the bonds they hold without facing certain tax complications.

Second, the bill extends how long these insurance companies can carry forward capital losses. Right now, if an insurance company loses money on investments, it can apply that loss to reduce its taxes for up to 5 years into the future. This bill would stretch that window to 10 years, giving companies more time to use those losses as a tax benefit.

The people most directly affected are mid-sized and large domestic insurance companies, particularly those selling life insurance and similar products to ordinary families. Indirectly, policyholders could be affected if these tax changes influence how financially stable their insurance company is or how it prices products. The bill does not change anything for small insurance companies that have made certain tax elections, foreign insurance companies, or Blue Cross Blue Shield type organizations.
ai_headline: Senate bill rewrites debt investment tax rules for domestic insurers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1335 reclassifies insurer debt investments and doubles the capital loss carryforward window from 5 to 10 years.
ai_sections.what: S 1335 removes debt investments such as bonds and loans from the definition of capital assets for certain insurance companies, changing how gains and losses on those holdings are taxed. The bill also extends the period during which these companies can apply capital losses against future taxes, stretching the window from 5 years to 10 years.
ai_sections.who: Mid-sized and large domestic insurance companies, particularly life insurers selling policies to families, are directly affected. Policyholders may be indirectly affected if the changes influence their insurer's financial stability or product pricing.
ai_sections.why: Reclassifying debt investments is intended to reduce tax complications when insurance companies sell bonds they hold. Extending the loss carryforward period gives companies more time to offset tax liability with prior investment losses, which could affect company finances and, indirectly, policyholders.

title: Vote Without Fear Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it a federal crime to knowingly carry a firearm inside or within 100 yards of a federal election site, such as a polling place or a location where ballots are being counted. A basic violation carries a penalty of up to one year in prison, a fine, or both. If someone brings a gun to an election site with the intent to commit a crime, the penalty increases to up to five years. If someone kills another person during such a violation, they face federal murder or manslaughter charges.

There are several exceptions built into the bill. On-duty law enforcement officers and authorized private security guards hired by the building owner may still carry firearms. A person may also have a firearm in their vehicle within that 100-yard zone as long as they do not take it out or display it. People who live, work, or own private property that happens to fall within 100 yards of an election site are also exempt as long as they are otherwise following the law.

This bill would affect anyone who legally or illegally carries a firearm and comes near a federal polling place or ballot-counting location on election days or during vote counting. It does not change gun laws in any other setting. It would apply across the country wherever federal elections are being administered.
ai_headline: Senate bill bans guns within 100 yards of federal election sites
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4121 would make it a federal crime to carry a firearm at or within 100 yards of a polling place or ballot-counting site.
ai_sections.what: S 4121 would make it a federal crime to knowingly carry a firearm inside or within 100 yards of a federal election site, including polling places and ballot-counting locations. A basic violation carries up to one year in prison, a fine, or both. Carrying a firearm with intent to commit a crime at such a site raises the penalty to up to five years, and causing a death during a violation triggers federal murder or manslaughter charges.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to anyone — with or without a legal permit to carry — who brings a firearm near a federal polling place or ballot-counting location on election days or during vote counting. On-duty law enforcement, authorized private security guards, and people who live, work, or own property within the zone are exempt, as are people who keep a firearm inside a vehicle without displaying it.
ai_sections.why: The bill would create a new category of federal firearms offense tied specifically to election administration sites. Anyone currently accustomed to legally carrying a firearm could face criminal liability on or near federal election sites unless they qualify for one of the stated exemptions.
ai_sections.cost: Violations carry a fine as a possible penalty, either alone or alongside imprisonment, but the bill specifies no fixed fine amount.
costChips: Up to 1 yr prison or fine or both
Intent to offend raises max to 5 yrs

title: Association Health Plans Act
ai_summary:
This bill would allow businesses and self-employed people to band together through trade or professional associations to buy health insurance as a group, even if those businesses are in completely different industries. Right now, federal law makes it harder for these "association health plans" to operate broadly. The bill would change that by officially recognizing a qualifying group of employers as a single "employer" under federal benefits law, letting them pool their members together to negotiate and purchase health coverage. To qualify, the association must have been around for at least two years, have at least 51 covered employees total, and must have been created for reasons beyond just buying health insurance.

The bill also includes rules about how premiums can be set. Insurers could charge individual member businesses different rates based on the specific health risk profile of their workers, as long as the overall pricing method is actuarially sound. Self-employed individuals who work at least 10 hours a week in their own business could also join these plans and be treated as both an employer and an employee for coverage purposes. Groups made up entirely of self-employed people would have to pool everyone together and charge each person the same rate.

The bill would mainly affect small business owners, self-employed workers, and trade or professional associations that want to offer health coverage. Supporters would say it gives smaller employers more buying power similar to what large corporations already have. The bill also states that these plans cannot deny coverage due to pre-existing conditions and cannot charge someone more based on their health status, keeping some existing consumer protections in place. It was introduced in the Senate in May 2025 and has been referred to committee.
ai_headline: Senate bill lets small businesses pool together to buy group health insurance
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1847 lets businesses and self-employed workers join association health plans across industries, with protections against pre-existing condition exclusions.
ai_sections.what: S 1847 would allow businesses and self-employed individuals to join trade or professional associations and purchase health insurance as a group, even across unrelated industries. Federal law currently limits how broadly these association health plans can operate. The bill formally recognizes qualifying employer groups as a single employer under federal benefits law, enabling pooled coverage negotiations.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects small business owners, self-employed workers who work at least 10 hours a week in their own business, and trade or professional associations seeking to offer health coverage. Associations must have existed for at least two years, have at least 51 covered employees total, and must have been formed for purposes beyond purchasing health insurance.
ai_sections.why: Smaller employers could gain collective purchasing power that currently is more accessible to large corporations. Insurers would be permitted to set different premium rates for individual member businesses based on their workers' health risk profiles, as long as the overall method is actuarially sound, which may affect how costs are distributed across members.

title: Protecting First Responders from Secondary Exposure Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill allows federal grant money to be used in a new way: to buy special containment devices for first responders and to train them on how to use those devices. The goal is to protect police officers, firefighters, paramedics, and other first responders from accidentally absorbing fentanyl or other dangerous drugs just by being near them at a scene. This kind of unintended exposure is called "secondary exposure," and it can be seriously harmful or even life-threatening.

Right now, there is already a federal grant program that helps fund law enforcement and public safety efforts. This bill simply adds a new item to the list of things that grant money can be spent on. It does not create a new program or require any new spending on its own. States and local agencies would still have to apply for the grants and choose to use the money this way.

The people most directly affected are first responders across the country, including police officers, EMTs, and firefighters who may come into contact with fentanyl or similar substances on the job. Local and state governments that receive these federal grants would have more flexibility in how they protect their emergency workers. Taxpayers and community members are also indirectly affected, since safer first responders means those workers can continue doing their jobs without being put out of service by accidental drug exposure.
ai_headline: Federal grants could fund fentanyl protection gear for first responders
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 77.

ai_sections.tldr: S 180 lets existing federal grant money pay for fentanyl containment devices and training for first responders.
ai_sections.what: S 180 adds a new allowable use to an existing federal grant program: purchasing containment devices and providing training to protect first responders from accidental fentanyl or dangerous drug exposure. The bill does not create a new program or mandate new spending. States and local agencies would still apply for grants and choose whether to use funds this way.
ai_sections.who: First responders including police officers, firefighters, paramedics, and EMTs are the people most directly affected. State and local governments that receive the existing federal grants would gain more flexibility in how they spend those funds.
ai_sections.why: First responders can be seriously harmed or killed by secondary exposure to fentanyl or similar substances encountered on the job. If first responders are incapacitated by accidental exposure, communities lose emergency service capacity.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not create new spending or appropriate new funds on its own. It expands what existing federal grant money may be spent on.

title: Keeping Obstetrics Local Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the Keeping Obstetrics Local Act, aims to help keep maternity and childbirth services open at hospitals that serve low-income or rural communities. Right now, many of these hospitals lose money on delivering babies because Medicaid pays them less than it actually costs to provide the care. The bill would require Medicaid to pay these hospitals at least 150 percent of what Medicare pays for the same services starting in 2027, and the federal government would cover a larger share of those costs. Small rural hospitals that deliver fewer than 300 babies a year would also receive special yearly payments to help cover the basic costs of keeping a labor and delivery unit open and staffed, even during slow periods.

The bill also expands coverage for pregnant and postpartum people on Medicaid and CHIP. Currently, some states end full coverage shortly after birth. This bill would make 12 months of full benefits after pregnancy mandatory nationwide, not just optional. It also creates new options for states to set up coordinated care programs for pregnant and postpartum patients, and it calls for better Medicaid coverage of services from doulas, midwives, and other maternal health workers, especially in rural areas. Mental health screenings during and after pregnancy would also receive more federal funding support.

The people most affected are pregnant women and new mothers who rely on Medicaid or CHIP, which together cover about 4 in 10 births in the United States. Rural communities and lower-income neighborhoods stand to benefit the most, since they are most at risk of losing local hospital maternity units due to financial pressures. Hospitals serving large numbers of Medicaid patients, as well as the states that run Medicaid programs, would also be directly impacted by the new payment rules and requirements.
ai_headline: Senate bill would set Medicaid floor for maternity pay at rural hospitals
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2059 would require Medicaid to pay rural and safety-net hospitals at least 150% of Medicare rates for maternity care starting in 2027.
ai_sections.what: The Keeping Obstetrics Local Act would require Medicaid to pay hospitals at least 150 percent of Medicare rates for maternity services beginning in 2027. Small rural hospitals delivering fewer than 300 babies per year would receive additional annual payments to keep labor and delivery units staffed. The bill would also make 12 months of full postpartum Medicaid and CHIP coverage mandatory nationwide.
ai_sections.who: Pregnant women and new mothers on Medicaid or CHIP are the primary population affected, a group that accounts for about 4 in 10 births in the United States. Hospitals serving large Medicaid patient populations, rural communities, and the states that administer Medicaid programs would also face direct changes under the new payment rules.
ai_sections.why: Many hospitals in rural and low-income areas currently lose money on maternity care because Medicaid reimbursements fall below the actual cost of providing those services. Without a payment floor, these hospitals face financial pressure that can lead to the closure of local labor and delivery units.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would cover a larger share of Medicaid costs for maternity services under the new payment rates. Small rural hospitals would also receive dedicated annual payments to offset the fixed costs of maintaining open labor and delivery units.
costChips: Federal share of maternity Medicaid costs rises
Annual payments for small rural hospitals
Applies to hospitals under 300 births a year

title: Family Vaccine Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill does two main things. First, it writes the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, known as ACIP, directly into federal law. ACIP is the expert group of doctors and scientists that currently reviews vaccines and recommends which ones Americans should get. Right now, ACIP exists through government regulations that can be changed or ignored more easily. This bill would make it a permanent part of federal law, require its meetings to be open to the public, and set rules for who can serve on it. It also requires that if the head of the CDC or other health officials decide to go against an ACIP recommendation, they must publicly explain why and notify Congress within 48 hours.

Second, the bill adds a rule to the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program, which is the federal program that pays people who are seriously harmed by certain vaccines. Specifically, it says that if the government wants to remove a vaccine from the list of vaccines covered by that program, or change what injuries qualify for payment, that decision must be backed by strong scientific evidence. This is meant to prevent those changes from being made for political or other non-scientific reasons.

This bill affects a wide range of people. Families and patients are affected because ACIP recommendations determine which vaccines are covered for free under most private health insurance plans and under Medicaid's Vaccines for Children program. Doctors, nurses, and pharmacists are affected because they follow ACIP schedules in their practices. People who have been injured by vaccines and rely on the compensation program are also directly affected. Essentially, anyone who receives vaccines or pays for health insurance in the United States has a stake in how this committee operates.
ai_headline: Senate bill locks vaccine advisory panel into federal law and shields injury program
last_action_text: Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3323 codifies the ACIP vaccine advisory panel into federal law and requires science-based evidence to change the injury compensation program.
ai_sections.what: S 3323 would write the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) into permanent federal law, replacing regulations that can currently be changed or ignored more easily. It requires ACIP meetings to be open to the public and mandates that any CDC official who overrides an ACIP recommendation must publicly explain why and notify Congress within 48 hours. The bill also requires that any changes to vaccines or injuries covered by the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program be backed by strong scientific evidence.
ai_sections.who: Families, patients, doctors, nurses, and pharmacists are affected because ACIP recommendations determine which vaccines are covered free under most private insurance plans and under Medicaid's Vaccines for Children program. People who have been seriously harmed by vaccines and rely on the compensation program are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Codifying ACIP into law would make it harder for future administrations to dismantle or bypass the committee through regulatory changes alone. Tying changes to the injury compensation program to scientific evidence standards would limit the ability to make those changes for political or non-scientific reasons.

title: Asunción Valdivia Heat Illness, Injury, and Fatality Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require the U.S. Department of Labor to create a new national safety rule specifically protecting workers from heat-related illness and injury on the job. Within one year of passing, the department would have to issue an initial standard. That standard would require employers to provide things like cool drinking water, paid rest breaks in the heat, access to shade or cool spaces, and policies to help workers adjust to hot conditions gradually. Employers would also need to have a written heat safety plan, train workers and supervisors to recognize heat illness, and provide any needed protective gear at no cost to the worker. All required training and materials would have to be available in the worker's language.

The bill covers most workers in the United States who fall under existing workplace safety law. It would especially affect people who work outdoors or in hot indoor environments, such as farm workers, construction workers, warehouse employees, landscapers, and factory workers. Employers in those industries would be responsible for following the new rules, and they could face penalties if they do not. Workers who report violations or safety concerns would be protected from being fired or punished by their employer.

The bill is named after Asunción Valdivia, a California farm worker who died in 2004 after picking grapes for ten hours in extreme heat. Currently, there is no single federal heat safety standard for most workers, though a few states have their own rules. This bill would create one national baseline that applies across the country.
ai_headline: Federal bill would set first national heat safety rule for U.S. workers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S4421-4422)

ai_sections.tldr: S 2298 would require the Labor Dept. to issue a national heat safety standard within one year, covering most U.S. workers.
ai_sections.what: S 2298 would require the U.S. Department of Labor to issue an initial national heat safety standard within one year of passage. The standard would require employers to provide cool drinking water, paid rest breaks, access to shade or cool spaces, a written heat safety plan, worker training, and free protective gear. All training and materials would have to be available in the worker's language.
ai_sections.who: The bill covers most workers in the United States who fall under existing workplace safety law. It would most directly affect outdoor and hot indoor workers, including farm workers, construction workers, warehouse employees, landscapers, and factory workers.
ai_sections.why: No single federal heat safety standard currently exists for most U.S. workers, though some states have their own rules. This bill would establish one national baseline applicable across the country, and employers who do not comply could face penalties.
ai_sections.cost: Employers would bear the cost of providing water, paid rest breaks, shade or cool spaces, a written safety plan, worker training, and any required protective gear at no charge to workers. Workers who report violations would be protected from termination or punishment.
costChips: Employers pay for gear, not workers

title: Vision Lab Choice Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill deals with vision insurance plans and the rules around how eye doctors can do their jobs. Right now, some vision insurance plans can restrict which labs or suppliers an optometrist uses when making glasses or contact lenses for patients. This bill would make it illegal for vision insurance plans to put those kinds of restrictions on optometrists. In other words, your eye doctor would be free to choose whichever lab or supplier they think is best for you, rather than being forced to use one picked by your insurance company.

The bill also sets rules about contract lengths between optometrists and vision insurance plans. Contracts could last no longer than two years at a time, and the eye doctor would have to agree before any contract gets extended. This gives optometrists more say over the terms of their agreements with insurance companies rather than being locked in indefinitely.

The people most directly affected are optometrists who participate in vision insurance networks, and their patients. If an eye doctor has more freedom to choose labs and suppliers, it could affect the quality, speed, or cost of eyeglasses and contacts that patients receive. The bill applies to both employer-sponsored vision plans and individual vision insurance plans. States still have the power to enforce these rules, and if a state already has its own vision insurance laws, those state laws would generally take precedence.
ai_headline: Senate bill would free eye doctors from insurer-picked suppliers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1716 bars vision plans from restricting which labs optometrists use and caps insurer contracts at two years.
ai_sections.what: S 1716 would prohibit vision insurance plans from restricting which labs or suppliers optometrists use when making glasses or contact lenses. It also limits contracts between optometrists and vision plans to a maximum of two years, and requires the optometrist's agreement before any contract is extended. The bill applies to both employer-sponsored and individual vision insurance plans.
ai_sections.who: Optometrists who participate in vision insurance networks are directly affected, along with their patients who receive eyeglasses or contact lenses. States retain enforcement authority, and existing state vision insurance laws generally take precedence over the federal rules.
ai_sections.why: Giving optometrists freedom to select their own labs and suppliers could affect the quality, speed, or cost of eyewear that patients receive. Limiting contract length and requiring optometrist consent before renewal changes the balance of negotiating power between eye doctors and insurance companies.

title: Lower Drug Costs for Families Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets prescription drug price increases by expanding and strengthening a system where drug companies must pay rebates when they raise prices faster than inflation. Currently, these inflation rebates apply mainly to drugs used by Medicare patients. This bill would extend those same rebate rules to cover drugs sold in the commercial market, meaning private insurance plans as well. It would also change the starting point used to measure price increases, moving the base year back from 2021 to 2016. Using an older starting point means more years of price hikes are captured in the calculation, which would generally result in larger rebates owed by drug companies.

The people most directly affected are drug manufacturers, who could owe significantly larger rebates to the federal government under the new rules. Private insurance companies and their customers could also see indirect effects, since the bill attempts to apply pricing pressure beyond just Medicare. The government would collect more rebate money, which could reduce federal spending on drug programs.

For everyday people, the intended effect is lower drug costs over time, though the bill does not directly set prices or cap what anyone pays at the pharmacy. Whether and how much patients actually save would depend on how insurance companies and employers pass along any savings. The bill was introduced in March 2025 and referred to the Senate Finance Committee, so it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Senate bill would extend Medicare drug price rebates to private insurance
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1186 would force drug makers to pay inflation rebates on commercial market sales, not just Medicare, using a 2016 price baseline.

The bill was introduced in March 2025 and referred to the Senate Finance Committee, where it has not yet become law.
