title: Bring Our Heroes Home Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new system to collect and make public government records about American military members and civilian workers who went missing during wartime, going back to December 7, 1941 (the attack on Pearl Harbor). It would set up a special archive called the Missing Armed Forces and Civilian Personnel Records Collection at the National Archives. Every federal agency, military branch, and government office would be required to search for any records they have about missing personnel, send copies to the National Archives, and make those records available to the public. The default rule is that records must be released unless there is a strong, specific reason to keep them secret.

To oversee this process, the bill creates a five-member independent review board appointed by the President with Senate approval. This board would have real power, including the ability to issue subpoenas, hold hearings, and override a government agency's decision to keep records secret. If an agency wants to withhold or heavily redact a record, it has to explain in writing exactly why, and the harm from releasing it must clearly outweigh the public's interest in knowing. After ten years, nearly everything must be released unless the President personally certifies that specific records still need to be withheld.

The people most directly affected are the families of service members and government workers who went missing in wars from World War II through recent conflicts and never had a full accounting. Researchers, historians, and the general public would also gain access to records that have long been classified or scattered across agencies. Federal agencies, military departments, and the intelligence community would be required to spend significant time and resources locating, reviewing, and turning over these records on strict deadlines.
ai_headline: New law would open wartime missing-personnel records to the public
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3226 requires federal agencies to declassify and archive wartime missing-personnel records going back to Pearl Harbor on Dec. 7, 1941.
ai_sections.what: S 3226 establishes the Missing Armed Forces and Civilian Personnel Records Collection at the National Archives, a centralized archive of government records about U.S. military members and civilian workers who went missing during wartime, covering conflicts from December 7, 1941 onward. Every federal agency, military branch, and government office must locate relevant records, send copies to the National Archives, and release them to the public by default. After ten years, nearly all records must be released unless the President personally certifies specific ones still require withholding.
ai_sections.who: Families of service members and civilian government workers who went missing in wars from World War II through recent conflicts are the most directly affected. Researchers, historians, the general public, and federal agencies and military departments required to locate and turn over records are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Records about missing wartime personnel have long been classified or scattered across multiple agencies, leaving families without a full accounting. Centralizing and releasing these records would give families, researchers, and the public access to information that has previously been difficult or impossible to obtain.

title: SCREEN Act
ai_summary:
The SCREEN Act would require websites that regularly publish or host pornographic content to use age verification technology before letting anyone access that content. Simply clicking a box to confirm you are an adult would not count. The site would need to use real technology, like checking a government ID or using another digital verification method, to determine whether a user is likely under 18. Websites would have one year after the bill becomes law to put these systems in place. The Federal Trade Commission would be in charge of enforcing the rules, conducting audits, and publishing guidance to help websites comply. Violations would be treated the same as unfair or deceptive business practices under existing FTC law.

The bill applies to any website that regularly makes sexual content available to users and is doing so with the intent to make money, even if that content is not its only source of income. It covers websites operating in the US or specifically targeting US users. These websites must also protect any personal data collected during the age verification process, keep that data only as long as necessary, and make their verification process publicly known. They can hire outside companies to handle verification, but the website itself remains legally responsible.

This bill affects several groups of people. Owners and operators of adult content websites would face new legal requirements and potential fines if they do not comply. Adults who use those websites would need to go through an age verification step before accessing content. Children under 18 would be blocked from accessing the content entirely, which is the bill's main goal. Privacy advocates may also take interest, since the bill involves collecting personal information to verify age, even though the bill does require websites to protect that data carefully.
ai_headline: Senate bill would require real ID checks before adult sites grant access
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: The SCREEN Act requires adult websites to verify user ages with real ID technology, not just a checkbox, within one year of passage.
ai_sections.what: The SCREEN Act requires websites that regularly publish or host pornographic content for profit to verify that users are not under 18 before granting access. Clicking a box to confirm age would not be sufficient. Sites must use technology such as government ID checks or other digital verification methods, and must implement these systems within one year of the bill becoming law.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to adult content websites operating in the US or targeting US users, including sites where pornography is not the only source of income. It directly affects site owners and operators, adults who use those sites, and children under 18 who would be blocked from access entirely.
ai_sections.why: The Federal Trade Commission would enforce the rules, conduct audits, and publish compliance guidance, treating violations the same as unfair or deceptive business practices under existing FTC law. Websites must also protect personal data collected during verification, retain it only as long as necessary, and publicly disclose their verification process, with the site remaining legally responsible even if a third party handles verification.
ai_sections.cost: Violations would be treated as unfair or deceptive business practices under existing FTC law, making non-compliant websites subject to penalties under that framework.

title: Kids Online Safety Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the Kids Online Safety Act, requires social media platforms, online video games, messaging apps, and video streaming services to take specific steps to protect users under age 17. Platforms must turn on their safest privacy and safety settings automatically for minors, rather than making families search for them. They must give kids easy ways to limit who can contact them, turn off features like infinite scrolling and autoplay, opt out of personalized recommendations, and restrict location sharing. Parents get tools to monitor screen time, block purchases, and manage their child's account settings. Platforms also have to respond to safety reports within 10 to 21 days and are banned from showing ads for drugs, alcohol, tobacco, gambling, or cannabis to users they know are minors.

The bill also requires large platforms with over 10 million monthly users to publish yearly public reports, reviewed by outside auditors, describing how many minors use their service, how the platform's design affects kids, and how well their safety tools are working. A new advisory council made up of parents, researchers, educators, youth representatives, and platform representatives would be created to advise Congress on online child safety issues. The government would also study how age verification could be done at the device level without collecting too much personal data.

A separate section of the bill affects all users, not just minors. Any platform that uses hidden algorithms to decide what content people see must clearly disclose that fact and give every user the option to switch to a simpler, non-personalized feed instead. Platforms cannot charge extra or penalize users who choose the non-personalized option. The Federal Trade Commission and state attorneys general would both have the power to enforce these rules.
ai_headline: Senate bill would require safest settings by default for minors on social media
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S2929-2930)

ai_sections.tldr: The Kids Online Safety Act forces platforms to auto-enable their safest settings for users under 17 and disclose algorithm use to all users.
ai_sections.what: The Kids Online Safety Act requires social media platforms, online video games, messaging apps, and video streaming services to automatically enable their safest privacy and safety settings for users under 17. Platforms must also give minors tools to limit contact, disable autoplay and infinite scroll, opt out of personalized recommendations, and restrict location sharing. A separate provision requires any platform using hidden algorithms to disclose that fact and offer all users a free, non-personalized feed alternative.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to social media platforms, online video games, messaging apps, and video streaming services. Users under 17 receive the core protections, while all users on algorithm-driven platforms are covered by the feed transparency provisions.
ai_sections.why: Platforms would be required to restructure default settings, build new parental controls, and respond to safety reports within 10 to 21 days, creating operational obligations across the industry. Large platforms with over 10 million monthly users would additionally face annual public reporting requirements reviewed by outside auditors.

title: DETERRENT Act
ai_summary:
The DETERRENT Act would require colleges and universities to be much more open about money and deals they receive from foreign sources. Schools must report gifts of $50,000 or more from foreign organizations or governments to the Department of Education. For countries or groups considered national security threats, like China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea, schools must report every gift no matter how small. Schools would also be completely banned from making contracts or business deals with those high-concern countries and entities unless they get special government permission, which must be renewed every year. All of this information would go into a public, searchable database so anyone can look it up.

The bill also requires colleges to track foreign gifts and contracts received by individual professors and researchers, not just the school as a whole. Faculty at schools receiving over $50 million in federal research funding would have to report any foreign gifts or contracts annually to their own institution. Schools must also have a written plan to watch for possible foreign spying through these financial relationships. Each school covered by this law must appoint a dedicated compliance officer to make sure all these rules are followed correctly.

Schools that break these rules face serious consequences. Fines can range from $50,000 up to twice the value of an unreported gift or contract, and repeated violations can cost a school a percentage of all its federal funding. A school that loses three separate court cases over violations could lose access to all federal student aid programs for at least two years. This bill primarily affects colleges and universities, their professors and researchers, and foreign governments or organizations that currently give money to or do business with American schools.
ai_headline: Senate bill demands colleges disclose foreign gifts and ban deals with rival nations
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: The DETERRENT Act forces colleges to publicly disclose foreign gifts and bans contracts with China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea.
ai_sections.what: The DETERRENT Act requires colleges and universities to report foreign gifts of $50,000 or more to the Department of Education, with no minimum threshold for gifts from countries deemed national security threats, including China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. Schools are banned from making contracts with those high-concern countries without annual government permission, and all disclosed information must be placed in a public, searchable database. The bill also requires faculty at schools receiving over $50 million in federal research funding to report foreign gifts and contracts annually, and each covered school must appoint a dedicated compliance officer.

ai_sections.why: Schools must develop written plans to monitor potential foreign spying through financial relationships, and a new layer of individual faculty reporting extends oversight beyond institutional-level disclosures. Repeated violations can cost schools a share of all federal funding, and three separate court losses over violations can result in losing access to all federal student aid programs for at least two years.
ai_sections.cost: Fines for violations range from $50,000 up to twice the value of an unreported gift or contract. Repeated violations can cost a school a percentage of all its federal funding, and schools that lose three separate court cases over violations can lose all federal student aid access for at least two years.

3 court losses risk all federal student aid

title: SHIPS for America Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill is a major effort by Congress to rebuild the American shipbuilding industry, grow the U.S. fleet of cargo ships, and expand the workforce of American sailors and shipyard workers. Right now, the U.S. has fewer than 200 large oceangoing ships, and only about 80 of them operate in international trade. Meanwhile, China has over 5,500 ships and builds vessels at a rate roughly 230 times greater than the U.S. The bill says this gap is a serious national security problem, because in a war or major crisis, the country might not have enough ships or trained sailors to move military supplies and keep the economy running. To fix this, the bill creates a new White House advisor called the Maritime Security Advisor, sets up a Maritime Security Trust Fund filled with fees and taxes collected from foreign ships entering U.S. ports, and uses that money to fund shipbuilding programs, loan guarantees for shipyards, and support for commercial fleets that fly the American flag.

The bill also tries to make sure more cargo shipped to and from the United States actually travels on American ships. It increases fees on foreign vessels, especially those connected to China, and strengthens rules requiring that goods paid for with U.S. government money be carried on American ships. It also creates a new program called the Strategic Commercial Fleet to subsidize American shipping companies operating internationally, similar to how the military already pays some ship operators to stay ready for wartime use.

A large section of the bill focuses on people. It aims to grow the number of licensed American merchant mariners, which currently stands at only about 12,000, by offering student loan forgiveness, job training programs, easier pathways for military veterans to become civilian mariners, and more funding for maritime academies. Shipyard workers would also see new training and recruitment programs. The bill affects American shipping companies, shipyards, maritime workers, military planners, and ultimately consumers, since stronger domestic shipping could influence the cost and reliability of imported goods.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets shipbuilding gap as China operates 5,500 vessels to U.S.'s 200
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1541 would rebuild U.S. shipbuilding and expand the merchant fleet, citing a national security gap with China's 5,500-ship fleet.
ai_sections.what: S 1541 would create a White House Maritime Security Advisor, establish a Maritime Security Trust Fund using fees from foreign ships, and fund shipbuilding programs and loan guarantees for shipyards. It would also launch a Strategic Commercial Fleet program to subsidize American shipping companies operating internationally. New rules would require more cargo tied to U.S. government money to travel on American-flagged ships.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects American shipping companies, shipyards, maritime workers, and military planners. Merchant mariners, maritime academy students, shipyard recruits, and military veterans seeking civilian mariner careers would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: The U.S. currently has fewer than 200 large oceangoing ships, with only about 80 in international trade, while China operates over 5,500 and builds ships at roughly 230 times the U.S. rate. Lawmakers say this gap could leave the country without enough ships or trained sailors to move military supplies or sustain the economy during a war or major crisis.
ai_sections.cost: The Maritime Security Trust Fund would be funded by fees and taxes collected from foreign ships entering U.S. ports, with higher fees targeting vessels connected to China. That money would pay for shipbuilding programs, loan guarantees, fleet subsidies, workforce training, student loan forgiveness, and increased funding for maritime academies.

Funds training, loans, and fleet subsidies

title: Stop Antisemitism on College Campuses Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change the rules colleges and universities must follow to stay eligible for federal student financial aid programs. Under current law, schools must meet certain requirements to participate in federal student loans and grants. This bill would add a new requirement: colleges cannot authorize, fund, or support any campus event that promotes antisemitism. The bill uses the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) definition of antisemitism, which covers hatred toward Jewish people and includes a list of specific contemporary examples.

If a college violates this rule, it could lose access to federal Title IV programs, which include Pell Grants and federal student loans. That is a significant consequence because most colleges depend heavily on that federal funding, and losing it would affect the entire school community, not just those involved in any particular event.

This bill most directly affects college administrators, who would need to screen campus events to avoid losing federal funding. It also affects students at those schools, since a loss of federal aid eligibility could make attending much more expensive or could disrupt their financial aid. Students who organize campus events, free speech advocates, and Jewish students and communities all have a stake in how this law would be applied. The bill was introduced in the Senate in March 2025 and has been referred to committee, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Senate bill would strip federal aid from colleges hosting antisemitic events
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.what: S 1182 would add a new eligibility requirement for colleges participating in federal student aid programs. Schools could not authorize, fund, or support campus events that promote antisemitism, as defined by the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance. The bill was introduced in the Senate in March 2025 and has been referred to committee, so it has not become law.

Because most colleges depend heavily on that funding, a loss of eligibility would affect the entire school community, not only those involved in a specific event.

title: Repeal the TikTok Ban Act
ai_summary:
This bill would undo a law passed in 2024 that required ByteDance, the Chinese company that owns TikTok, to sell the app or face a ban in the United States. If this bill became law, that sell-or-ban requirement would be completely erased, as if the original law never existed. Any official government designations of TikTok or similar apps as "foreign adversary controlled applications" would also be canceled.

The original 2024 law was based on national security concerns that a foreign-owned company could use the app to collect data on Americans or influence what people see. This bill argues, in effect, that the government should not have the power to ban or force the sale of an app, though the bill itself does not state a specific reason for the repeal.

This bill would most directly affect TikTok's roughly 170 million American users, as well as the creators and businesses that rely on the platform to reach audiences. It would also affect ByteDance and any other foreign-owned technology companies that could potentially be targeted under similar laws in the future. The bill was introduced by Senator Rand Paul and has been sent to the Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee, where it would need to advance before getting a full Senate vote.
ai_headline: Senate bill would wipe out TikTok sell-or-ban law entirely
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: S 153 would repeal the 2024 law forcing ByteDance to sell TikTok or face a U.S. ban, affecting roughly 170 million American users.
ai_sections.what: S 153 would repeal the 2024 law that required ByteDance, TikTok's Chinese parent company, to sell the app or face a ban in the United States. The repeal would erase the original law as if it never existed. Any government designations of TikTok or similar apps as foreign adversary controlled applications would also be canceled.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects TikTok's roughly 170 million American users, as well as creators and businesses that rely on the platform. ByteDance and other foreign-owned technology companies that could face similar laws in the future would also be affected.

The bill does not state a specific reason for the repeal, but its effect would be to remove the government's ability to ban or force the sale of the app under that law.

title: Health Marketplace for All Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new type of health insurance arrangement called a "health marketplace pool." Under current law, only actual employers can offer group health insurance plans to employees. This bill changes that by allowing any organization that forms one of these pools to be treated legally as an employer just for the purpose of offering group health coverage. That means groups of individuals, small businesses, associations, or other organizations could band together to buy health insurance as one large group, potentially getting lower rates that bigger employers usually get.

The pools would have to follow some rules. They cannot turn away members based on health status or pre-existing conditions. They must offer the same coverage to all members, and they can either buy insurance from an insurance company or self-insure. Interestingly, the pools could also offer drug-only plans covering prescription and over-the-counter medications, with no other medical benefits attached. The bill also makes clear that joining one of these pools does not make a member legally responsible as a fiduciary, meaning members would not carry the legal duties that come with managing a traditional employer-sponsored plan.

This bill would most directly affect people who currently lack access to affordable group health insurance, such as self-employed workers, gig workers, freelancers, and employees of very small businesses. It could also affect insurance companies, which might see new customers or new competition. Larger employers and traditional insurance markets could see indirect effects as well. People who already have employer-sponsored coverage or government programs like Medicare and Medicaid would not be directly impacted.
ai_headline: Bill would let freelancers and small businesses pool together to buy group health insurance
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 2086 lets individuals, gig workers, and small businesses form pools to buy group health insurance as one large employer.
ai_sections.what: S 2086 creates a new arrangement called a "health marketplace pool," allowing individuals, small businesses, and associations to band together and purchase group health insurance as a single large group. Under current law, only actual employers can offer group health plans, but this bill grants pool organizers legal employer status solely for the purpose of offering group coverage. Pools could buy insurance from a carrier or self-insure, and may also offer standalone drug-only plans covering prescription and over-the-counter medications.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects self-employed workers, gig workers, freelancers, and employees of very small businesses who currently lack access to affordable group health insurance. Insurance companies, larger employers, and traditional insurance markets could also see indirect effects, while people already covered by employer plans, Medicare, or Medicaid would not be directly impacted.
ai_sections.why: Pools gaining large-group purchasing status could shift pricing dynamics in insurance markets, potentially affecting rates and competition for existing insurers. The explicit rule that pool members carry no fiduciary legal duties changes the liability structure compared to traditional employer-sponsored plans.

title: Health Savings Accounts For All Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill makes big changes to Health Savings Accounts, which are special tax-free accounts people use to pay for medical costs. Right now, you can only open an HSA if you have a specific type of high-deductible health insurance plan. This bill removes that requirement, meaning anyone could open and contribute to an HSA regardless of what kind of health insurance they have, or even if they have no insurance at all. It also raises how much money you can put into an HSA each year, tying the new limit to the same higher caps used for workplace retirement accounts like 401(k)s. People age 50 and older could contribute even more.

The bill also expands what you can spend HSA money on tax-free. Currently the list covers doctor visits, prescriptions, and similar medical costs. This bill would add vitamins, dietary supplements, gym memberships, and wearable fitness trackers like smartwatches. It also lets you use HSA funds for direct primary care arrangements, where patients pay a flat monthly fee directly to a doctor. Another change allows people to use HSA money for medical expenses that happened before the account was opened, as long as those expenses occurred in the same tax year or the year before the account was set up.

A few other changes are included as well. If you make a paperwork or payroll mistake with your HSA contribution, you can correct it before your tax return is due without a penalty. When an HSA account holder dies, the account can now pass to a child, parent, or grandparent tax-free, not just a spouse. Finally, HSA funds would receive the same legal protections from creditors in bankruptcy that retirement accounts currently enjoy. These changes would affect anyone who currently has or might want an HSA, employers who contribute to employee HSAs, and people managing HSA funds after a family member's death.
ai_headline: Senate bill would open HSAs to all Americans, raise contribution caps
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3248 removes the high-deductible insurance requirement for HSAs and raises annual contribution limits to match 401(k) caps.
ai_sections.what: S 3248 removes the requirement that a person must have a high-deductible health insurance plan to open or contribute to a Health Savings Account. The bill raises annual HSA contribution limits to match the higher caps used for workplace retirement accounts like 401(k)s, with additional contributions allowed for people 50 and older. It also expands eligible HSA spending to include vitamins, dietary supplements, gym memberships, wearable fitness trackers, and direct primary care arrangements.
ai_sections.who: The changes would affect anyone who currently has or wants an HSA, employers who contribute to employee HSAs, and people managing HSA funds after a family member's death. People without any health insurance would also become eligible to open an HSA for the first time.
ai_sections.why: Removing the high-deductible plan requirement and raising contribution limits would alter how a broader population interacts with tax-advantaged medical savings. Expanding eligible expenses and inheritance options would change spending and estate decisions for current and future HSA holders.

title: Health Marketplace and Savings Accounts for All Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to health care law. First, it significantly expands Health Savings Accounts, or HSAs. Right now, HSAs are tax-free savings accounts you can only use if you have a specific type of high-deductible health insurance plan. This bill removes that requirement, meaning anyone could open and contribute to an HSA regardless of what insurance they have or whether they have insurance at all. It also raises the amount of money you can put in each year, lets you use HSA funds on vitamins, supplements, gym memberships, and fitness trackers, and allows unused HSA money to be passed on to a child or parent when the account holder dies, not just a spouse. HSA funds would also be protected from creditors in bankruptcy, the same way retirement accounts currently are.

Second, the bill creates a new type of organization called a "health marketplace pool." These pools would allow individuals, self-employed people, and small businesses to band together and buy health insurance as a group, the same way large employers already do. Currently, group health insurance is mainly available through employers. Under this bill, any qualifying organization could form one of these pools and offer group coverage to its members, potentially giving people access to lower group insurance rates even if their employer does not offer coverage.

The people most directly affected would be the roughly 28 million Americans who are uninsured or who buy their own individual health insurance, along with self-employed workers and small business employees who currently lack access to employer-sponsored group coverage. People who already have HSAs would also see expanded options for how they save and spend that money.
ai_headline: Senate bill would open health savings accounts to all Americans
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3362 opens HSAs to anyone regardless of insurance status and lets groups pool together to buy health coverage.
ai_sections.what: S 3362 removes the requirement that HSA holders carry a high-deductible health plan, raises annual contribution limits, and expands eligible spending to include vitamins, supplements, gym memberships, and fitness trackers. The bill also allows HSA funds to be inherited by a child or parent, not only a spouse, and protects those funds from creditors in bankruptcy. A second provision creates "health marketplace pools," letting individuals, self-employed people, and small businesses join together to purchase group health insurance the way large employers currently do.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 28 million Americans who are uninsured or who buy individual health insurance would be most directly affected, along with self-employed workers and small business employees who lack access to employer-sponsored coverage. People who already hold HSAs would also gain expanded saving and spending options.
ai_sections.why: Removing the high-deductible plan requirement and raising contribution limits would change how Americans can set aside pre-tax money for medical expenses. Creating health marketplace pools could shift how individuals and small businesses access group insurance rates that are currently available mainly through large employers.

title: Great American Healthcare Plan
ai_summary:
This bill makes three main changes to the US healthcare system. First, it expands Health Savings Accounts, which are special tax-free accounts people use to pay for medical costs. It raises how much money you can put in each year, lets more people open one even without a specific type of insurance plan, and allows HSA funds to be spent on more things like gym memberships, vitamins, healthy food, and health-sharing ministries. It also lets unused money from workplace health spending accounts roll over into an HSA, and allows family members like parents or children to inherit an HSA when the account holder dies.

Second, the bill requires hospitals, labs, imaging centers, and insurance companies to publicly post their actual prices for services. This means a hospital would have to show, on a free website, what it charges different insurance companies, what it charges people paying cash, and what the maximum and minimum prices are for each service. Fines would apply to hospitals that refuse to comply, and a senior hospital official would have to sign off saying the posted prices are accurate.

Third, the bill creates a new type of purchasing pool called a Health Marketplace Pool, which would let individuals, small businesses, and other groups band together to buy health insurance as a group, similar to how large employers currently get group rates. It also includes provisions related to cancer drug pricing and expanded access to certain experimental prescription drugs.

These changes would affect nearly all Americans in some way, but particularly people who currently use or are eligible for HSAs, patients trying to compare healthcare costs before receiving care, small business owners and self-employed individuals seeking affordable insurance options, and hospitals and insurers who would face new disclosure requirements.
ai_headline: HR 8324 would expand HSAs, force price disclosure, and create group insurance pools
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, Education and Workforce, the Judiciary, Armed Services, Veterans' Affairs, and Foreign Affairs, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8324 expands health savings accounts, mandates hospital price transparency, and lets small businesses pool insurance buying power.
ai_sections.what: HR 8324 makes three changes to US healthcare: it expands Health Savings Accounts by raising contribution limits, broadening eligibility, and allowing funds to cover gym memberships, vitamins, healthy food, and health-sharing ministries. It requires hospitals, labs, imaging centers, and insurers to publicly post actual prices for services, including rates negotiated with insurers and cash prices. It also creates Health Marketplace Pools, letting individuals and small businesses buy insurance collectively at group rates, and includes provisions on cancer drug pricing and access to certain experimental drugs.
ai_sections.who: The bill would affect nearly all Americans, with direct impact on HSA holders and those eligible to open one, patients comparing costs before receiving care, small business owners, and self-employed individuals. Hospitals and insurers would face new public disclosure requirements and potential fines.
ai_sections.why: Requiring hospitals and insurers to post prices publicly would create a searchable record of what different payers are charged for the same service. Allowing individuals and small businesses to pool purchasing would change how group insurance rates are currently structured for non-large employers.
ai_sections.cost: Hospitals that refuse to comply with price disclosure requirements would face fines. A senior hospital official would be required to certify that posted prices are accurate.
costChips: Fines for hospitals refusing to disclose

title: TREY'S Law
ai_summary:
TREY'S Law makes it illegal to enforce nondisclosure or confidentiality clauses in settlements or other contracts that prevent someone from speaking out about the sexual abuse of a child. In other words, if a lawsuit or legal settlement included a "stay silent" rule about child sexual abuse, that rule would now be void and unenforceable. Victims, alleged victims, or anyone with knowledge of such abuse could speak freely without worrying about being sued for violating a contract. The law applies to both new and old agreements, meaning contracts signed years ago are covered too.

The bill does allow some parts of confidentiality agreements to remain valid. For example, a settlement could still keep the dollar amount or payment terms private. What it cannot do is stop a victim or a witness from disclosing that the abuse happened or sharing facts about it. The law also overrides any state laws that would allow these kinds of silence clauses to be enforced, while still allowing states to pass their own laws that give victims even stronger protections.

This bill affects child sexual abuse survivors, witnesses or others who signed confidentiality agreements related to such abuse, and anyone who might try to legally enforce those agreements, such as alleged abusers or institutions. It is relevant in situations like civil lawsuits, out-of-court settlements, or employment agreements where silence about child abuse was made a condition. Supporters and critics alike would likely note it is a significant shift in how binding contracts involving child sexual abuse cases are treated under federal law.
ai_headline: Federal bill voids silence clauses tied to child sexual abuse cases
last_action_text: Held at the desk.

ai_sections.tldr: TREY'S Law makes confidentiality clauses that suppress child sexual abuse disclosures void and unenforceable in new and old contracts.
ai_sections.what: TREY'S Law makes it illegal to enforce nondisclosure or confidentiality clauses in settlements or other contracts that prevent someone from speaking out about the sexual abuse of a child. The law applies to both new and existing agreements, meaning contracts signed years ago are also covered. Confidentiality clauses covering financial details such as payment amounts or terms may still remain valid, but clauses that prevent disclosure of the abuse itself are void.
ai_sections.who: The law affects child sexual abuse survivors, witnesses, and anyone else who signed a confidentiality agreement related to such abuse. It also applies to parties who might try to enforce those agreements, including alleged abusers and institutions.
ai_sections.why: Victims, alleged victims, or anyone with knowledge of child sexual abuse could speak freely without risk of being sued for violating a contract. The law overrides state laws that would permit these silence clauses to be enforced, while still allowing states to enact stronger protections for victims.

title: Lower Your Taxes Act
ai_summary:
This bill would expand two existing tax credits and create a new monthly child benefit, while also raising some taxes on high earners and corporations to help pay for it.

For working families, the bill roughly doubles the Earned Income Tax Credit, which is a tax break for people who work but earn lower or moderate incomes. It raises the dollar amounts workers can receive, lets more people qualify, and lowers the income level at which the credit starts shrinking. It also removes the minimum age requirement, so workers 18 and older could claim it rather than having to be at least 25. Additionally, people in states that offer their own earned income credit that does not fully refund unused amounts would get extra federal payments to make up the difference.

The bill creates a new monthly child tax credit, paying families $350 per month for each child under 6 and $300 per month for each child aged 6 through 17. These payments would go out automatically each month rather than as a once-a-year tax refund. Families earning above roughly $112,500 to $150,000 per year, depending on filing status, would see reduced payments. To help cover these costs, the bill would raise the tax rate on capital gains income for high earners and increase certain corporate tax rates. The bill primarily affects low- and middle-income working adults, parents with children under 18, higher-income investors, and corporations.
ai_headline: HR 463 would send families up to $350 monthly per child and nearly double a key worker tax credit
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 463 creates monthly child payments of up to $350 and roughly doubles the Earned Income Tax Credit, funded partly by higher taxes on capital gains and corporations.
ai_sections.what: HR 463 expands the Earned Income Tax Credit by raising dollar amounts, broadening eligibility, and lowering the age requirement from 25 to 18. It creates a new monthly child benefit of $350 per child under 6 and $300 per child aged 6 through 17, paid automatically each month. Families earning above roughly $112,500 to $150,000 per year, depending on filing status, would receive reduced child payments.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects low- and middle-income working adults, parents with children under 18, higher-income investors, and corporations. Workers in states whose own earned income credits do not fully refund unused amounts would also receive additional federal payments under the bill.

Workers aged 18 to 24 who currently cannot claim the federal Earned Income Tax Credit would become newly eligible under this bill.
ai_sections.cost: Higher taxes on capital gains for high earners and increased corporate tax rates would help fund the expanded credits and new monthly child payments. The bill does not specify total spending or revenue figures in the summary.

title: Surface Transportation Research and Development Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Department of Transportation to continue and expand several research and technology programs related to roads, highways, and rail through 2031. It renews funding for university transportation research centers, a highway technology program, and an open research initiative that had been set to expire. It also creates a new internal council within the Department of Transportation to better coordinate how the department collects and manages transportation data, with the goal of reducing duplicate work and improving data quality.

The bill also kicks off a few specific studies and plans. It requires the government to study the safety effects of modern LED headlights, including whether bright headlights on cars and trucks create dangerous glare for other drivers, pedestrians, and cyclists. It directs the Federal Highway Administration to develop a national strategy for using reclaimed asphalt pavement, which is old road material recycled into new roads, as a potential cost-saving measure. It also expands rail research to include safety issues with hazardous materials transported by freight trains.

The people most affected by this bill are transportation researchers, university programs that receive federal research grants, state and local transportation departments, and eventually everyday drivers and commuters. The headlight study could lead to new safety rules affecting all car buyers. The recycled asphalt strategy could influence how road construction projects are carried out nationwide. Rail safety additions could affect freight companies and communities near rail lines.
ai_headline: Federal transportation research programs extended and expanded through 2031
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by Voice Vote.

It also launches a study on LED headlight glare safety, a national strategy for recycled asphalt pavement, and expanded rail research covering hazardous materials transport.

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 80 Atlantic Avenue in Oceanside, New York, as the "Detective Luis G. Alvarez Post Office".
ai_summary:
This bill officially renames a post office in Oceanside, New York. Specifically, the building at 80 Atlantic Avenue would be given the honorary name "Detective Luis G. Alvarez Post Office." The post office would still operate the same way it always has. The name change simply means that any official government documents, maps, or records referring to that building would use the new name going forward.

Detective Luis G. Alvarez was a New York City Police Department detective who worked in the rescue and recovery effort at Ground Zero after the September 11, 2001 attacks. He later developed cancer linked to his exposure at the site and became a well-known advocate for the September 11th Victim Compensation Fund before his death in 2019.

This bill affects very few people in a practical sense. Residents near that post office in Oceanside, New York, might see the name displayed on the building. It has no effect on mail service, postage rates, or government spending. Bills like this one are a common way Congress honors local figures, veterans, first responders, and community members.
ai_headline: Post office in Oceanside, NY to be named for 9/11 detective Luis Alvarez
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

The building continues to operate as before, and the change applies only to official government documents, maps, and records. Mail service and postage rates are not affected.

Alvarez was an NYPD detective who developed cancer after working in rescue and recovery at Ground Zero following the September 11, 2001 attacks, and who advocated for the September 11th Victim Compensation Fund before his death in 2019.

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 200 1st Avenue Southeast in Austin, Minnesota, as the "John Madden Memorial Post Office".
ai_summary:
This bill would give an official honorary name to the post office at 200 1st Avenue Southeast in Austin, Minnesota. If passed, that building would be formally known as the "John Madden Memorial Post Office." John Madden, the famous NFL coach and broadcaster, was born in Austin, Minnesota in 1936 and died in 2021. Naming a post office after someone is a common way Congress honors notable Americans.

The bill does not change how the post office operates, who works there, or how much anything costs. It simply adds an official name to the building. Any future government documents, maps, or records referring to that location would use the new name.

This bill mainly affects residents of Austin, Minnesota, who use that post office, as well as anyone who sends mail to or from that location. It was introduced by several members of Congress from Minnesota and has been sent to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform for review before it can move forward.

last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

title: Full-Service Community School Expansion Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would expand a federal program called Full-Service Community Schools. These are public schools that do more than just teach classes — they also connect students and families to health care, mental health services, housing help, food assistance, and other support services, all coordinated through the school itself. The bill would set aside large amounts of federal money for this program, starting at $500 million in 2027 and growing to $1 billion by 2031. Schools can apply for planning grants to get started, five-year grants to run the full program, or expansion grants if they already have a program and want to grow it.

To qualify, a school generally needs to have at least 40 percent of its students from low-income families, or be a school that the state has already flagged as needing extra support. Each participating school would be required to hire a community school coordinator — a full-time staff member who organizes all the services and partnerships. The bill also creates leadership teams that include parents, students, teachers, community members, and local organizations, all working together to decide what the school and neighborhood need most.

The people most directly affected would be students and families at low-income schools, especially those dealing with challenges like housing instability, lack of health care, or food insecurity. School districts, nonprofit organizations, local governments, tribal communities, and other community groups would also be involved as partners. Schools in rural areas and on tribal lands are specifically called out for extra attention and priority when grants are awarded.
ai_headline: Federal bill would fund health and housing services at low-income schools
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.what: HR 8752 would expand the Full-Service Community Schools program, which connects students and families to health care, mental health services, housing help, food assistance, and other support services coordinated through the school. Schools could apply for planning grants, five-year operating grants, or expansion grants. To qualify, a school generally must have at least 40 percent of students from low-income families or be designated by the state as needing extra support.
ai_sections.who: Students and families at low-income schools — particularly those facing housing instability, lack of health care, or food insecurity — would be most directly affected. School districts, nonprofit organizations, local governments, tribal communities, and community groups would participate as partners, with rural and tribal schools receiving priority consideration.

The bill also establishes leadership teams that include parents, students, teachers, community members, and local organizations to determine needs at the school and neighborhood level.
ai_sections.cost: Federal funding for the program would start at $500 million in 2027 and grow to $1 billion by 2031.

title: Full-Service Community School Expansion Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would expand a federal program that turns regular public schools into "full-service community schools." These are schools that go beyond just teaching classes — they also connect students and families with health care, mental health services, housing help, food assistance, job training, and other support services. The schools would have a paid coordinator on staff to manage all these services and partnerships with local nonprofits, hospitals, government agencies, and other community organizations. The bill proposes funding this program at $500 million in 2027, growing to $1 billion per year by 2031.

To qualify, a school generally needs to have at least 40 percent of its students eligible for free or reduced-price school lunches, or be a school that is already flagged by the state as needing extra support. School districts could apply for planning grants to get started, five-year grants to run the full program, or expansion grants if they already have community schools and want to grow the effort. The bill requires that schools form leadership teams that include parents, students, teachers, and community members, and that those meetings be open to the public.

The people most directly affected would be students and families in lower-income communities, as well as teachers and school staff at participating schools. School districts, nonprofit organizations, and local government agencies would also be involved as partners. States could also receive grants to support these schools at a broader level.
ai_headline: Senate bill would fund community services inside low-income public schools
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4504 would expand full-service community schools offering health, housing, and food aid, funded at up to $1B yearly by 2031.
ai_sections.what: S 4504 would expand a federal program that converts qualifying public schools into full-service community schools, adding on-site coordinators and connecting students and families with health care, mental health services, housing help, food assistance, and job training. Schools must have at least 40 percent of students eligible for free or reduced-price lunches, or be already designated by their state as needing extra support. Districts could apply for planning grants, five-year operating grants, or expansion grants, and must form public leadership teams that include parents, students, teachers, and community members.

ai_sections.cost: The bill proposes funding the program at $500 million in 2027, growing to $1 billion per year by 2031.

title: End Solitary Confinement Act
ai_summary:
This bill would ban the use of solitary confinement in all federal detention facilities, including federal prisons, immigration detention centers, and any private facilities that hold people under federal contracts. Currently, officials can lock someone in a cell alone for long periods as punishment or for security reasons. Under this bill, that practice would be largely eliminated. Instead, every person held in a federal facility would be guaranteed at least 14 hours per day outside their cell in shared spaces, with access to programs like education, job training, and mental health treatment. Isolation could only be used briefly in a true emergency, and even then only for a few hours at a time under strict rules. Certain groups, including people under 25 or over 55, pregnant women, people with disabilities or mental health diagnoses, and LGBTQ individuals, could never be placed in isolation at all.

The bill also sets up two layers of oversight. A community monitoring body made up largely of people who have personally experienced incarceration would be able to make surprise visits to any federal facility, interview detainees privately, and publish its findings. A separate Inspector General advisory board would produce annual reports on whether the law is being followed. Facilities and agencies would have to publicly report data every three months on how often and how long people are being isolated.

States and local governments that receive certain federal law enforcement grants would also feel the effects of this bill. They would need to show that their own jails and prisons follow similar rules, or face losing at least 10 percent of that federal funding. People who are harmed by violations of the law could sue in federal court and potentially receive money damages, including for emotional suffering.
ai_headline: Federal bill would ban solitary confinement across all U.S. detention facilities
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 2477 would ban solitary confinement in federal facilities and require 14 hours daily out-of-cell time for all detainees.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 2477 would eliminate solitary confinement in federal prisons, immigration detention centers, and private facilities operating under federal contracts. Detainees would be guaranteed at least 14 hours per day outside their cell with access to education, job training, and mental health programs. Isolation would only be permitted for a few hours at a time during a true emergency, under strict rules.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to all people held in federal detention facilities, including immigrants. People under 25 or over 55, pregnant women, people with disabilities or mental health diagnoses, and LGBTQ individuals could never be placed in isolation under any circumstances.
ai_sections.why: States and localities receiving certain federal law enforcement grants would be required to adopt similar rules or lose at least 10 percent of that funding. People harmed by violations could sue in federal court and seek money damages, including for emotional suffering.
ai_sections.cost: States and local governments that do not comply with similar isolation rules risk losing at least 10 percent of certain federal law enforcement grants. No federal spending figures are cited in the summary.

title: BLUE Act
ai_summary:
The BLUE Act would make it a federal crime to share personal information about federal law enforcement officers if the person sharing that information intends to physically block, interfere with, or get back at an officer for doing their job. This builds on an existing federal law that already makes it illegal to share private details about certain government workers to threaten or harm them. The new addition specifically covers situations where someone spreads an officer's personal information to disrupt a federal investigation, operation, or legal proceeding.

The bill also expands what counts as protected personal information. Under current law, sharing someone's home address or daily routine is restricted. This bill adds any physical location where a federal law enforcement officer is or is expected to be, whether they are on duty or off duty at the time. So for example, sharing that an officer will be at a specific place at a specific time, with the goal of interfering with their work or retaliating against them, could become a federal crime.

This bill mainly affects federal law enforcement officers, such as FBI agents, DEA agents, and similar federal workers, by giving them additional legal protections against a practice sometimes called "doxing." It also affects anyone who might share such location or personal information about those officers online or elsewhere. Average citizens would only be directly affected if they were involved in sharing that kind of targeted information about a federal officer.
ai_headline: Federal bill would make doxing of law enforcement officers a crime
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: The BLUE Act would make it a federal crime to share a law enforcement officer's location or personal data to interfere with or retaliate against them.
ai_sections.what: The BLUE Act would create a new federal crime for sharing personal information about federal law enforcement officers when the intent is to physically block, interfere with, or retaliate against them for doing their job. It expands existing federal law to also cover situations where someone spreads an officer's personal information to disrupt a federal investigation, operation, or legal proceeding. The bill broadens the definition of protected personal information to include any physical location where a federal officer is or is expected to be, whether on duty or off duty.
ai_sections.who: Federal law enforcement officers such as FBI and DEA agents would receive additional legal protections under this bill. Any person who shares targeted location or personal information about a federal officer online or elsewhere could be subject to the new federal crime.
ai_sections.why: Expanding the list of protected information and covered intent means more types of sharing could trigger federal criminal liability than under current law. Average citizens would only be directly affected if they were involved in sharing that kind of targeted information about a federal officer.

title: Respect Tribal IDs Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill requires all Department of Homeland Security immigration enforcement officers to complete training on how to properly interact with Native American tribal members. The training must be developed within 180 days of the bill becoming law and must be created with input from the Bureau of Indian Affairs and tribal representatives. Officers cannot perform any immigration enforcement duties until they have completed this training, and they must repeat it at least once a year.

The training covers several specific topics: how to recognize official tribal ID documents, how to accept those documents as valid proof of U.S. citizenship, proper behavior when interacting with enrolled tribal members, and the history of how American Indians became U.S. citizens. The training will also include a database of example tribal documents and hands-on practice exercises. Accepted tribal documents include tribal enrollment cards, certificates of degree of Indian blood, tribal census documents, and official letters on tribal letterhead.

This bill mainly affects two groups. First, it affects DHS immigration enforcement officers, who would be required to complete new training before doing their jobs. Second, it affects enrolled members of federally recognized Native American tribes, particularly those living near U.S. borders, who have sometimes had their tribal IDs questioned or rejected during immigration enforcement encounters. The Secretary of Homeland Security must report back to Congress within one year on how the training program is being put into practice.
ai_headline: DHS officers must train on tribal IDs before enforcing immigration law
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4516 bars DHS immigration officers from enforcement duties until they complete tribal ID training, repeated annually.
ai_sections.what: S 4516 requires all DHS immigration enforcement officers to complete training on interacting with Native American tribal members before performing any enforcement duties. The training must be developed within 180 days of enactment, with input from the Bureau of Indian Affairs and tribal representatives. It covers recognizing and accepting tribal enrollment cards, certificates of degree of Indian blood, tribal census documents, and official tribal letterhead letters as valid proof of U.S. citizenship.
ai_sections.who: DHS immigration enforcement officers are directly affected, as they cannot perform duties until trained and must repeat the training at least once a year. Enrolled members of federally recognized Native American tribes, especially those living near U.S. borders, are also affected, as their tribal IDs have sometimes been questioned or rejected during enforcement encounters.
ai_sections.why: Officers who have not completed the training are barred from immigration enforcement duties, creating a compliance requirement tied directly to job performance. The Secretary of Homeland Security must report to Congress within one year on how the training program is being implemented.

title: Election Mail Act
ai_summary:
This bill sets new rules for how the U.S. Postal Service and state election offices handle mail-in ballots and other election-related mail for federal elections. First, it requires the post office to process ballots on the same day they arrive at a facility, put a postmark showing the mailing date on every ballot envelope, and carry all election mail at the same speed as first-class mail. It also makes return postage free for completed mail-in ballots, and it bans the postal service from making operational changes that could slow down mail delivery during the 120 days before a federal election. That means no removing mail collection boxes without replacing them and no taking sorting machines out of service during that window.

Second, the bill requires states and local election offices to add trackable barcodes to mail-in ballot return envelopes so voters can follow their ballot through the mail, similar to tracking a package. Election offices must also use special labels and logos on ballot trays to make official ballots easier to identify and prioritize in the mail system. Starting in 2026, any mailed ballot for a federal election that is postmarked by Election Day must be accepted by election officials if it arrives within seven days after the election. States are still allowed to count ballots that arrive even later than that if their own laws permit it.

Third, the bill affects several specific groups. Voters who use mail-in ballots benefit from free return postage, faster processing, and the ability to track their ballots. State and local election officials must meet new requirements for barcodes and ballot labeling. The postal service must appoint election mail coordinators in its offices around the country and consult annually with Native American tribes to identify postal barriers to voting on tribal lands.
ai_headline: Senate bill sets same-day processing and free return postage for mail-in ballots
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2576 requires same-day ballot processing, free return postage, and mandates states accept ballots postmarked by Election Day if received within 7 days.
ai_sections.what: S 2576 requires the U.S. Postal Service to process ballots the day they arrive, postmark every ballot envelope, and carry election mail at first-class speed. Return postage on completed mail-in ballots would be free, and the postal service could not remove collection boxes or take sorting machines offline in the 120 days before a federal election. Starting in 2026, any ballot postmarked by Election Day must be accepted if it arrives within seven days after the election, though states may allow longer windows under their own laws.
ai_sections.who: Voters casting mail-in ballots, state and local election offices, the U.S. Postal Service, and Native American tribal communities are all directly affected. Election offices must add trackable barcodes and special labels to ballot return envelopes and trays, while the postal service must appoint election mail coordinators and consult annually with tribes.
ai_sections.why: The requirements create new operational obligations for the postal service and election offices, including technology, staffing, and procedural changes. The seven-day post-election acceptance window and the 120-day restriction on postal operations represent binding constraints on both federal and state-level election administration.

title: Vote at Home Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require all states to allow every registered voter to vote by mail in federal elections, meaning elections for president, U.S. Senate, and U.S. House. States would have to mail ballots to all registered voters at least two weeks before any federal election. Voters could then mail their completed ballots back for free, since the bill also eliminates postage costs for both sending and returning mail ballots. States could still keep in-person polling places open, but they could not add extra rules or requirements that would stop someone from choosing to vote by mail instead.

The bill also changes how voter registration works at the DMV. Right now, people can choose to register when getting or renewing a driver's license. Under this bill, eligible citizens who show proof of U.S. citizenship at the DMV would be automatically registered to vote unless they choose to opt out. If someone moves and updates their address at the DMV, their voter registration address would update too. Non-citizens would not be automatically registered, and anyone automatically registered by mistake would be protected from legal trouble as a result of that error.

This bill would affect all U.S. voters, state election offices, and the U.S. Postal Service. Voters who currently live in states that do not allow no-excuse mail voting would gain that option. State and local election offices would need to update how they print, mail, and process ballots. People who interact with their state DMV and are eligible to vote would also be affected through the automatic registration process.
ai_headline: All registered voters would receive mail ballots under Senate bill S 1887
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1887 requires states to mail ballots to every registered voter at least two weeks before federal elections, with free return postage.
ai_sections.what: S 1887 would require all states to mail ballots to every registered voter at least two weeks before federal elections, covering races for president, U.S. Senate, and U.S. House. Return postage for completed ballots would be free, and states could not impose extra rules that block a voter from choosing mail over in-person voting. The bill also makes voter registration at the DMV automatic for eligible citizens who show proof of U.S. citizenship, unless they opt out, and links address updates at the DMV to voter registration records.
ai_sections.who: All U.S. voters, state and local election offices, and the U.S. Postal Service would be affected. Eligible citizens who visit the DMV and non-citizens who could be registered by error are also directly covered by the bill's provisions.
ai_sections.why: Voters in states that currently do not allow no-excuse mail voting would gain that option under federal mandate. State and local election offices would need to update how they print, mail, and process ballots, and the DMV automatic registration process would require changes to how those agencies share data.
ai_sections.cost: The bill eliminates postage costs for voters returning mail ballots, meaning that cost would be absorbed elsewhere, though the summary does not specify who bears it.

title: POLL Act
ai_summary:
The POLL Act would set a federal rule that no voter in a federal election should have to wait more than 30 minutes to cast a ballot. Before each federal election, states would have to publish a public plan explaining how they will keep wait times under 30 minutes. After each election, the federal Election Assistance Commission would review wait times across the country. If voters in a given area waited more than 60 minutes, the Attorney General could step in and require that jurisdiction to follow a remedial plan to fix the problem in future elections. The bill also requires states to have a minimum number of voting machines, poll workers, and other resources at each polling location, with federal standards set by the Attorney General to guide that distribution. If equipment breaks down and causes long delays, poll workers must offer paper ballots right away.

The bill also lets individual voters sue in federal court if they waited more than 30 minutes. A voter could receive at least $50, plus $50 for each additional hour of waiting, along with attorney fees. If a court finds the long wait was caused by intentional vote suppression, those amounts jump to $650 and $150 per extra hour. The bill also bars the top state election official in each state from actively participating in federal political campaigns for any race they oversee, unless they fully recuse themselves from running that election.

This bill would affect all voters who participate in federal elections, meaning elections for President, U.S. Senate, and U.S. House. It would also directly affect state and local election administrators, who would need to meet new planning and staffing requirements. States could receive federal funding, up to $500 million per year, to help cover the costs of meeting these new standards.
ai_headline: Federal bill would cap voting wait times at 30 minutes nationwide
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: The POLL Act caps federal election wait times at 30 minutes and lets voters sue for at least $50 if that limit is broken.

States would be required to publish pre-election plans and meet minimum staffing and equipment standards at polling locations. Voters who wait more than 30 minutes could sue in federal court and receive at least $50, with higher amounts if a court finds intentional vote suppression.

The bill would also bar top state election officials from actively participating in federal campaigns for races they oversee unless they fully recuse themselves from running that election.
