title: Election Mail Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes several changes to how the U.S. Postal Service and state election offices handle mail-in and absentee ballots for federal elections. First, it requires the post office to process ballots on the same day they arrive at a postal facility, put a postmark on every ballot envelope showing when it was mailed, and treat all election mail with the same speed and priority as first-class mail. It also bans the post office from making operational changes, like removing mailboxes or shutting down sorting machines, during the 120 days leading up to a federal election. Completed mail-in ballots would be carried free of postage, meaning voters would not need a stamp to return them.

Second, the bill sets new rules for tracking ballots. States and local election offices would be required to put a tracking barcode on the return envelopes they send with mail-in ballots, unless they already have another system that lets voters track their ballot. Election officials would also have to use special labels and logos on ballot trays so postal workers can easily identify and prioritize them. The post office would be required to appoint an Election Mail Coordinator in each of its regional offices to work directly with election officials.

Third, the bill creates a national seven-day window after Election Day for mail-in ballots to be counted in federal races, as long as the ballot was postmarked by Election Day. This would apply starting with the November 2026 election. The bill also requires the Postmaster General to meet annually with Native American tribal governments to identify and address any postal barriers that make it harder for people living on tribal lands to vote.
ai_headline: Postal Service would be banned from removing mailboxes in the 120 days before federal elections
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on House Administration, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4915 sets same-day ballot processing, free return postage, and a 7-day post-Election Day counting window starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 4915 requires the U.S. Postal Service to process ballots the day they arrive, postmark every ballot envelope, and treat election mail at first-class speed. Mail-in ballots would be returned free of postage, and the post office could not remove mailboxes or shut down sorting machines in the 120 days before a federal election. Starting with the November 2026 election, states must count mail-in ballots received up to seven days after Election Day if they were postmarked by Election Day.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects voters who use mail-in or absentee ballots in federal elections, as well as state and local election offices that administer them. The U.S. Postal Service, its regional offices, and the Postmaster General all face new operational and reporting requirements.
ai_sections.why: Ballot tracking requirements and same-day processing rules would change how election offices and postal facilities coordinate during election periods. The seven-day post-Election Day counting window would affect when final results in federal races can be certified under state law.
ai_sections.cost: The summary does not include spending figures, funding amounts, or fine structures, but it does specify that voters would no longer pay postage to return completed ballots, shifting that cost elsewhere.

title: American Citizens First Act
ai_summary:
This bill would cut off most federal benefits for non-citizens living in the United States. That includes Medicaid (except emergency care), food stamps, housing assistance, federal student aid, and certain tax credits. It would apply to legal immigrants, visa holders, and others who are not U.S. citizens or nationals. It would also allow the government to strip citizenship from naturalized Americans who are convicted of, or found by the Department of Homeland Security to have taken part in, riots, violent protests, property destruction, or actions seen as threatening the constitutional order. Those people could then be deported through a fast-track removal process.

The bill also expands the government's power to quickly deport undocumented immigrants without a full court hearing, removing previous limits based on how long someone has lived in the country. It requires a full security re-screening of Afghan refugees, special immigrant visa holders, and parolees who came to the United States starting January 20, 2021, and freezes new Afghan refugee and visa processing until that review is certified complete. Federal resettlement funding for Afghans would also be paused during that period.

Finally, the bill changes how Temporary Protected Status works. TPS allows people from certain dangerous or disaster-affected countries to live and work in the United States temporarily. Under this bill, TPS for a country's nationals would automatically end if their crime rate, including traffic violations and minor offenses, exceeds the national average by 20 percent or more. This would apply retroactively to TPS designations made after January 2021, specifically naming Afghanistan, Haiti, Venezuela, and Somalia. People currently holding TPS from those countries could lose their protected status as a result.
ai_headline: Senate bill would strip benefits from legal immigrants and revoke naturalized citizenship
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3318 would end most federal benefits for non-citizens and allow citizenship revocation for naturalized Americans convicted of rioting or property destruction.
ai_sections.what: S 3318 would cut off most federal benefits for non-citizens, including Medicaid except emergency care, food stamps, housing assistance, federal student aid, and certain tax credits. It would allow the government to revoke citizenship from naturalized Americans convicted of, or found by the Department of Homeland Security to have participated in, riots, violent protests, property destruction, or actions seen as threatening the constitutional order. The bill also expands fast-track deportation authority, requires security re-screening of Afghan refugees and visa holders who arrived on or after January 20, 2021, and changes Temporary Protected Status rules so that TPS automatically ends for nationals of a country if their crime rate exceeds the national average by 20 percent or more.
ai_sections.who: The bill would affect legal immigrants, visa holders, and all non-citizens or non-nationals living in the United States, as well as naturalized American citizens. Afghan refugees, special immigrant visa holders, and parolees who arrived starting January 20, 2021, and people currently holding TPS from Afghanistan, Haiti, Venezuela, and Somalia would face specific additional consequences.
ai_sections.why: Non-citizens who lose federal benefits would no longer have access to programs such as Medicaid, food assistance, or housing support. Naturalized citizens whose citizenship is revoked could face deportation through a fast-track removal process, and TPS holders from named countries could lose their legal permission to live and work in the United States.
ai_sections.cost: Federal resettlement funding for Afghans would be paused during the period while security re-screening is completed and certified. New Afghan refugee and visa processing would also be frozen for that same period.

title: Naturalization Accountability Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes three changes to existing immigration and citizenship law. First, it says that if a naturalized U.S. citizen is convicted of any felony at any time, the government can revoke their citizenship. Under current law, a felony conviction is not automatically a reason to strip someone of citizenship. Second, it removes a five-year time limit that currently exists for revoking citizenship from people who joined certain banned organizations, such as groups considered totalitarian or treasonous, after becoming citizens. Right now, the government only has five years to act; this bill would eliminate that deadline entirely.

The third change deals with criminal penalties for people who obtained citizenship illegally or through fraud. Currently there is a ten-year statute of limitations on prosecuting that specific crime, meaning the government has ten years to bring charges. This bill removes that time limit for one particular offense, which involves illegally procuring naturalization, so prosecutors could bring charges at any point in the future, no matter how long ago the alleged wrongdoing occurred.

This bill primarily affects naturalized U.S. citizens, meaning people who were not born American but went through the legal process to become citizens. It does not affect people who were born as U.S. citizens, since their citizenship cannot be revoked in the same way. People who have a felony conviction after becoming citizens, or who joined certain organizations, would face a greater risk of losing their citizenship under this proposal.
ai_headline: Bill would let government strip naturalized citizens of citizenship after any felony
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4105 lets the government revoke naturalized citizenship after any felony and removes key deadlines for two other revocation actions.
ai_sections.what: S 4105 makes three changes to immigration and citizenship law. It allows the government to revoke naturalized citizenship following any felony conviction, removes the existing five-year deadline to revoke citizenship from people who joined certain banned organizations after naturalizing, and eliminates the ten-year statute of limitations on prosecuting one specific crime involving illegally obtaining naturalization.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects naturalized U.S. citizens, meaning people who were not born American but completed the legal process to become citizens. It does not affect people born as U.S. citizens, whose citizenship cannot be revoked in the same way.
ai_sections.why: Naturalized citizens with a felony conviction or ties to certain banned organizations would face a greater risk of losing their citizenship under these changes. Removing the statutes of limitations means the government could pursue revocation or prosecution at any point in the future, regardless of how long ago the conduct occurred.

title: Mail Ballot Integrity Act
ai_summary:
This bill would ban states from automatically mailing ballots to all registered voters for federal elections like congressional and presidential races. Right now, some states send every registered voter a mail ballot without them asking for one. Under this bill, that practice would be illegal. Instead, a voter would have to request a mail ballot and sign a statement, under penalty of perjury, confirming they qualify for one.

The bill creates a specific list of people who would be allowed to request a mail ballot. That list includes active-duty military members and their families, people who will be away from their home county on Election Day, voters aged 65 and older, people with certain disabilities, those who are hospitalized, people who are incarcerated but not for a felony, overseas residents, and a handful of other situations. Anyone who doesn't fit one of these categories would need to vote in person.

This bill would affect voters in every state, but it would have the biggest impact in states that currently mail ballots to all registered voters automatically, such as Colorado, Oregon, Washington, and Hawaii. People who currently rely on receiving a ballot without asking for one would now need to take an extra step to request it and prove they qualify. States would also need to update their election systems to comply if the bill becomes law.
ai_headline: Bill would end automatic mail ballot sends to all registered voters
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7874 would ban states from automatically mailing ballots, requiring voters to request one and prove they qualify under a specific list of eligible reasons.
ai_sections.what: HR 7874 would make it illegal for states to automatically mail ballots to all registered voters for federal elections. Voters would instead need to submit a request and sign a statement under penalty of perjury confirming they qualify. A defined list of eligible categories would determine who may request a mail ballot at all.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects voters in every state, with the greatest impact in states that currently mail ballots to all registered voters automatically, including Colorado, Oregon, Washington, and Hawaii. Voters who do not qualify under the bill's listed categories would be required to vote in person.
ai_sections.why: Voters who currently receive a ballot without asking for one would need to take an extra step to request it and demonstrate they qualify. States would also need to update their election systems to comply with the new requirements if the bill becomes law.

title: Vote at Home Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require every state to allow all eligible voters to cast their ballots by mail in federal elections, meaning elections for President, Senate, and House of Representatives. States would have to mail ballots to all registered voters at least two weeks before Election Day, and both sending and returning those ballots would be free of charge through the postal service. States could still run regular polling places, so voters who prefer to vote in person would still be able to do so.

The bill also makes changes to how people register to vote through their state's DMV. Right now, when you get or renew a driver's license, you can choose to register to vote at the same time. This bill would make that process simpler by cutting down on paperwork. It would also create automatic voter registration, meaning that if the DMV already has information showing you are a U.S. citizen, you would be automatically registered to vote unless you choose to opt out. If you move, your voter registration address would be updated automatically as well.

This bill would affect every eligible voter in the United States, especially people who find it hard to get to a polling place due to work schedules, disabilities, transportation issues, or other barriers. It would also affect state and local election officials, who would need to update their systems and processes to comply with the new rules. The DMV changes would apply to anyone who applies for, renews, or updates a driver's license.
ai_headline: All registered voters would receive mail ballots under new federal bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on House Administration, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2847 would require states to mail free ballots to all registered voters at least two weeks before federal elections.
ai_sections.what: HR 2847 would require every state to allow mail voting in all federal elections, covering races for President, Senate, and House. States would have to send ballots to all registered voters at least two weeks before Election Day, with postage covered in both directions. In-person polling places could still operate alongside the mail option.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects every eligible voter in the United States, particularly those with barriers to in-person voting such as work schedules, disabilities, or transportation problems. State and local election officials would also be required to update their systems and processes to meet the new rules.
ai_sections.why: States would need to build out mail ballot infrastructure and revise election administration procedures to comply. DMV agencies would also need to update systems to support automatic voter registration and address syncing for anyone who applies for, renews, or updates a driver's license.
ai_sections.cost: The summary does not include information about spending, funding, or fines associated with this bill.

title: SCAM Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the SCAM Act, would make it easier for the federal government to strip naturalized citizens of their citizenship. Right now, the government can already take away citizenship in certain cases, but this bill adds new specific reasons and new procedures. Under this bill, if a naturalized citizen joins or helps a terrorist organization within 10 years of becoming a citizen, defrauds a government program out of at least $10,000 within 10 years, or commits a serious felony or espionage crime within 10 years, the government can use that as strong legal evidence to take their citizenship away. The bill also allows the Attorney General, not just federal prosecutors, to bring these cases to court.

If citizenship is taken away under this bill, it would be treated as though the person was never a citizen to begin with, going all the way back to the date they were naturalized. That means the removal of citizenship is backdated, not just effective from the moment of the court decision. After losing citizenship, the person could then be quickly removed from the country through a fast-track deportation process, regardless of how long they have lived here or what their immigration status becomes afterward.

This bill affects only naturalized citizens, meaning people who were born in another country and went through the legal process to become U.S. citizens. It does not apply to people who are citizens by birth. People who could be affected include those convicted of fraud against government programs, espionage, serious crimes, or those found to have ties to terrorist groups, specifically if those actions happened within the first 10 years after they became citizens.
ai_headline: New bill would let feds revoke naturalized citizenship more easily
last_action_text: Read the second time. Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 301.

ai_sections.tldr: The SCAM Act adds new grounds to strip naturalized citizens of citizenship and allows fast-track deportation afterward.
ai_sections.what: The SCAM Act adds new legal grounds for the federal government to revoke naturalized citizenship, including joining a terrorist organization, defrauding a government program of at least $10,000, or committing a serious felony or espionage crime, all within 10 years of naturalization. The bill also allows the Attorney General, not just federal prosecutors, to bring these cases to court. If citizenship is revoked, it is backdated to the original naturalization date, as if the person was never a citizen.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects only naturalized citizens, meaning people born abroad who completed the legal citizenship process. It does not apply to birthright citizens.
ai_sections.why: People who lose citizenship under this bill could be placed into a fast-track deportation process regardless of how long they have lived in the United States or what immigration status they hold afterward. The backdating of citizenship removal may affect legal rights or protections that applied during the period between naturalization and the court decision.

title: SCAM Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it easier for the federal government to strip naturalized U.S. citizens of their citizenship. Right now, the government can pursue denaturalization mainly if someone lied or committed fraud during the application process itself. This bill would add three new reasons: if a naturalized citizen joins or helps a foreign terrorist organization, commits fraud against a government program totaling at least $10,000, or is convicted of a serious felony or espionage crime. All three of these triggers would have to happen within 10 years of becoming a citizen. The bill also lets the Attorney General directly start these legal proceedings, which is a new authority.

A key detail is how the bill is written: if someone does any of these things after becoming a citizen, that act is treated as automatic proof that the person was never truly qualified for citizenship in the first place, even if nothing seemed wrong at the time they applied. This legal approach makes it easier for the government to win a denaturalization case in court. Once someone loses their citizenship this way, it is treated as though they were never a citizen at all, and they can be removed from the country through a fast-track deportation process.

This bill only affects naturalized citizens, meaning people who were born in another country and went through the legal process to become American. It does not apply to people who were born as U.S. citizens. The affected group includes roughly 24 million naturalized citizens living in the United States. Critics and supporters will likely debate whether this approach is fair, but the bill itself is currently in committee and has not become law.
ai_headline: Bill would expand grounds for stripping naturalized citizens of citizenship
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7156 adds three new grounds to strip naturalized citizens of citizenship and allows fast-track deportation once denaturalized.
ai_sections.what: HR 7156 would add three new reasons the federal government can pursue denaturalization: joining or aiding a foreign terrorist organization, committing fraud against a government program totaling at least $10,000, or being convicted of a serious felony or espionage crime. All three triggers must occur within 10 years of naturalization. The bill also grants the Attorney General new authority to directly initiate denaturalization proceedings.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies only to naturalized citizens, not people born in the United States. Approximately 24 million naturalized citizens living in the United States would be affected.
ai_sections.why: Under the bill's legal framework, any qualifying act committed after naturalization is treated as automatic proof the person was never truly qualified for citizenship, making it easier for the government to win denaturalization cases in court. A person who loses citizenship this way is treated as though they were never a citizen and can be removed from the country through a fast-track deportation process.

title: Protecting and Preserving Social Security Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to Social Security. First, it would switch the way annual cost-of-living raises are calculated for Social Security recipients. Right now, raises are based on a general inflation measure. This bill would create a new price index specifically tracking what people 62 and older actually spend money on, such as healthcare and housing, and use that number instead. The idea is that spending patterns for older Americans are different from the general population, and their annual raises should reflect that.

Second, the bill changes how Social Security payroll taxes work for higher earners. Right now, Social Security taxes only apply to the first roughly $176,000 of a person's wages. This bill would phase out that cap between 2026 and 2031, so that by 2032, all wages above the current limit would be subject to Social Security taxes. This applies to both regular employees and self-employed people. In exchange, those higher earners would also receive a modest additional benefit credit for those extra earnings when they retire, though at a much lower rate than lower earners receive.

The people most directly affected are current Social Security recipients, who could see slightly larger annual raises under the new inflation formula, and workers who earn above roughly $176,000 a year, who would pay more in Social Security taxes starting in 2026. Employers of high earners would also pay more in matching payroll taxes. Lower and middle income workers would see no change in what they pay.
ai_headline: HR 4968 would raise Social Security taxes on wages above $176,000
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committees on Energy and Commerce, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4968 removes Social Security's wage cap for earners above ~$176,000 by 2032 and creates a new inflation index for recipients 62 and older.
ai_sections.what: HR 4968 makes two changes to Social Security. It replaces the current general inflation formula for annual cost-of-living raises with a new price index built around the actual spending patterns of people 62 and older, such as healthcare and housing. It also phases out the payroll tax wage cap between 2026 and 2031, so that starting in 2032 all wages above the current limit are subject to Social Security taxes.
ai_sections.who: Current Social Security recipients and workers earning above roughly $176,000 a year are most directly affected. Employers of high earners would also pay more in matching payroll taxes, while lower and middle income workers would see no change.
ai_sections.why: Older Americans tend to spend differently than the general population, particularly on healthcare and housing, which the current inflation measure does not specifically account for. Removing the wage cap would require higher earners and their employers to pay Social Security taxes on a larger share of income starting in 2026.
ai_sections.cost: Workers earning above roughly $176,000 would pay Social Security payroll taxes on all wages above that threshold starting in 2032, following a phase-in from 2026 to 2031. Employers of those workers would pay matching amounts, and higher earners would receive a modest additional retirement benefit credit at a lower rate than lower earners receive.
costChips: Higher earners get modest extra benefit

title: Protecting Circuit Boards and Substrates Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new federal tax credit equal to 25 percent of what a business spends on printed circuit boards that are made in the United States. Printed circuit boards are the flat green boards found inside nearly every electronic device, from phones and computers to cars and military equipment. Starting with purchases made after December 31, 2025, qualifying businesses can subtract 25 cents from their federal tax bill for every dollar they spend on American-made circuit boards.

The credit applies to businesses that buy or acquire these boards, not necessarily the companies that make them. So if a company assembles electronics and purchases circuit boards manufactured in the US, it can claim the credit. The boards must actually be fabricated, meaning physically produced from raw materials, inside the United States to qualify.

This bill mainly affects businesses across industries that use circuit boards in their products or operations, such as electronics manufacturers, defense contractors, automakers, and technology companies. It could also benefit American circuit board manufacturers, since the tax incentive gives their customers a financial reason to choose domestic suppliers over cheaper foreign ones, particularly from countries like China, which currently dominates global circuit board production.
ai_headline: New 25% tax credit targets purchases of US-made circuit boards
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4569 gives businesses a 25% federal tax credit on US-made printed circuit board purchases starting Jan. 1, 2026.
ai_sections.what: S 4569 creates a federal tax credit equal to 25 percent of what a business spends on printed circuit boards manufactured inside the United States. The credit applies to buyers of the boards, not only their makers, and covers boards that are physically produced from raw materials on US soil. Purchases must be made after December 31, 2025 to qualify.
ai_sections.who: The credit is available to any business that buys American-made circuit boards, including electronics manufacturers, defense contractors, automakers, and technology companies. Domestic circuit board fabricators are also affected, because the incentive gives their customers a financial reason to choose US suppliers.
ai_sections.why: The tax incentive shifts the cost calculation for businesses that currently source circuit boards from foreign suppliers, particularly in countries like China, which dominates global production. Companies that switch to domestic suppliers reduce their federal tax liability by 25 cents for every dollar spent on qualifying boards.
ai_sections.cost: For every dollar a qualifying business spends on US-made circuit boards, it may subtract 25 cents from its federal tax bill.
costChips: Applies to buyers, not only makers

title: Iran War Oil Crisis Windfall Profits Tax Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a special tax on large oil companies during a period of conflict with Iran. Specifically, it targets companies that produce or import more than 100,000 barrels of oil per day. For every barrel of oil they sell above $75 per barrel, they would have to pay a tax equal to 100 percent of that extra amount. For example, if oil is selling at $100 per barrel, the company would owe $25 in tax on each barrel. The tax would stay in place until the President officially declares that fighting with Iran has stopped, the Strait of Hormuz shipping lane is fully open again, and oil prices drop back below $75 per barrel.

All the money collected from that tax would go into a dedicated government fund called the Iran War Gasoline Price Relief Fund. The government would then calculate how much money is in that fund each quarter and divide it up equally among eligible American taxpayers as a rebate, similar to a tax credit. Most U.S. residents who file taxes would qualify. People who are claimed as dependents on someone else's taxes, non-resident foreign nationals, and estates or trusts would not be eligible. The rebate would be refundable, meaning people could receive it even if they owe little or no federal income tax.

This bill mainly affects large oil producers and importers on one side, and everyday American taxpayers on the other. Big oil companies doing business in the U.S. would face significantly higher costs during this period. Individual Americans, by contrast, would receive periodic payments intended to help offset higher gas and energy prices caused by the conflict. U.S. territories like Puerto Rico and Guam would also receive payments through a separate process outlined in the bill.
ai_headline: New bill would tax big oil profits and mail checks to Americans during Iran conflict
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8803 would tax large oil companies 100% on barrel sales above $75 and return that money equally to eligible U.S. taxpayers.
ai_sections.what: HR 8803 would impose a 100 percent tax on the amount oil companies receive above $75 per barrel during a conflict with Iran. It applies only to companies producing or importing more than 100,000 barrels per day. The tax ends when the President declares fighting has stopped, the Strait of Hormuz is fully open, and oil prices fall below $75 per barrel.
ai_sections.who: Large oil producers and importers operating in the U.S. would pay the tax. Most U.S. residents who file taxes would receive rebate payments, excluding dependents, non-resident foreign nationals, and estates or trusts.
ai_sections.why: Revenue collected would be distributed equally as refundable rebates to eligible taxpayers each quarter, including residents of U.S. territories like Puerto Rico and Guam through a separate process. Large oil companies would face significantly higher operating costs for the duration of the conflict period.
ai_sections.cost: All tax revenue would flow into a dedicated fund called the Iran War Gasoline Price Relief Fund and be divided equally among eligible American taxpayers as quarterly refundable rebates. At $100 per barrel, companies would owe $25 in tax on each barrel sold.
costChips: $25 tax per barrel if oil sells at $100
Rebates split equally among eligible filers

title: Tax Cut for Striking Workers Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would change the federal tax code so that strike pay is no longer counted as taxable income. Right now, when a union pays its members during a strike or lockout to help replace the wages they are not getting from their employer, that money is treated as regular income and taxed. Under this bill, those payments would be tax-free. The change would apply to payments received starting January 1, 2026.

The bill also makes sure that strike pay would still count as earned income for the purpose of the Earned Income Tax Credit. That matters because the EITC is a tax credit designed to help lower- and middle-income workers, and counting strike pay as earned income means workers would not lose access to that credit just because they were on strike.

This bill mainly affects union members who go on strike or are locked out by their employer and receive financial support payments from their union during that time. It could also affect workers covered under the Railway Labor Act, which covers railroad and airline employees. Workers who do not belong to a union or who do not receive strike benefits from a union would not be directly affected.
ai_headline: Strike pay would become tax-free income under Senate bill S 2779
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 2779 would make union strike and lockout pay tax-free starting January 1, 2026, while preserving Earned Income Tax Credit eligibility.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 2779 would remove strike and lockout payments from taxable income under the federal tax code. The change would apply to payments received on or after January 1, 2026. The bill also ensures that strike pay continues to count as earned income for purposes of the Earned Income Tax Credit.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects union members who go on strike or are locked out by their employer and receive financial support payments from their union. Workers covered under the Railway Labor Act, including railroad and airline employees, may also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, union strike and lockout payments are treated as regular taxable income, reducing the take-home value of those payments for workers. Maintaining strike pay as earned income for the Earned Income Tax Credit means workers on strike would not lose access to that credit during the period they are not receiving employer wages.

title: Tax Cut for Striking Workers Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would change the federal tax code so that strike pay is no longer counted as taxable income. Right now, when a union pays its members "strike benefits" to help replace lost wages during a strike or lockout, that money is treated like regular income and workers have to pay federal income tax on it. Under this bill, those payments would be tax-free at the federal level, similar to how certain other forms of compensation, like combat pay for military members, are excluded from taxes.

The bill applies specifically to payments made by tax-exempt labor unions to their members during a strike, lockout, or work stoppage covered under either the National Labor Relations Act or the Railway Labor Act. It would also count strike benefits as earned income for the purpose of the Earned Income Tax Credit, which could allow some lower-income workers to qualify for or receive a larger amount of that credit. The change would take effect for payments received starting January 1, 2027.

This bill would directly affect union members who go on strike or are locked out by their employer and receive financial support from their union during that time. It would not affect non-union workers or workers who are not receiving official strike pay from a labor organization. Employers and the federal government would also be affected, since workers would owe less in taxes and the government would collect less revenue from those payments.
ai_headline: Strike pay would become tax-free under proposed federal bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: Enhanced Small Business Growth Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes the tax code to give a bigger tax deduction to certain business owners who manufacture physical goods in the United States. Right now, many small business owners can deduct 20 percent of their business income from their taxes under an existing rule called the qualified business income deduction. This bill would raise that deduction to 30 percent for business owners who qualify as domestic manufacturers. To qualify, at least 85 percent of a business's income must come from making physical products, and at least 20 percent of the costs tied to those products must come from labor and overhead expenses inside the United States.

The bill also makes a smaller technical change to how taxable income is calculated for people who itemize their deductions, specifically removing charitable donations from the income figure used to determine the deduction amount. Both changes would take effect starting with the 2026 tax year.

This bill primarily affects self-employed people and owners of pass-through businesses, meaning businesses like sole proprietorships, partnerships, and S-corporations where income is reported on the owner's personal tax return rather than a separate corporate return. The biggest impact would be felt by small and mid-sized manufacturers who make physical goods and do a meaningful share of their production work inside the country. Larger corporations that pay the standard corporate income tax are not affected by this change.
ai_headline: Tax deduction for U.S. manufacturers would rise from 20% to 30% under House bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8755 raises the pass-through business income deduction to 30% for owners who manufacture physical goods mostly in the U.S., starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 8755 would increase the qualified business income deduction from 20 percent to 30 percent for business owners who qualify as domestic manufacturers. To qualify, at least 85 percent of a business's income must come from making physical products, and at least 20 percent of the costs tied to those products must come from labor and overhead expenses inside the United States. The bill also removes charitable donations from the taxable income figure used to calculate the deduction for people who itemize, with both changes taking effect in the 2026 tax year.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects self-employed people and owners of pass-through businesses, including sole proprietorships, partnerships, and S-corporations. Large corporations subject to the standard corporate income tax are not affected.
ai_sections.why: Small and mid-sized manufacturers who produce physical goods and conduct a meaningful share of their production work domestically would see the largest change in their deductible income. Owners who do not meet the 85 percent income or 20 percent labor and overhead thresholds would remain at the existing 20 percent deduction rate.

title: Manufactured Housing Community Sustainability Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new federal tax credit for owners of mobile home parks, also called manufactured housing communities, who sell their property to the residents who live there or to a qualifying nonprofit organization. Specifically, the seller would receive a tax credit equal to 75 percent of the profit they made on the sale. To qualify, the seller must have owned the property for at least two years, and the buyer must agree to keep running it as a manufactured housing community for at least 50 years. Both the buyer and seller have to sign legal documents confirming the deal meets all the rules.

The bill is aimed at encouraging more park owners to sell to residents rather than to commercial real estate investors or developers. When residents buy the land together, they form a cooperative where each household owns a share and votes on community decisions. The bill notes that in resident-owned communities, lot rent increases average less than 1 percent per year, compared to nearly 6 percent per year in commercially owned parks. If the new owner later breaks the 50-year promise and sells the land for another use, they face a penalty tax of 20 percent of the sale proceeds.

The people most directly affected are the roughly 22 million Americans who live in manufactured homes, many of whom are low- and moderate-income households. They currently own their home but not the land underneath it, which makes them vulnerable to rent increases, park closures, and costly forced moves. Park owners considering selling their property are also directly affected, since the tax credit is designed to make selling to residents financially attractive. The bill would take effect for tax years starting after December 31, 2026.
ai_headline: Tax credit would reward mobile home park sales to residents
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4613 offers park owners a 75% profit tax credit for selling to residents or nonprofits, effective after Dec. 31, 2026.

The seller must have owned the property for at least two years, and the buyer must commit to operating it as a manufactured housing community for at least 50 years. The credit would apply to tax years beginning after December 31, 2026.
ai_sections.who: Park owners considering a sale are directly affected, as the credit is designed to make selling to residents financially attractive. The roughly 22 million Americans living in manufactured homes, many of them low- and moderate-income households, are also directly affected as current or potential residents of these communities.
ai_sections.why: In resident-owned communities, lot rent increases average less than 1 percent per year, compared to nearly 6 percent per year in commercially owned parks. Residents who do not own their land remain vulnerable to rent increases, park closures, and costly forced moves if ownership shifts to commercial investors or developers.
ai_sections.cost: Sellers who qualify receive a tax credit equal to 75 percent of their profit on the sale. If a new owner later sells the land for another use before the 50-year period ends, that owner faces a penalty tax of 20 percent of the sale proceeds.

title: Frank Adelmann Manufactured Housing Community Sustainability Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new federal tax credit for owners of mobile home parks (also called manufactured home communities) who sell their property to the residents living there or to a nonprofit organization. Specifically, the seller would get a tax credit equal to 75 percent of the profit they made on the sale. To qualify, the seller must have owned the property for at least two years, and the buyer must agree in a legally binding covenant to keep the land as a manufactured home community for at least 50 years. The buyer also has to be structured as a resident-run cooperative or nonprofit, with democratic voting and a board of directors elected by the residents themselves.

The goal is to give park owners a financial reason to sell to residents rather than to commercial developers who might raise rents sharply or close the community entirely. When residents own the land under their homes through a cooperative, they control their own lot fees and have more housing stability. The bill also includes a penalty: if the new owner later breaks the 50-year covenant and converts or sells the land for another use, they owe a tax equal to 20 percent of what they receive from that future sale.

This bill most directly affects the roughly 22 million Americans who live in manufactured homes, particularly the lower-income households who rent the land beneath their homes in mobile home parks. It also affects current park owners, who could receive a significant tax benefit by choosing to sell to residents. Nonprofit housing organizations that help communities transition to cooperative ownership would also play a central role under this bill.
ai_headline: Tax credit would reward mobile home park sales to residents or nonprofits
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

To qualify, the seller must have owned the property for at least two years, and the buyer must commit by legally binding covenant to keep the land as a manufactured home community for at least 50 years. The buyer must also operate with democratic voting and a resident-elected board of directors.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the roughly 22 million Americans who live in manufactured homes, particularly lower-income households who rent the land beneath their homes. It also affects current park owners and nonprofit housing organizations that help communities transition to cooperative ownership.
ai_sections.why: Park owners who would otherwise sell to commercial developers now have a financial incentive to sell to residents instead, which could reduce instances of sharp rent increases or community closures. Resident-owned cooperatives would control their own lot fees, which may affect housing stability for lower-income manufactured home residents.
ai_sections.cost: Sellers who qualify receive a tax credit equal to 75 percent of their sale profit. If a qualifying buyer later breaks the 50-year covenant by converting or selling the land for another use, that buyer owes a tax penalty equal to 20 percent of the amount received from that future sale.

title: Rental Housing Investment Act
ai_summary:
This bill gives tax breaks to people and companies that build new rental housing. Specifically, it lets them write off a large chunk of the building's cost in the first year instead of spreading that deduction out over many years like current law requires. Under normal tax rules, a residential rental building is written off slowly over 27.5 years. This bill would let landlords and developers immediately deduct up to $150,000 per apartment unit when they put a newly built rental property into service. If a developer builds a 10-unit building, for example, they could deduct up to $1.5 million right away.

There is a bigger bonus available for affordable housing. If the property meets low-income housing requirements under existing federal law, the deduction jumps to $250,000 per unit. To keep the benefit fair, there is a clawback rule: if the property stops being used as a rental within 10 years of opening, the owner has to pay back the tax savings. For affordable housing units, that payback window stretches to 15 years. Only newly built properties qualify, not older buildings being bought or renovated.

This bill mainly affects real estate developers, landlords, and investors who build new residential rental properties. It could also indirectly affect renters if the tax incentive leads to more rental units being built, which could ease housing shortages in some areas. It does not directly give money to renters or change rent prices. The bill was introduced in the Senate in March 2026 and has been sent to the Finance Committee, where it would need approval before moving further.
ai_headline: Senate bill offers landlords up to $250,000 per unit in first-year tax deductions
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4080 lets developers deduct up to $150,000—or $250,000 for affordable units—per new rental unit in the first year instead of over 27.5 years.
ai_sections.what: S 4080 allows owners of newly built residential rental properties to deduct up to $150,000 per unit in the first year the property is placed in service, replacing the standard 27.5-year depreciation schedule. For properties that meet federal low-income housing requirements, the deduction increases to $250,000 per unit. Only newly constructed buildings qualify; purchases or renovations of existing properties are excluded.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects real estate developers, landlords, and investors who build new residential rental properties. Renters are not directly affected but could be indirectly affected if the incentive results in more rental units being built.
ai_sections.why: Owners who convert a property away from rental use within 10 years must repay the tax savings, or within 15 years for affordable housing units. The bill was introduced in the Senate in March 2026 and has been referred to the Finance Committee, where it must receive approval before advancing.

title: Increasing Opportunity For Reindustrialization Act
ai_summary:
This bill expands a program called Opportunity Zones, which currently gives investors a tax break when they put money into certain low-income areas. Right now, states can only designate a limited number of these zones, and the areas must meet income-based requirements. This bill would allow areas containing closed military bases, known as BRAC closures, to also qualify as Opportunity Zones, even if they are not low-income communities.

The bill also makes sure these former military base areas do not count against a state's existing cap on how many Opportunity Zones it can have. In other words, states could add these former base locations on top of their current allowance, not instead of them. This means more investment could potentially flow into communities that lost a military base and the jobs and economic activity that came with it.

The people most directly affected are residents and local governments in communities where a military base was shut down through the official base realignment and closure process. Investors who put money into these newly eligible zones would receive federal tax advantages. It could also affect real estate developers and businesses looking to build or expand in those areas. The bill was introduced in May 2026 and was sent to the Senate Finance Committee for review.
ai_headline: Senate bill opens Opportunity Zone tax breaks to former military bases
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4606 lets closed military base communities qualify for Opportunity Zone tax breaks without counting against state zone caps.
ai_sections.what: S 4606 would expand the Opportunity Zones program to include areas where military bases were closed through the official base realignment and closure process, even if those areas do not meet the program's existing low-income requirements. Former military base sites would also be exempt from the cap that limits how many Opportunity Zones each state can designate, meaning states could add them on top of their current allowance.
ai_sections.who: Residents and local governments in communities that lost a military base through the base realignment and closure process are most directly affected. Investors, real estate developers, and businesses considering activity in those areas would also be affected, as would states administering their Opportunity Zone designations.
ai_sections.why: Communities that lose a military base typically also lose associated jobs and economic activity. Extending Opportunity Zone eligibility to these areas could change where private investment flows and how former base properties are redeveloped.

title: MARCH for Military Servicemembers Act
ai_summary:
This bill would remove a section of federal law that restricts the U.S. Department of Defense from using its money or medical facilities to provide abortions. That restriction currently allows military medical facilities to perform abortions only in cases of rape, incest, or when the pregnancy threatens the mother's life. If this bill became law, those limits would no longer be part of the legal code, which could allow the military's medical system to offer abortion services more broadly.

The people most directly affected would be active-duty military members, their dependents, and others who receive healthcare through the military's medical system, such as at base hospitals or clinics. Military families are often stationed in states where abortion access may be limited, so the location of a base can affect what healthcare options are available locally. This bill is aimed at changing what options the military medical system itself could offer, regardless of the state where a base is located.

The bill was introduced in the Senate in May 2026 and sent to the Armed Services Committee, which would need to approve it before it could move forward to a full Senate vote. It has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Senate bill would lift abortion limits in military medical facilities
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4574 would remove the federal law limiting military medical facilities to abortions only in rape, incest, or life-threat cases.
ai_sections.what: S 4574 would repeal the section of federal law that restricts the Department of Defense from using its funds or medical facilities to provide abortions. Under current law, military medical facilities may only perform abortions in cases of rape, incest, or when the pregnancy threatens the mother's life. Removing this restriction could allow the military medical system to offer abortion services more broadly.
ai_sections.who: Active-duty military members, their dependents, and others who receive healthcare through the military medical system would be most directly affected. Military families stationed in states with limited local abortion access could see a change in what services are available through base hospitals and clinics.
ai_sections.why: Because military families are often stationed in states where abortion access is restricted, the location of a base can determine what healthcare options are available to them locally. This bill targets what the military medical system itself may offer, independent of the state where a base is located.

title: Stars and Stripes Editorial Independence Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill is about protecting the independence of Stars and Stripes, a news organization that has served U.S. military members since the Civil War. Right now, rules protecting its independence exist in military regulations, but those can be changed by the executive branch. This bill would write those protections directly into federal law, making them harder to remove. It requires the Defense Department to ensure Stars and Stripes operates like a professional news outlet, free from censorship, military public affairs influence, or political pressure. It also specifically bans the Defense Department from deliberately hiding unfavorable news about itself. The only allowed exceptions are for information that is classified or that could directly endanger American lives, and even then, both the publisher and a military commander must agree before anything is withheld.

The bill also sets rules about how Stars and Stripes is run day to day. It requires the organization to be led by a civilian with real journalism experience, not a military official. It allows Stars and Stripes reporters to access military bases to cover events, even when commercial reporters cannot. It also says the outlet must be allowed to buy and publish content from outside commercial news sources, and that civilian job candidates cannot be excluded from working there.

The people most directly affected are U.S. military members, Defense Department civilian employees, and their families, especially those stationed overseas or in combat zones who often rely on Stars and Stripes as their main source of independent English-language news. The bill also affects the Defense Department itself, which would face legal restrictions on how much influence it can have over the outlet's reporting.
ai_headline: Bill would write Stars and Stripes editorial independence into federal law
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4614 would move Stars and Stripes protections from military rules into federal law, shielding the outlet from Pentagon influence.
ai_sections.what: S 4614 would write existing editorial protections for Stars and Stripes directly into federal law, making them harder to remove than current military regulations. The bill would ban the Defense Department from censoring coverage, applying political pressure, or deliberately hiding unfavorable news about itself. Information may only be withheld if it is classified or could directly endanger American lives, and only if both the publisher and a military commander agree.
ai_sections.who: U.S. military members, Defense Department civilian employees, and their families are most directly affected, particularly those stationed overseas or in combat zones who rely on Stars and Stripes as a primary independent English-language news source. The Defense Department would face new legal restrictions on its influence over the outlet's reporting.
ai_sections.why: Because current protections exist only in military regulations, they can be changed or removed by the executive branch without an act of Congress. Writing these rules into federal law would create a higher legal bar for any future administration seeking to alter or eliminate them.

title: Servicemember Civilian Transition Support Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the Department of Defense to appoint a senior official whose sole focus is helping military service members successfully move from active duty into civilian life or into the reserve forces. That official must have real experience with veterans services and understand what the transition process involves. The appointment must happen within 90 days of the bill becoming law, and the Pentagon must brief Congress on how it is going shortly after.

The appointed official would act as the top advisor to Defense Department leadership on all transition-related programs. They would be responsible for reviewing existing programs like the Transition Assistance Program and SkillBridge, which currently help service members prepare for jobs and civilian life, and recommending ways to make those programs work better. They would also coordinate with other federal agencies, including the Departments of Veterans Affairs, Labor, and Education, as well as state governments and nonprofit organizations.

This bill directly affects the roughly 200,000 service members who leave active duty each year and their families. It does not create new transition benefits on its own, but it sets up a single point of accountability inside the Pentagon to make sure the programs that already exist are working as intended and that gaps get addressed. Lawmakers, veterans groups, and employers who hire veterans could also be indirectly affected by any program changes the new official recommends.
ai_headline: Pentagon would get a dedicated transition chief for departing troops
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4474 requires the Pentagon to appoint a senior transition official within 90 days to oversee programs serving the ~200,000 troops who leave active duty each year.
ai_sections.what: S 4474 requires the Department of Defense to appoint a senior official whose only job is overseeing the transition of service members from active duty to civilian life or the reserve forces. The official must have real experience with veterans services and must be appointed within 90 days of enactment. The Pentagon would then be required to brief Congress on the appointment shortly after it is made.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 200,000 service members and their families who leave active duty each year are directly affected. Lawmakers, veterans groups, and employers who hire veterans could be indirectly affected by any program changes the official recommends.
ai_sections.why: The bill establishes a single point of accountability inside the Pentagon to ensure existing transition programs are working as intended and that gaps are identified and addressed. It does not create new transition benefits but directs review and coordination of programs already in place, including the Transition Assistance Program and SkillBridge.

title: National Guard Relief Act
ai_summary:
This bill adds the National Guard Relief Foundation to the official list of recognized "military welfare societies" in federal law. Currently, that list includes a handful of other nonprofit organizations that provide financial aid and support to military members and their families. By being added to this list, the National Guard Relief Foundation would gain the same legal standing as those other groups, allowing it to operate in certain ways on military installations and receive cooperation from military departments.

In practical terms, this change could make it easier for the Foundation to reach National Guard members who need help, such as those facing financial hardship during or after deployments. Military welfare societies are often permitted to solicit donations and provide services through official military channels, which being on this recognized list can help enable.

This bill mainly affects National Guard members and their families, particularly those who might need emergency financial assistance or other support services the Foundation provides. It also affects the Foundation itself, which would gain new access and recognition under federal law. The bill does not create new government spending and does not change benefits or pay for service members directly.
ai_headline: HR 8848 adds National Guard Relief Foundation to federal military welfare list
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8848 grants the National Guard Relief Foundation official federal status as a military welfare society, enabling access to military installations.
ai_sections.what: HR 8848 adds the National Guard Relief Foundation to the federal list of recognized military welfare societies. This gives the Foundation the same legal standing as other nonprofits already on that list. The change allows the Foundation to operate on military installations and receive cooperation from military departments.
ai_sections.who: National Guard members and their families, especially those facing financial hardship during or after deployments, are the primary people affected. The National Guard Relief Foundation itself also gains new federal recognition and access.
ai_sections.why: Being on the recognized list can allow the Foundation to solicit donations and provide services through official military channels. This may make it easier for the Foundation to reach National Guard members who need emergency financial assistance or other support services.

title: Taxpayer Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill would stop the President and federal executive branch officials from cutting off or blocking federal funding to states that pay more in federal taxes than they receive back in federal money. These states are called "donor states" in the bill. Right now, there is no specific law preventing the executive branch from withholding grants or contracts from a state for political reasons. This bill would make that practice illegal, with one exception: funding could still be cut if the federal government's top auditor, the Comptroller General, finds actual fraud, waste, or abuse.

The bill also creates a special trust fund, called the Donor State Protection Trust Fund, filled with federal income tax money collected from donor states. If the executive branch illegally cuts funding to a donor state anyway, that state could draw money from this trust fund to replace what it lost. The fund would be capped at $4 trillion, and anything above that would go back into the general federal treasury.

This bill would directly affect states whose residents collectively pay more to the federal government than the state gets back, which tends to include higher-income states. It would also affect public schools, public hospitals, nonprofits, and local governments within those states, since they are covered under the bill's protections. Taxpayers in donor states, and anyone who depends on federally funded services there, would be most impacted if the law were enacted.
ai_headline: Bill bars White House from cutting funds to high-tax donor states
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4208 would ban the executive branch from withholding federal funds from states that pay more in taxes than they receive back.

These states are designated "donor states" under the bill. An exception allows funding to be cut if the Comptroller General finds actual fraud, waste, or abuse.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects donor states, which tend to be higher-income states. Public schools, public hospitals, nonprofits, local governments, and anyone who depends on federally funded services within those states are also covered under the bill's protections.
ai_sections.why: Currently no specific law prevents the executive branch from withholding grants or contracts from a state for political reasons. If enacted, this bill would create a legal remedy and a dedicated funding backstop for states that experience politically motivated funding cuts.

title: Veterans Hearing Aid Improvement Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of Veterans Affairs to run a two-year test program comparing over-the-counter hearing aids to the prescription hearing aids the VA currently provides. The VA would need to start the program within one year of the bill becoming law, run it at least two VA medical facilities in different regions, and split participants roughly evenly between the two types of hearing aids. At the end, the VA must report to Congress on how well each type worked and what it would cost the VA to permanently cover over-the-counter hearing aids going forward.

To qualify for the test program, a veteran must already be enrolled in VA health care, have an audiologist confirm they have mild-to-moderate hearing loss that an over-the-counter device could help, have no medical red flags against using one, and have a smartphone or internet access to use the device's app-based features. The program is specifically for veterans with mild-to-moderate hearing loss, not severe or profound hearing loss.

Separately, the bill directs the Government Accountability Office, which is Congress's independent watchdog agency, to study how well the VA currently serves veterans with mild-to-moderate hearing loss and report back within 18 months. That study could lead to recommendations for new or improved VA hearing benefits. The bill affects VA-enrolled veterans with mild-to-moderate hearing loss, VA audiologists and medical staff, and potentially the VA's budget if Congress later decides to expand coverage based on the results.
ai_headline: VA ordered to test over-the-counter hearing aids against prescription devices for veterans
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

A separate provision directs the Government Accountability Office to study how well the VA currently serves veterans with mild-to-moderate hearing loss and report findings within 18 months.

VA audiologists and medical staff at participating facilities are also directly affected.

The GAO study may separately produce recommendations for new or improved VA hearing coverage.

title: Veterans Hearing Aid Improvement Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of Veterans Affairs to run a two-year test program comparing over-the-counter hearing aids to the prescription hearing aids the VA already covers. The VA would have to start this program within one year of the bill becoming law, run it at two or more VA medical facilities in different parts of the country, and split participants roughly evenly between the two types of hearing aids. At the end, the VA must report to Congress on how well each type worked and how much each would cost if covered permanently.

To take part in the test program, a veteran must already be enrolled in VA health care, have a VA audiologist confirm they have mild-to-moderate hearing loss and no medical reasons to avoid over-the-counter devices, and have access to a smartphone or Wi-Fi because these hearing aids use apps to adjust settings. The over-the-counter devices must be FDA-cleared products. The VA will measure how participants say they feel their hearing improved and will also test their ability to understand speech in noisy settings.

The bill also directs the Government Accountability Office, an independent watchdog agency, to study how well the VA currently serves veterans with mild-to-moderate hearing loss and to send Congress its own recommendations within 18 months. The people most directly affected are VA-enrolled veterans who have mild-to-moderate hearing loss, since the program could eventually make it easier and potentially less expensive for them to get hearing aids through the VA.

ai_sections.who: The program is open to VA-enrolled veterans who have mild-to-moderate hearing loss confirmed by a VA audiologist, no medical reason to avoid over-the-counter devices, and access to a smartphone or Wi-Fi. The Government Accountability Office must also separately study how well the VA currently serves veterans in this hearing-loss category and send Congress its own recommendations within 18 months.
ai_sections.why: The pilot could eventually lead to changes in how the VA covers hearing aids for veterans with mild-to-moderate hearing loss, potentially expanding device options available through the VA. The GAO study and the VA's cost report will give Congress independent data on both the quality of current VA care and the financial implications of any permanent coverage expansion.
ai_sections.cost: The bill directs the VA to report to Congress on what it would cost to permanently cover over-the-counter hearing aids, but the summary does not state a specific funding amount or appropriation for the pilot program itself.
costChips: VA must report permanent coverage costs

title: Preserving Community Food Assistance Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes a rule about how food banks and similar community food programs operate. Right now, federal law says these programs must serve people living in their area, but does not spell out whether the programs can ask people to prove where they live. This bill would allow state agencies to decide whether local food assistance providers can ask people to show proof of residency, such as a photo ID, a piece of mail, or other documents showing where they live.

The key word here is "option." The bill does not require anyone to show ID to get food assistance. Instead, it gives each state the ability to set its own policy on whether local food pantries and food banks under this program may ask for residency documentation. States that do not adopt such a policy would continue operating as they do now.

The people most directly affected are the roughly 40 million Americans who use community food banks and emergency food programs each year, as well as the organizations that run those programs. If a state chooses to allow residency checks, some people who cannot easily produce that kind of documentation might face new barriers to getting food. Program administrators would also need to decide how to apply any new rules fairly and consistently.
ai_headline: States would gain authority to require residency proof at food banks
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8953 lets states decide whether food pantries can require residency documents; no mandate exists either way.
ai_sections.what: HR 8953 would give state agencies the option to allow local food assistance providers to ask people for proof of residency before receiving food. Acceptable documents could include a photo ID, a piece of mail, or similar records showing where someone lives. States that do not adopt such a policy would continue operating under current practice.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 40 million Americans who use community food banks and emergency food programs each year are most directly affected. Food bank and food pantry administrators would also need to develop and apply any new residency-check rules.
ai_sections.why: People who cannot easily produce residency documentation could face new barriers to receiving food assistance in states that adopt a residency-check policy. Program administrators would face the added responsibility of applying any new rules fairly and consistently across their organizations.

title: Protecting Seniors and Stopping Fraudsters Act
ai_summary:
This bill adds new rules to make Medicare's hospice and home health programs harder to cheat. Right now, some providers in these programs have been caught billing Medicare for patients who don't qualify, or simply stealing money from the program. The bill fights this by requiring more frequent inspections of newly opened hospice and home health agencies, especially ones that show warning signs like unusual billing patterns or sudden spikes in the number of providers opening in one area. Agencies that fail to report required quality information to the government would face much steeper payment cuts starting in 2029, jumping from a 2-4 percentage point reduction to 15 percentage points. The bill also requires tougher background checks, including fingerprinting of administrators and medical directors, for providers in areas considered high-risk for fraud.

The bill also adds protections directly for Medicare patients using hospice care. When someone enrolls in hospice, Medicare must send them a written notice within 15 days explaining what rights they are giving up, how to cancel their hospice enrollment, and how to switch providers. This is meant to make sure patients and families fully understand what they signed up for. The government would receive $100 million to carry out the increased inspections, and $6 million per year to cover the cost of sending out those patient notices.

This bill mainly affects Medicare beneficiaries who use hospice or home health services, as well as the companies and organizations that run those services. Taxpayers who fund Medicare are also affected, since the goal is to reduce money lost to fraud. Accreditation organizations that certify these providers would face stricter standards and new oversight as well.
ai_headline: Medicare hospice fraud bill tightens inspections and triples payment penalties
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 27 - 16.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8883 targets Medicare hospice and home health fraud with stricter inspections, 15-point payment cuts, and mandatory patient rights notices within 15 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 8883 adds anti-fraud rules to Medicare's hospice and home health programs, requiring more frequent inspections of new agencies showing warning signs such as unusual billing or sudden provider spikes in one area. Providers that fail to report required quality data would face payment reductions of 15 percentage points starting in 2029, up from the current 2-4 percentage points. Administrators and medical directors in high-risk areas would be subject to tougher background checks, including fingerprinting.
ai_sections.who: Medicare beneficiaries using hospice or home health services are directly affected, as are the agencies and organizations that run those services. Accreditation organizations that certify these providers would face stricter standards and new oversight.
ai_sections.why: Some providers have been caught billing Medicare for patients who do not qualify or stealing money from the program, and the bill is designed to reduce those losses. Taxpayers who fund Medicare are also affected by the outcome, since the goal is to limit money lost to fraud.
ai_sections.cost: The government would receive $100 million to carry out increased inspections and $6 million per year to cover the cost of sending patient rights notices.

title: Tornado Preparedness Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs two federal agencies, NOAA and FEMA, to work together to make tornado warnings faster and more reliable. NOAA would upgrade its detection tools, including radar systems and satellite technology, and use artificial intelligence to improve forecasting. The goal is to give people more accurate, localized warnings with enough time to take shelter. This was partly motivated by a 2025 tornado in the St. Louis area where warning sirens failed and public alerts were delayed.

The bill also creates a grant program run by FEMA that gives money to state and local governments to build or improve community storm shelters, upgrade warning sirens, and add backup power so systems keep working during a storm. Schools and mobile home communities are specifically mentioned as priority locations for new shelters. Communities with lower incomes or limited access to existing shelters would be first in line for funding.

This bill would most directly affect people who live in tornado-prone areas, especially in the Midwest and Southeast. Local emergency management agencies would apply for the federal grants and carry out the improvements. After the programs are up and running, NOAA and FEMA must report back to Congress on what was built, what was spent, and what problems still remain.
ai_headline: Federal bill pushes faster tornado warnings and new shelter grants
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8846 directs NOAA and FEMA to speed up tornado warnings and fund shelters, prioritizing low-income and mobile home communities.
ai_sections.what: HR 8846 directs NOAA to upgrade radar, satellite technology, and artificial intelligence tools to deliver faster and more localized tornado warnings. It also creates a FEMA grant program for state and local governments to build or improve storm shelters, upgrade warning sirens, and add backup power systems. Schools and mobile home communities are specifically named as priority locations for new shelters.
ai_sections.who: People in tornado-prone areas, particularly in the Midwest and Southeast, are the primary population affected. Local emergency management agencies would apply for and carry out the federally funded improvements.
