ai_sections.why: The bill was partly motivated by a 2025 tornado in the St. Louis area where warning sirens failed and public alerts were delayed. Communities with lower incomes or limited access to existing shelters would be first in line for grant funding.
ai_sections.cost: The bill establishes a FEMA grant program that distributes money to state and local governments, with low-income and shelter-limited communities prioritized for funding. No specific dollar amounts are stated in the summary.
costChips: FEMA grants to state and local govts

title: SECURE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a path to permanent legal residency (a green card) for certain immigrants who currently hold or previously held Temporary Protected Status (TPS). TPS is a program that lets people from countries hit by disasters, wars, or other crises stay in the United States temporarily. To qualify under this bill, a person would need to be from a country that was designated for TPS at some point, have lived in the US continuously for at least three years, and pass criminal and national security background checks. Family members, including spouses, children, and domestic partners, could also apply. The application fee would be capped at $1,440, with waivers available for minors, low-income applicants, people in foster care, and those with serious disabilities.

The bill also includes protections for applicants. While someone's application is being reviewed, they could not be deported and would be allowed to work legally in the US. Information provided in applications could not be used by immigration enforcement agencies against the applicant, though it could be shared for national security or serious criminal investigations. People who were previously removed from the country but would have qualified could apply from abroad in some cases.

The bill would also require the government to submit a detailed report to Congress any time a country's TPS designation is ended. That report would need to cover the country's economic stability, political conditions, and ability to absorb returning citizens. This affects TPS holders and former holders, their immediate families, and federal agencies that handle immigration applications and enforcement.
ai_headline: Senate bill offers green cards to longtime TPS holders after three years
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2106 would let TPS holders and former holders apply for a green card after 3 continuous years in the US.
ai_sections.what: S 2106 would create a pathway to permanent legal residency for people who hold or previously held Temporary Protected Status. Applicants must have lived continuously in the US for at least three years and pass criminal and national security background checks. Spouses, children, and domestic partners of qualifying applicants could also apply.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects current and former TPS holders from any country that has received a TPS designation, along with their immediate family members. Federal agencies that process immigration applications and handle enforcement would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Applicants would receive deportation protection and work authorization while their cases are pending. The bill would also require the government to report to Congress whenever a country's TPS designation ends, covering that country's economic, political, and repatriation conditions.
ai_sections.cost: The application fee would be capped at $1,440. Fee waivers would be available for minors, low-income applicants, people in foster care, and those with serious disabilities.

title: Improving Home Dialysis Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes what Medicare covers for people who do dialysis at home. Starting in 2028, Medicare would pay for two new types of support. First, it would cover "respite care," meaning a trained medical professional, like a registered nurse or certified technician, can come to the patient's home and help with dialysis if the patient temporarily cannot do it on their own. New home dialysis patients can use this for up to 20 sessions in a year during their first 30 days, and patients with a physical limitation can use it at any time. Second, Medicare would cover mental health services at home for new home dialysis patients during their first 60 days, up to 4 sessions.

The bill also sets up how Medicare will pay for these new services. Providers in rural areas would receive higher payments than those in non-rural areas. These payments would not be offset by cuts elsewhere in the Medicare budget, meaning the cost would be added on top of existing spending.

The people most directly affected are Medicare patients with kidney failure who dialyze at home, along with the medical professionals who provide dialysis and mental health care. Kidney failure patients are often elderly or disabled, and home dialysis requires significant skill and can be physically and emotionally demanding. This bill is aimed at making it easier and safer for those patients to manage treatment at home rather than traveling to a clinic.
ai_headline: Medicare would add home dialysis respite and mental health visits starting 2028
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 28 - 13.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8875 adds Medicare coverage for home dialysis respite care and mental health visits starting in 2028.
ai_sections.what: HR 8875 adds two new Medicare-covered services for home dialysis patients beginning in 2028. New patients may receive up to 20 respite care sessions in their first 30 days, and patients with a physical limitation may use respite care at any time. New patients may also receive up to 4 mental health sessions at home during their first 60 days.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects Medicare patients with kidney failure who dialyze at home, who are often elderly or disabled. It also affects the registered nurses, certified technicians, and mental health providers who deliver these services.
ai_sections.why: Home dialysis requires significant skill and can be physically and emotionally demanding, creating situations where patients temporarily cannot manage treatment alone. Expanding covered support services may affect how many patients choose or are able to continue dialysis at home rather than traveling to a clinic.
ai_sections.cost: Providers in rural areas would receive higher payments than those in non-rural areas. These payments would not be offset by cuts elsewhere in Medicare, meaning the cost is added on top of existing spending.
costChips: No offsets to other Medicare spending
Cost added on top of existing budget

title: Servicemember Civilian Transition Support Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a single senior official inside the Department of Defense whose only job is to oversee all programs that help active-duty military members move into civilian life or the military reserves. Right now, those programs are spread across different offices with no one person in charge of all of them. The bill requires this official to be appointed within 90 days of the law passing and must be someone with real experience in veterans services and military transition issues.

The official would act as the top advisor to Defense Department leadership on transition matters and would coordinate with other federal agencies like the Departments of Veterans Affairs, Labor, and Education. They would also review existing programs like the Transition Assistance Program and the SkillBridge Program, which help service members find jobs and training before they leave the military, and recommend ways to make those programs work better.

This bill mainly affects active-duty military members who are preparing to leave the service, as well as their families. It could also matter to veterans organizations, employers who hire veterans, and state and local governments that provide support services to veterans. The Defense Department would be required to brief Congress on how the new role is being set up within 90 days of the law taking effect.
ai_headline: Defense Dept. to get single transition czar for troops leaving service
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8751 creates one senior Defense Dept. official to oversee all military-to-civilian transition programs, to be appointed within 90 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 8751 establishes a single senior official inside the Department of Defense whose sole responsibility is overseeing all programs that help active-duty service members transition to civilian life or the military reserves. Currently, those programs are spread across different offices with no single person in charge. The official would also review existing programs such as the Transition Assistance Program and the SkillBridge Program and recommend improvements.
ai_sections.who: The new role primarily affects active-duty military members preparing to leave the service and their families. Veterans organizations, employers who hire veterans, and state and local governments that provide veteran support services may also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Consolidating oversight under one official could change how existing transition programs are coordinated and reformed across the Defense Department and partner agencies including the Departments of Veterans Affairs, Labor, and Education. Congress would receive a briefing on implementation within 90 days of the law taking effect.

title: Freedom From Union Violence Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes a federal law called the Hobbs Act, which makes it a federal crime to use threats or violence to interfere with commerce. Right now, there is a legal exemption that generally keeps federal prosecutors from using the Hobbs Act against unions during labor disputes, based on older court rulings. This bill rewrites the law to make it clearer that violent conduct during a labor dispute can still be prosecuted under federal law, even if it happens on a picket line or during a strike.

The bill does carve out a narrow exception. If violence is minor, isolated, and happens during otherwise peaceful picketing, it would be left to state and local law enforcement rather than federal prosecutors. However, if violence is part of a pattern or is coordinated, federal charges would apply. The maximum penalty under the bill is a $100,000 fine, up to 20 years in prison, or both.

This bill would most directly affect labor unions and their members, particularly those involved in strikes or picketing activity, since it removes the practical legal shield that has made federal Hobbs Act charges rare in labor disputes. It could also affect employers and businesses who might use the law to seek federal prosecution of strike-related incidents. Workers engaged in peaceful picketing would not face new legal risk under this bill, as that conduct remains protected.
ai_headline: Federal prosecutors could charge union violence under Hobbs Act
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5054 removes a longstanding legal shield letting federal prosecutors charge violent conduct during strikes under the Hobbs Act.
ai_sections.what: HR 5054 rewrites the Hobbs Act to allow federal prosecution of violent conduct that occurs during labor disputes, strikes, or picketing. A narrow exception protects minor, isolated violence during otherwise peaceful picketing, leaving those cases to state and local authorities. Coordinated or patterned violence would be subject to federal charges.
ai_sections.who: Labor unions and their members involved in strikes or picketing are most directly affected, as the bill removes the practical legal shield that has made federal Hobbs Act charges rare in labor disputes. Employers and businesses could also use the law to seek federal prosecution of strike-related incidents.
ai_sections.why: Workers engaged in peaceful picketing face no new legal risk under the bill, as that conduct remains protected. The change shifts enforcement authority over patterned or coordinated strike violence from state and local governments to federal prosecutors.
ai_sections.cost: Individuals convicted under the bill face a fine of up to $100,000, up to 20 years in prison, or both.
costChips: Fine and prison may be combined

title: DME Scammer Prevention Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to how Medicare handles claims for durable medical equipment, or DME. That is gear like wheelchairs, walkers, oxygen equipment, and similar supplies. First, starting January 1, 2027, suppliers of these items must submit their claims to Medicare electronically rather than on paper. Second, for most of these items, suppliers would have only 90 days after providing the equipment to submit their bill to Medicare, instead of the current window of up to a full calendar year. Items that already require a doctor visit before delivery, prior authorization, or that are billed on a monthly rental basis are not subject to the shorter 90-day deadline.

The goal is to reduce fraud and billing errors in the DME category, which has historically been a major target of Medicare scams. Faster, electronic claims are easier for Medicare's computer systems to flag for suspicious patterns, double billing, or other red flags before payment goes out the door. The bill also requires the Government Accountability Office to report to Congress by 2030 on how well Medicare's screening technology is catching bad claims under these new rules.

The people most directly affected are companies and individuals who sell or rent medical equipment to Medicare patients. Medicare beneficiaries who rely on that equipment could also be indirectly affected if suppliers struggle to meet the tighter deadlines. Taxpayers and the Medicare program as a whole stand to benefit if the changes succeed in cutting down fraudulent or wasteful payments.
ai_headline: Medicare DME suppliers face 90-day billing deadline and e-filing mandate starting 2027
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 25 - 19.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8871 cuts the Medicare DME billing window from one year to 90 days and requires electronic claims starting January 1, 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 8871 requires suppliers of durable medical equipment to submit Medicare claims electronically beginning January 1, 2027, replacing paper filing. For most DME items, suppliers will have only 90 days after delivering equipment to submit a bill, down from up to a full calendar year. Items requiring a prior doctor visit, prior authorization, or billed on a monthly rental basis are exempt from the shorter deadline.
ai_sections.who: Companies and individuals who sell or rent durable medical equipment to Medicare patients are directly affected by both the electronic filing and shortened billing requirements. Medicare beneficiaries who depend on that equipment could be indirectly affected if suppliers have difficulty meeting the tighter deadlines.
ai_sections.why: The DME category has historically been a major target of Medicare fraud, and electronic claims are easier for Medicare systems to flag for suspicious patterns or double billing before payment is issued. The Government Accountability Office is required to report to Congress by 2030 on how effectively Medicare's screening technology is catching problematic claims under the new rules.

title: Protecting and Preserving Social Security Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to Social Security. First, it would switch the formula used to calculate annual cost-of-living adjustments for Social Security benefits. Right now, those adjustments are based on a general inflation measure. This bill would create a new index specifically tracking what people 62 and older actually spend money on, like healthcare and housing costs that tend to hit older people harder. The idea is that benefit increases would better match the real expenses of the people receiving them.

Second, the bill changes how Social Security payroll taxes work for higher earners. Currently, workers only pay Social Security taxes on the first roughly $170,000 of their wages. Earnings above that amount are not taxed for Social Security. This bill would phase in Social Security taxes on those higher earnings between 2026 and 2032, until all wages above the current cap are fully subject to the tax by 2032. It also adjusts the benefit formula so that higher earners receive a small additional benefit based on those extra taxed earnings, though at a much lower rate than regular earnings.

The people most affected are current and future Social Security recipients who could see slightly higher annual benefit increases, workers who earn above the current payroll tax cap who would pay more in Social Security taxes, and self-employed individuals in the same higher income range. The bill also specifically protects recipients from losing Medicaid or Supplemental Security Income eligibility just because their Social Security check goes up under the new rules.
ai_headline: Senate bill would tie Social Security raises to what seniors actually spend
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2614 updates Social Security COLAs to track senior spending and phases in payroll taxes on wages above ~$170,000 by 2032.
ai_sections.what: S 2614 would replace the current Social Security cost-of-living adjustment formula with a new index that tracks spending patterns of people 62 and older, with a focus on costs like healthcare and housing. It would also phase in Social Security payroll taxes on wages above the current cap of roughly $170,000 between 2026 and 2032, so that all wages above that cap are fully taxed by 2032. Higher earners subject to the new taxes would receive a small additional benefit based on those extra taxed earnings, though calculated at a lower rate than regular earnings.
ai_sections.who: Current and future Social Security recipients, workers earning above roughly $170,000, and self-employed individuals in that same income range are the primary people affected. Recipients who see higher Social Security payments under this bill would be protected from losing Medicaid or Supplemental Security Income eligibility as a result.
ai_sections.why: Benefit increases tied to a senior-specific spending index could diverge from increases under the current general inflation measure, changing the dollar amounts recipients receive each year. Workers and self-employed individuals earning above the current payroll tax cap would pay Social Security taxes on a larger share of their income during the phase-in period and fully after 2032.
ai_sections.cost: Workers earning above roughly $170,000 would pay Social Security payroll taxes on wages above that threshold, phased in from 2026 through 2032. Self-employed individuals in the same income range would face the same new tax obligation.

title: Over-the-Counter Monograph Drug User Fee Amendments
ai_summary:
This bill renews and updates a program that requires companies making over-the-counter (OTC) drugs to pay fees to the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). OTC drugs are medicines you can buy without a prescription, like pain relievers, antacids, and cold medicines. These fees help fund the FDA's work reviewing and updating the safety and quality rules for those products, called "monographs." The program was set to expire and this bill would extend it through September 30, 2030.

The bill makes several technical changes to how the fees are calculated and collected. It adjusts the schedule for when payments are due, sets specific fee amounts for fiscal years 2026 through 2030, and allows for increases based on inflation or if the number of manufacturing facilities paying into the program grows significantly. It also expands the definition of what counts as an update to an OTC drug product to include changes to testing procedures that follow recognized international quality standards.

The companies most directly affected are the manufacturers and facilities that produce OTC drug products sold in the United States, since they are the ones required to pay the fees. Indirectly, this bill affects everyday consumers because the fees support FDA oversight of the OTC medicines people buy at pharmacies and stores. The bill does not change the drugs themselves or their prices directly, but it keeps the regulatory review process funded and running.
ai_headline: Senate bill extends FDA over-the-counter drug fee program to 2030
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 152.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2292 renews the FDA's OTC drug fee program through Sept. 30, 2030, keeping safety reviews of non-prescription medicines funded.
ai_sections.what: S 2292 renews a program requiring manufacturers of over-the-counter drugs to pay fees to the FDA through September 30, 2030. The bill sets specific fee amounts for fiscal years 2026 through 2030 and adjusts payment schedules and collection procedures. It also expands what qualifies as an OTC drug product update to include changes to testing procedures that meet recognized international quality standards.
ai_sections.who: Manufacturers and facilities that produce OTC drug products sold in the United States are directly required to pay the fees. Consumers who purchase non-prescription medicines at pharmacies and stores are indirectly affected because the fees fund FDA oversight of those products.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, the fee program would expire and the FDA's funding for reviewing and updating OTC drug safety and quality rules would not be sustained. Fee levels can increase based on inflation or significant growth in the number of manufacturing facilities paying into the program.
ai_sections.cost: OTC drug manufacturers pay fees to the FDA, with specific amounts set for fiscal years 2026 through 2030. Fee levels may rise due to inflation or growth in the number of participating manufacturing facilities.
costChips: More facilities can raise fee totals

title: ENFORCE Act
ai_summary:
The ENFORCE Act tightens federal laws around child sexual abuse material, including both real images and drawn or computer-generated obscene depictions. It clarifies that producing this material is a federal crime even if it was made locally, as long as the materials used or the distribution involves interstate commerce. It also adds a provision that during criminal court cases, this material must stay under control of the government or court and cannot be freely copied or shared, similar to protections already in place for real child pornography.

The bill removes the statute of limitations for crimes involving obscene visual depictions of child sexual abuse, meaning prosecutors can bring charges no matter how much time has passed. It also requires people convicted of these offenses to register as sex offenders, which was not previously required for this specific category of material. Additionally, people charged with these offenses can be held in jail before trial under a legal presumption that they should be detained, and after serving prison time they face a mandatory period of supervised release.

This bill affects federal prosecutors, law enforcement, and judges handling these types of cases. It also directly affects anyone who creates, distributes, or possesses obscene depictions of child sexual abuse, including animated or digitally generated material, by subjecting them to stricter penalties, registration requirements, and pretrial detention. Child victims depicted in the material are also affected, as they gain rights to access evidence in court proceedings under the same rules that apply to victims in real child pornography cases.
ai_headline: Federal bill strips time limits and adds registry rules for obscene child abuse imagery
last_action_text: Held at the desk.

ai_sections.tldr: The ENFORCE Act removes the statute of limitations and adds sex offender registration for obscene depictions of child sexual abuse, including drawn and digital material.
ai_sections.what: The ENFORCE Act expands federal criminal law to cover obscene visual depictions of child sexual abuse, including animated and computer-generated images, not only real photographs. It removes the statute of limitations for these offenses, requires sex offender registration upon conviction, and mandates pretrial detention and supervised release after prison. Evidence rules are tightened so this material must remain under government or court control during criminal proceedings.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects anyone who creates, distributes, or possesses obscene depictions of child sexual abuse, including digital or drawn material, subjecting them to stricter penalties, registration, and pretrial detention. Federal prosecutors, law enforcement, and judges handling these cases are also affected, as are child victims depicted in the material, who gain the same evidence-access rights as victims in real child pornography cases.
ai_sections.why: Removing the statute of limitations means charges can be brought no matter how much time has passed since an offense. Adding a pretrial detention presumption and mandatory supervised release expands the legal consequences beyond what previously applied to this category of material.

title: James T. Woods Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes it a federal crime to threaten a minor with sharing sexual images of them. Specifically, it targets situations where someone tells a child or teenager that they will release explicit images of them unless the child creates or sends more explicit images. This type of threat is sometimes called "sextortion." The bill adds this behavior to existing federal laws that already ban child sexual exploitation material.

The people most directly affected are children and teenagers who could be targeted by online predators using this kind of blackmail tactic. Under current law, actually producing or sharing such images is already illegal, but the act of threatening to do so as a way to coerce a child was not clearly covered as its own separate crime. This bill closes that gap.

Anyone convicted under this new provision would face the same federal penalties that apply to other child sexual exploitation crimes under the existing laws being updated. Law enforcement agencies that investigate crimes against children would also be affected, as this gives them a clearer legal tool to use when prosecuting this specific type of online threat.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets sextortion threats against minors as a standalone crime
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 346.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6719 makes it a federal crime to threaten a minor with releasing explicit images to coerce them into creating more.
ai_sections.what: HR 6719 creates a new federal crime targeting sextortion: threatening a minor with the release of explicit images unless the child produces or sends more explicit images. Current federal law already bans producing or sharing child sexual exploitation material, but did not clearly cover this specific type of coercive threat as its own offense. This bill closes that legal gap.
ai_sections.who: Children and teenagers targeted by online predators using blackmail tactics are the people most directly affected. Law enforcement agencies that investigate crimes against children gain a more specific legal tool for prosecuting this type of threat.
ai_sections.why: Because threatening to release explicit images of a minor was not previously defined as its own separate federal crime, prosecutors faced a gap in the law when pursuing this specific conduct. Convictions under the new provision carry the same federal penalties that apply to existing child sexual exploitation crimes.

title: A joint resolution disapproving the action of the District of Columbia Council in approving the Body-Worn Camera Transparency for Use of Force Temporary Amendment Act of 2026.
ai_summary:
This bill is Congress's way of rejecting a law recently passed by Washington D.C.'s local government. The D.C. Council passed a temporary law in April 2026 called the Body-Worn Camera Transparency for Use of Force Act, which dealt with rules around police body camera footage when officers use force. Because D.C. is not a state, Congress has the power to review and block D.C. laws before they take effect. This joint resolution, if passed by both the Senate and House and signed by the President, would cancel that D.C. law entirely.

The D.C. law being blocked appears to have set new rules about how body camera video is handled or released in situations where police use force. The exact details of those rules are not included in this resolution, but the title suggests it was aimed at making that footage more transparent or accessible to the public. By disapproving it, Congress would stop those transparency rules from going into place.

This bill directly affects residents of Washington D.C., since it overrules a decision made by their locally elected city council. It would also affect D.C. police officers and anyone involved in use-of-force incidents where body camera footage might be relevant. More broadly, it is an example of the unusual relationship between Congress and D.C., where the federal government holds authority over local laws in a way it does not with any of the 50 states.
ai_headline: Senate resolution moves to kill D.C. police body camera transparency law
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: SJRES 194 would cancel a D.C. law setting body camera rules for police use-of-force incidents if passed by both chambers and signed.
ai_sections.what: SJRES 194 is a joint resolution that would cancel a temporary Washington D.C. law called the Body-Worn Camera Transparency for Use of Force Act, passed by the D.C. Council in April 2026. That local law established rules around how police body camera footage is handled or released when officers use force. If this resolution passes both the Senate and House and is signed by the President, the D.C. law would be eliminated entirely.
ai_sections.who: Washington D.C. residents are directly affected, as this resolution overrules a decision made by their locally elected city council. D.C. police officers and anyone involved in use-of-force incidents where body camera footage is relevant would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: The D.C. transparency rules on body camera footage would not go into effect, removing whatever access or handling requirements the local law had established. This action reflects the unusual authority Congress holds over D.C. law, which does not apply to any of the 50 states.

title: A joint resolution disapproving the action of the District of Columbia Council in approving the Full Accountability in Arrest Reporting Temporary Amendment Act of 2026.
ai_summary:
This resolution is Congress's way of rejecting a law recently passed by the Washington, D.C. city council. The D.C. law in question, called the Full Accountability in Arrest Reporting Temporary Amendment Act of 2026, was passed by D.C.'s local government in April 2026. Because D.C. is not a state, Congress has the power to review and block D.C. laws before they take effect. This resolution, if passed by both the Senate and House and signed by the President, would cancel that D.C. law.

The D.C. law being blocked deals with how arrests are reported. Based on its title, it likely changed rules around how the D.C. government tracks and shares information about arrests made by local police. The word "temporary" in the title means the D.C. council passed it as a short-term measure, not a permanent change to local law.

This resolution directly affects residents of Washington, D.C., since it overrules a decision made by their locally elected city council. It also matters to anyone interested in how Congress oversees D.C.'s local government. The resolution was introduced by six Republican senators and has been sent to the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee, where it will be reviewed before any further votes take place.
ai_headline: Senate resolution seeks to cancel D.C. arrest reporting law
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: Six Republican senators introduced SJRES 195 to block a D.C. law changing how local arrest data is tracked and shared.
ai_sections.what: SJRES 195 is a congressional resolution that would cancel a Washington, D.C. law called the Full Accountability in Arrest Reporting Temporary Amendment Act of 2026, passed by the D.C. city council in April 2026. That local law changed rules around how the D.C. government tracks and shares arrest information. Because D.C. is not a state, Congress holds the power to review and block D.C. laws before they take effect.
ai_sections.who: The resolution directly affects Washington, D.C. residents, whose locally elected city council passed the law being overruled. Six Republican senators introduced the resolution, which has been referred to the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee.
ai_sections.why: If passed by both chambers and signed by the President, the resolution would void the D.C. council's decision on arrest reporting rules. It also raises broader questions about congressional oversight of D.C.'s local government.

title: Veterans 2nd Amendment Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change the rules about when the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) can share certain information about veterans with the FBI's background check system, known as NICS. Right now, if the VA decides a veteran needs someone else to manage their benefits — called a fiduciary — that determination alone can be reported to the background check system, which can prevent the veteran from legally buying a gun. This bill would stop that from happening automatically.

Under this bill, the VA could only send that information to the background check system if a judge or other court authority specifically rules that the veteran is a danger to themselves or others. In other words, a medical or financial decision by the VA would no longer be enough on its own to affect a veteran's gun rights. A court would have to be involved first.

This bill directly affects veterans who receive VA benefits through a fiduciary — typically veterans with mental health conditions, brain injuries, or other difficulties that make managing money hard. There are currently hundreds of thousands of veterans in this category. It also affects gun retailers who run background checks, and federal agencies involved in maintaining the NICS system. People on different sides of this issue have strong feelings about both veterans' rights and public safety, but the bill itself simply changes the legal threshold required before the VA shares this specific type of information.
ai_headline: Bill would require court order before VA shares veterans' gun-check data
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1041 would bar the VA from reporting fiduciary-assigned veterans to the federal gun background check system without a court order.
ai_sections.what: HR 1041 would change when the VA can report veterans to the FBI's NICS background check system. Under current law, a VA determination that a veteran needs a fiduciary to manage their benefits can alone trigger a NICS report, which may block that veteran from legally purchasing a gun. This bill would require a judge or court authority to first rule that the veteran is a danger to themselves or others before any such report is made.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects veterans who receive VA benefits through a fiduciary, a group that currently numbers in the hundreds of thousands and typically includes veterans with mental health conditions, brain injuries, or other difficulties managing finances. It also affects gun retailers who conduct background checks and federal agencies that maintain the NICS system.
ai_sections.why: Under the current system, a medical or financial decision by the VA can affect a veteran's gun rights without any court involvement. If enacted, this bill would raise the legal threshold required before that information is shared, shifting the triggering event from an administrative determination to a judicial one.

title: Lainie Jones Comprehensive Cancer Survivorship Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a broad set of programs and rules to improve care for people who have been diagnosed with cancer, from the moment of diagnosis through the rest of their lives. One of the biggest changes is that Medicare would be required to cover something called a "cancer care planning" visit, where a doctor or nurse practitioner creates a written plan for the patient covering their treatment, follow-up care, and emotional and social needs. The bill also directs the government to create a new way of paying doctors and hospitals for survivorship care, study how well that care has worked over the past 50 years, and fund community programs that help survivors stay active, manage anxiety and depression, eat well, and keep up with follow-up screenings.

The bill also expands Medicaid, which is the government health insurance program for lower-income people. States would be required to cover fertility preservation services for cancer patients whose treatment might affect their ability to have children in the future, with no out-of-pocket costs for patients. Medicaid would also have to cover transition care visits for children and teenagers who finish cancer treatment and move from specialist care to regular primary care. The Children's Health Insurance Program, known as CHIP, would have similar fertility coverage requirements added to it as well.

The bill affects a wide range of people. Cancer survivors of all ages, including children, teenagers, and adults, would gain new coverage rights and access to services. Lower-income survivors on Medicaid or CHIP would see the most direct new benefits. The bill also creates a grant program through the Department of Labor to help cancer survivors who struggle to keep or return to a job, offering help with transportation, childcare, job training, and applying for disability benefits. A formal Office of Cancer Survivorship would be set up inside the National Cancer Institute to lead research and education on these issues going forward.
ai_headline: HR 8839 would mandate Medicare and Medicaid cancer survivorship coverage
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8839 requires Medicare to cover cancer care planning visits and Medicaid to cover fertility preservation for cancer patients at no cost.
ai_sections.what: HR 8839 establishes new coverage requirements across Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP for cancer survivors, including written care planning visits, fertility preservation services, and transition care for children finishing treatment. The bill also creates a grant program through the Department of Labor to help survivors with employment challenges and establishes a formal Office of Cancer Survivorship within the National Cancer Institute. The government would also be directed to develop a new payment model for survivorship care and fund community programs supporting survivors' physical and mental health.
ai_sections.who: Cancer survivors of all ages are affected, with lower-income survivors on Medicaid or CHIP receiving the most direct new coverage benefits. Children and teenagers transitioning out of cancer treatment, as well as patients whose fertility may be affected by treatment, are specifically addressed.
ai_sections.why: Requiring Medicare to cover care planning visits and Medicaid to cover fertility preservation creates new mandatory spending obligations for federal and state programs. Establishing the Office of Cancer Survivorship and funding community and employment programs represents an expansion of federal agency responsibilities and grant activity.
ai_sections.cost: The bill directs funding for community survivorship programs, a Department of Labor grant program for employment assistance, and a new federal office within the National Cancer Institute, though the summary does not specify dollar amounts for any of these.
costChips: Dept. of Labor grants for survivor employment

title: Renter Resource Center Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) to create a Renter Resource Center within 180 days of becoming law. The center would include a toll-free phone number and a public website where renters can report disputes with their landlord, such as potential violations of federal or state law or other rental problems. HUD would be required to respond to renters who use the resource, share dispute information with other relevant federal agencies, and point renters toward the right state agency when their complaint involves state law. Large corporate landlords covered by the bill would also be required to notify their tenants about this resource at move-in and once a year after that.

The bill only applies to a specific type of landlord called a "covered large institutional investor." This means a for-profit company, fund, or investment group that controls at least 350 single-family rental homes across the country. Government agencies, nonprofits, and community land trusts are excluded. These large investors would also be required to report to HUD each year, listing how many single-family homes they control and where those homes are located.

The people most directly affected are renters living in single-family homes owned by large corporate landlords, as well as the investment companies that own those properties. HUD would take on new responsibilities to track, investigate, and report on rental disputes. Congress would receive an annual public report summarizing the disputes filed, though personal information about individual renters would be kept private.
ai_headline: New renter hotline and website required under House bill targeting corporate landlords
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8903 would create a HUD renter dispute center and require corporate landlords owning 350+ rentals to notify tenants and report annually.
ai_sections.what: HR 8903 would require HUD to establish a Renter Resource Center within 180 days of enactment, including a toll-free phone number and public website where renters can report disputes with landlords. HUD would be required to respond to complaints, share dispute information with relevant federal agencies, and direct renters to the appropriate state agency when state law is involved. Large corporate landlords covered by the bill would be required to notify tenants about the center at move-in and once per year thereafter.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects renters living in single-family homes owned by large corporate landlords, as well as for-profit companies, funds, or investment groups that control at least 350 single-family rental homes nationwide. Government agencies, nonprofits, and community land trusts are excluded from the bill's requirements.
ai_sections.why: HUD would take on new responsibilities to track, investigate, and report on rental disputes, and covered landlords would face new annual reporting and tenant notification obligations. Congress would receive an annual public report summarizing disputes filed, with personal information about individual renters kept private.

title: Habitable Housing Conversion Pilot Program Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a five-year test program run by the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD). The program would allow homeowners to take out government-backed loans to convert their basements into separate rental apartments. The government backing works like insurance for the lender, making it easier for homeowners to qualify for the loan. The converted basement unit would have to meet all local building and safety codes, and HUD would set additional safety rules. The program would cover up to 150,000 loans total, and after five years HUD would report to Congress on how well it worked.

The bill directs HUD to work with FEMA and the weather and oceans agency NOAA to find areas at higher risk from certain hazards. Properties in those higher-risk areas could be given priority for loans under the program. This suggests lawmakers are thinking about places where housing is in short supply partly due to weather or disaster-related damage.

The people most directly affected are homeowners who have an unfinished or unused basement and want to create a rentable apartment but may struggle to get a regular loan to pay for the work. Renters in areas with tight housing markets could also be affected, since more finished basement units would add new rental options. Local governments and contractors who handle renovation work would also feel the effects if the program moves forward.
ai_headline: HUD pilot would back loans for basement apartment conversions in up to 150,000 homes
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8854 launches a 5-year HUD pilot backing loans for basement-to-apartment conversions, capped at 150,000 loans.
ai_sections.what: HR 8854 would create a five-year pilot program under HUD allowing homeowners to take out government-backed loans to convert basements into separate rental apartments. The program would be capped at 150,000 loans total. After five years, HUD would be required to report to Congress on the program's results.
ai_sections.who: Homeowners with unfinished or unused basements who cannot easily qualify for conventional renovation loans are the primary targets of the program. Renters in tight housing markets, local governments, and renovation contractors would also be affected if the program is enacted.
ai_sections.why: Adding government-backed financing for basement conversions could increase the number of rental units available in areas with housing shortages. The bill also directs HUD to work with FEMA and NOAA to prioritize properties in areas at higher risk from weather or disaster-related hazards, indicating concern about housing supply in disaster-affected communities.

title: Amtrak Grant Flexibility Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for how Amtrak can use federal grant money it already receives. Right now, when Amtrak applies for certain federal infrastructure grants, it has to come up with a share of the project cost using non-federal money, meaning its own funds or state contributions. This bill would let Amtrak count other federal grant money it has already been given toward that required share, as long as that money comes from a different grant program than the one being applied for.

The bill applies to three specific federal grant programs: the National Infrastructure Project Assistance program (sometimes called MEGA grants), the Consolidated Rail Infrastructure and Safety Improvements program (CRISI grants), and the Federal-State Partnership for Intercity Passenger Rail program. For each of these, the bill adds a new rule allowing Amtrak to use its existing federal grant funds to meet the matching requirement, something it currently cannot do.

The people most directly affected are Amtrak as an organization and, indirectly, Amtrak passengers and communities that rely on passenger rail service. If Amtrak can more easily meet grant matching requirements, it may be able to apply for and receive more infrastructure funding, potentially leading to improvements on rail lines across the country, including both the Northeast Corridor and the broader national rail network. State and local governments that partner with Amtrak on rail projects could also be affected, since the matching burden might shift.
ai_headline: Amtrak bill would let federal grants count toward other grant matches
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8853 lets Amtrak use federal grant funds from one program to meet the matching requirement of a different federal grant program.
ai_sections.what: HR 8853 changes the matching rules for three federal grant programs: MEGA grants, CRISI grants, and the Federal-State Partnership for Intercity Passenger Rail program. Currently, Amtrak must use non-federal money to meet the cost-share requirement when applying for these grants. This bill would allow Amtrak to use federal grant funds it has already received from a different program to satisfy that requirement.
ai_sections.who: Amtrak is the most directly affected organization. Amtrak passengers, communities served by passenger rail, and state and local governments that partner with Amtrak on rail projects could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: If Amtrak can more easily meet matching requirements, it may be able to apply for and receive more infrastructure funding across both the Northeast Corridor and the broader national rail network. The matching burden on state and local government partners could also shift as a result.
ai_sections.cost: The bill addresses how existing federal grant funds already allocated to Amtrak can be applied toward matching requirements, but the summary does not specify any new spending amounts or cost figures.

title: Department of Justice Integrity Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill adds a new "cooling off" rule for lawyers who work for the federal government. Specifically, it targets attorneys who worked at the Department of Justice and personally played a significant role in prosecuting a business or negotiating a special legal agreement with a business. Under this rule, after leaving their government job, those attorneys would not be allowed to turn around and work for that same business in the same legal matter for at least one year. If they break this rule, they could face criminal penalties.

The bill covers a wide range of businesses, including corporations, partnerships, and LLCs. It applies specifically to situations where the former government attorney was directly and substantially involved in the case or agreement, not just loosely connected to it. The one-year waiting period only applies to that specific business and that specific legal matter they worked on while in government, not to their entire legal career.

This bill would most directly affect former federal prosecutors and DOJ attorneys who move into private legal practice. It is also relevant to large companies that face federal prosecution, since it would limit which attorneys they could hire for their defense. Supporters of such rules generally argue they prevent conflicts of interest, while critics sometimes argue they can restrict career opportunities for government lawyers. The bill was introduced in the House and referred to the Judiciary Committee.
ai_headline: New one-year ban proposed for DOJ attorneys who join former clients
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8861 would bar DOJ attorneys from working for a business they prosecuted or made a deal with for one year after leaving government.
ai_sections.what: HR 8861 adds a one-year waiting period preventing former Department of Justice attorneys from working for a business in the same legal matter they handled while in government. The restriction applies only when the attorney was directly and substantially involved in prosecuting that business or negotiating a special legal agreement with it. Violating this rule could result in criminal penalties.
ai_sections.who: The rule most directly affects former federal prosecutors and DOJ attorneys who move into private legal practice. Large companies facing federal prosecution are also affected, as the bill limits which attorneys they can hire for their defense.
ai_sections.why: Former government attorneys moving quickly to represent the same businesses they once prosecuted raises concern about conflicts of interest, which supporters of such rules cite as a reason for the restriction. Critics argue these rules can limit career opportunities for government lawyers.

title: Securing Innovation and Research from Adversaries Act
ai_summary:
This bill would ban anyone who receives federal research money from working with foreign organizations or individuals that appear on certain U.S. government watchlists. Those lists include entities flagged for national security risks, ties to China's military, forced labor connections, or other concerns. The ban covers a wide range of activities, including joint research projects, co-writing papers, sharing data, running joint labs, and hosting visiting scholars. A government office would write rules explaining exactly how the ban works and how it would be enforced.

The people and organizations most directly affected are universities, research institutions, companies, and individual scientists who receive federal grants or contracts. If any of them currently work with a foreign partner that appears on one of those watchlists, they would have to stop using federal funds for that relationship. Agency heads can grant exceptions if the collaboration is genuinely necessary for national security, public health, or science goals that cannot be met any other way, but they must notify Congress within 30 days of doing so.

Indirectly, this bill could affect foreign researchers, students, and scholars who work at or visit U.S. institutions funded by the government, as well as the pace and scope of international scientific work in fields like technology, medicine, and engineering. The bill does not target all foreign partnerships, only those involving entities already flagged by existing U.S. government lists.
ai_headline: Federal grant recipients face ban on work with watchlisted foreign entities
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8901 bars federally funded researchers from joint projects, data sharing, or hosting scholars tied to watchlisted foreign entities.
ai_sections.what: HR 8901 would prohibit anyone receiving federal research money from working with foreign organizations or individuals on certain U.S. government watchlists. The ban covers joint research, co-authored papers, data sharing, joint labs, and hosting visiting scholars. A government office would write rules explaining how the ban works and how it would be enforced.
ai_sections.who: Universities, research institutions, companies, and individual scientists who receive federal grants or contracts are most directly affected. Foreign researchers, students, and scholars working at or visiting U.S. federally funded institutions could also be affected indirectly.
ai_sections.why: Those currently working with a watchlisted foreign partner would have to stop using federal funds for that relationship. The bill could affect the pace and scope of international scientific work in fields like technology, medicine, and engineering.

title: Maximizing Transportation Efficiency Act
ai_summary:
This bill focuses on "transportation demand management," or TDM — a set of strategies designed to help people travel more efficiently by spreading out when, how, and how much people use roads. TDM includes things like carpooling, vanpooling, employer transit benefits, flexible work schedules, better parking pricing, trip-planning apps, and bike or pedestrian infrastructure. The bill adds an official federal definition of TDM to existing law and makes TDM projects eligible for several existing federal funding programs, including infrastructure grants that previously did not cover these kinds of activities.

A key part of the bill sets aside $20 million per year specifically for rural communities to develop and use TDM strategies. Rural areas often have fewer transportation options, higher rates of elderly and disabled residents, and households that spend more on getting around than their urban counterparts. The money could go to state transportation departments, local governments, tribal governments, transit agencies, nonprofits, and colleges working on rural transportation. They could use the funds for vanpool and carpool programs, commuter incentives, real-time travel information systems, and trip-planning technology. The bill also sets aside $20 million per year for smaller congestion relief projects costing between $500,000 and $10 million, removing a previous rule that limited that program to cities over one million people.

Overall, this bill primarily affects commuters, rural residents, employers, and state and local transportation agencies. It does not create a brand-new program but instead expands who can access existing federal transportation funding and what kinds of projects qualify.
ai_headline: Federal bill expands transportation funding to cover carpools, rural commutes
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7301 adds carpooling and commute programs to federal funding eligibility and sets aside $20M/year each for rural TDM and small congestion projects.
ai_sections.what: HR 7301 adds an official federal definition of transportation demand management (TDM) to existing law and makes TDM projects eligible for several federal funding programs that previously excluded them. TDM strategies include carpooling, vanpooling, employer transit benefits, flexible work schedules, parking pricing, trip-planning apps, and bike or pedestrian infrastructure. The bill does not create a new program but expands which projects and communities can access existing federal transportation funding.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects commuters, rural residents, employers, and state and local transportation agencies. State transportation departments, local governments, tribal governments, transit agencies, nonprofits, and colleges working on rural transportation are all eligible to receive funds.
ai_sections.why: Rural areas often have fewer transportation options, higher rates of elderly and disabled residents, and households that spend more on transportation than urban counterparts. Expanding eligibility for existing federal programs would change what kinds of projects qualify for funding and which communities can apply.
ai_sections.cost: The bill sets aside $20 million per year for rural communities to develop and use TDM strategies. It also sets aside $20 million per year for smaller congestion relief projects costing between $500,000 and $10 million, while removing a previous rule that limited that program to cities over one million people.

title: Agriculture Infrastructure Stability Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill directs the Federal Crop Insurance Corporation, which is the government agency that manages crop insurance programs, to research and develop a new type of insurance policy for farmers. The new policy would include a "harvest incentive," meaning it would cover farmers when they lose revenue, and it would reward or encourage them to actually bring in their crops. The agency must make this new policy available to farmers within two years of the bill becoming law, and it must report back to Congress within one year on how the research is going.

The bill affects American farmers who use federal crop insurance, which is a large portion of the country's agricultural producers. Currently, crop insurance can pay out when crops are damaged or destroyed, but this bill pushes toward a design that also gives farmers extra motivation to harvest even when conditions are tough. The idea is to keep more crops moving through the food supply chain rather than being left in the field.

Regular consumers and the broader food system could also be indirectly affected, since keeping more crops harvested can help stabilize food supplies. The bill itself does not set a price for the new insurance product or guarantee it will be created, since the agency still has to meet existing legal requirements before the policy goes live. It was introduced in the House and referred to the Agriculture Committee, so it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Federal crop insurance bill would add harvest incentive for farmers
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, Risk Management, and Credit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5633 directs the Federal Crop Insurance Corporation to develop a harvest-incentive policy and make it available to farmers within two years.
ai_sections.what: HR 5633 directs the Federal Crop Insurance Corporation to research and develop a new crop insurance policy that covers revenue losses and includes a harvest incentive to encourage farmers to bring in their crops. The agency must make the policy available to farmers within two years of enactment and report to Congress on its progress within one year. The policy cannot go live until the agency meets existing legal requirements, and the bill does not guarantee the product will be created.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects American farmers who participate in federal crop insurance programs, which includes a large portion of the country's agricultural producers. Consumers and the broader food system may be indirectly affected if more crops reach harvest rather than being left in the field.
ai_sections.why: Current federal crop insurance pays out when crops are damaged or destroyed but does not specifically incentivize farmers to harvest under difficult conditions. Adding a harvest incentive could affect how much of the food supply actually moves through the supply chain, with downstream effects on food availability.

title: RESTART Communities Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells two federal agencies, the Economic Development Administration (part of the Commerce Department) and the Environmental Protection Agency, to work together more closely on cleaning up and rebuilding polluted sites. Right now these agencies often work separately, one focused on the environment and one on the economy. This bill would require them to coordinate, share resources, and help communities access federal grants, loans, and expert advice both during and after a cleanup. Within three years, they would have to report to Congress on what they accomplished.

The bill focuses on sites that have been contaminated by hazardous materials, such as old factories or industrial properties. It would help local governments, community groups, businesses, and others involved in those sites get better access to federal support and information. It also includes job training, internships, and workforce programs to help people build careers in cleanup and economic recovery work.

The people most directly affected are residents and workers in communities dealing with contaminated land, especially those in economically struggling areas. Local governments, tribal nations, and nonprofit or private groups trying to redevelop those sites would also be impacted. The bill does not guarantee funding on its own but directs the two agencies to better coordinate the resources that already exist.
ai_headline: Federal agencies ordered to jointly tackle polluted site cleanups
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and in addition to the Committees on Financial Services, and Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8238 requires the EDA and EPA to coordinate cleanup and redevelopment of contaminated sites and report to Congress within three years.
ai_sections.what: HR 8238 requires the Economic Development Administration and the Environmental Protection Agency to coordinate their work on cleaning up and redeveloping contaminated properties such as old factories and industrial sites. The two agencies would be directed to share resources and help communities access existing federal grants, loans, and expert advice during and after cleanups. The bill also establishes job training, internships, and workforce programs tied to cleanup and economic recovery work.
ai_sections.who: Residents and workers in communities with contaminated land, particularly those in economically struggling areas, are the primary people affected. Local governments, tribal nations, and nonprofit or private groups working to redevelop contaminated sites would also be directly impacted.
ai_sections.why: Because the two agencies currently operate separately, communities dealing with polluted sites may not be getting coordinated federal support. Requiring joint coordination and a congressional report within three years creates accountability for whether the agencies improve access to existing resources.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not guarantee or authorize new funding on its own. It directs the two agencies to better coordinate resources that already exist.

title: Farm Rescue Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would change how the U.S. Department of Agriculture pays certain farmers under an existing program called Price Loss Coverage, or PLC. PLC payments are made to farmers when the market price for their crops falls below a set reference price. Normally, farmers wait until after the marketing year ends to receive the full payment. This bill would allow the USDA to pay farmers an advance of 40 to 50 percent of their expected 2025 PLC payment early, within 90 days of the bill becoming law, if the government projects those payments will be owed. The rest of the payment would follow later once the actual final numbers are known.

The bill applies to farmers who grow commodities covered under the PLC program, which includes crops like corn, wheat, soybeans, rice, and peanuts, among others. These farmers would have the option to choose whether they want the early partial payment or not. It is not automatic or required. The USDA would also have the authority to recover any advance payments if it turns out the crop price did not actually fall enough to trigger a payment in the first place.

The people most directly affected are farmers who participate in the PLC program and planted covered crops in 2025. The bill is essentially designed to get money into farmers' hands faster during a year when crop prices are expected to be low, rather than making them wait months for the full payment to arrive through the normal process.
ai_headline: Farm bill would speed up crop price support payments in 2025
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, Risk Management, and Credit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5473 lets USDA pay eligible farmers 40–50% of their 2025 Price Loss Coverage amount within 90 days, before final crop prices are settled.
ai_sections.what: HR 5473 would allow the USDA to issue advance payments of 40 to 50 percent of a farmer's expected 2025 Price Loss Coverage payment within 90 days of enactment, rather than waiting until after the marketing year ends. Farmers would choose whether to accept the early partial payment; it is not automatic or required. USDA would have authority to recover advance payments if final crop prices do not fall low enough to trigger a payment.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects farmers who participate in the PLC program and planted covered commodities in 2025, including crops such as corn, wheat, soybeans, rice, and peanuts. The USDA would administer the advance payment and any recovery process.
ai_sections.why: Farmers currently receive full PLC payments only after the marketing year concludes, which can mean waiting months after prices have already fallen. This bill would alter that timeline for the 2025 crop year, moving a portion of anticipated payments earlier in the cycle.

title: Southwestern Power Administration Fund Establishment Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a single, dedicated bank account in the U.S. Treasury specifically for the Southwestern Power Administration (SPA), a federal agency that sells and delivers electricity generated at federal dams and reservoirs across Arkansas, Louisiana, Missouri, Oklahoma, and Texas. Right now, the SPA keeps its money in several separate funds. This bill consolidates all of those into one unified fund. Money coming in from electricity sales, customer payments, and federal appropriations would all go into this one account, and the SPA would draw from it to pay for day-to-day operations, maintenance of power lines, building new infrastructure, and administrative costs. Any leftover money at the end of the year would be returned to the general U.S. Treasury.

The practical effect is mostly about financial management and bookkeeping. Instead of tracking multiple accounts under different rules, the SPA would work from one streamlined fund. The bill also gives the SPA a bit more flexibility by allowing it to commit to necessary expenses before Congress formally approves the money, with the understanding the fund will cover those costs later. This is similar to how other government-owned corporations manage their finances.

The people most directly affected are the SPA itself and the rural electric cooperatives, utilities, and municipal power systems in the south-central United States that buy wholesale electricity from it. Everyday residents in that region who get power from utilities served by the SPA could be indirectly affected if the change improves or changes how reliably the agency operates, though the bill does not change electricity rates or service areas.
ai_headline: Federal power agency in five southern states would consolidate funds under HR 2432
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Natural Resources, and in addition to the Committee on Appropriations, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2432 merges the Southwestern Power Administration's separate accounts into one Treasury fund covering operations, maintenance, and infrastructure.
ai_sections.what: HR 2432 creates a single dedicated Treasury account for the Southwestern Power Administration, replacing its current system of multiple separate funds. Money from electricity sales, customer payments, and federal appropriations would flow into this one account, which would also cover operations, maintenance, new infrastructure, and administrative costs. Unspent money at year's end would return to the general U.S. Treasury.
ai_sections.who: The Southwestern Power Administration, which sells and delivers electricity generated at federal dams and reservoirs in Arkansas, Louisiana, Missouri, Oklahoma, and Texas, is directly affected. Rural electric cooperatives, utilities, and municipal power systems in that region that purchase wholesale electricity from the SPA are also affected, as are residents they serve.
ai_sections.why: The consolidation shifts the SPA from tracking multiple accounts under different rules to managing one streamlined fund, a structure similar to how other government-owned corporations handle finances. The bill also allows the SPA to commit to necessary expenses before formal congressional approval, with the fund expected to cover those costs afterward.
ai_sections.cost: Money coming into the single fund would include electricity sales revenue, customer payments, and federal appropriations. Unspent balances would be returned to the general U.S. Treasury at the end of each year.

title: Agricultural Cooperative Energy Savings Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill makes one specific change to an existing law. Right now, a USDA program that helps pay for renewable energy systems and energy efficiency improvements is available to farmers and certain rural small businesses. This bill would add agricultural cooperatives with fewer than 2,500 employees to the list of groups that can apply for that funding. An agricultural cooperative is a business jointly owned by farmers or ranchers who pool resources together, like a grain elevator or dairy co-op.

The program being changed is the USDA Rural Energy for America Program, often called REAP. It provides grants and loan guarantees to help eligible groups install things like solar panels, wind turbines, or energy-saving equipment. By letting more cooperatives qualify, those groups could apply for money to reduce their energy costs.

This bill mainly affects agricultural cooperatives that currently do not qualify for REAP assistance, particularly mid-sized ones that have grown beyond the previous size limits. Farmers who are members of those cooperatives could indirectly benefit if their co-op lowers its operating costs through energy savings. Rural communities where these cooperatives operate could also see some effect, since co-ops are often major local employers and economic anchors.
ai_headline: USDA energy grants opened to agricultural cooperatives under House bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6093 adds agricultural cooperatives with fewer than 2,500 employees to USDA's REAP energy grant program.
ai_sections.what: HR 6093 amends existing law to allow agricultural cooperatives with fewer than 2,500 employees to apply for the USDA Rural Energy for America Program, known as REAP. REAP provides grants and loan guarantees for renewable energy systems and energy efficiency improvements, including solar panels, wind turbines, and energy-saving equipment. Previously, these cooperatives were not among the eligible groups for this funding.
ai_sections.who: Agricultural cooperatives that currently do not qualify for REAP assistance are the direct targets of this change, particularly mid-sized cooperatives that have grown beyond previous size limits. Farmers and ranchers who are members of those cooperatives, as well as rural communities where the co-ops operate, could be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Qualifying cooperatives would gain access to grants and loan guarantees for energy-related installations, which could alter their operating costs. Because agricultural cooperatives are often major employers and economic anchors in rural areas, changes to their financial position may have broader local effects.

title: FARM SAFE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require certain U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) programs to keep running even when the federal government shuts down due to a lapse in funding. Normally, during a government shutdown, most federal workers are sent home and many programs pause. The FARM SAFE Act would change that rule for programs that give financial help to farmers and ranchers who have been hit by natural disasters like droughts, floods, or wildfires.

Specifically, the bill covers USDA disaster assistance programs that help farmers recover losses from these kinds of events. USDA employees needed to run those programs would be classified as "essential" workers, meaning they would stay on the job during a shutdown instead of being furloughed. Those employees also could not be laid off through a reduction in force while carrying out this work.

The people most directly affected are farmers and ranchers across the country who rely on federal disaster assistance after a major weather event or other agricultural emergency. Without this bill, a government shutdown could delay or cut off that aid at a critical time. USDA employees who administer these programs would also be affected, since they would be required to keep working during a shutdown rather than being sent home.
ai_headline: FARM SAFE Act would keep disaster aid flowing to farmers during shutdowns
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, Risk Management, and Credit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5716 would require USDA disaster assistance programs for farmers and ranchers to keep operating during a government shutdown.
ai_sections.what: HR 5716, the FARM SAFE Act, would require USDA disaster assistance programs to continue operating during a federal government shutdown caused by a lapse in funding. USDA employees who run those programs would be classified as essential workers, keeping them on the job instead of furloughed. Those employees could also not be laid off through a reduction in force while carrying out this work.
ai_sections.who: Farmers and ranchers nationwide who rely on federal disaster assistance after droughts, floods, wildfires, or other agricultural emergencies are the primary people affected. USDA employees who administer these disaster programs would also be directly affected, as they would be required to keep working during a shutdown.
ai_sections.why: Under current rules, a government shutdown can delay or cut off disaster assistance at the same time farmers and ranchers are trying to recover from a major weather event. This bill would change that outcome by treating the relevant USDA programs and staff as exempt from standard shutdown furlough rules.

title: Access to Donor Milk Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill aims to make pasteurized donor human milk more available to babies who need it, especially premature or high-risk newborns whose mothers cannot produce enough milk. It updates the WIC program (which helps low-income women, infants, and children with food and nutrition) to allow states to use WIC funds to collect, store, and transfer donated breast milk to nonprofit milk banks. The bill also directs the federal government to create public awareness materials about donor milk and send them to doctors, midwives, lactation consultants, and new or expectant parents.

The bill creates a grant program that lets the federal government give money to nonprofit donor milk banks during emergencies, such as a presidentially declared disaster or a public health emergency. These grants, starting at $3 million, could help milk banks hire more staff, buy equipment, cover processing fees, and spread the word to families about where to get donor milk. Grants can also kick in if a milk bank is simply seeing a sharp rise in demand or running short on supplies, even outside a declared emergency.

The bill also asks the Department of Health and Human Services to hold a public meeting and then issue safety guidelines for how donor milk is collected, tested, stored, pasteurized, and distributed. This most directly affects premature and sick newborns in hospitals, their families, nonprofit milk banks, healthcare providers like pediatricians and lactation consultants, and lower-income families already enrolled in WIC.
ai_headline: Federal bill would expand donor breast milk access for high-risk newborns
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.
