ai_sections.tldr: HR 8545 lets WIC funds support donor milk banks and creates a $3M emergency grant program for nonprofit milk banks.
ai_sections.what: HR 8545 would allow states to use WIC program funds to collect, store, and transfer donated breast milk to nonprofit milk banks. It establishes an emergency grant program for those milk banks and directs the Department of Health and Human Services to develop safety guidelines and public awareness materials about donor milk.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects premature and sick newborns in hospitals, their families, nonprofit donor milk banks, and lower-income families enrolled in WIC. Healthcare providers including pediatricians, midwives, and lactation consultants would also receive the new public awareness materials.
ai_sections.why: States would gain a new funding mechanism through WIC to support donor milk infrastructure, which currently operates without that federal channel. Nonprofit milk banks facing emergencies, supply shortages, or sharp rises in demand would have access to federal grants that do not currently exist.
ai_sections.cost: The bill creates a grant program starting at $3 million, which could fund milk bank staffing, equipment, processing fees, and outreach to families. WIC funds, which support low-income women, infants, and children, would also be redirectable toward donor milk collection, storage, and transfer.
costChips: WIC funds redirected to milk banks

title: SNAP Back Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would bring back work requirement exemptions for three specific groups of people who receive SNAP benefits, commonly known as food stamps. Under current SNAP rules, most able-bodied adults without dependents must work, train for work, or volunteer a certain number of hours each month to keep receiving benefits. This bill would exempt homeless individuals, military veterans, and former foster youth up to age 24 from having to meet those requirements.

The bill was introduced in response to exemptions for these three groups being removed in earlier legislation. If passed, homeless people, veterans of any age, and young adults who aged out of the foster care system would be able to receive SNAP benefits without needing to meet the work or job training rules that apply to other adults in the program.

The people most directly affected would be homeless individuals who may face barriers to steady employment, veterans who may be between jobs or dealing with challenges after military service, and young adults who were in foster care and may lack stable housing or family support. It would not change SNAP rules for any other recipients.
ai_headline: Bill would restore SNAP work exemptions for veterans, homeless, and former foster youth
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5718 would exempt homeless individuals, veterans, and former foster youth up to age 24 from SNAP work requirements.
ai_sections.what: HR 5718 would restore exemptions from SNAP work requirements for three groups whose exemptions were previously removed. Under current rules, most able-bodied adults without dependents must work, train, or volunteer monthly to keep receiving benefits. This bill would free homeless individuals, veterans of any age, and former foster youth up to age 24 from those rules.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects homeless individuals, military veterans of any age, and young adults up to age 24 who aged out of the foster care system. No other SNAP recipients would see their rules changed.
ai_sections.why: Homeless individuals may face barriers to steady employment that make current work requirements difficult to meet. Veterans may be between jobs or navigating challenges after military service, and former foster youth may lack stable housing or family support.

title: Healthcare is Human Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new tax credit for licensed healthcare workers who provide medical services at certain locations. The credit ranges from $300 to $500 per month, depending on how many hours the worker put in that month. To qualify, a worker must have worked at least 80 hours per month for at least 8 months out of the year. The credit is only available through the end of 2030, after which it expires unless Congress acts to renew it.

The locations that qualify are either VA medical facilities or healthcare sites located in areas officially designated as having a shortage of medical professionals. Those shortage areas are communities where there are not enough doctors, nurses, or other providers to meet the local population's needs. Workers at those locations who earn under $200,000 a year (or $400,000 for married couples filing jointly) would be eligible for the credit.

This bill mainly affects licensed and certified healthcare professionals such as doctors, nurses, and similar providers who work in underserved communities or at VA facilities serving veterans. It does not cover home health aides, hospice workers in certain situations, or suppliers of medical equipment. The Government Accountability Office would be required to study whether the credit actually helped keep healthcare workers in shortage areas and improved care for veterans, and report back to Congress before the credit ends.
ai_headline: New tax credit targets healthcare workers in shortage areas and VA facilities
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7884 offers licensed healthcare workers $300–$500/month in tax credits for working in VA or shortage-area facilities through 2030.
ai_sections.what: HR 7884 creates a monthly tax credit of $300 to $500 for licensed healthcare workers who provide medical services at VA medical facilities or in areas officially designated as having a shortage of medical professionals. The credit amount depends on how many hours the worker logged that month. It expires at the end of 2030 unless Congress renews it.
ai_sections.who: Eligible workers include licensed and certified healthcare professionals such as doctors and nurses who work in underserved communities or at VA facilities. Home health aides, hospice workers in certain situations, and medical equipment suppliers are excluded, as are individuals earning over $200,000 per year or married couples filing jointly earning over $400,000.
ai_sections.why: The Government Accountability Office would be required to study whether the credit succeeded in retaining healthcare workers in shortage areas and improving care for veterans, reporting findings to Congress before the credit expires. The credit's built-in expiration date means its continuation beyond 2030 would require separate congressional action.

title: Repeal the NFA Act
ai_summary:
The National Firearms Act (NFA) of 1934 is a federal law that places special rules on certain types of weapons, including machine guns, short-barreled rifles and shotguns, silencers, and destructive devices like grenades. Under current law, buying or owning these items requires paying a $200 tax, registering with the federal government, passing a background check, and waiting for approval — a process that can take months. This bill would completely eliminate that law and all the rules that come with it.

If this bill passed, those federal registration requirements, taxes, and approval processes for NFA-regulated weapons would no longer exist. Items like silencers and short-barreled rifles could be bought and sold without the special federal paperwork currently required. It would not automatically change state laws, so rules would vary depending on where someone lives.

This bill would affect gun owners, buyers, and sellers across the country, particularly those interested in items currently regulated by the NFA. It would also affect gun dealers and manufacturers who currently handle NFA paperwork. Federal agencies like the ATF, which currently enforce NFA rules, would no longer have that regulatory responsibility. The bill was introduced in January 2025 and sent to the House Ways and Means Committee, where it would need to be approved before moving forward.
ai_headline: House bill would repeal federal registration rules for silencers and short-barreled rifles
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 335 would repeal the National Firearms Act of 1934, ending federal registration, taxes, and approval waits for currently regulated weapons.
ai_sections.what: HR 335 would repeal the National Firearms Act of 1934, eliminating federal requirements to register, pay a $200 tax, pass a background check, and wait for approval to own weapons such as machine guns, silencers, short-barreled rifles and shotguns, and destructive devices. Items currently regulated under the NFA could be bought and sold without the special federal paperwork now required. State laws would not automatically change, so rules would continue to vary by location.
ai_sections.who: The bill would affect gun owners, buyers, sellers, dealers, and manufacturers who currently handle NFA paperwork and transactions. Federal agencies including the ATF, which now enforce NFA rules, would lose that regulatory responsibility.
ai_sections.why: Removing these federal requirements would mean that weapons currently subject to months-long approval processes could be transferred without federal oversight. Because state laws would remain in place independently, the practical effect on any individual would depend on the state where they live.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would eliminate the existing $200 federal tax currently required to purchase or own NFA-regulated items.

title: Strengthening the Vaccines for Children Program Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes several changes to how childhood vaccines are paid for and distributed through Medicaid and the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program. The VFC program provides free vaccines to children who are uninsured, underinsured, on Medicaid, or meet certain other criteria. The bill expands who qualifies for free VFC vaccines to include children enrolled in CHIP, which is the Children's Health Insurance Program for families who earn too much to qualify for Medicaid but still cannot afford private insurance. It also raises the minimum payment that doctors and clinics receive for giving vaccines and providing counseling under Medicaid and CHIP, setting that payment at the same rate Medicare pays for those services through at least the end of 2028.

The bill also gives states a small financial bonus, a one percentage point increase in federal Medicaid funding, starting in 2027, but only if the state actively runs outreach programs to inform families about vaccine safety and the benefits of immunization. It also clarifies that doctors participating in the VFC program can charge a separate fee for each component of a combination vaccine, and allows tribal epidemiology centers to potentially access CDC health data to better track vaccination rates in Native American communities.

The people most directly affected are low-income and uninsured children, their families, and the healthcare providers who vaccinate them. Doctors and clinics may be more willing to participate in the VFC program if they receive higher reimbursement rates. State governments will also need to meet new outreach requirements to receive the increased federal funding. The CDC will be required to publicly report vaccination rate data broken down by age, race, region, and other factors for 2027 and 2028.
ai_headline: Bill expands free vaccines to CHIP kids and raises doctor pay rates
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8425 adds CHIP-enrolled children to the free VFC vaccine program and raises provider pay to Medicare rates through at least 2028.
ai_sections.what: HR 8425 expands the Vaccines for Children program to cover children enrolled in CHIP, which currently serves families who earn too much for Medicaid but cannot afford private insurance. The bill raises the minimum payment doctors and clinics receive for administering vaccines and counseling under Medicaid and CHIP to match what Medicare pays for those same services, through at least the end of 2028. It also clarifies that providers can charge separately for each component of a combination vaccine and allows tribal epidemiology centers to potentially access CDC health data to track vaccination rates in Native American communities.
ai_sections.who: Low-income and uninsured children, their families, and the healthcare providers who vaccinate them are most directly affected. State governments, tribal epidemiology centers, and the CDC also have new responsibilities or eligibility under the bill.
ai_sections.why: Higher reimbursement rates may affect whether doctors and clinics choose to participate in the VFC program. States that do not run qualifying outreach programs on vaccine safety and immunization benefits will not receive the increased federal funding available starting in 2027.
ai_sections.cost: States can receive a one percentage point increase in federal Medicaid funding starting in 2027, but only if they actively run outreach programs on vaccine safety and immunization. Provider reimbursement under Medicaid and CHIP is set at the Medicare rate for those services through at least the end of 2028.

title: Universal School Meals Program Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would make school breakfast and lunch free for every child enrolled in a participating school, no matter how much money their family earns. Right now, families pay different amounts depending on their income, with some kids getting free meals, some getting reduced-price meals, and others paying full price. This bill eliminates those categories and makes all meals free. The federal government would reimburse schools at set rates, starting at $3.28 per breakfast and $5.42 per lunch, with those amounts adjusting each year based on food cost inflation. Schools that use at least 25 percent locally grown food would get a small extra payment per meal.

The bill also cancels any unpaid meal debt families currently owe to schools, with the federal government reimbursing schools for those outstanding balances. It bans schools from shaming or singling out children over meal debt, such as giving them a special ticket or publishing their names. Free meals would also be extended to summer programs, afterschool care programs, child care centers, and juvenile detention facilities. The bill expands the Summer EBT program, which gives families grocery benefits during summer months when school is not in session.

Since free and reduced-price lunch eligibility is currently used across many other federal programs to measure poverty among children, this bill creates a new survey-based system to identify low-income students for those purposes. This change affects how programs like workforce training, college preparation grants, child care block grants, and science education funding determine which students or schools qualify for extra resources. The bill affects all school-age children in the country, their families, school cafeteria programs, and the many other federal programs that use school meal status as a measure of economic need.
ai_headline: All school meals would become free for every student under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committees on Agriculture, and Science, Space, and Technology, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8798 would make school breakfast and lunch free for all students, with federal reimbursement starting at $3.28 per breakfast and $5.42 per lunch.
ai_sections.what: HR 8798 eliminates the current tiered system of free, reduced-price, and full-price school meals and makes breakfast and lunch free for every child at participating schools. The federal government would reimburse schools starting at $3.28 per breakfast and $5.42 per lunch, with amounts adjusted annually for food cost inflation. Schools using at least 25 percent locally grown food would receive a small additional payment per meal.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects all school-age children and their families, school cafeteria programs, and the many federal programs that currently use free and reduced-price lunch eligibility to measure child poverty. Summer programs, afterschool care programs, child care centers, and juvenile detention facilities would also be included.
ai_sections.why: Because free and reduced-price lunch status is currently used to determine eligibility for programs including workforce training, college preparation grants, child care block grants, and science education funding, the bill creates a new survey-based system to identify low-income students for those purposes. Outstanding unpaid meal debt owed by families would be canceled, with schools reimbursed by the federal government for those balances.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would reimburse schools at starting rates of $3.28 per breakfast and $5.42 per lunch, adjusted annually for food cost inflation. Schools using at least 25 percent locally grown food would receive an additional per-meal payment, and the government would also reimburse schools for all currently unpaid meal debt.

title: Foundation for America’s Public Lands Reauthorization Act
ai_summary:
This bill renews and updates a nonprofit organization called the Foundation for America's Public Lands, which supports the Bureau of Land Management (BLM). The BLM oversees about 245 million acres of federal land, mostly in the western United States, used for things like grazing, mining, energy production, and outdoor recreation. The bill renames the foundation from its old name, sets aside up to $10 million per year for five years to fund it, and bars the foundation from using that money for lawsuits or lobbying Congress.

The bill also changes how the foundation's board of directors is structured. The board would grow over time from up to 12 members to up to 18 members. It requires that the board eventually include people with specific backgrounds, such as fossil fuel energy, renewable energy, cattle ranching on federal land, motorized recreation like off-roading, non-motorized recreation like hiking, hunting or fishing, and mining. At least one-third of board members must have education or experience in natural resources, conservation, or related fields.

This bill mainly affects people who use or work on federal public lands managed by the BLM, including ranchers, miners, energy companies, and outdoor recreationists. It also affects the federal government and anyone who donates to or works with the foundation. Ordinary Americans who visit or rely on BLM-managed lands could also be indirectly affected, since the foundation raises private money to support projects on those lands.
ai_headline: Bill renews BLM foundation with $10M yearly cap and new board rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4044 renews the BLM's nonprofit foundation, funds it up to $10M/year for 5 years, and expands its board to 18 members.
ai_sections.what: HR 4044 renews and renames the Foundation for America's Public Lands, which raises private funds to support the Bureau of Land Management. The bill authorizes up to $10 million per year for five years and prohibits the foundation from spending that money on lawsuits or lobbying Congress. It also expands the board of directors from up to 12 members to up to 18 members and requires seats for people with specific industry and recreation backgrounds.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the foundation, the federal government, and anyone who donates to or works with the foundation. It indirectly affects ranchers, miners, energy companies, outdoor recreationists, and any Americans who use BLM-managed lands.
ai_sections.why: The foundation raises private money to support projects on roughly 245 million acres of federal land used for grazing, mining, energy production, and recreation. Changes to board composition and funding rules would alter who shapes the foundation's priorities and how its money can be used.
ai_sections.cost: The bill sets aside up to $10 million per year for five years to fund the foundation, for a potential total of up to $50 million. The foundation is barred from using those funds for litigation or congressional lobbying.
costChips: No funds for lawsuits or lobbying

title: ENABLE Conservation Act of 2025
ai_summary:
The ENABLE Conservation Act of 2025 makes two small but specific changes to an existing farm conservation program called the Conservation Reserve Program, or CRP. The CRP pays farmers to temporarily take certain land out of crop production and use it for conservation purposes, like protecting soil, water, or wildlife habitat. This bill tweaks the rules around a specific part of the CRP called the State Acres for Wildlife Enhancement, or SAFE, initiative, which focuses on creating habitat for wildlife on farmland.

The first change allows land enrolled in the SAFE initiative to qualify for continuous enrollment. Continuous enrollment is a special pathway in the CRP that lets farmers sign up their land at any time without waiting for a formal signup period, and it often comes with extra incentives. Previously, SAFE land may not have clearly qualified for this easier enrollment process, so this change removes that barrier. The second change fixes a technical cross-reference error in the law, updating a section number so the rules about acreage limits point to the correct part of the statute.

This bill mainly affects farmers and landowners who participate in or are interested in the SAFE initiative, as well as the USDA staff who administer the CRP. It could make it easier and faster for willing farmers to enroll wildlife habitat land in the program. It does not create a new program or spend new money on its own, but rather adjusts the existing rules to reduce red tape.
ai_headline: Farm wildlife habitat land gets easier CRP enrollment path under HR 5365
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5365 lets SAFE initiative farmland qualify for continuous CRP enrollment anytime, removing a signup timing barrier for farmers.
ai_sections.what: HR 5365 amends the Conservation Reserve Program to allow land enrolled in the State Acres for Wildlife Enhancement initiative to qualify for continuous enrollment, which permits signup at any time rather than during formal signup periods. The bill also corrects a technical cross-reference error in the law by updating a section number related to acreage limits. It does not create a new program.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects farmers and landowners who participate in or are considering the SAFE initiative. USDA staff who administer the CRP are also affected by the rule and technical corrections.
ai_sections.why: Qualifying for continuous enrollment can allow farmers to sign up wildlife habitat land faster and may come with additional incentives under existing CRP rules. The technical fix ensures acreage limit rules point to the correct part of the statute, reducing potential administrative confusion.

title: Accountable Produce is Medicine Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill requires a federal agency called the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation to set up and test a new program that connects Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP patients to fresh fruits, vegetables, and other healthy foods as part of their medical care. At least five programs across the country would be chosen to participate for a minimum of two years, and the overall pilot must run for at least five years. Patients in those programs would receive things like nutrition counseling, telehealth check-ins, remote health monitoring, and regular supplies of healthy food at no cost to them, meaning no copays or deductibles. The agency must launch this pilot within 180 days of the bill becoming law.

To qualify for the program, a person must already be enrolled in Medicare, Medicaid, or CHIP, live in an area with limited access to health care such as a rural or medically underserved community, and have a chronic condition like diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity, or cardiovascular disease. A doctor or health care provider must refer them, and the program can remove participants who are not engaging or following through. The program tracks health measurements like weight, blood pressure, and blood sugar every three months to see if participants are improving.

This bill primarily affects low-income adults and children with diet-related chronic illnesses who rely on government health coverage and live in areas that already lack enough doctors or health resources. It also affects health care providers and community organizations that could apply to become one of the selected programs. The bill is a test to see whether giving people access to healthy food through the health care system can lower long-term medical costs and improve health outcomes.
ai_headline: Federal pilot would send free healthy food to low-income patients with chronic disease
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8355 would test a 5-year federal program giving free food and nutrition care to Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP patients with chronic conditions.
ai_sections.what: HR 8355 directs the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation to create and test a program that supplies healthy food, nutrition counseling, telehealth visits, and remote health monitoring to enrolled patients. At least five programs nationwide would participate for a minimum of two years, and the overall pilot must run for at least five years. The agency would have 180 days from enactment to launch it.
ai_sections.who: The program targets low-income adults and children who have Medicare, Medicaid, or CHIP coverage, live in rural or medically underserved areas, and have a chronic condition such as diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity, or cardiovascular disease. Health care providers and community organizations could apply to become one of the selected participating programs.
ai_sections.why: The pilot is designed to test whether integrating healthy food access into the health care system can lower long-term medical costs and improve health outcomes. Participant health measurements including weight, blood pressure, and blood sugar would be tracked every three months to evaluate whether conditions are improving.
ai_sections.cost: Patients in the program would pay no copays or deductibles for the food, nutrition counseling, telehealth visits, or remote monitoring they receive.

title: Kidd’s Stuttering Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires Medicaid and CHIP, the government health insurance programs for lower-income and certain other Americans, to cover speech therapy for children who stutter or have similar speech fluency problems. Starting in 2027, states must include this therapy as a covered benefit. The bill also requires that limits on this stuttering-related speech therapy cannot be stricter than the limits already applied to other types of speech therapy, such as therapy for language disorders. In other words, a child getting help for stuttering must be treated the same as a child getting help for a different speech issue when it comes to what the insurance will pay for.

The bill also adds stuttering screenings to routine well-child doctor visits for children between the ages of 2 and 6. By early 2026, the federal government must update its standard health quality measures to include checking for childhood speech fluency disorders. By 2027, these screenings must be a required part of Medicaid well-child checkups. Telehealth counts as an approved way to deliver the speech therapy, meaning a child can receive sessions through video calls, not just in-person visits.

The people most affected are children ages 2 to 5 whose families are enrolled in Medicaid or CHIP. Families who previously could not afford private speech therapy for a child who stutters may gain access to covered services under this bill. State Medicaid and CHIP programs would be required to update their coverage rules and could see increased costs, though the federal government funds a large share of those programs.
ai_headline: Medicaid and CHIP would cover stuttering therapy for children starting in 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6364 requires Medicaid and CHIP to cover speech therapy for children who stutter, with screenings added to well-child visits, starting 2027.
ai_sections.what: Starting in 2027, Medicaid and CHIP must cover speech therapy for children who stutter or have similar speech fluency problems, and coverage limits on that therapy cannot be stricter than limits applied to other types of speech therapy. Stuttering screenings must be added to routine well-child visits for children ages 2 to 6, with the federal government required to update its standard health quality measures to include childhood speech fluency disorders by early 2026. Telehealth is an approved delivery method, meaning therapy sessions may occur via video call rather than in-person only.
ai_sections.who: Children ages 2 to 5 whose families are enrolled in Medicaid or CHIP are the primary people affected. State Medicaid and CHIP programs must update their coverage rules to comply with the new requirements.
ai_sections.why: Families who previously could not afford private speech therapy for a child who stutters may gain access to covered services. State programs would be required to revise coverage rules and could face increased costs as a result of the expanded mandate.
ai_sections.cost: State Medicaid and CHIP programs could see increased costs from the expanded coverage requirements, though the federal government funds a large share of those programs.

title: Donald J. Trump $250 Bill Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new $250 bill featuring a portrait of former President Donald Trump. The Treasury Department would be required to produce these bills within one year of the bill becoming law. The bill also connects this new denomination to the 250th anniversary of the United States, which falls in 2026.

The bill also changes a long-standing rule. Current law prohibits putting the portrait of any living person on U.S. currency or government securities. This bill would create an exception to that rule specifically for current or former presidents, which would allow a living person like Trump to appear on money for the first time in American history.

This would affect every American who uses cash, since a new denomination would enter everyday circulation. It would also affect the Treasury Department, which would be responsible for designing and producing the new bills. Businesses and banks would need to update equipment like cash registers and ATMs to handle the new denomination. The bill was introduced in the House and referred to the Committee on Financial Services, where it would need to advance before becoming law.
ai_headline: Bill would put Trump on a new $250 bill, breaking a ban on living portraits
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1761 would create a $250 bill featuring Trump and remove the ban on living people appearing on U.S. currency.
ai_sections.what: HR 1761 would create a new $250 denomination featuring a portrait of former President Donald Trump. The Treasury Department would be required to produce these bills within one year of enactment. The bill ties the new denomination to the 250th anniversary of the United States in 2026.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects every American who uses cash, as well as the Treasury Department, businesses, and banks that would need to update equipment such as cash registers and ATMs to handle the new denomination.
ai_sections.why: Current law prohibits placing the portrait of any living person on U.S. currency or government securities. This bill would create an exception for current or former presidents, which would allow a living person to appear on U.S. money for the first time in American history.

title: Providing Robust Organics and Diets for Urban Communities Everywhere Act
ai_summary:
This bill renews and expands a federal office inside the U.S. Department of Agriculture called the Office of Urban Agriculture and Innovative Production. That office was set to expire in 2023, and this bill would extend it through 2030. The office supports farming and food growing in cities and towns, including things like community gardens, rooftop farms, and other non-traditional growing methods.

The bill also doubles the funding for this office, from $25 million per year to $50 million per year. That money goes toward grants, research, and programs that help people in urban areas grow food locally, which can improve access to fresh produce in neighborhoods that may not have many grocery stores nearby.

The people most affected by this bill are city residents, community gardeners, urban farmers, local nonprofits, and schools or organizations that run food programs in cities. It could also matter to people who live in "food deserts," meaning areas where fresh, healthy food is hard to find or afford. The bill does not change eligibility rules for any existing benefit programs like food stamps.
ai_headline: Urban farming office gets seven-year extension and doubled funding
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5804 extends the USDA urban agriculture office to 2030 and raises its annual funding from $25M to $50M.
ai_sections.what: HR 5804 renews the Office of Urban Agriculture and Innovative Production, which was set to expire in 2023, extending it through 2030. The office funds grants, research, and programs supporting city-based food growing such as community gardens and rooftop farms.
ai_sections.who: City residents, community gardeners, urban farmers, local nonprofits, and schools or organizations running urban food programs are most directly affected. People living in food deserts, where fresh and affordable food is scarce, may also be impacted.
ai_sections.why: Extending the office keeps federal support in place for non-traditional food growing in urban areas. Doubling the annual funding increases the resources available for local food production in cities, which can affect fresh produce availability in underserved neighborhoods.
ai_sections.cost: The bill doubles annual funding for the office from $25 million to $50 million per year. No changes are made to eligibility rules for existing benefit programs such as food stamps.

title: Sgt. Walter F. Hartnett IV Green Star Veterans Service Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create an official Green Star Service Flag for family members of veterans who died by suicide after September 11, 2001. The Department of Veterans Affairs would be responsible for designing the flag. Family members, including spouses, children, parents, and other close relatives, would be allowed to display it. Companies would be able to apply for a license to make and sell the flag, and anyone who makes it without a license could face a fine of up to $1,000.

The bill is named after Sgt. Walter F. Hartnett IV, a veteran who died by suicide. It is meant to give families a recognized symbol, similar to how Gold Star families have an official flag to honor loved ones who died in military service. The green color appears to be chosen to distinguish this flag from existing military memorial flags.

This bill directly affects the families of veterans who lost their lives to suicide since 9/11. It also affects businesses that might want to manufacture or sell the flag, since they would need government approval to do so. The bill does not provide any new financial benefits or services to these families — it focuses solely on creating this official symbol of recognition.
ai_headline: New flag proposed to honor families of veterans lost to suicide since 9/11
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6022 would create an official Green Star Service Flag for families of post-9/11 veterans who died by suicide.
ai_sections.what: HR 6022 would establish an official Green Star Service Flag for family members of veterans who died by suicide after September 11, 2001. The Department of Veterans Affairs would be responsible for designing the flag. Businesses could apply for a government license to manufacture and sell it.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects spouses, children, parents, and other close relatives of veterans who died by suicide after 9/11. It also affects businesses that wish to produce or sell the flag, since a government license would be required.
ai_sections.why: The bill is named after Sgt. Walter F. Hartnett IV and is modeled on the existing Gold Star flag tradition for families of service members killed in military service. It focuses solely on providing an official symbol of recognition and does not create new financial benefits or services for affected families.
ai_sections.cost: Manufacturers who produce the flag without a license could face a fine of up to $1,000.

title: Responsible Wildland Fire Recovery Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with who pays for wildfire recovery when the U.S. Forest Service (part of the Department of Agriculture) causes a wildfire through its own land management activities, such as a prescribed burn that gets out of control. Normally, federal recovery programs require states, tribes, local governments, or individuals to pay a portion of the costs themselves — called a "matching requirement" or "cost share." This bill would allow the government to waive that requirement, meaning the federal government could cover 100% of the recovery costs instead of making affected parties chip in.

The people most directly affected would be states, Native American tribes, local governments, and private individuals whose land, water, or property was damaged by a wildfire that the Forest Service caused. Without this bill, those parties could be stuck paying part of the cleanup and recovery bill even though the government started the fire. With this bill, the Secretary of Agriculture would have the option to let them off the hook for that matching payment.

It is worth noting that the waiver is not automatic — the bill says the Secretary "may" waive the requirement, meaning it is left to their discretion. The fire also has to be officially determined to have resulted from Forest Service management activities on National Forest land. The bill was introduced in November 2025 and has been sent to the House Agriculture and Natural Resources committees for review.
ai_headline: Bill would let feds absorb full wildfire recovery costs when Forest Service starts the fire
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5963 lets Agriculture waive cost-share requirements for wildfire recovery when the Forest Service caused the fire.
ai_sections.what: HR 5963 would allow the Secretary of Agriculture to waive the matching payment requirement for wildfire recovery programs when a fire is officially determined to have resulted from Forest Service land management activities on National Forest land. That waiver is discretionary, not automatic. The bill was introduced in November 2025 and is under review by the House Agriculture and Natural Resources committees.
ai_sections.who: States, Native American tribes, local governments, and private individuals whose land, water, or property was damaged by a Forest Service-caused wildfire are the parties directly affected. Under current rules, those parties must contribute a share of recovery costs even when the federal government started the fire.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, affected states, tribes, local governments, and individuals remain responsible for a portion of recovery costs for fires the federal government caused. With this bill, the Secretary of Agriculture would have discretion to remove that financial obligation, though no waiver is guaranteed.
ai_sections.cost: This bill addresses who pays wildfire recovery costs when the Forest Service causes a fire. Currently affected parties must pay a cost-share portion; the bill would allow the federal government to cover 100% of those recovery costs instead.
costChips: Affected parties now owe a cost-share

title: Supporting Survivors from Faith-based Communities Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a national resource center focused on helping survivors of domestic violence, sexual assault, dating violence, and stalking who come from religious or faith-based backgrounds. The federal government would award grants, up to $2 million per year from 2027 through 2031, to a group of qualifying nonprofit organizations working together. That group must include faith-based organizations and culturally specific groups with experience in this area. The center would provide training, guidance, and support to religious communities, clergy, shelters, law enforcement, courts, and other organizations so they can better understand and respond to abuse survivors whose faith plays a role in their lives.

The bill also addresses a specific problem called "religious divorce denial," which is when one spouse refuses to participate in a religious divorce process, leaving the victim stuck even after a legal civil divorce. The Attorney General would be required to develop model state laws that other states could choose to adopt to address this issue, taking into account different religious traditions while staying within constitutional limits.

The people most directly affected are abuse survivors who are part of a religious community, as well as the clergy, religious organizations, shelters, and legal and law enforcement professionals who work with them. The bill makes clear that grant money cannot be used to promote any religion, and that victim safety must always come first. Organizations that put family unity, marriage preservation, or religious values ahead of a victim's safety and choices would not be eligible for funding.
ai_headline: Bill would fund national center for faith-based abuse survivors
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8813 funds a national resource center for faith-based abuse survivors at up to $2M/year from 2027 to 2031.
ai_sections.what: HR 8813 would establish a national resource center to help survivors of domestic violence, sexual assault, dating violence, and stalking who come from religious or faith-based backgrounds. The center would provide training and guidance to clergy, shelters, law enforcement, courts, and other organizations. The bill also directs the Attorney General to develop model state laws addressing religious divorce denial, where a spouse refuses to participate in a religious divorce process after a civil divorce is finalized.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects abuse survivors in religious communities, as well as the clergy, shelters, legal professionals, and law enforcement who work with them. Nonprofit organizations, including faith-based and culturally specific groups, would be eligible to apply for grants to operate the center.
ai_sections.why: Without dedicated resources, religious communities and the professionals who serve them may lack guidance on responding to survivors whose faith shapes their experience of abuse and recovery. The religious divorce denial provision acknowledges that civil divorce does not always resolve a victim's legal or spiritual situation, leaving some survivors in prolonged vulnerable circumstances.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would award grants of up to $2 million per year from 2027 through 2031 to a qualifying coalition of nonprofit organizations. Grant funds cannot be used to promote any religion.
costChips: Up to $2M in grants per year

title: Advancing Quality U.S. Aquaculture Act
ai_summary:
This bill updates a 1980 law about aquaculture, which is the farming of fish, shellfish, and other aquatic animals or plants. The main changes are creating a formal advisory committee made up of 14 outside experts who will meet at least three times a year and give advice to the government on aquaculture practices, research, and barriers facing the industry. The bill also requires the government to review its national aquaculture plan and assess the state of the industry at least once every three years, and to send Congress a yearly report covering things like how much money the federal government spends on aquaculture and what obstacles farmers face. It also extends funding authority for existing aquaculture programs from 2023 to 2029, and fixes a longstanding typo in the original law.

The people most directly affected are aquaculture farmers and businesses across the country, including those who raise fish, grow shellfish, or run land-based fish farming operations. The advisory committee is specifically required to address their needs and provide technical help. Researchers, universities, and federal agencies involved in fisheries and agriculture would also be affected, since the bill increases reporting and coordination requirements among them.

For ordinary Americans, the indirect effect is that the bill is aimed at growing domestic seafood production. If the industry expands as a result, it could mean more American-grown seafood available in stores, potentially at lower prices and with less reliance on imports. The bill does not guarantee any of those outcomes, but that is the stated goal behind strengthening government support and oversight of the industry.
ai_headline: House bill creates aquaculture advisory panel and extends program funding to 2029
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5471 updates a 1980 aquaculture law by adding a 14-member advisory committee and extending program funding through 2029.
ai_sections.what: HR 5471 amends a 1980 aquaculture law to establish a 14-member advisory committee that must meet at least three times a year to advise the government on aquaculture practices, research, and industry barriers. The bill also requires a review of the national aquaculture plan and an industry assessment at least every three years, plus an annual report to Congress covering federal spending on aquaculture and obstacles farmers face. Funding authority for existing aquaculture programs is extended from 2023 to 2029, and a longstanding typo in the original law is corrected.
ai_sections.who: Aquaculture farmers and businesses nationwide, including fish, shellfish, and land-based fish farming operations, are the most directly affected, as the advisory committee is specifically required to address their needs and provide technical help. Researchers, universities, and federal agencies involved in fisheries and agriculture are also affected through increased reporting and coordination requirements.
ai_sections.why: The bill is aimed at expanding domestic seafood production, which could affect the availability and pricing of American-grown seafood and the degree to which the country relies on imports. The bill does not guarantee those outcomes but they represent the stated goal behind increased government support and oversight.
ai_sections.cost: The bill extends funding authority for existing aquaculture programs through 2029, and requires the government to report annually to Congress on federal spending on aquaculture, though no specific dollar amounts are stated in the summary.

title: PRIMATE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal to bring monkeys, apes, and other non-human primates into the United States from other countries. Right now, primates can be imported for various purposes, including scientific research. If this bill becomes law, that would generally stop. The only exception is for zoos and aquariums that are accredited by the Association of Zoos and Aquariums, and even those facilities would have to promise in writing that the animals will not be used for experiments or testing. U.S. Customs and Border Protection would be responsible for blocking any shipments that break the rule.

Anyone who violates the ban could face a fine of up to $50,000 per violation, and any illegally imported primate could be taken by the government. The Treasury Department would be required to write detailed rules explaining how the law works within one year of it passing.

The people and organizations most affected would be biomedical and pharmaceutical research companies and universities that currently import primates, mainly monkeys, to use in lab studies and drug testing. The United States imports tens of thousands of non-human primates each year for research purposes, so a ban would have a significant impact on that industry. Accredited zoos would largely be unaffected, as long as the animals they bring in are not used for research.
ai_headline: Bill would ban primate imports for research, sparing accredited zoos
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8471 would ban nearly all primate imports into the U.S., hitting biomedical and pharmaceutical research hardest.
ai_sections.what: HR 8471 would make it illegal to import non-human primates, including monkeys and apes, into the United States from other countries. The ban would cover primates currently brought in for scientific research, lab studies, and drug testing. Accredited zoos and aquariums would be exempt, provided they certify in writing that imported animals will not be used for experiments.
ai_sections.who: Biomedical and pharmaceutical research companies and universities that currently import primates for lab use would be most affected. U.S. Customs and Border Protection would enforce the ban, and the Treasury Department would be required to issue implementing rules within one year of passage.
ai_sections.why: The United States currently imports tens of thousands of non-human primates each year for research purposes, so the ban would have a significant impact on that industry. Research institutions relying on primate imports for drug testing and lab studies would need to find alternatives or curtail that work.
ai_sections.cost: Violators could face a fine of up to $50,000 per violation, and any illegally imported primate could be seized by the government.
costChips: Illegally imported animals subject to seizure

title: NEDD Act
ai_summary:
This bill is about drones and nuclear facilities. Right now, federal law bans most government agencies from buying or using drones made by certain foreign companies, mainly ones linked to China. A previous law already gave some agencies, like the State Department, exceptions to that ban for specific national security reasons. This bill adds the Department of Energy to that list of exceptions, meaning the Secretary of Energy could buy, operate, and spend federal money on those foreign-made drones under certain conditions.

The bill also expands the Energy Department's power to protect nuclear sites from drones. Currently, the law allows the department to defend nuclear weapons facilities from drone threats. This bill widens that protection to also cover facilities that store or transport nuclear materials, as well as places that make non-nuclear parts used in nuclear weapons, not just the weapons facilities themselves.

The people most directly affected are workers and officials at the Department of Energy and the nuclear facilities it oversees, such as national laboratories and weapons plants. More broadly, it could affect communities near those facilities, since expanded drone defense authority is meant to improve security at sites that handle sensitive nuclear materials. The bill does not directly change anything for the general public, but it is part of ongoing efforts in Congress to balance national security needs with rules about using technology from foreign adversaries.
ai_headline: Energy Department added to foreign drone exception, nuclear site defenses expanded
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2205 lets the Energy Department buy foreign-made drones and defend more nuclear sites from drone threats.
ai_sections.what: HR 2205 adds the Department of Energy to a list of agencies that may buy, operate, and spend federal money on drones made by certain foreign companies, including those linked to China, under specific conditions. The bill also expands the department's authority to defend against drone threats beyond nuclear weapons facilities to include sites that store or transport nuclear materials and facilities that manufacture non-nuclear components used in nuclear weapons.
ai_sections.who: Workers and officials at the Department of Energy and the nuclear facilities it oversees, such as national laboratories and weapons plants, are most directly affected. Communities located near those facilities may also be affected by the expanded drone defense authority.
ai_sections.why: The change is part of ongoing congressional efforts to balance national security needs with existing restrictions on technology from foreign adversaries. Expanding drone defense authority is intended to improve security at sites handling sensitive nuclear materials, though the bill does not directly alter anything for the general public.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes the Secretary of Energy to spend federal money on foreign-made drones under certain conditions, but the summary does not specify amounts or funding sources.
costChips: Federal funds may be spent on foreign drones

title: CREATE AI Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a government-run program called the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource, or NAIRR. Right now, doing serious AI research requires enormous computing power and large datasets, and mostly only big tech companies can afford those things. The NAIRR would act like a shared library for AI tools, giving researchers access to computing power, data, software, and testing environments through a central online portal. The National Science Foundation would run the program, and a private nonprofit organization would be chosen through a competitive process to handle day-to-day operations.

The program would be open to researchers, educators, and students at U.S. universities, nonprofits, government agencies, and small businesses that have received federal funding. People working for countries considered national security threats would be blocked from access. There would be a free basic level of access, and users could pay for more resources beyond that. The bill also requires safeguards around privacy, ethics, cybersecurity, and research integrity, and projects focused on AI safety would be given priority when resources are limited.

This bill most directly affects university researchers, college and graduate students, small tech startups, and government scientists who currently lack the expensive computing tools needed to do advanced AI research. Indirectly, it could affect the broader public by potentially broadening who gets to shape how AI is developed in the United States, rather than leaving that work mainly to a handful of large corporations.
ai_headline: New federal AI research program would open advanced tools to universities and small businesses
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2385 would create a shared federal AI research platform run by NSF, opening computing power and data to researchers locked out by cost.
ai_sections.what: HR 2385 would establish the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource, a federally run program providing researchers with access to computing power, datasets, software, and testing environments through a central online portal. The National Science Foundation would oversee the program, while a competitively selected private nonprofit would handle day-to-day operations. A free basic access tier would exist, with paid options available for users who need more resources.
ai_sections.who: The program would be open to researchers, educators, and students at U.S. universities, nonprofits, government agencies, and federally funded small businesses. People affiliated with countries designated as national security threats would be blocked from participating.
ai_sections.why: Currently, advanced AI research requires computing power and large datasets that mostly only large technology companies can afford, concentrating who shapes AI development. Expanding access to these tools could shift some of that influence toward a broader range of institutions and individuals.

title: To amend title 14, United States Code, to require the retention of certain enlisted members of the Coast Guard who have completed 18 or more, but less than 20, years of service, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill would require the Coast Guard to keep certain enlisted members on active duty if they are close to earning retirement benefits but have not yet reached the 20-year mark needed to qualify. Right now, a Coast Guard member can be involuntarily separated or denied reenlistment before hitting that 20-year milestone, which would mean losing their retirement. This bill would change that by requiring the Coast Guard to retain those members until they reach 20 years of service, so they can earn the retirement they were close to receiving.

The bill covers two groups. Full-time Regular Coast Guard members who are within two years of retirement eligibility must be kept on active duty until they qualify. Reserve members serving in an active status who have between 18 and 20 years of service also cannot be forced out without their own agreement, and they would be kept on until they either reach 20 years or until a set deadline, depending on exactly how many years they have already completed.

This bill directly affects enlisted Coast Guard members, both full-time and active-status Reserve, who are in the 18-to-20-year service window and facing involuntary separation or a denial of reenlistment. It does not apply to separations caused by physical disability or misconduct. In practical terms, it protects a relatively small group of service members from losing retirement benefits they were close to earning.
ai_headline: HR 2200 would bar Coast Guard from separating enlisted members within 2 years of retirement
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Coast Guard and Maritime Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2200 requires the Coast Guard to retain enlisted members within 2 years of the 20-year retirement threshold until they qualify.
ai_sections.what: HR 2200 would require the Coast Guard to keep enlisted members on active duty if they are within two years of earning retirement eligibility, rather than allowing involuntary separation or denial of reenlistment before they reach 20 years of service. Full-time Regular members in that window must be retained until they hit 20 years. Active-status Reserve members with 18 to 20 years of service cannot be forced out without their own consent, and are kept on until they reach 20 years or until a set deadline tied to how many years they have already completed.
ai_sections.who: The bill covers enlisted Coast Guard members, both full-time Regular and active-status Reserve, who are in the 18-to-20-year service window and facing involuntary separation or a denial of reenlistment. It does not apply to separations caused by physical disability or misconduct.
ai_sections.why: Under current rules, Coast Guard members can be involuntarily separated or denied reenlistment before reaching the 20-year mark, resulting in the loss of retirement benefits they were close to earning. This bill would close that gap for a relatively small group of affected service members.

title: To require the Secretary of Agriculture to release a reversionary interest in certain land in the Black River State Forest in Millston, Wisconsin, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill deals with a land swap in Millston, Wisconsin. Right now, about 32 acres of land inside the Black River State Forest is technically tied to the federal government through an old legal rule that says the land must stay in public use forever. A local business called Deli, Inc., which harvests sphagnum moss, wants to use that land. To make that possible, the bill would have the U.S. Department of Agriculture give up its legal claim on that land, allowing the state of Wisconsin to transfer it to the business.

In exchange, Deli, Inc. would hand over roughly 37 acres of land it owns nearby, and that land would be added to the Black River State Forest. So the forest would actually gain a little more land than it gives up. The bill sets this up as a conditional deal, meaning the federal government's release only happens if Wisconsin and Deli, Inc. finalize a written agreement to complete the swap.

The people most directly affected are the owners and employees of Deli, Inc. and the state agencies that manage Wisconsin's forests. For the general public, the net result is a small increase in state forest land in Jackson County, Wisconsin. The bill does not involve any money changing hands between the federal government and any other party.
ai_headline: Federal land claim on 32 Wisconsin acres would be released for moss harvester
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5866 releases a federal claim on 32 acres in Black River State Forest so Wisconsin can transfer the land to Deli, Inc.
ai_sections.what: HR 5866 would direct the U.S. Department of Agriculture to release its legal claim on roughly 32 acres inside the Black River State Forest in Millston, Wisconsin. That release would allow the state of Wisconsin to transfer those acres to Deli, Inc., a local business that harvests sphagnum moss. The deal is conditional and only takes effect if Wisconsin and Deli, Inc. finalize a written agreement.
ai_sections.who: The owners and employees of Deli, Inc. and the state agencies that manage Wisconsin's forests are most directly affected. Residents of Jackson County would see a net increase of roughly five acres of state forest land, as Deli, Inc. would contribute about 37 acres in return.
ai_sections.why: Because Deli, Inc. provides 37 acres in exchange for roughly 32, the Black River State Forest would end up with slightly more land than it transfers out. The federal government plays no ongoing role once the legal claim is released, and no money changes hands between federal and other parties.

title: EACH Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill, called the EACH Act of 2025, would require federal health programs to cover abortion services. Right now, a longstanding policy known as the Hyde Amendment blocks the federal government from paying for abortions in most cases. This bill would overturn that restriction, meaning programs like Medicaid, Medicare, CHIP, TRICARE for military members, the VA for veterans, and the Federal Employees Health Benefit Plan would all be required to include abortion coverage. It would also repeal a section of the Affordable Care Act that currently allows states to ban abortion coverage in their insurance marketplaces.

The bill would also stop the federal government from allowing states or private insurers to restrict abortion coverage in their own health plans. In other words, it would not just change what federal programs cover — it would also prevent states and private insurance companies from limiting abortion coverage within their plans. The bill specifically states it cannot be overridden by the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, which has sometimes been used to allow religious exemptions from covering certain medical services.

This bill would affect a wide range of people. Anyone enrolled in a federal health program — including low-income Americans on Medicaid, military families, veterans, federal workers, Native Americans served by the Indian Health Service, and people in immigration detention — would gain abortion coverage. It would also affect people with private insurance, since states and insurers could no longer restrict that coverage under federal law.
ai_headline: Federal bill would require Medicaid, VA, and military plans to cover abortion
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

ai_sections.tldr: The EACH Act of 2025 would end the Hyde Amendment and require federal health programs to cover abortion services.
ai_sections.what: The EACH Act of 2025 would repeal the Hyde Amendment, which currently blocks federal funding for abortion in most cases. It would require Medicaid, Medicare, CHIP, TRICARE, the VA, and the Federal Employees Health Benefit Plan to cover abortion services. It would also repeal a provision of the Affordable Care Act that lets states ban abortion coverage in their insurance marketplaces.
ai_sections.who: The bill would affect anyone enrolled in a federal health program, including low-income Americans, military families, veterans, federal workers, Native Americans served by the Indian Health Service, and people in immigration detention. People with private insurance would also be affected, as states and private insurers could no longer restrict abortion coverage under federal law.
ai_sections.why: Overturning the Hyde Amendment would represent a significant shift in federal abortion policy that has been in place for decades. States and private insurers that currently restrict abortion coverage would lose the legal authority to do so under federal law, and religious exemption claims under the Religious Freedom Restoration Act could not be used to override the bill's requirements.

title: Recycled Materials Attribution Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes it illegal for companies to falsely or misleadingly claim that a product contains recycled materials when advertising or selling it to consumers. It also formally approves a method called "mass balance accounting," which lets manufacturers track and assign recycled materials through a complex supply chain even when those materials are mixed together with non-recycled ones during production. To use this method legally, companies must have their claims verified by an independent third-party certifier. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) would be responsible for enforcing the rules and would be required to update its existing marketing guidelines, known as the Green Guides, within one year to reflect the new standards.

The bill affects businesses that make, market, or sell products with recycled content labels, including manufacturers of plastics, packaging, and other goods that use recycled feedstocks in their production process. It also affects consumers, who would have stronger protections against being misled by green marketing claims on products they buy. Notably, fuels cannot be labeled as "recycled content" under this bill, even if they are made using recycled materials.

The bill also overrides any state or local laws on the same topic, meaning a single national standard would apply everywhere in the country instead of a patchwork of different state rules. This matters for companies that sell products across multiple states, since they would only need to follow one set of recycled content labeling rules rather than potentially dozens of different ones.
ai_headline: Federal bill bans misleading recycled-content labels and sets one national standard
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7502 bans false recycled-content claims, approves mass balance accounting with third-party verification, and preempts all state labeling laws.
ai_sections.what: HR 7502 makes it illegal to falsely or misleadingly claim a product contains recycled materials. It formally approves mass balance accounting, a method that lets manufacturers track recycled materials through complex supply chains where those materials may be mixed with non-recycled ones, provided an independent third-party certifier verifies the claims. Fuels are explicitly excluded from recycled-content labeling even if made using recycled materials.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects manufacturers, marketers, and sellers of products with recycled-content labels, including those in plastics and packaging. Consumers gain stronger protections against misleading green marketing claims on products they purchase.
ai_sections.why: The FTC would be required to enforce the new rules and update its Green Guides within one year to reflect the new standards. The bill overrides all state and local recycled-content labeling laws, replacing them with a single national standard that applies uniformly across every state.

title: LOCAL Foods Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes a small but specific part of federal meat inspection law. Right now, only the person who personally raised the animal can slaughter it without a federal inspection, and only for their own household. This bill expands that exemption to include anyone who is a partial or full owner of the animal, not just the person who raised it. It also allows owners to hire someone to help with the slaughtering, butchering, or transporting of the meat, as long as the owner keeps track of and maintains control over their specific meat throughout the process.

The main practical effect is on community livestock ownership arrangements, sometimes called "herd shares" or cooperative ownership programs. In these setups, a group of people pool money to collectively own an animal, then split the meat. Under current law, this kind of arrangement can fall into a legal gray area. This bill would clearly allow those co-owners to have the animal slaughtered and the meat processed without requiring federal inspection, as long as the meat stays within the circle of owners, their households, guests, and employees.

This affects small-scale farmers, ranchers, and people who participate in community-supported agriculture or livestock share programs. It does not change rules for meat sold commercially in stores or restaurants, which still requires full federal inspection. Consumers who buy meat at grocery stores or markets would not be affected. The change mainly matters to people seeking a more direct, local connection to where their meat comes from.
ai_headline: Federal meat inspection exemption expanded to cover co-owners of livestock
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5341 lets partial livestock owners skip federal inspection when slaughtering animals they co-own, covering herd-share and cooperative arrangements.
ai_sections.what: HR 5341 amends federal meat inspection law to extend an existing exemption — previously limited to the person who personally raised an animal — to any full or partial owner of that animal. Owners may also hire help for slaughtering, butchering, or transporting meat, provided the owner maintains control over and tracks their specific portion throughout. The exemption applies only when meat stays within the circle of owners, their households, guests, and employees.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects small-scale farmers, ranchers, and participants in herd-share or cooperative livestock ownership programs. It does not affect consumers who purchase meat at grocery stores, markets, restaurants, or any other commercial outlets.
ai_sections.why: Community livestock ownership arrangements have operated in a legal gray area under current law; this bill would resolve that ambiguity by explicitly permitting co-owners to process animals without federal inspection. Rules governing commercially sold meat remain unchanged, and full federal inspection requirements stay in place for any meat entering commercial trade.

title: BE HEARD in the Workplace Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the BE HEARD in the Workplace Act, aims to strengthen protections against discrimination and harassment on the job. It would require employers with 15 or more employees to create and share written anti-harassment policies, and it would require workplace training on the topic. The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) would set up a new office to educate workers and employers about their rights. The bill also directs the government to study how common harassment is and what works to prevent it. For smaller businesses with fewer than 15 employees, the EEOC would provide free model policies and training materials they could use voluntarily.

The bill also makes several changes to existing civil rights laws. It explicitly states that discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity counts as illegal sex discrimination. It lowers the legal standard for proving harassment, making it easier for workers to win cases by showing that harassment changed their working conditions, rather than having to prove it was extremely severe. It removes caps on how much money a worker can receive if they win a discrimination lawsuit, gives workers more time to file complaints, and makes it harder for employers to force workers into private arbitration instead of court. It also limits the use of nondisclosure agreements that can silence harassment victims.

The bill affects a wide range of workers. Tipped workers, like restaurant servers, would see their base wages gradually raised until they match the regular federal minimum wage. Independent contractors, interns, volunteers, and trainees would gain new protections against harassment that currently apply mainly to regular employees. Federal government employees are also covered under parts of this bill. Essentially, the bill would touch nearly every working person in the country, as well as the businesses and agencies that employ them.
ai_headline: BE HEARD Act would extend harassment protections to millions of U.S. workers
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committees on the Judiciary, House Administration, Oversight and Government Reform, and Veterans' Affairs, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7583 would broaden who is protected from workplace harassment and make it easier to prove and win discrimination claims.
ai_sections.what: HR 7583, the BE HEARD in the Workplace Act, would require employers with 15 or more employees to adopt written anti-harassment policies and provide workplace training. It lowers the legal standard for proving harassment, removes caps on lawsuit damages, gives workers more time to file complaints, and limits forced arbitration and nondisclosure agreements. It also explicitly classifies discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity as illegal sex discrimination.
ai_sections.who: The bill covers a broad range of workers including regular employees, independent contractors, interns, volunteers, trainees, tipped workers, and federal government employees. Employers of all sizes are affected, with mandatory requirements applying to those with 15 or more employees and voluntary resources available to smaller businesses.
ai_sections.why: Lowering the legal standard for harassment and removing damage caps could lead to more workers filing and winning discrimination claims, changing how employers manage workplace conduct. Extending protections to contractors, interns, and tipped workers would shift existing legal obligations for a large share of the workforce that currently lacks those rights.

title: Boundary Waters Wilderness Protection and Pollution Prevention Act
ai_summary:
This bill would permanently ban copper, nickel, and other sulfide-ore metal mining on about 225,504 acres of federal land in northeastern Minnesota. That land sits in the Rainy River Watershed, which drains into the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. The ban covers new mining claims, leases, and mineral extraction. It would make permanent a 20-year mining withdrawal that the Interior Department put in place in January 2023. Some materials like sand, gravel, granite, iron ore, and taconite could still be removed, but only if the Forest Service determines it would not harm water quality, air quality, or the forest.

The main reason behind the bill is concern about acid mine drainage. When sulfide minerals are dug up and exposed to air and water, they can produce sulfuric acid and release harmful metals like lead, copper, and cadmium into waterways. The bill points to a study finding that all 14 U.S. copper sulfide mines examined had spills or leaks. Because the Boundary Waters and Voyageurs National Park are classified as top-quality water resources under federal and state law, supporters argue even a small pollution risk is too great. The area also borders Canada, and a 1909 international treaty requires both countries to keep shared border waters clean.

The bill directly affects mining companies that hold or sought mineral rights in that part of the Superior National Forest. It also affects the three Ojibwe tribal nations in the region, who hold treaty hunting and fishing rights there and whose interests the bill cites as a reason for protection. Outdoor recreation businesses, tourism operators, and the roughly 150,000 annual visitors to the Boundary Waters would be affected by whether or not industrial mining activity occurs nearby. Local residents and workers in northeastern Minnesota are also affected, since the area's economy includes both mining-related jobs and a large tourism and recreation industry.
ai_headline: Permanent mining ban proposed for 225,504 acres near Boundary Waters
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 588 would permanently ban sulfide-ore mining on 225,504 federal acres draining into the Boundary Waters and Voyageurs National Park.
ai_sections.what: HR 588 would permanently ban copper, nickel, and other sulfide-ore metal mining on about 225,504 acres of federal land in northeastern Minnesota. The land drains into the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. Sand, gravel, granite, iron ore, and taconite could still be removed if the Forest Service determines it would not harm water quality, air quality, or the forest.
ai_sections.who: Mining companies holding or seeking mineral rights in the Superior National Forest are directly affected, as are three Ojibwe tribal nations with treaty hunting and fishing rights in the area.
