Outdoor recreation businesses, tourism operators, roughly 150,000 annual visitors to the Boundary Waters, and local workers in northeastern Minnesota are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Sulfide minerals exposed to air and water can produce sulfuric acid and release harmful metals such as lead, copper, and cadmium into waterways. A study cited in the bill found that all 14 U.S. copper sulfide mines examined had spills or leaks, and the Boundary Waters and Voyageurs National Park are classified as top-quality water resources under federal and state law.

title: Price Gouging Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would make it a federal crime for businesses to charge what the law calls "grossly excessive" prices for goods and services. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) would be given the power to define exactly what that means, but the bill suggests that prices 20% or more above what a product normally sold for in the previous six months could qualify. During declared emergencies or major market disruptions — like natural disasters, public health crises, or sudden trade policy changes — businesses would be automatically suspected of price gouging if they raise prices significantly compared to what they charged in the 120 days before the disruption. Large companies with at least $1 billion in annual revenue, or companies that dominate their market, would face stricter rules and heavier fines. Penalties could reach 5% of a company's total annual revenue. Smaller businesses earning under $100 million annually have more protection if they can show that their price increases were caused by costs outside their control.

The bill also requires publicly traded companies to disclose detailed pricing information in their regular SEC financial filings whenever a major market disruption occurs. They would have to explain why prices changed, how their profit margins shifted, and what their pricing plans are going forward. This is meant to give the public and regulators more transparency into whether companies are profiting from crises.

The bill affects a wide range of people and organizations. Everyday consumers who buy groceries, medicine, fuel, or other goods during emergencies would be the intended beneficiaries. Businesses of all sizes — from large corporations to local retailers — would face new legal obligations around how they set prices. The FTC and state attorneys general would both get new tools to investigate and sue companies they believe are price gouging. The bill also sets aside $1 billion to fund the FTC's enforcement work through 2033.
ai_headline: Federal bill would make price gouging a crime with fines up to 5% of revenue
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Financial Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4528 would make "grossly excessive" pricing a federal crime, with fines up to 5% of a company's annual revenue.
ai_sections.what: HR 4528 would make it a federal crime for businesses to charge "grossly excessive" prices for goods and services. The FTC would define that standard, though the bill suggests prices 20% or more above a product's normal price over the prior six months could qualify. During declared emergencies or major market disruptions, businesses that raise prices significantly compared to what they charged in the 120 days before the disruption would be automatically suspected of price gouging.
ai_sections.who: Large companies with at least $1 billion in annual revenue, or companies that dominate their market, would face stricter rules and heavier fines. Smaller businesses earning under $100 million annually have more protection if they can show price increases were driven by costs outside their control.
ai_sections.why: The FTC and state attorneys general would gain new authority to investigate and sue companies suspected of price gouging. Publicly traded companies would also be required to disclose detailed pricing information, profit margin changes, and future pricing plans in SEC filings whenever a major market disruption occurs.
ai_sections.cost: Penalties could reach 5% of a company's total annual revenue. The bill sets aside $1 billion to fund FTC enforcement through 2033.
costChips: Fines up to 5% of annual revenue

title: Keep STEM Talent Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new immigration pathway for foreign students who earn a master's degree or higher in a STEM field from a U.S. college or university. If they have a job offer from a U.S. employer that pays above the median wage for that type of work, and they pass a labor certification process, they could apply for a green card (lawful permanent resident status) without being subject to the normal annual caps on green cards. Their spouses and children would also be eligible. The bill covers fields like computer science, engineering, math, biology, physical sciences, agriculture, and natural resources.

The bill also changes the rules for how foreign students on F-1 student visas apply for graduate STEM programs. Under current law, people on student visas generally cannot show that they intend to immigrate permanently, or they risk being denied. This bill removes that barrier specifically for graduate STEM students, letting them pursue a green card while still on a student visa. It also requires stronger background checks and credential verification for these students, comparable to the screening done for people applying from outside the U.S.

The people most directly affected are foreign nationals currently studying or planning to study for advanced STEM degrees at American universities, as well as the U.S. employers who hire them. American workers in STEM fields could also be affected, since the bill requires a labor certification process meant to confirm that hiring a foreign worker will not harm domestic workers. U.S. universities that enroll international graduate students would also see changes in how their applicants are processed and screened.
ai_headline: New green card path opens for foreign STEM graduate students with job offers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2627 creates a cap-exempt green card path for foreign nationals who earn a U.S. graduate STEM degree and have a qualifying job offer.
ai_sections.what: HR 2627 would establish a new immigration pathway allowing foreign nationals who earn a master's degree or higher in a STEM field from a U.S. institution to apply for a green card outside the normal annual caps. Eligibility requires a job offer from a U.S. employer paying above the median wage for that role and passage of a labor certification process. The bill also removes the existing rule that bars F-1 student visa holders from showing immigrant intent, specifically for graduate STEM students, and mandates stronger background checks and credential verification for that group.
ai_sections.who: Foreign nationals studying or planning to study for advanced STEM degrees at U.S. universities are the primary group affected, along with U.S. employers who hire them and their spouses and children who would also be eligible. American workers in STEM fields and U.S. universities that enroll international graduate students would also see direct effects.
ai_sections.why: The labor certification requirement is designed to determine whether hiring a foreign worker in a given role would harm domestic workers in that field. Changes to F-1 visa rules and screening requirements would alter how U.S. universities process and vet international graduate applicants.

title: COWS Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill, called the COWS Act (Converting Our Waste Sustainably Act of 2025), updates existing farm conservation programs to add new options for how livestock farmers handle animal manure. Right now, many farms store manure in large liquid lagoons, which release methane and other gases. The bill creates official definitions for "alternative manure management practices" and "composting practices," and adds them to the existing Environmental Quality Incentives Program (EQIP), which is a Department of Agriculture program that pays farmers to use environmentally friendly farming methods. These new options include things like compost barns, solar drying, and systems that separate solid waste from liquid waste instead of letting it sit in lagoons.

The bill also sets up a payment structure where the government can cover up to 100 percent of the costs for farmers who switch to these cleaner manure methods. At least half of that money would be paid upfront so farmers can afford the equipment and materials before the project is complete. The Department of Agriculture would be required to rank applications based on how much they reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve water quality, and would be directed to prioritize small and mid-sized farms, beginning farmers, and farmers from disadvantaged backgrounds. Groups of farmers could also apply together to share a composting facility.

This bill primarily affects dairy and livestock farmers across the country, especially smaller operations. It also affects the Department of Agriculture, which would need to create new technical standards, publish emissions reduction estimates, and work with outside experts to help farms make the switch. Nearby communities could also be affected, since better manure management can improve local air and water quality.
ai_headline: Farm bill adds composting and solar drying as manure alternatives
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

ai_sections.tldr: The COWS Act adds composting and alternative manure options to USDA's EQIP program, covering up to 100% of costs for qualifying livestock farmers.
ai_sections.what: The COWS Act (HR 5875) amends existing farm conservation programs to add alternative manure management practices, such as compost barns, solar drying, and solid-liquid separation systems, to the USDA's Environmental Quality Incentives Program (EQIP). The bill creates official definitions for these practices and requires the Department of Agriculture to develop technical standards and publish emissions reduction estimates. Applications would be ranked by how much they reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve water quality.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects dairy and livestock farmers nationwide, with priority given to small and mid-sized farms, beginning farmers, and farmers from disadvantaged backgrounds. The Department of Agriculture would take on new administrative responsibilities, and nearby communities could see changes in local air and water quality.
ai_sections.why: Current widespread use of liquid manure lagoons releases methane and other gases, and this bill would shift federal financial support toward practices that avoid that storage method. Groups of farmers could apply jointly to share a composting facility, affecting how operations coordinate at a regional level.
ai_sections.cost: The government could cover up to 100 percent of costs for farmers who switch to these cleaner manure methods. At least half of that payment would be made upfront, before a project is complete.

title: Habitat Enhancement Now Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates two federal grant programs run by the Department of the Interior to help wild duck populations recover. The first program would fund the placement and upkeep of "hen houses," which are cylindrical nesting structures mounted on posts in wetlands across the Prairie Pothole Region, a large area of the northern Great Plains that is the main breeding ground for most North American ducks. The second program focuses on California and would pay for creating nesting areas, building small ponds, and offering money to private landowners who are willing to improve duck breeding habitat on their own property. Both programs would begin within 180 days of the bill becoming law.

The money would come from the Department of the Interior's existing budget, with $3.5 million set aside for each program every year from 2026 through 2030, for a total of up to $35 million over five years. States, local governments, tribal governments, nonprofit groups, and private individuals could all apply for grants through a competitive process.

The people most directly affected are landowners, farmers, and conservation groups in the Prairie Pothole Region states such as North and South Dakota, Montana, and Minnesota, as well as those in California who work with wetlands and duck habitat. Hunters and wildlife watchers who depend on healthy duck populations would also be indirectly affected. The bill was introduced in July 2025 and has been sent to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets duck population recovery with $35 million in grants
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4591 would fund two Interior Department grant programs to restore duck habitat, totaling up to $35M over five years.
ai_sections.what: HR 4591 creates two federal grant programs administered by the Department of the Interior to support wild duck population recovery. The first funds placement and maintenance of cylindrical nesting structures called hen houses in wetlands of the Prairie Pothole Region. The second focuses on California, covering costs for nesting areas, small ponds, and payments to private landowners who improve duck breeding habitat on their property.
ai_sections.who: Landowners, farmers, conservation groups, state and local governments, tribal governments, and nonprofits in Prairie Pothole Region states such as North and South Dakota, Montana, and Minnesota, as well as those in California, are the most directly affected. Hunters and wildlife watchers who depend on healthy duck populations would also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Both programs must launch within 180 days of enactment, and eligible parties may apply through a competitive grant process. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources, where its progress will determine whether these programs are established.
ai_sections.cost: Funding would come from the Department of the Interior's existing budget, with $3.5 million allocated to each program annually from 2026 through 2030, totaling up to $35 million over five years.
costChips: Up to $35M total over five years

title: Pharmacists Fight Back [in Federal Employee Health Benefit Plans Act]
ai_summary:
This bill targets companies called pharmacy benefit managers, or PBMs. PBMs are middlemen hired by health insurance plans to handle prescription drug coverage. They decide which drugs are covered, how much pharmacies get paid, and how much patients pay out of pocket. The bill would set new rules for how PBMs must behave when working with health insurance plans offered to federal government employees and their families through the Federal Employees Health Benefits program.

Under this bill, PBMs would have to pay pharmacies a set, transparent amount for drugs based on publicly tracked pricing data, plus a standard dispensing fee. They could not steer patients toward pharmacies the PBM owns or has a financial relationship with. They also could not pocket drug rebates from manufacturers without passing the savings on to patients at the pharmacy counter. If a PBM breaks these rules, the federal Office of Personnel Management could fine it up to $10,000 per violation, and a PBM that racks up 10 or more fines within 10 years could be banned from working with federal employee health plans entirely.

The people most directly affected are the roughly 8 million federal employees, retirees, and their family members enrolled in federal health plans who use prescription drug benefits. Independent community pharmacies could also benefit, since the bill limits the ability of PBMs to favor their own affiliated pharmacies. Large PBM companies, which currently have significant control over how drugs are priced and distributed, would face new restrictions and financial penalties if they do not comply.
ai_headline: Federal employee drug plans would get new limits on pharmacy middlemen
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6610 sets transparency and anti-steering rules for PBMs serving 8 million federal employees and families, with fines up to $10,000 per violation.
ai_sections.what: HR 6610 places new rules on pharmacy benefit managers, the companies that manage prescription drug coverage for federal employee health plans. PBMs would have to pay pharmacies based on publicly tracked pricing data plus a standard dispensing fee, and would be required to pass drug rebates from manufacturers directly to patients at the pharmacy counter. They could not direct patients toward pharmacies they own or have a financial relationship with.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 8 million federal employees, retirees, and their family members enrolled in the Federal Employees Health Benefits program would be directly affected. Independent community pharmacies and large PBM companies that currently control drug pricing and distribution in these plans would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: PBMs would face new restrictions on steering, pricing, and rebate handling that could alter how prescription drug costs flow through federal health plans. Companies that do not comply face escalating consequences, up to and including a full ban from participating in federal employee health coverage.
ai_sections.cost: The Office of Personnel Management could fine a PBM up to $10,000 per violation. A PBM that receives 10 or more fines within 10 years could be banned from federal employee health plans entirely.

title: SEPSIS Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to create and maintain a dedicated sepsis team. That team would run public education campaigns about sepsis best practices for hospitals, collect better data on sepsis cases (especially in children), and work with other federal health agencies to develop standard ways of measuring how well hospitals are treating and preventing sepsis. Within one year of passage, the CDC director would be required to report to Congress on progress and then give annual updates on how hospitals across the country are doing.

The bill also lets the federal government set up a voluntary honor roll program for hospitals that do a good job detecting and treating sepsis early or that show clear improvement over time. Hospitals could apply, and the government would set public standards for what earns recognition. No hospital would be forced to participate. The bill authorizes spending up to $20 million per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund all of these activities.

This bill mainly affects hospitals, healthcare workers, and federal health agencies like the CDC and Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services. Patients, especially children, who are at risk for sepsis could also be indirectly affected if better hospital practices lead to earlier treatment. Sepsis is a life-threatening reaction to infection that kills about 350,000 Americans each year, so the people most directly impacted would be patients who develop serious infections and the medical staff who treat them.
ai_headline: CDC would get a dedicated sepsis team under bill costing $20M yearly
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7116 funds a new CDC sepsis team at up to $20M/year through 2030 to improve hospital detection and public education.
ai_sections.what: HR 7116 directs the CDC to create and maintain a dedicated sepsis team that would run public education campaigns, collect data on sepsis cases including in children, and develop standard measures for how well hospitals treat and prevent sepsis. The CDC director must report to Congress within one year of passage and provide annual updates on hospital performance. The bill also establishes a voluntary honor roll for hospitals that demonstrate strong or improved sepsis detection and treatment, with public standards for recognition.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects hospitals, healthcare workers, and federal agencies including the CDC and Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services. Patients at risk for sepsis, particularly children, could be indirectly affected by changes in hospital practices.
ai_sections.why: Sepsis kills about 350,000 Americans each year, creating ongoing pressure on hospitals and the medical staff who treat serious infections. Standardized measurement and public recognition programs could alter how hospitals prioritize sepsis detection and reporting.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $20 million per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund the CDC sepsis team, education campaigns, data collection, and the hospital honor roll program.
costChips: Covers CDC team, data, and honor roll

title: Abolish the ATF Act
ai_summary:
This bill would completely eliminate the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, known as the ATF. The ATF is a federal law enforcement agency under the Department of Justice that enforces federal laws related to guns, explosives, arson, and alcohol and tobacco trafficking. The bill's text is very short and simply states that the agency is abolished, without specifying what would happen to its responsibilities, employees, or ongoing investigations.

Because the bill does not include a transition plan, it is unclear who would take over the ATF's duties if this became law. Those duties currently include things like investigating illegal gun sales, tracking firearms used in crimes, regulating licensed gun dealers, and handling cases involving bombs or arson. Other agencies like the FBI or DEA might absorb some of those roles, but the bill does not address that.

This bill would most directly affect the roughly 5,000 ATF employees, federally licensed gun dealers who currently register and report with the ATF, and anyone involved in industries the agency regulates. More broadly, it could affect how federal gun laws are enforced across the entire country, since the ATF is currently the main agency responsible for that work.
ai_headline: House bill would abolish the ATF with no replacement plan
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 221 would abolish the ATF entirely, leaving no plan for who takes over gun, explosives, and arson enforcement.
ai_sections.what: HR 221 would abolish the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, the federal agency responsible for enforcing laws on guns, explosives, arson, and alcohol and tobacco trafficking. The bill's text is short and contains no transition plan, leaving open what would happen to the agency's responsibilities, employees, or ongoing investigations. Agencies such as the FBI or DEA might absorb some duties, but the bill does not address that.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 5,000 ATF employees would be most directly affected, along with federally licensed gun dealers who currently register and report with the ATF. Anyone involved in industries the ATF regulates would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Without a transition plan, it is unclear which agency would take over investigating illegal gun sales, tracking firearms used in crimes, regulating licensed gun dealers, and handling bomb and arson cases. Because the ATF is currently the main federal agency responsible for gun law enforcement, the bill could affect how those laws are enforced across the entire country.

title: NOAA Data Preservation Act
ai_summary:
This bill is about protecting data collected by NOAA, the federal agency that tracks weather, oceans, and the atmosphere. Right now, NOAA stores huge amounts of scientific data using cloud computing services, meaning outside companies host the data on remote servers. The bill says that the government cannot cancel any of those storage contracts unless it first has a solid plan to keep that data safe and accessible without any gaps. It also requires NOAA's own leadership to be involved in making sure nothing gets lost during any kind of transition.

The practical effect is that before the Department of Commerce could switch cloud providers or end a storage deal, it would have to show a clear step-by-step plan for moving the data somewhere else without interruption. The goal is to prevent a situation where NOAA's data becomes temporarily unavailable or permanently lost because a contract was ended before a backup arrangement was in place.

This bill would most directly affect NOAA itself, the companies that provide cloud storage to the government, and anyone who relies on NOAA's data. That includes weather forecasters, emergency managers, farmers, pilots, shipping companies, researchers, and the general public who depends on accurate weather warnings and climate information. Essentially, it is aimed at making sure that publicly collected scientific data stays protected and continuously available no matter what business or contract decisions are made.
ai_headline: Bill would block NOAA cloud contract cancellations without a data backup plan
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Natural Resources, and in addition to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6184 bars the government from ending NOAA cloud storage contracts until a gap-free data transition plan is in place.
ai_sections.what: HR 6184 prohibits the Department of Commerce from canceling NOAA's cloud storage contracts unless a complete, step-by-step plan exists to move the data without interruption. The bill also requires NOAA leadership to be directly involved in overseeing any such transition. The goal is to ensure that publicly collected scientific data remains continuously accessible regardless of contract or business decisions.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects NOAA, the federal agency that tracks weather, oceans, and the atmosphere, as well as the private companies that provide it with cloud storage services. Anyone who depends on NOAA's data is also affected, including weather forecasters, emergency managers, farmers, pilots, shipping companies, researchers, and the general public.
ai_sections.why: Without this requirement, a contract could be canceled before a backup arrangement is in place, leaving NOAA's data temporarily unavailable or permanently lost. The bill responds to that risk by making a verified transition plan a legal precondition for ending any storage agreement.

title: Designating the Guam and Western Pacific USDA Rural Development Office Act
ai_summary:
This bill officially names the Guam and Western Pacific USDA Rural Development office as the go-to federal office for rural development programs across a specific group of Pacific islands and nations. Those include Guam, the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, American Samoa, and three independent Pacific nations that have special agreements with the United States: the Marshall Islands, Palau, and the Federated States of Micronesia. The Department of Agriculture would have 90 days after the bill becomes law to make this designation official.

The bill also gives that office the same authority and responsibilities that any state-level USDA Rural Development office has. That means the office would be able to fully manage and distribute federal rural development programs in the region, such as loans and grants for housing, infrastructure, and businesses, without needing to go through another office for approval.

This primarily affects residents and communities in those Pacific territories and nations who rely on USDA Rural Development programs for things like affordable housing, water systems, and economic support. It also affects local government agencies and organizations in the region that apply for those federal funds. Essentially, it formalizes and strengthens the role of a local office that already exists, potentially making it easier and faster for Pacific island communities to access federal rural development resources.
ai_headline: Pacific islands office gains full state-level rural development authority
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6063 gives the Guam and Western Pacific USDA office full state-level authority to run rural development programs for six Pacific territories and nations.
ai_sections.what: HR 6063 officially designates the Guam and Western Pacific USDA Rural Development office as the lead federal office for rural development programs in Guam, the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, American Samoa, the Marshall Islands, Palau, and the Federated States of Micronesia. The bill grants that office the same authority and responsibilities as any state-level USDA Rural Development office, allowing it to fully manage and distribute loans and grants for housing, infrastructure, and businesses. USDA would have 90 days after enactment to make the designation official.
ai_sections.who: Residents and communities in those six Pacific territories and nations who rely on USDA Rural Development programs are directly affected. Local government agencies and organizations in the region that apply for federal funds are also affected.
ai_sections.why: The change formalizes and expands the authority of a local office that already exists, which could alter how quickly and directly Pacific island communities can access federal rural development resources. Removing the need for approval through another office changes the administrative path for programs such as affordable housing, water systems, and economic support.

title: Protection of Lawful Commerce in Stone Slab Products Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with lawsuits related to silica dust, a health hazard created when workers cut or grind stone products like quartz countertops. Right now, some workers who got sick from breathing silica dust on the job have sued the companies that made or sold the stone slabs. This bill would make those lawsuits illegal, both in federal and state courts. It would also force courts to throw out any of these cases that are already in progress when the law takes effect.

The bill's reasoning is that the manufacturers and sellers of the stone slabs did not do the cutting or drilling, so they should not be held responsible for dust created during that process. That work is done by separate companies called fabricators, who are already required by federal and state safety rules to protect their workers from silica dust. Congress argues that when fabricators ignore those safety rules and workers get hurt, the blame lies with the fabricator, not the company that originally made or sold the stone.

The people most directly affected are countertop fabrication workers, many of whom have developed a serious lung disease called silicosis from breathing silica dust at work. Under this bill, those workers could no longer sue stone slab manufacturers or sellers, even if they believe those companies share some responsibility. Stone slab manufacturers, importers, distributors, and retailers would gain legal protection from these specific types of claims. Workers would still potentially be able to pursue claims against their direct employer, the fabricator, or seek workers' compensation benefits.
ai_headline: Bill would bar silica dust lawsuits against stone slab makers and sellers
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 16 - 7.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5437 would ban silica dust injury lawsuits against stone slab manufacturers and sellers in all U.S. courts, including pending cases.
ai_sections.what: HR 5437 would make it illegal to sue stone slab manufacturers, importers, distributors, or retailers over silica dust injuries, in both federal and state courts. The bill would also require courts to dismiss any such cases already underway when the law takes effect. Workers could still pursue claims against their direct employer or seek workers' compensation benefits.
ai_sections.who: Countertop fabrication workers who have developed silicosis or other silica dust illnesses are the primary people losing legal options under this bill. Stone slab manufacturers, importers, distributors, and retailers would gain legal protection from this category of claims.
ai_sections.why: Workers who can no longer sue slab makers or sellers would have a narrower set of legal remedies, limited to claims against their direct employer or workers' compensation. The bill shifts legal responsibility entirely onto fabricators, the companies that cut and grind the stone, even in cases where workers believe manufacturers or sellers share some fault.

title: Justice Thurgood Marshall National Historic Site Establishment Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would make the elementary school that Thurgood Marshall attended in Baltimore, Maryland, an official affiliated area of the National Park System. The school, located at 1315 Division Street and now called the Justice Thurgood Marshall School, is already operating as a museum. The National Park Service studied the site in 2023 and determined it met the standards for national significance. Giving it this designation recognizes Marshall's historic importance, including his role in winning the Brown v. Board of Education case in 1954 and later becoming the first Black Justice on the U.S. Supreme Court.

The building would not be taken over by the federal government. It would stay owned and run by a local nonprofit called the Beloved Community Services Corporation. The National Park Service could offer technical help and money for things like preservation, educational programs, and marketing, but it would not be responsible for day-to-day operations or costs. The two organizations would sign an agreement spelling out each side's responsibilities.

This bill mainly affects residents of Baltimore and visitors to the city who might come to learn about Marshall's life and legacy. It also affects the nonprofit that runs the site, which would gain access to federal support and the prestige of a National Park System affiliation. Taxpayers could see some federal funds used for the site, though the exact amount is not specified in the bill.
ai_headline: Thurgood Marshall's childhood school in Baltimore seeks National Park affiliation
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1718 would affiliate Thurgood Marshall's Baltimore elementary school with the National Park System while keeping local nonprofit ownership.
ai_sections.what: HR 1718 would designate the Justice Thurgood Marshall School at 1315 Division Street in Baltimore, Maryland, as an affiliated area of the National Park System. The building already operates as a museum and was studied by the National Park Service in 2023, which found it met standards for national significance. Federal designation would not transfer ownership or daily operations to the government.
ai_sections.who: The site is owned and operated by a local nonprofit, the Beloved Community Services Corporation, which would retain control and gain access to federal technical and financial support. Baltimore residents and visitors interested in Marshall's legacy are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: The designation would formally recognize Marshall's historical importance, including his role in the 1954 Brown v. Board of Education case and his later appointment as the first Black Justice on the U.S. Supreme Court. The National Park Service could provide assistance for preservation, education, and marketing under a formal agreement with the nonprofit.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not specify an exact funding amount. Federal money could be directed toward preservation, educational programs, and marketing at the site.

title: Office of Management and Budget Inspector General Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new watchdog office specifically for the Office of Management and Budget, or OMB. The OMB is a powerful federal agency that oversees the federal budget, manages government spending, and coordinates policies across executive branch agencies. Right now, the OMB is one of the few major federal agencies without its own Inspector General, which is the type of independent official responsible for investigating waste, fraud, and abuse. This bill would add OMB to the list of agencies required to have one, and the President would have 120 days after the bill becomes law to appoint someone to the position.

The new Inspector General would be limited to investigating only the matters specifically assigned to OMB by law, meaning they would not have broad authority to look into other parts of the government. Like other Inspectors General across the federal government, this official would conduct audits and investigations to check whether OMB is operating properly and following the rules.

This bill would most directly affect federal employees and officials at OMB, as well as the broader government agencies whose budgets and policies OMB manages. For everyday Americans, the practical effect would be having an additional layer of oversight over the office that controls how trillions of dollars in federal spending are planned and managed each year.
ai_headline: Bill would give budget watchdog office its first independent inspector general
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2221 would require OMB, which oversees federal spending, to have its own Inspector General for the first time.
ai_sections.what: HR 2221 would create an Inspector General specifically for the Office of Management and Budget, one of the few major federal agencies currently without one. The Inspector General would conduct audits and investigations limited to matters legally assigned to OMB. The President would have 120 days after enactment to appoint someone to the position.
ai_sections.who: Federal employees and officials at OMB would be most directly affected. Government agencies whose budgets and policies OMB manages would also fall within the scope of this added oversight.
ai_sections.why: OMB oversees the planning and management of trillions of dollars in federal spending each year, and this bill would add an independent oversight layer to that office. The new official's authority would be limited to OMB's legally assigned responsibilities and would not extend to broader parts of the government.

title: Country of Origin Labeling Enforcement Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill is about beef labels at grocery stores and retailers. Right now, beef raised in another country can sometimes be labeled "Product of USA" if it was processed here in the United States. This bill would change the law to stop that practice. Stores would only be allowed to label beef as a U.S. product if the cattle were actually born, raised, and slaughtered in the United States. It also formally adds beef to the list of foods that must clearly show their country of origin on the label.

The bill also increases the penalty for breaking these rules. Currently, violations can result in a fine of $1,000 per incident. Under this bill, if a retailer mislabels beef, the fine would jump to $5,000 for every pound of beef that was incorrectly labeled. That is a much steeper punishment meant to push stores to take the labeling rules seriously. The bill also says that rulings from the World Trade Organization or other international groups cannot be used to override these labeling requirements.

This would affect grocery stores, butcher shops, and other retailers that sell beef directly to consumers. It would also affect beef importers and packers, since they would need to keep clearer records of where cattle come from. American cattle ranchers could be impacted too, since clearer labels might influence which beef shoppers choose to buy. Everyday consumers would see more specific information about where their beef actually comes from.
ai_headline: Bill would require beef sold in U.S. to be born, raised, and slaughtered here
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5818 would bar "Product of USA" beef labels unless cattle were born, raised, and slaughtered in the United States.
ai_sections.what: HR 5818 would change federal law so that beef can only be labeled "Product of USA" if the cattle were born, raised, and slaughtered in the United States. Beef processed here from foreign-raised cattle could no longer carry that label. The bill also formally adds beef to the list of foods required to show country of origin on the label.
ai_sections.who: Grocery stores, butcher shops, and other retailers that sell beef directly to consumers would need to follow the new labeling rules. Beef importers, packers, and American cattle ranchers would also be affected, as clearer origin records would be required throughout the supply chain.
ai_sections.why: Stricter labeling requirements could change which beef consumers choose to buy based on country of origin. Rulings from the World Trade Organization or other international bodies could not be used to override these requirements under the bill.
ai_sections.cost: The current fine for a labeling violation is $1,000 per incident. Under this bill, the penalty would increase to $5,000 for every pound of beef that is incorrectly labeled.

title: SNAP BACK Act.
ai_summary:
This bill is a response to situations where the federal government has a funding gap or government shutdown that could interrupt food assistance benefits. It would require that SNAP (food stamps) and WIC benefits keep flowing to eligible people no matter what, even if Congress has not yet passed a budget or spending bill for that year. It automatically sets aside whatever money is needed to keep both programs running without interruption.

The bill also puts strict rules on government officials. No one in the executive branch, including budget offices or the Agriculture Department, could hold back, delay, or freeze these funds. States and tribal governments would have to keep accepting and processing applications from people who qualify, and approved applicants would have to receive their benefits within five days. EBT cards, which are used to access both SNAP and WIC benefits, could not be shut down or restricted during any funding delay.

This bill directly affects the roughly 40 million Americans who rely on SNAP benefits and the approximately 6 to 7 million people who use WIC, including low-income pregnant women, new mothers, infants, and young children. It also affects state governments and federally recognized tribes that run these programs, and it includes a promise that the federal government will pay back any state or tribe that spends its own emergency money to keep benefits going during a federal funding lapse.
ai_headline: Bill would keep SNAP and WIC benefits flowing during government shutdowns
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5860 would automatically fund SNAP and WIC during federal funding gaps, barring officials from freezing benefits for ~47M recipients.
ai_sections.what: HR 5860 would require SNAP and WIC benefits to continue uninterrupted during federal funding gaps or government shutdowns, automatically setting aside whatever money is needed to keep both programs running. No executive branch official, including those at budget offices or the Agriculture Department, could hold back, delay, or freeze these funds. States and tribal governments would be required to keep processing applications, and approved applicants must receive benefits within five days.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the roughly 40 million Americans who rely on SNAP and the approximately 6 to 7 million people who use WIC, including low-income pregnant women, new mothers, infants, and young children. It also applies to state governments and federally recognized tribes that administer these programs.
ai_sections.why: Without this legislation, federal funding gaps can interrupt the flow of SNAP and WIC benefits, leaving tens of millions of low-income people without food assistance. The bill also creates an obligation for the federal government to reimburse any state or tribe that spends its own emergency funds to keep benefits going during a federal funding lapse.
ai_sections.cost: The bill automatically sets aside whatever federal funds are necessary to keep SNAP and WIC running during a funding gap, with no fixed cap stated. The federal government is also required to reimburse states or tribes that cover benefit costs out of their own funds during a lapse.

title: SAFE KIDS Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make surrogacy contracts legally invalid and unenforceable when the intended parent is a citizen or permanent resident of a country the U.S. government has designated as a "foreign entity of concern." That list currently includes countries like China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. In plain terms, if someone from one of those countries tries to hire an American woman to carry and give birth to their child, that contract would have no legal standing in the United States. There is one exception: if the intended parents are a married couple and at least one spouse is a U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident, the contract can still be valid.

The bill also creates a criminal penalty for surrogacy brokers, meaning the agencies or individuals who arrange these agreements. If a broker knowingly or recklessly helps set up one of these now-banned contracts, they could face a fine, up to one year in prison, or both. If a child is born under one of these voided contracts anyway, a family court in the surrogate mother's state would decide custody based on the best interests of the child, ignoring the original contract entirely.

This bill would most directly affect American women who work as surrogates, surrogacy agencies operating in the U.S., and citizens of the listed foreign countries who currently use U.S. surrogacy services. It would not affect surrogacy arrangements where both intended parents are American, or where a married couple includes at least one American. The bill has been referred to the House Judiciary Committee and has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Bill would void surrogacy contracts with citizens of China, Russia, Iran, North Korea
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR H1161-1162)

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7040 would make surrogacy contracts unenforceable if the intended parent is from a U.S.-designated foreign entity of concern.
ai_sections.what: HR 7040 would make surrogacy contracts legally invalid and unenforceable when the intended parent is a citizen or permanent resident of a country the U.S. government has designated a foreign entity of concern, a list that currently includes China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. An exception applies when the intended parents are a married couple and at least one spouse is a U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident. If a child is born under a voided contract, a family court in the surrogate's state would decide custody based on the best interests of the child, disregarding the original contract.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects American women who work as surrogates, surrogacy agencies and brokers operating in the United States, and citizens of the listed countries who currently use U.S. surrogacy services. It would not affect arrangements where both intended parents are American or where a married couple includes at least one American.
ai_sections.why: Surrogacy brokers who knowingly or recklessly arrange one of these now-banned contracts could face criminal penalties, removing a category of business activity currently available to U.S.-based agencies. Custody of any child born under a voided contract would shift from the terms of the original agreement to a court determination, creating legal uncertainty for all parties involved.
ai_sections.cost: Brokers who violate the law could face a fine, up to one year in prison, or both.
costChips: Brokers face fines if in violation
Up to 1 year in prison possible

title: Watershed Protection and Forest Recovery Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill gives the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Forest Service new legal authority to respond quickly to emergencies on National Forest land. Right now, the Forest Service's emergency watershed protection powers mainly apply to private and other non-federal land. This bill extends those powers to cover National Forests. When a natural disaster like a wildfire, flood, or landslide damages a forest and creates risks like erosion, flooding, or contaminated water supplies, the Forest Service could pay local partners to go in and do repair work fast, such as stabilizing soil, reducing runoff, and protecting water sources downstream.

The bill sets up a partnership system where state and local governments, tribal governments, water districts, and similar organizations can sign agreements with the Forest Service to carry out that emergency work. The federal government covers the full cost with no matching funds required from the local partner. Work must be finished within two years of the disaster, with up to three more years allowed for monitoring and maintenance. Partners are protected from most legal liability as long as they are not acting recklessly or negligently.

This bill would most directly affect communities that rely on water from National Forests, including cities and towns that get their drinking water from forest watersheds. It would also affect tribal nations, water utilities, and local governments near National Forests, particularly in the West where wildfires frequently damage forest land and threaten water supplies. The Forest Service itself would gain a faster, clearer process for responding to forest disasters without getting slowed down by the usual environmental review procedures.
ai_headline: Forest Service gains emergency repair powers on National Forest land
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5823 lets the Forest Service fund fast disaster repairs on National Forests through local partners at full federal cost.
ai_sections.what: HR 5823 extends the Forest Service's emergency watershed protection authority to cover National Forest land, which was previously excluded. After disasters such as wildfires, floods, or landslides, the agency can pay local partners to stabilize soil, reduce runoff, and protect downstream water sources. Work must be completed within two years of the disaster, with up to three additional years allowed for monitoring and maintenance.
ai_sections.who: Communities that draw drinking water from forest watersheds are most directly affected, including cities, towns, tribal nations, water utilities, and local governments near National Forests, particularly in the West. State and local governments, tribal governments, and water districts are eligible to sign partnership agreements with the Forest Service to carry out the repair work.
ai_sections.why: Without this authority, the Forest Service lacked a fast, clear process for funding emergency repairs on its own land after natural disasters. The bill also bypasses standard environmental review procedures, which the agency has cited as a source of delays in disaster response.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government covers the full cost of emergency work with no matching funds required from local partners.

title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to require that the Supreme Court of the United States be composed of nine justices.
ai_summary:
This bill proposes a change to the U.S. Constitution that would permanently lock the size of the Supreme Court at nine justices — one Chief Justice and eight Associate Justices. Right now, the number of Supreme Court justices is set by regular law passed by Congress, not by the Constitution itself. This means Congress could technically change the number up or down with a simple majority vote. This bill would make nine the permanent, unchangeable number by writing it into the Constitution itself.

Because this is a constitutional amendment, it is a very difficult process to complete. It would need two-thirds approval from both the House and Senate, and then three-fourths of all state legislatures — that is 38 out of 50 states — would have to ratify it within seven years for it to become law.

This bill affects all Americans since the Supreme Court makes final decisions on major legal questions that touch everyday life. It also directly affects Congress and future presidents, who would no longer have the power to add or remove justices. Supporters of fixing the number at nine argue it brings stability and prevents political manipulation of the Court. Those opposed might argue it removes flexibility that future generations could need. The bill was introduced in January 2025 and sent to the House Judiciary Committee, where it would need to advance before any further votes could take place.
ai_headline: Proposed amendment would fix Supreme Court at nine justices permanently
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported by the Yeas and Nays: 15 - 8.

ai_sections.tldr: HJRES 1 would write nine Supreme Court justices into the Constitution, requiring 38 states to ratify it within seven years.
ai_sections.what: HJRES 1 proposes a constitutional amendment that would permanently set the number of Supreme Court justices at nine — one Chief Justice and eight Associate Justices. Currently, Congress can change that number through regular legislation. Writing it into the Constitution would make nine the fixed, unchangeable number.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects all Americans, since the Supreme Court issues final rulings on major legal questions that touch everyday life. It also directly affects Congress and future presidents, who would lose the authority to add or remove justices.
ai_sections.why: Fixing the number at nine would remove Congress's and the president's ability to alter the Court's size for any reason in the future. Those who oppose the measure argue that permanently locking the number eliminates flexibility that future generations might need.

title: REAP Modernization Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill updates a federal program called the Rural Energy for America Program, or REAP, which helps farmers and rural small businesses pay for renewable energy systems like solar panels and wind turbines, as well as energy efficiency improvements. The bill raises the maximum grant amount for energy audits and assessments from $20,000 to $50,000. It also makes the application process simpler by creating a streamlined system, and it expands who can apply to include farming cooperatives and nonprofit organizations. The bill also sets aside at least 15 percent of program funds each year as a reserve to support less common or underused renewable energy technologies.

The bill adds greenhouse gas reduction as an official factor the government must consider when deciding which projects to fund. It also requires the Department of Agriculture to study "dual-use" energy systems, where farmers generate renewable energy and grow crops or raise livestock on the same land at the same time. A report on that study must be delivered to Congress within two years.

The people most directly affected are farmers, ranchers, agricultural cooperatives, and small businesses in rural areas across the country. They would have easier access to federal money for energy projects, more help navigating the application process, and potentially more funding options if this bill becomes law.
ai_headline: Rural energy grant cap rises to $50,000 under updated farm bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6287 raises REAP energy audit grants from $20K to $50K and expands eligibility to cooperatives and nonprofits.
ai_sections.what: HR 6287 updates the Rural Energy for America Program by raising the maximum grant for energy audits and assessments from $20,000 to $50,000. It simplifies the application process, reserves at least 15 percent of annual program funds for underused renewable energy technologies, and adds greenhouse gas reduction as an official funding consideration. The bill also directs the Department of Agriculture to study dual-use systems where farmers generate renewable energy and grow crops or raise livestock on the same land, with a report due to Congress within two years.
ai_sections.who: Farmers, ranchers, agricultural cooperatives, nonprofit organizations, and small businesses in rural areas are eligible to apply under the expanded program. Farming cooperatives and nonprofit organizations are newly added as eligible applicants under this bill.
ai_sections.why: Expanding eligibility and raising grant amounts would change how many applicants can access federal energy funding and at what level. The dual-use study and greenhouse gas requirement would introduce new criteria and research obligations into how the Department of Agriculture evaluates and reports on rural energy projects.
ai_sections.cost: At least 15 percent of annual REAP program funds must be set aside as a reserve for less common or underused renewable energy technologies.
costChips: 15% of annual funds reserved for rare tech

title: AADAPT Act
ai_summary:
This bill renews and expands a federal grant program called Project ECHO, which uses video technology to connect specialists with primary care doctors in underserved areas so those doctors can learn to handle complex medical conditions. The AADAPT Act keeps the existing program running and adds a new focus area: Alzheimer's disease and other forms of dementia. Starting within one year of passage, the government would award grants to organizations that can train primary care providers to better recognize and diagnose dementia early and deliver higher-quality care to patients.

The bill targets primary care doctors, nurses, and other licensed health professionals who work in rural areas, areas with doctor shortages, or who serve low-income and Native American communities. These providers often have limited access to dementia specialists, so the idea is to use online training tools to bring specialist knowledge to them without requiring patients or doctors to travel. Grant recipients would have to track whether the training actually improves patient outcomes and would need to report their findings to a federal advisory council on Alzheimer's research.

On the funding side, the bill authorizes up to 10 million dollars per year through 2032 for the broader Project ECHO program, and up to 1 million dollars per year from 2027 through 2032 specifically for the new dementia training grants. People most directly affected include primary care providers in underserved communities, and indirectly, their patients who have or may develop Alzheimer's disease or related dementias.
ai_headline: Federal dementia training grants would reach rural and underserved doctors
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported by the Yeas and Nays: 48 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3747 adds dementia-focused grants to Project ECHO, targeting primary care providers in rural and shortage areas, funded at up to $1M/year through 2032.
ai_sections.what: HR 3747 renews Project ECHO, a federal grant program that uses video technology to connect specialists with primary care providers in underserved areas. The bill adds a new focus on Alzheimer's disease and related dementias, requiring grants to be awarded within one year of passage to organizations that train primary care providers in early dementia recognition, diagnosis, and care quality. Grant recipients must track patient outcomes and report findings to a federal advisory council on Alzheimer's research.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects primary care doctors, nurses, and other licensed health professionals working in rural areas, doctor-shortage areas, or serving low-income and Native American communities. Patients who have or may develop Alzheimer's disease or related dementias in those communities are indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Primary care providers in these areas often lack access to dementia specialists, and the bill uses online training to deliver specialist knowledge without requiring travel by doctors or patients. Grant recipients are required to measure whether training improves patient outcomes, creating an accountability mechanism tied to federal advisory reporting.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $10 million per year for the broader Project ECHO program and up to $1 million per year from 2027 through 2032 for the new dementia-specific training grants.

title: PRECISE Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the PRECISE Act, is designed to make it easier and more affordable for farmers to adopt precision agriculture technology. Precision agriculture means using advanced tools like GPS guidance systems, soil sensors, satellite imaging, drones, and data software to manage crops and livestock more efficiently. The bill officially defines these terms in federal law and adds precision agriculture as an eligible use under existing farm loan and conservation programs. Farmers could use government-backed loans or loan guarantees to buy this kind of technology, and the government would be required to notify farmers participating in conservation programs that these loans are available to them.

The bill also increases financial support available to farmers who use precision agriculture for conservation purposes. Under an existing program called the Environmental Quality Incentives Program, the government can currently cover a portion of a farmer's conservation-related costs. This bill would allow that coverage to go up to 90 percent of costs when a farmer is specifically adopting precision agriculture practices. It also adds precision agriculture as a recognized activity under the Conservation Stewardship Program, which rewards farmers for maintaining and improving conservation efforts on their land.

The people most directly affected are farmers and ranchers, particularly those who want to reduce input costs like water, fertilizer, and chemicals but have struggled to afford the technology to do so. Rural businesses that help finance or support farming operations could also benefit. The bill does not create a new standalone program but instead adds precision agriculture into several programs that already exist, expanding what those programs cover.
ai_headline: PRECISE Act would open federal loans and cost-share funds to precision farm tech
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

ai_sections.tldr: The PRECISE Act adds precision agriculture to existing federal loan and conservation programs, with cost-share coverage rising to 90% for qualifying farmers.
ai_sections.what: The PRECISE Act defines precision agriculture in federal law and makes it an eligible use under existing farm loan and conservation programs. Farmers could use government-backed loans or loan guarantees to purchase GPS systems, soil sensors, drones, satellite imaging, and data software. The bill does not create a new standalone program but expands coverage within programs that already exist.
ai_sections.who: Farmers and ranchers who want to reduce costs for water, fertilizer, and chemicals but have had difficulty affording precision agriculture technology are the primary group affected. Rural businesses that help finance or support farming operations could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Adding precision agriculture to the Environmental Quality Incentives Program and the Conservation Stewardship Program changes what costs and activities those programs will cover. The government would also be required to notify farmers in conservation programs that precision agriculture loans are available to them.
ai_sections.cost: Under the Environmental Quality Incentives Program, the government can currently cover a portion of a farmer's conservation-related costs. This bill would allow that coverage to reach up to 90 percent of costs when a farmer is specifically adopting precision agriculture practices.

title: American Teacher Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal grant program to help states raise teacher pay. Starting in the 2026-2027 school year, the goal is to ensure that every full-time public school teacher earns at least $60,000 per year, with that minimum going up each year to keep pace with inflation. States would apply to the U.S. Department of Education for four-year grants, and they would have to show a plan for keeping salaries at that level even after the federal money runs out. At least 85 percent of grant funds would have to flow directly to local school districts. The bill also sets aside a small portion of funding for a national campaign to encourage more people, especially college students, to consider becoming teachers.

The bill focuses extra attention on school districts that serve high numbers of low-income students or are located in rural areas, giving those districts priority when grant money is handed out. States that already meet the $60,000 threshold but are struggling to keep up with rising costs could also apply for a separate cost-of-living adjustment grant. Importantly, the federal money is meant to add to existing teacher pay, not replace what states and districts are already spending.

This bill would primarily affect public school teachers across the country, especially those currently earning less than $60,000 a year. It would also affect state education agencies, which would be responsible for applying for the grants and distributing the money. School districts in low-income and rural communities stand to benefit the most. The bill does not apply to private school teachers.
ai_headline: Federal grants would set $60,000 floor for public school teacher pay
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2021 would fund state grants to bring all public school teachers to at least $60,000/yr starting in 2026-27.
ai_sections.what: HR 2021 would create a federal grant program through the U.S. Department of Education to help states raise public school teacher salaries to a minimum of $60,000 per year, beginning in the 2026-2027 school year. That minimum would increase annually to keep pace with inflation. States that already meet the $60,000 threshold but face rising costs could apply for a separate cost-of-living adjustment grant.
ai_sections.who: The bill would primarily affect full-time public school teachers currently earning less than $60,000 a year, along with state education agencies responsible for applying and distributing funds. Private school teachers are not covered. School districts serving high numbers of low-income students or located in rural areas would receive priority in grant distribution.
ai_sections.why: States would be required to demonstrate a plan for sustaining the salary levels after federal funding ends, placing a long-term financial obligation on state and local governments. A portion of funding directed toward a national recruitment campaign could shift the composition of the future teacher workforce.
ai_sections.cost: At least 85 percent of grant funds must flow directly to local school districts, limiting how much states can retain for administration. Federal funds are intended to supplement, not replace, existing teacher pay commitments from states and districts.

title: FAIR Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require immigration detention centers to give detainees access to "constituent services," which is the help that members of Congress provide to people in their home districts. Within 24 hours of arriving at a facility, detainees would have to receive a handbook explaining their rights and how to contact their congressional representative. Within 90 days of the law taking effect, detainees could request privacy release forms that allow a Congress member's office to look into their case. Detention centers would also have to provide computers, email, printing, and paper so detainees can stay in contact with congressional offices or others helping them. All of these materials and services must be available in the detainee's language, or an interpreter must be provided.

The bill affects two main groups. First, it directly affects people being held in immigration detention centers across the country, giving them a formal way to reach out to their elected representatives. Second, it places new requirements on the detention centers themselves, which must follow the new rules within 90 days and could face lawsuits if they do not comply.

If a detention center fails to provide these services, detainees or people helping them would have two options: file a complaint through the facility's own grievance process, or sue the responsible agency in federal court. The Department of Homeland Security would have 180 days to write the rules for how detention centers carry out the law, and 270 days to notify all detention facilities of those rules.
ai_headline: Detained immigrants would gain formal access to congressional help under HR 6149
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6149 requires detention centers to give detainees tools and information to contact their congressional representative within 24 hours of arrival.
ai_sections.what: HR 6149 requires immigration detention centers to provide detainees with a handbook within 24 hours of arrival explaining their rights and how to contact their congressional representative. Within 90 days of the law taking effect, detainees may request privacy release forms allowing a congressional office to review their case. Facilities must also supply computers, email, printing, and paper for detainees to communicate with congressional offices or others assisting them, and all materials must be in the detainee's language or an interpreter must be provided.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects people held in immigration detention centers nationwide, giving them a formal channel to reach elected representatives. It also places new compliance requirements on detention facilities themselves.
ai_sections.why: Detention centers that fail to comply would face complaints through their own grievance processes or lawsuits filed in federal court. The Department of Homeland Security would have 180 days to issue implementing rules and 270 days to notify all facilities of those rules.

title: Time Off to Vote Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require employers with 25 or more workers to give employees at least two consecutive paid hours off to vote in federal elections. That includes Election Day voting, early voting, dropping off a mail ballot in person, or other voting-related activities. The two hours must be paid, and employers cannot count lunch breaks or other regular break time toward that two hours. Employers can decide when during the workday the employee takes those two hours, but they cannot deny the time off altogether.

Employees who use this leave cannot lose any benefits they have already earned, and employers are not allowed to punish, demote, fire, or otherwise retaliate against a worker for taking the leave or for reporting a violation. The Department of Labor would be responsible for investigating complaints, and employers who break the rules could face fines of up to $10,000 per violation. Smaller businesses may receive lower fines based on their size and circumstances.

This bill would affect most private-sector employees across the country, since it covers any business in interstate commerce with 25 or more employees. It does not override state or local laws that already give workers more generous voting leave. It would not take effect until the first federal election after the bill is signed into law. Currently, voting leave laws vary widely by state, with some states already requiring paid time off and others requiring none at all.
ai_headline: Employers with 25-plus workers would owe 2 paid hours off to vote
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4908 requires employers with 25+ workers to give 2 consecutive paid hours off for federal election voting activities.
ai_sections.what: HR 4908 would require employers with 25 or more workers to provide at least two consecutive paid hours off for employees to participate in federal election voting activities. This covers Election Day voting, early voting, dropping off a mail ballot in person, and other voting-related activities. Regular break times and lunch breaks cannot be counted toward the required two hours.
ai_sections.who: The bill covers most private-sector employees at businesses engaged in interstate commerce with 25 or more employees. Employers can set when during the workday the leave is taken but cannot deny it entirely.
ai_sections.why: Voting leave requirements currently vary widely by state, with some states mandating paid time off and others requiring none, meaning many workers have no guaranteed access to paid voting leave. This bill would establish a single federal floor for employers of a certain size, which could affect scheduling and payroll practices nationwide.
ai_sections.cost: Employers who violate the rules could face fines of up to $10,000 per violation, though smaller businesses may receive lower fines based on their size and circumstances. The Department of Labor would be responsible for investigating complaints.

title: Medicare Home Health Accessibility Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes one specific rule in the Medicare program. Right now, Medicare will cover home health services if a patient needs skilled nursing care, physical therapy, or speech therapy — but occupational therapy alone is not enough to qualify someone for that coverage. This bill would add occupational therapy to that list, so a Medicare patient who only needs occupational therapy could now qualify to receive home health services covered by Medicare. The change would take effect starting January 1, 2026.

Occupational therapy helps people with everyday tasks like dressing, cooking, bathing, and moving safely around their home, often after an injury, illness, or surgery. Currently, a patient might genuinely need an occupational therapist to come to their home but cannot get Medicare to cover it unless they also happen to need one of the other qualifying services. This bill would remove that gap.

The people most directly affected are Medicare beneficiaries — generally adults 65 and older, as well as some younger people with disabilities — who need occupational therapy at home but do not currently meet Medicare's other qualifying requirements. It would also affect home health agencies and occupational therapists who serve Medicare patients, since more patients could become eligible for their services.
ai_headline: Medicare home health gap for occupational therapy would close under HR 2013
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2013 lets Medicare patients qualify for home health coverage through occupational therapy alone, starting January 1, 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 2013 adds occupational therapy to the list of services that can qualify a Medicare patient for covered home health services. Under current law, only skilled nursing care, physical therapy, or speech therapy can establish that eligibility. The change would take effect January 1, 2026.
ai_sections.who: Medicare beneficiaries — generally adults 65 and older, plus some younger people with disabilities — who need occupational therapy at home but do not qualify under the current rules are the primary group affected. Home health agencies and occupational therapists serving Medicare patients would also be affected, as more patients could become eligible for their services.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, a patient who needs only occupational therapy at home cannot receive Medicare coverage for it unless they also need one of the other qualifying services, creating a gap in coverage. Removing that restriction would expand the pool of Medicare patients who qualify for home health services.

title: Pregnancy.Gov Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the federal Department of Health and Human Services to build and run a new government website called pregnancy.gov within one year of the law passing. The site would let users enter their ZIP code to find nearby services for pregnant women, new mothers, and women raising young children. Those services could include medical care, financial help, housing, childcare, adoption resources, mental health support, and more. The site would also be available in multiple languages and would allow users to share their contact information to receive follow-up outreach about additional resources.

The bill sets rules about which organizations can and cannot be listed on the site. Any group that performs, refers for, or counsels in favor of abortion is specifically banned from appearing on the site or receiving grant money connected to this program. States would be invited to recommend local resources for the site, and up to $50 million in federal grants would be available between 2026 and 2030 to help states build systems that gather and organize those resources. The bill also defines the types of information the site must include, such as information about abortion risks, alternatives to abortion, and child development from conception.

This bill would most directly affect pregnant women and new mothers looking for local support services, as well as the organizations that provide those services. Healthcare providers, social service agencies, and state governments would also be involved in recommending and organizing the resources listed on the site. Organizations that provide abortion services or referrals would be excluded from the program entirely.
ai_headline: Federal bill orders pregnancy.gov site built within one year
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3287 would create pregnancy.gov to connect users by ZIP code to local services, barring any organization that performs or refers abortions.
ai_sections.what: HR 3287 would require the Department of Health and Human Services to launch pregnancy.gov within one year of enactment. Visitors could enter a ZIP code to find nearby services including medical care, financial help, housing, childcare, adoption resources, and mental health support. The site would be available in multiple languages and allow users to share contact information for follow-up outreach.
