ai_sections.who: Pregnant women, new mothers, and women raising young children are the primary intended users. Healthcare providers, social service agencies, and state governments would participate in recommending and organizing listed resources.
ai_sections.why: Organizations that perform, refer for, or counsel in favor of abortion would be excluded from the site and from any grant funding connected to the program. The site would be required to include information about abortion risks, alternatives to abortion, and child development from conception.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $50 million in federal grants would be available from 2026 through 2030 to help states build systems that gather and organize local resources for the site.

title: Gynecologic Pain Management Study Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of Health and Human Services to study why patients sometimes cannot get proper pain relief during gynecologic procedures, such as IUD insertions, biopsies, or other in-office treatments. The department would look at existing research and possibly conduct new research to figure out what is getting in the way, whether that is insurance not covering certain pain management options, doctors not being trained to offer them, or a lack of equipment and resources.

The study must include input from a wide range of groups, including patients, doctors, medical societies, public health experts, and device manufacturers. Within two years of the bill becoming law, the department would send a report to Congress laying out what it found and suggesting possible actions, either by federal agencies or by Congress itself.

This bill primarily affects women and people who undergo gynecologic procedures, as well as the doctors and clinics that perform them. It does not change any laws about how procedures must be done or require insurers to cover anything right now. It is a research and reporting measure, meaning its direct result is a government study and a set of recommendations, not immediate policy changes.
ai_headline: Federal study ordered on pain relief gaps in gynecologic care
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8159 directs HHS to study pain management barriers in gynecologic procedures and report findings to Congress within two years.
ai_sections.what: HR 8159 directs the Department of Health and Human Services to study why patients often cannot access adequate pain relief during gynecologic procedures such as IUD insertions and biopsies. The department will review existing research and may conduct new research to identify barriers, which may include insurance coverage gaps, lack of physician training, or insufficient equipment. Within two years of enactment, HHS must deliver a report to Congress that includes findings and possible recommended actions.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects women and people who undergo gynecologic procedures, as well as the doctors and clinics that perform them. Input must be gathered from a broad range of stakeholders, including patients, physicians, medical societies, public health experts, and device manufacturers.
ai_sections.why: The bill does not change any existing laws governing how procedures must be performed, nor does it require insurers to cover any pain management options at this time. Its direct outcome is a government study and a set of recommendations, leaving any future policy changes to subsequent action by federal agencies or Congress.

title: Workforce of the Future Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill does three main things. First, it requires the Secretaries of Labor, Commerce, and Education to work together and produce reports on how artificial intelligence is changing jobs in America. Those reports would identify which industries and workers are most at risk of being displaced by AI, which groups could benefit the most, and what skills people will need going forward. The reports must be completed within one to four years of the bill becoming law.

Second, the bill would create two grant programs. The Department of Education would receive up to $160 million to give grants to schools, community colleges, and other education organizations to expand tech and computer science education from elementary school through college. Half the money would go toward building tech education programs, and the other half would go toward training and recruiting teachers to teach those subjects. The Department of Labor would receive up to $90 million to give grants that help workers who are already employed in AI-affected industries learn new skills, as well as workers who recently lost their jobs because of AI.

The people most affected by this bill would be K-12 students, especially those in low-income, rural, or minority communities who currently have less access to technology education. Workers in industries where AI is growing fast, like manufacturing and service sectors, would also be a focus. Teachers, community colleges, technical schools, tribal colleges, and minority-serving institutions would be eligible to apply for grant funding. The bill was introduced in December 2025 and has not yet been passed into law.
ai_headline: HR 6621 would fund AI job studies and tech education grants for schools and workers
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6621 proposes $250M in grants for tech education and worker retraining as AI reshapes the job market.
ai_sections.what: HR 6621 requires federal agencies to study how artificial intelligence is changing jobs, then funds education and retraining programs based on those findings. The Department of Education would run a grant program for schools and colleges to expand tech and computer science education from kindergarten through college. The Department of Labor would run a separate grant program to help currently employed and recently displaced workers in AI-affected industries learn new skills.
ai_sections.who: K-12 students in low-income, rural, and minority communities who have less access to technology education are a primary focus, along with workers in industries such as manufacturing and service sectors where AI is growing fast. Teachers, community colleges, technical schools, tribal colleges, and minority-serving institutions would be eligible to apply for grant funding.
ai_sections.why: The bill responds to concerns that AI is displacing workers and creating uneven access to the skills needed in a changing economy. Without action, the reports and grant programs it proposes would not exist, leaving gaps in both data and workforce preparation.
ai_sections.cost: The Department of Education would receive up to $160 million for grants, split evenly between building tech education programs and training and recruiting teachers. The Department of Labor would receive up to $90 million for worker skills grants.
costChips: Half of education funds for teacher training

title: Community Connect Grant Program Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill reauthorizes and updates a federal grant program that helps bring broadband internet to rural communities. The Community Connect Grant Program gives money to organizations that want to build internet infrastructure in rural areas that currently have little or no high-speed internet access. The bill extends the program through 2030, keeping the funding available for several more years.

The bill also raises the speed standards for what counts as "acceptable" internet service. Under the old rules, an area only qualified for grants if its internet was slower than 10 Mbps download and 1 Mbps upload. The new bill raises those thresholds to 25 Mbps download and 3 Mbps upload, meaning more areas could qualify for grant money because those older speeds are now considered too slow. It also adds a rule that if another broadband funding program has already made a binding commitment to bring internet to an area, that area would not be eligible for these grants too, helping avoid duplicate spending.

The people most affected are residents of rural communities across the United States who currently lack reliable high-speed internet. Local governments, nonprofits, and utilities in those areas that apply for grant funding would also be directly impacted. Better rural broadband can affect access to remote work, online education, telehealth services, and other tools that depend on a solid internet connection.
ai_headline: Rural broadband grant program extended to 2030 with updated speed rules
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6070 extends the Community Connect Grant Program to 2030 and raises minimum qualifying speeds to 25/3 Mbps.
ai_sections.what: HR 6070 reauthorizes the Community Connect Grant Program, which funds broadband infrastructure in rural areas with little or no high-speed internet, through 2030. The bill raises the speed thresholds that determine grant eligibility from 10 Mbps download and 1 Mbps upload to 25 Mbps download and 3 Mbps upload. It also bars areas from receiving these grants if another broadband funding program has already made a binding commitment to serve them.
ai_sections.who: Rural residents across the United States who currently lack reliable high-speed internet are the primary people affected. Local governments, nonprofits, and utilities in rural areas that apply for grant funding are also directly impacted.
ai_sections.why: Raising the speed thresholds means areas with connections previously considered adequate may now qualify for grant money, potentially expanding the pool of eligible communities. The restriction on duplicate funding is intended to prevent overlap with other federal broadband programs already committed to specific areas.
ai_sections.cost: The bill reauthorizes an existing federal grant program that provides funding to build broadband infrastructure in rural areas, though no specific funding amounts are stated in the summary.
costChips: Federal grants fund rural broadband builds

title: Capital for Beginning Farmers and Ranchers Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new pilot loan program through the U.S. Department of Agriculture specifically for beginning farmers and ranchers. Right now, new farmers often have to pay for long-term startup costs, like building soil health, buying basic equipment, or setting up business systems, using short-term annual loans. That mismatch makes it hard to get established. This bill would allow the USDA to offer special "development loans" of up to $100,000 with low interest rates of 0 to 3 percent and repayment terms of 3 to 10 years, which better match how long those early investments actually take to pay off.

The loans could be used for things like purchasing starter equipment, establishing perennial crops or breeding animals, building a customer base, setting up bookkeeping or payroll systems, and meeting food safety or environmental rules. The collateral requirements are flexible, meaning lenders can ask for less security if the borrower has relevant farming experience. Along with the loan, borrowers would also receive training and support on topics like cash flow, taxes, business management, and regulatory compliance.

This program would affect people who are just starting out in farming or ranching and who need access to affordable startup capital. It does not replace existing USDA loan programs but adds a separate option alongside them. The USDA would have two years to set the program up and would report to Congress every two years on how it is working.
ai_headline: USDA pilot loans would give beginning farmers up to $100,000 at low interest
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, Risk Management, and Credit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5367 creates a USDA pilot loan program offering beginning farmers up to $100,000 at 0–3% interest over 3–10 years.
ai_sections.what: HR 5367 establishes a pilot loan program within the U.S. Department of Agriculture offering "development loans" of up to $100,000 to beginning farmers and ranchers. Loans would carry interest rates of 0 to 3 percent and repayment terms of 3 to 10 years. Eligible uses include starter equipment, perennial crops, breeding animals, bookkeeping systems, customer base development, and meeting food safety or environmental requirements.
ai_sections.who: The program targets people who are just starting out in farming or ranching and need affordable startup capital. Collateral requirements may be reduced for borrowers who have relevant farming experience.
ai_sections.why: Beginning farmers currently rely on short-term annual loans to cover long-term startup costs, creating a mismatch between loan terms and investment timelines. This program would add a separate financing option alongside existing USDA loan programs, and the USDA would be required to report to Congress every two years on program outcomes.
ai_sections.cost: Loans are capped at $100,000 per borrower with interest rates set between 0 and 3 percent. The bill does not specify a total program funding amount.

title: Anna’s Law of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal grant program run by the Department of Health and Human Services. The grants would pay for training programs that teach law enforcement officers and emergency medical technicians (EMTs) how trauma affects the brains and behavior of people who have experienced sexual assault, domestic violence, dating violence, or stalking. The training would help first responders recognize when their words or actions might accidentally make things worse for a survivor. New recruits at police and fire academies and EMS programs would need at least eight hours of this training, while officers and EMTs already on the job would need at least four hours every year. The federal government would also keep a public, searchable online list of qualified trainers.

The bill affects law enforcement agencies and emergency medical services at the state, local, and tribal level, since those are the organizations that could apply for the grants. It also directly affects survivors of sexual assault and related crimes, because the goal is to change how first responders interact with them. Trainers hired to deliver the programs would need to reflect a range of professional backgrounds and diverse racial, ethnic, and gender identities.

The Department of Health and Human Services would be required to send annual reports to Congress tracking how many agencies received grants, how well the training is working, case prosecution outcomes, and feedback from survivors themselves. Each grant would last one year. The bill does not set a specific dollar amount for the program.
ai_headline: Federal bill would fund trauma training for police and EMTs who respond to assault survivors
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3121 would create federal grants for trauma-informed training so first responders avoid re-harming survivors of sexual assault or domestic violence.
ai_sections.what: HR 3121 would establish a federal grant program administered by the Department of Health and Human Services to fund trauma-informed training for law enforcement officers and EMTs. New recruits would be required to complete at least eight hours of training, while officers and EMTs already working would need at least four hours every year. HHS would also maintain a public, searchable online directory of qualified trainers.
ai_sections.who: State, local, and tribal law enforcement agencies and emergency medical services organizations would be eligible to apply for the grants. Survivors of sexual assault, domestic violence, dating violence, and stalking would be directly affected by changes in how first responders interact with them.
ai_sections.why: The training is designed to help first responders recognize when their words or actions may unintentionally cause additional harm to survivors. HHS would be required to report annually to Congress on grant recipients, training effectiveness, case prosecution outcomes, and survivor feedback.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not specify a dollar amount for the grant program.
costChips: Grants are one year in duration

title: Protecting American Industry and Labor from International Trade Crimes Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new specialized unit inside the Department of Justice focused entirely on prosecuting trade crimes. The unit would be set up within the DOJ's Criminal Division within 120 days of receiving funding. It would hire dedicated prosecutors and support staff to investigate and prosecute crimes like smuggling goods into the country, lying to customs officials, evading tariffs and import fees, selling counterfeit products, trade-based money laundering, and importing dangerous or illegal goods. The bill would authorize $20 million for this effort in 2026, with at least 80 percent going directly to hiring and training prosecutors and investigators.

The bill targets people and companies that break trade laws, particularly those who cheat the system to avoid paying required import duties, smuggle prohibited goods across US borders, or launder money through fake trade transactions. It also covers importing products that violate food, drug, or chemical safety laws. The new unit would work alongside agencies like Customs and Border Protection and Homeland Security Investigations, and would also coordinate with trading partner countries to go after crimes that cross borders.

American workers, domestic manufacturers, and businesses that follow the rules are the ones this bill is meant to protect, since trade crimes can undercut them by allowing foreign competitors to sell goods at illegally low prices. The DOJ would have to report to Congress every year on how many cases were charged, how the money was spent, and what additional funding might be needed. The bill has support from members of both parties.
ai_headline: DOJ would get new trade crimes unit under bipartisan House bill
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 23 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1869 would create a DOJ trade crimes unit within 120 days of funding, authorized at $20M for 2026.
ai_sections.what: HR 1869 would establish a specialized trade crimes unit inside the DOJ's Criminal Division, operational within 120 days of receiving funding. The unit would prosecute smuggling, tariff evasion, customs fraud, counterfeit sales, trade-based money laundering, and imports that violate food, drug, or chemical safety laws. It would coordinate with Customs and Border Protection, Homeland Security Investigations, and foreign trading partners on cross-border cases.
ai_sections.who: The unit would hire dedicated prosecutors, investigators, and support staff. It would pursue individuals and companies that evade import duties, smuggle prohibited goods, or launder money through fake trade transactions.
ai_sections.why: Trade crimes can allow foreign competitors to sell goods at illegally low prices, which affects domestic manufacturers and businesses that comply with trade law. The DOJ would be required to report to Congress annually on cases charged, spending, and any additional funding needs.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $20 million for 2026, with at least 80 percent required to go directly toward hiring and training prosecutors and investigators.

title: EXPERTS Act of 2025
ai_summary:
The EXPERTS Act of 2025 is a bill that changes how federal agencies make rules and regulations. Its biggest focus is on transparency and conflicts of interest. When a company or group submits a scientific or economic study to support or oppose a proposed regulation, they would have to disclose who funded that study, who reviewed it, and whether there are any financial ties between the researchers and parties affected by the rule. If a publicly traded company submits false or misleading information during a rulemaking process, it could face fines starting at $250,000. The bill also requires agencies to publicly explain when they change or cancel a proposed rule, and it sets time limits on how long the government office that reviews major regulations can take to complete that review.

The bill also creates a new office called the Office of the Public Advocate inside the Office of Management and Budget. This office would help everyday people participate in the rulemaking process, translate regulatory information into plain and accessible language, and study how proposed rules might affect disadvantaged or minority communities. If a public petition related to a rule gathers more than 100,000 signatures, the relevant agency would be required to formally respond within 60 days. The bill also restores a legal principle that courts should generally defer to agencies when interpreting unclear laws within their area of expertise.

This bill would affect a wide range of people. Businesses and industry groups that regularly submit comments and studies during the rulemaking process would face new disclosure requirements. Ordinary citizens and community organizations would gain new tools to engage with the regulatory process. Federal agencies across the government would face stricter deadlines and transparency rules. Rules that were previously blocked by Congress through the Congressional Review Act could also be reinstated more easily under this bill.
ai_headline: New bill would require funders of regulatory studies to be named publicly
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: The EXPERTS Act of 2025 adds disclosure rules, fines, and a public advocate office to the federal rulemaking process.
ai_sections.what: The EXPERTS Act of 2025 changes how federal agencies create regulations by requiring disclosure of who funded and reviewed scientific or economic studies submitted during rulemaking, including any financial ties to affected parties. Agencies would have to publicly explain changes or cancellations of proposed rules, and time limits would apply to government review of major regulations. The bill also restores a legal principle directing courts to defer to agencies when interpreting unclear laws in their area of expertise.
ai_sections.who: Businesses and industry groups submitting comments or studies during rulemaking would face new disclosure requirements, while ordinary citizens and community organizations would gain new tools to participate. Federal agencies across the government would operate under stricter deadlines and transparency rules.
ai_sections.why: A new Office of the Public Advocate inside the Office of Management and Budget would be created to help everyday people engage with rulemaking and to study how proposed rules may affect disadvantaged or minority communities. Rules previously blocked through the Congressional Review Act could be reinstated more easily under the bill.
ai_sections.cost: Publicly traded companies that submit false or misleading information during a rulemaking process could face fines starting at $250,000.
costChips: Triggered by false or misleading submissions

title: CLEAR LABELS Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require prescription and over-the-counter drug labels to show more detailed information about where the drug and its key ingredients were made. Specifically, it would require drug packaging to identify the original manufacturer of each active ingredient, the manufacturer of the finished drug product, and any packer or distributor involved. This information could appear directly on the label or be accessible through a barcode or QR code that links to an online database. Manufacturers would also have to provide this information in paper form to anyone who asks. The bill gives the FDA authority to write the specific rules for how this works, and companies would have at least one year after those rules are finalized to comply.

The bill also makes a small change to customs law. Currently, imported products must be marked with their country of origin under a separate trade law. This bill says that finished drug products meeting the new labeling requirements would be exempt from that separate customs marking rule, since the drug label would already carry the required location information.

This bill affects drug manufacturers, packagers, and distributors who sell medications in the United States, including companies that source ingredients from overseas. It also affects everyday consumers and pharmacists, who would gain easier access to information about where a drug and its ingredients were produced. This could be relevant for people who want to know whether ingredients come from specific countries, such as China or India, where a large share of pharmaceutical ingredients are currently manufactured.
ai_headline: Drug labels would have to show where medications and ingredients were made
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8269 requires drug labels to identify manufacturers of active ingredients, finished products, and any packer or distributor involved.
ai_sections.what: HR 8269 would require prescription and over-the-counter drug labels to identify the original manufacturer of each active ingredient, the manufacturer of the finished drug product, and any packer or distributor involved. This information could appear directly on the label or be accessible through a barcode or QR code linked to an online database. Manufacturers would also have to provide this information in paper form upon request, and companies would have at least one year after FDA finalizes its rules to comply.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects drug manufacturers, packagers, and distributors that sell medications in the United States, including companies that source ingredients from overseas. Consumers and pharmacists would also be affected, gaining easier access to information about where a drug and its ingredients were produced.
ai_sections.why: People who want to know whether drug ingredients come from specific countries, such as China or India, where a large share of pharmaceutical ingredients are currently manufactured, would have a clearer way to find that information. The bill also exempts finished drug products that meet the new labeling requirements from a separate customs marking rule, since the drug label would already carry the required location information.

title: Hire Student Veterans Act
ai_summary:
This bill would expand an existing tax break called the Work Opportunity Tax Credit to include a new group of workers. Right now, businesses can get a tax credit when they hire certain veterans who face barriers to employment. This bill would add student veterans to that list, meaning companies could get a tax credit when they hire veterans who are currently using GI Bill benefits or similar military education programs to attend school.

The bill also adjusts a rule about how long the veteran has to work before the employer can claim the credit. For this new category of student veterans, the usual minimum employment period requirement would not apply, making it easier for employers to qualify for the credit even if the veteran works for a shorter time, which makes sense given that students often have more flexible or part-time work schedules.

The people most directly affected are businesses considering hiring veterans who are currently in school using federal education benefits, and the student veterans themselves, who may find it easier to get hired because their employer gets a financial incentive. Veterans using programs like the Post-9/11 GI Bill, Vocational Rehabilitation, or certain National Guard and Reserve education benefits would fall into this new category.
ai_headline: Bill would let employers claim tax credit for hiring student veterans
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6404 expands the Work Opportunity Tax Credit to cover veterans using GI Bill or similar education benefits while working.
ai_sections.what: HR 6404 would add student veterans to the list of workers covered by the Work Opportunity Tax Credit, an existing tax break for businesses that hire veterans facing employment barriers. To qualify, the veteran must be actively using GI Bill benefits or similar military education programs. The bill also waives the usual minimum employment period requirement for this new category, accommodating part-time or flexible student work schedules.
ai_sections.who: Businesses that hire veterans currently enrolled in school using federal education benefits would be directly affected. Student veterans using programs such as the Post-9/11 GI Bill, Vocational Rehabilitation, or certain National Guard and Reserve education benefits would fall under the new category.
ai_sections.why: Employers who previously could not claim the credit for hiring student veterans would become eligible under the expanded rules. Student veterans may find it easier to secure employment because their hire creates a financial incentive for employers.

title: Bridge the Gap for Rural Communities Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to how the federal government pays farmers in 2025. First, it temporarily removes the usual cap on how much money a single farmer or farming business can receive through two programs called Agricultural Risk Coverage (ARC) and Price Loss Coverage (PLC). These programs pay farmers when crop prices drop or their revenue falls below a certain level. Normally, there is a limit on how much any one farmer can collect, but this bill suspends that limit for the 2025 crop year only.

Second, the bill lets farmers get part of their expected 2025 payment early, before the full amount is calculated. Farmers who choose this option would receive 50 percent of their estimated payment by December 31, 2025. Once the actual numbers are finalized after the marketing year ends, they would get the remaining balance. If a farmer was overpaid, they would have to return the extra money, but without any interest penalty.

This bill primarily affects farmers who grow covered commodities like corn, soybeans, wheat, cotton, and similar crops, and who are enrolled in the ARC or PLC programs. Larger farming operations would benefit the most from removing the payment cap, since smaller farms are less likely to hit that limit in the first place. Rural communities that depend on farm income could also feel indirect effects if the extra payments help stabilize local economies during a difficult crop year.
ai_headline: Federal farm payment caps suspended and early payouts allowed for 2025 crop year
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, Risk Management, and Credit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5710 lifts 2025 payment caps for ARC and PLC farm programs and lets enrolled farmers receive 50% of estimated payments by Dec. 31, 2025.
ai_sections.what: HR 5710 suspends the usual dollar cap on payments to individual farmers through the Agricultural Risk Coverage and Price Loss Coverage programs for the 2025 crop year only. It also creates an early payment option, allowing enrolled farmers to receive 50 percent of their estimated 2025 payment by December 31, 2025, with the remaining balance paid after final figures are calculated. Farmers who receive more than they are owed must return the overage, but no interest penalty applies.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects farmers enrolled in ARC or PLC who grow covered commodities such as corn, soybeans, wheat, and cotton. Larger farming operations are most directly affected by the cap removal, since smaller farms are less likely to reach the existing limit.
ai_sections.why: Removing the payment cap changes how federal farm support is distributed, concentrating larger payments among bigger operations during a difficult crop year. Rural communities that rely on farm income could see indirect economic effects depending on how the additional payments circulate locally.
ai_sections.cost: The bill directs the federal government to pay out up to 50 percent of estimated 2025 ARC and PLC payments early, with full balances settled after the marketing year closes. Overpayments must be returned, but no interest is charged on amounts farmers owe back.
costChips: 50% of estimated payment paid by Dec. 31, 2025
Full balance paid after marketing year closes

title: Fentanyl is a WMD Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require a specific office within the Department of Homeland Security to officially treat illegal fentanyl as a weapon of mass destruction. The office involved is the Countering Weapons of Mass Destruction Office, which currently focuses on threats like chemical, biological, nuclear, and radiological weapons. By classifying illicit fentanyl under that same legal category, the office would be required to apply its resources, tools, and authorities to fighting the illegal fentanyl supply.

In practical terms, this would mean that the same government powers and programs used to track and stop WMD threats could also be directed at illegal fentanyl. This could include things like increased detection efforts at borders, intelligence gathering, and coordination with other agencies. The bill is short and narrow, making just this one change to existing homeland security law.

This bill would primarily affect how federal homeland security agencies operate and prioritize resources. It could indirectly affect border security personnel, law enforcement, and communities dealing with fentanyl-related drug overdoses. It would not directly change criminal penalties for individuals or create new laws targeting drug users. The bill was introduced in January 2025 and referred to the House Committee on Homeland Security, meaning it has not yet passed into law.
ai_headline: DHS fentanyl bill would grant WMD powers to fight illegal drug supply
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Emergency Management and Technology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 128 would make illicit fentanyl legally a WMD, letting a DHS office use its full anti-WMD tools against the illegal drug supply.
ai_sections.what: HR 128 would require the Department of Homeland Security's Countering Weapons of Mass Destruction Office to classify illicit fentanyl as a weapon of mass destruction. This would legally place illegal fentanyl in the same category as chemical, biological, nuclear, and radiological threats. As a result, the office could direct its existing resources, authorities, and programs toward combating the illegal fentanyl supply.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects how federal homeland security agencies operate and allocate resources. It could indirectly affect border security personnel, law enforcement agencies, and communities experiencing fentanyl-related overdoses.
ai_sections.why: Applying WMD-level authorities to illegal fentanyl could expand detection efforts at borders, intelligence gathering, and coordination across agencies. The bill does not change criminal penalties for individuals or create new laws targeting drug users.

title: New World Screwworm Preparedness Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill tells the U.S. Department of Agriculture to study how ready the country is to handle the New World screwworm, a parasitic fly whose larvae burrow into and eat the living flesh of warm-blooded animals. The screwworm was wiped out in the U.S. in the 1960s, but recent outbreaks in Mexico have raised concerns it could come back. The study must be completed within 90 days of the bill becoming law, and a full report with recommendations must be delivered to Congress within 6 months.

The study covers several areas: how likely the screwworm is to enter the U.S. and through what routes, whether the country has enough capacity to produce sterile flies (the main tool used to fight the pest), how well border inspections and detection systems are working, whether emergency response plans are ready, what new research is needed, and how well the U.S. is cooperating with other countries to keep the pest out. The Agriculture Department must also consult with farmers, veterinarians, wildlife officials, and scientists while conducting the study.

This bill most directly affects livestock farmers, particularly cattle and hog producers, as well as wildlife managers and veterinarians, especially in states bordering Mexico like Texas. If a screwworm outbreak occurred, it could cause serious economic damage to the livestock industry and harm wildlife populations. The bill does not spend any money or create new programs on its own; it simply requires a study and a report with recommendations for what steps should be taken next.
ai_headline: USDA ordered to assess U.S. readiness for screwworm return
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3806 requires USDA to study screwworm readiness within 90 days and report to Congress within 6 months.
ai_sections.what: HR 3806 directs the U.S. Department of Agriculture to conduct a readiness study on the New World screwworm, a parasitic fly whose larvae feed on the living flesh of warm-blooded animals. The study must be completed within 90 days of enactment, with a full report and recommendations delivered to Congress within 6 months. The bill does not create new programs or authorize spending; it requires only the study and report.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects livestock farmers, particularly cattle and hog producers, as well as veterinarians and wildlife managers, especially in states bordering Mexico such as Texas. The Agriculture Department must consult with farmers, veterinarians, wildlife officials, and scientists while conducting the study.
ai_sections.why: The screwworm was eradicated from the U.S. in the 1960s, but recent outbreaks in Mexico have raised concerns about its potential return. An outbreak could cause serious economic damage to the livestock industry and harm wildlife populations.

title: Fire Innovation Unit Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a seven-year pilot program run jointly by the U.S. Departments of Agriculture and Interior. The program is designed to test and deploy new wildfire technologies by pairing private companies, nonprofits, and universities with federal, state, tribal, and local fire agencies. Technologies that could be tested include drones and remote sensors for detecting fires, better communication tools for firefighters, AI-based fire modeling, home-hardening methods, and equipment to reduce the amount of burnable vegetation. Companies and organizations can apply to participate, and agencies can also flag technology partnerships that are already working well.

The goal is to find out which technologies actually work in real wildfire situations, how much they would cost to use widely, and how easily they could be scaled up across the country. The Secretaries of Agriculture and Interior must report to Congress within 180 days of launch and every year after that, covering what was tested, estimated costs, and recommendations for wider adoption. The program ends automatically after seven years.

This bill most directly affects federal land management agencies like the Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management, as well as state and local fire departments, tribal fire agencies, and private companies or researchers working on wildfire technology. Indirectly, it could benefit people living in fire-prone communities by helping get better fire prevention and response tools into the hands of firefighters sooner.
ai_headline: Seven-year wildfire tech pilot would pair companies with fire agencies
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6094 launches a 7-year federal pilot to test wildfire technologies by matching private innovators with fire agencies.
ai_sections.what: HR 6094 establishes a seven-year pilot program run jointly by the U.S. Departments of Agriculture and Interior to test and deploy wildfire technologies. Technologies under consideration include drones, remote sensors, AI-based fire modeling, firefighter communication tools, home-hardening methods, and vegetation-reduction equipment. The program accepts applications from private companies, nonprofits, and universities, and agencies may also flag existing partnerships that are already performing well.
ai_sections.who: The program directly affects federal land management agencies such as the Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management, as well as state, local, and tribal fire agencies, and private companies or researchers working on wildfire technology. People living in fire-prone communities are indirectly affected by outcomes that could influence how quickly better tools reach firefighters.
ai_sections.why: The program is designed to determine which technologies perform effectively in real wildfire conditions, what it would cost to deploy them broadly, and how readily they could be scaled nationwide. The Secretaries of Agriculture and Interior are required to report to Congress within 180 days of launch and annually thereafter, covering what was tested, estimated costs, and recommendations for wider adoption.

title: Kayleigh’s Law Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would require federal judges to automatically issue a permanent no-contact order against anyone convicted of certain serious crimes. Specifically, if someone is convicted of a violent felony or a sex-related felony under federal law, the court must order that person to have zero contact with their victim for the rest of that offender's life. Contact is defined very broadly and includes any form of communication, whether in person, by phone, through social media, through a third party, or by any other means. There is no fee charged to the victim to get this order.

The no-contact order can only be lifted in two situations. If the victim asks to have it removed because the offender received a pardon or commutation, or if the offender successfully gets their conviction dismissed or overturned on appeal, a judge can hold a hearing and potentially end the order. Otherwise it stays in place permanently. Violating the order would be treated as contempt of court.

This bill mainly affects crime victims and people convicted of federal violent or sexual felonies. Victims would receive a guaranteed lifetime layer of legal protection without having to go through extra steps or pay any costs. Offenders convicted of these crimes would face a permanent legal restriction on contacting their victim, regardless of how much time has passed since the conviction. The bill applies to federal cases but also states that it does not limit how individual states can apply similar protections within their own courts.
ai_headline: Federal bill would lock in lifetime no-contact orders for violent and sex crime convicts
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 23 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8481 would permanently bar federally convicted violent and sex offenders from any contact with their victims, at no cost to victims.
ai_sections.what: HR 8481 would require federal courts to automatically issue a permanent no-contact order whenever someone is convicted of a federal violent felony or sex-related felony. The order covers all forms of contact with the victim, including in-person, phone, social media, and contact through third parties. The order stays in place for the lifetime of the offender unless the victim requests removal following a pardon or commutation, or the conviction is overturned on appeal.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects victims of federal violent and sex crimes, who would receive automatic lifetime protection without taking extra legal steps or paying any fees. It also affects anyone convicted of these federal felonies, who would face a permanent legal restriction on contacting their victim regardless of time elapsed since conviction.
ai_sections.why: Victims would have a guaranteed legal barrier against offender contact without bearing any procedural or financial burden to obtain it. Offenders would remain under a court-enforceable restriction indefinitely, with violation treated as contempt of court, and individual states retain the ability to apply their own similar protections separately.
ai_sections.cost: No fee is charged to victims to obtain the no-contact order.

title: To amend the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act to provide for improved coordination between the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency and the Secretary of Agriculture, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill changes how the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) handles decisions about pesticides. Right now, the EPA can approve, restrict, or add safety requirements to pesticides largely on its own. Under this bill, the EPA would be required to work closely with the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) whenever it sets new safety rules for a pesticide, reviews an existing pesticide registration, or responds to endangered species concerns. The EPA would also have to publish a cost analysis showing how any new restrictions would affect farmers and other users, and explain in writing whether it used or rejected data provided by the USDA.

The bill affects several groups. Farmers and agricultural businesses would likely see more consideration given to the practical and financial impact of pesticide restrictions before those restrictions take effect. The EPA would have to weigh the economic costs to growers alongside safety concerns. State agencies that oversee pesticide use would also be included in those cost estimates.

The bill also touches on how the EPA responds when endangered species protections require changes to pesticide use. Currently, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Marine Fisheries Service can recommend actions to protect wildlife, and the EPA must follow certain rules under the Endangered Species Act. This bill would require the EPA to loop in the USDA during that process and share feedback with the wildlife agencies about how their recommendations affect pesticide users. Essentially, the bill adds more required collaboration and public documentation to pesticide decision-making across several federal agencies.
ai_headline: EPA would need USDA sign-off on pesticide safety rules under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5564 requires the EPA to collaborate with the USDA and publish cost analyses before imposing new pesticide restrictions.
ai_sections.what: HR 5564 requires the EPA to work jointly with the U.S. Department of Agriculture whenever it sets new pesticide safety rules, reviews an existing registration, or responds to endangered species concerns. The EPA would also have to publish a written cost analysis showing how any new restrictions would affect farmers and other users, and explain in writing whether it used or rejected data provided by the USDA.
ai_sections.who: Farmers, agricultural businesses, and state pesticide agencies would be directly affected, as the bill requires their economic interests to be weighed before restrictions take effect. The EPA, USDA, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, and National Marine Fisheries Service all have new coordination obligations under the bill.
ai_sections.why: The bill adds mandatory collaboration steps and public documentation requirements to a process that currently allows the EPA to act largely on its own. Decisions involving endangered species protections would also require the EPA to share feedback with wildlife agencies about how their recommendations affect pesticide users.

title: Revitalizing Rural Communities Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill updates a Department of Agriculture program called the Rural Economic Development Loan and Grant Program. Right now, that program is authorized to spend $10 million per year, but its funding period expired after 2023. This bill would restart it and increase the annual funding to $12 million per year, running from 2026 through 2030. The money goes to electric cooperatives and utilities in rural areas, which can then loan it out or grant it to local businesses and projects that create jobs and support economic growth.

The people most directly affected are residents of rural communities across the country. Local electric cooperatives use this funding to support things like new businesses, community facilities, and other projects that might otherwise struggle to get financing in less populated areas. The increase from $10 million to $12 million means slightly more money would be available each year for these kinds of projects.

This is a relatively narrow bill with a straightforward purpose: keep an existing rural development program running and give it a modest budget increase. It does not create a new program or make major policy changes. The bill was introduced in the House and referred to the Agriculture Committee, meaning it still has several steps to go through before it could become law.
ai_headline: House bill would restore rural development loans and lift annual cap to $12 million
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5598 would restart an expired USDA rural loan and grant program at $12M/year from 2026 to 2030, up from $10M.
ai_sections.what: HR 5598 would reauthorize the Department of Agriculture's Rural Economic Development Loan and Grant Program, which expired after 2023. The bill sets new funding at $12 million per year running from 2026 through 2030. It does not create a new program or make major policy changes.
ai_sections.who: Rural residents across the country are most directly affected. Electric cooperatives and utilities in rural areas receive the funds and then loan or grant them to local businesses and projects.
ai_sections.why: Without reauthorization, the program remains expired and rural electric cooperatives have no access to this federal funding source. The $2 million annual increase over the prior $10 million level means modestly more money would be available for projects in less populated areas that may struggle to get conventional financing.
ai_sections.cost: The program would be funded at $12 million per year from 2026 through 2030, compared to the previous authorization of $10 million per year.

title: CATCH IT Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how much federal money rural health clinics and hospitals can receive when they apply for grants through a U.S. Department of Agriculture program called the Community Facilities Grant Program. Right now, that program already helps rural facilities pay for construction and equipment. The CATCH IT Act would let rural health facilities get an extra 25 percentage points added to their federal funding share specifically when they use the money to buy or upgrade certain health screening equipment.

The equipment covered includes things like 3D mammography machines, CT scanners, ultrasound machines, colorectal cancer screening tools, mobile cancer screening units, and lab equipment used to detect multiple cancers early. Basically, if a rural clinic or hospital wants to add any of these tools, the federal government would cover a larger portion of the cost than it normally would under this grant program.

The people most affected are residents of rural communities who currently have limited access to preventive health screenings, and the small rural clinics and hospitals that serve them. By reducing the financial burden on those facilities, the bill aims to make it more affordable for them to offer cancer screenings and other early detection services that are more commonly found in larger cities. The bill was introduced in October 2025 and would take effect at the start of the next federal fiscal year after it becomes law.
ai_headline: Rural clinics could get 25 extra federal funding points for cancer screening gear
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5858 adds 25 percentage points to federal grants when rural health facilities buy specific cancer screening equipment.
ai_sections.what: HR 5858, the CATCH IT Act, would add 25 percentage points to the federal funding share rural health clinics and hospitals can receive under the USDA Community Facilities Grant Program when they purchase or upgrade certain cancer screening equipment. Covered equipment includes 3D mammography machines, CT scanners, ultrasound machines, colorectal cancer screening tools, mobile cancer screening units, and lab equipment for early multi-cancer detection. The bill was introduced in October 2025 and would take effect at the start of the next federal fiscal year after enactment.
ai_sections.who: Rural health clinics and hospitals seeking grants for screening equipment are directly affected. Residents of rural communities who currently have limited access to preventive cancer screenings are the intended beneficiaries.
ai_sections.why: By raising the federal share of grant funding for these specific purchases, the bill reduces the portion of costs rural facilities would need to cover on their own. This change could affect how many rural facilities are financially able to acquire cancer screening technology that is more commonly available in larger cities.
ai_sections.cost: The bill increases the federal funding share available through the existing USDA Community Facilities Grant Program by up to 25 percentage points for qualifying equipment purchases at rural health facilities. No specific dollar figures are provided in the summary.
costChips: Up to 25 extra federal funding points

title: No Federal Tax Dollars for Illegal Aliens Health Insurance Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for a federal program that lets states create their own versions of the Affordable Care Act (ACA) health insurance marketplace. Right now, some states have received special permission, called waivers, to use federal money in flexible ways to cover more people, including some immigrants who are not in the country legally. This bill would make it so that federal money passed to states through these waivers cannot be used to help pay for health insurance for people who are not U.S. citizens or legal residents. It also requires the federal government to cancel any existing state waivers that allowed that kind of coverage within 30 days of the bill becoming law.

The bill affects two main groups. First, it affects immigrants without legal immigration status who currently receive health insurance help through state waiver programs funded with federal dollars. Those people would lose access to that federally funded coverage. Second, it affects the states that set up these programs, such as California, which has used ACA waivers to extend coverage to undocumented residents. Those states would either need to fund that coverage entirely with their own money or stop offering it.

Currently this bill has only been introduced in the House and has not become law. It has been sent to two House committees for review. If it passed, the practical result would be that no federal tax dollars could flow through ACA waiver programs to subsidize health insurance for people living in the U.S. without legal authorization.
ai_headline: House bill would cut federal ACA waiver funds for undocumented immigrants
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7817 would bar federal ACA waiver money from covering undocumented immigrants and cancel existing state waivers within 30 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 7817 would prohibit federal money sent to states through ACA waiver programs from being used to pay for health insurance for people without legal immigration status. The bill would also require the federal government to cancel any existing state waivers that allowed such coverage within 30 days of enactment.
ai_sections.who: Undocumented immigrants currently receiving health insurance assistance through federally funded state waiver programs would lose access to that coverage. States such as California that built programs using ACA waivers to cover undocumented residents would be affected.
ai_sections.why: States that currently use federal waiver dollars for this coverage would need to fund it entirely with their own money or stop offering it. No federal tax dollars could flow through ACA waiver programs to subsidize health insurance for people living in the U.S. without legal authorization.
ai_sections.cost: Federal funds that currently flow to states through ACA waivers for undocumented immigrant coverage would be cut off. States choosing to maintain that coverage would bear the full cost themselves.

title: Balanced Agricultural Support and Efficiency Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how the federal government calculates "base acres" for farms that receive certain agricultural support payments. Base acres are a number assigned to a farm that determines how much money a farmer can receive under federal commodity programs. Right now, many farms still use base acre numbers that were set years ago and may not match what farmers are actually growing today. This bill would require the U.S. Department of Agriculture to do a one-time update, recalculating each farm's base acres using what was actually planted between 2020 and 2024.

The new calculation would look at the average of what each farm planted over those five years and adjust the base acres to reflect that recent history. So if a farmer has been growing more soybeans and fewer wheat acres than their old base acres show, the numbers would be updated to better match reality. The bill also accounts for acres that farmers were prevented from planting due to floods, droughts, or other disasters beyond their control.

This bill primarily affects farmers who grow covered commodities like corn, wheat, soybeans, rice, and cotton and who participate in federal farm support programs. It could increase payments for some farmers whose current planting reflects more acreage in a given crop than their old base acres show, while potentially decreasing payments for others. Farm owners, agricultural lenders, and rural communities tied to commodity farming would all have an interest in how this update plays out.
ai_headline: Federal farm payment formula would shift to reflect 2020–2024 planting data
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5551 requires USDA to recalculate farm base acres using average plantings from 2020–2024, changing how commodity payments are set.
ai_sections.what: HR 5551 would require the U.S. Department of Agriculture to do a one-time recalculation of base acres for farms enrolled in federal commodity support programs. The new figures would be based on the average of what each farm actually planted between 2020 and 2024. Acres left unplanted due to floods, droughts, or other disasters beyond a farmer's control would also be factored into the update.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects farmers who grow covered commodities such as corn, wheat, soybeans, rice, and cotton and who participate in federal farm support programs. Farm owners, agricultural lenders, and rural communities tied to commodity farming also have a stake in the outcome.
ai_sections.why: Because base acres were set years ago and may no longer match what farms are currently growing, the update could increase payments for some farmers and decrease payments for others. The shift would affect how much money individual farms are eligible to receive under federal commodity programs going forward.

title: Disaster Resiliency and Coverage Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a federal grant program to help homeowners make their homes stronger before a disaster strikes, rather than only helping after damage occurs. The federal government would give money to states and tribal governments, which would then pass grants of up to $10,000 to individual households in high-risk areas. The money could be used for things like reinforcing roofs, installing storm shelters, adding fireproofing materials, elevating homes above flood levels, or clearing flammable brush around a house. Only households earning under $250,000 a year (or $500,000 for couples filing jointly) would qualify, and the grants would not count as taxable income.

The bill also creates a 30 percent federal tax credit for homeowners who pay out of pocket for these same types of disaster-proofing improvements. So if someone spends $5,000 reinforcing their roof, they could get $1,500 back on their taxes. Additionally, the bill makes certain farm disaster assistance payments tax-free, and it makes any money received from similar state-run mitigation programs tax-free as well.

The people most directly affected are homeowners living in areas at high risk of natural disasters like hurricanes, wildfires, floods, tornadoes, and earthquakes. Renters would not qualify for grants but could benefit if landlords use the tax credit. Farmers who receive federal disaster aid would benefit from the tax exemption. Insurance companies and state regulators are also involved, since states must report on insurance availability in these risky areas as part of receiving the grant funding.
ai_headline: Federal bill would pay homeowners up to $10,000 to disaster-proof before storms hit
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1105 offers grants up to $10,000 and a 30% tax credit to help homeowners in disaster-prone areas harden their homes.
ai_sections.what: HR 1105 creates a federal grant program that sends money to states and tribal governments, which then distribute grants of up to $10,000 to qualifying households in high-risk areas. Funds can pay for roof reinforcement, storm shelters, fireproofing, flood elevation, or brush clearing. The bill also creates a 30 percent federal tax credit for homeowners who pay out of pocket for those same improvements.
ai_sections.who: Homeowners in high-risk natural disaster areas who earn under $250,000 a year (or $500,000 for couples filing jointly) qualify for grants. Farmers receiving federal disaster aid and renters whose landlords use the tax credit are also affected.
ai_sections.why: States receiving grant funding must report on insurance availability in high-risk areas, drawing regulators and insurers into the program's oversight structure. Grant money and qualifying state mitigation payments would not count as taxable income, and farm disaster assistance payments would become tax-free under the bill.
ai_sections.cost: Grants of up to $10,000 per household flow from the federal government through states and tribal governments to individual homeowners. The 30 percent tax credit reduces federal tax revenue for each qualifying out-of-pocket improvement expense.
costChips: Paid through states and tribal govts

title: Collegiate Housing and Infrastructure Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes the tax code to allow certain nonprofit organizations to give money to college fraternities and sororities without losing their tax-exempt status. Right now, if a nonprofit donates to a social organization like a fraternity or sorority, it risks being seen as no longer operating purely for charitable or educational purposes, which could cost it its tax benefits. This bill creates an exception, saying that donating to improve housing where most residents are full-time college students is still considered a charitable or educational activity.

The money given under this rule must be used to provide, improve, operate, or maintain housing where nearly all residents are full-time college students. The housing improvements allowed are similar to what a college would be permitted to do with its own dorms. There is one limit: the grant money cannot be used to build or upgrade fitness facilities. Donations that do follow the rules would also be eligible for deductions related to estate and gift taxes.

This bill mainly affects nonprofit organizations that want to donate to Greek-letter housing groups, as well as the fraternities and sororities themselves. It could also benefit college students who live in those houses if the funding leads to better or safer facilities. Donors who give to qualifying nonprofits could also see some tax benefits flow through indirectly. It does not appear to affect students who live in standard college dormitories or non-Greek off-campus housing.
ai_headline: Tax exemption rule would expand to cover grants for Greek housing
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2355 lets nonprofits donate to fraternity and sorority housing without risking their tax-exempt status.
ai_sections.what: HR 2355 amends the tax code to allow nonprofit organizations to donate to fraternity and sorority housing without jeopardizing their tax-exempt status. Grant money must be used to provide, improve, operate, or maintain housing where nearly all residents are full-time college students. Fitness facility construction or upgrades are explicitly excluded from allowable uses, and qualifying donations would also be eligible for estate and gift tax deductions.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects nonprofit organizations that donate to Greek-letter housing groups and the fraternities and sororities that receive those funds. College students living in those houses and donors who give to qualifying nonprofits may also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, donating to a social organization like a fraternity or sorority can be interpreted as operating outside purely charitable or educational purposes, putting a nonprofit's tax-exempt status at risk. This bill creates a specific exception for housing grants that meet the stated conditions, which could influence how nonprofits allocate charitable funds and how Greek housing is maintained or improved.

title: BECCS Advancement Commission Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new advisory group called the Bioenergy with Carbon Capture and Storage Advancement Commission, or BECCS Commission, housed inside the U.S. Department of Agriculture. BECCS is a process where plants or trees are burned to generate energy, and then the carbon dioxide released is captured and stored underground instead of going into the atmosphere. The commission would be made up of federal officials from the Agriculture, Energy, and Interior departments, along with representatives from the timber industry, state energy and forestry organizations, and the BECCS industry itself. They would meet at least once every three months and would not be paid for their time, though travel costs would be covered.

The commission's main job is to study BECCS technology and write a report to Congress within one year of its first meeting. That report would include recommendations on how the federal government could expand the use of BECCS power plants, make it easier to use wood and plant material from federal lands as fuel, and improve coordination between government agencies and the industry. The report would also look at how BECCS could affect jobs in local communities, energy prices and reliability, forest health, wildfire prevention, and even the growing energy needs of data centers and artificial intelligence systems. The commission would shut down 180 days after delivering that final report.

This bill mainly affects people who work in or live near the forestry and timber industries, rural communities that currently receive federal funding tied to forest land, energy companies interested in carbon capture technology, and federal land managers. It could also have broader effects on communities near federal forests, since the bill examines using wood from those lands as fuel. For now, the bill only creates a study group to make recommendations, so no policies would change until Congress acts on whatever the commission suggests.
ai_headline: New federal commission would study wood-burning carbon capture technology
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5597 creates a USDA advisory commission to study BECCS energy technology and report to Congress within one year; no policy changes yet.
ai_sections.what: HR 5597 establishes the Bioenergy with Carbon Capture and Storage Advancement Commission, housed within the U.S. Department of Agriculture. The commission would study BECCS technology, a process that burns plants or trees for energy while capturing and storing the carbon dioxide released underground. It would meet at least quarterly and must deliver a report with recommendations to Congress within one year of its first meeting, after which it shuts down 180 days later.
ai_sections.who: The commission would include federal officials from the Agriculture, Energy, and Interior departments, along with representatives from the timber industry, state energy and forestry organizations, and the BECCS industry. People most directly affected include forestry and timber workers, rural communities near federal forests, energy companies pursuing carbon capture, and federal land managers.
ai_sections.why: The report would examine how federal agencies could expand BECCS power plants, ease access to wood and plant material from federal lands as fuel, and improve coordination between government and industry. It would also assess effects on local jobs, energy prices, forest health, wildfire prevention, and the energy demands of data centers and artificial intelligence systems.
ai_sections.cost: Commission members would not be paid for their service, though travel costs would be covered.

title: Cheroenhaka (Nottoway) Indian Tribe of Southampton County, Virginia, Federal Recognition Act
ai_summary:
This bill would grant federal recognition to the Cheroenhaka (Nottoway) Indian Tribe, a Native American tribe based in Southampton County, Virginia. Federal recognition is an official legal status that the U.S. government extends to certain tribes, and this tribe currently has state recognition from Virginia but not federal recognition. The bill notes that Virginia's 1924 Racial Integrity Act, which wrongly reclassified many Native Americans as "colored or mulatto" in official records, made it extremely difficult for this tribe to gain recognition through the normal Bureau of Indian Affairs process, which is why Congress is acting directly.

If passed, the tribe and its roughly 325 enrolled members would become eligible for federal services and benefits available to other federally recognized tribes, such as certain health care, education, and social programs. The federal government would also be allowed to take land into trust for the tribe, meaning land the tribe already owned before 2007 in Southampton County could be designated as reservation land with special legal protections. A service area for delivering those federal benefits would be set up within 120 days of the bill becoming law.

The bill includes two notable limits. First, the tribe would not be permitted to conduct any gaming or casino operations under any circumstances. Second, the bill does not change any existing hunting, fishing, trapping, or water rights the tribe may or may not currently have. The tribe's existing leadership and membership rolls would remain in place.
ai_headline: House bill would give federal recognition to Virginia's Cheroenhaka Nottoway tribe
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5144 would federally recognize the ~325-member Cheroenhaka (Nottoway) tribe of Virginia, unlocking health, education, and social program eligibility.
ai_sections.what: HR 5144 would grant federal recognition to the Cheroenhaka (Nottoway) Indian Tribe, based in Southampton County, Virginia. The tribe currently holds state recognition from Virginia but not federal recognition. If enacted, the federal government could also take land the tribe owned before 2007 in Southampton County into trust as reservation land, and a federal service area would be established within 120 days of the bill becoming law.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the Cheroenhaka (Nottoway) Indian Tribe and its approximately 325 enrolled members. The Bureau of Indian Affairs and relevant federal agencies would be responsible for implementing the new recognition and service area.
ai_sections.why: Virginia's 1924 Racial Integrity Act wrongly reclassified many of the tribe's members in official records, making it extremely difficult to gain recognition through the standard Bureau of Indian Affairs process. Congress is acting directly because of that historical barrier, rather than requiring the tribe to pursue the normal administrative route. The bill prohibits the tribe from conducting any gaming or casino operations and does not alter any existing hunting, fishing, trapping, or water rights.

title: A bill to authorize, ratify, and confirm the Agreement of Settlement and Compromise to Resolve the Akwesasne Mohawk Land Claim in the State of New York, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill settles a long-running land dispute between the Akwesasne Mohawk people and the state of New York. The Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe and a related Canadian Mohawk group filed lawsuits in the 1980s and 1989 claiming certain lands in Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties in upstate New York were taken from them unlawfully. This bill officially approves a negotiated agreement that resolves those lawsuits without going through a court trial. The parties who agreed to the settlement include the Tribe, New York State, two counties, two towns, and the New York Power Authority.

The bill also formally recognizes certain lands owned by the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe in specific areas as "Indian Country." That legal status matters because it determines which laws apply there, including who has authority over criminal justice, taxation, and land use on those lands. Land already owned by the Tribe and land they buy in the future within the defined settlement areas would carry this status.

The people most directly affected are members of the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe and the Mohawk Council of Akwesasne, as well as residents and local governments in Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties in New York. The New York Power Authority is also involved, likely because some of the disputed land relates to infrastructure in that region. For the Tribe, the settlement provides legal certainty over their land rights. For local governments and landowners nearby, it brings a resolution to decades of uncertainty from the open lawsuits.
ai_headline: Senate bill settles decades-old Mohawk land dispute in upstate New York
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3475 approves a negotiated settlement resolving 1980s Mohawk land lawsuits in Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties, NY.
ai_sections.what: S 3475 approves a negotiated settlement that resolves lawsuits filed by the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe and a related Canadian Mohawk group in the 1980s and 1989 over lands in Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties in New York. The bill also formally designates certain Tribe-owned lands in the settlement areas as Indian Country, a legal status that determines which laws govern criminal justice, taxation, and land use there. This designation would apply to land the Tribe already owns and land it acquires in the future within those defined areas.
ai_sections.who: The Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe and the Mohawk Council of Akwesasne are the primary parties, along with New York State, Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties, two towns, and the New York Power Authority. Residents and local governments in those two counties are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: For the Tribe, the settlement establishes legal certainty over land rights that have been contested for decades. For nearby landowners and local governments, it ends uncertainty tied to the open lawsuits without requiring a court trial.

title: To authorize, ratify, and confirm the Agreement of Settlement and Compromise to Resolve the Akwesasne Mohawk Land Claim in the State of New York, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill settles a long-running land dispute between the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe (also known as the Akwesasne Mohawk) and the State of New York, two local counties, two towns, and the New York Power Authority. The tribe had filed federal lawsuits going back to 1982 claiming rights to certain lands in northern New York. Congress is being asked to officially approve a negotiated agreement that all the parties have already reached, which is a standard step required by federal law to make Native American land settlements legally final.

The agreement affects land in Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties in northern New York, near the existing Akwesasne reservation along the Canadian border. Under the deal, certain lands that the tribe owns or will acquire in defined areas would be formally recognized as "Indian Country," which is a legal status under federal law that affects how those lands are governed and taxed. The New York Power Authority, which operates hydroelectric facilities in that region, is also part of the settlement, suggesting the dispute involved land connected to power generation along the St. Lawrence River.

The people most directly affected are members of the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe, residents of Fort Covington and Bombay in New York, and people who live or own property near the settlement areas in Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties. Ratifying the agreement would bring legal certainty to land ownership questions that have been unresolved for over 40 years, ending ongoing litigation for all parties involved.
ai_headline: Federal bill would ratify 40-year land settlement with Akwesasne Mohawk Tribe
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2916 would ratify a negotiated settlement ending 40+ years of land dispute between the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe and New York.
ai_sections.what: HR 2916 asks Congress to ratify a negotiated land settlement between the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe and the State of New York, two counties, two towns, and the New York Power Authority.
