The agreement would formally recognize certain lands in Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties as Indian Country under federal law. Federal ratification is required by law to make Native American land settlements legally final.
ai_sections.who: The settlement directly affects members of the Saint Regis Mohawk Tribe and residents of Fort Covington and Bombay, New York. People who own or live near property in the settlement areas of Franklin and Saint Lawrence Counties are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Ratifying the agreement would resolve land ownership questions that have remained legally unsettled for over 40 years, ending ongoing litigation for all parties. The Indian Country designation would change how the affected lands are governed and taxed under federal law.

title: Pit River Land Transfer Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would transfer two parcels of land in California to federal trust status for the benefit of the Pit River Tribe. The first parcel is about 584 acres of federal land currently managed by the U.S. Forest Service. The second is about 40 acres the tribe already owns. Putting land "into trust" means the federal government holds the title on the tribe's behalf, and the land becomes part of the official Pit River Tribe Reservation. The bill also requires a survey of the federal land to be completed within 180 days of passage. One specific restriction is built in: the transferred land cannot be used for casino-style or bingo-style gambling under federal Indian gaming law.

The land involved is in an area Congress calls part of the Pit River Tribe's ancestral territory, located in northern California. The Pit River Tribe is a federally recognized tribe made up of several communities, including the XL Ranch, Big Bend, Likely, Lookout, Montgomery Creek, and Roaring Creek Rancherias. Once the transfer is complete, the land would be managed under the same federal rules that apply to other tribal trust lands across the country.

This bill primarily affects the Pit River Tribe and the federal agencies involved in the transfer, mainly the U.S. Forest Service and the Department of the Interior. It would have little direct impact on most Americans. Neighboring landowners and users of the current Forest Service land could be affected, since the land's management would shift from a federal agency to tribal administration, though existing road easements and public rights-of-way are specifically excluded from the transfer.
ai_headline: Federal bill would add 624 acres to Pit River Tribe reservation in northern California
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2400 would place 624 acres of land in northern California into federal trust for the Pit River Tribe, expanding their reservation.
ai_sections.what: HR 2400 would transfer two parcels of land in northern California into federal trust status for the Pit River Tribe. The first parcel is about 584 acres currently managed by the U.S. Forest Service; the second is about 40 acres the tribe already owns. Both parcels would become part of the official Pit River Tribe Reservation, and the federal land must be surveyed within 180 days of passage. The transferred land cannot be used for casino-style or bingo-style gambling under federal Indian gaming law.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects the Pit River Tribe, a federally recognized tribe made up of several communities in northern California, along with the U.S. Forest Service and the Department of the Interior. Neighboring landowners and current users of the Forest Service land could also be affected, as land management would shift from a federal agency to tribal administration.
ai_sections.why: The land would shift from U.S. Forest Service management to tribal administration under federal trust rules, changing how it is governed and used. Existing road easements and public rights-of-way are excluded from the transfer, though other access and use arrangements for neighboring landowners could change.

title: Quinault Indian Nation Land Transfer Act
ai_summary:
This bill would transfer about 72 acres of land in Washington State from the U.S. Forest Service to the Department of the Interior, which would then hold that land "in trust" for the Quinault Indian Nation. When land is held in trust this way, the federal government legally owns it but manages it for the tribe's benefit, and it becomes part of the Quinault Indian Reservation. The land in question is identified as "Allotment 1157" on an official map. The bill also requires the government to disclose any hazardous materials found on the land before the transfer is complete.

The bill includes a few specific limits. Gambling or casino operations would not be allowed on this land under federal gaming law. The bill also makes clear that it does not change or take away any rights the Quinault Indian Nation already has under the Treaty of Olympia, a treaty signed in 1855 and 1856.

This bill mainly affects the Quinault Indian Nation, a federally recognized tribe based in Washington State, as well as the U.S. Forest Service and the Department of the Interior. Local and state governments in Washington could also be affected, since trust land is generally not subject to state or local taxes or zoning rules. The broader public in the surrounding area could be affected depending on how the tribe chooses to use the land going forward.
ai_headline: Federal bill would transfer 72 acres in Washington to Quinault Indian Nation trust
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2389 moves 72 acres of Forest Service land in Washington into federal trust for the Quinault Indian Nation, expanding their reservation.
ai_sections.what: HR 2389 would transfer approximately 72 acres of U.S. Forest Service land in Washington State to the Department of the Interior, which would hold it in trust for the Quinault Indian Nation. The parcel, identified as Allotment 1157, would become part of the Quinault Indian Reservation. Before the transfer, the government must disclose any hazardous materials found on the land. Gambling and casino operations would be prohibited on the parcel under federal gaming law.
ai_sections.who: The Quinault Indian Nation, a federally recognized tribe in Washington State, is the primary party affected. The U.S. Forest Service, the Department of the Interior, and local and state governments in Washington are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Trust land is generally not subject to state or local taxes or zoning rules, which alters the regulatory and tax relationship between the parcel and surrounding local and state governments. The bill preserves existing Quinault Indian Nation rights under the Treaty of Olympia, signed in 1855 and 1856, and does not modify or remove them.

title: Quinault Indian Nation Land Transfer Act
ai_summary:
This bill would move about 72 acres of land in Washington State from the U.S. Forest Service to the Department of the Interior, which would then hold that land "in trust" for the Quinault Indian Nation. When land is held in trust this way, the federal government legally owns it but manages it for the tribe's benefit, and it becomes part of the Quinault Indian Reservation. The land in question is a specific parcel called Allotment 1157. The bill also states that no gambling or gaming operations can be built on this land.

title: Southwestern Power Administration Fund Establishment Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a single, dedicated bank account — called the Southwestern Power Administration Fund — held in the U.S. Treasury. Right now, the Southwestern Power Administration (SWPA), a federal agency that sells wholesale electricity generated at government-owned dams, keeps its money spread across several separate funds set up by different laws over the years. This bill consolidates all of that money into one unified fund, transferring existing balances and directing future revenues into it. Any money left over at the end of the year gets sent back to the general Treasury.

The SWPA is responsible for transmitting and marketing hydroelectric power from federal dams across Arkansas, Kansas, Louisiana, Missouri, Oklahoma, and Texas. The fund would pay for running and maintaining power lines, building new transmission infrastructure, and covering administrative costs. The bill also gives the SWPA more financial flexibility by allowing it to take on approved expenses before Congress has formally appropriated the money, with the fund later covering those costs.

This bill mostly affects how the federal government manages its own accounting and finances internally. The people most directly touched are customers of the SWPA, which are mainly electric cooperatives, municipal utilities, and public power districts in the south-central United States that buy wholesale power from the agency. Everyday residents in those states who get electricity through those local utilities could be indirectly affected, though the bill itself does not change electricity rates or service — it is primarily a financial housekeeping measure.
ai_headline: Federal bill merges scattered power agency funds into one Treasury account
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on Water and Power. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1034 consolidates all Southwestern Power Administration money into one Treasury fund, replacing several separate accounts created under different laws.
ai_sections.what: S 1034 creates a single Treasury account called the Southwestern Power Administration Fund, replacing several separate funds the agency currently holds under different laws. All existing balances would transfer into the new fund, and future revenues would flow into it going forward. Any unspent money at year's end would return to the general Treasury.
ai_sections.who: The Southwestern Power Administration, a federal agency that sells wholesale hydroelectric power from government dams in Arkansas, Kansas, Louisiana, Missouri, Oklahoma, and Texas, is the primary entity affected. Its customers — electric cooperatives, municipal utilities, and public power districts in the south-central United States — are the most directly touched outside the federal government.
ai_sections.why: Consolidating the accounts changes how SWPA manages its finances internally and gives the agency flexibility to cover approved expenses before formal congressional appropriation. Residents in those states who receive electricity through SWPA's utility customers could be indirectly affected, though the bill does not alter electricity rates or service levels.
ai_sections.cost: The fund would cover costs of operating and maintaining power lines, building new transmission infrastructure, and paying administrative expenses of the Southwestern Power Administration.
costChips: Covers power line operations and maintenance

title: Crypto ATM Fraud Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill sets new rules for businesses that run crypto ATMs, which are physical machines where you can exchange cash for cryptocurrency like Bitcoin. Under the bill, these companies would have to register their machines with the U.S. Treasury every 90 days, hire a full-time compliance officer, create a written anti-fraud plan, and use special software to flag wallets connected to known scams. Before a new customer makes a transaction of $500 or more, the company must get verbal confirmation from that customer over a live phone or video call that they understand the transaction and are not being pressured into it. The machines would also have to display clear warnings about common scams, such as fake government officials, fake job offers, and charity fraud.

The bill also sets limits and refund rights. New customers cannot send more than $2,000 in any 24-hour period or more than $10,000 total through a single operator's machines. If a customer was tricked into a fraudulent transaction, they can apply for a refund within 30 days by filing a police report. New customers who were scammed can get back the full amount they lost, including fees, while returning customers can only recover the fees. Companies that wrongly deny a refund could owe the customer three times the refund amount or $10,000, whichever is larger.

This bill mainly affects two groups. Businesses that own or operate crypto ATMs would face significant new costs and requirements, including hiring staff and updating their machines. Consumers who use crypto ATMs, particularly older adults who are common targets of these scams, could gain new protections and a clearer path to getting money back if they are defrauded.
ai_headline: Senate bill would require live phone checks before crypto ATM deals
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S1347-1348; text: CR S1348-1350)

ai_sections.tldr: S 710 requires crypto ATM operators to verify new customers by live call before any transaction of $500 or more.
ai_sections.what: S 710 sets new federal rules for businesses that operate cryptocurrency ATMs. Operators would have to register machines with the U.S. Treasury every 90 days, hire a full-time compliance officer, create a written anti-fraud plan, and use software to flag wallets linked to known scams. Machines must display warnings about scams including fake government officials, fake job offers, and charity fraud.
ai_sections.who: Businesses that own or operate crypto ATMs would face new registration, staffing, and technology requirements. Consumers who use these machines, particularly older adults who are common targets of crypto ATM scams, are the primary protected group.
ai_sections.why: New customers would be limited to $2,000 per 24-hour period and $10,000 total through a single operator's machines. Operators that wrongly deny a valid refund could owe the customer three times the refund amount or $10,000, whichever is larger.
ai_sections.cost: Operators who wrongly deny a refund could owe three times the refund amount or $10,000, whichever is larger. New customers who were defrauded can claim a full refund including fees within 30 days, while returning customers can recover fees only.
costChips: New customers: full refund plus fees

title: Global Respect Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would require the U.S. government to create and regularly update a list of foreign individuals who have committed serious human rights abuses against LGBTQI people. These abuses include things like torture, long imprisonment without a trial, forced disappearances, or other serious threats to a person's life or safety. Anyone placed on this list would be banned from entering the United States, and if they already have a visa, it would be canceled. The list would mostly be made public, though a classified section could exist for national security reasons. The president would also be encouraged to consider additional financial or economic sanctions against people on the list.

The bill also requires the State Department to track and report on violence and discrimination against LGBTQI individuals in countries around the world. A senior State Department official would be specifically assigned to monitor these issues. Annual human rights reports that the U.S. already produces about foreign countries would be updated to include information about how LGBTQI people are treated in those countries.

This bill primarily affects foreign government officials and other foreign nationals who are found responsible for these abuses, since they would face travel bans to the U.S. It also affects the State Department, which would take on new reporting and tracking responsibilities. It does not directly change any laws or policies inside the United States affecting American citizens.
ai_headline: Senate bill would bar foreign abusers of LGBTQI people from entering the US
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2180 would ban foreign nationals who commit serious LGBTQI abuses from entering the US and require new State Dept tracking reports.
ai_sections.what: S 2180 would require the U.S. government to create and regularly update a list of foreign individuals who have committed serious human rights abuses against LGBTQI people, including torture, prolonged detention without trial, and forced disappearances. Anyone placed on the list would be banned from entering the United States, and any existing visas would be canceled. The bill would also update existing annual U.S. human rights reports to include information on how LGBTQI people are treated in countries around the world.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects foreign government officials and other foreign nationals found responsible for these abuses, who would face U.S. travel bans and visa cancellations. The State Department would also be affected, taking on new responsibilities to monitor, track, and report on LGBTQI rights conditions globally, including through a newly assigned senior official.
ai_sections.why: The list would be mostly public, though a classified section could exist for national security reasons, and the president would be encouraged to consider additional financial or economic sanctions against those listed. The bill does not directly change any laws or policies inside the United States affecting American citizens.

title: Quapaw Tribal Settlement Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would allow the U.S. Treasury to pay $137.5 million to the Quapaw Nation and certain individual Quapaw tribal members to settle a long-running legal case. The case, originally filed in 2013, went through a special federal court process, and a review panel issued a recommendation in 2020 about what the U.S. government should pay. This bill simply gives the government the legal authority to actually make that payment, transferring the money into a special account managed by the Department of the Interior.

Once the money is in that account, the Quapaw Nation and the other claimants in the lawsuit would need to agree on how to divide it among themselves. The bill gives them 45 days to enter mediation with a neutral third party to work out a distribution plan. If they reach an agreement, the Secretary of the Interior distributes the funds accordingly. If they cannot agree after 18 months, the Secretary steps in and holds a formal hearing to decide how the money gets split.

This bill directly affects the Quapaw Nation, a federally recognized tribe based primarily in northeastern Oklahoma, and the specific individual tribal members named in the original 2013 lawsuit. For most Americans, there is no direct impact. The $137.5 million would come from the general U.S. Treasury, meaning it is funded by existing federal money rather than a new tax.
ai_headline: Senate bill unlocks $137.5 million settlement for the Quapaw Nation
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 630 gives the U.S. Treasury legal authority to pay $137.5M to the Quapaw Nation to settle a 2013 lawsuit.
ai_sections.what: S 630 authorizes the U.S. Treasury to pay $137.5 million to settle a lawsuit originally filed in 2013 by the Quapaw Nation and certain individual tribal members. The money would be transferred into a special account managed by the Department of the Interior. The claimants would then have 45 days to begin mediation to agree on how to divide the funds, with the Secretary of the Interior stepping in to decide if no agreement is reached within 18 months.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects the Quapaw Nation, a federally recognized tribe based primarily in northeastern Oklahoma, and the specific individual tribal members named in the 2013 lawsuit. For most Americans there is no direct impact.
ai_sections.why: A federal review panel issued a recommendation in 2020 on what the government should pay, but the payment could not be made without explicit legal authority. This bill provides that authority, moving the case toward a resolution that has been pending since 2013.
ai_sections.cost: The $137.5 million would come from the general U.S. Treasury, funded by existing federal money rather than a new tax.

title: BEACH Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill updates a law called the BEACH Act, which was originally passed in 2000. It renews federal grant money, up to $30 million per year, for state and local governments to test the water quality at public beaches along coasts and the Great Lakes. The money had not been authorized since 2005, so this bill extends it from 2025 through 2029. It also requires the EPA to make sure its guidance to states uses the latest water testing technology.

The bill expands what the grant money can be used for. States and local governments could now use the funds not just to test water at the beach itself, but also in shallow upstream waterways nearby that might be carrying pollution toward the beach. They can also use the money to track down the specific sources of any contamination they find, such as a sewage overflow or a polluted stream, rather than just detecting that a problem exists.

This affects anyone who swims, surfs, or recreates at public coastal beaches or Great Lakes beaches in the United States. It also directly involves state and local health and environmental agencies, since they are the ones applying for and using these grants. If contamination is found, beach managers are required to notify the public, so better monitoring could mean more timely warnings when water is unsafe to use.
ai_headline: Federal beach water testing grants renewed through 2029 under updated BEACH Act
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: S 508 revives lapsed federal beach water testing grants up to $30M/year for states, extended through 2029.
ai_sections.what: S 508 reauthorizes federal grants under the BEACH Act, originally passed in 2000, to fund water quality testing at public coastal and Great Lakes beaches. The authorization lapsed after 2005 and this bill extends it from 2025 through 2029. Grant funds can now also be used to test shallow upstream waterways near beaches and to identify specific contamination sources, not just detect that a problem exists.
ai_sections.who: State and local health and environmental agencies apply for and administer the grants. Anyone who swims, surfs, or recreates at public coastal or Great Lakes beaches in the United States is affected.
ai_sections.why: Better and broader monitoring could produce more timely public notifications when water is unsafe, since beach managers are already required to notify the public when contamination is found. The bill also requires the EPA to ensure its guidance to states reflects the latest water testing technology.
ai_sections.cost: Federal grants are authorized at up to $30 million per year, running from 2025 through 2029. The authorization covers state and local governments as the recipients of those funds.

title: Wildlife Health Coordination and Zoonotic Disease Prevention Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new government program called the Wildlife Health Coordination and Zoonotic Disease Program, housed within the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. The program would hire six specialized coordinators: one national coordinator, one focused on Tribal nations, and four covering different regions of the country. These coordinators would act as go-betweens connecting federal agencies like the CDC, USDA, and Department of Homeland Security with state and Tribal wildlife and public health officials. Their main jobs would be sharing information about disease outbreaks, helping states and tribes apply for funding, spreading best practices for managing sick animals, and reporting to Congress on gaps in the current system. The bill would set aside $900,000 per year starting in 2027 to fund these positions.

The bill is focused on diseases that spread between animals and humans, called zoonotic diseases. Examples the bill specifically mentions include highly pathogenic avian influenza (bird flu), chronic wasting disease in deer and elk, and bovine tuberculosis. Congress notes that more than 60 percent of known human infectious diseases originally came from animals, and that outbreaks are becoming more common. The 2022 bird flu outbreak alone cost the federal government over $2.5 billion in payments to affected farmers as of early 2025.

The people most directly affected by this bill would be federal agency employees, state wildlife and agriculture officials, and Tribal wildlife managers who would work with or become these coordinators. Indirectly, rural communities, farmers, and livestock producers would be affected because better coordination could mean faster responses to outbreaks that threaten their animals and livelihoods. The general public could also benefit through earlier detection of diseases that might otherwise spread to humans.
ai_headline: New federal program would place six wildlife disease coordinators across the U.S.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4451 would fund six wildlife-human disease coordinators at $900,000/year starting 2027.
ai_sections.what: S 4451 would establish the Wildlife Health Coordination and Zoonotic Disease Program inside the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. The program would hire six coordinators — one national, one focused on Tribal nations, and four regional — to connect federal agencies including the CDC, USDA, and Department of Homeland Security with state and Tribal wildlife and public health officials. Their duties would include sharing outbreak information, helping states and tribes apply for funding, and reporting to Congress on gaps in the current system.
ai_sections.who: Federal agency employees, state wildlife and agriculture officials, and Tribal wildlife managers would work directly with or serve as these coordinators. Rural communities, farmers, livestock producers, and the general public would be indirectly affected through changes in how quickly disease outbreaks are detected and managed.
ai_sections.why: Congress notes that more than 60 percent of known human infectious diseases originally came from animals and that outbreaks are becoming more common. The 2022 bird flu outbreak alone resulted in over $2.5 billion in federal payments to affected farmers as of early 2025.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would set aside $900,000 per year beginning in 2027 to fund the six coordinator positions. The 2022 bird flu outbreak cost the federal government over $2.5 billion in payments to affected farmers as of early 2025.
costChips: Bird flu cost U.S. over $2.5 billion

title: Merchant Banking Modernization Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes a rule about how long large banks are allowed to hold certain types of investments. Right now, banks that own financial holding companies can make what are called "merchant banking investments," which means they can invest money in private companies that are not publicly traded on a stock market. Federal regulators currently have flexibility to set how long banks can hold these investments, and in practice the rules have generally allowed around 10 years. This bill would set a minimum holding period of 15 years, meaning banks could keep these investments for at least that long before being required to sell them.

The practical effect is that banks would have more time to wait for a private company investment to grow in value before they have to exit it. For investments a bank already holds when the bill becomes law, the 15-year clock would restart from when the original investment was first made, potentially giving those banks even more time. Supporters would say this gives banks the patience to support businesses through their full growth cycle. Critics might argue it allows banks to hold riskier, less liquid assets for longer periods. The bill does not eliminate regulatory oversight, it just sets a floor on how long the holding period must be allowed to last.

This bill most directly affects large financial institutions, specifically bank holding companies that qualify as financial holding companies under federal law. It also indirectly affects private companies that receive investment from those banks, since banks could remain invested in them longer. Everyday banking customers are not directly impacted by this change, though shifts in how banks manage their investment portfolios can have broader effects on financial stability over time.
ai_headline: Senate bill would extend bank private investment hold period to 15 years
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2663 sets a 15-year minimum holding period for merchant banking investments, up from the current roughly 10-year practice.
ai_sections.what: S 2663 would establish a 15-year minimum holding period for merchant banking investments made by financial holding companies, replacing a flexible regulatory standard that has generally allowed around 10 years. Banks would be required to hold these private company investments for at least 15 years before being required to sell them. For investments already held when the bill becomes law, the 15-year period would restart from the date the original investment was first made.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects large financial institutions that qualify as financial holding companies under federal law. It also indirectly affects private companies that receive investment from those banks, since banks could remain invested in them for longer periods.
ai_sections.why: Extending the minimum holding period means banks would be permitted to keep less liquid, privately held assets on their books for longer before regulators could require an exit. For investments already held at the time of enactment, the retroactive restart of the clock could extend the permissible holding period beyond what current rules allow.

title: Yuhaaviatam of San Manuel Nation Land Exchange Act
ai_summary:
This bill sets up a land swap in San Bernardino County, California, between the federal government and the Yuhaaviatam of San Manuel Nation, also known as the San Manuel Band of Mission Indians. The tribe would hand over about 1,460 acres of land it owns to the United States, and in return, the federal government would transfer about 1,475 acres of San Bernardino National Forest land to the tribe. The exchange is meant to give the tribe access to land that is part of its ancestral territory. The U.S. Forest Service would keep the right to use certain forest roads that run through the land being transferred to the tribe.

The bill includes a specific condition to protect the Arrowhead landmark, a well-known natural rock formation in the San Bernardino Mountains. The tribe must sign an agreement with the Forest Service to preserve the historical and cultural character of that site. The land the tribe gives up would become part of the San Bernardino National Forest and be managed under normal national forest rules.

The people most directly affected are the Yuhaaviatam of San Manuel Nation and residents near the San Bernardino National Forest in Southern California. Hikers, forest users, and local communities could see changes in who manages certain parcels of land in the area. The bill was introduced in the Senate in September 2025 and has been sent to the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs for review.
ai_headline: Federal land swap would return San Manuel ancestral acres in San Bernardino
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2796 swaps ~1,460 tribal acres for ~1,475 San Bernardino National Forest acres to restore ancestral land access.
ai_sections.what: S 2796 authorizes a land exchange between the federal government and the Yuhaaviatam of San Manuel Nation in San Bernardino County, California. The tribe would transfer approximately 1,460 acres it owns to the United States, and the federal government would transfer approximately 1,475 acres of San Bernardino National Forest land to the tribe. The exchanged land is described as part of the tribe's ancestral territory.
ai_sections.who: The Yuhaaviatam of San Manuel Nation, also known as the San Manuel Band of Mission Indians, is the primary party affected. Residents near the San Bernardino National Forest, including hikers, forest users, and local communities, could also see changes in land management in the area.
ai_sections.why: The land the tribe gives up would become part of the San Bernardino National Forest and be managed under standard national forest rules, changing oversight of those acres. The bill also requires the tribe to sign an agreement with the U.S. Forest Service to preserve the historical and cultural character of the Arrowhead landmark, a natural rock formation in the San Bernardino Mountains, as a condition of the transfer.

title: Cooperative Watershed Management Program Reauthorization Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill renews a federal grant program that helps local groups manage watersheds, which are the areas of land that drain into rivers, lakes, and streams. The program gives money to "watershed groups," which are partnerships made up of farmers, ranchers, local governments, businesses, and others who share an interest in keeping a local water supply healthy. The bill keeps the program running through 2031 and sets aside up to $40 million per year to fund it.

The bill makes several updates to how the program works. It raises the maximum annual grant from $100,000 to $150,000 and lets high-performing groups apply for up to two extra years of funding beyond the original grant period. It also officially includes Native American tribes as eligible members of watershed groups, especially tribes with ancestral ties to the land in a given watershed. Groups dealing with drought, wildfire, or other natural disasters get added priority for receiving grants. The bill also allows groups to use grant money for things like writing future grant applications, hiring project managers, and getting engineering help.

This bill mainly affects people in rural and western parts of the country where water management is a daily concern. Farmers, ranchers, tribal communities, local governments, and conservation groups that rely on healthy watersheds would be the most directly impacted. Ordinary residents who depend on those water sources for drinking water, agriculture, or recreation could also see indirect benefits if local watershed projects improve water quality and supply.
ai_headline: Federal watershed grant program renewed through 2031 with higher funding caps
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on Water and Power. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4041 extends a watershed grant program through 2031, raises annual grants to $150,000, and sets $40M/year in funding.
ai_sections.what: S 4041 reauthorizes a federal grant program that funds local watershed groups managing land that drains into rivers, lakes, and streams. The bill extends the program through 2031, raises the maximum annual grant from $100,000 to $150,000, and allows high-performing groups to apply for up to two additional years of funding. Groups may now use grant money for writing future applications, hiring project managers, and obtaining engineering assistance.
ai_sections.who: Watershed groups eligible for grants are partnerships that may include farmers, ranchers, local governments, businesses, and Native American tribes with ancestral ties to a given watershed. Rural and western communities, tribal communities, and conservation groups that depend on healthy water sources are most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Groups dealing with drought, wildfire, or other natural disasters receive added priority for grants under the updated program. Residents who rely on affected water sources for drinking water, agriculture, or recreation could see changes in water quality and supply depending on how local projects are carried out.
ai_sections.cost: The bill sets aside up to $40 million per year to fund the program, which runs through 2031. Individual grants are capped at $150,000 per year.

title: No Lifeline for Dead People Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets the Lifeline program, which is a federal program that helps low-income Americans pay for phone and internet service. Right now, some states are allowed to use their own eligibility systems to decide who qualifies for Lifeline instead of using the federal government's system, called the National Verifier. This bill would end that option and require all phone and internet companies to use only the National Verifier to check whether a customer qualifies before signing them up.

The bill's name refers to concerns that some people enrolled in Lifeline may be deceased or otherwise ineligible, which can happen when eligibility checks are less thorough. By requiring everyone to go through the National Verifier, which can cross-check federal databases, supporters say it would reduce fraud and waste in the program.

The people most directly affected are low-income residents in states that currently use their own eligibility systems rather than the federal one. If those state systems are currently faster or easier to navigate, those residents might face a different sign-up process. Phone and internet companies operating in those states would also need to change how they verify customers before enrolling them in Lifeline benefits.
ai_headline: Senate bill would force all Lifeline signups through federal verifier
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3954 would end state-run eligibility checks for the Lifeline phone and internet subsidy program, requiring all providers to use the federal National Verifier.
ai_sections.what: S 3954 would eliminate the option for states to use their own eligibility systems for the Lifeline program, which helps low-income Americans pay for phone and internet service. All phone and internet companies would be required to verify customer eligibility exclusively through the federal National Verifier before enrolling anyone in the program. The National Verifier cross-checks federal databases to confirm eligibility.
ai_sections.who: Low-income residents in states that currently use their own eligibility systems would face a different sign-up process than they do now. Phone and internet companies operating in those states would also need to change how they verify customers before enrolling them in Lifeline benefits.
ai_sections.why: Supporters say requiring the National Verifier would reduce fraud and waste by catching deceased or otherwise ineligible enrollees that less thorough state systems may miss. Residents in states where existing state systems are faster or easier to navigate could experience a more complicated or unfamiliar enrollment process.

title: Strategic Subsea Cables Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill focuses on protecting the underwater fiber-optic cables that carry most of the world's internet traffic across oceans. These cables are critical infrastructure, and Congress is concerned they could be cut or damaged by hostile foreign actors. The bill would create a new interagency committee bringing together agencies like the Departments of State, Defense, and Homeland Security to better coordinate the U.S. government's approach to keeping these cables secure, permitted, built, and repaired. It also requires the State Department to hire dedicated experts on undersea cables and increase U.S. involvement in the International Cable Protection Committee, a global organization focused on cable safety.

The bill would allow the President to impose sanctions, including freezing assets and banning visas, on any foreign individual or entity found responsible for intentionally damaging these cables in ways that harm U.S. national security. It also requires the government to regularly report to Congress on the cable-related activities of China and Russia, including their ability to cut cables at great ocean depths and any suspicious behavior by their ships near cable networks.

The bill affects several groups. Federal agencies would need to set up new coordination systems and hire new staff. Private companies that own and operate undersea cables would gain a clearer government partner to work with and would receive better threat information sharing. American internet users are not directly impacted, but the goal of the bill is to prevent disruptions to the global internet connections that millions of people rely on every day.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets foreign threats to undersea internet cables
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 327.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3249 creates an interagency committee and sanctions authority to protect undersea fiber-optic cables from foreign sabotage.
ai_sections.what: S 3249 would establish a new interagency committee drawing together the Departments of State, Defense, and Homeland Security to coordinate security, permitting, construction, and repair of undersea fiber-optic cables. The bill also grants the President authority to impose sanctions, including asset freezes and visa bans, on foreign individuals or entities that intentionally damage these cables in ways that harm U.S. national security. Additionally, it requires regular congressional reports on cable-related activities by China and Russia, including their deep-ocean cutting capabilities and ship movements near cable networks.
ai_sections.who: Federal agencies would be required to build new coordination systems and hire dedicated undersea cable experts, including new State Department staff and a stronger U.S. presence in the International Cable Protection Committee. Private companies that own and operate undersea cables would gain a clearer government point of contact and improved access to threat information.
ai_sections.why: The cables covered by this bill carry most of the world's internet traffic across oceans, and Congress has identified hostile foreign actors as a potential threat to their integrity. Disruption to these cables could affect global internet connectivity that millions of people rely on daily.

title: Albuquerque Indian School Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would transfer about 9.89 acres of federal land in Albuquerque, New Mexico, to the U.S. government to hold "in trust" for the benefit of 19 Native American Pueblos in New Mexico. The land was historically part of the Albuquerque Indian School, a federal boarding school that operated for decades. Right now the land is managed by the General Services Administration, which handles federal property. Within 90 days of the bill passing and after any federal workers or offices on the site have moved out, the land would be handed over to the Department of the Interior to hold on behalf of the 19 Pueblos listed in the bill.

Once transferred, the land must be used for education, health, cultural, business, or economic development purposes for those 19 Pueblos. The bill specifically bans any form of gambling or gaming on the property. Existing easements, utility agreements, and other legal arrangements already attached to the land would stay in place. One of the three parcels includes a large warehouse, and the federal government would keep the right to access that parcel temporarily to retrieve or move any federal property still stored there.

This bill primarily affects the 19 named Pueblo tribes in New Mexico, who would gain control over land with historical significance to their communities. It also affects federal agencies that currently manage the property, since they would need to relocate before the transfer happens. General Albuquerque residents living near the site could see changes in how the property is developed or used over time.
ai_headline: Federal land from historic Albuquerque Indian School set for transfer to 19 New Mexico Pueblos
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 3219 would transfer 9.89 acres of former federal boarding school land in Albuquerque to 19 Pueblo tribes.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 3219 would transfer approximately 9.89 acres of federally managed land in Albuquerque, New Mexico, to the Department of the Interior to hold in trust for 19 Native American Pueblos. The land was historically part of the Albuquerque Indian School, a federal boarding school that operated for decades. Once transferred, the property must be used for education, health, cultural, business, or economic development purposes, and gambling or gaming is explicitly prohibited.
ai_sections.who: The 19 named Pueblo tribes in New Mexico would gain control of the land, while federal agencies currently managing the property would be required to relocate before the transfer. Residents near the site in Albuquerque could see changes in how the property is developed over time.
ai_sections.why: Federal agencies currently managing the land, including the General Services Administration, would need to move personnel and offices off the site within 90 days of the bill passing before the transfer can occur. The federal government would retain temporary access to one parcel containing a warehouse in order to retrieve or relocate any federal property still stored there.

title: Pit River Land Transfer Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would transfer about 584 acres of national forest land in California to the federal government to hold "in trust" for the Pit River Tribe. When land is held in trust, the federal government officially owns it but manages it on behalf of the tribe, and it becomes part of the tribe's reservation. The bill requires a survey of the land to be completed within 180 days of passing. Roads and public rights-of-way on the property, about 20 acres, are not included in the transfer and would remain accessible to the public. The bill also specifically bans the tribe from using this land for casino-style or bingo-style gambling.

This bill directly affects the Pit River Tribe and its member communities in northern California, including the XL Ranch, Big Bend, Likely, Lookout, Montgomery Creek, and Roaring Creek Rancherias. It also involves two federal agencies: the U.S. Forest Service, which currently manages the land under the Department of Agriculture, and the Department of the Interior, which would take over responsibility after the transfer.

For most California residents, the practical impact would be small. The land shifts from general national forest management to tribal trust status, meaning the tribe gains greater control over how it is used. Existing roads and easements on the property are protected, so public access to those specific areas would not change.
ai_headline: Federal bill would transfer 584 acres of national forest to Pit River Tribe
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2871 would move 584 acres of California national forest into federal trust for the Pit River Tribe, adding land to their reservation.
ai_sections.what: S 2871 would transfer about 584 acres of national forest land in California to the federal government to hold in trust for the Pit River Tribe, making the land part of the tribe's reservation. A survey must be completed within 180 days of the bill passing. Roughly 20 acres of roads and public rights-of-way are excluded from the transfer and remain publicly accessible, and gambling is explicitly prohibited on the land.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the Pit River Tribe and its member communities in northern California, including the XL Ranch, Big Bend, Likely, Lookout, Montgomery Creek, and Roaring Creek Rancherias. It also shifts land management responsibility from the U.S. Forest Service under the Department of Agriculture to the Department of the Interior.
ai_sections.why: The land would move from general national forest management to tribal trust status, giving the tribe greater control over how it is used. For most California residents, the practical impact would be small, as existing roads and easements are protected and public access to those areas would not change.

title: Let Experienced Pilots Fly Act
ai_summary:
This bill would raise the mandatory retirement age for commercial airline pilots from 65 to 67. Right now, federal law requires pilots flying large commercial airliners to stop flying at age 65. If this bill becomes law, those pilots could keep flying until they turn 67. For certain smaller commercial operations run by large charter-style carriers, the bill would allow pilots to fly until age 70, though those companies would have to officially choose that option and stick with it. The bill also makes clear that pilots already over 65 when the law passes can only continue or return to flying under specific conditions.

The bill includes some safeguards around health and training. Pilots over 60 would still need a first-class medical certificate, renewed every six months. The FAA could also require more frequent medical checks for older pilots if new research shows it is needed for safety. All pilots would still have to go through the same FAA-approved training programs regardless of age.

This bill mainly affects commercial airline pilots, the airlines that employ them, and air travelers. Pilots nearing 65 who want to keep working would have more time in their careers. Airlines dealing with pilot shortages could retain experienced workers longer. Pilot unions would need to be part of any changes to labor contracts that result from the new rules. Passengers would fly with older pilots on average, though supporters and critics disagree about what that means for safety.
ai_headline: Senate bill would let airline pilots fly past age 65
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4452 would raise the mandatory retirement age for commercial airline pilots from 65 to 67, with 70 allowed for some charter-style carriers.
ai_sections.what: S 4452 would raise the mandatory retirement age for commercial airline pilots from 65 to 67. For certain smaller commercial operations run by large charter-style carriers, pilots could fly until age 70 if the company formally opts into that provision. Pilots already over 65 when the law takes effect could only continue or return to flying under specific conditions.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects commercial airline pilots nearing or past age 65, the airlines that employ them, and air travelers. Pilot unions would need to be involved in any resulting changes to labor contracts.
ai_sections.why: Pilots nearing 65 who want to keep working would have more time in their careers, and airlines facing pilot shortages could retain experienced workers longer. Passengers would fly with older pilots on average, though supporters and critics disagree on what that means for safety.

title: Albuquerque Indian School Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would transfer about 9.89 acres of federal land in Albuquerque, New Mexico, to the U.S. government to hold in trust for the benefit of 19 Native American Pueblos in New Mexico. The land was historically part of the Albuquerque Indian School, a federal boarding school, and is currently managed by the General Services Administration. The transfer must happen within 90 days after the bill becomes law, once any federal agencies currently using the property have moved out. Once transferred, the land would be treated as tribal trust land, meaning the federal government holds the title but the Pueblos control how it is used.

The 19 Pueblos named in the bill would be allowed to use the land for education, health care, cultural activities, business, and economic development. Any existing utility agreements, easements, or other legal arrangements already attached to the property would stay in place. The federal government also keeps a limited right-of-way on one portion of the land to allow retrieval or relocation of any remaining federal property. Gambling of any kind is explicitly prohibited on the transferred land.

This bill primarily affects the 19 named Pueblo tribes, who would gain access to land with historical significance to their communities. It also affects the General Services Administration, which currently manages the property and would give up control. Non-tribal residents of Albuquerque and Bernalillo County would see a small parcel of federal land converted to tribal trust land, which changes how local and state jurisdiction applies to that property.
ai_headline: Federal land from historic Albuquerque Indian School set for transfer to 19 Pueblos
last_action_text: Received in the Senate.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6162 transfers 9.89 acres of former Albuquerque Indian School land to federal trust for 19 New Mexico Pueblos within 90 days of enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 6162 would transfer approximately 9.89 acres of federal land in Albuquerque, New Mexico, currently managed by the General Services Administration, to the U.S. government to hold in trust for 19 Native American Pueblos. The land was historically part of the Albuquerque Indian School, a federal boarding school. The transfer must occur within 90 days of enactment, after any federal agencies using the property have vacated, and gambling is explicitly prohibited on the land.
ai_sections.who: The 19 named Pueblo tribes are the primary parties affected, gaining use of historically significant land for education, health care, cultural activities, and economic development. The General Services Administration, which currently manages the property, would relinquish control, and non-tribal residents of Albuquerque and Bernalillo County would see the parcel shift from federal to tribal trust jurisdiction.
ai_sections.why: Converting the land to tribal trust status changes how local and state jurisdiction applies to that property, affecting the legal framework governing the area. Existing utility agreements, easements, and other legal arrangements tied to the property would remain in place, and the federal government retains a limited right-of-way to retrieve or relocate any remaining federal property.

title: Safe Cloud Storage Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with a specific legal problem that law enforcement agencies face when investigating crimes against children. Right now, companies that provide cloud storage services are sometimes hesitant to store illegal images of child abuse on behalf of police and prosecutors, because simply possessing that material could expose the company to criminal or civil liability. The Safe Cloud Storage Act would protect approved vendors from that legal risk, as long as they are operating under a contract with a law enforcement or prosecutorial agency and following strict rules.

To qualify for this protection, a storage company must meet serious security requirements. These include using strong encryption, limiting which employees can access the material, following federal cybersecurity standards set by the National Institute of Standards and Technology, and passing an independent cybersecurity audit every year. The stored evidence must stay within the United States. If a law enforcement contract falls apart for any reason, the company must notify the Justice Department and keep the evidence safe until it is properly transferred to another agency. The liability protection does not apply if the company acts negligently, recklessly, or with bad intent.

This bill primarily affects two groups. First, it affects private technology and cloud storage companies that might contract with police departments or prosecutors to store digital evidence in child exploitation cases. Second, it affects law enforcement and prosecutorial agencies at the federal, state, and local levels that need secure, long-term storage for this type of evidence to build and maintain criminal cases. Everyday citizens are not directly regulated by the bill, but could be indirectly affected if it makes it easier to prosecute these crimes.
ai_headline: Safe Cloud Storage Act shields vendors holding child abuse evidence for police
last_action_text: Held at the desk.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3023 lets approved cloud vendors store child abuse evidence for law enforcement without criminal or civil liability, if strict security rules are met.
ai_sections.what: S 3023 would protect cloud storage companies from criminal and civil liability when storing illegal child abuse images on behalf of law enforcement or prosecutors. To qualify, vendors must use strong encryption, restrict employee access, meet federal cybersecurity standards set by the National Institute of Standards and Technology, and pass an independent cybersecurity audit every year. Evidence must remain within the United States, and if a contract ends, the company must notify the Justice Department and preserve the material until it is transferred to another agency.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects private cloud storage and technology companies that contract with police or prosecutors, and federal, state, and local law enforcement and prosecutorial agencies that need long-term digital evidence storage. Everyday citizens are not directly regulated but could be indirectly affected if the bill makes these crimes easier to prosecute.
ai_sections.why: Cloud storage companies currently risk criminal or civil liability simply by possessing this material, even when assisting law enforcement, which can make them unwilling to provide storage services. Without willing vendors, agencies may struggle to securely store digital evidence needed to build and maintain child exploitation cases. The liability shield does not apply if a company acts negligently, recklessly, or with bad intent.

title: Scientific Integrity Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require every federal agency that funds, conducts, or oversees scientific research to create and follow a written scientific integrity policy. Each agency would have 90 days after the bill becomes law to put that policy in place and get it approved by the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. The policies must prohibit things like suppressing research findings, pressuring scientists to change their results for political reasons, or retaliating against researchers who refuse to do so. Each agency would also have to hire a Scientific Integrity Officer, a career employee with a science background, to oversee and enforce these rules.

The bill also sets rules about transparency and accountability. Agencies must publish their policies online, train employees on the rules, and report annually on how many complaints were filed and what happened with them. If someone outside the official process overrules the Scientific Integrity Officer on a dispute, the agency must report that to Congress within 30 days. Every five years, each agency's policy must be reviewed and approved again. Two years after passage, the Government Accountability Office would also conduct an independent review of how well agencies are following the rules.

The people most directly affected are federal scientists, engineers, and contractors who work on government-funded research, as well as the agencies that employ or fund them, such as NASA, the EPA, the NIH, and others. Indirectly, it affects anyone who relies on government science to inform public policy, which includes most Americans, since federal research shapes decisions about health, the environment, and national security.
ai_headline: Federal agencies would be required to adopt written scientific integrity policies
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4545 would require every federal research agency to adopt a scientific integrity policy within 90 days and hire an enforcement officer.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 4545 would require every federal agency that funds, conducts, or oversees scientific research to create a written scientific integrity policy within 90 days of enactment and have it approved by the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. These policies must prohibit suppressing findings, pressuring scientists to alter results for political reasons, and retaliating against researchers who refuse. Agencies must publish policies online, train employees, and report annually on complaints filed and their outcomes.
ai_sections.who: Federal scientists, engineers, and contractors working on government-funded research are most directly affected, along with the agencies that employ or fund them, such as NASA, the EPA, and the NIH. Anyone who relies on government science to shape public policy on health, the environment, or national security is indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: If an official outside the formal process overrules a Scientific Integrity Officer, the agency must notify Congress within 30 days, creating a new oversight mechanism over internal disputes. Two years after passage, the Government Accountability Office would conduct an independent review of agency compliance, and each agency policy must be renewed every five years.

title: STRIDE Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the U.S. Secretary of State to work with allied countries that make or design computer chips to build a shared system for keeping advanced semiconductor technology out of the hands of China and other adversaries. The idea is that U.S. export rules work better when other major chip-making nations, such as Japan, the Netherlands, and South Korea, follow similar rules at the same time. The bill sets specific goals for that coordination, including matching up rules on manufacturing equipment, design software, raw materials, and repair services for chip-making tools.

The bill also creates a consequence system for allies that do not cooperate enough. If the Secretary of State decides a partner country is not doing its part to stop chip technology from reaching restricted nations, the government must notify Congress within 30 days and convene a review board within 21 days to come up with a response. That response could include expanding U.S. export restrictions so they apply to products made in the non-cooperating country using American technology, a tool known as the Foreign Direct Product Rule, or adding companies in that country to a restricted trade list.

This bill would primarily affect U.S. diplomats and trade officials who manage export controls, as well as foreign governments that produce chip-making technology. American semiconductor companies and their overseas suppliers could also be affected if new export restrictions are put in place against non-cooperating countries. Everyday consumers are not directly targeted by the bill, though the chip industry touches many products people use daily, from phones to cars.
ai_headline: U.S. bill pushes allies to jointly block chip tech from China
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 44 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6058 would coordinate U.S. semiconductor export rules with chip-producing allies and penalize countries that don't comply.
ai_sections.what: HR 6058 directs the Secretary of State to build a shared export-control system with allied chip-producing nations, including Japan, the Netherlands, and South Korea. The coordination would cover manufacturing equipment, design software, raw materials, and repair services for chip-making tools. Countries that do not cooperate could face expanded U.S. export restrictions or be added to a restricted trade list.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects U.S. diplomats and trade officials who manage export controls, as well as foreign governments that produce chip-making technology. American semiconductor companies and their overseas suppliers could also be affected if new export restrictions are imposed against non-cooperating countries.
ai_sections.why: If an ally is found to be under-cooperating, the government must notify Congress within 30 days and convene a review board within 21 days to determine a response. Possible responses include applying the Foreign Direct Product Rule to goods made in non-cooperating countries using American technology, which could disrupt existing trade relationships in the global chip supply chain.

title: COP Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes it a federal crime to threaten a minor with the distribution of child sexual abuse material. Specifically, it targets a tactic used by online predators called "sextortion," where someone threatens to share or release sexual images of a child unless that child produces and sends more sexual content. The bill adds this type of threat itself as an illegal act under existing federal law, even if no images are actually shared.

Before this bill, federal law already banned producing, distributing, or possessing child sexual abuse material. This bill fills a gap by criminalizing the threat to distribute such material when that threat is used to pressure a child into creating more. It covers both real minors and people the person making the threat believes are minors.

This bill most directly affects anyone who uses these kinds of threats to manipulate or coerce children online, making that behavior a federal offense with serious penalties. It also affects minors who are targeted by online predators, as well as law enforcement, who would gain a clearer legal tool to prosecute this specific behavior. Parents and guardians of children who use the internet may also want to be aware of this law.
ai_headline: Federal bill makes sextortion threats against minors a standalone crime
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 3704 makes it a federal crime to threaten a minor with releasing sexual images to pressure them into producing more.
ai_sections.what: S 3704 makes it a federal crime to threaten a minor with the distribution of child sexual abuse material, even if no images are actually shared. The bill targets sextortion, a tactic where predators threaten to release sexual images of a child unless that child produces and sends more sexual content. It covers threats made against real minors and against people the offender believes are minors.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects online predators who use these threats to coerce children, making that behavior a federal offense. It also affects targeted minors, law enforcement, and parents or guardians of children who use the internet.
ai_sections.why: Federal law already criminalized producing, distributing, and possessing child sexual abuse material, but did not explicitly cover the act of threatening to distribute it as a coercive tool. This bill closes that gap, giving law enforcement a clearer legal basis to prosecute this specific behavior.

title: Strength in Diversity Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal grant program called the Strength in Diversity Program, run by the U.S. Department of Education. School districts, state education agencies, and regional education groups could apply for two types of grants: planning grants (up to one year) to study and design diversity strategies, and implementation grants (up to three years, possibly extended) to actually carry them out. The grants would be awarded competitively, meaning applicants have to make a case for why their plan would work. The bill authorizes funding starting in 2027 and running for six years, though the exact dollar amount is not specified in the bill itself.

The money could be used for things like redrawing school attendance boundaries, creating new school assignment systems, improving transportation so students can attend schools outside their immediate neighborhood, recruiting more diverse teachers, and building programs that bring students from different backgrounds together. Applicants must show they have support from families and communities, and they must report annually on their progress. The bill also includes a clear statement that the federal government cannot control what schools teach or how they run their day-to-day operations.

This bill would primarily affect public school students, parents, and school administrators in districts that currently have significant racial or economic segregation and achievement gaps between student groups. Eligible school districts could receive federal dollars to redesign how students are assigned to schools. Students in lower-income households or in heavily segregated schools are the main intended beneficiaries, as the goal is to give them greater access to a wider range of schools.
ai_headline: Federal diversity grant program targets segregated school districts
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

Grant funds could be used for redrawing school boundaries, redesigning student assignment systems, expanding transportation, recruiting diverse teachers, and creating cross-background programs. Applicants must demonstrate community and family support and submit annual progress reports.

Students from lower-income households and heavily segregated schools are the primary intended population.

title: CRISIS Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new pathway for certain Russian scientists and engineers to get permanent U.S. residency. Specifically, it allows Russian citizens who have earned a doctoral degree in a STEM field, such as artificial intelligence, semiconductors, cybersecurity, or space technology, to apply for special immigrant status. They do not need a job offer from a U.S. employer to qualify. The program would run for four years, from 2026 through 2029, with a cap of 3,000 people per year. Their spouses and children could also come with them.

Applicants would have to pass background checks and go through security vetting similar to what refugees face when entering the United States. The government would collect fingerprints and other personal information, and decisions on applications would be made within 90 days of receiving all required documents. These immigrants would not count against the normal yearly limits that apply to most green card categories.

This bill would directly affect Russian nationals with advanced STEM degrees who want to live and work permanently in the United States. It could also affect U.S. universities, tech companies, and research institutions that might benefit from access to this pool of highly trained workers. More broadly, the bill is framed around national competition with Russia, aiming to attract scientific talent that might otherwise remain there or go elsewhere.
ai_headline: Bill would admit up to 3,000 Russian STEM PhDs per year without a job offer
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3536 offers Russian STEM doctoral graduates a direct path to U.S. permanent residency, capped at 3,000 per year from 2026 to 2029.
ai_sections.what: HR 3536 creates a special immigrant status for Russian citizens who hold doctoral degrees in STEM fields including artificial intelligence, semiconductors, cybersecurity, and space technology. No job offer from a U.S. employer is required to apply. The program runs from 2026 through 2029, with a cap of 3,000 people per year, and qualifying immigrants would not count against standard green card limits.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects Russian nationals with advanced STEM degrees seeking permanent U.S. residency, along with their spouses and children. U.S. universities, tech companies, and research institutions would also be affected as potential destinations for this talent pool.
ai_sections.why: The bill is framed around national competition with Russia, aiming to draw scientific talent that might otherwise stay there or move to other countries. Applicants must pass background checks and security vetting similar to the refugee screening process, with decisions issued within 90 days of a completed application.

title: Returning Illegals over Turmoil Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets non-citizens who are convicted of certain offenses during periods of civil unrest or declared emergencies. Specifically, it makes a non-citizen deportable if they are convicted of assaulting or attempting to assault a police officer or military member, participating in a riot, inciting violence, or destroying government property such as buildings, vehicles, or monuments. The bill applies to undocumented immigrants, DACA recipients, and legal permanent residents (green card holders). Once removed under this law, a person would be permanently banned from returning to the United States.

The bill also removes nearly all legal options a person might normally use to fight deportation. Forms of protection like asylum, cancellation of removal, and withholding of removal would not be available to someone removed under this law. People detained under it would be held without the option for release while their case is pending. During any declared national, state, or local emergency, enforcement becomes mandatory and the government could use a faster deportation process that offers fewer legal protections.

This bill would most directly affect non-citizens living in the United States, including green card holders and DACA recipients, who are convicted of riot-related or assault-related offenses. It does not apply to U.S. citizens. Family members of affected individuals could also feel indirect consequences if a relative is deported and permanently barred from reentry.
ai_headline: Bill would permanently bar non-citizens convicted of riot offenses from U.S.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3859 makes non-citizens deportable for riot or assault convictions and permanently bans their reentry, stripping most legal defenses.
ai_sections.what: HR 3859 makes non-citizens deportable if convicted of assaulting a police officer or military member, participating in a riot, inciting violence, or destroying government property. Anyone removed under this law would face a permanent ban from returning to the United States. During declared national, state, or local emergencies, enforcement becomes mandatory and a faster deportation process with fewer legal protections applies.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to undocumented immigrants, DACA recipients, and legal permanent residents. U.S. citizens are not affected, but family members of deported individuals may face indirect consequences from permanent separation.
ai_sections.why: People subject to removal under this bill would lose access to asylum, cancellation of removal, and withholding of removal, and would be held without the possibility of release while their case is pending. The permanent reentry ban means those removed would have no legal pathway to return to the United States.

title: America First Citizenship and Allegiance Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make the process of becoming a U.S. citizen through naturalization stricter in several ways. First, it raises the bar on the civics and English tests that applicants must pass. Instead of just knowing the basics, applicants would need to score at least 80 percent correct, with at least half the questions focused on the Constitution, Declaration of Independence, Bill of Rights, and the oath of allegiance. The test would be given in English. Before taking the oath of citizenship, each applicant would also have to sit through formal instruction explaining exactly what the oath means and then sign a statement confirming they understood it and are taking it willingly.

Second, the bill creates tougher consequences for dishonesty during the naturalization process. If an applicant is caught lying, hiding information, using fake documents, or blocking background checks, their application would be automatically denied. For serious cases involving terrorism, espionage, gang or cartel ties, human trafficking, or certain other crimes, the person could be permanently banned from ever becoming a citizen. For less serious fraud, there would be a ten-year waiting period before reapplying. The bill also increases criminal penalties for naturalization fraud, with prison sentences ranging up to 25 years depending on what crimes were involved.

The bill primarily affects people currently going through or planning to apply for U.S. citizenship through naturalization. It does not change the citizenship status of anyone who is already a citizen. It also directs the government to create new study materials tied to the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence and requires annual reports to Congress tracking how the new rules are working.
ai_headline: Naturalization bill sets 80% test score floor and new fraud bans
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4547 raises the civics and English test passing score to 80%, adds fraud penalties, and can permanently bar some applicants.
ai_sections.what: S 4547 tightens naturalization requirements by raising the civics and English test passing threshold to 80 percent, with at least half the questions drawn from the Constitution, Declaration of Independence, Bill of Rights, and oath of allegiance. Applicants must also complete formal instruction on the meaning of the oath and sign a statement confirming they understood and took it willingly. The bill stiffens fraud consequences, including automatic denial for lying or falsifying documents, permanent bans for cases tied to terrorism, espionage, gangs, cartels, or human trafficking, and a ten-year waiting period for less serious fraud, while increasing prison sentences for naturalization fraud up to 25 years.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects people currently applying for or planning to apply for U.S. citizenship through naturalization. It does not change the status of anyone already holding citizenship.
ai_sections.why: Applicants who cannot reach the 80 percent threshold or who have any fraud finding on their record will face denial or lengthy delays in obtaining citizenship. People connected to serious crimes such as terrorism or human trafficking would be permanently barred from ever naturalizing.

title: Dismemberment Abortion Ban Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would make it a federal crime for a doctor to perform what it calls a "dismemberment abortion." That term refers specifically to a surgical abortion procedure, used most often in the second trimester, where instruments like forceps are used to remove a fetus piece by piece. A doctor found guilty could face a fine, up to two years in prison, or both. There is one exception: the ban does not apply if the procedure is needed to save the life of the pregnant woman due to a physical health emergency. The bill does not ban other abortion methods, such as suction-based procedures or medication abortions.

The bill also creates a path for civil lawsuits. A woman who had the procedure performed on her in violation of this law, or a parent of a minor in that situation, could sue the doctor for money damages, including three times the cost of the procedure plus potential punitive damages. The woman herself cannot be prosecuted or sued for damages under this law, only the doctor can face criminal charges.
