This bill would affect doctors who perform second-trimester surgical abortions, as well as pregnant women seeking that specific type of procedure. It would apply nationwide, in any state, because it is a federal law added to the U.S. criminal code. States may still have their own separate abortion laws that could be stricter or more permissive regarding other methods.
ai_headline: Federal bill would criminalize second-trimester surgical abortion method
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8603 would make one specific second-trimester surgical abortion method a federal crime, exposing doctors to fines or up to two years in prison.
ai_sections.what: HR 8603 would add to the federal criminal code a ban on the surgical abortion procedure in which forceps or similar instruments are used to remove a fetus piece by piece, most commonly performed in the second trimester. The ban applies nationwide regardless of state law. It does not cover suction-based procedures or medication abortions, and includes an exception when the procedure is needed to save the pregnant woman's life due to a physical health emergency.
ai_sections.who: Doctors who perform this specific second-trimester surgical abortion procedure could face criminal charges and civil lawsuits. Pregnant women seeking the procedure cannot be prosecuted or sued under this bill, though their access to this particular method would be affected in every state.
ai_sections.why: A doctor found guilty could face a fine, up to two years in prison, or both. A woman on whom the procedure was performed in violation of the law, or a parent of a minor in that situation, could sue the doctor for damages including three times the cost of the procedure plus potential punitive damages.
ai_sections.cost: A doctor found liable in a civil suit could owe the patient damages equal to three times the cost of the procedure, plus potential punitive damages.
costChips: Triple the procedure cost in damages

title: John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill updates and strengthens the Voting Rights Act of 1965. It changes the rules for which states and local governments must get federal approval before changing their voting laws, a process called "preclearance." Under the old rules, a fixed list of states had to get this approval. Under this bill, any state or local government with 10 or more voting rights violations in the past 25 years would need preclearance. It also creates a new category where certain specific types of voting changes, like reducing polling locations, adding stricter ID requirements, or cutting multilingual voting materials, trigger a preclearance requirement in areas with large minority populations, no matter where in the country they are located.

The bill also makes it easier to challenge voting rules in court. It spells out a clearer legal test for proving that a voting rule unfairly burdens minority voters, even without proving the government intended to discriminate. It lists specific things courts should and should not consider when deciding these cases. It also makes it easier for courts to quickly block a voting rule while a lawsuit is ongoing, and it limits a state's ability to use an upcoming election as a reason to keep a challenged rule in place.

The bill affects state and local governments across the entire country, not just those in the South as older versions of the law focused on. It also affects voters, particularly racial minorities and people who speak languages other than English, by giving them more tools to challenge voting rules they believe are unfair. Additionally, it requires governments to publicly post detailed information about any voting rule changes within 48 hours, and to share data about polling place resources before each federal election.
ai_headline: Senate bill rewrites preclearance rules to cover any state with repeat voting violations
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary. (text: CR S4821)

ai_sections.tldr: S 2523 updates the Voting Rights Act so any government with 10+ violations in 25 years needs federal approval before changing voting laws.
ai_sections.what: S 2523 updates the Voting Rights Act of 1965 by replacing a fixed list of states subject to preclearance with a conduct-based trigger: any state or local government with 10 or more voting rights violations in the past 25 years must get federal approval before changing its voting laws. Certain specific changes — such as reducing polling locations, adding stricter ID requirements, or cutting multilingual voting materials — require preclearance in areas with large minority populations anywhere in the country. Governments must also publicly post details of any voting rule changes within 48 hours and share polling place resource data before each federal election.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects state and local governments across the entire country, not only those previously covered under older versions of the law. Racial minorities and voters who speak languages other than English are directly affected, as the bill provides them with additional legal tools to challenge voting rules.
ai_sections.why: The bill establishes a clearer legal test for proving a voting rule unfairly burdens minority voters without requiring proof of intentional discrimination, and it makes it easier for courts to quickly block a challenged rule while a lawsuit is ongoing. It also limits a state's ability to use an upcoming election as justification for keeping a challenged voting rule in place.

title: Geothermal Gold Book Development Act
ai_summary:
This bill tells the Department of the Interior to create an official guidebook, called the "Gold Book," that explains the standard rules and steps for leasing and getting permits to develop geothermal energy on federal lands. The department would have one year to figure out what those procedures should be, and then 180 days after that to publish the finished guide. Before publishing it, officials must talk to other federal agencies and outside experts, including energy developers. The guide would then be reviewed at least every five years and updated as needed.

Geothermal energy comes from heat naturally stored underground. Right now, companies wanting to tap that energy on federal land have to navigate a leasing and permitting process that can vary depending on which local office they deal with. This bill aims to make that process more consistent and predictable by putting the rules in one clear, standardized place that both government field offices and private developers can follow.

The people most directly affected are companies and developers who want to build geothermal power projects on land owned by the federal government, as well as Bureau of Land Management staff who review and approve those projects. More broadly, it could affect communities near federal lands where geothermal development might happen, since the guide is also meant to ensure environmental responsibility is built into the process from the start.
ai_headline: Federal lands geothermal leasing to get standardized rulebook under new bill
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 573.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5617 directs Interior to publish a standard geothermal leasing guide for federal lands within 18 months.
ai_sections.what: HR 5617 requires the Department of the Interior to create an official guide, called the "Gold Book," that standardizes leasing and permitting procedures for geothermal energy development on federal lands. The department has one year to establish the procedures and 180 days after that to publish the guide. The guide must be reviewed at least every five years and updated as needed.
ai_sections.who: Companies and developers seeking to build geothermal projects on federal land are directly affected, as are Bureau of Land Management staff who review and approve those projects. Communities near federal lands where geothermal development could occur are also affected, as environmental responsibility is built into the process.
ai_sections.why: Currently, the leasing and permitting process for geothermal development on federal land can vary depending on which local office handles it. The bill would place all rules in one standardized guide, making the process more consistent and predictable for both government field offices and private developers.

title: Carried Interest Fairness Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes how certain investment managers pay taxes on a type of earnings called "carried interest." Right now, fund managers at private equity firms, hedge funds, and venture capital funds often receive a share of their fund's profits as compensation. Under current law, that share is taxed at the lower capital gains tax rate, even though the managers earned it by doing their jobs rather than by investing their own money. This bill would require that income to be taxed as ordinary income instead, which is typically taxed at a higher rate. It would also count that income toward self-employment taxes.

The bill draws a distinction between money a fund manager actually invested themselves and money they earned through their services. If a manager put in their own cash and earned returns on that real investment, those returns could still qualify for lower capital gains tax rates. But the portion of profits they earn as a reward for managing the fund, without putting their own money at risk, would be taxed like a regular paycheck. The bill also sets up a stiff 40 percent penalty for managers who try to get around these new rules.

This bill primarily affects general partners and managers at private equity funds, hedge funds, venture capital firms, and similar investment vehicles. Ordinary investors who simply put money into these funds would not be directly affected. The bill does not impact typical workers, small business owners, or everyday taxpayers. It is aimed specifically at a narrow group of high-earning financial professionals who currently benefit from a tax treatment that critics call a loophole and supporters call appropriate for risk-based compensation.
ai_headline: House bill would end lower tax rate on fund managers' carried interest pay
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1091 would tax carried interest as ordinary income, not capital gains, and add a 40% penalty for evasion.
ai_sections.what: HR 1091 would require fund managers to pay ordinary income tax rates on carried interest, the share of fund profits they receive as compensation for managing the fund. Under current law, that income is taxed at the lower capital gains rate. The bill would also subject carried interest to self-employment taxes.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects general partners and managers at private equity funds, hedge funds, venture capital firms, and similar investment vehicles. Ordinary investors who put money into these funds would not be directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Fund managers who earn profits through their own invested capital, rather than through services rendered, could still qualify for capital gains tax rates. Managers who earn profit shares without putting their own money at risk would instead be taxed at the higher ordinary income rate, and those who attempt to circumvent the new rules would face a 40 percent penalty.
ai_sections.cost: The bill imposes a 40 percent penalty on fund managers who attempt to get around its new tax rules.

title: NASA Reauthorization Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill reauthorizes NASA, meaning it sets goals and approves a budget for the agency to keep operating. For 2026, it would authorize about $24.4 billion for NASA across areas like space exploration, science, aeronautics, and education. The bill supports continuing the Artemis program, which aims to land astronauts on the Moon again and eventually send people to Mars. It also addresses the future of the International Space Station, which is planned to be safely brought out of orbit and burned up over the ocean by around 2031. NASA would be directed to work with private companies on things like new spacesuits, commercial space stations, and lunar landing systems.

The bill affects a wide range of people and organizations. NASA employees and astronauts are directly impacted, as the bill shapes their missions and work priorities. Private aerospace companies stand to gain contracts for things like building commercial space stations, lunar landers, and deorbit vehicles for the ISS. Universities and research institutions are included through STEM education funding and research partnerships. The bill also touches on everyday life through sections on using NASA technology to help fight wildfires and improve drone traffic management in U.S. airspace.

A few less obvious pieces are also included. The bill directs NASA to help develop a standardized time system for the Moon, similar to how we use time zones on Earth, to support future missions. It calls for research into hypersonic aircraft, advanced air travel like flying taxis, and hydrogen-powered planes. There is also a restriction that blocks federal money from going toward certain space activities involving China. Overall, the bill is primarily about keeping NASA funded, focused, and competitive on the global stage.
ai_headline: House bill sets $24.4B NASA budget and backs Moon-to-Mars push
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 37 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7273 would authorize about $24.4B for NASA in 2026, funding Artemis Moon missions, ISS deorbit, and commercial space partnerships.
ai_sections.what: HR 7273 reauthorizes NASA by setting agency goals and approving a budget to keep it operating. The bill supports the Artemis program, which aims to return astronauts to the Moon and eventually send people to Mars, and directs NASA to partner with private companies on spacesuits, commercial space stations, and lunar landing systems. It also addresses wildfire technology, drone traffic management, hypersonic aircraft research, hydrogen-powered planes, and the development of a standardized lunar time system.
ai_sections.who: NASA employees, astronauts, private aerospace companies, universities, and research institutions are directly affected through mission priorities, contracts, and education funding. The bill also restricts federal money from going toward certain space activities involving China.
ai_sections.why: The International Space Station is planned to be safely brought out of orbit and burned up over the ocean by around 2031, and the bill directs NASA to manage that transition. Without reauthorization, NASA would lack a defined budget and set of priorities for 2026 and beyond.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize approximately $24.4 billion for NASA for 2026, covering space exploration, science, aeronautics, and education.

title: Forever Chemical Regulation and Accountability Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill, called the Forever Chemical Regulation and Accountability Act of 2026, targets a group of man-made chemicals known as PFAS, or "forever chemicals." These chemicals are found in many everyday products like nonstick cookware, water-resistant clothing, food packaging, and firefighting foam. They are called forever chemicals because they do not break down naturally and can build up in the environment and in people's bodies over time. The bill would require companies to stop making and using these chemicals for non-essential purposes within 10 years, and it would ban any detectable release of these chemicals into the environment after that same deadline. Some specific products, like carpets, food packaging, cosmetics, and children's products containing PFAS, would face earlier bans ranging from 1 to 5 years after the bill becomes law.

The bill sets up a process to figure out which uses of PFAS chemicals are truly necessary, meaning no safer option exists and the use is critical for health or safety. The National Academies of Sciences would review the science and advise the EPA on which uses qualify as essential. Companies would also have to report how much of these chemicals they use, where they release them, and what alternatives might exist. Federal agencies would be directed to stop buying products that contain PFAS whenever possible.

This bill would affect a wide range of people and groups. Manufacturers and businesses that currently use PFAS in their products, from clothing makers to food packagers to chemical producers, would need to find alternatives or stop using these chemicals. Ordinary consumers could see product changes in items like waterproof jackets, nonstick pans, and packaged foods. Communities near industrial sites that have experienced PFAS contamination in their water or soil would also be affected, as the bill includes provisions for cleanup research and gives everyday citizens the right to sue companies or the EPA if the law is not being followed.
ai_headline: Senate bill would ban most PFAS chemicals within 10 years
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works. (text: CR S1370-1380)

ai_sections.tldr: S 4153 would phase out PFAS "forever chemicals" within 10 years and ban all environmental releases after that deadline.
ai_sections.what: The Forever Chemical Regulation and Accountability Act of 2026 would require companies to stop making and using PFAS chemicals for non-essential purposes within 10 years and ban any detectable release of these chemicals into the environment after that same deadline. Products like carpets, food packaging, cosmetics, and children's items containing PFAS would face earlier bans ranging from 1 to 5 years after enactment. The National Academies of Sciences would advise the EPA on which uses qualify as truly essential, meaning no safer alternative exists and the use is critical for health or safety.
ai_sections.who: Manufacturers and businesses currently using PFAS, including clothing makers, food packagers, and chemical producers, would need to find alternatives or stop using these chemicals. Consumers could see changes in everyday products such as waterproof jackets, nonstick pans, and packaged foods, and communities near contaminated industrial sites would be affected by new cleanup research provisions.
ai_sections.why: PFAS chemicals do not break down naturally and accumulate in the environment and in human bodies over time. Citizens would gain the right to sue companies or the EPA if the law is not followed, and federal agencies would be directed to stop purchasing PFAS-containing products whenever possible.

title: Geothermal Royalty Reform Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how companies pay royalties when they use geothermal energy on federal land to generate electricity. Right now, the law calculates royalties based on overall production from a geothermal lease. This bill would require those royalties to be calculated separately for each individual power plant using that geothermal resource, rather than lumping all production together under one lease.

The practical effect is that if a company operates multiple power plants drawing from the same leased geothermal area, each plant would have its own royalty calculation. This could change how much companies owe the federal government depending on how production is spread across their facilities.

The people most directly affected are companies that own and operate geothermal power plants on federal land, particularly in western states like Utah, Nevada, and California where most U.S. geothermal energy is produced. The federal government and, by extension, taxpayers are also affected, since royalties from federal land leases go into the U.S. Treasury and are often shared with the states where the resources are located.
ai_headline: Federal geothermal royalties would shift to a per-plant calculation
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 575.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5638 would require geothermal royalties on federal land to be calculated per power plant rather than per lease.
ai_sections.what: HR 5638 changes how royalties are calculated when companies use geothermal energy on federal land to generate electricity. Under current law, royalties are based on total production across a geothermal lease. This bill would require a separate royalty calculation for each individual power plant drawing from that leased resource.
ai_sections.who: Companies that own and operate geothermal power plants on federal land are most directly affected, particularly those in Utah, Nevada, and California. The federal government and taxpayers are also affected, as are the states where these resources are located.
ai_sections.why: If a company runs multiple power plants from the same leased geothermal area, each plant would carry its own royalty obligation under this bill. Depending on how production is distributed across a company's facilities, the total amount owed to the federal government could change.
ai_sections.cost: Royalties from federal land leases flow into the U.S. Treasury and are shared with the states where the resources are located. The bill could alter how much companies owe the federal government, though the summary does not specify a dollar amount.

title: To amend title 38, United States Code, to prohibit an educational institution from withholding a transcript from an individual who pursued a course or program of education at such institution using Post-9/11 educational assistance.
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal for colleges and universities to withhold a student's academic transcript just because that student owes money to the school, as long as the student used Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits to pay for their education. Right now, many schools have a policy of holding transcripts as leverage when students have unpaid balances. This bill would remove that option for schools when the student in question used veterans' education benefits.

The bill only applies to students who used the Post-9/11 GI Bill, which is a federal program that helps pay for college and job training for veterans and some of their family members who served after September 10, 2001. If a school is approved to accept GI Bill benefits, it would have to hand over transcripts to these students even if there is an outstanding debt. The school could still try to collect the debt through other legal means, it just could not hold the transcript hostage.

This directly affects veterans and eligible dependents using Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits, as well as the colleges and universities that enroll them. For veterans, a withheld transcript can block them from transferring to another school, applying for jobs, or pursuing further education. Schools would need to update their billing and transcript release policies to comply if this bill becomes law.
ai_headline: Bill bars colleges from withholding transcripts of GI Bill veterans over unpaid debt
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5436 would ban schools from withholding transcripts from Post-9/11 GI Bill students with unpaid balances.
ai_sections.what: HR 5436 would make it illegal for colleges and universities to withhold academic transcripts from students who used Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits, even if those students have an unpaid balance with the school. Schools approved to accept GI Bill benefits would be required to release transcripts regardless of outstanding debt. Schools would retain the right to pursue unpaid balances through other legal means.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects veterans and eligible dependents who used Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits to pay for their education. It also affects the colleges and universities that enroll these students and that are approved to accept GI Bill benefits.
ai_sections.why: A withheld transcript can prevent veterans from transferring to another school, applying for jobs, or pursuing further education. Schools would need to update their billing and transcript release policies to comply with the law if it is enacted.

title: Improving Emerging Tech Opportunities for Veterans Act
ai_summary:
This bill directs the Department of Veterans Affairs to help veterans find jobs in fast-growing tech fields like artificial intelligence and semiconductor manufacturing. The VA would be required to work with businesses, schools, and nonprofits to figure out which tech industries are likely to hire a lot of people and which education programs best prepare veterans for those jobs. It would also speed up the government's approval process for those programs so veterans can enroll sooner.

The bill also updates an existing VA education program to officially include "emerging technologies" alongside "high technology," so veterans using their education benefits can apply them to newer fields that may not have fit neatly under the old definition. Information about these tech job opportunities and approved programs would need to be prominently featured on the VA's website and included in the Transition Assistance Program, which is a program that helps servicemembers prepare for civilian life when they leave the military.

This bill mainly affects veterans and active-duty servicemembers who are planning to use VA education benefits for career training, particularly those interested in tech fields. It also involves private-sector tech employers, trade schools, colleges, and nonprofits that might partner with the VA. The partnership and research requirements in the bill are set to expire on September 30, 2027, meaning they are temporary unless Congress acts to extend them.
ai_headline: VA directed to connect veterans with AI and semiconductor jobs
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7103 requires the VA to partner with businesses and schools to steer veterans into fast-growing tech fields like AI and semiconductor manufacturing.
ai_sections.what: HR 7103 directs the Department of Veterans Affairs to identify high-hiring tech industries and approve education programs that prepare veterans for those fields, with a faster approval process so veterans can enroll sooner. The bill also expands the official definition used in an existing VA education program to include "emerging technologies" alongside "high technology," allowing veterans to apply their education benefits to newer tech fields. Information about qualifying jobs and programs must appear prominently on the VA website and within the Transition Assistance Program.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects veterans and active-duty servicemembers planning to use VA education benefits for career training in tech. Private-sector tech employers, trade schools, colleges, and nonprofits that partner with the VA are also involved.
ai_sections.why: Veterans seeking careers in growing tech sectors may face delays or gaps in coverage under the current system, and the bill responds to those structural barriers by expanding definitions and speeding up approvals. The partnership and research requirements sunset on September 30, 2027, meaning the framework is temporary unless Congress extends it.

title: Diesel Prices Relief Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a temporary federal tax holiday on diesel fuel, lasting from whenever the law is signed until January 1, 2027. Right now, the federal government charges about 24 cents per gallon in taxes on diesel fuel. This bill would set that tax rate to zero for the remainder of 2026. It also suspends a small additional fee that normally goes into a fund for cleaning up leaking underground fuel storage tanks.

To make sure roads and infrastructure programs are not hurt, the bill requires the U.S. Treasury to replace the lost tax money out of the general federal budget. That means the Highway Trust Fund, which pays for road and bridge projects, would still receive the same amount of money it normally gets, just from a different source. So highway funding would not be cut under this plan.

The bill mainly affects businesses and workers in industries that rely heavily on diesel, such as trucking, farming, construction, and shipping. It could also affect everyday consumers indirectly, since lower diesel costs for trucking companies can sometimes lead to lower prices on goods that are transported by truck. The bill includes language stating it is Congress's intention that fuel sellers pass the savings on to customers rather than keep them as extra profit, and it gives the Treasury Department authority to enforce that goal, though the bill does not spell out specific penalties for companies that do not pass the savings along.
ai_headline: Federal diesel tax dropped to zero through end of 2026 under new House bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8772 would zero out the federal 24-cent-per-gallon diesel tax from enactment through Jan. 1, 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 8772 would set the federal diesel fuel tax rate to zero from the date of enactment through January 1, 2027. The current federal tax on diesel is approximately 24 cents per gallon. The bill also suspends a separate fee that normally funds cleanup of leaking underground fuel storage tanks.
ai_sections.who: Industries that depend heavily on diesel, including trucking, farming, construction, and shipping, are the primary direct targets of the bill. Everyday consumers could be indirectly affected if reduced diesel costs lead to lower prices on goods transported by truck.
ai_sections.why: The bill includes non-binding language stating Congress intends for fuel sellers to pass savings to customers rather than retain them as profit, though no specific penalties are established for sellers who do not comply. The Treasury Department is given authority to enforce that goal, but enforcement mechanisms are not spelled out in the bill.
ai_sections.cost: The bill requires the U.S. Treasury to replace lost diesel tax revenue from the general federal budget so that the Highway Trust Fund continues receiving its normal level of funding. No specific dollar amount for the revenue replacement is stated in the bill.
costChips: Treasury backfills lost diesel tax revenue

title: AIPAC Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes a law called the Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA), which currently requires people and groups working on behalf of foreign governments to register with the U.S. government and disclose what they are doing. Right now, FARA mainly applies to groups that directly receive money or orders from a foreign government. This bill would expand that definition to include American organizations that do not take direct foreign funding but whose lobbying work mainly benefits a foreign country's interests. Signs that a group might qualify include regularly pushing for policies that match a foreign government's goals, coordinating with foreign officials, or even having a foreign country's name in the organization's title.

If a group is reclassified under this expanded definition, it would have to register as a foreign agent and publicly report its activities, just like groups that do receive direct foreign funding. Any U.S. citizen could also file a complaint with the Department of Justice asking investigators to look into whether a group should be registered. The Attorney General would have 180 days to publish clear guidance explaining exactly how the new rules work, including real-world examples.

This bill would most directly affect American advocacy and lobbying organizations whose work strongly aligns with the interests of a specific foreign country, even if those groups are funded entirely by American donors. It could also affect individual lobbyists working for such organizations. Ordinary Americans would not face new requirements, but they would gain the ability to formally request a federal investigation if they believe a lobbying group should be registering under FARA but is not.
ai_headline: Bill would require US lobbying groups aligned with foreign interests to register as foreign agents
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8809 expands FARA to cover US advocacy groups that advance foreign government interests, even without direct foreign funding.
ai_sections.what: HR 8809 expands the Foreign Agents Registration Act to cover American organizations whose lobbying work mainly benefits a foreign country, even if those groups receive no direct foreign funding. Indicators that could trigger registration include regularly pushing for policies that match a foreign government's goals, coordinating with foreign officials, or having a foreign country's name in the organization's title. Groups reclassified under the new definition would have to register as foreign agents and publicly report their activities.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects American advocacy and lobbying organizations whose work strongly aligns with the interests of a specific foreign country, as well as individual lobbyists working for such organizations. Ordinary Americans are not subject to new requirements but gain the right to file a formal complaint with the Department of Justice requesting an investigation into whether a group should be registered.
ai_sections.why: Any US citizen could file a complaint with the Department of Justice asking investigators to look into whether a group should be registered under FARA. The Attorney General would have 180 days to publish clear guidance on how the new rules work, including real-world examples.

title: Board of Veterans Appeals Annual Report Transparency Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill requires the Board of Veterans Appeals, which is the government body that handles veterans' benefit disputes, to add new information to its yearly public report. Specifically, the Board's chairperson would have to explain why certain cases were not resolved on time and why others were sent back for additional review, a process called a remand. For each reason identified, the report would also have to show how many cases and what percentage of cases were affected by that reason.

The Board of Veterans Appeals handles thousands of cases each year from veterans who are appealing denied or reduced benefits claims. When cases drag on too long or get kicked back without a final decision, veterans can wait years for an answer. This bill would require the Board to publicly explain what is causing those delays and remands, rather than just reporting that they happened.

This bill directly affects veterans and their families who have pending or future benefit appeals. It also affects the staff and leadership of the Board of Veterans Appeals, who would be required to analyze and publish this new information. The general public and members of Congress would also have access to this data, which could be used to evaluate how well the system is working.
ai_headline: Veterans appeals board would have to explain delays in annual report
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6698 requires the Board of Veterans Appeals to publicly explain causes of delayed and remanded cases in its yearly report.
ai_sections.what: HR 6698 requires the Board of Veterans Appeals to add new information to its annual public report. The Board's chairperson would have to explain why certain cases were not resolved on time and why others were remanded, meaning sent back for additional review. For each reason identified, the report must also show the number and percentage of cases affected.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects veterans and their families who have pending or future benefit appeals. It also affects the staff and leadership of the Board of Veterans Appeals, who would be responsible for analyzing and publishing the new information.
ai_sections.why: Currently the Board reports that delays and remands occur but is not required to explain the reasons behind them. This bill would make that explanatory data available to the general public and members of Congress, who could use it to evaluate how well the appeals system is working.

title: U.S. Customs and Border Protection Officer Retirement Technical Corrections Act
ai_summary:
This bill fixes a retirement benefits problem for a specific group of U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officers. Back in 2008, a law was passed giving CBP officers better retirement benefits, including higher pension payments and the ability to work past the normal mandatory retirement age. However, some officers got their job offers before the July 6, 2008 cutoff date but did not actually start working until after that date. Because of this timing gap, those officers were denied the better benefits, even though they were essentially hired under the old rules.

The bill corrects that by treating these officers as if they were already on the job on July 6, 2008, which makes them eligible for the enhanced retirement benefits. This includes higher minimum pension payments. It also covers officers who have already retired, meaning they could receive back pay adjustments to their pensions. The Department of Homeland Security would have 120 days to identify all affected officers and share their information with the Office of Personnel Management, which would then make the corrections.

The bill directly affects a relatively small group of CBP officers who fell into this specific hiring gap around 2008. It does not change retirement rules for current or future CBP hires. The Government Accountability Office would also be required to review how CBP handles retirement benefit eligibility overall and report its findings to Congress within 18 months, which could lead to broader policy improvements down the road.
ai_headline: Senate bill restores retirement benefits to CBP officers hired before 2008 deadline
last_action_text: Held at the desk.

ai_sections.tldr: S 727 grants enhanced retirement benefits to CBP officers hired before July 6, 2008 but who started work after that cutoff date.
ai_sections.what: S 727 corrects a retirement benefits gap for U.S. Customs and Border Protection officers who received job offers before July 6, 2008 but began work after that date. The bill treats those officers as if they were employed on July 6, 2008, making them eligible for enhanced pension benefits established by a 2008 law. Officers who have already retired may receive back pay adjustments to their pensions.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects a relatively small group of CBP officers who fell into the specific hiring gap around the July 6, 2008 cutoff. It does not change retirement rules for current or future CBP hires.
ai_sections.why: The Department of Homeland Security would have 120 days to identify affected officers and provide their information to the Office of Personnel Management, which would then make the necessary corrections. The Government Accountability Office would also be required to review how CBP handles retirement benefit eligibility overall and report findings to Congress within 18 months.

title: In God We Trust Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the federal government to display or inscribe the words "In God We Trust" on all federal public buildings. The Administrator of General Services, which is the agency that manages federal buildings and property, would have one year after the bill becomes law to make sure the motto appears in a prominent place on every public building under its control. "In God We Trust" is already the official national motto of the United States, established by Congress in 1956.

The bill covers what federal law calls "public buildings," which includes a wide range of government-owned or leased facilities such as courthouses, office buildings, post offices, and other structures used by federal agencies. The requirement applies to all such buildings, meaning potentially thousands of federal properties across the country would need to add a display or physical inscription if they do not already have one.

This bill would primarily affect the General Services Administration, which would be responsible for carrying out the requirement and covering any related costs. It could also affect people who work in or visit federal buildings, since they would see the motto displayed. Some people support such displays as a recognition of American tradition, while others have raised questions in the past about whether government display of religious language is appropriate under the First Amendment. This bill has been introduced and referred to a Senate committee, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on.
ai_headline: Senate bill would put national motto on all federal buildings
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2736 would require "In God We Trust" displayed on every federal public building within one year of enactment.
ai_sections.what: S 2736 would require the words "In God We Trust" to be displayed or inscribed in a prominent place on all federal public buildings. The General Services Administration would have one year after enactment to carry out the requirement. Federal public buildings include courthouses, office buildings, post offices, and other facilities owned or leased by federal agencies.
ai_sections.who: The General Services Administration would be primarily responsible for implementing the requirement across potentially thousands of federal properties. People who work in or visit federal buildings would encounter the motto on display.
ai_sections.why: Supporters view such displays as recognition of American tradition, as "In God We Trust" has been the official national motto since 1956. Others have raised First Amendment questions about whether government display of religious language is constitutionally appropriate.
ai_sections.cost: The General Services Administration would be responsible for covering any costs related to adding displays or inscriptions to federal buildings.

title: Farm and Family Relief Act
ai_summary:
This bill would send one-time cash payments to American farmers who lost money growing crops in 2025. If the government determines that a farmer's costs to grow a crop were higher than what they could expect to earn selling it, the farmer would receive a payment covering 65 percent of that gap. This applies to common crops like corn, soybeans, wheat, cotton, and rice, as well as specialty crops like fruits, vegetables, nuts, herbs, and Christmas trees. Sugar beet growers and timber industry businesses would also receive separate assistance. Individual payment amounts are capped, generally at $125,000 to $250,000 depending on how much of a person's income comes from farming, and up to $900,000 for specialty crop producers. The bill sets aside roughly $5.8 billion total for these farm payments.

The bill also delays a scheduled change to how states and the federal government split the cost of running the SNAP food assistance program, commonly known as food stamps. Under current law, states were set to take on a larger share of those costs starting in 2026 and 2027. This bill would push those dates back by about four years, which means states would not face those increased costs as soon.

Finally, the bill would cancel the tariffs put in place by several executive orders signed by the current administration, including tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico, and under the broad global tariff order. It would also create a new office within the U.S. Forest Service to help turn government-funded research into commercial products, and fund a program to expand international markets for American timber. People most affected by this bill are farmers, farm workers, timber industry workers, and lower-income households that rely on SNAP benefits.
ai_headline: Farmers facing 2025 crop losses would get one-time federal payments under HR 7206
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Agriculture, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7206 sends one-time payments to farmers who lost money on 2025 crops, delays SNAP cost shifts to states, and cancels several tariffs.
ai_sections.what: HR 7206 provides one-time cash payments to American farmers whose 2025 crop production costs exceeded expected earnings, covering 65 percent of that gap. Eligible crops include corn, soybeans, wheat, cotton, rice, fruits, vegetables, nuts, herbs, and Christmas trees, with separate assistance for sugar beet growers and timber businesses. The bill also delays increased state cost-sharing for SNAP by roughly four years and cancels tariffs established by several executive orders, including those on imports from Canada and Mexico.
ai_sections.who: Farmers, farm workers, and timber industry workers are the primary recipients of payments and trade relief under this bill. Lower-income households that depend on SNAP benefits are also affected by the delay in state cost-sharing changes.
ai_sections.why: Delaying the SNAP cost-sharing shift means states will not face higher program expenses as soon as current law requires, which could affect how states plan their budgets. Canceling the listed tariffs would change import costs on goods from Canada, Mexico, and other countries covered by the broad global tariff order.
ai_sections.cost: The bill sets aside approximately $5.8 billion total for farm payments. Individual payments are generally capped at $125,000 to $250,000 depending on farming income share, and up to $900,000 for specialty crop producers.

title: Protecting Health Care and Lowering Costs Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill does two main things. First, it would repeal certain health care provisions that were passed earlier in 2025 as part of a larger budget law (Public Law 119-21). The bill does not spell out exactly which provisions those are in its own text, but it would wipe them off the books and return the rules to what they were before that law passed. Second, the bill would make permanent a broader eligibility rule for Premium Tax Credits, which are government subsidies that help people pay for private health insurance bought through the ACA marketplace.

Right now, under current law, those tax credits are available to people earning up to 400 percent of the federal poverty level. This bill would remove that income cap entirely, meaning people earning above 400 percent of poverty could also qualify for help paying their premiums. The amount of help anyone gets is still based on a sliding scale tied to income, so higher earners would get less assistance, but they would no longer be cut off completely.

This bill affects people who buy their own health insurance through the ACA marketplace rather than getting it through a job or a government program like Medicaid or Medicare. That includes self-employed workers, part-time workers, early retirees, and others who shop for individual coverage. Repealing the earlier budget law provisions could also affect a broader group depending on what those provisions contained, but the bill itself does not describe them in detail.
ai_headline: Bill would remove income cap on ACA premium subsidies and erase 2025 budget health rules
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4849 removes the 400% poverty income cap on ACA premium tax credits and repeals unspecified 2025 budget health provisions.
ai_sections.what: HR 4849 would repeal certain health care provisions from Public Law 119-21, a budget law passed earlier in 2025, returning rules to what they were before that law. It would also make permanent the removal of the 400 percent federal poverty level income cap on Premium Tax Credits, which help people pay for private health insurance through the ACA marketplace. People above that income threshold could qualify for assistance, though the subsidy amount would still decrease on a sliding scale as income rises.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects people who buy individual health insurance through the ACA marketplace, including self-employed workers, part-time workers, and early retirees. Depending on what the repealed 2025 budget provisions contained, a broader group of people could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Removing the income cap would extend eligibility for premium assistance to people who currently receive no subsidy because their earnings exceed 400 percent of the federal poverty level. Repealing the 2025 budget law provisions would reverse changes whose specific effects the bill does not describe.

title: Restoring Food Security for American Families and Farmers Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill is a direct response to another piece of legislation that was recently passed. A larger budget bill called the reconciliation act (based on H. Con. Res. 14 in the 119th Congress) included sections numbered 10101 through 10108 that made changes to nutrition programs. This bill would erase those specific sections, putting the nutrition rules back to exactly what they were before that reconciliation bill passed.

Because the full text of those sections 10101 through 10108 is not included here, the exact changes being reversed are not spelled out in this bill itself. Based on the bill's title and the fact that it was referred to the Agriculture Committee, those original sections likely made cuts or rule changes to federal food assistance programs such as SNAP (food stamps), school meals, or similar nutrition programs. This bill would undo whatever those changes were.

The people most directly affected would be anyone who receives federal food assistance, including low-income families, children who rely on school meal programs, and elderly or disabled individuals on food benefits. Farmers and food retailers who participate in these programs could also be affected, since the programs influence how food is purchased and distributed. This bill was introduced by a large group of House members but has not yet passed, so no changes have taken effect from it.
ai_headline: House bill would restore nutrition program rules cut in reconciliation act
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6088 would repeal sections 10101–10108 of a reconciliation act to restore prior federal nutrition program rules.
ai_sections.what: HR 6088 would repeal sections 10101 through 10108 of a reconciliation act based on H. Con. Res. 14 from the 119th Congress. Those sections made changes to federal nutrition programs, and this bill would erase them entirely, returning the rules to what they were before the reconciliation act passed. The exact content of those sections is not spelled out in this bill itself.
ai_sections.who: People who receive federal food assistance, including low-income families, children who depend on school meal programs, and elderly or disabled individuals, would be most directly affected. Farmers and food retailers who participate in these programs could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: If passed, this bill would undo whatever cuts or rule changes the reconciliation act made to programs such as SNAP or school meals. The programs in question influence how food is purchased and distributed, so changes to them can affect both recipients and the businesses that participate.

title: Officer John Barnes Act
ai_summary:
This bill focuses on how quickly the federal government must respond to benefit claims filed by certain law enforcement officers or their families. Right now, there is no strict deadline for the Bureau of Justice Assistance to tell claimants whether they qualify for benefits under the Public Safety Officers' Benefits program. This bill would require the Bureau to give claimants a decision within 270 days of receiving their claim.

The Public Safety Officers' Benefits program provides financial support to the families of police officers, firefighters, and other public safety workers who are killed or seriously injured in the line of duty. The problem this bill addresses is that claimants have sometimes waited years without getting a clear answer on whether they qualify. The 270-day deadline would force the government to process these claims more quickly.

This bill mainly affects public safety officers and their families who are waiting on benefit decisions after a death or serious injury on the job. It does not change who qualifies for benefits or how much money they receive. It simply sets a time limit to make the process faster and less uncertain for people who are already dealing with very difficult circumstances.
ai_headline: Bill sets 270-day deadline for public safety benefit rulings
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1360 would require the Bureau of Justice Assistance to decide public safety benefit claims within 270 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 1360 would require the Bureau of Justice Assistance to issue a decision on Public Safety Officers' Benefits program claims within 270 days of receiving them. The bill does not change who qualifies for benefits or the amount paid. It only establishes a processing deadline where none currently exists.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects public safety officers and their families who file benefit claims after a death or serious injury on the job. This includes families of police officers, firefighters, and other public safety workers.
ai_sections.why: Without a deadline, claimants have sometimes waited years for a decision on whether they qualify for benefits. Setting a 270-day limit would reduce uncertainty for people dealing with difficult circumstances following a line-of-duty death or serious injury.

title: Officer John Barnes Act
ai_summary:
This bill sets a time limit on how long the federal government can take to decide whether someone qualifies for a specific financial benefit. Right now, there is no legal deadline for how long that decision can take. If this bill becomes law, the Bureau of Justice Assistance would have to give claimants an answer within 270 days (about nine months) of receiving their application.

The benefit involved is the Public Safety Officers' Death and Disability Benefit Program. This program pays money to the families of police officers, firefighters, and other public safety workers who are killed or permanently disabled in the line of duty. Families have sometimes waited years to find out whether they qualify, leaving them in financial uncertainty during an already difficult time.

This bill would mainly affect the surviving family members of fallen or seriously injured public safety officers who file a claim under this program, as well as the Bureau of Justice Assistance, which administers it. It does not change who qualifies for the benefit or how much money is paid out. It simply requires that applicants get a yes or no answer within a set timeframe.
ai_headline: Bill would give families of fallen officers a 270-day claim deadline
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 571 requires the Bureau of Justice Assistance to decide public safety officer benefit claims within 270 days of application.
ai_sections.what: S 571 would set a 270-day deadline for the Bureau of Justice Assistance to decide whether applicants qualify for the Public Safety Officers' Death and Disability Benefit Program. Currently, no legal deadline exists for how long that decision can take. The bill does not change eligibility requirements or payment amounts.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects surviving family members of police officers, firefighters, and other public safety workers killed or permanently disabled in the line of duty. It also affects the Bureau of Justice Assistance, which administers the program.
ai_sections.why: Without a deadline, families have sometimes waited years for a decision, creating prolonged financial uncertainty after losing or seriously injured a family member in the line of duty. Setting a fixed timeframe would require the agency to act within about nine months of receiving a claim.

title: Protecting Free Vaccines Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires health insurance plans to keep covering certain vaccines at no cost to patients through the end of 2029. Specifically, it locks in the list of vaccines that were officially recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices as of October 25, 2024. That means if any vaccine recommendations are removed or rolled back after that date, insurance plans would still have to cover those vaccines for free until January 1, 2030. This applies to private insurance plans, employer-sponsored plans, Medicare, Medicaid, and the Children's Health Insurance Program.

The bill appears to be a response to concerns that federal health agencies might remove certain vaccines from their recommended lists, which could allow insurers to start charging patients for those vaccines. Under current law, insurers generally must cover vaccines that are on the official recommended list without charging a copay or deductible. By setting a fixed date of October 25, 2024, this bill ensures that any vaccines that were free on that date stay free regardless of what happens to the official recommendations between now and 2030.

This bill would affect nearly all Americans who have health insurance, including children covered by CHIP, low-income adults on Medicaid, and seniors on Medicare. It also affects pregnant people on Medicaid by guaranteeing they can get recommended vaccines at no out-of-pocket cost. The bill does not make any vaccine mandatory and does not require anyone to get vaccinated. It only addresses what insurance plans must cover and what they can charge patients.
ai_headline: Bill locks in free vaccine coverage through 2029 under all major insurance types
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5448 requires insurers to keep covering vaccines recommended as of Oct. 25, 2024 at no cost through Jan. 1, 2030.
ai_sections.what: HR 5448 requires health insurance plans to continue covering vaccines at no cost to patients through January 1, 2030. The bill locks in the vaccine recommendations made by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices as of October 25, 2024, meaning insurers cannot begin charging for those vaccines even if recommendations are later removed. The bill does not mandate vaccination and only governs what insurers must cover and what they may charge.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects nearly all Americans with health insurance, including children covered by the Children's Health Insurance Program, low-income adults on Medicaid, seniors on Medicare, and pregnant people on Medicaid. It applies to private insurance plans and employer-sponsored plans as well.
ai_sections.why: If federal health agencies remove vaccines from official recommended lists, insurers could under current law begin charging patients for those vaccines. This bill prevents that outcome for any vaccine that was covered free as of October 25, 2024, for the period through the end of 2029.

title: 911 SAVES Act
ai_summary:
This bill is about how 911 dispatchers are officially categorized in a government job classification system called the Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) System. Right now, 911 dispatchers are not listed under "Protective Service Occupations," the same broad category that includes police officers, firefighters, and EMTs. The bill would require the Office of Management and Budget to move 911 dispatchers into that protective services category within 30 days of the bill becoming law.

The main reason this matters is that the SOC system is used to collect and analyze data about jobs across the country. How a job is classified can affect things like how workforce data is gathered, how the job is studied and understood by researchers and policymakers, and how the profession is recognized in official government records. Supporters of the change argue that 911 dispatchers do life-saving work similar to other first responders, including coaching callers through emergencies, handling hostage situations, and dealing with serious trauma on the job.

The people most directly affected are the roughly 100,000 911 dispatchers and emergency communications workers across the United States. The reclassification would not automatically change their pay or benefits, but it could influence future policy discussions, research, and funding decisions related to their profession. It could also affect how their jobs are compared to others in government data and reports.
ai_headline: Bill would reclassify 911 dispatchers as protective service workers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 637 would require OMB to move 911 dispatchers into the protective services job category within 30 days of enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 637 would require the Office of Management and Budget to reclassify 911 dispatchers under "Protective Service Occupations" in the Standard Occupational Classification System within 30 days of becoming law. Currently, 911 dispatchers are not grouped in the same broad category as police officers, firefighters, and EMTs. The SOC system is used to collect and analyze workforce data across the country.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 100,000 911 dispatchers and emergency communications workers in the United States are most directly affected. Researchers, policymakers, and government agencies that rely on SOC data would also be affected by the change.
ai_sections.why: How a job is classified in the SOC system can influence how workforce data is gathered, how the profession is studied, and how it appears in official government records. The reclassification would not automatically change pay or benefits, but could affect future policy discussions, research, and funding decisions related to the profession.

title: Tenants’ Right to Organize Act
ai_summary:
This bill focuses on the rights of renters who live in federally assisted housing, meaning people who use Housing Choice Vouchers (also called Section 8) or live in apartments built using Low-Income Housing Tax Credits (LIHTC). It gives these tenants the legal right to form tenant organizations, hold meetings, hand out flyers, knock on neighbors' doors, and speak to the media or government officials about their housing conditions. Landlords and housing agencies would be required to recognize these groups, respond in writing to their concerns within 60 days, and provide meeting space. Landlords could not punish tenants for organizing, and if a landlord takes a negative action against a tenant within 180 days of that tenant doing something protected under this bill, it would automatically be assumed to be retaliation unless the landlord can prove otherwise.

The bill also sets up an enforcement system. Tenants could file complaints with the Department of Housing and Urban Development or take landlords to court directly. HUD would have to create a formal complaint process within one year and report to Congress every three months on how many complaints were filed and how they were handled. Landlords who violate the rules in LIHTC properties could lose their tax credit status.

The people most affected are lower-income renters living in federally subsidized housing across the country. This includes voucher holders renting from private landlords and residents of affordable apartment complexes that receive tax credits. Landlords, property management companies, public housing agencies, and state housing agencies would also face new legal obligations. Nonprofit organizations would become eligible for federal grants to help train and assist tenants in organizing.
ai_headline: Bill would give federally assisted renters the right to organize
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3049 grants tenants in Section 8 and LIHTC housing legal protections to organize, with landlord retaliation presumed if action follows within 180 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 3049 would give tenants in federally assisted housing the legal right to form organizations, hold meetings, distribute materials, and speak to the media or government. Landlords would be required to recognize tenant groups, respond in writing to their concerns within 60 days, and provide meeting space. Tenants could file complaints with HUD or sue landlords directly, and HUD would have one year to create a formal complaint process and report to Congress every three months.
ai_sections.who: Lower-income renters using Housing Choice Vouchers or living in Low-Income Housing Tax Credit apartments would gain new legal protections. Landlords, property management companies, public housing agencies, state housing agencies, and nonprofit organizations would all face new obligations or become eligible for new federal grants.
ai_sections.why: Landlords who retaliate against tenants within 180 days of protected activity would be presumed to have acted unlawfully unless they can prove otherwise. LIHTC landlords who violate the rules could lose their tax credit status, and nonprofits would become eligible for federal grants to support tenant organizing.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would make federal grants available to nonprofit organizations to train and assist tenants in organizing. No specific funding amounts are stated in the summary.

title: Lowering Drug Costs for American Families Act
ai_summary:
This bill does three main things to try to lower what Americans pay for prescription drugs. First, it expands the existing program that lets the federal government negotiate drug prices directly with pharmaceutical companies. Right now Medicare can negotiate prices for up to 20 drugs per year; this bill would raise that to 50 drugs per year. It also requires negotiators to consider what other wealthy countries like Canada, France, and the United Kingdom pay for the same drugs. Importantly, the negotiated prices would not just apply to Medicare patients but could also extend to people with private health insurance, though private insurers could opt out of participating.

Second, the bill extends a penalty system called inflation rebates into the commercial insurance market. Currently, if a drug company raises its prices faster than inflation, it has to pay a rebate back to Medicare. This bill would apply a similar rule to drugs sold to people with private insurance, not just Medicare patients.

Third, starting in 2027, the bill would set a cap on how much people with private insurance have to spend out of pocket on prescription drugs each year. That limit would be $2,000 per person for prescription drugs alone. It would also cap insulin costs at $35 per month, with no deductible allowed for insulin.

This bill affects a wide range of Americans. People with private health insurance through their jobs or the individual market, Medicare patients, people with diabetes who rely on insulin, and employers who provide health coverage would all be touched by these changes. Drug manufacturers and insurance companies would also face new rules and potential financial impacts under this legislation.
ai_headline: Bill would expand drug price negotiations and cap out-of-pocket costs at $2,000
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6166 raises the Medicare drug negotiation limit from 20 to 50 drugs yearly and caps private insurance out-of-pocket drug costs at $2,000.
ai_sections.what: HR 6166 would raise the number of drugs Medicare can negotiate annually from 20 to 50, require negotiators to consider prices paid in countries like Canada, France, and the United Kingdom, and extend negotiated prices to people with private insurance. Starting in 2027, out-of-pocket prescription drug costs for people with private insurance would be capped at $2,000 per person per year, with insulin capped at $35 per month and no deductible allowed for insulin. The bill would also apply inflation rebate penalties, currently limited to Medicare, to drugs sold in the private insurance market.
ai_sections.who: People with private health insurance through employers or the individual market, Medicare patients, and people with diabetes who rely on insulin would be directly affected. Drug manufacturers and insurance companies would face new rules under this legislation, and employers who provide health coverage would also see changes.
ai_sections.why: Extending inflation rebates to the commercial market creates a financial penalty for drug companies that raise prices faster than inflation outside of Medicare. Private insurers have the option to opt out of applying negotiated Medicare drug prices to their plans, which may limit how broadly the negotiated prices take effect.
ai_sections.cost: Drug manufacturers would owe rebates to the commercial insurance market when they raise prices faster than inflation, mirroring the existing Medicare penalty. Private insurers could opt out of the negotiated pricing system, affecting how costs are distributed across the market.
costChips: Rebates owed when prices beat inflation

title: Biosimilar Red Tape Elimination Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how the FDA handles "biosimilars," which are copycat versions of expensive biological drugs like insulin or certain cancer treatments. Right now, a biosimilar has to go through an extra approval step to be labeled "interchangeable," meaning a pharmacist can swap it for the brand-name drug without the doctor writing a new prescription. This bill eliminates that extra step. Instead, once a biosimilar is approved as safe and similar enough to the original drug, it is automatically considered interchangeable.

The change would take effect 60 days after the bill becomes law. Biosimilars already on the market would also automatically get the interchangeable label at that point. The bill does protect any existing "exclusivity" deals, meaning if a biosimilar company already earned a period of market protection for being the first interchangeable version, that protection stays in place until it expires.

This bill most directly affects patients who take biological drugs, which are often among the most expensive medications available. If pharmacists can more easily substitute lower-cost biosimilars without extra steps, patients could see more competition and potentially lower prices at the pharmacy. It also affects drug manufacturers, both the companies that make brand-name biological drugs and the companies trying to bring cheaper biosimilar versions to market. The FDA would need to update its guidance documents within 18 months to reflect the new rules.
ai_headline: FDA biosimilar interchangeability rule eliminated under HR 5526
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5526 removes the FDA's extra interchangeability approval step, letting pharmacists auto-substitute biosimilars once they are approved as safe and similar.
ai_sections.what: HR 5526 eliminates the separate FDA approval step that biosimilars must currently pass to be labeled interchangeable with a brand-name biological drug. Once a biosimilar is approved as safe and sufficiently similar to the original, it is automatically considered interchangeable. The change takes effect 60 days after enactment and applies to biosimilars already on the market at that time.
ai_sections.who: Patients who take biological drugs are most directly affected, as pharmacists would be able to substitute lower-cost biosimilars without a new prescription. Brand-name biological drug manufacturers and biosimilar makers are also affected, and the FDA must update its guidance documents within 18 months.
ai_sections.why: Removing the extra approval step could increase the number of biosimilars eligible for pharmacy-level substitution, which may introduce more competition in the market for biological drugs. Existing exclusivity protections earned by biosimilar companies for being first to achieve interchangeable status remain in force until they expire.

title: RESET Act
ai_summary:
The RESET Act would ban certain online platforms from letting anyone under age 16 create or keep an account. Once the law takes effect, platforms would have to identify accounts belonging to users they know are minors, notify those users, and then delete the accounts within set deadlines. When an account is closed, the platform must delete the user's personal data, though it must first give the minor a 90-day window to request and download a copy of their own information.

The Federal Trade Commission would be in charge of enforcing this law, and breaking it would be treated the same as an unfair or deceptive business practice under existing consumer protection law. State attorneys general could also sue platforms on behalf of their residents. Importantly, the bill prevents states from passing their own separate rules on this same topic, meaning federal law would be the single standard across the country.

This bill directly affects children under 16 and their parents, as well as the companies that run social media and similar online platforms. Teens who currently have accounts on covered platforms would lose them if the platform becomes aware they are under 16. Platform companies would face legal and financial penalties for knowingly allowing underage users to maintain accounts.
ai_headline: Federal bill would ban under-16 accounts on major online platforms
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: The RESET Act would ban anyone under 16 from holding accounts on covered online platforms, with FTC enforcement and no state opt-outs.
ai_sections.what: The RESET Act would prohibit covered online platforms from allowing users under age 16 to create or maintain accounts. Platforms that become aware a user is under 16 must notify that user and delete the account within set deadlines, along with the user's personal data. Before deletion, platforms must give the minor a 90-day window to download a copy of their own information.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects children under 16 and their parents, who would lose existing accounts on covered platforms. It also affects companies operating social media and similar online platforms, which would face legal and financial penalties for knowingly allowing underage accounts.
ai_sections.why: Teens who currently hold accounts on covered platforms would have those accounts terminated once the platform becomes aware of their age. The bill also preempts state-level laws on this topic, making federal law the single national standard and removing states' ability to set their own rules.
ai_sections.cost: Violations would be treated as unfair or deceptive business practices under existing consumer protection law, exposing platforms to financial penalties under that framework.

title: To amend the Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974 to establish certain procedures for consideration of annual appropriation bills, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules Congress follows when passing its annual spending bills, which are called appropriations bills. These are the bills that fund the federal government each year. Right now, Congress often misses its deadlines and relies on temporary funding patches called continuing resolutions. This bill tries to push Congress to finish its real spending bills on time by adding two new requirements.

The first change applies to the Senate. It sets a 20-hour time limit on debate for each annual spending bill. This would prevent senators from using lengthy debate to delay or stall a vote. The second change applies to the House of Representatives. It says the House cannot take a break of more than three days during the month of July unless it has already passed all of its annual spending bills for the upcoming fiscal year, which starts October 1. Basically, lawmakers would have to stay in Washington and keep working through July until the spending bills are done.

This bill directly affects members of Congress and how they run their schedules and legislative process. Indirectly, it affects all Americans, because government spending bills fund programs like the military, federal agencies, national parks, and many other services people rely on. If Congress finished these bills on time more consistently, it could reduce uncertainty caused by government shutdown threats and last-minute temporary funding deals.
ai_headline: House and Senate face new deadlines to pass annual spending bills on time
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Rules, and in addition to the Committee on the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8380 caps Senate spending bill debate at 20 hours and bars House recesses in July until all spending bills pass.
ai_sections.what: HR 8380 changes procedural rules for passing annual federal appropriations bills. It limits Senate floor debate on each spending bill to 20 hours and prohibits the House from taking recesses longer than three days in July unless all annual spending bills for the upcoming fiscal year have already passed. The fiscal year begins October 1.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects members of Congress and how they manage their legislative schedules. Indirectly it affects all Americans, because appropriations bills fund programs including the military, federal agencies, and national parks.
ai_sections.why: Congress currently misses spending deadlines and relies on temporary funding measures called continuing resolutions. More consistent on-time passage could reduce uncertainty from government shutdown threats and last-minute temporary funding arrangements.

title: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Sexual Harassment and Assault Prevention Improvements Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill updates rules for how the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, or NOAA, handles sexual harassment and sexual assault within its workforce.
