NOAA is the federal agency that oversees weather forecasting, fisheries, and ocean research. The bill requires NOAA to collect and publicly report more detailed data on harassment and assault cases, including what happened in each case and what discipline was given. It also creates a stronger confidential reporting option, meaning someone can report an incident without it automatically starting a formal investigation, and sets clear rules for the rare situations where a victim's identity might need to be shared.

The bill adds new protections for people who work on vessels and fishing operations connected to NOAA, such as fisheries observers who monitor commercial fishing boats. If a vessel operator learns about a sexual harassment or assault incident involving one of these workers, they must now report it quickly to the Coast Guard. The bill also removes some narrow legal language that previously limited when sexual assault on a vessel could be treated as a prohibited act under federal fisheries law, making it easier to hold people accountable in those situations.

People directly affected include NOAA employees, commissioned officers in NOAA's uniformed service, fisheries observers, and crew members on vessels that work under NOAA contracts. The bill also bars anyone convicted of certain sexual offenses from serving as a NOAA commissioned officer. Overall, the bill tightens reporting requirements, strengthens victim privacy, and closes some gaps in existing law.
ai_headline: NOAA harassment rules tightened with new vessel reporting mandates
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2406 tightens NOAA harassment rules, adds vessel reporting duties, and bars sex offenders from commissioned officer roles.
ai_sections.what: HR 2406 updates how NOAA handles sexual harassment and assault cases by requiring more detailed public data reporting, including case outcomes and disciplinary actions. It creates a stronger confidential reporting option that does not automatically trigger a formal investigation. The bill also removes narrow legal language that previously limited when sexual assault on a vessel could be treated as a prohibited act under federal fisheries law.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects NOAA employees, commissioned officers, fisheries observers, and crew members on vessels under NOAA contracts. Anyone convicted of certain sexual offenses would be barred from serving as a NOAA commissioned officer.
ai_sections.why: Vessel operators connected to NOAA, such as those working with fisheries observers, must now report harassment or assault incidents to the Coast Guard quickly, adding a new layer of accountability in commercial fishing settings. Closing the gap in federal fisheries law makes it easier to hold people accountable for sexual assault occurring on vessels.

title: Federal Animal Research Accountability Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill requires research institutions that receive federal funding from the National Institutes of Health (NIH) to report more detailed information about the animals they use in their studies. Each year, these institutions would have to file a form with NIH listing how many animals they bred, housed, and used in research. The report must break down that information by species and by how much pain or distress the animals experienced during experiments, including whether pain-relieving drugs were given or withheld for scientific reasons.

Once an institution files its report, NIH would be required to post that information in a public, searchable online database within three months. This means anyone could look up how many and what kinds of animals a particular research institution is using, and under what conditions. The new reporting rules would take effect two years after the bill becomes law, giving institutions time to prepare.

This bill affects universities, hospitals, and other research organizations that use NIH funding to conduct medical or behavioral research involving animals. It also affects NIH itself, which would need to build and maintain the public database. Ordinary citizens, journalists, and watchdog groups would be able to access the data once it is posted. The bill does not ban or restrict animal research — it only requires more detailed and publicly available recordkeeping about it.
ai_headline: NIH-funded labs would face new public animal research reporting rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3295 requires NIH-funded institutions to publicly report animal research data by species and pain level in a searchable database.
ai_sections.what: HR 3295 requires research institutions receiving NIH funding to file annual reports detailing how many animals they bred, housed, and used in research. Reports must break down data by species and by the level of pain or distress animals experienced, including whether pain-relieving drugs were given or withheld. NIH would then publish this information in a public, searchable online database within three months of each filing.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects universities, hospitals, and other research organizations that use NIH funding for animal research, as well as NIH itself, which must build and maintain the database. Ordinary citizens, journalists, and watchdog groups would have access to the published data.
ai_sections.why: The new rules would make animal research data publicly accessible in a standardized, searchable format for the first time under this requirement. Institutions would have two years after enactment to prepare for the reporting obligations before they take effect.

title: Designation of English as the Official Language of the United States Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would make English the official language of the United States by writing that into federal law. It would require that official government business, including laws, regulations, publications, and public proceedings, be conducted in English. There are exceptions built in, such as situations involving public health and safety, national security, international trade, the rights of crime victims and defendants, disability education requirements, and the preservation of Native American and Native Alaskan languages. Government employees could still speak informally in other languages, as long as their actual official duties are carried out in English.

The bill also sets rules for people seeking to become U.S. citizens. It would require that naturalization ceremonies be held in English, and it directs the Department of Homeland Security to create a new standardized English language test for citizenship applicants within 180 days of the law passing. The test would be based on the idea that citizens should be able to read and generally understand documents like the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution. Exceptions to the English testing requirement would be allowed only in rare cases, such as for asylum seekers.

This bill would most directly affect people who interact with the federal government in languages other than English, including immigrants going through the naturalization process, as well as federal agencies that currently provide services or materials in multiple languages. It would also affect private and public employers, since the bill states that English-only workplace policies would be presumed to be legal under federal law. The bill was introduced in the House in March 2025 and has been referred to committee, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: House bill would make English the official U.S. language and tighten citizenship tests
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1772 would make English the official U.S. language, require English-only government business, and mandate a new citizenship language test.
ai_sections.what: HR 1772 would establish English as the official language of the United States in federal law and require that all official government business, including laws, regulations, publications, and public proceedings, be conducted in English. The Department of Homeland Security would have 180 days to develop a standardized English test for citizenship applicants, benchmarked against the ability to read and generally understand documents like the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution. Naturalization ceremonies would also be required to be held in English.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects immigrants going through the naturalization process and people who interact with the federal government in languages other than English. It also affects federal agencies that currently provide services or materials in multiple languages, as well as private and public employers, since English-only workplace policies would be presumed legal under the bill.
ai_sections.why: Federal agencies that currently offer multilingual services or materials would need to reassess or curtail those offerings. People seeking citizenship who are not yet proficient in English, along with those who rely on government services in other languages, could face new barriers under the law.

title: Offshore Parity Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would expand the offshore boundary that Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama control in the Gulf of Mexico. Right now, most U.S. states control waters up to 3 miles from their coastline, but Florida and Texas on the Gulf side already control waters out to about 9 miles (3 marine leagues). This bill would give Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama the same 9-mile limit, if those states request it within 5 years and meet certain requirements set by the federal government, such as showing they have the resources and ability to manage the area responsibly.

Within that expanded zone, the three states would gain control over oil, gas, and energy leases. They could set their own rental fees and royalties for new leases, collect that revenue directly, and take over management of existing federal leases in the area. The states would also take on the legal responsibility that comes with that control, including any costs if a company successfully sues over how a lease is handled. The bill also extends each state's authority over fisheries management out to the same 9-mile boundary, though the federal government would keep control over highly migratory fish species, endangered species, and anything tied to international agreements.

The people most directly affected are residents of Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama, along with oil and gas companies, commercial and recreational fishermen, and energy lease holders operating in that offshore zone. State governments in those three states would gain new powers and new responsibilities. The federal government, specifically the Department of the Interior, would give up some of its current management role in that area.
ai_headline: Three Gulf states would gain control over 9-mile offshore zone under new bill
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8542 would expand Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama's offshore boundary from 3 to 9 miles, matching Florida and Texas.
ai_sections.what: HR 8542 would extend the offshore boundary of Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama from 3 miles to 9 miles in the Gulf of Mexico, matching the existing limit already held by Florida and Texas on the Gulf side. The three states would gain authority over oil, gas, and energy leases in the expanded zone, including the ability to set rental fees and royalties, collect revenue directly, and take over management of existing federal leases. Each state's fisheries management authority would also expand to the 9-mile boundary, though the federal government would retain control over highly migratory species, endangered species, and matters tied to international agreements.
ai_sections.who: Residents of Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama are most directly affected, along with oil and gas companies, commercial and recreational fishermen, and energy lease holders operating in that offshore zone. The Department of the Interior would give up part of its current management role, while the three state governments would gain new powers and take on new legal and financial responsibilities.
ai_sections.why: States that choose to request expanded boundaries would assume legal liability for lease management decisions, including costs if a company successfully sues over how a lease is handled. The federal government's oversight role in that offshore area would be reduced wherever a qualifying state takes over.

title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to establish an exception for multiemployer plan participants to the requirements for automatic enrollment.
ai_summary:
This bill changes a rule about retirement savings plans. A recent law called SECURE 2.0 required most employer retirement plans to automatically enroll new workers and automatically increase their savings contributions over time. This bill would create an exception to that requirement for "multiemployer plans," which are retirement plans jointly run by a group of employers and a labor union, common in industries like construction, trucking, and entertainment.

Under this exception, multiemployer plans would no longer be required to automatically enroll workers or automatically raise their contribution rates. These plans are already governed by contracts negotiated between employers and unions, so supporters of the bill argue that automatic enrollment rules are difficult to apply to plans that work differently from standard single-employer plans. The change would apply starting with the 2025 tax year.

The people most directly affected are workers covered by union-negotiated multiemployer pension or retirement plans, along with the employers and unions that manage those plans. This includes a large number of workers in trades and industries where union contracts set the terms of retirement benefits. Workers in these plans would not automatically be enrolled in savings programs under this rule, though their unions could still negotiate enrollment terms through collective bargaining.
ai_headline: Bill would exempt union-run multiemployer plans from auto-enrollment rule
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6685 exempts multiemployer retirement plans from SECURE 2.0 auto-enrollment and auto-escalation rules starting in tax year 2025.
ai_sections.what: HR 6685 creates an exception to a SECURE 2.0 requirement that employer retirement plans automatically enroll new workers and automatically increase their contribution rates over time. The exception applies specifically to multiemployer plans, which are retirement plans jointly administered by a group of employers and a labor union. The change would take effect beginning with the 2025 tax year.
ai_sections.who: Workers covered by union-negotiated multiemployer retirement plans are most directly affected, as are the employers and unions that manage those plans. These workers are concentrated in industries such as construction, trucking, and entertainment, where union contracts govern retirement benefits.
ai_sections.why: Without this exception, multiemployer plans would be subject to the same automatic enrollment and contribution-escalation mandates as standard single-employer plans, which supporters argue are difficult to apply to plans governed by collective bargaining contracts. Workers in affected plans would not be automatically enrolled in savings programs under this rule, though unions could still negotiate enrollment terms through collective bargaining.

title: Protecting America's Property Rights Act
ai_summary:
This bill sets new rules for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, the two large government-backed companies that buy home mortgages from lenders. Right now, when someone buys a home, lenders use title insurance or similar products to protect against problems like hidden liens, ownership disputes, or errors in property records. This bill would require Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to only buy mortgages that use title and lien protection products regulated by a state insurance authority or a state financial regulator. Newer, less regulated alternatives to traditional title insurance have been entering the market, and this bill would effectively push those products out of the mortgage system unless they are properly overseen by state regulators.

If a mortgage does not use a qualifying, state-regulated protection product, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac would have to hold an extra 1% of that loan's remaining balance as a financial cushion. That added cost would likely discourage lenders from selling those mortgages to Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac, which would make it harder for borrowers to get those loans in the first place. The bill gives the regulator that oversees Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac 180 days after passage to write the rules explaining how this works in practice.

This bill most directly affects mortgage lenders, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, title insurance companies, and companies offering newer title alternatives. It could also affect everyday homebuyers if lenders change which loan products they offer based on what Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac are willing to purchase.
ai_headline: New bill would limit which title products Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac accept
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3206 requires Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to only buy mortgages using state-regulated title or lien protection products.
ai_sections.what: HR 3206 would require Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to purchase only mortgages that use title and lien protection products regulated by a state insurance authority or state financial regulator. Newer, less regulated alternatives to traditional title insurance would be pushed out of the mortgage system unless they meet that oversight standard. The regulator overseeing Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac would have 180 days after passage to write the implementing rules.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects mortgage lenders, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, traditional title insurance companies, and companies offering newer title alternatives. Everyday homebuyers could also be affected if lenders change which loan products they offer based on what Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac will purchase.
ai_sections.why: If a mortgage uses a non-qualifying protection product, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac would be required to hold an extra 1% of that loan's remaining balance as a financial cushion. That added cost would likely discourage lenders from selling those mortgages to Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac, making it harder for borrowers to obtain those loans.
ai_sections.cost: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac would be required to hold an extra 1% of a loan's remaining balance as a financial cushion for any mortgage using a non-qualifying title product. This added requirement would likely discourage lenders from originating loans with those products.
costChips: Cost falls on Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac

title: DRIVER Act
ai_summary:
The DRIVER Act would require car manufacturers to give vehicle owners free, real-time access to all the electronic data their car collects. This includes information from sensors, GPS, and other onboard systems. Owners could share that data with any repair shop, app, or other service they choose, and manufacturers could not charge extra fees or force owners to use special tools just to read their own car's data. The bill also requires that owners have a way to delete personal information stored in their vehicle.

The bill puts limits on how manufacturers and fleet owners can sell certain sensitive data, specifically location information, biometric data like facial recognition or voice, and data about driving behavior. If a company wants to sell that kind of data, they must first give the vehicle owner a clear chance to opt out. The bill also bans selling any vehicle data to China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, and Venezuela. The Federal Trade Commission would be responsible for enforcing the law, and companies that break the rules could face FTC penalties.

This bill affects anyone who owns, leases, or regularly drives a car in the United States, as well as businesses that own fleets of vehicles. It also affects automakers and any companies that currently buy or use data collected by modern connected vehicles. If passed, it would override any state laws covering the same ground, meaning one national standard would apply everywhere.
ai_headline: Car owners would get free access to all data their vehicle collects
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: The DRIVER Act would give car owners free real-time access to their vehicle's data and limit how that data can be sold.
ai_sections.what: The DRIVER Act would require automakers to give vehicle owners free, real-time access to all electronic data their car collects, including from sensors, GPS, and onboard systems. Owners could share that data with any repair shop, app, or service they choose, and would have the right to delete personal information stored in the vehicle. Manufacturers could not charge fees or require special tools for owners to read their own data.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects anyone who owns, leases, or regularly drives a car in the United States, as well as businesses with vehicle fleets. It also applies to automakers and any companies that currently buy or use data collected by connected vehicles.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the bill would override all state laws covering the same ground, establishing a single national standard. The Federal Trade Commission would enforce the law, and companies that violate the rules would be subject to FTC penalties.
ai_sections.cost: Companies that violate the rules face FTC penalties, though the summary does not specify penalty amounts.

title: Safe Streets for All Reauthorization and Improvement Act
ai_summary:
This bill updates a federal grant program called Safe Streets and Roads for All, which was created in 2021 to help cities, towns, and tribal governments reduce traffic deaths and serious injuries. The bill does two main things. First, it requires that at least 20 percent of the program's money go toward planning grants, which help communities study their roads and develop safety action plans before doing any construction. Second, it adds 5 billion dollars in new funding to keep the program running from 2027 through 2031, extending it beyond its current end date.

The people most directly affected are local and regional governments, metropolitan planning organizations, and tribal nations that apply for these grants. They can use the money to plan and build things like safer crosswalks, better bike lanes, improved road markings, or other changes meant to lower the number of people killed or hurt in traffic crashes. The planning grant requirement is meant to make sure smaller communities with fewer resources still have access to funds to get started, even if they are not yet ready for a full construction project.

Everyday residents could see effects through safer streets and roads in their communities, particularly in areas that have historically had high rates of pedestrian or cyclist injuries. The bill does not create a brand-new program; it simply continues and adjusts one that already exists. It was introduced with support from members of both parties and has been sent to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure for review.
ai_headline: Federal road safety grant program extended with $5B and new planning rules
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5452 adds $5B to extend the Safe Streets and Roads for All grant program through 2031 and reserves 20% for planning grants.
ai_sections.what: HR 5452 amends the existing Safe Streets and Roads for All federal grant program, which was established in 2021 to help reduce traffic deaths and serious injuries. The bill sets aside at least 20 percent of program funds for planning grants and adds $5 billion in new funding to extend the program from 2027 through 2031.
ai_sections.who: Local and regional governments, metropolitan planning organizations, and tribal nations are the eligible applicants for these grants. Everyday residents in communities with high rates of pedestrian or cyclist injuries are the people most likely to see on-the-ground effects.
ai_sections.why: The planning grant requirement is intended to ensure that smaller or less-resourced communities can access funds to develop safety action plans even before they are ready for construction projects. Extending funding through 2031 continues a program that would otherwise expire under its current authorization.
ai_sections.cost: The bill adds $5 billion in new federal funding to cover the program from 2027 through 2031.

title: Local Law Enforcement Support Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill expands what local police departments can spend federal grant money on. Right now, two existing federal programs — the Byrne JAG program and the COPS program — already send money to local law enforcement agencies. This bill adds new approved spending categories to those programs, so departments have more flexibility in how they use those funds.

The new things local police could spend the grant money on include hiring and training officers, buying protective gear, fighting cybercrime with digital tools, using drones, and upgrading crime-solving technology like DNA testing equipment, ballistics tools, and video analysis software. Departments could also use the money to better communicate with and support victims of violent crime.

This bill directly affects local and state law enforcement agencies that apply for federal grants. It could also affect the communities those departments serve, since better-equipped and better-staffed police forces may change how crimes are investigated and how victims are treated. It does not create a new grant program or add new federal spending on its own — it simply widens the list of allowed uses within funding programs that already exist.
ai_headline: Federal grant rules expanded to cover drones, DNA tools, and officer hiring
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8564 widens what local police can buy with existing federal Byrne JAG and COPS grant money, adding tech, gear, and hiring.
ai_sections.what: HR 8564 adds new approved spending categories to two existing federal grant programs, Byrne JAG and COPS, that already fund local law enforcement. Police departments could use those funds for hiring and training officers, protective gear, drones, cybercrime tools, and crime-solving technology such as DNA testing equipment, ballistics tools, and video analysis software. Grant money could also go toward communication with and support for victims of violent crime.
ai_sections.who: State and local law enforcement agencies that apply for Byrne JAG or COPS grants are directly affected. The communities those departments serve may also be affected, depending on how agencies choose to use the expanded funding options.
ai_sections.why: Widening the list of allowed uses gives law enforcement agencies more flexibility in how they allocate existing federal funds. Changes to equipment levels, staffing, and victim services could alter how crimes are investigated and how victims are treated in affected jurisdictions.
ai_sections.cost: This bill does not create a new grant program or add new federal spending on its own. It expands only the permitted uses within funding programs that already exist.
costChips: Expands existing Byrne JAG and COPS funds

title: VA Police Recruitment and Retention Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would stop the Department of Veterans Affairs from lowering the job classifications of its police officers and other law enforcement employees. When a federal position gets "downgraded," the job title and pay grade drop, which usually means the employee earns less money over time. This bill would make that kind of change illegal for VA law enforcement jobs, and it would cancel any downgrades that already happened on or after October 1, 2025, restoring those positions and paying back any wages that were lost.

The bill also applies retroactively, meaning it looks backward in time. If a VA police officer's position was quietly reclassified to a lower pay level between October 2025 and when this bill becomes law, that change would be reversed. The officer would go back to their previous grade and receive any back pay they missed out on during that period.

This bill mainly affects VA police officers and other law enforcement staff who work at VA hospitals, clinics, and facilities across the country. It also limits what the Office of Personnel Management, which oversees federal workforce rules, can do regarding these specific jobs. Veterans who use VA facilities could be indirectly affected, since the bill is aimed at making it easier to recruit and keep qualified security personnel at those locations.
ai_headline: VA police officers shielded from job downgrades under new House bill
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8010 bans VA from downgrading law enforcement job titles and pay, and reverses any downgrades made on or after October 1, 2025.
ai_sections.what: HR 8010 would permanently ban the Department of Veterans Affairs from lowering the job classifications or pay grades of its police officers and other law enforcement employees. Any downgrades that took effect on or after October 1, 2025 would be cancelled, and affected employees would have their previous positions restored. The bill also requires back pay to be issued for any wages lost during the period between a downgrade and the bill becoming law.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects VA police officers and law enforcement staff who work at VA hospitals, clinics, and facilities across the country. It also restricts the Office of Personnel Management from making classification changes to these specific positions.
ai_sections.why: Without the bill, VA law enforcement employees remain vulnerable to job reclassifications that reduce their pay over time. The bill is framed around the goal of recruiting and retaining qualified security personnel at VA facilities, which could indirectly affect veterans who use those locations.

title: Chip Security Act
ai_summary:
The Chip Security Act requires the U.S. Department of Commerce to add security features to advanced computer chips before they are sold or shipped to other countries. Within 180 days of the law passing, any high-powered chip covered by U.S. export rules would need to include location-tracking technology so the government can verify the chip stays where it was approved to go. If a company that exported a chip finds out it has been moved to an unauthorized location, handed to an unauthorized user, or tampered with, they must report that to the government right away.

The law also directs the Commerce Department to study and possibly require additional security features over time, such as tools that could disable or limit a chip that was stolen or smuggled, or methods to confirm what the chip is actually being used for. The department must report its findings to Congress within one year and roll out any approved additional security measures within two years after that. Each year for three years following that, the government must review new security technologies and consider updating the requirements.

This bill mainly affects U.S. companies that manufacture or export advanced semiconductors and AI chips, particularly those already subject to U.S. export controls. It also affects foreign businesses and governments that purchase these chips, since the chips could be monitored for location and usage. Everyday consumers would likely feel little direct impact, though the rules could influence the global supply of high-end chips used in data centers, AI systems, and other advanced computing products.
ai_headline: New law would require location tracking on exported AI chips
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 42 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3447 requires location-tracking technology in advanced exported chips within 180 days and mandates reporting if chips are moved or tampered with.
ai_sections.what: The Chip Security Act requires the Commerce Department to add location-tracking technology to advanced chips covered by U.S. export rules before they are sold or shipped abroad. Companies that export these chips must immediately report to the government if a chip is moved to an unauthorized location, handed to an unauthorized user, or tampered with. The law also directs the Commerce Department to study and potentially require additional features such as disabling tools or usage-verification methods, with findings due to Congress within one year and approved measures rolled out within two years after that.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects U.S. companies that manufacture or export advanced semiconductors and AI chips already subject to U.S. export controls. Foreign businesses and governments that purchase these chips are also affected, as chips could be monitored for location and usage.
ai_sections.why: The tracking and reporting requirements would create new compliance obligations for chip exporters and expand government visibility into where high-end chips go after sale. Annual reviews of security technology are required for three years following implementation, meaning requirements could continue to evolve and affect the global supply of chips used in data centers, AI systems, and advanced computing products.

title: Grocery, Farm, and Food Worker Stabilization Grant Program Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new grant program run by the U.S. Department of Agriculture. The program would send money to unions and membership organizations that represent farmworkers, meat processing workers, and grocery store workers. The money would be used to make direct payments to those workers when a natural disaster or other major disaster strikes and disrupts their jobs or income. Congress would authorize up to $50 million to fund the program.

The workers most directly affected are people who grow and harvest food, work in meatpacking plants, and work in grocery stores. These are jobs that often cannot be done remotely and can be severely disrupted by events like hurricanes, floods, or other large-scale emergencies. The grants would not go straight to individual workers. Instead, they would go to the unions or worker organizations those employees belong to, which would then distribute stabilization payments to members.

Four years after the program starts, the Department of Agriculture would be required to report back to Congress on how the program worked and what impact it had. The bill was introduced in March 2026 and has been sent to the House Agriculture Committee, where it would need to be approved before moving further through the legislative process. It has not yet become law.
ai_headline: USDA disaster grant program would funnel aid through farmworker unions
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7938 would give up to $50M to worker organizations to make disaster payments to food-sector employees.
ai_sections.what: HR 7938 would create a USDA grant program to send money to unions and membership organizations representing farmworkers, meat processing workers, and grocery store workers. Those organizations would then distribute direct stabilization payments to their members when a natural or major disaster disrupts jobs or income. Four years after the program begins, USDA would be required to report to Congress on its results.
ai_sections.who: The workers directly affected are people who grow and harvest food, work in meatpacking plants, and work in grocery stores. Grants would go to the unions or worker organizations those employees belong to, not to individual workers directly.
ai_sections.why: The food-sector jobs covered by this bill typically cannot be done remotely, making workers more exposed to income disruption during large-scale emergencies like hurricanes or floods. Routing funds through worker organizations rather than directly to individuals would add a distribution step between the federal government and affected workers.
ai_sections.cost: Congress would authorize up to $50 million to fund the program.

title: Farewell to Foam Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would ban the sale and distribution of three specific types of foam plastic products made from expanded polystyrene, starting January 1, 2028. The banned items are foam food containers like cups, plates, bowls, clamshells, and egg cartons; foam packing peanuts used to cushion items during shipping; and foam coolers used to keep things cold. The ban would not apply to foam containers used to ship medicines, medical devices, or biological products like vaccines.

The ban would apply to anyone involved in making, importing, distributing, or selling these products, including restaurants, grocery stores, schools, hospitals, and retail stores. The first time a business breaks the rule, the EPA would send a written warning. After that, fines would kick in, starting at $250 for a second violation, $500 for a third, and $1,000 for each violation after that. Smaller businesses face some limits on how often they can be fined within a seven-day period.

This bill would affect a wide range of people and businesses across the country. Restaurants and food vendors that currently use foam takeout containers would need to switch to other materials like paper, cardboard, or plastic alternatives. Shipping companies and online retailers that use packing peanuts would need to find substitute materials. Consumers who buy foam coolers for picnics or camping would no longer be able to purchase them after the deadline. Manufacturers that currently produce these foam products would need to change or stop that part of their business.
ai_headline: Foam cups, packing peanuts, and coolers face U.S. sales ban by 2028
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1918 bans sale of foam food containers, packing peanuts, and coolers starting January 1, 2028.
ai_sections.what: HR 1918 would ban the sale and distribution of expanded polystyrene foam food containers, packing peanuts, and coolers starting January 1, 2028. The ban covers anyone involved in making, importing, distributing, or selling these products. Foam packaging used to ship medicines, medical devices, or biological products like vaccines is exempt.
ai_sections.who: The ban applies to restaurants, grocery stores, schools, hospitals, retail stores, shipping companies, online retailers, and manufacturers. Consumers would also be affected, as foam coolers would no longer be available for purchase after the deadline.
ai_sections.why: Restaurants and food vendors would need to switch to alternative materials such as paper, cardboard, or other plastics for takeout containers. Manufacturers that currently produce these foam products would need to change or cease that part of their business.
ai_sections.cost: First-time violations result in a written warning from the EPA. Fines start at $250 for a second violation, $500 for a third, and $1,000 for each violation after that, with some limits on fine frequency for smaller businesses within a seven-day period.

title: One Nation, One Visa Policy Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require that any person holding a Chinese passport must have a valid visa before being allowed to enter the United States. Right now, there is a special visa waiver program for the U.S. territory of Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands that allows Chinese tourists to visit without getting a visa in advance. This bill would end that exception and block any similar programs from being created or funded in the future.

The bill also specifies that "China" includes Hong Kong and Macau, even though those two places have some separate travel documents and rules. So passport holders from all three of those places would be covered by this requirement.

This mainly affects Chinese tourists, students, business travelers, and others who currently use or might use visa-free entry programs, especially in Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands. It would also affect those U.S. territories economically, since they rely heavily on tourism from China. American businesses and universities that work with Chinese visitors could also feel the impact if the visa process becomes a bigger barrier to entry.
ai_headline: Bill would strip visa-free entry for Chinese passport holders in Guam
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7780 would end visa-free entry for Chinese passport holders in Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands.
ai_sections.what: HR 7780 would require all Chinese passport holders, including those from Hong Kong and Macau, to obtain a valid visa before entering the United States. The bill would end the existing visa waiver program that allows Chinese tourists to visit Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands without a visa. It would also block any similar programs from being created or funded in the future.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects Chinese tourists, students, and business travelers who currently use or could use visa-free entry, particularly in Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands. American businesses, universities, and the economies of those U.S. territories would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands rely heavily on tourism from China, so ending visa-free entry could have economic consequences for those territories. The added visa requirement could also reduce the number of Chinese visitors reaching American businesses and universities that depend on that travel.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would block future funding for visa waiver programs similar to the one being eliminated.
costChips: Future visa waiver programs blocked from funding

title: SHUSH Act
ai_summary:
This bill would remove silencers (also called suppressors) from the list of heavily regulated items under the National Firearms Act. Right now, buying a silencer requires a $200 federal tax, a background check, a months-long wait for government approval, and registration with the federal government. The SHUSH Act would eliminate all of that, treating silencers the same as ordinary gun accessories like scopes or grips, which anyone legally allowed to own a firearm can buy without extra federal steps.

The bill also has two other notable parts. It would block states and cities from creating their own special taxes or registration requirements for silencers, overriding those local laws. It would also apply the tax refund rules going back to 2015, meaning people who paid the $200 federal tax on silencer transfers after that date could potentially get that money back.

This bill affects several groups. Gun owners who want to buy silencers would face far fewer restrictions and lower costs. Dealers who sell silencers would see a simpler sales process. People living in states that currently ban or heavily restrict silencers could be affected, since the bill would remove some state-level rules, though states could still ban silencers outright through other laws. Federal agencies that currently manage silencer registrations, like the ATF, would have a reduced role in overseeing these devices.
ai_headline: Bill would strip federal registration rules from gun silencers
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: The SHUSH Act would remove the $200 tax, background check, and federal registration currently required to buy a silencer.
ai_sections.what: The SHUSH Act would remove silencers from the National Firearms Act's heavily regulated items list, eliminating the $200 federal tax, background check, months-long wait, and federal registration now required to buy one. Silencers would instead be treated like ordinary gun accessories such as scopes or grips. The bill would also block states and cities from imposing their own special taxes or registration requirements on silencers.
ai_sections.who: Gun owners seeking to buy silencers and dealers who sell them would face fewer federal restrictions and a simpler sales process. Federal agencies currently overseeing silencer registrations, including the ATF, would have a reduced oversight role.
ai_sections.why: People in states that currently ban or heavily restrict silencers could be affected, since the bill would remove some state-level rules, though states could still ban silencers outright through other laws. Federal agencies managing silencer registrations would see their role in overseeing these devices significantly reduced.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would eliminate the $200 federal tax currently charged on silencer transfers. People who paid that tax on transfers occurring after 2015 could potentially receive a refund under the bill's retroactive provisions.

title: New Producer Economic Security Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new federal grant program inside the U.S. Department of Agriculture called the New Producer Economic Security Program. The program would send money to organizations like nonprofits, tribal governments, community lenders, and universities, which would then pass that money along to beginning and struggling farmers and ranchers. The help could come as grants, loans, or down payment assistance to help people buy or keep farmland, cover closing costs, fix up soil or water, build infrastructure, or get legal and business advice.

The people this program is designed to help are called "qualified beneficiaries." These are farmers, ranchers, or forest owners who are either brand new to farming, only farm on rented land, have low incomes, or are otherwise considered economically disadvantaged. The bill also gives priority to projects that help farmworkers, support tribal land access, or help families pass farms down to the next generation instead of selling to outside buyers.

The bill authorizes Congress to appropriate whatever funding is needed, but it does not set a specific dollar amount. It also sets up a stakeholder committee to help the USDA decide how to hand out grants fairly. Anyone who runs a farm operation already for more than 10 years and is financially stable would generally not qualify for the direct assistance side of this program.
ai_headline: New USDA grant program targets beginning and low-income farmers
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, Risk Management, and Credit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2536 creates a USDA grant program funneling money through nonprofits and lenders to new and struggling farmers and ranchers.
ai_sections.what: HR 2536 establishes the New Producer Economic Security Program inside the U.S. Department of Agriculture. The program would distribute funds through intermediary organizations, including nonprofits, tribal governments, community lenders, and universities, which would then provide grants, loans, or down payment assistance to eligible farmers and ranchers. Allowed uses include buying or keeping farmland, covering closing costs, improving soil or water, building infrastructure, and obtaining legal or business advice.
ai_sections.who: Eligible recipients, called qualified beneficiaries, are farmers, ranchers, or forest owners who are new to farming, farm only on rented land, have low incomes, or are otherwise economically disadvantaged. Farmers who have operated for more than 10 years and are financially stable would generally not qualify for direct assistance.
ai_sections.why: The program could expand land access and financial support for people entering or struggling within farming, with priority given to farmworkers, tribal land access, and intergenerational farm transfers over sales to outside buyers. A stakeholder committee would advise the USDA on distributing grants, which may shape how equitably funds reach intended groups.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes Congress to appropriate whatever funding is needed but does not set a specific dollar amount.
costChips: Funding level set by future appropriations

title: Restoring Access for Detainees Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the federal government to give people held in immigration detention free phone access. Within the first five hours of being detained or moved to a new facility, a detainee would get at least one free call of 10 minutes or more to notify a family member of their location. Each month, detainees would receive at least 200 free minutes to call family. Calls to lawyers, immigration courts, consulates, oversight offices, and certain government agencies would be unlimited and free. Calls to lawyers and oversight officials could not be recorded or monitored unless a court issued a warrant. The bill also says detention facilities can set reasonable rules about when and where calls happen, but they cannot cap time spent talking to a lawyer or count incoming calls against a detainee's free minutes.

The people most directly affected are immigrants held in civil immigration detention, which can include people awaiting deportation hearings or other immigration proceedings. These individuals are not held as part of a criminal sentence. The bill also affects the Department of Homeland Security, which would be responsible for setting up the program and paying for it. The bill points to funding already given to ICE through a recently passed law as the intended source of money.

This bill was introduced in the House and referred to the Judiciary Committee, meaning it has not become law. It essentially tries to bring back a free phone program that existed during the COVID-19 pandemic and later ended, while adding new rules about lawyer calls and family notification.
ai_headline: House bill would give immigration detainees free phone access each month
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5941 would give immigration detainees 200 free minutes monthly and unlimited free calls to lawyers and courts.

Detainees would receive at least 200 free minutes per month for family calls, plus unlimited free calls to lawyers, immigration courts, consulates, oversight offices, and certain government agencies. Calls to lawyers and oversight officials could not be recorded or monitored without a court-issued warrant.

The Department of Homeland Security would be responsible for administering and funding the program.

Detention facilities would retain authority to set reasonable rules on when and where calls take place, but could not cap time spent with lawyers or count incoming calls against a detainee's free minutes.

title: Infrastructure Expansion Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill deals with who can be held legally responsible when a construction worker gets hurt on a federal infrastructure project. Right now, some states have laws called "absolute liability" rules, which means that if a worker falls or is injured by a gravity-related hazard on a job site, the property owner or contractor can be held fully responsible no matter what. This bill would end that standard for any project that receives federal money, federal tax breaks, or requires federal permits. Instead, it would replace absolute liability with "comparative negligence," which means a court would look at whether the injured worker also played a role in causing their own injury, such as ignoring safety training, not using provided safety equipment, or being impaired by drugs or alcohol.

The bill would also move these legal cases from state courts into federal courts, and it would override any state laws that conflict with it. So states that currently have stronger protections for injured workers on construction sites would lose the ability to enforce those protections on federally funded projects. Workers' compensation laws, however, would not be affected by this bill.

The people most directly affected are construction workers who do physical labor on federally funded infrastructure projects like highways, bridges, airports, and buildings. Property owners and contractors on those same projects would also be affected, since they would face a different and generally lower level of legal liability if a worker is injured. The bill would apply to any project where federal assistance is accepted starting January 1, 2026.
ai_headline: Federal bill would strip stronger state injury protections from construction workers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3548 replaces absolute liability with comparative negligence on federally funded construction projects, overriding stronger state worker protections.
ai_sections.what: HR 3548 would end absolute liability standards for worker injuries on construction projects receiving federal money, tax breaks, or permits, replacing them with comparative negligence. Under comparative negligence, courts would consider whether an injured worker contributed to their own injury, such as by ignoring safety training, refusing provided equipment, or working while impaired. The bill would move these cases from state courts to federal courts and override conflicting state laws, effective for projects accepting federal assistance starting January 1, 2026.
ai_sections.who: Construction workers injured on federally funded infrastructure projects like highways, bridges, airports, and buildings are directly affected, as are the property owners and contractors working on those same projects. States that currently have stronger absolute liability protections for workers would lose the ability to enforce those protections on federally funded projects.
ai_sections.why: Workers in states with absolute liability laws would have reduced legal standing after an injury on a federally funded project, since a court could reduce or limit their recovery based on their own share of fault. Property owners and contractors on federally funded projects would face a generally lower level of legal liability than they currently do in states with absolute liability rules.

title: Congressional Tribute to Constance Baker Motley Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill directs Congress to award a congressional gold medal to Constance Baker Motley, who passed away in 2005. She was a pioneering civil rights lawyer who helped win landmark cases including Brown v. Board of Education, and she later became the first Black woman elected to the New York State Senate, the first woman to serve as Manhattan Borough President, and the first Black woman appointed to a federal judgeship. The medal would be created by the U.S. Treasury Department and presented to her son and niece at a formal ceremony.

The bill also allows the U.S. Mint to produce and sell bronze copies of the gold medal to the public. The cost of making the gold medal comes from an existing U.S. Mint fund, and any money made from selling the bronze copies goes back into that same fund, so there is no additional cost to taxpayers.

This bill directly affects the Motley family, who would receive the medal. It also involves the U.S. Mint, which would design and produce the medals. Members of the general public who are interested in history or collecting could purchase bronze copies. The congressional gold medal is one of the highest honors Congress can give to a civilian, so the bill is essentially a formal national recognition of Motley's lifetime of public service.
ai_headline: Gold medal bill honors trailblazing civil rights lawyer Constance Baker Motley
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

The U.S. Treasury Department would create the medal, which would be presented to her son and niece at a formal ceremony. The U.S. Mint would also be authorized to produce and sell bronze copies of the medal to the public.

The congressional gold medal is one of the highest honors Congress can give to a civilian, making this bill a formal national recognition of her lifetime of public service.

title: Fair Access to Banking Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require large banks, credit unions, and payment card networks to serve any customer whose business is legal under federal law. Specifically, banks with more than $50 billion in assets could not refuse a customer just because of the industry they work in or because of "reputational risk." If a bank does deny someone service, it would have to put the reason in writing, and that reason must be based on specific, measurable financial risk standards, not general opinions about the customer's business or politics. Banks that break these rules could lose access to emergency government lending programs, like the Federal Reserve's discount window, which banks rely on when they need short-term cash.

The bill also covers payment card networks, like Visa or Mastercard, prohibiting them from blocking legally operating businesses from using their systems based on political or reputational concerns. Violations by payment networks could result in financial penalties. The Automated Clearing House network, which handles direct deposits and electronic payments, would also be off-limits to large banks and credit unions that refuse legal businesses.

People or businesses that are wrongly denied banking services could sue the bank directly in federal court without going through a government agency first. If they win, they could receive three times their actual damages plus legal fees. The bill would mainly affect the largest financial institutions and would most directly benefit businesses in industries that have reportedly struggled to get banking services, such as firearms dealers, fossil fuel companies, and certain other legal but controversial sectors.
ai_headline: Large banks would lose Fed emergency lending if they turn away legal businesses
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 987 bars banks over $50B in assets from refusing legal businesses, with loss of Fed discount window access as penalty.
ai_sections.what: HR 987 would require banks with more than $50 billion in assets, credit unions, and payment card networks to serve any customer whose business is legal under federal law. Banks that deny service must provide a written reason based on specific, measurable financial risk standards, not opinions about a customer's industry or politics. Payment card networks like Visa or Mastercard and the Automated Clearing House network would also be prohibited from blocking legally operating businesses on political or reputational grounds.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects the largest banks, credit unions, and major payment card networks. Businesses in industries such as firearms dealers and fossil fuel companies that have reportedly struggled to access banking services would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Banks that violate the rules could lose access to the Federal Reserve's discount window, a key source of short-term emergency lending. Wrongly denied customers could sue directly in federal court and, if successful, collect three times their actual damages plus legal fees, while payment networks face financial penalties for violations.
ai_sections.cost: Violating payment card networks face financial penalties. Banks found in violation risk losing access to Federal Reserve emergency lending programs, and successful plaintiffs can recover triple damages plus attorney fees.
costChips: Triple damages plus legal fees if plaintiff wins
Banks risk losing Fed discount window access

title: IRA Charitable Rollover Facilitation and Enhancement Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes a tax rule about how people can donate money from their retirement accounts to charity. Right now, Americans age 70½ or older can transfer money directly from an IRA to most charities without paying income tax on that withdrawal. This is called a "qualified charitable distribution." However, current law blocks people from using this tax benefit when donating to a donor-advised fund, which is a special account held at a sponsoring organization that lets donors recommend where their charitable gifts go over time. This bill would remove that restriction and allow IRA charitable rollovers to go into donor-advised funds.

Who would this affect most directly? People age 70½ and older who have traditional IRAs and want to give to charity using a donor-advised fund. These individuals could transfer up to the allowed limit (currently $105,000 per year) directly from their IRA to a donor-advised fund without counting that money as taxable income. Financial institutions and nonprofits that manage donor-advised funds, such as community foundations and large financial firms with charitable arms, would also be affected as they could receive more contributions through this channel.

The bill does not create a new tax deduction. It simply expands an existing one to cover a type of charitable account that was previously excluded. People who do not have IRAs, are under age 70½, or who do not use donor-advised funds would not be directly affected by this change.
ai_headline: IRA charitable rollover ban on donor-advised funds would end under HR 2891
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2891 lets Americans 70½+ roll up to $105,000 yearly from IRAs into donor-advised funds tax-free, reversing a current exclusion.
ai_sections.what: HR 2891 removes a restriction that blocks people from using the qualified charitable distribution tax benefit when donating IRA funds to a donor-advised fund. Currently, Americans age 70½ or older can transfer up to $105,000 per year directly from an IRA to most charities without counting that money as taxable income, but donor-advised funds are excluded from that option. This bill would extend the existing benefit to cover donor-advised funds.
ai_sections.who: The change would most directly affect Americans age 70½ and older who hold traditional IRAs and wish to give to charity through a donor-advised fund. Financial institutions and nonprofits that manage donor-advised funds, such as community foundations and large financial firms with charitable arms, would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Donor-advised fund sponsors could receive more contributions through the IRA rollover channel, expanding the flow of charitable dollars into those accounts. People who do not have IRAs, are under age 70½, or do not use donor-advised funds would not be directly affected by this change.

press_names: IRA Charitable Rollover Facilitation and Enhancement Act

title: In God We Trust Act
ai_summary:
This bill would require the federal government to display the phrase "In God We Trust" on all federal public buildings. The Administrator of General Services, who oversees federal properties, would have one year after the bill becomes law to make sure the motto is placed in a prominent spot on every qualifying building. Federal public buildings include courthouses, office buildings, and similar government-owned or leased facilities across the country.

The bill would affect anyone who works in or visits federal buildings, since they would see the national motto displayed there. It would also require the General Services Administration to spend time and money carrying out the requirement across what amounts to thousands of federal properties nationwide.

"In God We Trust" has been the official national motto of the United States since 1956 and already appears on U.S. currency. This bill would extend that display to federal buildings specifically. The bill was introduced in the House and sent to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, where it would need to be approved before moving further through Congress.
ai_headline: Bill would put national motto on all federal buildings
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8826 would require "In God We Trust" displayed prominently on every federal public building within one year of enactment.
ai_sections.what: HR 8826 would require the phrase "In God We Trust" to be displayed in a prominent location on all federal public buildings, including courthouses, office buildings, and government-owned or leased facilities. The Administrator of General Services would have one year after enactment to carry out the requirement across thousands of federal properties nationwide.
ai_sections.who: Anyone who works in or visits a federal building would encounter the motto on display. The General Services Administration would be responsible for implementing the requirement.
ai_sections.why: The General Services Administration would be required to spend time and resources placing the motto across thousands of federal properties nationwide. The bill remains in the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, where it must be approved before advancing further in Congress.
ai_sections.cost: The General Services Administration would be required to spend time and money carrying out the requirement across thousands of federal properties nationwide.

title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to limit the number of consecutive terms that a Member of Congress may serve.
ai_summary:
This bill proposes a change to the U.S. Constitution that would set limits on how long members of Congress can serve back-to-back. Senators could serve no more than two consecutive terms (12 years in a row), and House members could serve no more than five consecutive terms (10 years in a row). After hitting that limit, a person would have to wait at least one year before running again. They are not banned from Congress forever, just required to take a break before returning.

Because this is a constitutional amendment, it would not become law just by passing Congress. Two-thirds of both the House and Senate would need to approve it, and then three-fourths of all state legislatures (38 out of 50 states) would have to ratify it within seven years. That is a high bar to clear. The bill also specifies that terms already served before ratification would not count toward the limit, so the clock would start fresh for everyone currently in office.

This would directly affect current and future members of Congress and, by extension, their constituents. Voters in any district or state could find that a long-serving representative they support is required to step aside temporarily. On the other hand, it could open seats to new candidates who might otherwise struggle to unseat an incumbent. The bill does not affect the President or any other federal officeholder.
ai_headline: Proposed amendment would cap consecutive congressional terms at 10–12 years
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HJRES 120 would limit senators to 2 consecutive terms and House members to 5, with a required 1-year break before returning.
ai_sections.what: HJRES 120 proposes a constitutional amendment limiting senators to two consecutive terms (12 years) and House members to five consecutive terms (10 years). After reaching the limit, a member must wait at least one year before running again. Terms served before ratification would not count toward the new limits.
ai_sections.who: The limits would apply to all current and future members of the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives. The bill does not affect the President or any other federal officeholder.
ai_sections.why: Long-serving incumbents who reach the consecutive-term limit would be required to temporarily vacate their seats, which could affect constituents who support them. The mandatory break could also create openings for new candidates who might otherwise struggle to defeat an incumbent.

title: REVOKE Act
ai_summary:
This bill would allow the government to take away security clearances from former Defense Department employees and retired military members if they lobby on behalf of certain Chinese companies. Specifically, it targets companies that the Defense Department has labeled as Chinese military companies and that also appear on a Treasury Department watchlist. In other words, if a former Pentagon official or retired service member gets paid to represent or advocate for one of these companies in Washington, they would lose their access to classified information. The Defense Secretary can make exceptions for up to 180 days if national security requires it, but must explain that decision to Congress.

The bill affects two main groups of people. The first is former civilian employees of the Department of Defense, such as analysts, managers, or contractors who once worked for the Pentagon. The second is retired or separated military members, like former generals or officers. Both groups are sometimes hired after their government service to work as lobbyists or consultants, which is commonly called the revolving door between government and private industry.

For most everyday Americans, this bill would have no direct impact. Its focus is narrow, targeting a specific group of former government insiders who choose to work for companies on these Chinese military watchlists. Supporters might say it protects national security by reducing the risk that sensitive government knowledge gets used to help Chinese military-linked businesses. Critics might raise concerns about how far the government can go in restricting what private citizens do after leaving public service.
ai_headline: Former Pentagon officials could lose security clearances for lobbying Chinese military firms
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 3181 would revoke security clearances from ex-Pentagon staff and retired military who lobby for Chinese military-linked companies.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 3181 would strip security clearances from former Defense Department employees and retired or separated military members who lobby for companies the Defense Department has labeled as Chinese military companies and that also appear on a Treasury Department watchlist. The Defense Secretary may grant exceptions lasting up to 180 days if national security requires it, but must notify Congress when doing so.
ai_sections.who: The bill targets two groups: former civilian Defense Department employees such as analysts, managers, or contractors, and retired or separated military members such as former generals or officers. Everyday Americans are not directly affected by this legislation.
ai_sections.why: Removing clearance access could reduce the risk that sensitive government knowledge is used to benefit Chinese military-linked businesses. The bill also raises questions about how far the government can restrict the private-sector activities of former public servants.

title: Professional Degree Access Restoration Act
ai_summary:
This bill would undo recent cuts to federal student loan limits for people in graduate and professional school, such as law school, medical school, and master's degree programs. A previous law reduced how much money these students can borrow through federal loans. This bill would bring those limits back up to where they were before that cut took effect.

The main effect is that graduate and professional students would once again be able to borrow more money through the federal loan program to cover the cost of their advanced degrees. Without this change, students who hit the lower borrowing cap may have to turn to private loans, which often come with higher interest rates and fewer borrower protections than federal loans.

This bill directly affects people currently enrolled in or planning to attend graduate or professional degree programs, such as medical, dental, law, or business school. It would not affect undergraduate students. Taxpayers could also be affected, since higher federal loan limits mean the government is taking on more lending. The bill was introduced in the Senate in March 2026 and has been sent to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions, where it will be reviewed before any further action is taken.
ai_headline: Senate bill would restore higher federal loan limits for graduate students
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4039 would reverse recent cuts to federal borrowing limits for graduate and professional school students.
ai_sections.what: S 4039 would restore federal student loan limits for graduate and professional students to the levels that existed before a recent law reduced them. Students in programs such as law, medical, dental, and business school would be able to borrow more through the federal loan program than they currently can. The bill does not affect undergraduate students.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects people currently enrolled in or planning to attend graduate or professional degree programs. Taxpayers are also affected, as higher federal loan limits mean the government takes on more lending.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, graduate students who reach the lower borrowing cap may have to turn to private loans, which often carry higher interest rates and fewer borrower protections than federal loans. The bill was introduced in the Senate in March 2026 and has been referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions for review.
ai_sections.cost: Higher federal loan limits would result in the government taking on more lending, according to the summary. No specific dollar figures are provided.

title: Improve and Enhance the Work Opportunity Tax Credit Act
ai_summary:
The Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC) is an existing federal tax break that rewards businesses for hiring people from certain groups who often have a harder time finding jobs. This bill would extend that tax credit through 2030 and make it more generous. Right now, employers can claim 40% of a new hire's first-year wages up to a set limit. This bill raises that rate to 50% and adds a bonus credit for workers who stay on the job for at least 400 hours, encouraging employers to keep these workers longer rather than just hiring and quickly moving on. The wage limits used to calculate the credit would also increase each year with inflation, so the benefit doesn't lose value over time.

The bill also expands who counts as an eligible hire. Currently, food stamp recipients must be under age 40 to qualify; this bill removes that age cap entirely. It also adds military spouses as a new qualifying group, meaning a business that hires the spouse of an active-duty service member can now claim the credit. Special adjustments are made for veterans, long-term welfare recipients, summer youth workers, and workers in agriculture and the railroad industry.

This bill mainly affects two groups: businesses that hire workers from struggling or underrepresented populations, and the workers themselves. Employers in manufacturing, construction, energy, healthcare, and infrastructure are specifically mentioned as targets for outreach. Federal agencies would be required to actively inform business leaders in those fields about the credit. Workers who benefit include veterans, people receiving food assistance, welfare recipients, ex-felons, people with disabilities, and now military spouses.
ai_headline: Tax credit for hiring hard-to-employ workers would rise to 50% under Senate bill
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 3265 extends the Work Opportunity Tax Credit through 2030, raises the employer credit rate from 40% to 50%, and adds military spouses as a new qualifying group.
ai_sections.what: S 3265 would extend the Work Opportunity Tax Credit through 2030 and raise the credit rate employers can claim from 40% to 50% of a new hire's first-year wages. A bonus credit would apply when a hired worker stays on the job for at least 400 hours. Wage limits used to calculate the credit would increase annually with inflation, and the age cap for food stamp recipients would be removed entirely.
ai_sections.who: Employers in manufacturing, construction, energy, healthcare, and infrastructure are the primary business targets, with federal agencies required to actively inform leaders in those fields about the credit. Eligible workers include veterans, food assistance recipients, welfare recipients, ex-felons, people with disabilities, and military spouses, who are newly added under this bill.
ai_sections.why: Raising the credit rate and adding a retention bonus changes the financial incentive structure for employers, rewarding longer job tenure rather than short-term hiring. Removing the age cap for food stamp recipients and adding military spouses expands the pool of workers whose hiring generates a tax benefit for businesses.

press_names: Improve and Enhance the Work Opportunity Tax Credit Act

title: PRC Military and Human Rights Capital Markets Sanctions Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would ban Americans from buying, selling, or holding stocks and other investments tied to certain Chinese companies. Specifically, it targets companies already flagged by the U.S. government for connections to China's military, involvement in human rights abuses, use of forced labor, or violations of U.S. export rules. Within 90 days of becoming law, the President would have to publish one combined public list of all these banned companies with identifying codes so investors can look them up easily.

Anyone who already owns these investments would have 180 days to sell them and get out. After that deadline, holding onto those securities would be illegal. The bill covers not just the flagged companies themselves, but also any companies that are owned or controlled by them, meaning the ban could extend to subsidiaries and related businesses.

The bill affects any U.S. citizen, permanent resident, or company operating under U.S. law. Penalties for breaking the rules are steep. Civil fines can reach $250,000 or twice the value of the illegal transaction, whichever is higher. For willful violations, criminal penalties can go up to $1 million in fines and 20 years in prison for individuals. This would most directly impact individual investors, mutual funds, pension funds, and financial institutions that currently hold shares in any of the listed companies.
ai_headline: Bill would ban Americans from holding stock in flagged Chinese firms
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.
