The President would have 90 days to publish a single combined public list of banned companies with identifying codes. Any company owned or controlled by a flagged firm would also fall under the ban.

title: Parents Over Platforms Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires app stores (like Apple's App Store or Google Play) to figure out whether their users are adults or minors when they sign up, and to share that age information with app developers when a user or their parent agrees to it. App stores would also have to give parents the ability to block their child from downloading or using certain apps. App developers, in turn, would be required to use that age information to give minors a different, more restricted experience, and they would be banned from showing personalized ads to anyone identified as under 18. Developers must also get parental consent before letting minors access content the developer has flagged as inappropriate for unsupervised use.

The Federal Trade Commission would enforce the rules, treating any violations the same way it handles unfair or deceptive business practices, which can result in significant financial penalties. App stores and developers get some legal protection if they make honest, good-faith efforts to follow the law, even if age estimates turn out to be wrong. The bill also overrides any existing or future state laws covering the same ground, making this a single national standard.

The people most directly affected are parents of minors, children and teenagers who use apps, app store operators like Apple and Google, and the companies that build apps. The bill would take effect two years after it becomes law, giving businesses time to build the required systems.
ai_headline: Senate bill sets national age-verification rules for app stores and developers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4349 requires app stores to verify user ages, share data with developers, and ban personalized ads for minors under 18.
ai_sections.what: S 4349 requires app stores to determine whether users are adults or minors at sign-up and share that age information with app developers when a user or parent consents. App stores must also give parents tools to block their child from downloading or using certain apps. Developers must use the age data to provide minors a more restricted experience, obtain parental consent before allowing minors to access content flagged as inappropriate, and are prohibited from showing personalized ads to anyone identified as under 18.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects parents of minors, children and teenagers who use apps, app store operators such as Apple and Google, and the companies that build apps. The Federal Trade Commission would be responsible for enforcing the law.
ai_sections.why: Violations would be treated the same as unfair or deceptive business practices under FTC enforcement, which can result in significant financial penalties. The bill would create a single national standard by overriding any existing or future state laws covering the same ground, and businesses would have two years after enactment to build the required systems.
ai_sections.cost: Violations can result in significant financial penalties enforced by the FTC. No specific dollar amounts or compliance costs are specified in the summary.

title: Campaign Event Contract Integrity Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets a specific type of financial conflict of interest involving political prediction markets. These are online platforms where people can bet real money on the outcomes of elections and other political events. The bill says that anyone working for or closely connected to a political campaign, party, or political action committee cannot trade these contracts if they have secret inside information about that campaign. This includes staffers, pollsters, consultants, fundraisers, and even family members who act on someone's behalf. They also cannot tip off other people to trade using that inside information, and no one can trade on their behalf as a workaround.

The bill also puts responsibilities on the prediction market platforms themselves. These companies would have to set up systems to spot and stop suspicious trading, keep records, and report shady activity to the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, which is the federal agency that oversees commodity and derivatives markets. Large traders on these platforms would have to disclose whether they are connected to a campaign or have access to private campaign information. The CFTC would have 180 days to write the specific rules spelling out how all of this works.

People who break the rules could face fines up to $250,000 or three times whatever profit they made, whichever is larger. They could also be banned from these markets and forced to give back any money they gained illegally. The bill does not affect regular researchers, journalists, or anyone trading based only on publicly available information. It mainly affects campaign insiders who might have an unfair advantage over ordinary bettors on political prediction platforms.
ai_headline: Bill bars campaign insiders from trading on political prediction markets
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8771 bans campaign insiders with private information from trading political prediction market contracts and sets fines up to $250,000.
ai_sections.what: HR 8771 prohibits people connected to political campaigns, parties, or PACs from trading contracts on political prediction markets if they hold nonpublic campaign information. The ban extends to tipping off others or using a third party as a workaround. Prediction market platforms would be required to monitor suspicious activity, keep records, and report to the Commodity Futures Trading Commission.
ai_sections.who: The bill covers campaign staffers, pollsters, consultants, fundraisers, and family members acting on an insider's behalf. Researchers, journalists, and anyone trading solely on public information are not affected.
ai_sections.why: Campaign insiders with nonpublic information could hold a structural advantage over ordinary bettors on these platforms. The bill creates disclosure requirements for large traders and directs the CFTC to issue implementing rules within 180 days.
ai_sections.cost: Violators face fines up to $250,000 or three times the profit gained, whichever is larger, and must return any illegally obtained money. They can also be banned from participating in these markets.

title: Rural Grants Transparency Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the U.S. Department of Agriculture to make its rural development grant programs easier to understand and navigate. Right now, small towns, rural nonprofits, and cooperatives often struggle to figure out how to apply for federal grants because the process is complicated and hard to find. The bill directs USDA to create a plain-language "Roadmap" for each grant program, posted on a single website, that explains what the program does, what documents applicants need, how applications are scored, and why applications commonly get rejected. This information must be updated at least four times a year.

The bill also requires USDA to send applicants clear notifications at each step of the process, including a confirmation when their application arrives, a notice if something is missing, and an explanation if they are turned down. If an application is denied, the agency must explain in plain language what hurt the application and how to do better next time. USDA also has to publish a searchable database showing who received grant awards, where projects are located, how much money was awarded, and what the project is for. For competitive grants, the agency must publicly post exactly how applications are scored and what factors carry the most weight.

This bill mainly affects rural communities, small local governments, rural nonprofits, cooperatives, and USDA staff who work in state and local offices. It does not create new grant money or change who qualifies for existing programs. The bill specifically states it must be carried out using resources USDA already has, with no new funding required, and must be fully in place within 180 days of becoming law.
ai_headline: USDA must simplify rural grant applications within 180 days
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9101 orders USDA to publish plain-language grant guides, applicant notices, and award data using existing staff and funds.
ai_sections.what: HR 9101 directs the U.S. Department of Agriculture to publish a plain-language "Roadmap" for each rural development grant program on a single website, covering eligibility, required documents, scoring criteria, and common reasons for rejection, updated at least four times per year. The bill also requires USDA to notify applicants at each stage of the process, explain denials in plain language, and maintain a searchable public database of grant awards showing recipient, location, amount, and project purpose. For competitive grants, USDA must publicly post how applications are scored and which factors carry the most weight.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects rural communities, small local governments, rural nonprofits, and cooperatives that apply for USDA rural development grants. USDA staff in state and local offices are also directly affected by the new disclosure and notification requirements.
ai_sections.why: Small towns and rural organizations currently face a complicated and hard-to-navigate application process that can result in missed funding opportunities. The bill does not create new grant money or change existing eligibility rules, so its effects are limited to how information is communicated and how the process is documented.
ai_sections.cost: The bill explicitly states it must be carried out using resources USDA already has, with no new funding authorized or required.

title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Energy relating to "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Walk-In Coolers and Walk-In Freezers".
ai_summary:
This bill cancels a rule that the Department of Energy issued in December 2024. That rule would have set new energy efficiency standards for walk-in coolers and freezers, which are the large refrigerated storage units commonly used in grocery stores, restaurants, warehouses, and food distribution centers. Congress used a law called the Congressional Review Act, which allows lawmakers to vote to block federal agency rules they disagree with. Once this resolution was signed, the Department of Energy's rule became void and unenforceable.

The people most directly affected are businesses that own or buy walk-in coolers and freezers. Under the canceled rule, manufacturers would have had to build these units to meet stricter energy efficiency requirements, and businesses purchasing new equipment would have had to meet those standards too. Supporters of canceling the rule argued the requirements would raise equipment costs. Supporters of the original rule argued it would lower electricity bills over time and reduce energy use.

Because the rule is now blocked, manufacturers and buyers of walk-in coolers and freezers will continue to follow the older, existing energy standards rather than the newer, stricter ones. This affects a wide range of industries, including supermarkets, restaurants, hotels, and food storage and distribution businesses across the country.
ai_headline: Walk-in cooler efficiency rule blocked, older standards remain in effect
last_action_text: Became Public Law No: 119-7.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress voided a Dec. 2024 DOE rule on walk-in cooler/freezer efficiency; older standards now apply to manufacturers and buyers.
ai_sections.what: The House passed HJRES 24, using the Congressional Review Act to cancel a Department of Energy rule issued in December 2024. That rule would have required walk-in coolers and freezers to meet stricter energy efficiency standards. With the rule now void, manufacturers and buyers must follow the older, existing energy standards instead.
ai_sections.who: Businesses that own, buy, or manufacture walk-in coolers and freezers are most directly affected. This includes supermarkets, restaurants, hotels, and food storage and distribution businesses across the country.
ai_sections.why: Supporters of canceling the rule argued the stricter requirements would raise equipment costs. Supporters of the original rule argued it would have lowered electricity bills over time and reduced overall energy use.

title: No Rigged Grocery Prices Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal for grocery stores and food delivery apps to use your personal data to charge you higher prices than other shoppers. For example, if a store's software noticed that you live in a wealthier zip code or that you've bought a certain product many times before, it could not use that information to raise the price just for you. The ban applies to large grocery stores of at least 15,000 square feet and to delivery services like Instacart or DoorDash. There are some exceptions, such as loyalty programs you voluntarily sign up for, temporary discounts meant to keep customers, or price differences based on real costs like shipping or taxes.

The bill also adds a couple of consumer protection rules for online grocery shopping. Stores must tell you if an item's availability has changed or if something is sold by weight. Delivery services must get your permission before swapping out an item you ordered for a different one. If a company breaks these rules, the Federal Trade Commission can treat it as an unfair or deceptive business practice and take legal action.

The bill affects everyday shoppers who buy groceries in stores or through delivery apps, as well as the large grocery chains and delivery companies that would need to change how their pricing software works. The bill also requires the Department of Labor to study whether electronic price tags on store shelves are reducing the number of grocery store jobs, though that study has no immediate effect on anyone.
ai_headline: Bill would ban grocery stores from using your data to raise your prices
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8895 would bar large grocery stores and delivery apps from using personal data to charge some shoppers higher prices than others.
ai_sections.what: HR 8895 would make it illegal for large grocery stores and food delivery apps to use shoppers' personal data to set higher prices for certain individuals. The ban covers stores of at least 15,000 square feet and delivery platforms such as Instacart or DoorDash. Exceptions exist for voluntary loyalty programs, temporary retention discounts, and price differences tied to real costs like shipping or taxes.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects everyday shoppers who buy groceries in stores or through delivery apps. It also applies to large grocery chains and delivery companies that would need to change how their pricing software works.
ai_sections.why: If enacted, the Federal Trade Commission could treat violations as unfair or deceptive business practices and take legal action against companies that break the rules. The bill also requires the Department of Labor to study whether electronic shelf price tags are reducing grocery store jobs, though that study has no immediate effect on anyone.

title: Campaign Funds Integrity Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes it illegal for political campaigns to use their donated money to place bets on prediction markets. Prediction markets are platforms where people can bet real money on the outcome of future events, like who will win an election or whether a certain law will pass. Under this bill, candidates and their campaign committees would be banned from using donor contributions to participate in those kinds of financial contracts.

The bill sets real consequences for breaking the rules. Campaigns that violate it could face civil fines through the Federal Election Commission. If someone knowingly and willfully breaks the law, they could also face criminal penalties, including up to five years in prison. The FEC would be responsible for writing specific rules explaining what is and is not allowed, and it could refer serious cases to the Justice Department for prosecution. Normal, low-risk financial activities like keeping money in a bank account or investing in a standard mutual fund would still be allowed.

This bill primarily affects political candidates, their official campaign committees, and other political committees that raise and spend money under federal election law. It could also affect campaign staff or treasurers who manage those funds. Everyday donors are not directly regulated by this bill, but it is designed to protect them by ensuring the money they give to a campaign is used for campaigning and not for speculative financial bets.
ai_headline: Bill bans campaigns from gambling donor funds on prediction markets
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8912 bars political campaigns from using donated money to bet on prediction markets, with fines and up to 5 years in prison for violations.
ai_sections.what: HR 8912 makes it illegal for political campaigns to use donated funds to place bets on prediction markets, which are platforms where people wager real money on future events like election outcomes. Standard low-risk financial activities such as bank accounts and mutual fund investments would still be permitted. The Federal Election Commission would write specific rules defining what is and is not allowed under the law.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly applies to political candidates, their official campaign committees, and other political committees that raise and spend money under federal election law. Campaign staff and treasurers who manage those funds could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Violations could result in civil fines through the FEC or criminal prosecution referred to the Justice Department. Knowingly and willfully breaking the law could carry criminal penalties including up to five years in prison.
ai_sections.cost: Campaigns that violate the law could face civil fines from the FEC. Criminal violations can result in penalties of up to five years in prison.
costChips: Up to 5 years in prison for willful violations

title: No U.S. Funding for UNIFIL Act
ai_summary:
This bill would stop the United States from sending any money to UNIFIL, which is a United Nations peacekeeping force that has been stationed in southern Lebanon since 1978. The UN Security Council voted in 2025 to end UNIFIL's mission by December 31, 2026. This bill says that starting October 1, 2027, no U.S. government agency — including the State Department or the military — can contribute any funds to UNIFIL or any organization that replaces it.

The bill also includes a backup rule in case the UN changes course and decides to keep UNIFIL going past December 2026. If that happens, the U.S. would automatically subtract an amount equal to UNIFIL's entire yearly budget from its broader UN peacekeeping payments. Basically, the U.S. would refuse to pay the UN back for whatever UNIFIL costs until the mission ends.

This bill mainly affects U.S. foreign policy and how the country spends money on international peacekeeping. It does not directly impact most Americans in their daily lives, but it could matter to people who follow U.S. involvement in Middle East security or United Nations affairs. UNIFIL currently includes troops from dozens of countries, and the U.S. contributes to its funding as part of its regular UN dues. This bill was introduced by members of both parties, suggesting some bipartisan agreement on the issue.
ai_headline: U.S. would cut all funding to UN Lebanon peacekeeping force starting 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on Armed Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9020 bars all U.S. funding to UNIFIL or any successor force beginning October 1, 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 9020 would prohibit any U.S. government agency, including the State Department and the military, from contributing funds to UNIFIL or any organization that replaces it, starting October 1, 2027. The UN Security Council voted in 2025 to end UNIFIL's mission by December 31, 2026. The bill also includes a backup provision that would automatically reduce U.S. broader UN peacekeeping payments by an amount equal to UNIFIL's full yearly budget if the UN extends the mission past December 2026.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to all U.S. government agencies that contribute to UN peacekeeping funding. It was introduced with support from members of both parties, indicating bipartisan agreement.
ai_sections.why: If the UN extends UNIFIL beyond December 2026, the U.S. would withhold an amount equal to UNIFIL's entire annual budget from its broader UN peacekeeping payments. The bill reshapes U.S. foreign policy on Middle East security and the country's financial role in United Nations peacekeeping operations.
ai_sections.cost: The U.S. currently contributes to UNIFIL's funding as part of its regular UN dues. If UNIFIL continues past December 2026, the U.S. would automatically reduce its broader UN peacekeeping payments by the full amount of UNIFIL's yearly budget.
costChips: U.S. funds UNIFIL via regular UN dues
Deduction equals UNIFIL's full yearly budget
Applies if mission extends past Dec 2026

title: To direct the Secretary of State to take actions with respect to certain foreign affairs matters.
ai_summary:
This bill gives the State Department several new rules and powers related to how it handles maps, flags, foreign aid, technology, and its own staff training. It would ban the State Department from using maps that show the body of water between the U.S. and Mexico as anything other than the "Gulf of America." It also limits which flags can be flown over State Department buildings to a specific approved list, which includes the U.S. flag, flags of other countries, tribal flags, and a few official government flags, but nothing else.

The bill also makes changes to foreign aid. It would stop the State Department from giving grants to nonprofits or international organizations unless those groups follow specific federal rules published in early 2026 that restrict funding related to abortion services and certain gender and diversity programs. It also gives the Secretary of State the legal authority to officially merge USAID, the main U.S. foreign aid agency, into the State Department, potentially eliminating USAID as a separate agency. Additionally, the bill allows small drones under 55 pounds to be used in certain international anti-drug efforts without previous legal restrictions that applied to larger aircraft.

Finally, the bill requires all U.S. diplomats and Foreign Service workers to complete a new training course on what it calls "America First Principles" before they can be posted at any overseas embassy or diplomatic mission. The State Department would also get new flexibility to pursue technology research and development contracts outside the usual competitive bidding process through a pilot program running until 2031. This bill affects U.S. diplomats, foreign aid organizations, USAID employees, and any nonprofits that receive State Department funding.
ai_headline: State Department bill rewrites flag, map, aid, and diplomat training rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9087 overhauls State Dept rules on maps, flags, foreign aid grants, USAID's existence, drones, and diplomat training.
ai_sections.what: HR 9087 imposes new rules on the State Department covering six areas: required map terminology, approved flags over department buildings, conditions on foreign aid grants, a potential merger of USAID into the State Department, drone use in anti-drug operations, and a mandatory training course for diplomats. Foreign aid grants to nonprofits and international organizations would be blocked unless those groups comply with specific federal rules from early 2026 restricting funding tied to abortion services and certain gender and diversity programs. A pilot program running until 2031 would let the State Department pursue technology research and development contracts without standard competitive bidding.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects U.S. diplomats and Foreign Service workers, USAID employees, nonprofits and international organizations that receive State Department funding, and foreign aid organizations. It also affects any entity involved in international anti-drug efforts where small drones under 55 pounds may now be used.
ai_sections.why: Diplomats and Foreign Service workers who have not completed the new "America First Principles" training would be barred from overseas postings, affecting the department's ability to staff embassies and diplomatic missions. Nonprofits and international organizations that do not comply with the 2026 federal funding restrictions would lose access to State Department grants, potentially reducing the pool of eligible partners for U.S. foreign assistance programs.

title: Strategy for Crisis Management Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill requires the Secretary of Defense to create a formal plan for handling potential crises in the South China Sea, specifically situations involving China that could put U.S. interests, people, or allies at risk. The plan would identify the most likely trouble spots in the region and build a detailed "playbook" for each one. Each playbook would lay out step-by-step response options, ranging from military moves to diplomatic and economic actions, and would include an analysis of the costs, risks, and potential humanitarian impacts of each option. The Defense Secretary would have to work with the State Department, Commerce Department, Treasury Department, and other agencies to build these plans.

The bill also requires the Pentagon to update these playbooks at least twice a year to keep them current. Within 120 days of the bill becoming law, the Defense Secretary must report to Congress on progress, and within one year must deliver a full summary of the completed strategy. The reports would be unclassified but could include a classified section for sensitive details.

This bill mainly affects the U.S. Department of Defense and other federal agencies involved in national security and foreign policy. It does not directly change laws or programs that affect everyday Americans at home. However, it could indirectly affect U.S. residents by shaping how the government responds to conflicts in a region that handles a large share of global trade, which can influence prices and economic stability.
ai_headline: Pentagon ordered to draft South China Sea crisis playbooks
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4609 directs the Defense Secretary to build and update crisis response playbooks for South China Sea scenarios involving China.
ai_sections.what: S 4609 requires the Secretary of Defense to develop a formal crisis response plan for potential conflicts in the South China Sea involving China. The plan must identify the most likely trouble spots and build a detailed playbook for each one, covering military, diplomatic, and economic response options along with analysis of costs, risks, and humanitarian impacts. Playbooks must be updated at least twice a year.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects the U.S. Department of Defense, which leads the effort, along with the State Department, Commerce Department, Treasury Department, and other national security and foreign policy agencies. It does not directly affect everyday Americans, though it could indirectly affect U.S. residents through its influence on how the government responds to conflicts in a region central to global trade.
ai_sections.why: The South China Sea handles a large share of global trade, meaning conflicts there can influence prices and economic stability for people in the United States. The bill shapes how multiple federal agencies coordinate responses to scenarios that could put U.S. interests, people, or allies at risk.

title: Aquatic Invasive Species Control and Prevention Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill updates a 1990 law that deals with invasive species in U.S. waters — things like Asian carp, zebra mussels, and other non-native creatures that can damage ecosystems and hurt fishing, recreation, and local economies. It creates two new grant programs: one to fund research into better methods for controlling or wiping out invasive species, and another to fund quick action when a new invasive species is first detected. It also requires the government to develop a national plan for inspecting and cleaning boats at launch sites, set up a monitoring system to track how invasive species move through major waterways like the Chicago Sanitary Ship Canal and the Mississippi River, and study whether existing rules make it harder to respond fast when a new threat shows up.

The bill affects a wide range of people and groups. Boaters and recreational fishers could see new inspection or decontamination checkpoints at boat launches on federal waterways. States, tribal governments, universities, and environmental nonprofits become eligible to apply for the new grant money. Federal agencies like the Fish and Wildlife Service would take on new responsibilities for planning and coordination. Commercial shipping is touched by a small provision encouraging ships to install better ballast water treatment systems, which is one way invasive species hitch rides between waterways.

Overall, the bill authorizes roughly $49.5 million per year through 2031 across all its programs. It does not guarantee that money will actually be spent — Congress would still need to approve the funding each year through the regular budget process. The bill is focused on preventing new invasions and responding more quickly when they happen, rather than changing penalties for individuals or creating new restrictions on everyday recreational use of public waters.
ai_headline: House bill targets invasive species with grants and faster response rules
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and in addition to the Committee on Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8876 authorizes up to $49.5M/year through 2031 to fund invasive species research, rapid response, and boat inspections on U.S. waterways.
ai_sections.what: HR 8876 updates a 1990 law on aquatic invasive species by creating two new grant programs — one for research into control methods and one for rapid response when a new species is first detected. It also directs the government to develop a national boat inspection and cleaning plan, build a monitoring system for major waterways like the Mississippi River and Chicago Sanitary Ship Canal, and study whether existing rules slow down emergency responses. The bill authorizes roughly $49.5 million per year through 2031 but does not guarantee spending, as Congress must still approve funds annually.
ai_sections.who: Boaters and recreational fishers may encounter new inspection or decontamination checkpoints at boat launches on federal waterways. States, tribal governments, universities, environmental nonprofits, and federal agencies like the Fish and Wildlife Service are directly affected as grant applicants or new coordinators. Commercial shipping is touched by a provision encouraging better ballast water treatment systems.
ai_sections.why: Invasive species such as Asian carp and zebra mussels can damage aquatic ecosystems and harm fishing, recreation, and local economies. Faster detection and response mechanisms could change how quickly federal and state agencies are able to act when a new threat appears in a waterway.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes roughly $49.5 million per year across all programs through 2031. That ceiling requires separate annual congressional approval before any money is actually spent.

press_names: Aquatic Invasive Species Control and Prevention Act

title: Prevent Government Shutdowns Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would make it so the federal government can never fully shut down due to a failure to pass a budget. If Congress misses its deadline to fund the government, money would automatically keep flowing at the same rates as the previous year's budget. This automatic funding would renew itself every 14 days until Congress passes a real spending bill. The goal is to keep federal agencies open and workers paid even when lawmakers cannot agree on a new budget.

To pressure Congress into doing its job faster, the bill adds real consequences for members of Congress and certain White House budget officials when the automatic funding kicks in. During that period, those officials cannot use government money or even campaign funds for official travel, with a few narrow exceptions like returning to Washington or responding to a national security emergency. Congress would also be forced to stay in session and could not take breaks longer than 23 hours, and lawmakers could only vote on a very short list of urgent matters until the budget standoff is resolved.

This bill affects the entire federal government and everyone who depends on it. That includes federal workers who would otherwise be furloughed, military personnel, Social Security and food assistance recipients, national park visitors, and anyone waiting on government services. Members of Congress and White House budget staff would face direct personal restrictions on their activities. Ordinary Americans would mostly feel the effect through continued government services that would otherwise stop during a shutdown.
ai_headline: Bill would end federal shutdowns by auto-funding government at prior-year rates
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Appropriations, and in addition to the Committees on Rules, House Administration, Oversight and Government Reform, and the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5130 keeps the federal government funded automatically at prior-year levels if Congress misses its budget deadline, renewing every 14 days.
ai_sections.what: HR 5130 would prevent full federal government shutdowns by automatically continuing funding at the same rates as the previous year's budget whenever Congress fails to pass a spending bill by its deadline. That automatic funding would renew every 14 days until a real budget is enacted. The bill also restricts congressional and White House budget officials from official travel and limits what Congress can vote on during any automatic-funding period.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects all federal agencies, workers, military personnel, Social Security and food assistance recipients, and anyone relying on government services. Members of Congress and certain White House budget officials face direct personal restrictions while automatic funding is active.
ai_sections.why: Federal workers and service recipients who would otherwise face furloughs or interrupted payments would instead see continued operations under the automatic funding mechanism. Members of Congress would face travel bans and mandatory session requirements, and could only vote on a narrow list of urgent matters until the budget impasse ends.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would sustain federal spending at prior-year rates automatically during any lapse period, meaning existing funding levels continue without new congressional approval until a budget passes.
costChips: No new appropriation needed to trigger

title: Prevent Government Shutdowns Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill is designed to prevent the federal government from shutting down when Congress fails to pass a budget on time. Right now, if Congress misses the deadline to fund the government, agencies lose their money and must stop most operations. Under this bill, funding would automatically continue at the same rate as the previous year's budget, renewing every 14 days until Congress passes a real spending bill. This keeps government programs and services running without interruption.

The bill also puts pressure on Congress to actually do its job. If the government is running on this automatic funding, lawmakers would not be allowed to vote on most other business until they pass a budget. They also could not take long recesses or adjourn for more than 23 hours at a stretch, and they could not use government funds or even campaign funds for most official travel. The idea is to make a budget standoff uncomfortable enough that Congress is motivated to resolve it quickly.

This bill affects nearly everyone in some way, since it touches all federal programs funded through the normal budget process, including agencies that handle national defense, transportation, health programs, and more. It directly affects members of Congress and their staff, as well as Office of Management and Budget employees, by restricting their travel and limiting what legislation they can work on during a funding gap. People who rely on federal services or work for federal agencies would be the main beneficiaries, since the bill aims to keep those services running even when budget negotiations break down.
ai_headline: Automatic funding bill would end government shutdowns under S 2721
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2721 auto-renews prior-year funding every 14 days during a budget lapse and bars most congressional business until a real budget passes.
ai_sections.what: S 2721 would automatically continue government funding at the prior year's spending level if Congress misses its budget deadline, renewing that funding every 14 days until a formal spending bill is enacted. The bill also restricts congressional activity during any funding gap, barring most votes on other legislation, limiting recesses to no more than 23 hours, and prohibiting use of government or campaign funds for most official travel.
ai_sections.who: The restrictions apply directly to members of Congress, their staff, and Office of Management and Budget employees. People who rely on federal services or work for federal agencies are affected, as the bill governs the continuity of all programs funded through the normal budget process, including those covering national defense, transportation, and health programs.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, a missed budget deadline cuts off agency funding and halts most government operations. The combined pressure of automatic funding and restrictions on congressional activity is intended to create conditions that motivate lawmakers to resolve budget disputes more quickly.

title: Prevent Government Shutdowns Act
ai_summary:
This bill tries to prevent government shutdowns by automatically keeping the government funded if Congress misses its budget deadline. Right now, when Congress fails to pass a spending bill on time, the government can run out of money and shut down, sending workers home and stopping services. Under this bill, if that happens, federal programs would automatically continue receiving money at the same rate as the previous year's budget, renewing every 14 days until Congress passes a real spending bill. This means agencies could keep running without interruption while lawmakers work out a deal.

The bill also puts pressure on Congress to actually finish its budget work. During any period when the automatic funding kicks in, members of Congress and White House budget officials would be banned from taking official trips away from Washington, and they could not use campaign funds for travel either. Congress would also be largely blocked from voting on anything other than the spending bills needed to end the budget standoff. Lawmakers could not take long recesses or adjourn for more than 23 hours at a time. The idea is to make it uncomfortable and inconvenient for politicians to leave town without finishing their job.

The people most directly affected are federal workers and anyone who uses government services, since they would no longer face sudden shutdowns. Members of Congress, their staff, and Office of Management and Budget employees would face the travel restrictions. Regular Americans who rely on programs like food assistance or federal loans would also be affected, since those programs would keep running automatically instead of stopping when budget deadlines are missed.
ai_headline: Bill would auto-fund government when budget deadlines are missed
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Appropriations, and in addition to the Committees on Rules, House Administration, Oversight and Government Reform, and the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5870 would keep federal programs funded automatically every 14 days if Congress misses its budget deadline, avoiding shutdowns.
ai_sections.what: HR 5870 would automatically continue federal funding at prior-year levels if Congress fails to pass a spending bill on time. The automatic funding would renew every 14 days until lawmakers pass a new budget. Federal agencies would keep operating without interruption during that period.
ai_sections.who: Federal workers and people who rely on government services, including food assistance and federal loan programs, would no longer face sudden shutdowns. ai_sections.why: During automatic funding periods, lawmakers and White House budget officials would be banned from official travel outside Washington and blocked from using campaign funds for travel. Congress would also be barred from voting on most other legislation, and recesses would be limited to no more than 23 hours at a time.

title: Colorado Wilderness Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would set aside more than 700,000 acres of land in Colorado as official wilderness areas. That means these lands, mostly managed by the Bureau of Land Management and the U.S. Forest Service, would be permanently protected from development, road-building, and most motorized use. The bill creates more than 25 new named wilderness areas and expands a few existing ones, covering places like the Dolores River Canyon, Redcloud Peak, and Browns Canyon. Some additional areas are labeled "potential wilderness," meaning they could receive full wilderness protection later once certain temporary uses end.

People most directly affected are those who use or work on these lands. Hikers, campers, and wildlife watchers would generally gain more protected land to enjoy. However, off-road vehicle users, mining companies, and energy developers would no longer be allowed to operate in the newly protected areas. Ranchers who currently graze livestock on some of these lands can continue doing so under existing rules. Water rights that were already legally established before the bill passes would not be changed, and existing water facilities like irrigation ditches can still be maintained.

The bill also includes specific rules to make sure military helicopters can still fly over the new wilderness areas for training. Colorado state government keeps its authority over wildlife and fish management. Lands within the wilderness boundaries that are privately owned or owned by Colorado would only become part of the wilderness if the federal government purchases them voluntarily.
ai_headline: HR 7425 would permanently protect over 700,000 Colorado acres as wilderness
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7425 designates 700,000+ Colorado acres as wilderness, banning development and most motorized use across 25+ new named areas.
ai_sections.what: HR 7425 would designate more than 700,000 acres in Colorado as official wilderness, creating over 25 new named wilderness areas and expanding some existing ones. Covered locations include the Dolores River Canyon, Redcloud Peak, and Browns Canyon. Some areas receive a "potential wilderness" label, allowing full protection once current temporary uses end.
ai_sections.who: Hikers, campers, and wildlife watchers would gain access to expanded protected land, while off-road vehicle users, mining companies, and energy developers would be barred from the newly designated areas. Ranchers with existing grazing permits may continue operations, and established water rights and irrigation facilities would remain unaffected.
ai_sections.why: Permanently barring development, road-building, and most motorized use would limit future economic activity on these lands. Private or state-owned land within the wilderness boundaries would not be included unless the federal government voluntarily purchases it.

title: A bill to redesignate a playground in the New River Gorge National Park and Preserve in the State of West Virginia as the "Hearts of Gold Playground: In Honor of West Virginia Children and Families Impacted by Childhood Cancer".
ai_summary:
This bill would officially rename a specific playground inside New River Gorge National Park and Preserve in West Virginia. The playground sits in the Grandview area of the park, near the amphitheater. Its new official name would become the "Hearts of Gold Playground: In Honor of West Virginia Children and Families Impacted by Childhood Cancer." All government maps, documents, and records would be updated to use the new name.

The bill does not change how the playground is run, who can use it, or how the park operates. It does not allocate any money or change any park policies. It simply gives the playground a new official name to recognize children and families in West Virginia who have been affected by childhood cancer.

This bill mainly affects visitors to New River Gorge National Park, West Virginia residents, and park staff who manage signage and official records. It was introduced by both of West Virginia's U.S. Senators and was sent to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources for review.
ai_headline: West Virginia playground to be renamed in honor of childhood cancer families
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

title: Mullica River Watershed Wild and Scenic River Study Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the federal government to study the Mullica River watershed in New Jersey to see if it qualifies for official "Wild and Scenic River" designation. That is a federal status that protects certain rivers from development like dams or other construction that could change their natural character. The study would look at the Mullica River and many of its connected streams and tributaries, covering a large portion of the Pinelands region of southern New Jersey. The Secretary of the Interior would have three years to complete the study and report the findings to Congress after funding is provided.

It is important to understand that this bill does not actually designate the river as wild and scenic. It only starts the process of studying whether that designation makes sense. No new restrictions on land use or water use would automatically result from this bill passing. Congress would need to act again after seeing the study results before any formal protections could take effect.

This bill would most directly affect residents, landowners, outdoor recreationists, and local governments in the counties surrounding the Mullica River watershed, which runs through Atlantic, Burlington, and Ocean counties in New Jersey. Environmental researchers, state agencies, and federal land managers would also be involved in conducting the study. Anyone who lives near, works along, or recreates on these waterways could eventually be affected if a full designation follows from the study's findings.
ai_headline: Federal study ordered for Mullica River wild and scenic designation
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4595 orders a study of the Mullica River watershed in NJ to assess wild and scenic status; no protections take effect yet.
ai_sections.what: S 4595 directs the Secretary of the Interior to study the Mullica River watershed in New Jersey to determine whether it qualifies for Wild and Scenic River designation, a federal status that shields rivers from dams and development that could alter their natural character. The study covers the Mullica River and many of its connected streams and tributaries across a large portion of the Pinelands region. The Secretary has three years after funding is provided to complete the study and report findings to Congress.
ai_sections.who: Residents, landowners, outdoor recreationists, and local governments in Atlantic, Burlington, and Ocean counties would be most directly affected. Environmental researchers, state agencies, and federal land managers would also be involved in conducting the study.
ai_sections.why: If Congress acts on the study results and grants formal designation, restrictions on development and construction along these waterways could follow. This bill alone creates no new land use or water use restrictions; a separate act of Congress would be required before any protections take effect.
ai_sections.cost: The summary references that the three-year study period begins after funding is provided but does not specify an amount, source, or who pays.

press_names: Mullica River Watershed Wild and Scenic River Study Act

title: WAGES Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new tax credit for employers who run or participate in official apprenticeship programs registered with the federal government. For every quarter they employ apprentices, qualifying businesses can get back 50 cents for every dollar they spend on apprentice wages, up to $5,000 per apprentice per quarter, plus up to $50,000 per quarter in other program costs like classroom instruction, on-the-job training expenses, and extra pay for experienced workers who mentor apprentices. If the credit is larger than what the employer owes in payroll taxes, the government refunds the difference. The credit only applies to the first two years of each apprentice's participation in a program, and businesses cannot claim it for expenses already covered by other federal programs like the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act.

The bill also makes it easier for employers to give apprentices small awards, like tools or equipment tied to their training, without those gifts being taxed as regular income. The tax-free limit for these kinds of awards is raised to $1,500, or up to $5,000 if the employer has a formal award plan in place.

The people most directly affected are private-sector employers in fields like construction, manufacturing, healthcare, technology, and the skilled trades, as well as workers who want to earn while they learn a profession. Apprentices themselves are not receiving a direct tax break, but the bill is designed to make it cheaper for companies to hire and train them, which could expand the number of apprenticeship slots available. Government agencies are generally excluded from claiming the credit, though colleges, universities, and hospitals run by government entities may qualify.
ai_headline: Senate bill offers employers up to $5,000 per apprentice each quarter in new tax credits
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4463 gives employers a 50% wage credit up to $5,000 per apprentice per quarter for federally registered apprenticeship programs.
ai_sections.what: S 4463 creates a refundable quarterly tax credit for employers running or participating in federally registered apprenticeship programs. The credit covers 50 cents per dollar spent on apprentice wages, up to $5,000 per apprentice per quarter, plus up to $50,000 per quarter in program costs such as classroom instruction, on-the-job training, and mentor pay. The credit applies only during an apprentice's first two years in a program and cannot be combined with funding already received through other federal programs like the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act.
ai_sections.who: Private-sector employers in construction, manufacturing, healthcare, technology, and the skilled trades are the primary targets, while government agencies are generally excluded, though government-run colleges, universities, and hospitals may qualify. Workers entering apprenticeship programs are indirectly affected, as the bill is designed to expand the number of available apprenticeship slots.
ai_sections.why: By reducing the wage and training costs employers face, the bill could increase the number of apprenticeship positions companies are willing to offer. The refundable structure means employers receive a payment from the government when the credit exceeds their payroll tax liability.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government pays the credit directly to employers when it exceeds payroll taxes owed, covering 50% of apprentice wages up to $5,000 per apprentice per quarter and up to $50,000 per quarter in additional program costs. Employers cannot claim the credit for costs already covered by other federal programs.

title: WAGES Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill creates a new tax credit for businesses that hire and train workers through official apprenticeship programs registered with the federal government. For every quarter a qualifying apprentice is on the job, the employer can get back 50 cents on the dollar for wages paid to that apprentice, up to $5,000 per apprentice per quarter. Employers can also get a 50% credit for other program costs, like classroom instruction, on-the-job training expenses, and extra pay given to experienced workers who mentor apprentices. If the credit is larger than what the employer owes in payroll taxes, the government refunds the difference. The credit applies to apprentices for up to two years from when they start the program.

The bill also makes a small change to how certain gifts or awards given to apprentices during their training are taxed. Right now, employers can give employees tax-free achievement awards up to $400. Under this bill, employers could give apprentices training-related awards worth up to $1,500 tax-free, or up to $5,000 if the award is part of a formal award plan. There are rules to prevent abuse, such as blocking the credit if the employer already received federal funding for the same wages or expenses.

This bill would mainly affect private-sector employers in industries like construction, manufacturing, healthcare, and information technology, as well as the workers who enter those fields through apprenticeships. Business owners who currently avoid apprenticeship programs because of the startup costs could find it more affordable. Workers without four-year college degrees who are looking for a path to a skilled, higher-paying job could benefit if more employers choose to offer these programs. Federal and state governments are generally not eligible for the credit, though some hospitals and universities are.
ai_headline: New tax credit would pay back half of apprentice wages, up to $5,000 per quarter
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8624 gives private employers a 50% tax credit on apprentice wages and training costs, up to $5,000 per apprentice per quarter.
ai_sections.what: HR 8624 creates a tax credit for employers who hire apprentices through federally registered apprenticeship programs. The credit covers 50% of wages paid to qualifying apprentices, capped at $5,000 per apprentice per quarter, and 50% of other program costs such as classroom instruction, on-the-job training expenses, and pay given to mentors. The credit is refundable, meaning employers receive the difference as a cash refund if the credit exceeds their payroll tax liability. It applies for up to two years from an apprentice's start date, and employers cannot claim it for wages or expenses already covered by federal funding.
ai_sections.who: Private-sector employers in construction, manufacturing, healthcare, and information technology are the primary targets of the bill. Some hospitals and universities are eligible, but federal and state governments are not. Workers without four-year college degrees who enter skilled trades through apprenticeships are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Employers who currently find apprenticeship startup costs prohibitive may face different cost calculations if the credit is enacted. Workers seeking pathways to skilled, higher-paying jobs without a four-year degree would see more or fewer opportunities depending on how employers respond to the changed incentives.
ai_sections.cost: The government would refund the difference when the credit exceeds an employer's payroll tax liability, making the credit a direct federal expenditure in those cases. Employers can also give apprentices training-related awards worth up to $1,500 tax-free, or up to $5,000 under a formal award plan, compared to the current $400 limit for standard employee achievement awards.

title: SLUSH FUND Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a 100 percent tax on money received from certain legal settlements. Specifically, it targets funds that come from lawsuits filed by a current or former U.S. president, their family members, or businesses they control, when those lawsuits were filed against the federal government or a federal agency. That means if someone receives a payout from one of those settlement funds, the entire amount would be taxed away by the federal government. The bill also makes clear that this money would not count as regular taxable income under normal income tax rules, so only this special 100 percent tax applies. Anyone who deliberately avoids paying this tax faces an additional penalty equal to 50 percent of what they owe.

The bill also requires the people managing these settlement funds to report payments to the IRS and send notices to anyone who receives money, similar to how a bank sends you a tax form. Those reports would also be made available to the public within one month. Managers who fail to file the required paperwork could face a $10,000 fine per violation.

This bill would mainly affect any individual or organization that receives money from a legal settlement fund connected to a lawsuit that a president or their family or affiliated businesses brought against the U.S. government. It would apply to payments made on or after May 20, 2026. Ordinary Americans who have no connection to presidential lawsuits or settlement funds would not be directly affected.
ai_headline: Bill would tax away 100% of settlements from presidential lawsuits against U.S. government
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4616 would impose a 100% tax on any settlement money paid out from lawsuits a president or family filed against the federal government.
ai_sections.what: S 4616 would create a 100 percent tax on money received from certain legal settlement funds connected to lawsuits filed by a current or former U.S. president, their family members, or businesses they control against the federal government or a federal agency. The taxed amount would not count as regular taxable income, so only this special tax applies. Payments made on or after May 20, 2026 would be covered.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects any individual or organization that receives money from one of these qualifying settlement funds. Ordinary Americans with no connection to presidential lawsuits or settlement funds would not be directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Anyone who receives a covered settlement payment would lose the entire amount to the 100 percent tax. Managers of settlement funds who fail to file required IRS reports face fines, and recipients who deliberately evade the tax face an additional penalty.
ai_sections.cost: Recipients who deliberately avoid the tax face an additional penalty equal to 50 percent of what they owe. Fund managers who fail to file required paperwork face a fine of $10,000 per violation.

title: SLUSH FUND Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a 100 percent tax on money received from certain legal settlements. Specifically, it targets anyone who receives a payment from a fund that was set up because of a lawsuit filed against the U.S. government by a current or former president, their close family members, or a company those individuals control. The tax equals the full amount received, which effectively wipes out any payout. The money is not counted as regular income for other tax purposes, but a separate 50 percent penalty on top of the tax can be added for anyone who willfully avoids paying it.

The bill also sets up a reporting system. Whoever manages the settlement fund must file paperwork with the IRS listing who got paid and how much, and that information must be made public within one month. Recipients must also receive a written notice by January 31 of the following year informing them the payments are taxable. Fund managers who fail to file the required reports face a $10,000 fine per violation.

This bill would affect any person or organization that receives a payout from a settlement or court verdict in a case where a president, their spouse, close relatives, or a business they control sued the federal government. That could include the individuals themselves, family members, or business entities connected to them. Ordinary Americans with no connection to a president or their family would not be affected by this bill.

title: Dietary Supplements Access Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change the tax rules so that money spent on dietary supplements counts as a qualified medical expense. That matters because people with Health Savings Accounts (HSAs), Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs), Health Reimbursement Arrangements (HRAs), or Archer Medical Savings Accounts can already use those accounts to pay for medical expenses with pre-tax dollars. If this bill passes, they could use those same accounts to buy dietary supplements like vitamins, minerals, and herbal products, saving money by not paying income tax on those purchases. There is a cap, though: only up to $500 per year counts ($250 for married people filing taxes separately). Energy drinks, sodas, and similar products are specifically excluded, even if they are marketed as supplements.

This bill would affect people who have employer-sponsored FSAs or HRAs, or who have opened their own HSA, typically through a high-deductible health insurance plan. These account holders could pay for eligible supplements using pre-tax dollars, which effectively reduces the real cost of those products. People without any of these special accounts would not see a direct financial benefit from this bill.

The changes would take effect starting January 1, 2027. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been sent to the Finance Committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on or become law. It does not require a doctor's recommendation to use the benefit, and it does not change what supplements are legal to sell, only how they are treated for tax purposes.
ai_headline: Senate bill would let HSA and FSA holders buy supplements tax-free
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4587 would let HSA, FSA, HRA, and Archer MSA holders buy dietary supplements with pre-tax dollars, up to $500/year, starting 2027.
ai_sections.what: S 4587 would classify dietary supplements — including vitamins, minerals, and herbal products — as qualified medical expenses under federal tax law. Account holders could then use pre-tax dollars from HSAs, FSAs, HRAs, or Archer Medical Savings Accounts to buy them, up to $500 per year, or $250 for married people filing separately. Energy drinks, sodas, and similar products are explicitly excluded even if marketed as supplements.
ai_sections.who: People who hold employer-sponsored FSAs or HRAs, or who have opened their own HSA — typically through a high-deductible health insurance plan — would be eligible. People without any of these accounts would not see a direct financial benefit.
ai_sections.why: Purchasing eligible supplements with pre-tax dollars would effectively reduce the out-of-pocket cost of those products for eligible account holders. No doctor's recommendation would be required to use the benefit, and the bill would not change which supplements are legal to sell.

title: No Tax on Border Patrol Agent Overtime Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make overtime pay earned by U.S. Border Patrol agents tax-free at the federal level. Right now, overtime income is taxed like regular income for most workers. This bill would change the tax code so that certain extra pay Border Patrol agents receive above their base salary would not count as taxable income. That includes things like supplemental pay, premium pay, and standard overtime pay specific to how Border Patrol agents are compensated under federal law.

The change would apply starting with the 2026 tax year. The bill builds on a broader concept already in the tax code called "qualified overtime compensation," which was recently established to exempt some overtime pay from taxes for workers generally. This bill specifically adds Border Patrol agents to that category using the unique pay structure that already exists for them under federal employment law. It does not include hazardous duty pay, which would still be taxed.

This bill directly affects active Border Patrol agents who earn extra pay beyond their base salary. If passed, those agents would take home more of their overtime earnings because that portion of their paycheck would no longer be subject to federal income tax. It would also affect federal tax revenue, since the government would collect less in taxes from that group of workers. The bill was introduced in the Senate and referred to the Finance Committee, so it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: Senate bill would exempt Border Patrol overtime from federal income tax
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4580 would make overtime and supplemental pay for U.S. Border Patrol agents tax-free starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: S 4580 would remove federal income tax from overtime, supplemental, and premium pay earned by U.S. Border Patrol agents, starting with the 2026 tax year. It uses the agents' existing pay structure under federal employment law to define which earnings qualify. Hazardous duty pay is specifically excluded and would remain taxable.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects active U.S. Border Patrol agents who earn pay beyond their base salary. It was introduced in the Senate and referred to the Finance Committee, so it has not yet become law.
ai_sections.why: Agents would keep a larger share of their overtime earnings because that income would no longer be subject to federal income tax. The federal government would collect less tax revenue from this group of workers as a result.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would collect less in tax revenue from Border Patrol agents because their overtime and supplemental pay would no longer be taxed. The summary does not specify a dollar amount for that revenue loss.

title: No Tax on Border Patrol Agent Overtime Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make certain extra pay received by U.S. Border Patrol agents tax-free. Specifically, it targets pay that goes above their basic salary, including overtime pay, supplemental pay, and premium pay that agents earn under a special federal pay system designed for Border Patrol work. Hazardous duty pay would not be included in the tax break. The change would take effect starting with the 2026 tax year.

The bill does this by adding Border Patrol agent overtime and extra pay to an existing tax code category called "qualified overtime compensation." That category was created to allow overtime earnings to be excluded from federal income tax. This bill expands that existing rule to cover the unique pay structure that Border Patrol agents work under, since their pay is calculated differently than a standard hourly worker covered by regular labor law.

The people directly affected are active Border Patrol agents who receive pay above their base salary. For those agents, this could mean a larger take-home paycheck since that extra pay would no longer be subject to federal income tax. The bill does not affect the general public's taxes, and it does not change Border Patrol staffing levels, enforcement policies, or agency budgets. It is currently in the early stage of the legislative process, referred to the House Ways and Means Committee, and has not yet become law.

The bill works by adding Border Patrol extra pay to an existing tax code category called "qualified overtime compensation," expanding a rule that already allows some overtime earnings to be excluded from federal income tax.

title: Freedom from Taxes Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would eliminate several federal taxes that currently apply to certain heavily regulated firearms and accessories covered under the National Firearms Act (NFA). Right now, the NFA requires people to pay a $200 tax when they transfer or make items like silencers, short-barreled rifles, short-barreled shotguns, and machine guns. It also requires dealers and manufacturers of these items to pay a special annual tax. This bill would set all of those taxes to zero, effectively making them free to process.

The bill would also expand an existing excise tax exemption so that it covers all NFA transfers, not just a narrow category. These changes would kick in about 90 days after the law is passed. Importantly, the bill does not change any other NFA rules, such as the background check process, the registration requirement, or the overall list of regulated items. Those rules would remain in place.

This primarily affects gun owners, buyers, and sellers who deal in NFA-regulated items, as well as the firearms dealers and manufacturers who currently pay the special occupational tax. It would also reduce federal tax revenue, since the government currently collects money from each of these transactions and registrations. Average gun owners who only buy standard firearms not covered by the NFA would not be directly affected.
ai_headline: Bill would zero out federal taxes on silencers and short-barreled firearms
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8921 sets NFA transfer and manufacturing taxes to zero but keeps background checks and registration rules intact.
ai_sections.what: HR 8921 would set to zero the $200 federal tax currently charged each time a silencer, short-barreled rifle, short-barreled shotgun, or machine gun is transferred or made under the National Firearms Act. It would also eliminate the special annual occupational tax paid by dealers and manufacturers of these items, and expand an existing excise tax exemption to cover all NFA transfers. Changes would take effect roughly 90 days after enactment.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects gun owners, buyers, sellers, dealers, and manufacturers who deal in NFA-regulated items. Owners of standard firearms not covered by the NFA would not be directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Because the federal government currently collects revenue from each NFA transfer and from annual dealer and manufacturer registrations, eliminating those taxes would reduce federal tax revenue. All other NFA requirements, including background checks, registration, and the list of regulated items, would remain unchanged.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government currently collects a $200 tax per NFA transfer and annual occupational taxes from dealers and manufacturers; this bill would reduce that revenue by setting all of those taxes to zero.
