title: Restoring the American Homebuyers Dream Act
ai_summary:
This bill would change a longstanding rule about tax privacy. Right now, the IRS keeps tax information confidential, even for people who file using an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number, or ITIN. ITINs are issued to people who do not have a Social Security number but still pay U.S. taxes. This bill would allow the Department of Homeland Security to submit written requests to the IRS and receive personal information about ITIN holders, including their name, address, filing status, and information about spouses or adult dependents. This would be a significant change to Section 6103 of the tax code, which has traditionally protected all taxpayer data from being shared with law enforcement agencies for non-tax purposes.

The stated goal of the bill is immigration enforcement. The bill's findings section argues that people in the country without legal status have used ITINs to obtain home loans, which the bill's authors claim has contributed to rising housing costs for American citizens. The information shared with DHS could be used to identify and locate individuals for immigration proceedings.

The people most directly affected would be ITIN holders, a group that includes undocumented immigrants but also some legal residents, foreign nationals with U.S. income, and others who cannot get a Social Security number. U.S. citizens would not be affected since they use Social Security numbers, not ITINs. The bill could also affect people who share a tax return with an ITIN holder, such as spouses or adult dependents listed on a filing.
ai_headline: Bill would let DHS request tax data on ITIN holders from IRS
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8920 would let DHS request personal tax data on ITIN holders from the IRS for immigration enforcement.
ai_sections.what: HR 8920 would amend Section 6103 of the tax code to allow the Department of Homeland Security to submit written requests to the IRS for personal information about Individual Taxpayer Identification Number holders. That information could include names, addresses, filing status, and details about spouses or adult dependents listed on a return. This would be a significant departure from longstanding IRS rules that protect all taxpayer data from being shared with law enforcement for non-tax purposes.
ai_sections.who: ITIN holders would be most directly affected, a group that includes undocumented immigrants, some legal residents, foreign nationals with U.S. income, and others ineligible for a Social Security number. People who share a tax return with an ITIN holder, such as spouses or adult dependents, could also have their information shared. U.S. citizens who file using Social Security numbers would not be affected.
ai_sections.why: The bill's stated purpose is immigration enforcement, and information obtained from the IRS could be used to identify and locate individuals for immigration proceedings. The bill's findings also assert that ITIN holders have used the numbers to obtain home loans, which the authors claim has contributed to rising housing costs, though this framing is the bill authors' own and not an independent finding.

title: Affordable Housing Credit Carryback Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes a tax rule that affects how the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) can be used. Right now, if a business or investor earns this tax credit but cannot use all of it in the current year, they can only carry it forward to reduce future tax bills. This bill would allow them to instead carry the credit back up to five years, meaning they could apply it to taxes they already paid in the past and potentially get a refund.

The main people affected are real estate developers and investors who finance affordable housing projects. These projects can be expensive and unpredictable, so sometimes a developer ends up with more tax credits than they can use in a given year. Being able to go back five years and apply those credits to past tax bills would give them quicker access to cash, which supporters say could encourage more affordable housing construction.

Renters and low-income households are indirectly affected, since the LIHTC program is one of the biggest tools the federal government uses to fund the building of affordable apartments. If the change makes it more financially attractive to invest in these projects, it could influence how many affordable units get built. The bill does not directly change rents, eligibility rules, or housing assistance programs.
ai_headline: Tax credit bill would let affordable housing investors claim past-year refunds
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9012 lets LIHTC holders carry the credit back up to five years to claim refunds on taxes already paid, instead of only forward.
ai_sections.what: HR 9012 changes the carry rules for the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC). Currently, unused credits can only be carried forward to reduce future tax bills. This bill would also allow those credits to be carried back up to five years, letting holders apply them to taxes already paid and potentially receive a refund.
ai_sections.who: Real estate developers and investors who finance affordable housing projects are the primary people affected. Renters and low-income households are indirectly affected because the LIHTC program is one of the federal government's largest tools for funding affordable apartment construction.
ai_sections.why: Allowing a five-year carryback would give developers quicker access to cash when they end up with more credits than they can use in a given year. This could influence how many affordable housing units get built, though the bill does not directly change rents, eligibility rules, or housing assistance programs.

title: Young Adult Tax Credit Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new monthly tax credit for young adults between the ages of 18 and 24. Starting in 2027, eligible individuals would receive $500 per month, either paid out monthly or as a yearly lump sum. The amount would be adjusted over time to keep up with inflation. Young adults can receive the money directly if they file their own taxes and are not claimed as a dependent by someone else. If a parent or guardian claims an 18-to-24-year-old as a dependent on their taxes, the parent would receive the credit instead. The payments would be sent electronically and cannot be seized by debt collectors or garnished by courts.

To get the credit, a person must be a U.S. citizen, national, or resident between 18 and 24 years old. The IRS would set up an online portal where people can sign up, update their information, and choose between monthly payments or a single yearly payment. If someone receives more money than they are actually entitled to due to fraud or intentional misuse, they would have to pay it back. The portal would be available in multiple languages and work on mobile phones.

The bill also requires the IRS to run a public outreach campaign to make sure people know about the credit, especially those who do not normally file taxes or have bank accounts. U.S. territories like Puerto Rico and American Samoa are also included, with the federal government sending funds to those governments to distribute locally. The bill was introduced in May 2026 and has been sent to the House Ways and Means Committee for review.
ai_headline: Monthly $500 tax credit for adults 18–24 proposed starting in 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9031 would give eligible U.S. adults ages 18–24 a $500 monthly tax credit beginning in 2027, paid electronically and protected from debt seizure.
ai_sections.what: HR 9031 would create a monthly federal tax credit of $500 for eligible adults between the ages of 18 and 24, beginning in 2027. Recipients could choose monthly payments or a single annual lump sum, with the amount adjusted over time for inflation. Payments would be made electronically and could not be seized by debt collectors or garnished by courts.
ai_sections.who: Eligible individuals must be U.S. citizens, nationals, or residents between 18 and 24 years old who file their own taxes and are not claimed as a dependent. If a parent or guardian claims an 18-to-24-year-old as a dependent, the parent receives the credit instead.
ai_sections.why: Young adults who do not typically file taxes or hold bank accounts may still access the credit through an IRS online portal available in multiple languages and on mobile phones. U.S. territories including Puerto Rico and American Samoa are included, with federal funds sent to those governments for local distribution.
ai_sections.cost: The summary does not specify a total program cost, but the federal government would fund payments to U.S. territories for local distribution in addition to direct payments to eligible individuals on the mainland.
costChips: Federal funds sent to U.S. territories

title: Modernizing Agricultural and Manufacturing Bonds Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to tax rules around government-issued bonds. First, it updates rules for "small issue manufacturing bonds," which are a type of tax-exempt bond that state and local governments can issue to help smaller manufacturers borrow money at lower interest rates. The bill raises the borrowing limits significantly — from $10 million to $30 million per project and from $40 million to $120 million total per company — and ties those limits to inflation going forward so they don't lose value over time. It also expands the definition of "manufacturing" to include facilities that produce certain intangible products, like software or other intellectual property described in the tax code.

Second, the bill changes rules for bonds that help first-time farmers buy land and equipment. Right now, these bonds — which work similarly by letting governments offer lower-cost financing — are capped at $450,000 per farmer. This bill raises that cap to $1 million and removes a separate, lower limit that applied specifically to used farm equipment. It also switches how "substantial farmland" is measured, using the average farm size in a county instead of the median, which can make more land eligible for the program.

The people most directly affected are small and mid-sized manufacturers looking to finance facility expansions or new production, and people trying to buy a farm for the first time. State and local governments that issue these bonds are also affected, since they administer these programs. The changes do not affect everyday consumers directly, but could influence local job markets and agricultural access in communities where these bonds are commonly used.
ai_headline: Senate bill triples borrowing limits for small manufacturers and first-time farmers
last_action_text: Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2100 raises tax-exempt bond caps for small manufacturers from $10M to $30M per project and for first-time farmers from $450K to $1M.
ai_sections.what: S 2100 raises the borrowing limits on two types of tax-exempt government bonds: small issue manufacturing bonds and first-time farmer bonds. For manufacturers, the per-project cap rises from $10 million to $30 million, and the per-company total rises from $40 million to $120 million, with both figures indexed to inflation going forward. For first-time farmers, the cap rises from $450,000 to $1 million, the separate limit on used farm equipment is removed, and the measure of qualifying farmland shifts from median to average county farm size.
ai_sections.who: Small and mid-sized manufacturers seeking to finance facility expansions or new production are directly affected, as are people buying a farm for the first time. State and local governments that issue and administer these bonds are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Raising the caps and indexing them to inflation means more projects and purchases could qualify for lower-cost government-backed financing over time. Shifting the farmland measure from median to average county size could make more land eligible for the first-time farmer program.

press_names: Modernizing Agricultural and Manufacturing Bonds Act
